Original_Search_Title: Christmas Then And Now...
Headline: Kenya: Christmas Then And Now...
Body_Text:
Nairobi — I don't have new clothes for Christmas. Oh, how I miss Christmas clothes. I think the only thing I miss about the day are the new clothes.
I would know it's Christmas because I smelled new, I smelled like a shop. New shoes, new shirt, new pair of jeans, always complete with a tag hanging from it.
And, of course, these came with a new walking style because new shoes always somehow made you walk funny. Growing up has robbed Christmas of some of its novelty. Today, Christmas has become sober and predictable. It has grown up.
Only children can truly enjoy Christmas because it has an air of spiritual romanticism. It's the bearded fellow who slinks down chimneys bearing gifts. It's the jingles and carols.
It's the flashy red and white colours and Christmas trees that blink in darkness. It's the tenderness that your parents address you with. And it's the food, lots of food. Plus they let you stay out late on Christmas. They let you become unruly.
Growing up, Christmas was mostly spent in the village. And nobody complained because it was fun. It was what I like to call an African Christmas.
Instead of snow, there was grass. We played on the grass and in the thickets, and we climbed trees, ate mangoes and watched the green hills.
Instead of reindeer, there were cows and goats. Family was big back then and family stayed together. Your uncles patted you on the head and asked you the questions you hated to be asked, "What position where you last term?"
If you had a good grade, they fished for loose change in their pockets and handed them to you. If you had dropped, they would lightly remind you the virtues of working hard. Most of those uncles have passed on.
Christmas meant watching grandfather wobble on his walking stick to his seat under the tree where he listened to an old National radio.
Grandpa was the closest thing to Santa Claus with his white hair. But grandpa wouldn't climb down any chimney, not in his clean pressed pants, shirt and tie. Back then, they wore ties to sit under a tree.
People respected themselves enough to dress decently. Christmas also meant going to Church and sitting for hours on end. And the truth is that church was boring because it cut into our play time.
We would fiddle uncomfortably in our chairs willing the time to pass quickly. But at the end of church, a big feast awaited. There was chicken and rice and rice and meats, and beans and cassava and vegetables and ugali.
It was a food festival and the whole extended family would eat and laugh together. And they would eat, not with forks and knives but with their fingers. I saw the biggest ugali being moulded into enormous lumps those days, a lump big enough to give you a concussion if it was hurled at your head.
But the feast didn't start before someone stood up to bless the food, and that someone would normally be an ageing person who obviously wasn't hungry enough because they would pray until the stew grew leaves.
The women would all be in colorful kitenges and the men in trousers and shirts firmly tucked in. Everybody was well-scrubbed. And people smiled a lot, even at strangers.
People reached out, they invited neighbours who didn't have to eat with them, even if they weren't dressed as nicely. And all this love was felt by the village dogs as well because apart from being well-fed from the left-overs, nobody kicked them around on Christmas day.
There was an air of generosity in the air. An air of togetherness. Christmas was that big then and we all looked forward to the day. Today, (maybe because I'm much older) Christmas is spent behind gates and long fences.
It is spent with family and sometimes friends as well. Alcohol seems to be the centre of gravity in such functions because perhaps that's the only way people can enjoy each other's company.
Children now ask about Santa Claus, not about grandpa and that's okay really because it's a different reality. The innocence is all but gone from Christmas because even when you are knocking back your whisky, there is always that niggling voice at the back of your mind saying, "Psst, school fees, school fees!"
Still, it's a great time to be thankful. A wonderful day to reflect on relationships. A good time to give without expecting something in return. The best gift you can give someone this Christmas is to extend a gesture of friendship. That, to me, is better than new clothes. Okay, almost.
Merry Christmas!

Original_Search_Title: Why Customs Can't Enforce New Import Policy
Headline: Nigeria: Why Customs Can't Enforce New Import Policy
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Nigeria Customs Service has said it cannot implement the new policy on textiles and other items that government announced their removal from the prohibition list until formal circular is received from the federal government.
According to a statement signed by the deputy public relations officer of the service, Joseph Attah, the Service does not formulate policies but implement them when so directed.
Although he acknowledged that the federal government did announced a new policy on textile and other items, he noted that no formal circular on the decision has been received by the Service to enable the Comptroller-General of Customs commence implementation.
He said the service did not refuse to implement the new policy as alleged in some media.
"For the avoidance of doubt, let it be known that the CGC, members of the management team and indeed every Customs officer welcome the review, as it will help to reduce smuggling as well as increase our revenue collection," the statement stated.
The statement however said it was wrong to assert that the CGC is delaying the implementation of a policy that will increase its revenue collection, adding that the service is ready and willing to implement the policy and indeed any other, as soon as government circular is received.
The federal government had a few weeks ago, removed some items, including textiles, furniture, toothpick and cassava among others from the import prohibition list and also approved the extension of age limit for vehicular imports to 15 years, which hitherto was pegged at 10 years.
The import trade policy reviews were contained in a memo sent to the controller-general of Customs and the three destination inspection service providers, Cotecna Inspection limited, Global Scan System and Societte Generale de Surveillance by the federal ministry of finance.
But in the last few weeks, various commands of the customs have continued to seize textiles and other imports already removed from the prohibition list, thereby eliciting criticism from the public.
For instance, the Apapa area one command of the service in conjunction with the newly inaugurated task force at the weekend announced the seizure of 23 containers, most of which are laden with textile materials.
But head of the task force, comptroller Edike Eporwei said the imports were seized because they contravened section 46 subsection b, c, d, and e of the Customs and Excise Management Act (CEMA).

Original_Search_Title: Iron Bar Wielding Thugs Kill Six in Mukono
Headline: Uganda: Iron Bar Wielding Thugs Kill Six in Mukono
Body_Text:
IRON-bar killers are on rampage again. The thugs have killed six people and injured several others in Mukono district within three weeks, causing fear among residents.
The thugs target mostly boda boda riders and women working in markets, whom they waylay at night.
Yesterday morning, a prominent Mukono florist, Godfrey Gamwanga, became the latest victim.
The killers, who reportedly move in groups, attacked Gamwanga at about 5:00am as he walked to his workplace in Ntaawo zone in Mukono town, where he operates a nursery bed of flowers and trees.
According to the Goma LC3 chairman, Erisha Mukasa Nkoyooyo, who was among the first people at the scene of crime, the gang hit Gamwanga with a metallic object.
Gamwanga's body was found near Kob Medical Care, a few metres from his home. Besides being neighbours, Nkoyoyo and Gamwanga were friends. Nkoyoyo said the killers went away with Gamwanga's two cell phones, and an unspecified amount of money.
Residents said several people had been attacked in a similar way and six of them killed. They blamed the Police for not providing enough security as the festive season approaches.
"We wonder why the Police are deployed in areas that have no problems, leaving the most dangerous unattended to," a resident said.
Others killed in the past three weeks include 20-year-old Violet Nalubwama, a student of Kampala University. She was killed on December 1, near Rider Hotel in Seeta. Residents said they heard her screaming and found her body in a trench with deep cuts on the head.
Henry Kabuubi, 29, a resident of Kisenyi-Namiryango village in Seeta, was killed on December 13 and his body dumped on a railway line. Residents suspected that he was attacked over a sh50,000 note he had during the day.
Also killed during the same period was Saba Munyanwali, a bodaboda rider, at Mukono Taxi Park. He was stabbed in the stomach.
In addition, Nalongo Namusisi, was struck at Kitega near Wantoni market in Mukono town as she went to work in the morning. The iron bar killers also murdered Reagan Amanya, another bodaboda rider in Seeta town, and took off with his motorcycle.
Other victims survived, but are still nursing wounds. These include Jackson Sseremba, a builder based in Mukono town, who was hit as he walked to Seeta town in the morning.
Moses Lubulwa and Moses Muhumuza, both bodaboda riders, are still undergoing treatment after they were hit and their motorcycles taken. Steven Lutalo and John Nkugwa also survived by a whisker. Last month, three people were killed and many injured in various towns of Mukono district.
Those killed include Josephine Namubiru, who was frying cassava in her stall near Rider Hotel at about 6:00am. The killers went away with her mobile phone and petty cash.
Saad Kalyango, a teacher at Vincent Alex Primary School, Dickson Ssentongo, a journalist attached to Prime Radio, were also murdered in a similar way.
Seeta Police Police boss Abdul Nasser recently said the Police were holding village meetings to sensitise the public on ways of avoiding such attacks. The Police criminal investigations chief, Edward Ochom, said the criminals were mostly former convicts.
A survivor's tale
Sam Ejibua, 33, said he was attacked by two iron bar-wielding men at 4:00am on December 11, as he walked to his house in Zone 9, Mutungo, Nakawa Division. He said the attack occurred on his way from a dinner at Hotel Africana.
Ejibua added that the thugs took off with his wallet and a Nokia mobile phone. The wallet contained sh250,000, my identity and ATM cards. The iron bar dislodged his molar tooth and broke the nasal bridge. He was saved by a neigbhour, who carried him to a nearby clinic.
"I reported the case to Kitintale Police post. The Police said they could not help me, not even to trace my phone. I lost interest in the matter," Ejibua said.

Original_Search_Title: Yet Another Industrial Policy Somersault
Headline: Nigeria: Yet Another Industrial Policy Somersault
Body_Text:
My tenure coincided with the tenure of three Presidents, four Finance Ministers, Four Ministers of Commerce & Industry and four Comptroller Generals of Customs . . . consistency of policy for at least 15 to 20 years is critical to economic takes off and sustained growth," Former MAN President, Alhaji Bashir Borodo.
Barley three months after Borodo uttered those words during Manufacturers Association of Nigeria 38th annual general meeting, stakeholders in the industrial sector were awakened to a morning of "yet another policy somersault."
To their chagrin, breaking news reported that the Federal Government's lifted the seven year's ban on some items, like textiles, furniture and cassava from the list of prohibited items.
The decision was contained in a circular issued by the Minister of Finance, Mr. Olusegun Aganga, to the Comptroller-General, Nigeria Customs Service. The government also approved an import duty rate tariffs of between 10 percent and 20 percent.
In a swift reaction, the National Union of Textile and Garment and Tailoring Workers of Nigeria,(NUTGTWN), said that wholesale import policy undermines local manufacturing firms.
The Union's General Secretary, Comrade Issa Aremu, observed that Nigeria is already a dumping ground for all manner of imported goods, including ammunition packaged as industrial goods.
"For government to approve the lifting of the ban is to further give legitimacy to criminal smuggling.
Coming at the time everybody has been so excited about the good work the current administration is doing to revive the textile industry, ban lift is a policy spanner thrown into the wheels of revival of industries in general."
"Import kills local industry and poses serious threat to the national economy," said Aremu
Similarly, the Director-General, Nigerian Textile Manufacturers Association, Mr. Jayeola Olanrewaju described the ban lifting as a set-back to government's efforts to revive the ailing textile sector.
"We cannot compete with imported textiles under the current business environment.
"If within one year the market is saturated with imported textiles, local industries will have problems selling and many of them will certainly close down,"he said.
He foresees that the lifting of the ban placed on the importation of textile materials may threaten the repayment of loans taken under the Textile Cotton Revival Fund.
According to him, the loan beneficiaries are being exposed to unfair competition through unbridled importation of textile materials. "The N100bn revival fund from which textile manufacturers borrowed to revitalise their operations, may be difficult to repay," he said.
The General Manager, Haffar Industrial Company Limited, Mr. Michael Adebayo, said the development was not in the interest of the nation's economy, adding that it negated every aspect of what the government preaches.
Adebayo recalled that stakeholders only proposed lifting of ban on some fabrics, like lace materials and guinea brocade that could not be produced locally. "We were taken aback that government did not take the advice and instead went ahead to lift the ban," he said.
Furniture and Allied Product Manufacturers Association of Nigerian (FAPMAN) also joined in thew wailing over the lifting of the ban on furniture products.
According to Mr Segun Adetiba, President FAPMAN, imported furniture should remained ban so that members of the association can enjoy what they have invested in the business.
He noted that the decision of the federal government to lift ban at this time may have far reaching negative consequences on the local manufacturers, as most local manufacturers will be forced to abandon production and start importation which will cause 1.23 million job loss, aggravate unemployment and criminal activities and insecurity of lives and property, total strangulation of the local market as patronage for locally made products may decline.
However, in the words of the Minister of Commerce and Industry, Senator Jubril Martins-Kuye, the new policy was devised to stem loss of revenue to government through smuggling activities at the country's borders.
"The reality on ground has informed government to streamlined policy on importation of goods on what operate in Benin republic, Togo and Ghana which would yield more revenue to government. We are not surrendering to the will and wish of World Trade Organisation (WTO). We are just doing what is right, proper and appropriate to our economy and for our People."
The Minister posited that if government continues to put goods that cannot be produce locally on the ban lists, unpatriotic Nigerians will find way to circumvent the law and the goods will find their way into our various markets and homes.
"So Nigeria has lost on both sides. The new duty and levy of 35-40 per cent would enable Nigeria Customs to generate more revenue for government which would eventually spread to other sector of the economy," said Martins-Kuye
Be that as it may be, the number of textile factories operating in the country is said to have declined to 25 from 124 (1996 statistic), while the number of workers in the industry has slumped to 24,000 from 130,000, what does the future hold for the industry under an opened door or liberalised market? We shall have to wait and see.

Original_Search_Title: There Are Many Antoinettes, Few Musevenis in Uganda
Headline: Uganda: There Are Many Antoinettes, Few Musevenis in Uganda
Body_Text:
I was in Paris recently and visited the place de' Concorde where the famous Queen Marie Antoinette was beheaded by the French commoners.
I also visited the famous city of Versailles to see the palace where Antoinette lived with her King Louis. Why did I go there? I wanted to stand at the point where Antoinette made her world wide known statement: "If you have no bread why don't you eat cakes?"
This statement was made to a hungry mob of French peasants who came to the palace crying out to their King about their lack of food. The Queen's response was cruel and showed that she was out of touch with the commoners' problems. This led to the French revolution in which the Queen was beheaded and the monarchy overthrown.
There are many lessons to learn from this. Are today's politicians in touch with the people or do we only seek them during election time to give them a dime for their vote? Because a dime is so dear to them, they accept it and wait for another, four-and-half years later.
For four years they wallow in poverty, nobody cares. Some sleep in makeshift shelters, use kaveera for toilet, which they toss onto rubbish heaps or into the Nakivubo Channel or in bushes.
They live side by side with waste, have jiggers from feet to their lips and private parts. They scavenge in their poorly attended to gardens for weeds, which they cook with cassava or sweet potatoes for their daily feed. In my community travels, I did not see a well attended to garden of vegetables; that is why I call it scavenging for a weed that is edible.
Museveni's era has worked so hard to reduce poverty. The Government has fulfilled all its obligations to reduce poverty. Therefore, its is wrong for the opposition to tell the masses that Museveni will leave them in poverty pakalast. Let us give credit where it is due even to our perceived enemies.
Indicators of poverty reduction are access to clean water, health services (within 5kms), universal education and access roads to communities. All these are the responsibilities of the Government. Whether you like Museveni or not, he has done very well in these areas and must be applauded.
However, there are other indicators of poverty reduction, which communities must provide for themselves. These include good shelter, hygiene, clothing and adequate food.
Nevertheless, Museveni has also done well in this area by promoting the Prosperity-for-all programme. When one sees us walking through people's gardens, peeping into their houses and latrines, it is not simply a joke. I would have expected every politician, whether in the Government or the opposition to do the same. But I have only seen two solitary individuals: Museveni and his deputy. As a result, many peasants have improved their way of life. Supposing many more politicians were doing this!
I see very many Antoinettes in this country; even those who lie to gain political capital are Antoinettes. If you tell people Museveni will keep them in poverty pakalast (forever), you are an Antoinette. If you say because Bukenya is a Catholic therefore sectarian, you are an Antoinette. Let us stick to issues and develop our country.
During the Luwero war, very few people in Kakiri, Masulita and Nakaseke thought the liberator would reach Kampala.
The Arch de Triumph reminded me of the Napoleonic wars that culminated in the building of this famous structure in the middle of Paris. Our people (the NRA) triumphed and ushered in a new system of democratic rule, eliminated state-inspired killings and brought about development. Is it not time to build the Ugandan Arch de Triumph somewhere in the arteries leading to Kampala? It is good to remember history.
When I visited the Eiffel Tower, a Frenchman told me that in the beginning, the French did not like the tower and they wanted to pull it down several times. However, they are now so proud of it and over 10 million tourists visit it every year.
Sometimes, I hear calls of "Agende, Agende" to our Eiffel Tower, what a pity! Leave him alone; some people still want to see him around.
The last 20 years or so have been the best years for Uganda. Now I go to all corners of the world and whenever I say I am a Ugandan, the reply is: "You have done well." This is a big contrast from what it used to be in the previous regimes. Let us give our President a massive thank you vote pakalast.
Looking back now, I know that by now I would be long dead if it were not for the patience of Museveni. I have been accused by high ranking politicians of mobilising to overthrow him. I looked at him and there he was; composed, calm and smiling. I feared the smile because I was told former president Idi Amin used to smile and in another hour you would be dead. I once told this to my mother, she shed a tear of happiness and said a prayer for me, thanking the Virgin Mary for protection.
There are many Antoinettes in our society, including big political leaders but very few Musevenis. Now that I am still alive, I will give him my vote, and so will my mother and several of my relatives, clansmen and in-laws. Those who tell lies will perish like Antoinette.

Original_Search_Title: Lifting of Ban On Textiles, Others, Tragic for Local Industries, Labour
Headline: Nigeria: Lifting of Ban On Textiles, Others, Tragic for Local Industries, Labour
Body_Text:
Organised Labour in the nation's Textile industry, has faulted Government lifting of ban on the importation of textile materials, furniture, toothpicks and cassava products while raising the age limit on importation of vehicles into the country from 10 to 15 years is indeed tragic for a country that is striving to be one of the 20 leading industrialists.
Under the umbrella of the National Union of Textile and Garment and Tailoring Workers of Nigeria, NUTGTWN, lamented that the recent lifting of the ban on importation of Textile fabrics and others was a complete policy somersault from the policy thrust of the federal government which aimed at growing non-oil sector in general and manufacturing in particular.
In a statement by its General Secretary, Comrade Issa Aremu, the union argued that, "The lifting of the ban on the importation of textile materials, furniture, toothpicks and cassava products by the Federal government succumbing to World Bank pressure while also raising the age limit on importation of vehicles into the country from 10 to 15 years is indeed tragic for a country that is striving to be one of the 20 leading industrialists.
The government banned the importation of textile materials and a number of products such as furniture in response to mass pressure from National Union of Textile Garment and Tailoring Workers of Nigeria (NUTGTWN) and Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) that wholesale importation undermines local manufacturing firms.
The current lifting of ban is a complete policy somersault from the policy thrust of the federal government which is aimed at growing non-oil sector in general and manufacturing in particular. Wholesale and indiscriminate import of goods such as toothpicks, textiles, furniture on which Nigeria has comparative advantage has been one of the failure-factors that killed local industries. Previous governments of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo and late Alhaji Umaru Musa Yar'Adua following engagements with critical stakeholders put a legitimate ban on some of these products that Nigeria has comparative advantage.
Even at that the ban was abused due to lack of capacity and genuine commitment of Customs and the Standard Organisation of Nigeria (SON) to monitor import and enforce standards respectively."Nigeria is already a dumping ground for all manner of imported goods, including ammunition packaged as industrial goods.

Original_Search_Title: BATN Foundation Inaugurated Anambra Community Centre
Headline: Nigeria: BATN Foundation Inaugurated Anambra Community Centre
Body_Text:
Awka — The British America Tobacco Nigeria (BATN) Foundation has commissioned a Vocational Training Centre for the Ndikelionwu community in Orumba North Local Government Area of Anambra State.
The Executive Director of the foundation, Mr. Gbenga Ibikunle, said at the commissioning ceremony that Nigeria can solve her unemployment problems by the establishment of vocational institutions.
He said it was in response to employment needs of Nigeria that BATN Foundation has supported vocational enterprise institutions in Jere and Kaduna in Kaduna State; the Blind School at Oshodi, Akinyele School for the Handicapped and the Business Apprenticeship Training College, Zaria through infrastructure rehabilitation and development.
"The large army of Nigerian youths who are unable to gain admission to tertiary institutions can also avail themselves of the great opportunities in vocational education," Ibikunle said.
The centre at Ndikelionwu is fully equipped to cater for trainings in Information and Communication Technology (ICT), Computer Studies, Cookery and Soap Making.
The BATN Foundation's executive director thanked the traditional ruler of Ndikelionwu and famous novelist, Prof. Chukwuemeka Ike, and the National Directorate of Employment for their assistance in getting the centre started off.
The traditional ruler of the community said the vocational centre was a follow-up to the establishment of a Cassava Processing Mill funded by BATN.
"I took them to a bungalow I built in 1964 (when I was the Registrar of the University of Nigeria) to empower my parents economically in their old age. They considered the complex suitable, and undertook to rehabilitate it," Ike said of the building housing the vocational centre.
Deputy Governor of Anambra State, Mr. Emeka Sibeudu, who officially inaugurated the centre, said the government would encourage people who are prepared to bring good things to Anambra State. He decried the emigration from rural areas, and urged youths to explore the rich resources that abound in rural areas.
He said the process of recruiting teachers to the service of the state was still ongoing, explaining that the delay in the process was caused by the need to ensure that the applicants met set standards.

Original_Search_Title: Ogun, Shandong Province Develop Industrial Garden
Headline: Nigeria: Ogun, Shandong Province Develop Industrial Garden
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — The growing bilateral economic relationship between Ogun State and the Shandong Province of China has further been consummated with the visit of Chinese leading businessmen and economic leaders from the region and the commencement of the development of an industrial garden.
The economic delegation which ended its three-day visit to Ogun State on Wednesday came to cement investment deals in paper and pulp manufacturing, solar energy, winery, cassava processing and hospitality.
According to a statement by the Chief Press Secretary to Ogun State Governor, Mr. Gbenro Adebanjo, the delegation led by Director General of Shandong Provincial Economic and Information Technology Committee, Mr. Guo Shuyu came under the auspices of the Ogun Shandong Business Council which was established last year when the Ogun State Governor Otunba Gbenga Daniel led an economic delegation to China on an investment drive.
The executive secretary of the Council, Mathew Li, said they were in Ogun to do a follow up on the various understanding reached whenw the Governor visited Shandong, one of China's provincial economic giants.
He said during the visit they explored business and investment opportunities and also began preliminary works on the industrial garden which is being developed in the Ikenne axis of the state.
Executive director of the Ogun Shandong Business Council, Otunba Lawal Solarin said the visit was a milestone as the mutually beneficial economic relationship between both states has now been firmly established.
He said business men from Ogun State could take advantage of this when they are in China as the office of the council in Jinan, Shandong was already operational while the counterpart office at OPIC building, Abeokuta has also been opened.
The Ogun-Shandong Business Council is entirely a private sector initiative co-sponsored by the Ogun and Shandong governments.
It is established towards promoting, protecting, and developing all matters affecting commerce, industry and other businesses within the purview of the relationship between Ogun State and the Province of Shandong.

Original_Search_Title: Manufacturers Fault Revised Import Prohibition List
Headline: Nigeria: Manufacturers Fault Revised Import Prohibition List
Body_Text:
Stakeholders in the manufacturing sector have kicked against the federal government for lifting the ban on some items on the import prohibition list.
The federal government last week Tuesday lifted the ban on the importation of textiles, furniture, cassava and extending the age limit of second-hand vehicles from 10 year to 15 years and other sundry items,
The stakeholders who expressed their views condemned the development and described it as insensitivity on the part of government.
They were of the view that lifting of the ban was a set-back on government's efforts to revive the textile sector as well as a disservice to the nation's economy.
Secretary-General, National Union of Textile, Garments and Tailoring Workers of Nigeria (NUTGWN), Mr. Isa Aremu, said the action was a set-back on the economy and would jeopardise the industry.
Aremu said: "The forces to legalise importation of textile products have been on for a long time. Why should the government concede to it now?
"The administration that put the ban in place has vision and should not be truncated for any reason. The ban is till very valid.
" The Asians behind it should be made to set up factories in Nigeria. We must look at the reasons why government should now find importation more attractive," he added.
He warned that Nigeria 's race towards year 2020 target to join the league of most industrialised nations would remain a myth unless the problem of infrastructures, stable policies and protection are put in place.
He regretted that government should have properly implemented the ban rather than lifting it.
His words: "More textile industries in Nigeria are dying. They have struggled for too long without success to swim through the troubled waters of poor infrastructure, high taxation, and activities of illegal importers and inconsistency of government policy."

Original_Search_Title: Government's New Import Circular
Headline: Nigeria: Government's New Import Circular
Body_Text:
In a recent Import Circular addressed to the Comptroller-General of Nigeria Customs Service (NCS) as well as to key import control officers and signed by the Honourable Minister of Finance, Olusegun Aganga, government released new import duties and levies, while removing a number of products from the country's import prohibition list.
According to the circular, cassava products would henceforth attract an import duty of 20% and a 15% levy, while toothpicks would attract a 20% duty and an additional 20% levy; same as furniture and textiles fabrics. In addition, some health and energy drinks (Power Horse, Red Ginseng etc) have been removed from the prohibition list, and are now to attract a 10% levy, while it extended the age of vehicles which can be imported into the country from 10 to 15 years.
This, for all practical purposes is a welcome development given that Nigeria over the years has lost several billions of Naira essentially due to the activities of smugglers, who prefer to patronize the ports of neighbouring countries from where they bring these same goods into the country. A recent World Bank report indicates that about N600 billion cargos of banned goods usually escaped the customs and entered Nigeria's markets principally from the Cotonou Port in the Republic of Benin. This represents about 15% of Nigeria's annual imports. Similarly, about 90% of Benin's poultry imports are smuggled into Nigeria, where frozen poultry imports are banned. Moreover, older fairly used cars also find their ways into Nigeria, through the same sources in spite of governments' ban on cars beyond ten years.
It is equally estimated that Nigeria looses as much as N750 billion annually to smugglers and corrupt custom officials as a result of the ban on textiles alone. Hence, government's latest action may possibly eliminate this drain pipe by ensuring that such loopholes often exploited by smugglers and corrupt customs officials are permanently blocked even as the accruing revenues would now go into the coffers of government. In any case, what indeed is the use of banning, when the same products end up finding their way into the country? Who would have for instance believed that lace materials or even energy drinks, which have hitherto flooded our markets, were prohibited items? The truth of the matter is that the long list of prohibited goods has facilitated substantial smuggling and consequent rent seeking by customs officials since such bans are often difficult to enforce given the country's porous borders.
It will be recalled that Nigeria's main trade policy instruments from the mid 1970s shifted markedly away from tariffs to quantitative import restrictions, particularly import prohibition and import licensing. At the same time, once goods are prohibited, it is the consumers that bear the burden of higher prices, poor quality and limited variety of locally produced alternatives even as prohibition is usually rendered impotent by large scale smuggling. Need we say that this has continued in spite of stiff penalties imposed on those involved?
Nevertheless, we should not see the latest action undertaken through the government action as an end in itself. At best, it should be interpreted as a stop gap measure, indeed as a means to an end. On the surface both the World Trade Organisation [WTO] as well as the globalisation process it has facilitated preach and promote open trade, yet the challenge for our policy markers remains how best to protect the country's infant industries by ensuring that they are not over exposed to unfair competition from bigger and more established firms. Some level of restriction may be necessary and every country does it. We do not support the total opening of our borders to imported goods, which obviously will turn us into a dumping ground and further destroy our economic aspirations.
Of course, the least the government can do to assist the local manufacturers is to ensure a friendly business environment in terms of infrastructure support, eliminating multiple taxation and enhancing access to cheap credit. It is self evident that no business operating in Nigeria at the moment can ever compete in the global environment given the almost total absence of a conducive operating environment. It is possibly against this background that several manufacturers operating in the country now see the latest government circular on imports as double jeopardy, given that they lack a friendly business environment, while the little protection that they have hitherto enjoyed from foreign competition has now been removed.
We therefore call on government to redefine its policy especially in terms of providing definite timeframes under which the new tariff regime will operate; initiating concrete long term strategies aimed at improving Nigeria's business environment; and, re-strategising to enhance local productivity. It is only then that we will have something definite to cheer about.

Original_Search_Title: Policy Upturn on Textile Materials And Others
Headline: Nigeria: Policy Upturn on Textile Materials And Others
Body_Text:
The lifting of ban on the importation of textile materials, furniture, cassava products and toothpicks, no doubt, has the capacity to affect the nation's social and economic life fundamentally.
The same could be said of the raising of the age limit of imported vehicles from 10 to 15 years. So, to check whatever negative consequences that might occur as a result of those measures, the government needs to constantly monitor with a view to reviewing and, if necessary, reversing its decisions.
Government must not hesitate to yield to any superior judgment in the months ahead if it hopes to achieve economic equilibrium. The very argument that warranted the imposition of the embargo two years ago makes government's continued responsiveness pertinent. Spearheaded by the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN), stakeholders in the economic sector had contended that the situation that allowed the shipment of such items into the country could only compound the already parlous condition of local manufacturers, as they would find it extremely difficult to compete favourably with makers of imported goods.
It is understandable therefore, that pro-ban bodies like MAN are now criticising the new official resolution - what appears to be a policy somersault. However, it is noteworthy that in removing the commodities from the import prohibition list, the government has simultaneously placed a 15 per cent surcharge and 20 per cent duty on them. The government, in doing so, hopes to discourage massive dumping of the items in the country. The figures will be upwardly reviewed as time goes on, according to reports.
Also, the explanation by the Minister of Finance, Mr. Olusegun Aganga, on the matter the other day provides the rationale behind the latest government's action. As he put it, "Duties ranging from 30 per cent to 40 per cent will be imposed on products including furniture, textiles and drinks. The ban on the items was 'ineffective,' and led to smuggling from neighbouring countries. That meant the government lost revenue." He also declared that part of the monies so derived will be used to boost the capacity of the Bank of Industries (BOI) to finance small and medium businesses and even large industries like textile.
On the face value, that clarification should re-assure the local manufacturers and economic watchers about government's intentions. In any case, while it is its responsibility to protect domestic production, it is also expected to ensure that if local supply fails to meet demand, the citizens do not suffer endlessly. Care should also be taken to consider other conflicting but legitimate interests. Freight forwarders and importers, for instance, have always claimed that the type of ban that has just been lifted constitutes an obstacle to international trade. Some may even see it as undue protectionism.
Ultimately, the government should, at least, urgently address the factors that incapacitate the local manufacturing of such materials that do not require hi-technology. Issues like unavailable or prohibitive credit facilities and decrepit infrastructure, particularly public electricity, ought to have been adequately taken care of long ago. Although no nation on earth is completely self-sufficient, it is a mark of gross underdevelopment for any country to interminably import items it can easily produce.
For, at stake are critical concerns like job creation and sustainability, micro and macro prosperity, national pride and the overall wellbeing of the people. So, pragmatic steps must be taken to ensure that this nation moves beyond the temporary benefits of the de-listing of these items from the import restriction list. Focusing on the more enduring merits should engage our policy makers now.

Original_Search_Title: Publisher Holds Party Over Shoneyin's Novel
Headline: Nigeria: Publisher Holds Party Over Shoneyin's Novel
Body_Text:
A book party for the novel, The Secret Lives of Baba Segi's Wives, written by poet Lola Shoneyin, will hold on Friday, December 17, at the French Cultural Centre in Abuja.
The event, is organised by Cassava Republic Press and is presented in collaboration with the French Cultural Centre. It will feature music, entertainment and a reading by the author.
There will be a gate fee of N1,500 per person, which includes a copy of the book. The book, which has won rave reviews in the United States and the United Kingdom, describes the upheaval that follows when polygamist Baba Segi marries his fourth wife, Bolanle. A graduate amongst the other semi-literate wives, Bolanle, is hated from the start. Worse, her arrival threatens to do more than simply ruffle feathers. She's unwittingly set to expose a secret that her co-wives intend to protect, at all costs.
Known for her poetry, this is Shoneyin's first novel, and it is a light and ironic exposé of the rotten innards of Baba Segi's polygamous household. In this cleverly-plotted story, Shoneyin shows how women in contemporary Nigeria can be restricted, controlled and damaged by the men in their lives.
Cassava Republic Press was founded in 2006 and based in Abuja. It publishes high-quality fiction and non-fiction for adults, teens and children. Lola Shoneyin was born in Ibadan, Oyo State, and educated at the Ogun State University and the London Metropolitan University. Her first collection of poetry, So All the Time I Was Sitting On An Egg, was nominated for the ANA poetry prize in 1997 and her work has appeared in several international journals such as Callaloo, Orbis and Glendora Review. She is married with four children.
The French Cultural Centre is located at 52 Libreville Street, off Aminu Kano Crescent, Wuse II. Gate opens at 6 pm.

Original_Search_Title: Don't Turn Country Into a Dumping Ground, Atiku Warns
Headline: Nigeria: Don't Turn Country Into a Dumping Ground, Atiku Warns
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Atiku Abubakar Campaign Organisation has expressed reservations over the plan by the federal government to lift the ban on the importation of textile and sundry items such as tooth picks and cassava, noting that it appears that the President Goodluck Jonathan administration has run out of ideas on how to run the economy.
It would be recalled that the Finance Minister, Olusegun Aganga took the decision because the World Bank had complained that the nation's list of banned items was "unnecessarily too long" and also to check smuggling and increase the revenue accruable to the federal government from import duties.
"While we condemn the activities of smugglers, it is questionable whether the unreflective opening up of the nation's economy to the dumping of all sorts of imported goods, including tooth picks, is the best way to go about it", the campaign organization said yesterday in a statement.
The campaign organisation noted that smuggling exists partly because of the high cost of doing business in Nigeria, which not only militates against the growth of local industries but also makes local products more expensive relative to what you find in other countries.
It recalled that a survey conducted as part of its membership operational audit in January 2010 by the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN), showed that 839 manufacturing companies closed shop in 2009 as a result of harsh operating environment.
"Wouldn't focusing on improving the environment for businesses in Nigeria be a more efficient way of combating smuggling while simultaneously stimulating industrialisation and job creation?" it queried.
We are rather aghast that the Finance Minister, who had claimed that "the economy is too technical for some Nigerians to understand" seems, by this move, to be contradicting the objectives of earlier government policies.
"It is curious that the same government which in June 2010 approved the release of N24 billion for the resuscitation of the ailing textile industries in Kaduna and also approved N15 billion for the Kaduna Independent Power Project (IPP) could initiate a policy in negation of that initiative.
One would think that the objective of the government's support in June was to resuscitate these textile mills and nurture them to health and maturity. Now the minister has opened the doors for massive importation of textiles without considering the possible impact of this on the government's plan to resuscitate the local textile industry. Where is then the economic nationalism that should inform economic policy?", the statement concluded.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Lifts Ban On Textile Materials, Toothpicks
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Lifts Ban On Textile Materials, Toothpicks
Body_Text:
The Federal Government has made a volte-face on its economic policy, as it has lifted the ban placed on the importation of textile materials, furniture, toothpicks and cassava products.
It also raised the age limit placed on importation of vehicles into the country, from 10 to 15 years.
The Federal Government had in 2008 placed a ban on all types of textile materials into the country.
The new economic policy, which is contained in a circular signed by the Minister of Finance, Mr. Olusegun Aganga, has been distributed to the Nigeria Customs Service (NCS) and Destination Inspection Agents (DIAs).
In removing the items from the import prohibition list, the government introduced a 20 per cent duty on the products and a 15 per cent surcharge as new levy.
The circular stated that government had also introduced 10 per cent duty rate and another 10 per cent levy on health and energy drinks which had been excluded from import prohibition.
This followed the pressure from the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) which argued that allowing importation of textile materials into the country was killing local manufacturing firms.
However, since the ban, importers and freight forwarders have cried out that such ban was a stumbling block to international trade.
It is envisaged that the new economic policy is likely to pitch manufacturers against the Federal Government.
Also in the circular, government raised the age limit of vehicles to be imported into the country from 10 years to 15 years.
What this means is that older fairly used cars can now come into the country through approved routes instead of the current trend where such older vehicles are smuggled in through unapproved border routes.
A source close to the Ministry of Finance told THISDAY yesterday that one of the reasons why government removed textile materials from import prohibition list was to check the high rate of smuggling them into the country.
Freight forwarders, who spoke to THISDAY, commended the Federal Government for the new policy, saying the measure would check years of smuggling of the products into the country.
Founder, National Associa-tion of Government Approved Freight Forwarders (NAGAFF), Dr. Boniface Aniebonam, said the measure showed that the present administration is a listening government.
Aniebonam said unbanning the items, particularly textile materials and the review on age limit on vehicles to be imported would address the problems of smuggling.
Meanwhile, Nigeria will post a budget deficit of about 6.1 per cent of its Gross Domestic Product (GDP) this year following a drop in its projected revenue, Finance Minister Olusegun Aganga has said.
Bloomberg quoted the finance minister yesterday to have put the estimated expenditure this year at N5.16 trillion just as he placed the forecast revenue at N3.18 trillion.
"There were shortfalls in both oil and non-oil revenue," the minister was quoted to have said.
The National Assembly had approved a N4.67 trillion budget after making additions and other changes. The revision was as a result of a drop in oil prices and additional expenditures to cover increased pay and expenses due to next year's election.
On plans to impose tariff on goods that had been previously prohibited from being imported into the country to protect domestic manufacturers and boost revenue, Aganga said: "Duties ranging from 30 per cent to 40 per cent will be imposed on products including furniture, textiles and drinks. The ban on the items was 'ineffective,' and led to smuggling from neighbouring countries that meant the government lost revenue.
"Part of the proceeds from the tariffs will be used to fund the state-owned Bank of Industries, which lends to small businesses while some of it will also be used to support the textile industry."
The finance minister also reiterated that he expects the proposed Sovereign Wealth Fund (SWF) for investing savings during periods of high oil revenue to become operational before the end of the tenure of the current government in May next year.
The Federal Government plans to start the SWF, which Aganga said might improve the country's rating with Fitch Ratings, with $1 billion.
With Agency Report

Original_Search_Title: Presidency, National Assembly Trade Words Over Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Presidency, National Assembly Trade Words Over Economy
Body_Text:
Benin City — Speaker of the House of Representatives, Hon. Demeji Bankole has accused Ministries, Departments and Agencies of government of collecting N3 trillion, adding that investigations by the National Assembly have revealed that such money was collected without remitting same to the Federal Treasury.
Also, the Governor of Central Bank (CBN), Mallam Sanusi Lamido Sanusi has said the bulk of federal government spending ends at the door step of the National Assembly because according to him, 25 per cent of the federal government budget spending was always given to that arm of government alone.
Just as Speaker Bankole announced this in Benin, while delivering the 35/36th Convocation Lecture of the University of Benin titled, "The Challenges and Prospects of Transforming the Nigerian Democratic Polity", the CBN Governor made the revelation while delivering the 8th Convocation Lecture of Igbinedion University, Okada with the topic: "Future Prospect of Nigeria Economy".
According to the Speaker, the renewed commitment of the National Assembly to oversight activities as it relates to tracking and monitoring of budget implementation, has led to the recovery of over N750 billion unspent funds in two years.
"Before now, such discoveries and re-channeling of the unspent funds for the good people was unheard of," Rt. Hon. Bankole said, adding that the National Assembly maintains that the rule of law, which is one of the tenets of democracy, must be followed.
Quoting the 2010 UNDP Human Development Index which ranked Nigeria 142 in the Low Human Development Category out of 169 countries surveyed globally, the Speaker observed that with a reservoir of highly talented and resourceful persons, Nigeria has no business being identified with nations on UNDP Low Human Development Category.
He therefore solicited for investment in education, saying that the nation stood to benefit from positive development of increased investment in education.
"It is a known fact that, without quality research which results in improvement in Science and Technological inventions, we cannot solve the multi-faceted problems of development currently starring Nigeria on the face the face", he said.
Bankole, while disclosing that the electoral reforms of the National Assembly were targeted at greater transparency and credibility of elections, called on Nigerian youth to refuse to be used for electoral rigging and political violence by self serving politicians.
He advocated for a return to the practice of fiscal federalism as the basis for speedy development of the country, pointing out that a situation where Councils and some States become actively only after they return from the Federation Accounts Allocation Committee meeting in Abuja was not sustainable.
Bankole therefore, called for all hands to be on deck, saying that the current efforts of President Goodluck Jonathan, especially in the areas of Power supply, critical infrastructure and electoral reforms have combined to commence the process of laying a solid foundation for sustainable national development.
On his part, Mallam Sanusi said that in an economy that is monolithic and import dependence that is susceptible to shock either through global oil prices or the activities of militants in the Niger Delta, such huge spending on one arm of government was unnecessary.
He described Nigeria as a country that imports what it produces and exports what it does not have.
According to him, "Nigeria is the sixth world producer of crude oil, 8th in natural gas, but imports petroleum products; Nigeria finds it difficult to get the tenets of democracy but exported democracy to Liberia; it is a country that finds it difficult to generate 4,000 MWT of electricity, but exports power to Niger Republic; it is the world's number one in terms of cassava production, but no starch and ethanol industry that can create jobs to engage its teaming graduates".
The CBN boss opined that because such structural deficiency and policy problem, government finds it difficult to create job, adding "the problem in the country is not about knowing the solution to the problems, but because the solutions is not in the interest of a small group of people who always want to hold the country to ransom".
He noted that if Nigeria desire to develop it must get its policies right, break away from the past (dictates of foreign powers), encourage the establishment of private refineries and focus on reform.
"The major reason why Asian economy is advancing while that of Nigeria and most African countries retrogress is that whereas Asian Countries say no to IMF loan and its conditionality, Nigeria and other African Countries are saying yes", Sanusi said.

Original_Search_Title: Author to Challenge Plagiarised Fela's Story in Court
Headline: Nigeria: Author to Challenge Plagiarised Fela's Story in Court
Body_Text:
Lagos — Cassava Republic has disclosed that sections of the Carlos Moore autobiography, Fela: This Bitch of a Life, which the author claims were used without his permission in the successful Broadway musical, "Fela!" are available only in the Nigeria edition published by Cassava Republic Press.
The two sections entitled "Afa Ojo," more commonly known as the "Black Pages," are a mystical side of Fela's mother calling her son to continue the struggle. Moore is suing the producers of the musical on the grounds that the Black Pages were lifted almost verbatim from the original book even though Moore is not acknowledged as one of the writers of the production, nor is he part of the royalty pool. The Cassava Republic Press version of the biography is the only edition which features the Black Pages. Other versions sold in the United States and the United Kingdom do not.
Earlier this month, Moore asked the courts to close the show, claiming playwright Jim Lewis and director Bill T. Jones stole his work. In the $5 million copyright suit, the author says in 2007 he was offered $4,000 for the rights to his biography, but rejected the sum as "grossly insufficient."
Moore says he was asked to consult on the show in 2009, and believed that he would be compensated for this work. But, according to court papers, after he demanded "an advance and participation in the pool," no other offers were made. Moore claims that his book was to create the musical without his "knowledge, authorisation or consent." The lawsuit also said Moore also was never compensated for a videotaped interview he did to support the show.
It would be recalled that Carlos Moore wrote the official biography of Fela Kuti in 1982 after extensive interviews with the "Abami Eda" at home in Lagos. At the time, Fela had confessed to Moore that he felt suicidal. Years of police beatings and attacks by the authorities had taken their toll. On his return to Paris to write up the tapes, Moore decided to include two mystical sections where he imagined Fela's mother, Funmilayo Ransome-Kuti, speaking to her son and urging him to continue living. "We will never know whether these deeply spiritual pages encouraged Fela to keep on - all we know is that he was ecstatically happy with the finished work," says Cassava Republic.
The Cassava Republic Press version of the re-published biography (available in Nigeria) is therefore a collector's item for those fortunate to be able to get hold of this version. The book includes a brilliant introduction and a moving and frank epilogue by Carlos which contextualises the 1982 story in light of a reassessment of Fela's legacy.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Rallies Community Support in Fighting GBV
Headline: Rwanda: Minister Rallies Community Support in Fighting GBV
Body_Text:
Gisagara — The Minister of Gender and Family Promotion, Dr Jeanne d'Arc Mujawamariya, on Thursday, launched "16 days of activism" in Gisagara District, calling for more efforts from the community in the fight against Gender Based Violence (GBV).
The international event is celebrated annually to create awareness against gender-based violence.
Mujawamariya called on residents of the district to be on the look out for any suspected cases of GBV and report them to relevant authorities.
"You should speak out against any case of GBV in your communities, you should keep a watchful eye on what happens to your neighbour and ensure timely reporting of such cases for investigation," she told residents of Muganza Sector.
Muganza Sector has registered tremendous progress in scaling down cases of GBV through local initiatives. Residents have established a special intervention team which is called in to intervene in suspected cases of GBV.
Members of these teams have been equipped with mobile telephones which they use to communicate to law enforcement organs in case of suspected crime.
The Sector has also established a special evening for women, where they meet and discuss issues they are faced with, including conflicts within their homes.
This year's campaign is being marked under the theme: "Strengthen the collaboration of the security organs, population and other instances in ending all forms of violence against women."
Dr. Mujawamariya also noted that fighting GBV goes in tandem with fighting illiteracy and poverty, noting that the two are major causes of conflict in homes.
She warned against sending children, especially girls, on night errands and called for the legalisation of marriages as one way of ensuring harmony amongst couples and their children.
Citing the Land Law, MP Winifried Niyitegeka noted that a number of laws have been enacted in support of the fight against GBV.
"Most of the complaints that we receive are land related. We need cooperation from all stakeholders to ensure that this and other laws are applied," she said.
Figures from the Rwanda National Police indicate a remarkable decrease in reported cases of GBV.  A Police report states that 1,345 GBV cases were reported in 2010, compared to 2,033 in 2006. It also shows that 264 rape cases were reported this year, down from 403 in 2006.
The report also indicates that 16 women were killed by their spouses between 2007 and 2009 while 35 women were killed in 2006 alone.
The event was preceded by the construction of houses for three vulnerable women and cultivation of cassava on over five hectares of land.
According to Franklin Murangira, from Care International- one of the partners in the campaign, discussions and sensitisation exercises will continue at village level and 171 couples are expected to formalise their marriages during this period.

Original_Search_Title: Health Report Unearthes 200 Ghost Workers
Headline: Uganda: Health Report Unearthes 200 Ghost Workers
Body_Text:
A special investigation unit set up by President Yoweri Museveni has unearthed over 200 ghost health workers on the payroll.
The unit has also established that several other health workers are always absent, while others are on study leave but are still getting their full monthly salaries.
This is in addition to centres, which sources say could be about 110. The health centres listed by the health ministry are non-existent, according to a new report on health care delivery in the country.
The report filed by a unit which monitors the country's health system, also says Uganda is losing about sh26b annually due to absenteeism of workers from health units.
The country's loss, according to the report, is being estimated at a daily health worker absentee levels at 37%.
But the new report shows a grim picture of the real average absenteeism levels to be as high as 88% in some health centres, meaning that the sh26b loss could be an underestimation.
The unit started work in 2009 and it is headed by Dr. Diana Atwine.
Ghost health centres
Sources in the health ministry revealed that the existence of about 110 health centres is still being questioned.
The unit has so far combed through only half of the country's districts, and reports that the "central inventory list of health facilities was also not regularly updated."
For instance in northern Uganda, health facilities that closed during the insurgency were still on the list, while new health units built after the war are not reflected.
Another case is that of Igayaza health centre II in Kibaale district, which was listed as a health centre IV.
In the last financial year, two health facilities, formerly in Nyadri, received sh105m as development fund.
One of them is Burua health centre II, which does not exist, and Cilio health centre III, which has been receiving money for constructing a maternity ward but has never put it in place.
The team also unearthed three ghost health centres of Biso, Bugoigo and Buhaba in Buliisa district. They had been receiving medicine for over four years.
Most of the non-existent facilities are health centres II and IIIs,. It is suspected that the fraud is the work of officials in the districts collaborating with officials from the health ministry.
The registration of health centres is done at the recommendation of the district health officers, who are also responsible for their supervision.
The report adds that about 2,400 government health centres receive drugs from the National Medical Stores.
Ghost workers
Ghost health workers were also discovered on government payrolls in a number of districts, including Mukono, Soroti, Gulu and Kampala districts.
The report also noted that payrolls were not updated and still had names of retired or dead workers.
The report cited Mulago Hospital, where the names of over 100 workers appeared more than once on the personnel's list.
It was noted that most people, who retired or are transferred, and others who absconded duty, continue to receive salaries.
The unit also notes that only a handful of staff members at Mulago are always on duty.
"Many staff members were found to work in HIV/malaria projects and are paid to work full-time, while at the same time they are full-time employees of Mulago. This means that they are illegally paid twice," it said.
Absenteeism
The inspection unit visited over 140 health facilities and found about 88% of health workers were absent during official hours. Staff audits conducted also confirmed an extremely high level of absenteeism.
In Mukono, the report says, 88% of in-charges from the 42 health facilities visited were absent. Some of the reasons given for this were workshop or seminar attendance and administrative duties.
Illegal fees and sale of drugs
Since the early 1990s, the Government has given high priority to the improvement of the health sector, including abolition of user fees, construction of health centres, scaling up immunisation exercises, decentralisation and allocating additional funding to the health sector.
However, the unit found that some health facilities continue to charge user fees, thereby undermining the existing policy framework.
It cited Lusalirwa and Mundade health centres in Mubende ask patients, where patients are asked to bring food stuffs like cassava and eggs in exchange for treatment.
The report also notes that Koch Goma health centre III in Amuru was found to get money from expectant mothers as an 'appreciation' for the midwife's services.
Sections, including surgery, radiology, labour ward, as well as the general ward, in Mulago were reported to levy charges on patients.
The report says Mulago administration confirmed some of these findings and acknowledged some of the fees levied on patients.
Other health facilities were found selling medicines that are meant to be dispensed free-of-charge.
The reported highlighted Mulago, saying large consignments of government medicines are sold in the private wing pharmacy as the general wards and pharmacies experience massive stock-outs.
Understaffing
Over 65% of health facilities visited were found to be severely understaffed.
The most common explanation given was that it was impossible to fill all the vacancies without exceeding the budget ceiling.
In other districts, people declined to take up their appointments, citing poor remuneration.
In hard-to-reach districts, there were no applicants when positions were advertised.
The report recommends a fundamental overhaul of the health sector.

Original_Search_Title: Bwanika - I Am Still the New Kid On the Block
Headline: Uganda: Bwanika - I Am Still the New Kid On the Block
Body_Text:
In 2006, a fairly new face was thrust on the Ugandan political scene. Spotting a neatly kept beard and speaking in a baritone, the 39-year-old Dr Abed Bwanika seemed to take everyone by surprise when he ran for the presidency.
That he was able to raise signatures from all over the country for nomination--and even posted more votes than Mama Miria Obote, who had the backing of the Uganda Peoples Congress party-confirmed the arrival of a new kid on the block.
But that was five years ago. Today, Dr Bwanika is an easily recognisable face.
The chilly Thursday morning when I went to meet him for this profile interview, he was addressing a press conference. It was three days to nomination and the voters register had not yet been publicised.
"This morning we are going to join the Electoral Commission in prayers," he tells the more than a dozen journalists present. "But we don't see the register. Are we going to pray to God to get the voters register?"
When I finally sit down with him for a one-on-one in the dingy eating place on Kampala Road, the veterinary doctor launches into explaining his vision for this country with the ease of a seasoned pastor talking about his favourite Bible verse.
"In 2011 we are changing the government. We are praying for a peaceful transition and that Museveni goes peacefully. We want to put it into his mind that there is life after the presidency."
But is this not easier said than done? I ask him. "Not at all. Ugandans are tired of this regime. The corruption is sickening. This government considers corruption as a way of life. The people stealing claim they went to the bush. As Ugandans, the mistake we made was not to agree on how much we should have paid these bush war heroes."
In an animated tone, Dr Bwanika adds: "But as Bwanika, Ugandans owe me nothing. Just their votes. At least I won't have people taking me hostage that they gave me cassava during the bush war." The last line elicits laughter from both of us.
(President Museveni led a five-year guerrilla struggle against the second Obote government before taking over power in January 1986 - Editor)
Reclining in the white plastic chair, Dr Bwanika, who now heads the People's Development Party, a political outfit he says has a national presence, says: "We have evaluated these last five years and seen that our health sector is suffering. We must support the health sector and stop this business of people going abroad to seek treatment. This will be a central focus of my government. I will also design specific interventions into issues of the elderly, youth and women."
I take the discussion back to corruption-and we agree that it could be the country's biggest undoing currently. I push him to state exactly how he intends to sort out this mess.
Erosion of values
The veterinary doctor first takes a deep breath. He tells me corruption is a manifestation of people failing to love their country. An erosion of values.
"Our biggest problem is lack of a values system. We must cultivate this," says Dr Bwanika. "I will create a ministry of national guidance and planning. This will be a key ministry that will guide the country in terms of national values--and raise a new generation of Ugandans who love Uganda."
"There have been claims that you are actually a Museveni dummy," I shoot the question. "That you have secretly met the President and he's funding you."
The question seems to take him by surprise. He looks at me probingly before answering in a rather polite tone. "I last met President Museveni in 2006 in State House with other opposition leaders. I have never met him since then. But I have twice written to him; in 2007 and 2008. In the first letter, I wanted discuss with him how Uganda could tap into its human resource for national development.
"In the second letter, I was telling the President about the need to have a national dialogue. Uganda today is more fragmented than it was at independence."
So, what did the President make of the letters? "He never replied," says Dr Bwanika. "State House even never acknowledged receipt of the letters. That was the last time I tried to make contact with Museveni."
What about the Born-Again Movement? Has he tried to position himself as their candidate of choice? "Not at all. Of course it is true that I am born again. But if the born-agains choose to support me, it is not because of religion. Rather, it is because I offer a programme that many Ugandans identify with."
In 2006, many a voter agreed that Dr Bwanika's manifesto summed in the four-way approach--Make Uganda global food basket, global tourist centre, regional shopping centre and fight corruption-might have been one of the best leadership alternatives.
That, together with being a fresh face on the stage could perhaps explain why he was able to defeat at least a candidate with a party's backing. But can Dr Bwanika still claim to represent a freshness he did then in an atmosphere visibly polarised by the Museveni-Besigye fight?
"I represent a new generation with new ideas," he insists. "My ideas are those of a progressive Uganda where everyone has an equal opportunity. Ugandans want progressive leadership. I offer it."
And maybe nothing better illustrates this progression or claim to it than the nomenclature of Dr Bwanika's children. Married to Dr Gladys Bwanika, who holds a PhD in fish biology and lectures at Makerere University, the couple has three sons.
Prince Chosen, 15, Prince Delight, 14 and three-year-old Prince Wise Nsimbi. Dr Bwanika's journey to the presidency is obviously a tough ride. But who knows, he might after all be chosen by delightful and wise voters--if his children's names are a clue to go by.

Original_Search_Title: Iron Bar Hitmen Come Back for Xmas
Headline: Uganda: Iron Bar Hitmen Come Back for Xmas
Body_Text:
As we prepare for Christmas and end of year festivities, iron bar hitmen have resurfaced, terrorising many parts of Kampala and its suburbs.
Joseph Kanyike left home at 5:00am to go to work, but half a kilometre away from home, danger was waiting for him. Before reaching the main road where he would get a taxi, iron-bar hit men struck.
When daylight came, residents of Kireku near Bweyogerere in Wakiso district were shocked to see somebody lying flat at the railway crossing point. They called the Police, which took Kanyike to Mulago. On reaching the mortuary, they discovered he was still breathing and he was taken to the casualty ward.
At the British American Tobacco (BAT) where Kanyike works, colleagues thought he had skipped work that day. Back at his uncle's home, where he lives, they he was at work. They only became uneasy when night fell and he was not back. His phone was also off. They tried to call a few friends, but none of them knew his whereabouts.
The following day, Kanyike's uncle went to BAT and was told he had not worked the previous day.
However, news had spread that iron bar hit men had attacked people around Bweyogerere.
"His uncle went to look for him in the mortuary but did not find him. He then went to the casualty ward and there he was," said Mrs. Mercy Muwanga, Kanyike's aunt. "He had just got the job."
Kanyike is is still on medication for pain and he sometimes makes uncoordinated statements. "Doctors told us his brain will stabilise after some time," said Mrs. Muwanga.
Kanyike is not an isolated victim. In many places around Kampala, iron bar hit men are striking again. Mainly in groups of two to three, the gangs hang out in dark roadside covers in early morning or late in the night. They attack mostly those who move alone.
Normally, one thug pretends to be walking slowly alone on the road. As soon as the victim draws near, others jump out of their hiding and pounce on the victim. They surprise the victim so that he is not able to make an alarm. They then go on to remove any valuable items they can find in the bag or pocket.
Whereas the practice is spread all around Kampala, the belt between Bweyogerere and Mukono appears to be the most dangerous.
In Seeta near Mukono, three people have been struck dead and others left unconscious in recent weeks. Among the deceased include Namubiru Josephine who was struck near Ridar Hotel at about 6:00am. The thugs found her at a stall frying cassava. They took her mobile phone and money.
Near Seeta, at Nantabuulirirwa, a journalist, Dickson Ssentongo, was struck dead but the assailants took none of his possessions.
Other individuals identified by the Police as Lutalo Steven, John Nkugwa, Moses Lubulwa and Jackson Seremba were struck but survived.
The OC of Seeta Police station, Abdul Nasser, said the Police has held sensitisation meetings with the communities but residents have not been cooperative.
In Mukono Division, the danger zone is Butebe. The area LC1 chairman, Richard Aliwaali, said many of his people have been struck with iron bars by unknown people.
Last month, Saad Kalyango, a teacher at Vincent Alex Primary School in Mukono town, was struck dead as he returned home in the night. His phone, shoes and money were stolen.
At Namasuba on the Kampala-Entebbe highway, an LC1 chairman, Fred Kabanda said two people had been struck within two weeks. They were, however, not residents of the area. One was identified as John Ntambi, a trader in Kisekka Market and the other was not identified.
Kabanda attributes the rise in the crime to the approach of Christmas festivities. He warned residents to be careful, especially those who return from work late or go very early in the morning.
Ronald Sserunkuuma, 24, a second year student of information technology at Datamine Techinical and Business Institute was hit in Najjanankumbi in August. "I had been watching a football match and on my way home, I found three men. Two stood on one side of the road and the other on the opposite side," he said. As Sserunkuuma passed in between, one struck him and he fell. Others joined beat, searched him and took sh5,000. "That is the money I had on me. They did not see my phone," he said. "I managed to escape and ran home."
The LC1 chairperson of Najjanankumbi's Namuli zone, David Musisi, said iron bar attacks are rampant in his area but many victims don't report to authorities. He said Kiwanuka and Kagabi roads are deadly spots, where thugs operate. He appealed to Police to identify such spots and work with LCs to arrest the thugs.
In Kalerwe, the criminal investigations chief, Edward Musinguzi, said the thugs have changed from iron bars to knives to avoid easy detection.
"For three months, we have not recorded any iron bar case but we are getting reports that thugs now wield knives. They waylay traders on the roads and threaten to stab them if they don't surrender money," he said.
"We have arrested one of their leaders, one Itwau, with exhibits and he is awaiting trial at the High Court. But his colleagues are still operating,' Musinguzi said.

Original_Search_Title: Iron Bar Hitmen Come Back for X-Mas
Headline: Uganda: Iron Bar Hitmen Come Back for X-Mas
Body_Text:
AS we prepare for Christmas and end of year festivities, iron bar hitmen have resurfaced, terrorising many parts of Kampala and its suburbs.
Joseph Kanyike left home at 5:00am to go to work, but half a kilometre away from home, danger was waiting for him. Before reaching the main road where he would get a taxi, iron-bar hit men struck.
When daylight came, residents of Kireku near Bweyogerere in Wakiso district were shocked to see somebody lying flat at the railway crossing point. They called the Police, which took Kanyike to Mulago. On reaching the mortuary, they discovered he was still breathing and he was taken to the casualty ward.
At the British American Tobacco (BAT) where Kanyike works, colleagues thought he had skipped work that day. Back at his uncle's home, where he lives, they he was at work. They only became uneasy when night fell and he was not back. His phone was also off. They tried to call a few friends, but none of them knew his whereabouts.
The following day, Kanyike's uncle went to BAT and was told he had not worked the previous day.
However, news had spread that iron bar hit men had attacked people around Bweyogerere.
"His uncle went to look for him in the mortuary but did not find him. He then went to the casualty ward and there he was," said Mrs. Mercy Muwanga, Kanyike's aunt. "He had just got the job."
Kanyike is is still on medication for pain and he sometimes makes uncoordinated statements. "Doctors told us his brain will stabilise after some time," said Mrs. Muwanga.
Kanyike is not an isolated victim. In many places around Kampala, iron bar hit men are striking again. Mainly in groups of two to three, the gangs hang out in dark roadside covers in early morning or late in the night. They attack mostly those who move alone.
Normally, one thug pretends to be walking slowly alone on the road. As soon as the victim draws near, others jump out of their hiding and pounce on the victim. They surprise the victim so that he is not able to make an alarm. They then go on to remove any valuable items they can find in the bag or pocket.

Original_Search_Title: Six Battle for Nebbi District Chair
Headline: Uganda: Six Battle for Nebbi District Chair
Body_Text:
THE Nebbi LC5 chairman, John Wapokra, will not seek re-election because he now belongs to the newly created Zombo district. Unfortunately, he failed in the NRM primaries for the Zombo seat.
Six contenders were nominated to replace him. They include, former chairman George Alenyo (Independent), who was defeated by Wapokra in 2006, George Othuba (Independent), Sylvester Donge Opar (DP), Robert Okumu (NRM), Wilfred Thogang (UPC) and the former vice-chairman, Marious Okwir (FDC).
The people of Nebbi hope that the new chairman will improve the standard of education, fight poverty and improve on infrastructure.
Nebbi is the poorest district in the sub-region, with the majority of the people living in grass-thatched huts.
In Jonan County, the major economic activity is fishing but the fish has been depleted due to over-fishing and using poor methods. They hope leaders will give them an alternative economic activity.
In Padyere, people grow coffee, cotton, cassava growing, millet, potatoes and sorghum, but at subsistence level.
Both Padyere and Jonam have 70,000 voters. Alenyo, Donge, Thogang and Okumu come from Jonam, while Okwir and Othuba hail from Padyere.
