"It was in secondary [school] that challenges were there, especially with the war in northern Uganda. We had no desks to sit in, no learning aid, no textbooks, win-doors without shutters, not enough teachers for all subjects -- yet you are expected to compete nationally with schools in non war affected areas."
Even as the war ended, Byaruhanga saw many of the same destructive narratives from her childhood repeated in Uganda's new public education system. While financial access to primary school education was extended in 1997 under then President Museveni's Education Strategic Investment Plan (ESIP), the investment of one quarter of the country's GDP on education continued to leave children behind. In its 2001 report on the state of the country's school system, Uganda's Bureau of Statistics found that only 21% of girls and 22% of boys are enrolled in secondary school. With such low enrollment rates and troubling attendance records, many Ugandan children are still not getting an education that allows them to compete academically with countries throughout the world.
"The quality of education offered was so poor. Many of the good teachers left for the city to go to better paying districts. For girls in particular, the attitude of the people towards their education was so negative. Girls were not looked at as people who could make it thru education and therefore I had a conviction that if I open a mixed school, and equal attention is given to both sexes, I could prove to the public that the girls are very intelligent and can compete favorably with boys."
After years of working as a teacher and tirelessly seeking reform from the public system, Byaruhanga decided to venture out on her own to provide a better quality of education for Uganda's youth.
"I felt I could serve better in the private sector where I have control over other resources and make things happen faster than the bureaucratic way of handling things in the public sector. But I had no capital nor did I have collateral to get a loan or even a business to earn from. I knew the only way to go was to do what was within my reach."
When Byaruhanga's father gave her piece of his land to raise profits for the school, many in Lira were outraged. "The clan never believed a woman should own anything but thankfully, my father helped me by standing in the gap when the clan members swore that blood would flow if he gave his land to me." Choosing to grow a garden full of cassava, Byaruhanga worked to raise money for the school she had in mind.
"I had nothing to make anybody believe in me and all I could do was to use my own energy to plough the garden. The income I raised from the cassava was used for making 25 wheel-barrows that I hired out to wheel-barrow pushers at $6.25 per day and I would save this."
After three years of selling her goods at makeshift canteens and saving, Byarunhanga had enough to begin building the Lira Integrated School.
While the initial capital provided a boost, there were still many hurdles to overcome, many of them from the very community she was trying to serve.
"The district officials never believed in me because of being a woman...I was undermined because I was a attempting to do what even the rich men have not ventured into. I had to request my husband to help me go through the process by putting him at the forefront. I felt segregated from members of the family and rejected too. I was seen as a mad person venturing into an impossible project."
Ironically, many in the community saw Byaruhanga's ambition as an unfortunate result of her own schooling.
"Many in the community at that time saw what I was doing as strange and were just waiting to see my downfall since they had grown up with me and come from the same background of poverty. They thought I was crazy and that could it be the degree I got from the university that was disturbing me and making me over ambitious. It was not until the buildings reached some level that the community started believing in what I was doing."
Now, many of her doubters have come to believe. Since opening in 2000, the Lira Integrated School has grown to include nursery, primary and secondary schools, and boarding quarters. The school serves 1,500 students and subsidizes 10 percent of the children attending.
The Lira School offers academic as well as vocational courses with the emphasis on creating citizens as well as students.
"We look at the total upbringing of the child," says Byaruhanga. "That is, the formal education is of quality, the vocational side is catered for by training them in skills that in case they fall out of school, they can never be stranded in life."
After living through what is now taught in her student's history classes, Byaruhanga says she has seen a fundamental shift in the way that her students learn about Uganda.
"During the early age of Uganda, history was more of African traditional society and it was just about discipline, respect, uncles, aunties, relatives and the past events like the Old Stone Age, Middle Stone Age etc. Today history has been included as a compulsory subject, which was not the case when I was growing up. For these children, history is no longer just the classics, it is in everything. For Africa now, history means digging closer to our destination as nation states and looking critically how they were formed instead of memorizing dates and phrases like fables."
As the students at her school examine their nation's past, Mrs. Byaruhanga hopes they will be part of changing its future. With a goal to build the Lira Integrated School into an internationally recognized university, she is working to expand the school's focus on research, innovation, and ultimately, eradicating poverty. With her determination, it is possible Mrs. Byarunhanga may help write a new chapter in Uganda's history.

Original_Search_Title: NDLEA Arrests Two Suspects
Headline: Nigeria: NDLEA Arrests Two Suspects
Body_Text:
Lagos — Its spokesman, M. Mitchell Ofoyeju, in a statement said the suspects, Uche Emmanuel, 39 and Akhabighmhe Bernard, 43, were arrested a the Murtala Muhammed International Airport, Ikeja, Lagos, on May 17.
He said the suspects were arrested while attempting to board a South Africa flight three days after the drugs were intercepted.
"The drugs consisted of 36.kg of methamphetamine, hidden inside cassava paste meant for export to Japan while the remaining 26.5kg tested positive for amphetamine and was to be exported to South Africa," said the statement.
It added that the associates of the suspects in the destination countries had also been arrested, and that the suspects have made confessional statement.
According to the statement, the arrest brings to 21 the number of suspected traffickers arrested by the Airport Command in May.
It explained that 19 males and 2 females were arrested with 91.3kg of narcotics consisting of 23.2kg of cocaine, 4.9kg heroin, 36.5kg methamphetamine and 26.5kg amphetamine.

Original_Search_Title: Nwaubani is Rainbow's Book Club Host June
Headline: Nigeria: Nwaubani is Rainbow's Book Club Host June
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigeria's latest literary star, Adaobi Tricia Nwaubani, author of I Do Not Come To You By Chance and winner of the Commonwealth Writers Prize, will be special guest at the Rainbow Book Club June reading.
"Any opportunity to share my words with more people is always welcome and the Rainbow Book Club is a wonderful platform," Nwaubani said. "I'm quite delighted about this invitation and look forward to being with the beautiful people of the Garden City over the weekend."
The reading takes place on Sunday, June 6, 2010, at the Le Meridien, Ogeyi Place in Port Harcourt.
This follows a string of readings which began in Lagos on May 15, following the release of her book in Nigeria, published by Cassava Republic. The book is also published in the UK and the U.S., giving Nwaubani the distinction of being the only Nigerian writer fully educated and based in Nigeria, currently on the international literary scene.
I Do Not Come to You by Chance is a novel that gives in-depth insight into the 419 phenomenon in Nigeria, capturing the whole story from an angle that only an 'insider' can.
"Kingsley is a young man eager to help his family and change the world. But when his once-proud family descends into poverty after his father falls ill, he is forced to turn to his mother's infamous brother, Cash Daddy, who runs a successful empire of email scams, relieving gullible Westerners of their hard-earned money.
Unconditional family support is the Nigerian way but the hand Cash Daddy
extends in charity has consequences. As Kingsley is drawn into Cash Daddy's outlandish world, he soon learns that nothing in Nigeria comes for free."
It was gathered that the book would also be available for sale at the Rainbow Book Shop, 20 Igbodo Street, Old G.R.A., Port Harcourt.

Original_Search_Title: Oni Condoles With Family of Poisonous Cassava Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Oni Condoles With Family of Poisonous Cassava Victims
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ekiti State governor, Mr. Segun Oni, has paid a condolence visit to the family of Mr. Kayode Ogunlusi, whose six children died due to alleged consumption of poisonous cassava flour at Ayegunle -Ekiti in Ijero Local Government Area of the state.
In his condolence message at the palace of Alayegunle of Ayegunle Ekiti, Oba Johnson Aderiye, the governor, who said that the death of the six children was a huge loss to the community, prayed for the repose of the souls of the victims.
The governor, who donated undisclosed amount of money to the family, affirmed that the state government was not happy with the development, noting that measures would be put in place to curtail future occurrence through a sensitisation campaign on the effective way of processing raw cassava.
While noting that the present method of processing cassava was not safe enough for consumption, he advised the people to reverse to the old system, which he noted allows the release of poisonous element known as 'cyanide' from the commodity before consumption.
"We should ensure that cassava is processed the way it used to be. There are modern ways of processing it; but it does not mean that whatever is modern is right. We should go back to the old way that ensures that poisons are eliminated from the product before consumption," Oni urged.
Oni said that government was taking nothing for granted on the issue, stressing that relevant agencies were already investigating the matter to find out the root cause of the disaster.
Also, the state Commissioner for Health, Dr. Femi Thomas, advised the people to always ensure that raw cassavas are fermented in water before further processing; he said the measure would ensure that poisonous substances are removed from it before consumption.
He lamented the death of the children, noting that the state government made efforts to save the victims.
While commending the Governor for holding the citizenry in high esteem, the Alayegunle of Ayegunle Ekiti, Oba Aderiye thanked the Governor for the visit.
The monarch promised the continual support of the people of the area for the present administration in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Annual Inflation Rate on the Rise
Headline: Tanzania: Annual Inflation Rate on the Rise
Body_Text:
Dar Es Salaam — THE annual headline inflation rate for the year ended April 2010 went up to 9.4 per cent compared to 9.0 per cent registered in the year ended March, this year.
The overall index increased to 185.6 in April, this year from 169.7 in April 2009. Food inflation rate has increased to 9.8 per cent in the year ended April, this year from 9.7 per cent in the year ended March, this year.
According to National Bureau of Statistics consumer price index for April states that non food inflation has also increased to 8.5 per cent in the year ended April, this year from 7.8 per cent in the year ended March, this year.
The monthly headline inflation rate for the month of April, this year went down by 0.1 per cent while the Overall Index decreased to 185.6 in April, this year from 185.7 in March the same year.
The decrease under consideration was highly attributed to the decrease of food prices by 0.1 per cent. Food items that contributed to price decrease includes; cereals, cassava, potatoes, cooking bananas, fruits, beans and nuts.
However, non food inflation rate has increased by 0.1per cent in April, this year. Non food items that had increased prices include drinks, charcoal, furniture, household equipments, and recreation items.
However Uganda's annual headline inflation rate for the year ending April, this year went down further to 5.9 per cent from 7.5 per cent in March in the same year.
The Kenya's month-on-month overall inflation rate declined from 4.0 per cent in March, this year 4. 0 to 3.7 per cent in April in the same year.

Original_Search_Title: Man Loses Five Kids to Amala Meal
Headline: Nigeria: Man Loses Five Kids to Amala Meal
Body_Text:
Ado Ekiti — Tragedy struck at Ado Ekiti, Ekiti State yesterday, as Mr. Kayode Ogunlusi's family of Ayegunle Ekiti in Ijero local government, lost five of its children after a meal of Amala(local cassava flour).
When Daily Champion visited Ogunlusi at the Ado Ekiti Teaching Hospital's Complex, where the children were hospitalized before their death, he looked unruffled.
The 45-year old man identified the names of the late children as Iyabo, Rotimi, Pelumi, Abigael, and the baby of the house Ade , who was four years old.
It will be recalled that recently, a man identified as Mutiu, a native of Ibadan, Oyo State, an Ado Ekiti based butcher lost five of his seven children in a similar manner, after they were locked in the booth of his Mercedez Benz car by unknown persons.
It was gathered that the family of 16- Ogunlusi, two wives and 13 children ate the cassava flour meal in the night only to wake up with strange feelings in the various parts of their body.
After taking the meal, the children felt some discomfort in their stomachs and were rushed to the State Teaching Hospital, Ado Ekiti where they were given medical attention before they finally gave up the ghost.
When our Correspondent visited the Hospital's children ward, it was reliably gathered that the remaining two children, who did not die on Monday, died in the early hours of yesterday after several attempts by the doctors and nurses to revive them.
A family source revealed that a dog which ate out of the meal was earlier discovered dead in the compound three hours after taking the food, but the family did not suspect any fowl play.
The source said while the parents vomited, the children complained of stomach ache and were taken to a private hospital for treatment from where they were referred to the Ado Ekiti University Teaching Hospital, UTH.
Ogunlusi confirmed that the first wife, Peju, prepared the soup with which the family consumed the cassava flour which was prepared by the first born of the family, Iyabo, 18 years old.
He said: "The Ewedu and stew were prepared by Peju, who is the first wife, while the cassava flour was prepared by Iyabo, who was my first born. Around 8 pm, we all ate, but about 4 am, Peju started vomiting heavily all the food and was getting weak.
"After we took care of her, Iyabo was complaining of stomach ache, I took her to a private hospital, but she was not responding, and was also getting weak, within a short period, they could not talk again. I therefore rushed them to the general hospital.
The senior wife, Peju, who is pregnant, kept asking after the welfare of the children, saying: "Please, where are my children, I don't know how it happened, I prepared the soup, so, I don't know what happened."
The junior wife, Abigael, who was receiving treatment as at the time of the visit to the Hospital, has been discharged.
The doctor in charge, Dr. Olusoji Abidemi said the first child was brought into the hospital dead while several efforts made to revive the remaining children failed.
He said the death might not be unconnected with shoddy processing of the cassava flour, saying the poisoning may be as a result of chemical reactions occasioned by poor fermentation and processing.

Original_Search_Title: Tragedy as Family Loses Five After Cassava Flour Meal
Headline: Nigeria: Tragedy as Family Loses Five After Cassava Flour Meal
Body_Text:
Ado-Ekiti — Tragedy yesterday struck in the home of Mr. Kayode Ogunlusi in Ayegunle-Ekiti in Ijero Local Government area of Ekiti State as he lost five of his children after eating food prepared from cassava flour.
The affected children, who gave up the ghost simultaneously on Monday at the State University Teaching Hospital, Ado-Ekiti after the consumption of the meal, which was also consumed by other members of the family include Iyabo, 18, Rotimi, 12, Pelumi, 8, Abigael, 5 and Ade, whose age was put at about 4
Sources told THISDAY at the hospital complex in Ado-Ekiti yesterday that, the five deceased were among the 13 children of Ogunlusi
When THISDAY visited the children ward of the hospital, it was reliably gathered that, while three of the children died on Monday the remaining two gave up the ghost in the early hours of yesterday after several attempts by the doctors and nurses to revive them.
A family source disclosed that the family of 16 made up of the father, the two wives- Peju and Abigael- and their 13 children ate the cassava flour meal on Saturday night only to wake up with strange feelings in the various parts of the body on Sunday morning.
Besides, further information has it that even their domestic dog which ate out of the meal was found dead in the compound about three hours after taking the food, but they never suspected anything.
It was further gathered that, the family grounded cassava to make garri and after extracting the one needed for it the sieved product was taken to the grinding mill to make cassava flour after drying it in the sun.
The source said the parents were only vomiting, but the children complained of serious stomach ache and were taken to a private hospital for treatment from where they were referred to the Ado Ekiti University Teaching Hospital, UTH.
While narrating the incident, Ogunlusi confirmed that the first wife, Peju prepared the soup with which the family ate the cassava flour which was prepared by the first born of the family, Iyabo.
According to the father, who looks dejected and worn out, "the Ewedu and stew was prepared by Peju, who is the first wife, while the cassava flour was prepared by Iyabo, who is my first born, around 8 pm, we all ate, but about 4am, Peju was vomiting heavily all the food and was getting weak.
"After we took care of her, about 10am, Iyabo was complaining of stomach ache, I took her to a private hospital, but was not responding, and was also getting weak, within a short period, they could not talk again, I therefore rushed them to the general hospital.
When contacted on her hospital bed, Peju, who is pregnant could not talk as the death of her children is being kept away from her, but she kept asking after the welfare of her children . All she could utter was , "Please, where are my children, I don't know how it happened, I prepared the soup, so, I don't know what happened".
The junior wife, Abigael was also receiving treatment as at the time of this report
The Doctor in Charge, Dr. Olusoji Abidemi, said the first child was brought into the hospital dead while several efforts made to revive the other four children failed as they died on Tuesday morning.
He said the cassava flour might not have been properly processed and urged the people to always ensure that cassava are adequately processed and properly fermented before consumption

Original_Search_Title: Family Loses Three Children After Cassava Meal
Headline: Nigeria: Family Loses Three Children After Cassava Meal
Body_Text:
The family of Mr Kayode Ogunlusi in Ayegunle-Ekiti in Ijero Local Government Area of Ekiti State on Monday lost their children after taking a cassava meal prepared by the family.
Five other members of the family, who also ate the cassava meal, are now on admission at the emergency unit of the University of Ado-Ekiti Teaching Hospital, Ado-Ekiti.
Ogunlusi, the father of the deceased children, who only regained consciousness after receiving medical attention, said the family ate the cassava meal on Sunday night as dinner.
He explained that few hours after taking the meal, two of the children started vomiting, while one became unconscious.
The father, who disclosed that the food was prepared by his wife, Abosede, said members of the family were later taken to a private hospital in the town for initial treatment by their neighbours.
The family, including the mother of the children, was referred to the teaching hospital where doctors confirmed three of the children dead.
The father, who could not ascertain the cause of the incident, confirmed that the dog that ate the remnant of the food also died.
The deceased children, two boys and a girl, have since been buried by the family.
Speaking on the incident, Dr Olusoji Abidemi, who is the head of the emergency unit of the hospital, said the cause of the incident might be food poisoning.
He said: "the cassava might have not be properly processed adding that when food, especially cassava, is not properly prepared, it could lead to the situation at hand."
Adibemi urged the people to always process food properly before eating. NAN.

Original_Search_Title: Two Held Over Defiling 12-Year-Old Girl
Headline: Uganda: Two Held Over Defiling 12-Year-Old Girl
Body_Text:
Kampala — The police in Tororo are holding two men suspected to have defiled a 12-year-old girl in turns.
Enock Ochwo and John Sande were arrested at Nawire village in Paya sub-county for molesting the girl on Saturday night.
The district criminal investigations boss, Noah Byamugisha, confirmed the arrest of the suspects on Monday.
He said they would be charged with aggravated defilement. The victim (name withheld) has been admitted at Tororo Hospital.
A medical report indicated that the victim sustained extensive raptures in her genitals.
The report further indicated that samples of male semen were extracted from the genitals of the girl.
The victim had reportedly gone to the market at around 7:00pm to buy sauce for the family's supper meal when the suspects attacked her.
She narrated that Sande dragged her to a cassava garden and defiled her, while Owor held her mouth to stop her from screaming.
She added that when Sande had finished, the suspects changed roles.
They later attempted to flee but were traced and arrested by the residents.
The victim said she had earlier recognised the suspects with the aid of light at the market stall where she had bought fish.

Original_Search_Title: Judge's Houses Demolished
Headline: Uganda: Judge's Houses Demolished
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Police in Entebbe are hunting for 14 people accused of demolishing houses belonging to Justice Steven Kavuma of the Court of Appeal.
The suspects, some of them kanyamas (muscle men), allegedly demolished the judge's houses in Kiwulwe village, according to the Kampala metropolitan Police spokesperson, Henry Kalulu.
Benson Sunday, an engineer, is said to have hired them. Four private security guards were hired to oversee the demolition, Kalulu told journalists yesterday.
The men, he said, demolished two houses, leaving them inhabitable.
William Bbosa, the property caretaker, was away at the time.
"Their mission was to demolish the property and then move on to destroy a nearby resident's cassava garden," Kalulu said.
He added that there has been a long-standing row between the property owner and the tenants, among them the judge.

Original_Search_Title: Humanitarian Corridor Opened for Trapped Civilians in Northeast
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Humanitarian Corridor Opened for Trapped Civilians in Northeast
Body_Text:
Bunia — The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) government has opened a humanitarian corridor to provide safe passage for hundreds of civilians trapped by fighting between the army and rebel militias in Orientale Province, northeastern DRC.
"We decided on this corridor as we have registered hundreds of deaths and severe disease among the civilian population trapped between the militias and FARDC [Congolese army]. With the corridor they are able to get to safe zones," Rustic Avo, Ituri District deputy commissioner, said.
According to the local authorities, at least 5,000 civilians have been trapped since December 2009 in the Mukato Ngazi area about 100km south of Bunia, following a major government offensive against rebels in Potopoto, Mokato and Tchey, all south of Bunia, the main town in Ituri.
"They have suffered and are continuing to suffer. FARDC mistook them for rebels... and the rebel militias used them as shields - looting, torturing and raping them," said a local Ngity (ethnic group) leader, who preferred anonymity, in Walendu Bindi area.
On 12 March, Walendu Bindi's civil society said soldiers had shot at a group of women among this population who had gone to the fields in search of cassava leaves for food. Six women allegedly died, among them two who were pregnant.
A group of student activists, who went into the area in March, expressed concern.
"We only spent four days [there] and counted 21 dead people and several graves here and there; most of the dead were mothers and children," said Adaba Masumbuko, group leader. The area also lacked sanitary structures and schools.
"I counted about 300 sick people who could not stand..." said Kobvo Angaika, another member of the group. They, and their dependants, are clearly not able to use the humanitarian corridor, he said.
The UN Refugee Agency has deployed two trucks in the village of Ozoba, in Irumu Territory, to transport the displaced who are weakened by trekking long distances.
Deaths along the way
According to Ituri deputy commissioner Avo, about 10 of the civilians have died along the way. Four more died in Aveba, 90km south of Bunia. "We were thinking that the very sick would be evacuated by helicopter," he said.
The Lutheran World Foundation has started distributing food aid to some 47,000 displaced people in the region. Médecins Sans Frontières Swiss has also set up a transit health site in Aveba where care for sexual violence victims is being provided.
So far, Ituri's population movement commission has registered 167,000 displaced people of whom 64 percent are in Irumu, south of Bunia, where the army fought FRPI (Front de Résistance Patriotique en Ituri)/FPJC (Front Populaire pour le Justice au Congo) militias.
An estimated 2,000 FRPI/FPJC militia members are yet to be disarmed, according to the national disarmament programme.
District officials, however, note that the number of displaced is unclear as some people have sought refuge in Uganda.
According to the UN Mission in the DRC (MONUC), rebel militias are seeking to replenish their ammunition and food supplies following recent army and MONUC operations against them.
At present, the area south of Bunia remains dangerous with military escorts required to get there, said the head of the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs in Orientale Province, Jean Charles Dupin.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Niger Bridge - Story of Lokoja's Homeless
Headline: Nigeria: Niger Bridge - Story of Lokoja's Homeless
Body_Text:
Lokoja — A traveller from Abuja would, on reaching the River Niger Bridge at Jimata, a sleepy village, about 20 kilometres from Lokoja, the Kogi State capital, unconsciously get so carried away by the splendour of the historical river, that he/she hardly imagines that some humans inhabit the area immediately beneath this bridge. While this may seem to be a fantasy to many, our correspondent who went beneath the bridge discovered the unknown world of its dwellers.
Upon spotting this reporter, these dwellers, in the twinkle of an eye, developed cold feet. Some of them abruptly took to their heels. The reason was obvious. They mistook the reporter for a task force official. Thanks to one of them, Abubakar Yakubu, who summoned courage and curiously quizzed: "Oga what brought you here? I hope you are not here to arrest us. You can see people running away".
Convinced that the 'stranger' in their midst was not on a 'mischievous' mission, these dwellers staggered back and simultaneously reacted thus: "You are welcome oga. What should we offer you? How about food? Will you take some fish? Just make a choice."
22-year-old Musa Ahmed is a comedian. He says he has been living under the River Niger Bridge since 2006. He ecstatically provides an insight into what attracted him and his co-dwellers to live under the bridge: "We were afraid when we saw you. We wouldn't want anybody to spoil our fun here. This is another world entirely. Life is sweet here. You cannot understand what I mean, until you try to join us. We live and get our means of livelihood here.
"I have been living here for the past four years. I am a comedian, and this is where I entertain my audience every night. If you come here around 10pm, you would see how I make people laugh with my talent. It was here I discovered my talent in comic performances, and it has been a wonderful experience. I make at least N500 every night. One thing you would discover is that almost everybody living under this bridge is battling with homelessness. We are a people saddened by our inability to afford living in the expensive city of Lokoja. Here, no landlord is on our neck".
Razaq Adekola, a basket weaver, says he has lived under the bridge for seven years. Living there, he contends, is accidental. "How could one just choose to live under this bridge?" he asks. He hints that the harsh socio-economic condition of the state pushed him and his co-inhabitants there, stressing that nearly all of them there are victims of the general problem of homelessness, staring millions of poverty-stricken Nigerians in the face.
"My brother, what can we do? Life must go on. Our situation is a typical example of the suffering unleashed on the common man in this country. Just like many of my co-inhabitants here, I left Lokoja metropolis in 2003 when my landlord ejected me from his house, because I could not afford to pay the rent. I was initially paying 25, 000 as rent, before he increased it to N30, 000. It was then an old friend of mine invited me. I did not even know that he was living here. I thought he had left Lokoja for another place. He was the one who consoled me that living here could bring succour.
"To be frank, I have no regret living here. It was here I learnt how to weave baskets. This has been serving as a source of living since I settled here. Now, I am able to send my daughter to a primary school around this area. Can you believe that living inside the town was very difficult for me? I just pray that government officials do not come to drive us away from here", he wished.
46-year-old Bello Musa describes the River Niger Bridge as a meeting point for the helpless and the weak, in Kogi State. It has, he posits, afforded them the opportunity, not only to share feelings, but also to fashion out ways of addressing their collective plights.
"One thing I am sure of is that none of us was happy coming here, in the first instance. We were constrained to live here. But on getting here, we realised it is a blessing. Apart from living here, many of us are also engaged in one or two things to keep body and soul together. Under this bridge, I peel cassava, and it is from the proceeds I make here that I fend for my family. As you can see, a lot of activities are taking place here. Most of our customers come from nearby villages. For me, I don't think I will go back to the city", he maintained.
32-year-old Habibat Labaran proclaims that she gave birth to her first and only daughter under this bridge. How did she get there? She explains: "Our house in Lokoja was destroyed by a storm during the last raining season, and our landlady, a very poor old woman, could not afford to repair the house. Our co-tenants who could afford to rent houses in other locations within the city did so, while some of us who could not afford to do so, relocated here. It was here I gave birth to my daughter. Life here is not bad, except for the fact that malaria dey plenty".
However, Madam Azizat Hammed, one of the dwellers under the bridge, acknowledges that she lives there with palpable fear. Why? She knows the danger inherent in such living conditions. "Anything can happen to us. The bridge can collapse. Also, dangerous animals in the river can attack us, and when this happens, there would be nobody to come to our rescue. That is my fear. We all know it is a great risk we are taking. But we just have to survive , since we cannot afford to live in the city", she said.
Rasheed Basheer, a civil engineer based in Lokoja, is worried that some people reside under this bridge, without minding the danger it portends. He wonders why they are ruling out the possibility that the bridge can collapse at any time. In his view, no socio-economic condition warrants taking such a 'risk'. He simply concludes that living under the River Niger Bridge is suicidal.
"It is very risky and suicidal! They have no excuse living under this bridge. Are they the only poor people in the country? Must they expose themselves to danger all in the name of having nowhere to live? Have they forgotten that this bridge was constructed by human beings? Or are they ruling out the fact that this bridge can collapse one day? It was constructed many years ago. Many vehicles ply it every day, and there is a tendency that it gets over-burdened, which further implies that it could weaken and ultimately collapse. When this happens, I doubt if those living under it would not fall victims. So, poverty or homelessness should not be an excuse for risking one's life", contended the engineer.
For a Lokoja-based psychologist, Mr Charles Onah, when one is frustrated, one tends to consciously compromise one's safety. According to him, "the indisputable fact is that these people living under the bridge are victims of the poor socio-economic conditions of the country. Nobody could willingly take to such a deplorable place as his/her abode. Such a thing, we all know, is necessitated by one's poor living condition. I mean that these people are frustrated, and they are very likely to be ready to sacrifice safety for survival. So, to them, the question of safety does not arise. What matters to them is their survival. They don't care whether it is dangerous living under the bridge or not".
Also confirming that people live under the River Niger Bridge, owing to circumstances beyond their control, the Director-General of Kogi State Sanitation and Waste Management Board, Alhaji Yusuf Yahya, submits "the whole thing boils down to frustration. It is in our mandate in the Sanitation and Waste Management Board , to avert avoidable disasters , wherever they could occur. It is very unfortunate that out of frustration, some people have taken to such a place to live. This is very dangerous. Anything could happen to the bridge. It could collapse. "
On what the authority is doing to avert the impending doom, Yahya explains that his board has been "counselling the people to vacate the place. Apart from those who claim to be residing there, owing to the critical problem of homelessness, there are others who go there to smoke, drink and engage in all sorts of criminal activities. Just last year, we collaborated with the police to eject these persons from that place, and we have also informed the police of other places across the state where miscreants live.
"Sometime ago, the police carried out some preventive operations there, but it seems the people there are stubborn. We learnt that they have gone back there. My staff members have also been involved in counselling them. They all claim that they are homeless, but we are not in a position to ascertain this. All I can assure you is that we will continue to drive them away from that uninhabitable place".

Original_Search_Title: Religious NGO Delivers Goods to Returnees
Headline: Angola: Religious NGO Delivers Goods to Returnees
Body_Text:
Uíge — The northern Uige province-based US Non-Governmental Organisation (Caritas) will deliver basic commodities to assist Angolans expelled from the two neighbouring republics of Congo, currently settled in the region.
The information was disclosed by the local director of Caritas, Joana Alberto, who added that so far the institution has delivered products such as rice, maize and cassava flour, cooking oil, beans as well as second-hand clothes, shoes and blankets.
Caritas in Uige province is involved in communitarian projects and upgrading of women leaders from various communities of the region, through the Catholic Church Women Promotion Association (Promaica).

Original_Search_Title: My Surname Has Not Contributed to My Status -Tafawa Balewa
Headline: Nigeria: My Surname Has Not Contributed to My Status -Tafawa Balewa
Body_Text:
Jhalil Tafawa Balewa shares names with the only Prime Minister of Independent Nigeria, Sir Abubakar Tafawa Balewa. The United States of America-based physician is a nuclear scientist. Dr., as he is called by many, is also the chairman of the organising committee of sub-region African Global Sister Cities Foundation (AGSCF), an organisation set up by former President of the United States, Dwight Eisenhover, in 1956. His mother took him to Switzerland when he was just two years, so Jahlil says he knows little or nothing about his family.
REPORTER, Anthonia Soyingbe, met him recently in Lagos and got this father of four daughters talking about his growing up years, the impact of his mother on his life, and why his children are all in the medical field.
HOW COME you speak Yoruba fluently since you grew up abroad?
My mother is from Ogbomosho in Oyo State and I grew up with her.
It is very rare to see Nigerians like you, who have spent most of their life abroad dress in traditional attire like this one you have on. What is the spirit behind this your attire?
An icon is never too far from the Oakwood. I have always been me and I am very proud to be an African man. I encourage my staff in the U.S., including the whites, to put on African outfit on Fridays and I go the extra mile to purchase African traditional attires for them. I think Africans in the Diaspora appreciate African culture more than those who reside here because they are so familiar with the culture and they don't appreciate it. For someone like me who has been alien to other countries for a long time it is very necessary for me to savour the beauty and the culture. My children take garri (cassava flakes) with cold water and groundnut, and when you see them taking it you will think they have lived all their lives in Nigeria.
The first thing that will attract any stranger to you is your surname. Has the name paved way for you in life and contributed to your present status in life?
Unfortunately no, not for me, but I am sure it has paved way for other people. I left Nigeria when I was two years old. My mother was a gynaecologist and she took me to Switzerland when I was just two years old. I went to the United Nations International School because I was the main focus of her attention. I went to an English public school for my A' levels and for my O' levels I went to Greenmore College from there I went to medical school. I finished from medical school before I was 20 years old. I made a name for myself outside the shores of Nigeria. I was away for 47 years nobody really knew who I am.
Don't you sometimes flaunt the name even outside the shores of Nigeria?
I don't like that question, but I never flaunted it.
You are the chairman organising committee of sub-region African Global Sister Cities Foundation (AGSCF) and a conference is scheduled for May in Ghana. Can you tell us about that?
One of the things I think you need to understand is what Sister Cities is all about. We have developing nations, developed nations and under developed nations in collaboration with the developed nation's cities and developing nations cities. Developed nations help the developing nations in the development of their cities to create employment and other things in these cities. The cultures of the developing nations cannot totally imbibe the cultures of the developed nations but they need to adapt what is good for them from the developed nations. It is a continuum thing. A long time ago at the United States we used to have a Sister Cities programme with Lagos, Atlanta and Cotonou in the Republic of Benin, where we used to collect books for the libraries, we try to encourage the businessmen of the developing nations. They come to the United States and see what could be of help to their people and then tie them with businessmen from the U.S. in the same line of business. The cultural exchanges are very important, likewise the business exchanges are very important to us. We encourage intellectuals from these two developing countries for them to have American experience and go back to their countries with whatever they learnt from U.S. Sister Cities experience is a very broad spectrum. There are so many things we could achieved from it.
Has Nigeria benefited in any way from Sister Cities?
Nigeria used to benefit from it during the time (Brig Gen Mohammed Buba) Marwa was the military administrator of Lagos State. We had a lot of things happening - cultural exchanges and encouraging businessmen from Nigeria to come to the U.S. Unfortunately, that programme died after a while. The Sister Cities experience is something Africa needs to know about and bring their experience to develop their nations but they are not doing that. By the grace of God with the likes of Mrs. Adigwe the promoter/coordinator of AGSCF in ECOWAS, we will get there.
The conference will focus on local government chairmen and traditional rulers. Why is AGSCF focusing on these grassroots leaders?
A lot of people may not agree but our politics is still relatively feudal, we still believe in our traditional rulers and the people that are closest to the grassroots are the traditional rulers. Closest in democracy to the traditional rulers are the local government chairmen. The people closest to the people are the ones who can develop them the more because they are the ones closest to them. Encouraging LG chairmen to work better with their people is the very best thing. You can transfer technology and culture in such a way that will gear them up in doing more for their people. We will give awards to those who are doing the best for the grassroots. Giving the award may sound somehow but we just want to appreciate good governance. They are examples and they have to be epitome of goodness for them to help our people better. We are appreciating people who are doing it without any thought of being given an award. With this they will know that there is a particular pathway for them to have glory. It is a catalyst for all elected government officials to know that the rest of the world is watching and we want them to do what they are elected to do.
How is your family?
My oldest daughter is a surgeon. Her name is Dr. Latefah Balewa Small. My next daughter did a combined degree in medicine and in law. She has a doctor of medicine and a doctor of jurisprudence degrees. Her name is Fatima. The next one Hauwa is finishing her Ph.D in clinical psychology this summer and the youngest is at Harvard Medical School.
You have them all girls?
Yes, I feel good and happy about that. I was brought up by a woman, a very special and a very brilliant woman. A lot of people talk differently about women in Nigeria, which to me is lack of proper understanding. If God made Adam first and he made a woman from the rib of Adam, it tells you something that the next mother must be more perfect than the first mother. Women in general have capabilities than most men don't. I can't go through the pain of bearing a child and I will run mad if I menstruate, I don't know anyone who was born without being part of a woman. Even if you are cloned you are still part of a woman. Women are people we need to really treasure. Most of the developing nations have more women than men and we are just wasting a lot of our resources by not encouraging the womenfolk in bringing up the nation. China gives women equal opportunities like the men. We only empower women by word of mouth and we blame it on religion and culture. Every opportunity I have I let people know that when God created us He gave each and everyone of us the same spirit. Cultural nuance can create slavery but slavery is long dead, we need to realise that our children are watching us. My children are good examples. I will put my children against other children at any point in time because they are all like me. You can't put information in a computer and expect something different. Everybody should be given and equal opportunity to contribute to the development of their environment. I have been known as a feminist for a long time because of my stand on women issues and gender equality. I was brought up by my mother, who was very strong and wonderful; she has instilled a lot of virtues and morals in me.
What are those virtues?
My mother believed that everyone was born equally. She also believed that to get to the top you must go through some enlightening. She taught me so much and as such there is nothing a woman can do that I can't do except the natural things. I can cook very well and my children will prefer that I cook than their mother. I also learnt from my mother that you have to share what you have with others, including your talents and any knowledge that you can't impart you don't have it. She also taught me that if you don't use what you have you are going to lose it. My mother adopted a lot of children and none of us ever got what we wanted. You may get a banana, I may get a pear, someone else may get a pineapple, another person may get an apple and if we need a cocktail of fruits we have to come together and share or else you take what you have.
How far with the adopted children; are you still together?
I used the word adopted because I am speaking in English. They are my sisters; we didn't have any separation. There is no reason for me not to get along with my sisters; we are very close. I grew up knowing them as my sisters and they are still my sisters.
Your mother was a gynaecologist, you are a medical practitioner and your four daughters are toeing your line. Does medicine run in the family?
The most important thing about talent is also exposure. You may have talent for a certain thing, but you need exposure or else the chances of reaching that goal may be arduous. My children have been exposed to medicine from when they were born. When each of my children turns seven I take them to work in the clinic - they clean, they take the garbage out and they do other petty stuff in the clinic.
Is it that you want them to be familiar with your profession or you just need more hands to work for you in your clinic?
No, the reason we don't have legacy here in Nigeria amidst the children of the so-called rich men is because we treat them special as if they are out of this world. If any of my children wants to have pocket money, they need to know that I worked and I am still working for my money. So they do cleaning and as they are getting older they can do patient registration and they can do patient re-call, they can do the section and medical history. The only way they could earn pocket money is for them to work in my clinic. I have two clinics in the U.S.
Now that you are here in Nigeria, who sees to the smooth running of your clinics in U.S.?
It is wonderful if you train your children, then there can be continuity and legacy. I have two older children, Lateefah and Fatima, and they are both managing the clinics. I don't have problem with that because my children are equal to the task.
Seeing Nigerians in Diaspora like you back home at this point in time, one thing that will come to the mind of many is that you are around to seek political office because 2011 is very much around the corner. Are you around for that purpose?
Every single one of us is political right from when we were born. Any right thinking person will want to change his or her environment for good. Unfortunately, most of the politicians in Nigeria are people who have not seen how things work in other parts of the world and when they do, they are seeing it on vacation and when they come back there seems to be a block with what they have seen there and what we have here. We really need to expand in Nigeria and see what other countries are doing. Nigerians need to give some of us a chance to transfer our thoughts and technology, which we have acquired over the years abroad.
Are you hoping that Nigerians will give you that chance?
I didn't say that and I don't want to answer that.

Original_Search_Title: Rot in Psi Discovered; Sfo to Begin Investigations
Headline: Ghana: Rot in Psi Discovered; Sfo to Begin Investigations
Body_Text:
Hanna Tetteh, Trade Minister
The Serious Fraud Office (SFO) will soon begin investigations into massive financial impropriety alleged to have taken place in the President's Special Initiatives (PSI) introduced under the erstwhile New Patriotic Party Government.
This
follows the release of an audit report indicting managers of the initiatives of gross financial malfeasance and total mismanagement.
Trade Minister Hanna Tetteh described the full details of the report as a complete Â"disasterÂ" making it difficult to continue any of what was supposed to be laudable initiatives to boost the country's ailing economy and to provide jobs to people especially in the rural areas.
Ex-president Kufuor introduced the initiatives comprising production and export of textiles, production of cotton, Salt mining, cassava production and distance learning.
These projects were expected to add an estimated amount of between six hundred thousand to one million cedis to the country's Gross Domestic Product.
Unfortunately, the initiatives according to the trade minister have failed to achieve the expected results, and look more like an albatross on the neck of the government.
Hanna Tetteh told Citi News on Wednesday that the extent of mismanagement has made it rather impossible to sustain any of the initiatives.
Â"As far as the management of the PSIs was concerned, it was a disaster-all of them, without exception,Â" she disclosed.
She chronicled a tall list of irregularities including evasion of taxes, flagrant violation of the procurement law, and defective accounting procedures.
It is however not clear if any of the managers of the initiatives will be hauled before court.
Story by Nathan Gadugah/Myjoyonline.com/Ghana

Original_Search_Title: 'Warri South-West Among Best LGs in Delta'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Warri South-West Among Best LGs in Delta'
Body_Text:
Mrs. Rose Tulu is the leader of Warri South West legislative arm and presently the only woman council legislative leader in Delta State. In this interview with SPECIAL CORRESPONDENT Sunday Apah, she speaks on her mission in politics, and her challenges as a legislator, among other issues.
Excerpts:
HOW did you join politics?
I joined politics in 1983, before returing back to do business. But I discovered that I have a role to play in politics, and came back during the administration of Chief James Onanefe Ibori in 1999, where I was PDP woman leader in my Isaba ward.
What inspired your participation in politics?
What inspired me into politics was the zeal in me to positively affect the grassroots and the polity for better. That was my real inspiration. When I saw what governors where doing for their people when I was young, I said to my self that I must be in politics to assist my people in my own little way.
What is your take on complaints by electorates that those they elect abandoned them after being elected?
It is not true that they are being abandoned because you can not really satisfy everybody that works for you; but if you get to satisfy at least 75 per cent of the electorate or the people, then you have tried.
As a leader, what has been your achievements so for?
Our achievements are too numerous to mention, since the inception of our administration. My council is one of the best local government areas in Delta State so far in terms of infrastructural development and human empowerment.
What about your ward?
Even in last year's budget, there were two projects for my ward: building of two teachers' quarters and provision of a cassava processing machine. And in this year's budget, the local government chairman made provision for good things for the people of my ward with the proposed renovation of our market for the women, building of centre for self delivery and renovation of classroom blocks.
Does it mean you have fulfilled your electioneering promises?
At least I have done 75 per cent of what I promised the people. During my campaign, I promised them that I will buy free note books for students of JSS 1 to JSS 3 and primary three to six, which I did August last year.
What have been your challenges?
One of my major challenges is that people who supported us are not always satisfied, but we are still begging them that life is not as they think. Things can not be done all at the same time. I want to appeal to them to be patient with us, for with time there will be a better life for everybody as far as democracy is concerned.
How would describe the administration of Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan?
Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan is the best governor I have ever seen in my life as far as Delta State is concerned.
Any advice for Ijaw sons who are jostling to become the governor of Delta State come 2011?
I am supporting Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan because politics is all about continuity, the governor needs to continue from where he stops, or end it where he wants. I want the Ijaw nation to give him more support again.
Would you want to re-contest in 2011?
Well, it is not my position to say that I want to go back; if my people want me back for second term, then I will return and continue with my good work and even attract more dividends of democracy to my ward. So what I have done will speak for me. Just like what the governor has done, that is speaking for him.

Original_Search_Title: Officials to Improve Flow of Rural Products
Headline: Angola: Officials to Improve Flow of Rural Products
Body_Text:
Cabinda — The government of northern Cabinda province is studying mechanisms to improve the supply of products ploughed by peasants from Buco Zau district to the province capital at reasonable prices, Angop learnt from a local source.
According to the municipal Administrator, everything is being done to create these mechanisms with the purpose of trading products (banana, cassava, sweet potatoes and yam) at the markets of province as well as in the bordering Massabi area.
Marta Lelo, who spoke on the benefits of peace in the country, said that this has been an handicap for her administration since the production percentage is higher and therefore producers have found great difficulties to distribute goods.
The official believes that soon the government will take measures to streamline the flow of these products to the province capital.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Should Apologise to Locked-Up Communities - Oni
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Should Apologise to Locked-Up Communities - Oni
Body_Text:
Ado — Ekiti State governor, Engr Segun Oni has said government at all levels ought to tendered an unreserved apology to communities that are locked-up due to lack of access roads.
The governor was speaking in Ado Ekiti, yesterday while receiving the Ponyon community in Yagba Local Government of Kogi who had paid him a thank-you visit over the construction of 11 .64km road linking Ponyon to Irele in Ikole Local Government area of the state
Oni said his government would remain committed to the task of opening up communities in the State.
He said it was criminal for people to be locked-up because of government's inability to provide access roads, adding that government should be full of apologies to the communities that have been locked-up for years.
"We are opening up communities and we will continue to do that without any apology.
"I was confronted with the reality of the necessity of access roads during the campaign for the rerun election last year when we went to Iyemero, Ikole Local Government. That town never had road since it was founded.
"When we got to Iyemero, the people were singing and dancing, saying I have transformed their lives by giving them money. It was when I asked one of their leaders to explain what they meant that the crowd was told that 200 heaps of cassava that used to sell for N3,500 before the road was constructed is now being sold for N42,000. I now further realised the full import of road because a lot of people had been locked-up to disadvantage by successive governments and this is criminal," Governor Oni said
Speaking earlier, the traditional ruler of the of the community, Oba Bola Ikuponiyi commended Oni for uplifting the community.
"we are short of words and expressions to demonstrate how deeply we have been positively touched by this unmerited favour which God Almighty has used you to give to us."

Original_Search_Title: Japan Grants $97,000 for Cassava Processing in Oyo
Headline: Nigeria: Japan Grants $97,000 for Cassava Processing in Oyo
Body_Text:
The Government of Japan yesterday approved a grant of US$ 97,023 for cassava processing project to Ifeledun Cassava Processing Cooperative Investments and Credit Society in Oyo town, Oyo State.
The component to be provided through the grant include: Cassava processing equipment, three graters, one flash drier, one hammer mill and one borehole with a water tank.
Presenting the grant to the beneficiaries in Abuja, Deputy Chief of Mission, Embassy of Japan in Nigeria, Mr Shigeru Hamano,said the grant which is under the Japanese Grant Assistance for Grassroots Human Security Projects (GGP) is to improve cassava processing in Oyo state.
He said the project is also to support the United National Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO) in Nigeria, adding that it hoped the project will contribute to community development.
Since independence, the Japanese assistance to Nigeria has amounted to about US4.2 billion (about 600 billion naira).
Under the GGP, 107 projects with a total amount of US$5,838,045 have been implemented throughout Nigeria since 1998.
Speaking on behalf of the beneficiaries, Mrs Grace A Alade thanked the donors and promised to work seriously to make sure that the real objective of the grant is achieved.

Original_Search_Title: UN Backs Project to Improve Cassava Processing
Headline: Nigeria: UN Backs Project to Improve Cassava Processing
Body_Text:
The United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO), Japan and a Nigerian cassava processing group today signed an agreement to support a project to improve cassava processing in a bid to enhance the quality and value of food in the African country.
UNIDO will facilitate the collaboration arrangement between the Japanese Government and Ifelodun Cassava Processing, a non-governmental organization, and provide technical assistance in the implementation of the project. The NGO received a grant of $97,023 from Japan while about $100,000 will be contributed by UNIDO.
"We hope that this project, funded by the Government of Japan and supported by UNIDO, will contribute to community development in Oyo state," said Shigeru Hamano, Japan's deputy ambassador to Nigeria.
"UNIDO's role is to provide smallholder processors with the technical support that will enhance large output and good quality of product to help them meet international standards. The components to be provided under the funding include cassava processing equipment such as graters, flash dryer, hammer mill and a borehole," said UNIDO's national programme officer in Nigeria, Reuben Bamidele.

Original_Search_Title: Umaru Swaray, 'Alone and Isolated by Other Refugees'
Headline: Uganda: Umaru Swaray, 'Alone and Isolated by Other Refugees'
Body_Text:
Nakivale — Umaru Swaray, 33, was a child soldier during Liberia's civil war. Separated from his family, he walked for months crossing the continent to Uganda, searching for his family, who had escaped the war. He found them in Kampala in 2004 - on the day they were leaving for the Netherlands.
Swaray's parents and nine brothers had been granted refugee status by the Dutch government but their reunion came too late for him. IRIN spoke to him at Nakivale refugee settlement in western Uganda:
"My mother was holding me so tight in her arms and wouldn't stop crying. I knew there was something she didn't have the courage to tell me.
"We were too many, the government said. So I had to stay in Uganda.
"It was hard to hold back the tears while telling my mother not to worry about me, that it was for the best. That at least they would be fine.
"One day we will be together again, I told her. But I wonder if that will ever happen.
"I have learned a trade to make a living. I worked as a barber for some time in Kampala. But when I was threatened by a Liberian, the Ministry of Disaster Management and Refugees decided to send me to Nakivale to ensure my personal safety.
"I have lived here since 2008, in a mud hut which I built myself. I have a small garden where I grow cassava and other vegetables. This small house reminds me of the one I was in with my family in Bomi county, Liberia.
"Life is hard here. I don't feel good here. I am the only Liberian in the settlement and as such I am isolated from the other people living here. I tried to mingle with the Congolese, my mother is Congolese after all; with Somalis and Rwandans, but everyone sticks to his own people. I have no friends.
"I wish I could find a single friend among them, someone to talk to. I wish I could at least do what I love, open a barber shop. But I am too afraid even to try. I am sure no-one would come to me. Here everyone goes to the shops of people belonging to their tribal affiliation. Why would they go to a Liberian?
"So the only thing I wish for myself is be able to join my family and have a normal life, just like everyone else.
"I was told this may not be possible. Since I am too scared for my life to go back to Liberia, I have no other option than to stay here. At least I feel safe here.
"I would go anywhere, anywhere, if I could find some peace of mind. Every man needs that."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: 12,000 Displaced in Western Part of Country
Headline: Uganda: 12,000 Displaced in Western Part of Country
Body_Text:
Kampala — About 12,174 people have been displaced in five districts in the Rwenzori-Kigezi region following landslides and floods that hit the region last week.
Kabarole district was the most affected with 3,873, followed by Kasese (3,640), Kisoro (2,324), Bundibugyo (1,532) and Kabale (805), according to a situation analysis report by the Uganda Red Cross Society.
The disaster preparedness ministry yesterday said it had dispatched 200 bags of maize flour and 60 bags of beans.
The minister, Prof. Tarsis Kabwegyere, said more food and non-food supplies would follow.
The Red Cross and the district leaders mobilised 300kg of cassava flour and 70 blankets, which were distributed to the affected families.
The Red Cross secretary general, Michael Nataka, advised the public to heed the warning messages about the pending disasters in their areas.
The disaster preparedness ministry early this month warned that 500,000 people from the Rwenzori and Kigezi regions in western Uganda were at risk of landslides and floods.

Original_Search_Title: Son of Owino Cassava Seller Excels
Headline: Uganda: Son of Owino Cassava Seller Excels
Body_Text:
Kampala — Mohammed Musa had to study under the pressure of not being able to pay his school fees on time.
His father, Rajab Musa, sold cassava in Owino Market to meet his school requirements.
The Kakungulu memorial student, who scored 23 points by getting 4ABBA in history, economics, geography and Islam, thanked his head teacher, who he said was patient with him.
"The news of my success did not come as a surprise to me because I set a target of 23 points for the final exams. I am proud of achieving the goal," Musa said.
He said his father constantly reminded him to work hard so that he relieved of university fees.
Musa, who wants to do business computing at Makerere University, added that he used to print badges on t-shirts for school clubs and label uniforms for pocket money.

Original_Search_Title: Mother Rescues Son From Being Beheaded By Uncle
Headline: Uganda: Mother Rescues Son From Being Beheaded By Uncle
Body_Text:
Kampala — WHEN Vincent Tamale visited his sister Gertrude Nakabugo in Nabukalu zone in Bwaise, Kawempe division, the latter thought it was just a courtesy call.
Nakabugo, a mother of three, got the shock of her life when her ownl brother - same mother, same father - took a knife to her seven-month-old son, George Kavuma.
Tamale alias Meddie Musiramu was arrested for attempting to cut off his nephew's head. Tamale, a resident of Kawempe-Mbogo, is disabled but held the little boy tight on the ground in an attempt to cut off his head.
Tamale, who recently converted from Christianity to Islam, is known for duplicating keys in and around Kampala city. "You just take a copy of any key and you will have a duplicate in 15 minutes," says a boda boda rider in Kawempe.
The victim's elder brother, Rogers Kitengo, 4, reportedly found Tamale cutting his younger brother's neck and made an alarm that attracted neighbours. Residents say they found the kid trapped in Tamale's disabled legs with a knife in his hands. The mother pulled away her son, but the toddler had sustained some injuries on the neck.
The incident happened in the morning hours of February 12. Tamale is usually ferried on either a boda boda or in a wheelbarrow to wherever he wants to go, according to Nakabugo.
Nakabugo says Tamale paid her a visit on Thursday evening (February 11) and slept over.
"But he was behaving in a suspicious way all night long. He was unsettled right from the time he entered the house but I did not pay much attention to it because he has always visited me," Nakabugo narrates.
Nakabugo says when day broke, she asked her brother to leave on grounds that she would not be able to look after him. "I bathed my son at around 8:00am on Friday and put him to bed. Then I proceeded to my place of work," she remembers. Nakabugo sells fried cassava a stone's throw away from her single-room house in Nabukalu zone.
"As I was starting to fry the cassava, Kitengo came running and crying, saying 'uncle is cutting off the baby's head'. I abandoned everything and ran home." Nakabugo says she pulled away her crying son and rushed to Kawempe Police Station to report the case.
After reporting, she took the child to Mulago Hospital where he is still undergoing treatment, although he was discharged. The boy is slowly recovering.
"He was crying in pain because he (Tamale) had started cutting him and blood had started oozing," Nakabugo says.
As Nakabugo went to the Police, irate residents who had gathered, wanted to lynch Tamale whom residents say looked as if he was possessed. It was the timely intervention of the Police that saved Tamale's life. But the Police had to struggle to put Tamale onto their patrol truck as he attempted to resist arrest.
Nakabugo, who does not stay with the father of her children, opened up a case of attempted murder against Tamale on file number 22/12/02/2010 at Kawempe Police Station. But by February 14, Tamale had refused to make a statement to the Police.
Tamale is said to have remained numb, stripped naked and fought fellow inmates. When he was given a private cell where he was being detained, he also attempted to fight the Police officers. "Although he is disabled, he has a lot of power in the upper part of his body," notes a Police officer.
Kawempe division Police CID chief Paul Mugisha, says Tamale was taken to Butabika Hospital for psychiatric evaluation. "As per now, we presume he is a lunatic," Mugisha observes.
He says the next step that the Police will take will be based on the medical report by psychiatrists at Butabika. "The doctors will advise us. We have not prosecuted him as for now," Mugisha explains.
Earlier, Police said Tamale would face charges of attempted murder. Nakabugo and Tamale are children to Leokadia and the late Kaabunga Mugerwa of Maddu-Gomba, Mpigi district. Residents suspect Tamale's case could have been an attempted ritual murder.
In September last year, the Police issued guidelines to the public to curb ritual murders that claimed about 21 lives between January and August 2009. The anti-sacrifice trafficking taskforce chief, Moses Binoga, notes that out of the 21 people murdered, 13 were juveniles.
Binoga says most of the victims of child sacrifice have not been under the care of their biological parents and that the killers take advantage of the laxity of the caretakers. He appealed to the public to watch out for traditional healers, who are believed to be the perpetuators of the practice.
Binoga says they had intensified investigations into cases of human sacrifice and had arrested 91 suspects since January, 32 of whom had been taken to court. He appeals to families to report cases of missing members to ensure timely investigations and prosecution of suspects.
He advises parents to gather reasonable information about people like maids and family members with whom they entrust their children. It is suspected that some rich people use the human parts in shrines in the belief that they evoke business success, while others use them on construction sites claiming that they dispel bad spirits that may lead to the building's collapse.

Original_Search_Title: Researchers Dispute WHO Stand on Malaria Deaths
Headline: Ugandan Researchers Dispute WHO Stand on Malaria Deaths
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Contrary to a recent World Health Organisation survey, Ugandan researchers say the problem of increased resistance to malarial drugs is more likely related to improper use of medications than the presence of fake and poor-quality medications.
This non-compliant behaviour among Ugandan patients stems primarily from cultural beliefs and ignorance, said Dr Myers Lugemwa, the officer in charge of malaria research at the ministry of health.
And this pattern could significantly increase resistance to treatment if not addressed, he warned.
While acknowledging the presence of fake and poor-quality drugs, Dr Lugemwa invokes Uganda's policy for ensuring the quality and safety of malaria medications.
"We cannot just have people die because the drugs are not good," he told The EastAfrican. "We have tested them and found them effective."
His observations follow the release of a report, on February 8, which concluded that the most effective type of malaria-fighting drugs sold in three African countries - including Uganda - are often of poor quality, raising fears of increased resistance.
The use of poor-quality medications lower's the body's ability to resist the malaria parasite.
Nearly 200 drug samples have gone full quality-control testing at a US laboratory to examine the amount of active ingredients present and drug purity.
Forty-four per cent of samples from Senegal failed the full quality test, followed by 30 per cent from Madagascar and 26 per cent from Uganda.
Overall, between 16 per cent and 40 per cent of artemisinin-based drugs sold in Senegal, Madagascar and Uganda failed the quality testing for reasons including impurities and lack of active ingredients, the survey found.
The study was the first part of a 10-country examination of antimalarials in Africa by the US and the WHO.
Artemisinin-based drugs are the only affordable treatment for malaria left in the global medicine cabinet.
Other drugs have already lost effectiveness due to increased resistance.
Ugandan experts sought to clarify the research findings, based on their work with patients.
They acknowledged a high failure rate for drugs 10 years ago, but said the rate has improved significantly in recent years, hence the need to focus on other causes of resistance.
For example, Ugandan researchers say patients are likely to stop taking medication when they begin to feel better, or to share their drugs with neighbours and friends who have not been properly diagnosed.
"There are problems with consumers due to self-medication and incomplete doses. As a result, drugs become ineffective," said Fredrick Ssekyana, the spokesperson for the Uganda National Drug Authority (NDA).
NDA confirmed that drug counterfeits are in the market, a global problem they say they are working with Interpol to curb.
"We have people who change expiry dates, drug labels and even make pills using cassava flour," said Ssekyana.
The three-country report also found bad drugs at both the public and the private health facilities, meaning governments - some buying medicines with donor funds - are not doing enough to keep poor-quality pills out.
All of the drugs tested from the Ugandan public sector, however, passed the tests.
"There are countries where donated medicines are not subjected to quality controls; they're just accepted," he said.
"There are countries in Africa where Chinese products have been donated and found to be unacceptable later in the public sector."
Donations come in during epidemics or seasonal disease outbreaks.

Original_Search_Title: Citizens Need Permanent Status - Says U.S. Rep.
Headline: Liberia: Citizens Need Permanent Status - Says U.S. Rep.
Body_Text:
US Representative Keith Ellison says there is a need for Liberians on Deferred Enforced Departure (DED) to get permanent status (Green Card). Rep. Ellison made the statement on Sunday, February 14 during an immigration service organized by the Organization of Liberians in Minnesota and the Liberian Ministers Association held at Bethel Outreach Church in Robbinsdale, Minnesota.
The State of Minnesota is hosting thousands of Liberians who left their native land in the 1990s as a result of the civil war. His statement comes against the backdrop of the pending expiry of the Deferred Enforced Departure (DED) this year.
Along with top US Congressmen, Twin Cities have been pledging and bracing for action to fill the absence of thousands of Liberians whose temporary protected status nears an end. On several separate occasions, the Federal acted prudently extending DEDs for thousands of Liberians residing in the country, somehow illegally.
Former President George Bush twice extended the stay of the Liberians following interventions by two key US Congressmen as well as the Liberian Government. As an October in 2007 deadline approached for thousands of Liberians to leave the United States, pockets of the Twin Cities face potentially substantial losses of everything from rent checks to nursing-home workers.
As a result, Minnesota's Liberian community -- one of the largest outside West Africa - has been gaining allies for its push to have Congress step in. It may be recalled that lobbying delegation from Fairview Health Services made rounds on Capitol Hill, for example, stressing an urgent need to keep Liberian health-care workers on their jobs in Minnesota, said Mary Edwards, Fairview's vice president of public policy.
At some Minnesota nursing homes, Liberians fill one in four jobs -- dispensing medications, cleaning rooms and running the laundries. Homes throughout the Twin Cities have employees at risk of deportation, the Minnesota Health & Housing Alliance learned in a survey of its member facilities.
"They play such a huge role in what we do in every nursing home in the metro area," said Judy Christopherson, human resources director for Jones-Harrison Residence in Minneapolis. "We will do everything we can to help them remain here."
At the same time, city, church, school and real estate officials are bracing for the potential loss of several thousand residents in several Twin Cities suburbs, foreseeing a cascade of problems if Liberians are forced to leave.
Many Liberians are planning to take Tuesday off work and spend the day circulating petitions and telephoning political offices to plead for action on their immigration problem. Since 1991, Liberians have been allowed to live and work in the United States while a bloody civil war raged in their homeland. The trouble began when the U.S. Department of Homeland Security announced last fall it was ending the permission, called Temporary Protected Status (TPS) in October of 2007.
It argued that Liberia has stabilized, and a U.N. force was on the ground maintaining order in the country and there is a democratically elected government that is trying to mend the broken pieces.
Homeland Security hasn't said how many Liberians have to leave. By most estimates, there are at least 1,000 in Minnesota on temporary status and nearly 4,000 nationwide. (Minnesota also is home to thousands of Liberians who have become U.S. citizens or won permanent residency and are not affected by the ruling.)
Some of the Liberians have been living most of their lives outside their homeland. Maima Sylla of Brooklyn Park, for example, was taken to the United States when she was 7 and left with relatives. According to her, she heard that her father and grandparents died during the war, and she has lost contact with her mother who fled to Ivory Coast.
"How can I get sent back to a country that I know nothing about?" asked Sylla, who's now 27. "I have no family there. I don't know anyone there." But Homeland Security says that those who have to leave have known all along their status was temporary and that they shouldn't have assumed the shelter they were granted was a pathway to staying permanently.
A glut of houses, apartments
Amid the uncertainty, Brooklyn Park -- home to Minnesotan's largest concentration of Liberians - braced for hundreds of homes and apartments to flood the real-estate market. Counting the Liberians' spouses and U.S.-born children, city officials estimate 3,000 to 5,000 people in Minnesota could be pulling up stakes.
"We anticipate a spike of houses for sale," Joel Spoonheim, Brooklyn Park economic and redevelopment director, told city officials in a recent memo. "Our fear is that this may result in a significant increase in rental property or vacant homes as the ownership market may not be able to absorb this abrupt influx."
Brooklyn Park and nearby cities stand to lose more than neighborhood stability. Liberians have woven their West African ways into the culture of the northern Twin Cities area. Liberian immigrants ladle spicy cassava gravy over steaming rice at ethnic restaurants. They score goals for their soccer teams and raise their voices in passionate prayer at churches.
"Most people don't realize what a significant role the Liberians have come to play in our community," said the Rev. Steve Larson, Senior Pastor at Redeemer Covenant Church on Brooklyn Blvd. "When they are gone, we will realize it."
But how many will leave?
Numbers are hard to come by because many Liberians won't talk about their immigration status. "People are very secretive with that issue, even in the church," said the Rev. Charles Goah, a Liberian who is senior pastor at United Christian Fellowship Church in Minneapolis.
