Goah and Larson lead a group of area pastors that has set a top priority on ministering to the temporary Liberians -- if they can find them.
Schools face uncertainty
When the reports about Liberians being on the verge of deportation surged, schools planning for next fall of the period faced the same uncertainty. It was gathered that Liberia was represented more than any other country in Osseo Area Schools' English language learner's classes. At least 300 students in the classes were born in Liberia or countries where Liberians took refuge during the civil war.
"We may lose hundreds of students, but we can't anticipate that with any accuracy," said Lorilee Andreini, Osseo's English language learner's coordinator at the time. Nathan Kreie, who teaches about 40 Liberian students at Park Center High School, gets hints, but the signals aren't always easy to read. Some students have told him they've stopped answering phones at home for fear immigration authorities are already closing in. Others say they plan to hide when the time comes to leave.
Legislation before Congress would allow them to apply for permanent residency. Two Minnesota Democrats are co-sponsors: Sen. Amy Klobuchar and Rep. Keith Ellison.

Original_Search_Title: Voter Shot in Chaotic Mbale By-Election
Headline: Uganda: Voter Shot in Chaotic Mbale By-Election
Body_Text:
Mbale — What was a largely peaceful Mbale Municipality by-election turned chaotic yesterday afternoon when a minister's bodyguard allegedly shot a supporter of Dr Shinyabulo Mutende, the NRM independent candidate.
Trouble, according to eyewitnesses, began when a car carrying state ministers Michael Werikhe (housing), Asuman Kiyingi (lands) and Workers' MP Sam Lyomoki blockaded a vehicle carrying Dr Mutende's supporters on Naboa Road. The ministers were campaigning for the official NRM candidate, Mr John Wambogo.
The two groups had a standoff, with each refusing to give way. In the melee, Dr Mutende's supporters reportedly opted to swerve past the ministers' car-but in the process, brushed the posh Pajero landcruiser.
According to Dr Lyomoki, their bodyguard was forced to shoot because Mr Mutende's supporters were scampering after damaging the politician's car. "Our bodyguard shot but in the air, not at them," Dr Lyomoki told Daily Monitor yesterday.
Incensed Mr Mutende's supporters, led by Mbale District Chairman Bernard Mujasi then stormed the Mbale Central Police Station, demanding the ministers' arrest. In their company was a bleeding Abdullah Kayiwa, the supporter allegedly shot by Werikhe's bodyguard. "We have been fighting for democracy. These wrong elements within the NRM party are trying to derail us by causing chaos. Is this the democracy we have been fighting for? How does a prominent person like Werikhe do that to his own people?" Mr Mujasi asked at the police station
The bleeding Kayiwa was then rushed to Mbale Hospital in a police pick-up. When Daily Monitor visited him minutes later, he was being attended to by a nurse. A policewoman deployed to watch over him at the hospital told this newspaper that she was under instructions not to let anyone talk to Mr Kayiwa.
Mr Iddi Ssenkumbi, the eastern region police spokesperson, said the bodyguard had been arrested and the Force was still investigating the matter. "We have to come to the bottom of this case. They have recorded statements at the police," Mr Ssekumbi told journalists.
For a by-election that had received a lot of media hype, caused a late appearance of the President, seen a district chairman break away from his party to support an independent candidate-one would have expected a massive turn out of voters to make a final statement on what had been a month-long political drama. But that was not the case in Mbale Municipality yesterday.
Whereas polling stations had opened by 7.30am, by midday, barely one third of the 20,661 voters had turned out to vote at the 72 polling centres.
The reasons were as various as the players. They ranged from poor voter sensitisation, missing names in the register to fears of violence and weather vagaries. "We started early in the morning but voters are trickling in slowly hopefully by afternoon the numbers will have increased," said Mr Joseph Biribonwa of the Electoral Commission interviewed in the early hours.
The FDC flag bearer, Mr Jack Wamanga Wamai, who cast his vote at Fairway Primary School, said the updated voters register had not been given to the parties early enough. He also pointed at the heavy deployment of security personnel as not just a deterrent to his supporters but a cover for the ruling party to rig.
Hidden ballot box
At Busamaga polling station, voting came to a temporary halt when FDC strongman Nandala Mafabi and his supporters allegedly "discovered" a ballot box stashed in a nearby cassava garden. There were claims of another ballot box being hidden in a church. But Mr Tom Buruku, an electoral commissioner, told Daily Monitor that the Busamaga incident was blown out of proportion.
"That was a new polling station created after we created new wards. The law demands that when you create a new ward, you must have a polling station. Residents had been sensitised about this new polling station and there was no need for the confusion," he said.

Original_Search_Title: BOL Calls for Quality Foods in International Market
Headline: Nigeria: BOL Calls for Quality Foods in International Market
Body_Text:
Lagos — Bank of Industry (BoI) has urged Small and Medium Scale Industries (SMIs) in the country to strive towards producing quality goods that would compete favorably in the international market.
Managing Director of the Bank, Mr. Evelyn Oputu who made this call in Lagos at a one day training organized by African Growth Opportunity Act (AGOA) and BOI for local producers said there was need for Nigerians to manufacture products that would be marketable in the globally.
She said that although the BOI mandate is to provide financial assistance for the establishment of medium scale projects in the country, the local producer needs to show commitment in what ever they are doing.
She said that the bank would only assist Nigerians who are prepared to move to the level of their production and add value to their products.
The BOI boss said that the bank also helped in the expansion, diversification and modernization existing enterprises and rehabilitation of the ailing industries.
Also speaking, The Nigeria Ambassador to United States of America Ms. Robin Sanders said that AGOA has offered a competitive advantage for African made products to enter United States of America market.
She said that the Nigeria has a lot of products like cassava, ginger, foot wears, and nuts among others to be exported to US.
"We want to see some of these products exported to USA in larger quantity this year," she added.
However, AGOA has helped to boost sub-Sahara African export as a total of $44.2 billion were exported in 2006, an increase of 16 per cent.
It also provide an opportunity to diversify what Africa sells to the US while petroleum product still make up the largest share of AGOA export. Non- oil export from Africa reached $3.2 billion in 2006.

Original_Search_Title: World Bank to Give Us$198m for Gulu-Arua Roads
Headline: Uganda: World Bank to Give Us$198m for Gulu-Arua Roads
Body_Text:
Kampala — The ministry of Finance is due to sign an agreement for 198 million US dollars with the World Bank for the construction of the Gulu-Atyak and the Gulu-Arua road networks.
World Bank country director Kundhari Kadiresan says the funding of the roads is vital as the two roads connect Uganda to southern Sudan.
She says the fund will also help in strengthening issues of road safety as Uganda is one of the countries with the highest cases of road carnage in the world.
Meanwhile, Kadiresan says the World Bank is also set to fund a 30 Million US dollar project aimed at improving research in Cassava production.

Original_Search_Title: NAPEP - States Should Devote 5 Percent Budget to Poverty Eradication
Headline: Nigeria: NAPEP - States Should Devote 5 Percent Budget to Poverty Eradication
Body_Text:
State governments should put 5 percent of their annual budgets into Commercial and Microfinance banks for onward lending to the poor people, National Coordinator of the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP) Dr. Magnus L Kpakol has said.
Speaking yesterday in Abuja when he paid a courtesy visit to the Corporate Office of Media Trust, Kpakol said the arrangement is part of NAPEP's new strategy in fighting poverty this year.
He said the states should identify areas of comparative advantage which they want to promote like groundnut processing, cassava processing etc. and give directives to the financial institutions to lend the money in those areas.
He said: "We've decided to come up with a new strategy this year that will allow us fight poverty in a more visible manner. The primary view in Nigeria is that you should give money to the poor but that is not the essence. At best we should give credit to people through the commercial and microfinance banks to go and start businesses."
He said if the country is going to be successful in the fight, it has to involve the private sector to operate effectively, adding that NAPEP will give motivation and encouragement to commercial and microfinance banks to lend to the people.
"We will use Federal government money and partner with the state and local governments to promote the creation of jobs, businesses and make sure that people save money to take care of their children's education and health needs," he said.
He said they are going to work with the states and local governments to cut the interest rates to make it affordable for the poor people, adding that they will monitor the activities of the financial institutions to make sure that the loan disbursement reaches the target beneficiaries.
On Keke NAPEP, Kpakol said about 980 kekes have not been cleared by the supplier because of demurrage during the port congestion, adding that he has pleaded with the port Authority reduce the demurrage to the supplier.
He said they have successfully allocated the Keke to all the state government but some have not gone to take delivery of their own.
On the price of the Keke, Dr Kpakol said the actual price is N450, 000 per keke but they are giving it at a subsidised price.

Original_Search_Title: My Publications Not Influenced By Religion - Bakare-Yusuf
Headline: Nigeria: My Publications Not Influenced By Religion - Bakare-Yusuf
Body_Text:
Publisher of Cassava Republic, Dr. Bibi Bakare-Yusuf, is among the few Nigerian publishers creating waves in proactive publishing. In this instance, young writers are encouraged to prove their mettle through her rare assistance and tutoring. In this interview with SENIOR CORRESPONDENT, Yemi Adebisi, she speaks on new standard for publishing in the country as well as her belief and philosophy of life. Excerpt:
Young Nigerian writers have faced different kinds of difficulties in publishing their works, because of bureaucratic constraints they encounter in the hands of publishers. Given this scenario, do you believe in the existence of Nigeria Publishers Association (NPA)?
I know there is a publishers' association, but the kind of publishers that come under the banner of the association are mostly textbook publishers. What we (Cassava) and Kachifo are doing, is trying to break that mode, and although as far as I know anyway, they are also moving to educational books. But for now, they publish novels and authors without asking them to pay. I am talking of those kinds of publishing. There are two of us that I know, with BookKrafts in Ibadan and I think they go on royalties. We need more of publishers like that in a nation of over 140 million people.
What would you regard as the selling point that distinguishes and makes your publishing outfit more attractive to people?
Which people, readers or writers now?
Both
I think for the writers, the fact is that they are going to work with a publisher that would be serious about editing their works, because when you say you write a book, it is not a book, it is a manuscript. It only becomes a book when a whole group of people, a whole group of other talents come together i.e. an editor, a copy editor, a proofreader, a cover designer, a graphics designer, a layout person all come together to turn the manuscript into a book. So, if writers want to go to a publishing house where they would feel loved as number one; number two, their works would be taken seriously, they would be subjected to the rigours that the manuscript deserves to get it to a different level, get it beyond what the author had imagined. I think that would attract people to us and I am hoping that people are building more confidence to consider publishing with us.
On the readers' side, Cassava Republic is building up a brand slowly, and I think whether we like it or not, people will always judge a book by its cover. I am one of those people who judge a book by its cover. I don't often read Nigerian books, not because they are not good, but because I don't like the quality. My relationship with books is tactile. It involves experiencing the thrill, the colour they have used, the layout, the cover design. All of these affect how I relate when I have come to buy a book. And what people have said over and over again about Cassava Republic is that our books are beautiful and are objects of beauty. I think a book should do that. It should be an object of beauty. A book should be something you can carry, something you can put on your coffee table and enjoy looking at, even if you don't open it. But, hopefully, one day you will open it and read. So, I think on those two levels from the writers' perspective of creating a home where they can trust, that not only will you edit their books and market them well, but will also pay their royalties. You are doing this not just to have a creative outfit, but to also make money. But in your head you know very few writers the world over make money. There are very few, you can count them.
You spent most of your years in the States as well as the UK and some say you are too English in your approach to issues, your dressing, your philosophy of life and so on. What do you have to say to this?
I am too English! I think every time you live in a society and you are untouched or unaffected by that society, then that means you are a stone. And I am affected by probably having lived in the UK and I think for the better, not for the worst. I was in the United Kingdom for the most significant part of my life actually, and I think that I am very British, but also very Nigerian. I was born in Nigeria, but what people tend to see is the veneer of Westerness or Englishness or Britishness. They don't see the Nigerianess until we start having certain issues. People think about my philosophy of life, because Nigerians are overly religious and they are always interested in finding out your religion. And I am not particularly interested in engaging people about my religion. However, I am interested in engaging people about ethics, living an ethical life. That is important to me, but for a religious life, I am not interested. Whatever religion I choose is personal to me. I hear people talking about having a personal relationship with God; if it is so personal then keep it to yourself. Don't discuss it with people. That is why it is personal. However, in terms of public discourse, how do you live an ethical life? How do you look at all other people around you and say how can I help my brother or sister and be of use to society? There is no point in talking religion all day long, and yet we are the most corrupt country in the world.
It is not a particularly interesting area of discussion for me, talking about religion. I don't care what your religion is; I don't care whether you are from Kafanchan or Oyo or wherever. I care about what comes out of your mouth. My favourite philosopher is Frantz Fanon. One of his books says, "Oh my body, make me always a man who questions." For me, if that is all I can do, then fine. I don't care whether people say I am English or whatever they think I am doesn't bother me. I think, the core of who I am, is actually more Nigerian than British.
Does that mean you are not concerned about what happens after leaving this world?
I think we need to focus on what it means to be here; what are the impact and effects we are having on people now, on the environment. Because when we are dead, we are dead. However, if we use the concept of heaven and earth as a kind of a guide so that we can become ethical beings, perhaps if I do wrong, I might not go to heaven. I might go to hell if it helps you, but I am not interested in that kind of concept of hell, perhaps heaven but not hell. Because I don't believe there is such a thing as hell. I think that I am more interested in the philosophy of a figure like Esu, the Yoruba Orisa, trickster god. I am interested in that, because being that there is concept of truth, there is no absolute concept, because there are many truths. There are ways in which we can live our lives that will be meaningful to you and I. There are ways in which we can think about good or bad only in contexts depending on the historical moment we live in. Once upon a time, the Christian god was used to justify slavery. That's the reality, because the first ship that carried the slaves was called Jesus. So, Christianity has a bloody history, just like Islam. Does it mean we are going to stop being Muslims or Christians? No, it doesn't. It just means that at different times in histories, they had to go through certain moments. So, for me, the idea that something is absolutely good or bad is problematic to me.
How did you come about this worldview?
I am a questioning human being, and I had always been interested in why, even before I left Nigeria. I grew up in a Muslim household and I loved going to the mosque, but at the same time I always questioned why should I not go to the mosque when I was menstruating. Why is it okay to go this day and not that day? From those kinds of experience, it opened up the world to question everything I loved. I travelled round the world, going to Islamic countries, because I am actually interested in Islamic history, philosophy, architecture, contributions and civilisation.
Do you run into a problem and think there is a god somewhere that would help you?
No I don't. I have a fragile health, but I don't think prayers would do me any good. I think that what I need when I am ill or feeling depressed, I don't think of praying, but to gather my friends to get a bottle of wine, drink and dance and be merry. I am serious. I feel that things I need in the world, other human beings can give them to me. Nature can give them to me. So, I don't go to any external being outside of myself, and besides there are too many people asking God for help. I don't want to add to His or her woes. God is a woman by the way.
God is a woman?
Since we don't know whether he is a He or She, I will proclaim her in my own image. So, like I said there are too many people who don't believe in other humans, and I want to leave her to concentrate on them and solve my own problems through my friends.
How much of this has affected the quality of your work as a publisher?
It doesn't. It just means that I am open-minded and when I get some whack crazy authors telling me some whacky stuff, I am open enough to allow them express themselves to believe that this is their world and that is what they want to let everyone hear about. I think I am in a privileged position, my worldviews put me in a wonderful position to receive people writing all kinds of interesting things that would force us to think of who we are, what we see in the mirror and if we don't like what we see, do we want to change it?
Do you intend to sell this same idea and philosophy to your children?
I am already selling it to your children, never mind my own children because the books your children are going to be reading are going to have stories and themes that would force them to ask questions of you, as a father and mother.

Original_Search_Title: Police Hold Five Suspects over Robbery
Headline: Uganda: Police Hold Five Suspects over Robbery
Body_Text:
Kampala — SECURITY officials on Thursday night and Friday morning arrested five suspects in separate operations over the theft of household items from the home of the Anglican Bishop of Northern Uganda.
The prime suspect is Job Oliver, 24, a resident of Lower Mican in Kanyogoga parish, who was arrested on Thursday night in a joint operation by the Police and other security organs.
The other four suspects are Richard Odong, 19, Fred Ocaya, 30, Justo Wokorac, 47 and James Omony, 20. The suspects are held at Gulu Central Police Station.
The thieves broke into the house of Bishop Johnson Gakumba last Tuesday night and stole household property including gifts worth over sh1m donated to him during his consecration on December 20.
The Police said they had recovered a solar lamp, a 2kg tin of ground-nut and sim-sim paste, five cassava tubers, a carton of family biscuits and two pairs of window curtains.
Gulu district Police commander John Ematu reported that Oliver, the key suspect, faces theft charges and other related counts.

Original_Search_Title: 'Modernisation Has Killed Traditional Ways of Checking Communal Excesses'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Modernisation Has Killed Traditional Ways of Checking Communal Excesses'
Body_Text:
Daniel Abomtse is the Ter Tyoshin, a second class traditional ruler in Gwer West local government area of Benue State. Barely months on the throne, Abomtse reveals in this concluding part of the interview with Correspondent Terna Doki, in Makurdi, the challenges of his kingdom and laments that the colonialists distorted their cultural values in many respects. He explains why the Tiv people do not celebrate Yam festivals and appeals to government to turn attention to his domain, which is badly in need of infrastructural development, among other issues. Excerpts...
A lot of people wondered why you wanted change in name of your title to Ter Tyoshin. Has the change been effected officially?
Yes, we are now in the process of amending the title through the legislature. And for now, my people do address me as Ter Tyoshin and not Ter Nagi anymore, pending when the final amendments will be done by the state Assembly. I know that this year has already run out, so I know it will be done by next year.
Sir, you said colonialists altered aspects of the culture of Tyoshin people, which to a large extent is negative influence. What leftovers of the Whiteman's sojourn in your domain will you still cherish?
We cherish western education that he brought and the money system. He also brought religion which in one way or the other has dealt a big blow on our mechanism of checking the community through witchcraft and deistic practices. Religion also came with education, which today I can speak glowingly in English language. We can also talk about the electricity he brought and the modernism he brought, changes in social lives, access roads were created an electricity fanned improved life. Again, originally, we were on exchange marriage system, which he abolished and introduced the monetised marriage, which we now use.
What can you say are the negative influence of the Whiteman on the community, apart from distorted traditional names?
In spite of the good things the Whiteman brought, you will discover that the young ones now no longer respect elders. Once you acquire western education, you don't fear witchcraft and deistic practices again, which in the days of yore, we used in checking excesses of society. This has given room to what we know as militia groups springing up. When death occur in a particular community, the Tiv culture prescribed that we must go on traditional inquest; we must consult the oracle and the oracle is the best medium we can use to determine the causes of the death. In Tiv custom, cosmology and worldview, we don't accept death ordinarily; it is blamed on two things - either doomed by the deistic or witchcraft to die. So death must have causative agent and we don't just merely attribute it to God. We attribute it to the gods of the land and so we must consult the oracle. Nowadays, the religion we practice does not recognise the oracle practice.
In what ways has the abandonment of fetish practice affected the people?
It has dealt a very strong blow to the society and you know that witchcraft has two functions - benevolent and malevolent functions. When it is benevolent, it is there trying to check the excesses of anybody who is attempting to go beyond the society norms and values. When it is malfunction, we can invoke it to strike or attract to death anybody who has proved disobedience to the society. So, in a way, I can say the Whiteman has given us a very big blow. And in our marriage system too, it is no longer stable because marriage has been monetised. In the past, we use to adopt a mechanism of check and balances. If you give me your daughter, I will also give you my daughter to marry. And if my daughter bears off-springs may be, two or three and yours happen to do same at my end, if one death is recorded on one side, one death will also be recorded this side balance up based on the principles of exchange marriage system. It is an equilibrium principle also. So, that's what we use to do, using our cultural practices then. But now, you can take money and marry elsewhere. But before now, it was even an abomination for you to travel elsewhere and take a wife, except you marry within your clan, to preserve the trinity inheritance of your people.
Would you now advocate that Tiv people should revert to the old system of marriage by barter?
I will not completely support that. But I can say in areas where we can see relics of good things in the culture, let us sustain them, particularly in the oracle consultation. I am a born again child of God, but I cannot usurp the powers of elders. Tivs believe in the religion of the elders or the worldview of their religion that once death occurs, consult the oracle. And they will check members within themselves and that used to bring sanity in the community, because once an elder decrees, everybody will bow. But nowadays, if you decree anything, somebody, who is money big, will come and hire some boys to go and kill you. We have cases of silent assassinations and killings of elders like that. Now religion prohibits life-taking, but the youths of nowadays, do not respect human life.

Original_Search_Title: Mimiko Preaches Love for Less-Privileged
Headline: Nigeria: Mimiko Preaches Love for Less-Privileged
Body_Text:
Wife of Ondo State governor, Mrs. Olukemi Mimiko, has called on Nigerians to contribute to the up keep and welfare of the needy in the society.
Mrs. Mimiko made the plea over the weekend while celebrating the Christmas and end of the year party with the inmates of Ago-Ireti Leprosarium and Ondo State Children's Home, Oba-Ile. She said the visit afforded her the opportunity to felicitate with the less privileged particularly the children, describing them as special gift from God.
According to her, "Anyone who is blessed with such gifts should treat them with love and care instead of abandoning them. "The end of the year activities are to re-assure our children and the less privileged in our society that they are always being considered in government programmes and policies.
"Let me place it on record here that the present administration in the state is very passionate about the present and future of our children through numerous programmes and policies to avail children the opportunities of development and attaining their God-given potentials in all ramifications." She however presented various items and cash gift to the inmate and the children.
Items presented include tubers of yam, kegs of palm oil, cartons of soya beans oil, bags of rice among others.
In her address at the Children's Home, the Commissioner for Women Affairs and Social Development, Mrs. Bukola Tenabe, noted that the state Governor, Dr. Olusegun Mimiko, has approved the construction of a new and modern hostel as well as a reception hall to solve the accommodation problems being faced by the home.
She therefore commended the wife of the governor for her visit to the Home, saying, "this was largely due to the recognition of the happiness and joy which her visit has always brought to the staff and children of the Home."

Original_Search_Title: Yuletide - Karu Orphanage Receives Donations
Headline: Nigeria: Yuletide - Karu Orphanage Receives Donations
Body_Text:
Management of Karu Children's Home yesterday said the home has received donations worth thousands of naira from individuals and corporate organizations during this yuletide season.
Secretary to the home's administrator, Joseph Daniel, said the donated items include bags of rice, cartons of Indomie Noodles, toiletries, tooth paste, olive oil, juice packages, tubers of yam, and cash, among others.
He disclosed that the home currently has 27 children under its care, adding that the major challenge facing in the place is inadequate water supply.
He called on parents to desist from the habit of child abandonment and maltreatment, saying that every child is important to the society.

Original_Search_Title: Atiku's Politics of Entitlement
Headline: Nigeria: Atiku's Politics of Entitlement
Body_Text:
THE reported events of Thursday November 16, 2010, highlighted what Nigerians knew all along. For starters, a group known as Coalition of Atiku Northern Supporters (CANSU) who are staunch supporters of the former Vice-President, Alhaji Atiku Abubakar, was reported to have issued a statement.
In their statement, they alluded to the fact that crisis would engulf Nigeria if Atiku who is the candidate for the North did not get the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) nomination. They stated that the crisis they would cause would be much greater in proportion to the Boko Haram massacres! In their own words:
"This is no threat. Boko Haram will be a child's play compared to the actions our members can take. We have been patient enough. And enough, they said is enough. The presidency is our right. The fact that we bowed to allow a Southerner, Chief Obasanjo, who has now turned to be a betrayer and a disgrace to us, to become president in 1999 does not mean we do not know what to do to reclaim our right. We are happy that Turaki has now seen reason with us that what Nigeria needs is a violent change and not a peaceful one.
"We wish to state equivocally, without any ambiguity, that we, the Atiku supporters, are prepared for any eventuality should our patron be refused the ticket he has worked for. Our position remains: Atiku Presidency 'Za mu iso waje ko wace irin hali' (We will get there by whatever means) may Allah, the Almighty not CANSU, cancel Nigeria...".
When the above statement was made, I was thinking that they were bunch of fanatics who were getting restive that their candidate was about to lose the nomination and so resorted to threat to intimidate the country. However, to my greatest surprise, the former Vice President, Atiku Abubakar, not only endorsed their statement but also rationalized it! In a statement by his Campaign Organization, he quoted the late President John F. Kennedy saying: "Those who make peaceful resolution impossible make violent revolution inevitable".
Instead of the former president disowning the group's statement, he poured fuel into the already volatile situation. It could now be seen that the group is being sponsored by Atiku himself. That little bird dancing fearlessly in the street has a drummer by the bush side urging it on.
It is most unfortunate that the person aspiring to lead the country would sanction violence to achieve his selfish ambition of being president. In my place, we have a proverb which says that it is when the wind blows that we see everything hidden under the chicken's 'underwear'.
The nomination battle has exposed Atiku Abubakar for what he is. He believes that power belongs to the North and that they only leased it to the South and now wants to take back what belongs to them. To him and some people from the North that have same mentality, Nigeria is their birthright and only they could decide what they would do with it.
Whoever denies them of their birthright would be dealt with. To them it does not matter what any part of the country thinks, they owned Nigeria! This means, if you are from any other section from the country, or even other major ethnic groups, good luck to you. To him, the North holds the yam and the knife, whoever the North gives a piece of the yam would eat it.
Out of Nigeria's 50 years of independence, the North has ruled for 38 years leaving the South with only 12 years. In fact, out of the 12 leaders Nigeria has produced since independence, the North accounted for nine! On the South's side, Gen J.T.U. Aguiyi-Ironsi, Chief Ernest Shonekan and Olusegun Obasanjo shared the nine years among themselves. As John Adams would say: "Facts are stubborn things; and whatever may be our wishes, our inclinations, or the dictates of our passion, they cannot alter the state of facts and evidence".
This is our history and they are indisputable. Many people from the North, including Atiku want to maintain that status quo. Left for Atiku, anyone else can go to hell! Where is Atiku's sense of justice? He is touting justice and fair play as slogan without understanding what it means or so blinded by his ambition.
That arrogance and the air of entitlement is what the problem is. The North and even some people from the South believe that no one can smell any federal appointment let alone the presidency unless they lick the North's behind. Most Northerners grew up with the same 'born to rule' mentality.
Do you blame them? Since they were born, every president had come from the North, whether military or civilian; so the attitude would be that it is theirs for the asking. So if anyone from the South becomes president, it would just be like the person is holding it in trust for them, or they just wanted to take a little break. Now that the shoe is on the other feet, it seems like the heavens is about to fall on top of the earth.
There is a wise saying: "Let the Chairman take the place of the Provost, so as to see what it feels like to be on your feet during meetings". How about the South East and many ethnic minorities that have never tasted the presidency? Have they lost hope in the union called Nigeria? Of course, they are still alive even if their hope is wavering.
This reminds me of the event in the Holy Book, the Bible, where the Israelites were about to be delivered from Egypt. The heart of Pharoah and his servants were turned against his people and they said: "Why have we done this, that we have let Israel go from serving us".
The action of Atiku and some of his Northern supporters reminds me also of the actions of the supporters of Senator John McCain
Mr. Chukwudi NWOKOYE writes from Maryland, USA.

Original_Search_Title: 'Gwer West Backward, Needs Development'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Gwer West Backward, Needs Development'
Body_Text:
Daniel Abomtse is the Ter Tyoshin, a second class traditional ruler in Gwer West local government area of Benue State. Barely months on the throne, Abomtse reveals in this interview with Correspondent Terna Doki, in Makurdi, the challenges of his kingdom and laments that the colonialists distorted their cultural values in many respects. He explains why the Tiv people do not celebrate Yam festivals and appeals to government to turn attention to his domain, which is badly in need of infrastructural development, among other issues. Excerpts...
You are barely few months on the throne now, and were elevated from being a journalist to suddenly preside over your people, some of them, old enough to be your grand father. How has been the experience?
It is quite interesting and challenging; interesting because as young as I am, between the age bracket of 40 and midway 50, God has saddled me with the task of overseeing the traditional affairs of my people, as it has to do with the customs, values and norms of my people. And it is challenging because there are so many problems on hand awaiting solution. First and foremost, my people, the people of Tyoshin, in Gwer West local government are backward in development. I am talking about availability of infrastructure, that is, access roads, electricity, and pipe borne water, health facilities and so forth. Only recently I was on air, both in the electronic and print to seek support for re-construction of a culvert that caved in along Naka road. I took the matter to FERMA and other relevant agencies and FERMA in its wisdom, responded and the construction is fast going on. Soon, the portion that caved in and split the road will be completed or re-built.
Are there other constraints as regards divergent parties?
Yes, we have political gladiators. You can see them in so many camps, pursuing individualistic interest. Recently I called a meeting of stakeholders and I emphasised on this issue of oneness of purpose, so that we can be together and build a strong, developed society for even the Tyoshin man or woman yet unborn. But the biggest challenge facing my people and I now, like I said earlier, is lack of infrastructural development. You know there are no access roads and the only trunk 'A' road - the Makurdi -Adoka road is impassable and this is a federal road.
Have you sought assistance from the federal government in this regard?
We are still appealing to the Federal Government through its relevant agencies to come to the aid of Tyosin people, by seeing to it that that road is rehabilitated or re-constructed. I will also appeal to the state government to step up actions in seeing that the only road that was budgeted for - the Taraku-Aondoana-Naka-Agagbe road - that the contractors are mobilised to site to commence work, so that we can give our people a new lease of life in terms of infrastructural development. There are other issues too, but as time goes on, definitely, we will overcome, particularly when one looks round to consider the way and manner valuable economic trees are depleted in this local government.
What particular trees are we looking at here and what is being done about this situation?
We have iron trees and locust beans, amongst many others; and we know too well that these trees add value to life. Together with my council and relevant stakeholders, I am taking steps to end this practice. I have also taken proactive steps to stop bush burning in the area in order to preserve our ecosystem. Also the process to reduce litigation over land is on, just as we are tackling the issue of the militia group popularly known as 'Alayo' that has been taken lives wantonly. So gradually we have been giving our community new lease of life and surely, in the shortest possible time, one would come to understand why Gwer West should be ranked with the best local governments in the state, in terms of development, peace and tranquility.
You ascended the throne in the midst of serious tension and protest that many thought you might not survive it, but you weathered the storm. How did you manage it?
(Laughs) First, leadership comes from God. Once the Almighty anoints you, you become a leader. I intentionally initiated these moves for peace, by first of all visiting my co-contestants, those who lost out. And through the process of talking with them, peace has returned and fraternal relationship has been created. Also, I visited other aggrieved persons who were not even contestants; I made it a point of duty to visit them in their various abodes. It was a positive step towards peace. And I don't think now that I have any other group or a person that is aggrieved as I am talking.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Foodstuff Skyrocket, as Citizens Celebrate Christmas
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Foodstuff Skyrocket, as Citizens Celebrate Christmas
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerians are lamenting the high prices of foodstuff as they shop for Christmas tomorrow. They complain that the prices are beyond their reach and blamed the worsening economy for their plight.
Daily Independent investigations revealed that prices of foodstuff, which showed marginal increases during the last Sallah celebration, have risen almost beyond the reach of an average Nigerian.
Consequently, traders complain of low patronage, blaming it on high transportation costs, high inflation rate, poor economy and lack of disposable income.
A bag of Stallion and Tomato brands of rice that was sold for N6,500, N6,700 now sells for N7,500, N7,700, while others like Cap rice range from N8,000 to N10,000.
Basket of fresh tomatoes that used to be N3,500, N4,000 now sells at N6,000, N7,000 depending on healthy they are. Also, basket of pepper ranges from N4,500 to N5,5000. 25 litres of vegetable oil and palm oil now go for N7,800.
Onions are very expensive in the market and most consumers could not afford it. A basket of onions now sells for N12,000 and N14,000 depending on the size.
Some people believe that the festive season provides an opportunity for traders to make huge profits through price hikes.
In the survey, commodities which prices shot up included rice, chicken, beef, tomatoes, groundnut oil, palm oil, pepper, yams, onions and potatoes.

Original_Search_Title: Human Suffering Escalates
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Human Suffering Escalates
Body_Text:
Abidjan — For three days 25-year-old, Ousmane Traoré attended the private clinic in the populous district of Abobo, north of Abidjan. Suffering from gunshot wounds to the head and abdomen as a result of the Ivorian opposition demonstrations, he was forced to leave the main hospital in Treichville, south of Abidjan, due to a lack of assistance.
"When I was shot by the police, a man took me to the hospital for treatment. I was bleeding heavily", says Traore. However, after waiting for thirty minutes, he had still not been attended to. One doctor admitted that he had been ordered not to attend to protesters who had been injured.
According to Traore, he was not alone in this situation. A dozen other wounded protestors were also forced to find alternative treatment, as they were denied treatment by government health services.
Lamine Tiote lost a friend as a result of the indifference of staff at the local hospital. "My best friend from my neighbourhood died on Sunday. We waited for hours at the hospital to be attended to. Unfortunately, he died of his wounds in my arms", he said. "I just screamed for help but nobody listened to me. It was unbelievable", he added bitterly.
Both Ousmane Traoré and Lamine Tiote are among the "walking wounded", who were injured as a result of protests, which called for the departure of the incumbent President Laurent Gbagbo. According to the UN, 50 people have so far been killed in these skirmishes.
The African Union and the West African Economic Community, ECOWAS have demanded that Gbagbo step down. According to Amnesty International, eyewitnesses said that protestors who were seriously injured during the previous day's mass protests in Abidjan were denied medical treatment amid threats from security forces.
"This is not fair. We have already received scores of wounded members of the security forces. Despite our limited services, we provide assistance to all", said Gabriel Beugré, director of the Military hospital of Abobo, who also has three opposition protesters under his care.
Earlier this week, national television broadcast a visit by the Minister of Health for the Gbagbo's government to the wounded in hospital. Since the 28th November elections, the country has remained tense. Four days after the elections took place, the independent election commission,IEC released provisional results, with Ouattara receiving 54 percent of the vote and Gbagbo 46 percent.
The Constitutional Council, challenged the body with its president, Paul Yao N'Dré, arguing that the IEC had not followed the established guidelines. The next day N'Dré nullified the IEC's provisional results, declaring Gbagbo the winner with 51 percent of the vote against 49 percent for Ouattara.
Gbagbo and Ouattara were both sworn in as president within hours of one another, with each forming their respective governments Fears are growing that the dispute between Gbagbo and Quattara, internationally recongised a winner of the election could tip the country back into civil war.
The closure of the borders and ongoing internal clashes between supporters of the two presidents, have hampered the entry of food products into the country, which has led to an increase in food prices.
With the current political instability, civilians are not only living with the threat of violence but also with higher market prices. "We can't get anything on the market. Food prices have risen by 50 to 75 percent", complained Catherine Kouassi from Yopougon, an area in the north of the capital.
High food prices have been blamed on difficulties experienced in trying to access and supply the various markets in the Ivorian economic capital. "The truckers have decided to increase the transportation costs, which means higher prices for the products which are sold," said Rosine Tah, as she shopped for eggplant and yam.
At Plateau Dokoui market, a pile of ten eggplants is now selling at $1 dollar against 50 cents a month ago. A kilogramme of meat costs $2,50 to $3. "If we are not careful, people will not be able to afford to eat in the coming days", warns Thomas Konan, an economic analyst from Abidjan.
Thirty-five-year-old taxi driver Raoul Kodjo is still waiting for the situation to normalise. "It's been a week since my business came to a sudden halt. But the resources are dwindling at home.
We need to find a political quick-fix to get us out of this crisis. Otherwise, people will be in agony here ", he said.
It has since been reported that the UN refugee agency, UNHCR, has beefed up its relief supplies to accommodate refugees fleeing Ivory Coast's post- election crisis and could now handle up to 30,000 people. Adrian Edwards, UNHCR's spokesman said, "Over the past few days the UNHCR has been beefing up its contingency arrangements for Cote d'Ivoire in light of the continued instability there".
"Ahead of the weekend, we airlifted additional supplies to Liberia and Guinea from our emergency stockpile in Copenhagen. We currently stand ready to cope with the needs of up to 30,000 refugees," he added.

Original_Search_Title: Northern Chiefs Unhappy Over Poor Roads
Headline: Ghana: Northern Chiefs Unhappy Over Poor Roads
Body_Text:
The refusal of successive governments to pay attention to the extremely poor road networks in the Northern Region, has stirred the chiefs and people in 12 deprived communities along the Yendi-Bimbilla road, to desperately remind the government to walk its talk of ensuring a better Ghana for all.
The irate chiefs and their people, with support from some Community Based Organisations (CBOs), including the Ghana Development Community Association (GDCA), have therefore, embarked on massive demonstrations to back their demand for the immediate construction of the Yendi-Bimbilla road.
They complained that this particular road had been captured in almost every national budget without any work commencing on it, but the 2011 budget, recently read by the Finance Minister, Dr. Kwabena Duffuor, according to the people, did not mention the Yendi-Bimbilla road, which they said was a ploy by the government to deprive them of their share of road development.
However, the protestors, including the chiefs, wielded tree branches and placards displaying inscriptions like 'No road - No vote'; 'Our foodstuffs are rotting in the bush'; '2011 budget is incomplete' and 'Where is the Better Ghana?" among others.
The spokesperson of the chiefs and people, Seidu Shaini Mohammed, in a petition presented to the Nanumba North District Assembly for onward transmission to the President, maintained that the nature of the Yendi-Bimbilla road had brought serious socio-economic setbacks to the people.
According to the protestors, there had been several cases of sudden deaths among pregnant women, who were unduly delayed on their way to the health centres as a result of the poor nature of the roads.
They asserted that some women in labour had died on bicycles and motorbikes, because vehicles were not ready to ply the road. Several food items, including, yam, maize, rice and soya bean have also been locked up in the bush, due to the poor nature of road in the Nanumba District.
Meanwhile, the Yendi-Bimbilla road, which is classified as an international road and connects travelers from the Northern Region to the Greater Accra and Volta regions, Togo and Burkina Faso.
The aggrieved chiefs and people are therefore demanding that the ruling government commence and complete the construction of the road before the 2012 general elections, or risk losing their votes.

Original_Search_Title: Mpuasuman in Tears Over Total Neglect as It Celebrates Annual Munufie Yam Festival
Headline: Ghana: Mpuasuman in Tears Over Total Neglect as It Celebrates Annual Munufie Yam Festival
Body_Text:
Though it was a very colourful an occasion during the celebration of the Annual Munufie Yam festival by the chiefs and people of Mpuasuman at Japekrom in the Jaman South District of Brong-Ahafo on Saturday November, 13 this year, and which was characterised by traditional drumming and dancing to depict the culture of the traditional area, the plight of the area could not be concealed by the Paramount Chief, Okatakyie Amoa Aturu Nkonkonkyia.
It was very touchy when Okatakyie Amoa Aturu Nkonkonkyia stated that Mpuasuman had many infrastructural challenges, continuing that Japekrom was slowly being turned into a ghost town, partly with the help of past governments.
"What have we done to deserve this? The past governments have turned deaf ears to our pleas, complaints and requests. We sometimes feel like a rejected child," the Omanhene stated. He emphasised that the relocation of government offices and officials to New Drobo, under the pretext of it being the district capital, had contributed to the collapse of the local economy.
According to him, the once-vibrant lorry park and market had turned desolate, partly with the support of past regional administrations, adding that the previous government was in office for eight years, and still, was unable to complete the building of a fire station for the district.
He said for eight years the area had begged consistently, but in vain, for the building of a permanent slaughter house.
Okatakyie Nkonkonkyia noted that currently, the site where animals are slaughtered, and sold to people for consumption, was not only deplorable, but shameful and a health hazard to the community, and equally so where the meat is sold - in the remains an old building with nothing like modern amenities for the hygienic handling of meat.
He continued that the new building to replace the dilapidated one was also started by the previous administration, but now remains an uncompleted building like several others.
The Paramount Chief called on the government to come to the aid of the people of Japekrom. "Today, on the celebration of the 2010 Munufie Yam Festival, the cry of Mpuasuman, to the able government of President Mills, is in reference to the quotation in the Bible "come over to Macedonia". We equally cry loudly "come over to Japekrom and have mercy on us," Okatakyie Nkonkonkyia appealed.
This year's Munufie Yam Festival, under the theme, "Education - the bedrock for development," was used to launch an Education Endowment Fund to support needy but brilliant students in the area.
The Paramount Chief acknowledged the need to build a solid foundation for the youth who are the future leaders, which he said, was the duty and responsibility of the state of Mpuasuman.
He pleaded with the President to help obtain at least one second cycle institution like a Technical/Vocational institute, a Health Training Centre, Fire Service Training Center, or a boys Senior High School that would bear the name of Japekrom.
Okatakyie Nkonkonkyia noted that there had being an uproar and discussions on the country's justice system, emphasising that the very foundation and the guardian of the constitution was the justice system, therefore, one could not stress the importance of the institution.
He reminded that the government that Nananom could play a vital role in the administration of justice, if proper resources are given, and it is left with the government to tap the resources to help reduce the work load on the courts, in order to foster fast and effective administration of justice in the country.
According to the Chief, it was deplorable to hear that cases as old as ten, twenty and some thirty years were lingering in the high courts, to the burden of the citizens who are mainly seeking justice under the constitution.
He continued that the financial costs of such justice, sometimes become so unbearable that many citizens rather choose to stay silent while their rights are been trampled upon, adding many of the violent clashes and conflicts in societies is a result of this.
The Paramount Chief appealed to the government that chiefs of today understand what the rule of law and human rights was about, and that given the proper tools, they can would not only assist in the administration of justice, but also revive the best part of the country's culture and family life.

Original_Search_Title: N87 Billion to Elect Treasury Looters?
Headline: Nigeria: N87 Billion to Elect Treasury Looters?
Body_Text:
Democracy is popularly defined as a system of government designed by the people for the people. The adoption of this style as best practice of governance by several nation states is supported by the far reaching economic and social progress made by those countries with strong democratic principles and structures.
In this regard, the superior prosperity of the United States of America is seen as the result of its strong democratic structure, while the comparative poverty in North Korea, for example, is contrasted with the relative prosperity of its more democratic Southern neighbour.
Now, democracy invariably implies the adoption of the doctrine of one-man one vote as the state is characterised by formal equality of rights and privileges for all citizens. The system also expects eligible citizens to exercise their right to vote or be voted for. Thus, the principle of democracy would be breached wherever eligible citizens are denied their vote whether as a result of negligence or deliberate government manipulation. However, above conditionalities of voting rights must first be predicated on a formal agreement by groups of people who have freely expressed their mutual desires to live together and be governed within an accepted framework of rules and procedures generally defined as a Constitution! Vital ingredients for a successful Constitution must also include individual freedom of speech, equity and justice! Thus, a social contract, which creates a master/slave relationship or 'monkey de work, 'baboon de chop' syndrome is unlikely to sustain an enduringly stable community or nation.
It is for this reason that although hundreds of nation states may claim to be democratically inclined, a country such as ours also professes democracy but regrettably, the practice is inequitable and unjust because the motivation for electoral office and leadership is driven by primitive self-interest! This is the critical difference between the continuous progress in social welfare of so-called economically advanced countries and retrogressive, poverty-stricken state of most of those countries, which are endlessly developing!
A fine example of inequality and injustice that continue to deform our progress is evident in the size and value of remuneration or emolument packages enjoyed by those who are elected or selected to make our laws or administer our welfare! Nigerians have indeed been kept in the dark these last ten years or so, on the burden of cost of operating our democracy, even when it is clear that such secrecy is against the grain of transparency and accountability expected in a true democracy. However, the cat was let out of the bag recently when former President Obasanjo addressed the Niger State Senior Civil Servants at a retreat on Wednesday, 4/8/2010; Uncle Shege's admonition was that "we should look closely at the Legislature so that they can be more open and transparent in the way they do their work. Nobody knows in detail how much it costs us to maintain a senator. I believe it is more than N250m".
Well, the gentleman's guestimate must be respected to be fairly accurate; after all, he was our country's president for eight years. In any event, he remains a critical member of the ruling party and may still have fair estimates of what his 'boys' are earning in the field.
In any event, Obasanjo's guestimate appears to be corroborated by Professor Itse Sagay, a respectable and accomplished constitutional scholar in a lecture entitled "Legislating for the common good: contemporary issues and perspective" Sagay's observation are weighty and serious, especially in a country which United Nations Development indicators put amongst the poorest nations in the world, with over 70 per cent of population i.e. over 100 million people living below the poverty line with incomes of less than $2/day!
"Sagay noted that in 2009, a senator earned N240m in salaries and allowances, while his House of Representatives counterpart earned N203.76m. In other words, a senator earned about $1.7m and the members of the House earned $1.45m annually. By contrast, a U.S. senator earned $174,000 and in the UK, a parliamentarian earned about 64,000 pounds, annually.
"In 2009, the federal legislature received N102.8bn comprising N11.8bn as salaries and N90.96bn (non taxable) as allowances. Is the taxpayer getting value for this colossal sum in the current democratic dispensation? Should five per cent of Nigeria's annual budget be spent on 109 senators and 360 House of Representatives members? In other words, should 469 Nigerians gulp five per cent of our budget leaving the remaining 150m of us to N1,000 each?
"Barack Obama, the President of the richest country in the world earns $400,000 annually. The British Prime Minister, Mr. David Cameron earns Â£190,000 annually, while a Nigerian senator earns $1.7m. It's absurd. It is as someone has called it a feeding frenzy
"The Senate President is reported to be earning N250m quarterly or N83.3m per month while his deputy earns N50m monthly. The Senate has allocated N1.02bn as a quarterly allowance to its principal officers, known collectively as Senate leadership. Each of the other principal officers earns N78m every three months or N25m per month.
"This tragic state of affairs is unsustainable. Those in this feeding frenzy are endangering our democracy."
However, the curious twist in above reports is the response of the National Assembly, who did not refute the allegations as incorrect, but helplessly pointed fingers at whom they called the founder or progenitor of the abuse of public trust!
In a report titled "OBASANJO'S GOVERNMENT PROMOTED CORRUPTION" on pg 6 in Punch edition of 6/8/2010, "Members of House of Representatives under the aegis of the Minority Caucus on Thursday accused the administration of former President Olusegun Obasanjo of promoting corruption. They spoke against backdrop of media reports on Thursday, in which Obasanjo was credited as saying that members of National Assembly padded budgets and in some cases budgeted for ministries and agencies, which they did not ask for.
"He was also credited as saying that with the connivance of bureaucrats such funds were usually withdrawn from budgets for projects that did not benefit the people."
But leader of the minority caucus in the House, Alhaji Mohammed Ndume, described Obasanjo's comments as "unfair and uncharitable, arguing that lawmakers would not have bothered if the former president had said that Nigerian politicians were corrupt," They accused Obasanjo's government of doling out N50m to each member of National Assembly to support his failed bid for a third term in office.
Alhaji Ndume further noted that ..., "National Assembly has 80 percent People Democratic Party members in Senate and in House of Representatives and Obasanjo is the father and godfather of PDP.
"If that is so, then it means if the children are corrupt, then the father and grandfather are also corrupt!
"We all know the level of corruption in this country. It was during his eight-year rule that corruption moved from a low level to a higher level."
Whao! Fellow Nigerians, are we talking of criminal offences here and not just an unemployed hungry and deprived Nigerian stealing a tuber of yam to keep body and soul together? Well, if anyone believes that this level of sleaze is only peculiar to the Federal Legislature, then he should do a rethink! State Legislatures, Local Government Councils have learnt to play the game of 'follow your leader' very well! No wonder elections into public office at all levels have often become a 'do or die' matter to 'serve'! No wonder, a once unemployed rascal with connections acquires a N20m Hummer Jeep as soon as he is selected by his godfather as a local government chairman!
Well, we might ask if the Legislatures by themselves determine outrageous and vexatious emoluments, the answer is of course, no! Revenue Mobilisation and Fiscal Committee is constitutionally saddled with this responsibility, but any hope that this committee's decisions are above board were jettisoned by horrid allegations of corruption leveled against this committee when its members paid a courtesy call on Governor Amechi recently in Port Harcourt! The Rivers state government challenged the Committee Members to do their jobs transparently and should refrain from battering the prompt payments of states allocations with kickbacks or indeed predicating size of allocations and location of oil wells and related revenues on 'friendliness' of state governors. Amechi declared that states should never be made to go cap in hand to Abuja every month to collect their allocations!
Well, from above, it is clear we are in deep doodle! Where do the principles of equity, justice transparency, 'one man one vote' come in this mess?
Any hope that our democracy is on course has been finally shattered by former Governor Donald Duke's recent expose on how INEC is compromised by incumbent Governors. Donald Duke has cautioned against any optimism in the electoral process, even if the electoral commission is headed by a saint! Duke's expose makes interesting reading; in serious countries, the baby-face ex governor would be invited for debriefing by anti corruption and other law enforcement agencies! Duke has brazenly told us how he won his elections or was he narrating experiences which he garnered from the grapevine about what transpired in states other than Cross River?
In view of Duke's expose, we may just have kissed the over N87bn hurriedly passed by National Assembly goodbye! How Jega hopes to order and receive 150,000 or so units of data capture equipment and other imported equipments within three months will be a big challenge, especially as such equipment are not off the shelf and may require 6 - 8 weeks or more to manufacture and consolidate for shipment with another 3 - 4 weeks lead-time before arrival, port clearing and distribution. This would be an uphill task therefore, to complete a new voters' register and organize elections in four weeks before the January election; all these portend failure, but Nigerians are eternal optimists!
Even if all goes well and by some miracle the logistics are possible, and an excellent free and fair elections take place, are we likely to have credible leaders who truly wish to serve? The answer will still be a capital NO! You can use best election practices to choose a leader amongst "Ali Baba's forty thieves", but what you will get may be adjudged to be free and fair, but you will end up with a THIEF as victor! The political space in our country today promotes the survival of thieves and opportunists because the motivations as enshrined in 1999 constitution are self-serving and unjust and oppressive. Only a truly federal constitution will give birth to a new birth in our country and not fraudulently imposed 1999 version that will govern 2011 elections.
This article was first published on 16/08/2010, before the CBN Governor's recent revelations on legislators' oppressive emoluments, and the forward shift of the elections to April 2011.

Original_Search_Title: Tiv Students Commemorate Zaki Biam Military Invasion
Headline: Nigeria: Tiv Students Commemorate Zaki Biam Military Invasion
Body_Text:
Makurdi — The 2001 Zaki Biam military invasion has resurrected in poems with glistening images, as Tiv students lament the continuous torture, injustices and pains inflicted on the Tiv nation by the Nigerian nation-state.
National President, Community of Tiv Students (CTS), Simon Aondosuwe Tyoakaa, made the lamentations in Makurdi during the launch of a collection of poems on the Zaki Biam pogrom, titled, "Zaki Biam and other poems," authored by a journalist and poet, Samson Kukwa Yanor, to mark the ninth anniversary of the military invasion.
Tyoakaa said prior to 2001, Zaki Biam was the pride of Tiv nation, which truly earned Benue State the appellation of "food basket of the nation", as the mere mention of the name invoked the feelings of massive yam production in the Middle Belt.
He stated that "Today a new feeling is attached to the name. Our precious name is attached to genocide, killings and what have you? What can we do to regain the lost, but cherished status of this name is today a source of concern for Tiv students and other Tiv authors in the world, hence the reason for this book".
The CTS president further lamented that the reason for the unfair treatment of the Tivs in the Nigerian nation-state is the misconception of their accommodating, hospitable, simple and tolerant spirit, often perceived erroneously as sheepishness and stupidity.
"Today, the bones of our forefathers remain restless in their graves as they come to realize that their efforts in sustaining the spirit of "Ayatutu" (spirit of togetherness) has not been cherished by the living generation," Tyoakaa.
He added that though the Tivs fought hard for the unity of Nigeria during the civil war, yet apart from the national pains inflicted on the people from time immemorial , other ethnic groups which participated on the negative side like the Igbos, have five states and the Tivs have just a state and no one seems worried.
The CTS president further lampooned Tiv leaders for their docility in the face of the atrocities committed against the Tiv nation.
His words: "What is really wrong with our nation, the Tiv nation that we cannot react to this quantum of injustices and unfairness? Why is it that the appointment of Chief Mike Aondoakaa (SAN) as Minister of Justice raised so much dust in the South, but no one among us negatively reacted to his sharp removal by the present administration? Why must it be a Tiv man that should bear the name of a "former SAN"?
"How many of us are today Generals in the Armed Forces? How can the acclaimed fourth largest ethnic group in a country of over 250 ethnic groups have no single member representing it at the Federal Executive Council and yet eye-brows are not raised?"
He cautioned Nigeria not to push the Tiv nation to the wall to the extent of compelling the thoughts of behaving like the Niger Deltans, by establishing the Movement for the Emancipation of Ayatutu Kingdom (MEAK) before the Tiv nation will be accorded its due and fair share in the federation or a ministry of Ayatutu would be established for Tiv people.
The students reminded Tiv leadership to wake up from slumber to embrace its responsibilities and, before they rob the younger generation of Tivs, their future, as in Nigeria "nobody does it for anyone."
Reviewer of "Zaki Biam and Other Poems," Maria Ajima, said, although written in 2003, the collection of poems coming out in 2010 is a sign of the poet's personal spirit of perseverance, which he is willing to share with the world by boldly coming out in print.
She said Yanor requires the encouragement of the world, as according to her, "every black man needs a copy of this book because we all suffer under the same fate."

Original_Search_Title: Up, Close And Candid With Nollywood's Most Wicked Actress
Headline: Kenya: Up, Close And Candid With Nollywood's Most Wicked Actress
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Nigerian actress Patience Ozokwo has become a classic in her own time by playing the "evil woman" in dozens of Nollywood films.
She is ever so malicious in her roles as the wicked mother-in-law, the dishonest servant, the witch doctor and crafty mistress.
She is so good at her job that she occasionally is moved to tears when she watches herself on screen.
In one memorable scene -- the kind that wrenches viewers' emotions -- she snatches a baby's food and throws it away.
In another, she poisons a baby! But, off the screen, she is a cheerful family woman.
"You Kenyan men do not know how to greet women," she said at the beginning of our interview. "You don't just say 'hi'. You need to make them feel warm and embrace them."
That was last Thursday, the day after she arrived in Nairobi to attend the Tafrija Festival, a musical and cultural event held last night and sponsored by the Nigerian High Commission.
She is so well-known that it was not possible for her to sneak into the country unnoticed as fans who got wind of her arrival were waiting to catch a glimpse of one of Africa's most famous actresses.
"They welcomed me like a king. I have yet to recover from such a rousing welcome," she said at her hotel suite at the Nairobi Safari Club.
She has no trouble explaining her dual character. "When I appear as the bad woman, I only see myself as the mirror of what should not be happening in society," she said, breaking into laughter.
"I use these scenes to minister to wicked souls filled with jealousy, hatred and envy."
The widowed mother of four got into the movie business almost by accident. She was retrenched from Radio Nigeria in January 1985 and then went into business and later into movies.
Veteran Nigerian comedian Chika Okpala, popularly known as Chief Zebrudaya Okoroigwe Nwogbo, alias 4.30, jokingly invited her to play the wife of top Nollywood actor Pete Eneh in a TV commercial.
That became her ticket to fame. Some of her popular movies screened in Kenya include Old School, Love After Love, Fishers of Men and 2 Rats where she acted with the "two little boys" Chinedu Ikedieze and Osita Iheme. Unknown to many fans, both are adult men and graduates in mass communication.
The name Mama G stuck to Mrs Ozokwo after an unforgettable role she played in the movie Old School. The cast kept on singing "Mama G, Gee for General. General in all the Nkwobi and Odekwu joints in all Western Africa"
Mama G is involved in helping widows and other women to create job opportunities and hopes to be remembered for touching the lives of many people.
Mama G is a teetotaller, doesn't smoke and loves going to church when she can. While in Kenya, she misses her favourite meal of pounded yams and onugbu or bitter leaf soup.

Original_Search_Title: Man Dies, Several Injured as Lorry Overturns in Cross River
Headline: Nigeria: Man Dies, Several Injured as Lorry Overturns in Cross River
Body_Text:
A passenger in a lorry loaded with yams lost his life while scores of others were injured, when the vehicle heading for Calabar from Ogoja crashed, Wednesday.
The vehicle, with registration number AE 685 LA - Adamawa, skidded off the road at Apiapum in Obubra Local Government Area of Cross River State, in its bid to avoid hitting a pedestrian, who was at the time, crossing the road.
When the lorry fell, it spilled its contents, burying some of the passengers, who apparently were on top of the goods.
At the news of the mishap, several locals ran to the scene and were able to rescue passengers, who were trapped under the yams. Several of the victims were rescued but a passenger, did not make it alive, as the truck crushed him under the vehicle.
Efforts by the people, mostly youths to roll over the truck to recover the body was futile as it was too heavy.
Eye witnesses said that the major cause of the accident was the bad state of the road, which lacked clearing areas due to the deep cuts along the borders of the tarred road.
They said that the phenomenon, which is at both sides of the road stretches about 6kms and narrowing the road to only the tarred section had often put motorists and pedestrians at risk.
They, meanwhile, called on government to urgently see to it that the road sides were filled up to provide opportunity for vehicles to be able to clear off the road, whenever the need arose.
Victims of the disaster, most of whom had major fractures on their limbs were taken to the Obubra General Hospital.

Original_Search_Title: Deltans Will Still Invest On Uduaghan - Darah
Headline: Nigeria: Deltans Will Still Invest On Uduaghan - Darah
Body_Text:
GORDIANA DARAH is the Director General of Uduaghan Campaign Organisation. ln this interview with Correspondent, FELIX IGBEKOYI, he maintains that Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan, removed by the Court of Appeal a fortnight ago, will win the re-run election, based on his convincing records and support from the state and national headquarters of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP). Excerpts:
As the Director General of Uduaghan Campaign Organisation, what is the position of the organisation to the sacking of Dr. Uduaghan from office by the Court of Appeal?
This development concerns the Appeal Court ruling in Benin that nullified the 2007 election of Dr. Uduaghan. The judgment says that there should be a rerun. So, we in the Uduaghan Campaign Organisation respect the verdict of the Court and we abide by the requirements. For us, we are battle ready any day. Our campaign organisation is election-ready; it is because the organisation has a formidable, directorate under my management. The directorate has functions, mobilisation, campaigns in print media, welfare, transport, issues, enlightenment all these goes with electoral warfare and are covered by the organisation.
How are you going to prepare within the next three months to deliver Dr. Uduaghan?
The judgment has only accelerated the momentum of work we are supposed to do. It has shortened the initial calculation of voters' registration in January, then elections in April next year. Now we are battle ready for the new election. We are back to 2007, and we are armed with experience, knowledge, confidence and optimism about the candidacy of Dr. Uduaghan. Our confidence is boosted by the unequivocal and endorsement of Uduaghan by the headquarters of the PDP in Abuja. The National chairman has said without any equivocation that Dr. Uduaghan is the sole candidate for the PDP in this election. We are further empowered by the solidity of the Delta State Executive of the PDP under the chairmanship of Barrister Nwaoboshi, who has called a meeting of the party and affirmed total backing for the PDP in the state for the re-election of Uduaghan on the basis of excellent performance for the past three and half years, on the basis of the proud legacy of PDP, as the moderniser of Delta State. The position is backed up by abundant evidence of government work at various layers of society and various senatorial districts. So, we are waiting for INEC to announce the date.
What gives you the confidence that Dr. Uduaghan will win the election?
I have no doubt that on the criteria of good work by PDP and Dr. Uduaghan no other political party in Delta State can match us on the election field. I can promise that we will trash any party in the elections because we have overwhelming support of the state. This support is evident in the members of House of Assembly in PDP. The party controls the executive in the 25 local governments in Delta State, has 12 years history of having executed the must ambitions and most gigantic infrastructure that any state government whatever, wherever. PDP under Uduaghan, has the highest rate of employment of new staff, the only one in Nigeria that is running a state-funded free medical care for children age 0-5 and for all pregnant women without discrimination of state of origin. That is welfare scheme; the only one that is building an international airport to promote business and to make Asaba the economic hob of both South East and South South, the only one that has put street and highway lighting covering more than 100 kilometers; pays one of the best and highest salaries to over 50,000 civil servants and teachers. We have teachers in every village in Delta State, over 1,500 villages. They get regular salaries. Those are the passes the electorates will look at. They will invest in Uduaghan again, and in PDP again because they are sure that their future is assured. That is the basis of our optimism.
In the light of many factions in the party, don't you think that will be a minus for the party during the election?
It is normal to have factions in the party. That is the dynamism of Delta politics; you must have interest groups within the party. I once said that anybody who disagrees within the party is dissident, not an opponent. Your opponent is somebody who is in another party. No other party in Delta State has capacity to win the general election. You must be present. Your presence is not just offices and signposts. You must also have councillors, members of House of Assembly, chairmen of the wards. It is not magic. It is not coup d'etat. We are talking about but election. There is no party in Delta State that has that spread of achievements; not what you are going to do but what you have done before. There is disagreement everywhere and we thrash it out in the open. I have no fear that such division will cost us any electoral fortune. We have enough people supporting PDP. The country is PDP. The national chairman said they couldn't afford to have an error in Delta State. The state is the center of gravity of political powers in the South South. So, it is a national interest. The PDP national will join that of Delta State. From my campaign organisations, then you talk of hundreds of voluntary groups, Uduaghan Vanguard, Uduaghan Revolution, Women for Uduaghan, Youths for Uduaghan, Drivers for Uduaghan and others. They are all going to go to the field to campaign. It is a total mobilisation war.
What happens if Uduaghan is not fielded as the candidate, either, as some of his opponents have been envisaging, the court stops him or otherwise. Where do you get the spare tyre?
That does not arise. The party in Abuja has said it without equivocation. Uduaghan is the man with whom they are well pleased. The party in the state has confirmed their position. We the commanders of the campaign in the state are to join the national body of the party to make sure our candidate wins. I cannot speculate on the court stopping Dr. Uduaghan from re-run. If it arises we know we are capable. We had had this difficulty before. Two weeks to Ibori's second tenure we were in court in Abuja.
