Some ungrateful people in Delta State and PDP claimed that Ibori was an ex-convict. We battled that case and won it. Let another one come that is not a problem for us. We have sufficient experience as election generals. We have scars all over our body as a demonstration of things we have done before. If another one comes PDP National and state will handle it.
The other faction loyal to Chief Edwin Clark is saying they want PDP to win the election but do not want Uduaghan to run and the state party exco dissolved. Can you react?
I don't want to talk about E.K. Clark here. If I want to do, I will call for a conference and talk only on E.K. Clark, then have sufficient time to marshal my position point by point. I have said it before that elders' forum is not a part of the constitution of PDP. It is not recognised by PDP constitution. So it is a voluntary organisation, an NGO. You cannot use that to judge the strength of my party. The party has executives at ward levels. There is no elders' council that can win my ward. We are not talking of organisations talking in the air. The election will not be won with interviews in the media, but the number of teachers you are paying, the pregnant women that have benefited from you, how many WAEC and NECO registration fees you have paid, how many taxis and buses you have provided for people. These are issues that touch human beings. I cannot waste my knowledge and professorial power on illegal bodies.
What if the other parties bring a more popular person to the contest?
I won't worry about that. We are prepared for it. We are battle ready. I am not saying we will win it 100 per cent. It is a competition. But there is no other party in Delta now that has their organisational presence, experience, record of performance in which to campaign more eloquently than I will do. The party will not be imported from outside; it must have members of the State Assembly, chairmen of councils, wards. It is not a chairman of one individual. And there is nobody in Delta State who has capacity to compete for governorship that I don't know. I am qualified to be a candidate too. Nobody of that charisma can come to upset PDP. It is not in the horizon. And if the person appears we will battle him to submission.
How soon will the organisation flag off the campaign?
It is the same machinery. Each council has a coordinator; elders' council that is over 200, also has their structure. They are all united simultaneously. My body will not go to their base. The ball is in the court of INEC now, let it just give a date it will be a peaceful and interesting exercise. We are not engaged in any vituperation, abuses and others we are only naming the things we have done and those we are doing, nobody has such portfolio.
Is the organisation for the Uduaghan 2011 still the one to work for his re-run or is there a crack?
The campaign for 2011 was the former organisation that was handling preparation under Dr. Ifeanyi Okowa. This one that was setup in July is called Uduaghan Campaign Organisation. I am the Director General. We have moved beyond that level. It is a more formidable electoral army now.
There was jubilation in some areas on the verdict. Does that mean people are happy?
I did not see it in camera. Two or three drunken okada men speeding, is that what we call jubilation? They are specifically irrelevant. Of course people from another party can do that. It is natural reaction. It is not jubilation but going to the polls, preaching, putting something in the ballot boxes. You may be drunk over it but it will not give you any victory in Ughelli North and South. Ughelli South, is the base of Professor Utuama, we are solid there. In Ughelli North, we have natural dissidents. But the most articulate of them contesting with Uduaghan has moved to another party. We expect that party will express some joys but we will test the pounded yam at the point of eating.

Original_Search_Title: 'Why Igbo New Yam Festival Can't Be Wished Away'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Why Igbo New Yam Festival Can't Be Wished Away'
Body_Text:
Eze Uche I.C. Dimgba, Eze Udo Ndigbo of Ikeja Local Government of Lagos State, is a staunch member of Ohaneze Ndigbo. He was among the personalities that graced the occasion of Iriji Festival, hosted by Eze Ndigbo in Lagos recently, where he spoke to journalists on Igbo culture, politics, crime in the South East and other issues. Correspondent ANDREW UTULU was at the session. Excerpts:
What would you say about iriji festival that is celebrated annually by Ndigbo?
It is our right. This is what Igbo do to showcase our culture and tradition. Every year during this New Yam festival, we come together to access, to rededicate ourselves for the future, to access what we have done for the year and have merriment and plan for the future. Also traditional dancers are always invited, both big masquerades and small ones. Everyone is invited to come and showcase the Igbo culture and tradition.
But there are those who are of the opinion that this aspect of Igbo culture should be cancelled or abolished. How would you react to that?
What they are saying is just not correct because there is nowhere in the world where tradition does not exist. Any tribe that forgets its tradition has already eliminated itself from the earth. So it is our culture and tradition and nobody has the right to stop it. It is not like a habit but a culture. It has been there right from the beginning, so nobody can change it. All we need to do is to continue to modernise it. If there are bad aspects of the tradition, we remove them and introduce new ones but I say frankly that nobody can wipe it away.
There have been rumours of wrangling in Igbo communities in Lagos State. But today there are a lot of Igbo personalities, royal fathers around. What does the crowd say of the crisis among Igbo in Lagos?
I want to tell you that if there was crisis, that was before. But now there is no crisis. If you had attended the Igbo day celebration which comes every September 29 at the National Stadium, you would agree with me that if anybody says there is wrangling within Igbo community, that person must be talking about the past, not now. The only thing you can tell me now is that in politics, everybody has his own candidate, people might disagree on candidate but if you want to tell me about unity among the Igbos; we are united as entity and we are speaking in one voice.
How would you appraise the Igbo in the governance of Lagos State?
We have not gotten our fair share in the governance of Lagos State because we were told that the Igbo's are 43 percent of the total population in Lagos. And if you look at the 43 percent of the Lagos State population, it means we can even produce the governor. But if you come to look at it we have produced nothing. It is only two commissioners and you can agree with me that a commissioner can be kicked out anytime by the governor.
So what we are now telling the Lagos State government, if they want us to be part and parcel of the state, they must allow us to begin to contest in all elective positions. We have the right to produce people in the Lagos State Assembly from the federal level anywhere we have the numerical strength to produce federal legislators but we have not been allowed to do that. So I think we are being marginalised in Lagos State. We have invested our money here, we pay our tax here, we are law-abiding citizens, and so I do not see why we should not be allowed to produce people in government.
Have you made any effort in making an Igbo man the deputy governor of Lagos State or an Igbo man to be in the State House of Assembly come 2011?
That is exactly what I have just said that we are consulting. We are having meetings, we have told our people who are credible enough and capable to come out to bring out their curriculum vitae, let us know the areas where they could be good. We are not talking about just putting people there, who will go out and embezzle money, we are talking about people who are credible people who are going there to give the dividend of democracy both to Lagos State and the people.
What are the Ezes and the highly influential individuals doing concerning the kidnappings in the South East?
We have been shouting about this. The kidnapping issue is above the state governments. This is because the kinds of weapon that the kidnappers are using, the weapon from the Nigeria police cannot match with them. It is only the army that can subdue them, and you can see now that the army has been drafted in and they are being dealt with.
A friend of mine called me from Aba and said that he was calling from the city and that there was no problem. I think things are coming back to normalcy with the way the army is dealing with the situation.
What would you say is the importance of culture to the transformation and the objective of the Igbo man as a person?
Well, it is through our culture that we know that we are together. If you come here today and you see Igwe sitting down alone, that means he has no root and that the culture of the people is dead. But for you to come and see even young people coming to learn, to see what this culture is all about, how it is done, how the New Yam festival is performed, that means that we are still very strong in one and our culture is still alive.
What advice would you give the electorate regarding 2011, considering the crisis in the South East as regards insecurity of life given that the governors are not doing much to address the situation according to the people living in that part of the country?
This is not the problem of the governors alone. This started from the Federal Government. The marginalisation of the Igbo is what started these problems. A lot of people are coming out from the universities and unfortunately the majority of them are Igbo but they will not get jobs because of marginalisation.
And now I do not think the states have the capability of absorbing all of them. So, you see it started from the head down to the states where these problems are happening.
It is therefore the Federal Government that will structure the country very well, absorb the youths supposed to be gainfully employed. Although, we equally know that some youths are unemployable, no matter what you give to them, they cannot do anything. They are lazy.
But I tell you, there are some who have brains, who can be employed, who can do something if given the opportunity. Banks are not working. In a civilised society, you can develop something and you walk to the bank and the bank will ask you to go and bring your father, your mother and everything you have put together before you can receive something to start with. So you cannot do anything on such situation. So it is from the Federal Government that this will be corrected. When it is well restructured and everybody has the belief in the government, this thing will just die a natural death. People do not believe in Nigerian government, it not working.
Nobody trusts. We have a situation whereby a leader is lying and he wants you to believe him because he has power. This is the problem.

Original_Search_Title: 'The Country is Not Irredeemable'
Headline: Nigeria: 'The Country is Not Irredeemable'
Body_Text:
The multifaceted challenges facing Nigeria are not irredeemable as Nigerians are fundamentally good people who can turn around the bad image given other Nigerians by few unscrupulous elements, former Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) chairman and presidential aspirant of the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN), Mallam Nuhu Ribadu has said.
He was speaking yesterday in Abuja after being conferred with the traditional title of "Aka Ji Egbe of Ndi Igbo" meaning, "the Lion with a gun that defends the nation" by the Ndigbo Community of Pyakassa in the FCT.
He said that if some people can get it wrong and the label is being shared by all, some other people should do the right thing which will be shared by all.
"I believe in one Nigeria, God has chosen to make us one, the potentials of Igbo, Hausa, Ijaw, Yoruba, Fulani and many other tribes being together as a nation is yet to be utilized for its goodness; that is the challenge we face and we are going to play our part to restore our honour and dignity, it is possible and we are going to do it," Ribadu said.
He said he has been offered many titles across the country, but he had politely turned them down, pointing out that this would be the first title he would accept.
Ribadu said he had great respect for Igbo people because they were "unique and exceptional" and dismissed insinuations that he was accepting the title because of his presidential ambition.
"You hardly find people like the Igbos for their friendship and industry. Igbos are the greatest gift God has given to Nigeria; they are known for hardwork and working for the comfort of their people," the former EFCC boss said.
The Ochigbo of Pyakassa and leader of the delegation, Eze John Bull Udemba, said the title conferment on Ribadu was supposed to have been done last October at Pyakassa during the New Yam Festival but Ribadu was not in the country then.
Spokesman of the delegation, Evangelist Mark Madu, in a citation titled "Mallam Nuhu Ribadu, an Epitome of Good Success", said that before Ribadu's modest effort at the EFCC, the nation had witnessed unprecedented level of moral decadence, corruption and insincerity which nearly collapsed the nation's economy.

Original_Search_Title: Kidnapping, Not Igbo Culture - Ohazulike
Headline: Nigeria: Kidnapping, Not Igbo Culture - Ohazulike
Body_Text:
The National President, Association of Ndi-Eze Ndi-Igbo in Diaspora, Eze Nwabueze Ohazulike, has attributed the spate of kidnaping and other violent crimes ravaging the South-East zone to the long neglect of the region, widely acknowledged as producing the bulk of the nation's wealth.
Ohazulike, who noted this at this year's annual New Yam Festival, Iri ji, in Lagos, however, argued that one way government can contend the horrifying situation was to evolve intelligent security system, backed up by database, as obtained in developed nations, to track down criminals indulged in the vices.
He also condemned those behind bomb explosions in the country and appealed to traditional rulers to join forces with government to fight the menace while calling on students to shun cultism. He noted that poor academic performance starts from hideous associations.
The Eze Ndi-Igbo noted that culture and traditional values were pivotal to the socio-economic and technological advancement of the country and warned that it should not be trampled upon as, according to him, people who crush their culture are heading for extinction.
"It is pertinent, at this juncture, to inform you that the title of "Eze Ndi-Igbo" which is registered with the CAC, Abuja, remains intact. Anybody who tries to jettison the appellation without the consent of its members challenges the constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Confessions of a Robbery Suspect - How We Killed Four Policemen in Ogun State
Headline: Nigeria: Confessions of a Robbery Suspect - How We Killed Four Policemen in Ogun State
Body_Text:
Some time in July 2010, the Nigeria Police Force suffered a huge lose, when four of its personnel were murdered in cold blood, by some unknown gunmen. The dare-devils ambushed their red- colored Mazda saloon car, along Ijesha- Ijebu-Ilishan Road in Ago- Iwoye area of Ogun State, killing all four occupants. Their AK-47 rifles were carted away.
News of this incident which made headlines in most national dailies, sent tears running down the eyes of both relatives and also colleagues of these officers.
Police authorities represented by their spokesman in Ogun State, Olumuyiwa Adejobi , described the incident "as one of the hazards associated with the profession," and he also boasted that the police will do all within its powers to fish out the perpetrators.
In spite of Police efforts to track down the fleeing bandits, they continued with their onslaught in the area. Three weeks after, they regrouped, added more men to their number and attacked the Ikpero-Ijebu branch of Intercontinental Bank, and made away with a large sum of money.
They carried out the operation uninterrupted for hours after which they went to a rendezvous at a secluded location in Ikorodu area of Lagos State, where they shared their loots. But luck ran out of two members of this notorious gang, who got intercepted on their way home, by a team of Rapid Response Squad (RRS), on a routine patrol at Ogolonto suburb of Ikorodu.
They were accosted at a very dark portion of the road while trying to fix a bad tire. They were searched and a large sum of money was found in the boot of their car.
This however, aroused suspicion of the police, who promptly placed the duo under arrest. One of them attempted to escape, but was shot dead. His colleague, Kabiru Kpokpola,31, was transferred to the dreaded Federal Special Anti-Robbery Squad (FEDSARS), Adeniji Adele, Lagos for profiling. There it was discovered that he is a member of a notorious armed robbery gang that have been unleashing terror in most parts of the country.
Crime Alert spoke with the surviving suspect, Kabiru Kpokpola and he gave a startling narration of how he joined the gang, the operations they had , why they killed the policemen and how they planned and robbed the bank.
Excerpts;
I am from Ewo, Osun State. My gang does not have a name. The squad leader is Akin. I started robbery in 2008 and I joined the gang in January in 2009. We met first, some where around Ijebu-ode, which was where I joined the gang. Though before I joined Akins gang, I had worked with one other gang at Akure and they specialized in snatching exotic cars. We had two successful operations and we snatched over three cars. But I preferred Akins gang, that was why I went for him. We met at Isheri in Ijebu and they took me on a mission that same night. We snatched a Toyota Camry saloon car valued at N3m.
The first operation with the gang was along Ilesha road in Isheri, we were five in number and we used two vehicles. One of these vehicles was driven by one Akin, all he does is surveillance. He alerts us when there is danger. And we, in our own vehicle, look out for potential targets, which are usually exotic cars with very good market value.
And during this mission, we drive very slowly, and whenever we see an exotic car with tinted glass, we don't attack them because we can't see the occupant. When we saw the Toyota Camry from behind, we slowed down a bit. When it was ahead of us, we went after it and got it intercepted. We had two Ak-47 rifles and we terrified the male driver and the female occupant with our weapons and they scurried for safety, while I drove the car away.
When we got to our rendezvous, I handed the car over to Akin. Few days later, he called me to meet him at Ogere toll-Gate between Lagos and Ibadan and there, he gave me sixty thousand naira as my share and assured he would keep in touch with me.
Akin and other members of his gang carried out other missions. The second job I did with them was along Ijebu-ode, Shagamu Expressway. We snatched one vehicle and employed same method. The third time we went for an operation was in July 2010. We saw a small red- colored Mazda car between Ago-Iwoye and Ilesha. The occupants were policemen and they had four Ak-47 rifle with them.
I told my colleagues not to go after them because that was not the type of car we were looking for and they told me that they wanted their rifle. I groaned at this because I wasn't ready for gun fight with the policemen and I advised that we take another route.
The guy who sat on the front passenger seat with an Ak-47 rifle , we use to call him Taju, told me that all we needed was their weapon and while I was trying to persuade Taju that we don't have to take this risk, and that this policemen were not looking for us, one of the guys at the back passenger seat with an Ak- 47 opened fire on the policemen's vehicle and it lost control and ran into the bush.
We closed the gaps and they shot into the car sporadically. Myself, Taju and the other guy who shot at the car were ordered to alight and take away the guns from the policemen. I moved slowly because I was scared and they barked at me to move quickly and take away the weapons. After we recovered the arms, we took them to Akin and we were unable to work for that day.
Three weeks later, Akin invited me to our rendezvous where he said we had to rob a bank. On that day, I was very broke and I needed money and I had no option other to comply with his orders. Before we went for the operation, he brought in another gang and the name of their leader is Igwe. I don't know if he is an Igbo man but he speaks Yoruba fluently. But before we embarked on the operation, we met at a spot in Ilesha. We both drove into a bush where we drew our plans for the operation.
The plans were that since we have six Ak- 47 rifles in our gang, each of us should be armed while members of the other gang will also use theirs. We also had one car and they had one too. Our plan was that one of our cars will be driven to the nearest junction and there we would stay to ward off any threat while the other gang's vehicle will stop in front of the bank and they will shoot into the air to indicate that the operation has begun. Igwe and one other person from his gang will go into the bank and come out
at exactly 15 minutes with our loot.
When the operation commenced, Igwe went through the security door. He was holding a gun and an axe. He told the other guy with him to hold on to the door when it opens while he breaks through the other security door. I think that was how he got into the bank. It was a successful operation and we drove down to a secluded area in Ikorodu where we shared our loot. I was given N350, 000.
After that, every body left to their various destinations and I went with one other member of my gang. It seems like he resides in Lagos. I went with him because I wanted to get off at Ketu , where I can get a bus heading for Ibadan where I reside. When we got to Ogolonto, Baba stopped and he said he wanted to buy yam for his family and he went to the trunk of his car and brought out a mask we wore during the operation and he inserted his share of his money in it and I had mine close to me in my seat. I was worried and I told Baba that this place was not too safe and we left.
We moved a little distance and the tire got bust and while we were trying to fix it, a team of Policemen attached to Rapid Respond Squad (RRS) came and they inquired what we were doing. I told them that we were only trying to fix our tires, but they insisted on searching our vehicles and they saw the money. I attempted to run, but I fell into a deep ditch and Baba raced a long distance but he was shot at the back.
I was arrested and I gave the policemen a different name. However, when I came here, one of the policemen called me by my name and I denied it, but I did not know they had some people in the cell that knew me very well. They are receivers of stolen vehicles based in Abuja. Baba Later died from the bullet injury and I'm left alone.

Original_Search_Title: Governors' Forum - Caught in the Web of 2011 Politicking
Headline: Nigeria: Governors' Forum - Caught in the Web of 2011 Politicking
Body_Text:
Following the failed attempt by a select group of governors to unseat Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara State as the chairman of the all powerful Nigeria Governors' Forum, NGF, relative peace seems to have returned to the Forum. But is the crisis over? Attributed to 2011 politicking, the little scratch observed early in the day may have developed into a painful boil that is being forcefully covered. Stanley Nkwocha traces the genesis.
Perhaps a third eye was needed to clearly read the writing on the wall. The Governors' Forum may have been an organ that was set up to promote the unity amongst the governors who hitherto lined up according to regional affiliations, but when ahead of the 2007 general elections, then President Olusegun Obasanjo moved to enthrone two serving governors ( Yar'Adua and Jonathan) as the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) candidates for the election, unknowingly he redefined the status of the governors, moreso, when former Governor of Ekiti State, Ayo Fayoshe was at a point asked to head a committee amongst the governors to select the Party's ticket hopefuls.
The Forum was that powerful. To wish the Forum away in any election is to say the least to commit political suicide. Hence, not even the obvious ruthlessness of then President Obasanjo could bend the governors for theirs had become a formidable weapon that could destabilize the best of political strategists and make others go on their knees.
In 2007, the heat presently witnessed in the polity was completely absent in the polity as quietly it had been resolved that power be returned to the North. All eyes were on the North for Obasanjo's successor, while the South-east and South south then battled to get the vice presidential slot. The South-south, represented by Goodluck Jonathan picked it after what was clearly a viviparous display of the stake of the governors. No thanks to the delegates they control.
To stand the chance of winning the primaries of any election as the law presently is, is a function of the delegates. But if the political equation is to be further analysed, then it must be pointed out that while the delegates are determining factors in any primaries, the governors of the various states directly control them and so could be said to be the constant in the equation, while aspirants who hope on them inevitably become the variables in this political equation. The need, therefore, to exert some measure of control amongst the governors is clearly understood.
As 2011 inches closer, the controversy over zoning has all but fuelled the politicking that is going on across the parties, and not just the PDP. The NGF inclusive. Need it be reemphasized that the battle between the North and the South-south over who produced the next President, following the declaration of President Jonathan to continue in office in 2011 in the demise of late President Shehu Yar'Adua, has infiltrated the governors' camp. Knowing full well that they, in this case have the knife and the yam, it is only natural that interest groups spring up, as unlike 2007, among the PDP governors, the center does not hold. The battle is clearly along the interest lines of aspirants who are clamoring for the support of the various governors.
Nobody expected it, but the sudden declaration of the interest of Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara State to vie for the presidency come 2011 has all but altered the political equation amongst the governors. While some find the agitation amusing, others find it as awkward taking into cognizance his headship of the body. But Saraki's continued leadership of the body was throwing up controversy and except he resigned, it would lead to crisis. Indeed, it was just a matter of time for the bubble to burst and it did burst in style.
Penultimate Friday, eleven governors met at the Ogun State Governors' lodge in Asokoro, and in a most coup -like plot, 'overthrew' Saraki as the Governors' Forum chairman. In his place, Otunba Gbenga Daniel was nominated as the new chairman of the Forum.
Speaking on behalf of the Forum, shortly after the meeting, Benue state governor, Mr Gabriel Suswam said Otuba Daniel was chosen due to his exemplary leadership qualities, his track record and experience as a second term governor.
Suswam added that the Ogun governor was unanimously elected by consensus of all the members of the Forum and thus has been mandated to co-ordinate the Forum's activities, and ensure that it responds to national issues appropriately.
Adding his voice, Akwa Ibom governor Godswill Akpabio said, Daniel's election was based purely on his performance as a capable administrator with outstanding qualities.
No sooner had the seeming coup succeeded when it collapsed like a pack of cards - talk about a plot that was half hatched as the barrage of criticism that trailed Daniel's emergence knew no bounds.
The Northern Governors' Forum quickly dissociated itself frm the election of Otunba Gbenga Daniel as Chairman of the Nigerian Governors' Forum (NGF) . Dr. Muazu Babangida Aliyu, chairman of the Forum, said in Minna that the governors would never accept the process that threw in Daniel. Aliyu argued that the NGF "is a non partisan organisation," adding that its membership cut across party lines. He alleged that some people wanted to hijack the Forum for selfish interest ahead of 2011.
Daniel's kinsman and Osun State Governor, Prince Olagunsoye Oyinlola also claimed ignorance of Daniel's election, saying, he travelled out of the country when the agreement was reached. His Chief Press Secretary, Lasisi Olagunju, last Monday said he had not taken a stand on the issue as he had just returned frm the biennial general meeting of the International Golf Federation which held in Buenos Aires, Argentina.
Earlier, Governors Adams Oshiomhole of Edo State and his Kano State counterpart, Ibrahim Shekarau had expressed their displeasure with Daniel's enthronement. A statement signed by Sule Ya'u Sule, spokesman of the Kano State Governor, Malam Ibrahim Shekarau, had dissociated the governor frm the purported appointment of Governor Gbenga Daniel of Ogun State as the new chairman of the NGF.
"To the best of His Excellency's knowledge, there was never a meeting where a decision to appoint a new chairman of the forum was reached. A situation where some few PDP governors could sit and take a decision on behalf of 36 governors is not acceptable," Shekarau stated.
On his part, Comrade Oshiomhole described it as "embarrassing and unfair". According to the governor, "there was this news item credited to Governor Gabriel Suswam of Benue state in which he stated that, Governor Gbenga Daniel was elected as the chairman of the Governors' Forum. I was taken aback when I heard the story".
The Governor disclosed that the out-going chairman, Dr. Bukola Saraki had called a meeting of the Governors' Forum for Wednesday November 3, saying part of the agenda for the meeting was to elect a new chairman for the forum.
Comrade Oshiomhole who expressed worry about the behaviour of some governors over the issue said: "If a forum of thirty six governors cannot freely choose a chairman of the Governors' Forum, then we cannot be talking about a free and fair election in 2011".
So did the backlash continue including that which came frm Rights activist Shehu Sani, when he said: "Gbenga Daniel as the chairman of the Forum is a desecration of whatever noble objective the forum stands for. OGD's record of arbitrariness in his state disqualifies him frm leading other governors with credible antecedents. If OGD is the choice of Jonathan, then it is a confirmation of Jonathan's sinister and mischievous intention towards 2011 elections,".
Meanwhile, denying insinuation that Daniel's election was part of a ploy to put President Goodluck Jonathan's henchmen in the saddle ahead of 2011, Benue governor, Gabriel Suswan, said it was borne out of the need to make the Forum stronger.
"We need our Governors Forum because we're known to be responding to issues. There were bomb blasts in Abuja without any response frm the Forum. And there are a lot of other issues that have happened in this country without responses frm our Forum due to our chairman's engagement in his presidential election campaign. So, what we want is responsive leadership," Suwsan had explained.
The explanations may continue, a new chairman might not have emerged after the late night Thursday meeting, but if the truth must be told a grave political discord has been sown in the NGF. The actors may deny this and use Shakespearian quotes, garnished with facial smiles to underplay the current tension building up. But the truth is that the NGF may never be the same again. Not until the 2011 elections come and go, suspicion for now reigns supreme amongst the governors. The lone voice spoken with may become elusive as the election creeps in.

Original_Search_Title: Presidential Advert Campaigns - What Really is the Message?
Headline: Nigeria: Presidential Advert Campaigns - What Really is the Message?
Body_Text:
My nine year old son is surprisingly enamoured with the charm and charisma of the late Nobel Peace Laureate, Rev. Martin Luther King that he has almost mastered his famous 'I have a dream speech.' Surprisingly, he was also awake on the 20th of January 2008 to listen to the electoral victory speech of President Barrack Obama. He keeps asking me why our own politicians always seem not too sure of how to read even written speeches and how he likes 'those people who can speak well without reading from a paper.'
While good speeches or excellent advert campaigns do not make for good leadership, it goes a long way in unearthing the depth of intuitiveness and sense of purpose of both candidates and their campaign teams. In the past 11 years of our democracy, we have witnessed a largely uninspiring method of campaigns.
As we all expect the INEC chairman, Prof. Attahiru Jega to perform the miracle of a free and fair election in less than six months, mum has been the word for our politicians. No major attractions, no sparks, no compelling discussion of ideas and no display of the real understanding of our precarious situation.
So far, as regards the most important elective position in the land - the Presidency, it has been only the candidates of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), that have been appearing in the media and to think of it that the party is yet to conduct its primaries, is quite telling. That we have not heard any convincing policy issues or talking points from the candidates that intend to slug it out at the primaries of the different political parties should worry us all.
For now, what we read and see from the electronic and print media appear like a badly planned personality contest presented in a lyrical contest. There has been no specifics, no good grasp of issues and policy directions coming from the candidates.
In the world's most populous black nation of about 150 million people, it is a thing of worry that the Presidential advert campaigns come out so hollow and a grand departure from core issues to self-deifying copies. It is either the candidates have nothing to say or their handlers are not in tune with how political messages are packaged to maximise its efficacy or both.
In any case, the adverts we have in the media so far are a reflection of what our political values have been since independence. Less emphasis has been put on real issues and campaign promises. What we have been saddled with has been egoistic projection of ethnic, religious, personality and elitist bent in the most annoying fashion.
When all the so-called human rights and democracy advocates make so many fusses about free and fair elections in 2011, it becomes very annoying when all they talk about is the INEC chairman as if he has any magic wand. What would he do in an environment where politicians do not believe that they have to convince potential voters through words and actions?
What would come of an electoral system where grandstanding and not the logic of modern day political and economic policy debates is the pre-occupation of candidates? To worsen matters, the so-called opposition parties seem too deep in slumber to radically depart from the precedents the PDP has set. They all have been planning for too long and like one great philosopher posited, the forest would be too silent if all birds were to wait to be the best singers before they sing.
Mediocrity in the political landscape can only be eliminated when like potential grooms, politicians, effectively woo voters like potential brides. The whole essence of electoral campaigns is for candidates to summarise their ideas so convincingly and honestly over time in such a way that they create indelible, yet memorable impression in the potential voters.
Because our politicians take voters for granted, we would continue to have violence and rigging during elections. Any well informed politician ought to know that there are different segments of the voting public. Each segment has its own peculiar problems which it expects every candidate to present potential solutions to. It is only on the basis of the futuristic projections of solutions that voters make their choices.
If we have not had this system of campaign strategy, it does not take rocket science to see why politicians use other none edifying methods to grab power. As a woman, a politician that promises good healthcare programmes with details and some empathy for maternal and child mortality would be of interest to me.
Politicians in other climes fail to either be nominated by their party or are rejected at the polls by voters based on what is evaluated as their cerebral and intellectual depth and so even before elections, media analysis of their intellectual capacity are laid bare. A Sarah Palin who knew next to nothing about the African continent knew what that did to her electoral value. In modern political and economic climate, there are no half measures. Analogue minds cannot fit into the digital super highway.
Sweeping folktale-like campaign lyrics and annoyingly lengthy prose narratives do not exhibit any endearing political relationship with voters. Hate them or like them, you cannot ignore the late Zik of Africa's campaign message. The late Aminu Kano wrote his name in gold through his message to the talakawas. Neither can one ignore the late Awolowo's or M.K.O Abiola's message of 'HOPE'.
In politics, the voters are captured through the creation of hope in the things to come. That is why an Obama rode into the White House with his message of 'Change' while a Senator John McCain's preachments about his patriotic exploits could not sail despite the nobility it symbolises.
Any politician worth the votes that really give political mandates must be well versed enough to convincingly analyse the problems and policy inconsistencies that have made Nigeria one of the most poorly governed nations on earth.
The message coming from any politician normally shows the compassion, the vision, the humanity, the selflessness and the commitment that great leaders are imbued with. When our sensibilities are assaulted with these vacuous media ranting by those who want to be in positions to lead us and determine our children's future, we should all be genuinely alarmed.
The tendency for some of these candidates might be to descend on their media hands for the hollow messages. But that would be like the proverbial man who trips as he stumbled on a stone as a result of negligence and who in turn begins to curse the ground for his misfortune.
Like the legendary M.K.O Abiola used to say, no one can shave your hair in your absence. We all know that all politicians have input in how they are packaged. So, it's pretty much like computer print out. What you feed into the hard disk is normally what is printed out.
The basic problem is that we have not set standards for those who operate in the political space. So, some of them present themselves as if they are the best thing that happened to us after pounded yam. They take so much for granted that to them, their assumed personalities ought to be a one-way ticket to whatever political office they aspire for.
Convincing, sincere and meaningful campaign messages backed with excellent personal and professional pedigree endears candidates to voters. These are the ones who win elections without stories of violence because they are always well loved and the voters speak at the polls. With what we have now, Prof. Jega must need the services of some magicians.

Original_Search_Title: Governors' Palace Coup - Beginning of the End?
Headline: Nigeria: Governors' Palace Coup - Beginning of the End?
Body_Text:
I watched, fascinated, as a farcical and surreal drama played out itself on the television screen. Some Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) governors had gathered to announce the replacement of their erstwhile chairman, Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara State, with Gov. Gbenga Daniel of Ogun State. The decision was reportedly made by some governors who attended the fund-raising event for President Jonathan Goodluck in Abuja during the weekend. The governors involved met at the Ogun State Governor's Lodge in Abuja in the early hours of the morning.
There's no gainsaying the fact that under Saraki, the Nigerian Governors Forum (NGF) rapidly grew in national influence and stature. There isn't a decision on any important policy matter that doesn't bear the imprimatur of the NGF. It isn't just a mere cliché when political pundits aver that whenever the NGF sneezed, the rest of the nation was bound to catch cold! It's this overwhelming influence that has caused many Nigerians to start calling for its dissolution. If recent happenings in that august body are anything to go by, such Nigerians may soon have their wish fulfilled.
Clearly, Saraki's stock has considerably appreciated in value given the deft manner he has been managing the affairs of the Governors Forum. He was perceived by many astute observers as a smooth operator, clinical finisher and a cool professional during times of national crises. His demeanor and performance are attributes any leader an underdeveloped and crisis-prone nation like Nigeria needs to possess to be effective. His decision to join the race for the PDP's presidential ticket has clearly provided a golden opportunity for many of his colleagues who, either out of envy or ambition or a combination of both, chose to stick treacherous knives into his body.
To the uninitiated, the pronouncements of Benue State Gov. Gabriel Suswam, spokesman of the 'coupists,' seemed reasonable enough. What could be more straightforward and logical than replacing a chairman that has decided to seek greener pastures elsewhere? But it's clearly obvious that there's much more to the matter than meets the eye. Shorn of grandstanding by the 'coupists,' one can easily see that it is just more of the same dirty politics at play here. The argument that Saraki's involvement in the race for the presidency has placed the NGF in a compromising position doesn't hold water at all. To be taken seriously, the 'coupists' should've equally argued that Jonathan should resign from office so that governance doesn't go to the dogs while he's out campaigning. The not-so-subtle implication of the Suswam-led mutiny is that the purportedly sacked chairman of the NGF is traveling a lonely road to own political funeral. The Bukola Saraki Campaign Organization (BSCO) has disclosed that its principal has already served notice of his resignation from office in order to devote more time to his campaign hence there was no justification for the insurrection by a group of PDP governors. But you know how this works. There's already blood in the water, or so some imagine, and the sharks are beginning to circle the intended victim with increasing frenzy. Everything about the drama points to a conspiracy. What those involved fail to understand is that in their bid to rubbish a 'perceived enemy of a friend,' they are also undermining the NGF. For starters, does the NGF consist only of PDP governors? Why did the 'coupists' dispense with the pretence of even seeking the input of governors from the stables of other political parties?
The 'coupists' have treated their colleagues with undeserving contempt and ought to publicly apologize to them for the embarrassment of hearing the decision on the airwaves like the rest of Nigerians. Our elders say that dirty hands cannot mold clean pounded yam. This was the point Edo State Gov. Adams Oshiomhole was making when he compared the action of the 'coupists' to "electing a chairman through the back door." Besides, if chieftains of the dominant political party are allowed to get away with their cavalier handling of the 'Saraki affair,' can we still expect the 2011 elections to be credible, free and fair?
I completely agree with an anonymous South-South governor who views the entire drama as "part of a larger ploy to elect a partisan chairman that would be sympathetic to Jonathan's ambition to contest the 2011 presidential polls." It isn't just a mere coincidence that all the governors involved are die-hard opponents of zoning, with many among them even serving as zonal coordinators of the Jonathan/Sambo Presidential Campaign Organization. The serial bashing Jonathan has suffered in the hands of the governors - such as e-registration, sequence of primaries and dissolution of some state party executive committees - has gone a long way in making him appear un-presidential and vulnerable, hence the quest for a sympathizer to occupy the all-powerful NGF chairmanship position. Some folks very high up had obviously concluded that as long as Saraki remained the NGF chairman Jonathan would've to sleep with both eyes open wide!
In a strange twist of politics, the greatest opponents of zoning - who want to be seen as the most loyal supporters of Jonathan today - are those who stoutly defended zoning as an equitable formula for power rotation in the not-too-distant past! A couple of examples would suffice. At a reception organized for him in mid-June this year by the All Christian Church in Ugba, Logo Local Government Council Area, Suswam harped on the virtues of zoning, advising "aspirants on the platform of PDP, whom zoning will not favor, not to defect to other parties" (The Nation, 14/6/2010; page 61). The same Suswam is singing a radically different tune a few months later! Another example is Gbenga Daniel. The man never missed an opportunity to emphasize that his legacy would be to use the zoning formula to make Ogun West senatorial zone produce the governor of Ogun State in 2011 in the interest of equity and fair-play. Today, the same Daniel is not only singing a new tune but has since been appointed JSPCO South-West coordinator, and is now the chairmanship choice of anti-zoning 'coupists'!
I must give it to President Jonathan on this instance. He has displayed a commendable mastery of the game of power politics by putting two universal laws into operation: discovering each major player's weakness and applying the appropriate thumbscrew to get the desired effect, as well as making erstwhile opponents to become more Catholic than the Pope in terms of their loyalty and support. Still, there are many who believe that actions like those of the 'coupists' are symptomatic of increasing desperation by the Jonathan camp.
All hope isn't yet lost. The governors have scheduled an all-important meeting for today to discuss the palace coup and other related matters - including the election of a new chairman. Would the NGF implode into factions because of the overarching designs of power-hungry politicians or would its members somehow find the will to put its interests above the interests of any single individual, no matter how highly placed? Shall the NGF emerge from this self-inflicted crisis with renewed vigor and amity or shall we see an organization, living on its past glory, wobbling and fumbling its way into irrelevance and oblivion? Before sunset today, the answers will be very clear.

Original_Search_Title: I'm an Expert Cook - Princess Latifah
Headline: Nigeria: I'm an Expert Cook - Princess Latifah
Body_Text:
Princess Latifah Mahmodu Lamura is a designer and one of the nation's fashion icons reputed for promoting Nigeria and indeed, Africa's culture in the Diaspora. Married to a French, the Edo State born celebrity insists that her marriage hasn't in any way diminished her love for Nigerian foods. In today's edition of Celebrity Tummy Talk, the very charming Princess talks about the secret of her good look and healthy living.
Enjoy!
Were you really born a princess or is it just a name?
I was born a real princess to a royal family in Edo State.
So, what is the popular food of the Edo people?
In Edo State, we enjoy pounded yam and vegetable. That's the special delicacy there.
And how often do you eat pounded yam?
I eat pounded yam once in every three months. It was more regularly consumed when I was young. But as one got older, it became less attractive. I had to cut down on my in take of heavy, starchy meals.
As much as possible, you want to run away from such meals food at a certain age because you want to stay fit. These days I eat only foods that are easily digestible. I stick to continental food which is very light. I enjoy as often as possible salad and potato which digests easily.
Although, I go for some African cuisines pounded yam meal is listed on my menu because of the heaviness. I like moi-moi because it is very light and I could do with that throughout the day.
I also like rice and fried yam but I don't make it a regular meal of it. Perhaps one of my most favourite meals is Amala because of its digestible nature.
And what goes with the Amala?
I love to eat it with vegetable soup (Efo Riro) mixed with all kinds of condiments including fish, shrimps, dry fish and so on.
Can you cook?
I'm a genius when it comes to cooking. I'd taken part in different food competitions during my secondary school days and I excelled on top of the pack. I have no stress when it comes to cooking. And that explains why, I find it difficult to eat badly prepared meals. I know a well-prepared meal when I taste one. And if it's tasteless, I won't take it.
My role model when it comes to cooking is Princess Folawiyo Abba. She is a real mother and a fantastic cook. Anytime, I have the opportunity to taste her food, it tells you that whoever prepared the meal is a good cook. And anyone who has attended her party would testify to that.
Was there a time you played any pranks to get your favourite kind of food?
I think I would give credit to my mum when it comes to that. It was difficult to play pranks because food was always on the table. We were always looking forward to going home after school hours because of her special delicacies. So, my mum is also a great cook and I took after her.
I remember back then how she used to prepare my favourite food, Jollof rice with snail. She had a time-table for us. As a matter of fact, we didn't have time to play any pranks because we had what we wanted on the table.
Is there a special way to prepare your favourite meal of snails?
People from my state prepare it very well. I have attended parties where snails were served but I've not tasted anyone that tastes exactly like ours. I've seen a situation where I've had to drop food because of the way, the snails were prepared.
How do you prepare your own snail?
First you clean it with salt and lime. And you put them in your pot without too much water. Then, you add garlic. But you must ensure that the water in the pot dries with the snail before you could have a good taste. The next thing to do is fry the snails and boil them again with fresh pepper. Again you must let the water dry with the snails. Serve with your favourite drink.
Do you exercise?
Although I'm a busy person, I still make out the time to exercise. I also club and party. And as much as possible, I dance to burn out fat and keep fit. When I was in school, I did a lot of sporting exercise. I was the champion in high jump and that's why I'm still looking healthy today.
Do you do fruits?
I love fruits and I eat plenty of apples, oranges, grape and banana. But I try not to do over do things. I take fruits on a daily basis.

Original_Search_Title: One Dies in Yangoji Auto Crash
Headline: Nigeria: One Dies in Yangoji Auto Crash
Body_Text:
One person died while eleven others sustained serious injuries on Wednesday morning in a fatal motor accident that occurred at Yangoji village, along Kwali-Lokoja highway.
A witness said the accident happened at about 7:20 am when a trailer with registration number XA 210 GWA overtook another vehicle from behind and collided with a Nissan pickup van with registration number XC 7116 LFA, loaded with yams, killing the driver instantly.
"The eleven injured people sitting on top of the yams on the pickup van who sustained various degrees of injuries were rushed to the hospital by the men of the Federal Road Safety Corp (FRSC)," the witness said.
Our reporter who was at the scene of the accident few minutes after the crash, reports that the road was blocked as a result of the accident.
Yangoji unit commander of the Federal Road Safety Commission (FRSC,) Abdullahi Umar, blamed the accident on the trailer's driver for overtaking wrongly, adding that the corpse of the dead driver was collected at the scene by relatives of the deceased for burial.
He said the 11 injured people were taken to Kwali General Hospital for treatment.
In a related development, 15 people were seriously injured yesterday morning in another accident that occurred at Abaji, along the same road.
The Abaji unit commander of the FRSC, Mr. Joseph Ezeh, who confirmed the accident, said the accident occurred around 7:00 am, when an 18-seater commuter hiace bus with registration number XA 374 BKW overtook dangerously and rammed into a Volkswagen Golf with registration number BJ 395 KUJ, leaving 15 passengers seriously wounded.
He disclosed that 5 people escaped unhurt while the 15 injured victims were taken to Abaji General Hospital.

Original_Search_Title: 'Kidnapping Has Never Been a Culture of Igbos'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Kidnapping Has Never Been a Culture of Igbos'
Body_Text:
The national president of the Association of Ndi Eze Ndi-Igbo in Diaspora, Eze Nwabueze Ohazulike, has attributed the spate of kidnapping and other violence crimes ravaging the South- East zone of the country to the long neglect of the region, widely acknowledged as producing the bulk of the nation's wealth.
Ohazulike, who disclosed this at this year's annual new yam festival, Iri ji, in Lagos, however, said one way government can contend the horrifying situation was to evolve intelligent security system, backed up by database, as obtained in developed nations, to track down criminals indulged in the vices.
He also frowned at those behind bomb explosions in the country and appealed to traditional rulers to join forces with government to fight the menace while calling on students to shun cultism as noting that poor academic performance starts from hideous associations. "It is a bereft of Godliness" he said.
The Eze Ndi Igbo pointed out that culture and traditional values are pivotal to socio-economic and technological advancement of the country and warned that it should not be trampled upon as according to him people who crush their culture are heading for extinction.

Original_Search_Title: Coasting Home Despite the Opposition
Headline: Nigeria: Coasting Home Despite the Opposition
Body_Text:
Over the next several weeks, candidates for elective offices and their supporters will beat the roads and rivers to sell their agenda, and with them lurking in the shadows will be their detractors - critics and their hired hands. Every democratic society thrives on a viable opposition, one that understands the collective will of a people and strives to ensure that the government in power does not deviate from that goal. Sadly, opposition in Nigeria has largely become a bread-and-butter affair. Politicians switch alliances so flittingly that the substance is relegated to the dustbin.
Between parties and within parties, the bird-hopping gives the image of desperation for power and patronage, and in the process every imaginable kind of arsenal is deployed to justify their acts. It is the challenge the Nigerian politician faces, in a society where half-truths, lies and rumours have lives of their own in the run-up to elections.
The 2011 election promises to be a watershed given the promises by INEC to umpire a free and fair poll. It is the promise that is driving a contest of several hopefuls in several states and at the centre. All is fair in politics, it is said, but as the campaigns come along, not a few politicians are saying the game is not being played by the rules. At the federal level, a flurry of accusations have been thrown back and forth between the campaign organizations of President Goodluck Jonathan and former president Ibrahim Babangida over the state of security in the country, with the arrest and questioning of the Babangida campaign director general, Dr. Raymond Dokpesi, over the October 1Independence Day bomb blast in Abuja.
As it is in the centre so it is in the states. One state that promises to be a center of interest for political watchers is Imo where a bitter election battle fought in 2007 only ended a few months ago when the Supreme Court dismissed the claims against the victory of Governor Ikedi Ohakim. Already, as the elections approach, the gladiators are getting ready for battle. Among those expected to run for the office of governor are the incumbent and some of the candidates from the last election, notably Senator Ifeanyi Ararume and Chief Martins Agbaso. Owelle Rochas Okorocha, a well-known name who ran for president in 2007, also seems to be stepping down his ambition, giving signs of interest in the governorship race on the platform of the ruling People's Democratic Party, PDP. The gladiators have since taken their campaigns to the street and media, with allegations and counter-allegations and claims of all sorts.
Governor Ohakim will be the man to beat and as incumbent his scorecard is the subject. Many believe that the criticisms against him are indicative of the desperation of the opposition. From his religious life to his work in office, opposition politicians have sought to paint the governor in very uncomplimentary colours. In the latest such attempt, Governor Ohakim, who is a prominent member of the Anglican Communion of the Christian faith, was pictured as disrespectful to priests and the Church. In an overwhelmingly Christian state such as Imo, the implications of this, as elections draw near, is only too clear. Here is the background to this latest onslaught against the governor: A priest of the Catholic Church was manhandled by security men attached to the Government House, Owerri, a few months back. The security men claimed that the priest was obstructing the convoy of the Governor and, as has become the practice with ill-trained security officials, they pounced on the Reverend Father. The outrageous action of the security officials was condemned by the governor who also led his entire cabinet to the Catholic Archbishop of Owerri to tender an unreserved apology to the on bishop, the priest in question and the Church. Ohakim followed up with a public apology in a broadcast to the people of the state. But that seemed not to be enough. Understandably, opposition politicians would just not let it go away. They had been armed with the arsenal to attack the governor by men who were supposed to be working for the governor. The issue became a major talking point in the state and gained prominence in many news and feature publications nationwide. But aides of the governor have put up a spirited fight explaining that although the governor was unaware of the overzealous action of the security men who were not employed by him but were detailed to him from the Police Central Command, he had sufficiently taken responsibility by immediately condemning their action and tendering a public apology to the priest and the church. They argue that the opposition was diverting public attention from the real issues of governance and development that should be on the front burner of debate because it was clear that the governor was on course in these areas. Interestingly, religious leaders in Imo state, including the Catholic Church, have commended the governor's reaction to the issue of the priest and his determination to sanitize the conduct of security men operating in the state. Both the Christian Association of Nigeria and the Pentecostal Fellowship in Imo published statements asking that the issue be rested. Yet, the assault on Father Okorie makes the rounds in some circles. The Catholic Archbishop of Owerri Diocese, The Right Reverend Anthony Obinna does not want the church associated with the incident. He considers it closed. The state branch of CAN in a statement signed by its chairman, Cosmos Ikechukwu, said, "The Imo state branch of CAN is happy to note that the Governor took responsibility for the misbehavior of his security aides and has accordingly apologized to the catholic community and the people of Imo state. CAN also admitted with joy the Christian spirit of fortitude exhibited by Archbishop Obinna. The governor's reaction to the behavior of the security personnel may well have taken the sail off those who intended to play politics with the issue as Bishops of both the orthodox and Pentecostal wings have commended his reaction as a symbol of humility and decency. Issues involving the church and respect for pastors are very touchy in the South East, where Christianity is the dominant faith. Several years ago the government of Governor Chimaroke Nnamani ran into trouble with a Catholic priest Revd Father Mbaka over an incident that dogged Nnamani to the end of his government, but Ohakim was more proactive, accepting responsibility and assuaging anger, making it pretty difficult for the opposition to capitalize on that incident.
The Imo State governor has stated over and again that he would rather be debated on the issues affecting the people of the state than be the subject of innuendoes. He has them aplenty. One of these is the claim that the state government is employing scarce state funds to dredge the Nworie River, a river that is strategic to flood control in Owerri, with the drainages in the city emptying into the belly of the river. At least one point the government and the opposition agree on is the importance of the river to the capital city. Silt formation over the years has flattened the river bed and disturbed the free flow of water. This became very evident following the massive environmental work by the Ohakim government in Owerri beginning in 2007. The Clean and Green project which the administration initiated unblocked the drainages clogged by filth and mounds of waste that made Owerri up to that time one of Nigeria's dirtiest state capitals. Drainages were built and the government embarked on a project to dredge the Nworie River to accommodate the free flow of water and to open water transport with its attendant environmental beauty. But political opponents of the administration have condemned the Nworie project as a White Elephant and a scheme to siphon money from the state purse. Many releases have been made by these opponents claiming that over N7billion had been expended by the state government on the dredging of Nworie and that most of that sum was looted. For a long time, the Nworie issue and the allegations of N7billion naira looted funds raged. But it has since been found that the Nworie project is actually a federal government project attracted to the state by the Ohakim administration.
The Nworie dredging is a N7.8billion NDDC project in Imo State and so the case of the state appropriating or misappropriating billions of Naira through the project does not arise since it does not handle the money for the project. "Ohakim's sin in this area would appear to be his proactive stand on the issues affecting Imo State and his networking to get the federal government involved in solving them, and it is unfortunate that these critics want Ndi Imo to take them seriously when they would rather stand idle and watch its people suffer", says Imo State Commissioner for Information and Strategy, Dr. Kelechukwu Okpalaeke, rather sarcastically.
Imo seems to be reaping from its Clean and Green project which was designed to position the state as an attractive tourist destination of choice and a living community. Today, it is regarded as one of Nigeria's cleanest states. The Federal Government in a 2008 assessment declared Owerri as the cleanest state capital in Nigeria. The federal government also in 2009 declared the state the safest and most proactive in security matters after a visit by the Inspector General of Police Mike Okiro. That result was the product of a determined effort to make the state's citizens and visitors safe, even during the usually turbulent festival periods of New Yam and Christmas. And together, the efforts are designed to build a new Imo State thriving on the new global economy, say government sources.
Arguing the case, a businessman in Owerri, Chukwudube Obiakor, says "most of those opposing Ohakim know the truth that you cannot build an economically strong state without the kind of investments being made, but it is in the nature of politicians to paint everything as negative to advance their political careers".
Today's global economy is not driven by commodities alone, new sectors like tourism and hospitality have become big revenue earners. This is why progressive economies seek to build cities that are attractive and secure, and that can add value to the lives of tourists. Soon after the Ohakim government took over, it developed a masterplan for Imo. One of the key strands of this plan is investments in infrastructure and productive industries.
Yet, raising the money for capital investments has been tough, say government sources, mainly because of the huge burden of recurrent expenditure on the government's purse, which has also gone down. The state government wants to raise a N40 billion Bond to finance some of its capital projects, including a 'world-class' leisure facility planned for the scenic and serene Oguta Lake, one of
Nigeria's major tourist attractions long left to lie fallow. Government planners speak glowingly of the Oguta project, as keying nicely with the security, environment and infrastructure investments of the government, which they say will ensure a steady flow of patronage. Opposition politicians are scared the money if sourced will be misapplied. They hinge their arguments on the situation in some states. Interestingly, the same situation is playing out in Ogun State where the state government has sought without luck to raise a N100 billion bond, with opposition from the state legislators. Imo State's difference, government officials like to point out, is the argument that the government has evidence of good application of its earnings, and the money will go into economic projects. One point not being hammered is that unlike the situation in Ogun where Governor Gbenga Daniel's second term is running out, Ohakim has a possible second term to run during which time the bond will be liquidated.
The governor's aides insist that their principal is keen to lay a structure for the smooth running of the state several years hence. One of the sad facts of Nigeria's last several years is the total absence of planning. Governments have never deemed it fit to plan for replacements of infrastructure and human capacity. Imo State wants to buck this trend. In 2009 the state government launched a massive recruitment to rejuvenate the civil service. This is because much of the work force in the state civil service today is made up of people above 50 years, it is an aging civil service and as they prepare to retire the government sees the need to bring in younger and educated people to the bureaucracy. Interestingly, a program designed to show the transparency of the process to recruit 10,000 civil servants is being criticized as exploitative. One of the issues raised is that the job was given to a management consulting firm when it could have been handled cheaply in-house, the other is that the applicants were made to buy scratch cards at N2,000 each and apply online. The state government argues that though it is true that recruitment could be made in-house, given the provincial nature of most Nigerians, even the critics will find greater ammunition for criticism if the exercise is done by government officials. It also argues that in several states and federal agencies, applicants pay for cards to apply, so how can that of Imo, which interestingly is being handled by a firm of credible auditors, be different and attract so much angst? By handing the exercise over to the consultants, the state government is sure to get the best hands available. From reports the consultants have done the first part of their job and about 5,000 candidates scored 50percent and above in the recruitment tests. To the credit of the state government, not a single report has been made of any state official being bribed to employ anyone in the exercise, while it is commonplace to hear of bribes running into several thousands of naira by applicants in other states and federal agencies to secure jobs. The current recruitment exercise being made by the NDLEA has already raised hairs over the allocation of jobs to nominees of federal lawmakers. Of note is the fact that while his critics make mockery of the jobs, the labour unions think differently. The state chapter of the Trade Union Congress of Nigeria in a letter signed by the chairman, Comrade O. J. Onyekawa, commended the state government for the mass employment initiative. "The TUC will ever remain grateful to your administration for providing dividends of democracy to the people," the Congress wrote to the governor.
Ohakim's good fortune is the fact that the message to discredit his works seem to be falling on deaf ears, as opinion leaders in the state such as Chief Emmanuel Iwuanyanwu, Senator Arthur Nzeribe and Chief Hope Uzodinma, are known supporters. Elder citizens of the state and of Igbo extraction speak well of his achievements. Chief Emeka Anyaoku, the retired Secretary General of the Commonwealth. He said during a visit to Owerri: "I see Governor Ikedi Ohakim as a Nigerian leader of Igbo extraction and I hope and wish that his political progress will be based on that . . . His Clean and Green Programme is something close to my heart. What he has pioneered here in Owerri is a project I will be telling my colleagues in Switzerland, Headquarters of the WWF, that something very good is happening in this part of the world." Anyaoku is president of the World Wildlife Federation, WWF.
In spite of the goodwill he enjoys, Ohakim appears to have rolled up his sleeves and appears set for the battle ahead. He says that, "Whereas politics can be a popularity contest, leadership is about taking hard but necessary decisions for the sake of the people. We have gone beyond politics, especially retrogressive politics. We are now at the level of providing leadership to our people. We are not perfect, nor have we achieved all we want to do for Imo State. We may have made some mistakes, but we are human. While we will always welcome constructive criticisms, visionary policies and programmes will always prevail over the nay-sayers!"
The signs that Ohakim may just be holding all the aces emerged a few weeks ago when the deputy governorship candidate to Senator Ararume in the 2007 elections, Barrister Bethel Nzimako, from Owerri zone, led some members of that campaign organisation to defect to the governor's camp. Nzimako's reason, among others, is that Ohakim be allowed to complete the turn of the Okigwe zone, so that Owerri zone will rule in 2015. It is one salient point the opposition is losing out on. The rotation of offices in Imo State is so neatly observed that people view with some circumspection promises by fresh candidates to do just one term. One analyst argued that the Owerri zone will be more comfortable supporting the governor to do four more years after which, statute-barred, the governor retires and supports Owerri zone to produce the next governor, than for a man who may seek to stay another four years and torpedo the power sharing arrangement. Governor Udenwa, from Orlu zone, ruled for eight years 1999-2007; Ohakim from Okigwe zone if he gets a second term will rule till 2015, after which Owerri zone gets the shot. People fear that if either of Ararume or Okorocha or even Agbaso get elected the neat power sharing arrangement will be distorted. It is this and many political realities that make an Ohakim victory stronger even now.
Dr Okpaleke is the Commissioner for Information Imo State

Original_Search_Title: The Story of Citizen Christopher
Headline: Nigeria: The Story of Citizen Christopher
Body_Text:
By profession, he was a gateman, a down-to-earth gate keeper of a sprawling neighbourhood in Lagos. He made no pretext about being a gateman: he was proud of his profession. His existential value rested with that. His real name was Christopher. All the neighbours simply knew him by that name unmindful of his surname or where he came from. Judging from his appearance, he could pass for Chesterton's village idiot, but Christopher was not an idiot at all. He brought colour and elegance to the job hitherto taught to be for never-do-wells of this world in my neighbourhood.
My neighbourhood is the type that Peter L. Berger and Richard John Neuhaus would describe as a mediating structure in the face of the fruitless efforts, or better put, failure of the State to provide the basic social amenities for the citizens or the enabling environment for the citizens to use their talents and create wealth for themselves. We have a Neighbourhood Government (NG) run by democratically-elected neigbours. The NG ranks as one of the most important things in the organization of our lives.
Since the local government system has collapsed, the NG is the only government we can see and touch everyday. Through our vibrant NG we have carried out many social projects that have positively improved the lot of many neigbours. Our NG chairman, a no-nonsense retiree, is revered and feared by all. He could do and undo in the neighbourhood. He could even settle disputes and tenancy matters involving neigbours.
Come to my neigbourhood and see how democracy works. Elections are always free and fair. No rigging. No gerrymandering of any sort. Problems are tackled through Committees. There are different Committees for tacking different problems. For example, we have the Electricity/Power Committee in charge of electricity supply. Equally are the Environmental sanitation Committee, Finance Committee, End-of-the-Year/Christmas Party Committee; Road maintenance Committee and the Security Committee. For obvious reasons, the Security Committee and the Electricity Committee are the most important Committees.
Tyranny is abhorred in my NG. Binding decisions are majority decisions reached at properly-constituted NG meeting. For example, many years ago, a former chairman of the NG unilaterally thought that the best way to protect the lives and property of neigbours, amid the rampart armed robbery incidents in the area, was to hire some fearful-looking Ijebu-Ode juju guards to take over the security of my neigbourhood. According to him, the Ijebu-Ode juju guards have the reputation for tracking down armed robbers with their powerful juju which functions through remote-control. All what the juju guards do at night whilst the neigbours are asleep is to perform their juju sacrifice in the neigbourhood with their cocks and tubers of yam and afterwards any armed robber that dares enters the neighbourhood will be caught dead or alive. Anyway, the former chairman's unilateral action drew the ire of the neighbours. To cut the long story short, one day the neigbours came together and chased out the Ijebu-Ode juju guards from their neighbourhood after rebuking the chairman for turning their clean neighbourhood into a Juju neigbourhood.
That was the neighbourhood in which Christopher found himself. As I said earlier, he brought style to the gateman job hitherto thought to be for misfits and societal dregs. Although Christopher was poor he was a young man with almost unstoppable passion to conquer his poor world and shine forth to high heavens. His contagious smile while manning the gate endeared him to the hearts of many neighbours. He dressed neatly in his cheap clothes. He was contended with his little pay. He extorted no money from any neighbor. He never fought any neighbour or abused any neighbor.
Impressed by Christopher's uncommon character, a Pan-African University (PAU), Lekki, Lagos lecturer who was a neigbour invited Christopher for a job interview at PAU. We later gathered that Christopher excelled at the interview and was employed as a security officer at PAU. And so we lost Christopher, a big loss indeed. Since then smile has disappeared from our neighbourhood. Till date Christopher's absence from our neigbourhood is still palpably felt. The corrugated iron street gate looks drier. No life. No hope.
But something happened last week which shows that a diligent worker can reach to the peak of a his/her working career. I was invited to PAU for a conference. I dread going to Lekki for anything, but I couldn't resist attending this particular important conference. No sooner had I entered the PAU lobby than a voice kept calling and trailing behind me: "Oga Sonnie", Oga lawyer Sonnie". I turned and saw a smiling figure whose face I could hardly recognize. "Oga, you still remember me? I am Christopher. I used to be the security man for your street. Long time, sir", the figure kept repeating. After a moment I recognized the figure as Christopher. He gave a warm embrace. "Oga, they have promoted me. I am no longer a security man. They have even sent me for training", he said smilingly.
Christopher's story underlies the importance of doing one's work with passion, great dedication and love. Societal progress is inversely related to the work culture prevailing in that society. If people who work in the so-called low cadre jobs do not take their jobs seriously, the society will suffer for it on the long run. Christopher's rise to stardom has shown that the star is the limit of any worker, whether intellectual or manual worker, who does his/her diligently or conscientiously. St. Josemaria, the founder of Opus Dei, taught that every ordinary work done very well for love of God is a means of improving the social condition of the society.
But unfortunately we live in a society where people who do manual jobs or menial jobs or jobs that require lower skill do not take pride in their jobs. The general impression is the CEO jobs are the only important jobs to be taken seriously. We live in a society where the only measure of professional success is the size of one's car, one's house or probably one's fat bank account. We live in an age of great ostentation, colourful adverts and ego-amassing. To many, the only thing that counts in life is high-sounding job titles. And that is why Nigeria continues to lack skilled automobile mechanics, gatemen, plumbers, dish washers, shoemakers, tailors, car washers and others.
Humble and silent jobs that make society tick are often ignored or despised as nothing. But Christopher has shown that a gateman can contribute to the building of the Nigerian society through his gate-keeping. If the countless young men and women loitering the streets of Nigeria toady in idleness can take pride in the dignity of human work like Christopher, there will be fewer scoundrel in our midst today. Every day in front of my office, even as early as 8.15 a.m, I see a woman in orange defiantly clutching the bloom stick and sweeping my street. What is her name? We do not bother to know.
Whose mother is she? Who cares to know?. She is a sleep sweeper by profession. Certainly this woman's profession is as important as that of Governor Fashola.
