With her simple bloom she has been able to add colour and taste to the art of governance in Lagos State. When the history of Lagos State under Fashola is written the story of this woman and many of her ilks will be told with joy. It is impressive seeing former Niger Delta militants sitting down to learn new job techniques in Lagos and other places. An idle mind, they say, is the devil's workshop. If these ex-militants manage to learn new job skills they probably will find no time in the future to engage in any unwholesome practice.
A menial worker who has doubt about the grandeur of his job may comfort himself with the reflection that he is worthless. But he is mistaken. As a gateman it never crossed the mind of Christopher that he was useless. Filled with optimism and faith, Christopher smiled at everyone. He rendered selfless service to everyone in my neighbourhood. Today he has climbed the professional ladder. The sky is now his limit, thanks to his pride in the dignity of human labour.

Original_Search_Title: The Lagos in Vancouver
Headline: Nigeria: The Lagos in Vancouver
Body_Text:
There it stood, innocuous enough. Between two other stores. A corner of Lagos right inside Greater Vancouver. Never was a sight so welcome. Which was saying a lot, for a city that had so many spectacular sights on offer. But there they were, shunning those spectacular sights and embracing this innocuous one one fine afternoon: six Senior Advocates of Nigeria - two of them former NBA Presidents - and one, well, observer
Day 3 of the IBA conference and all seven meet up at the general registration area of the Vancouver Convention Centre. They have one goal. Seek out Lagos Corner, a Nigerian restaurant in Burnaby, a nearby suburb.
Or as Kemi Pinheiro, SAN says, to give it legal flavour, 'A fact finding trip to look for Nigerian food'.
The seven pile into two taxis outside the Convention Centre. Former NBA President, Wole Olanipekun, SAN former NBA President, Rotimi Akeredolu, SAN, and Adebayo Adelodun, SAN in the first taxi. Yusuf Ali, SAN, Kemi Pinheiro, SAN, Toyin Pinheiro, SAN and this writer in the other.
'For security reasons two NBA Presidents shouldn't be in the same car!' protest some of the silk in the second taxi, jokingly.
The trip seems unduly long, possibly because of the anticipation. Talk in the vehicle centres on, what else? Legal matters. Lawyers, Judges, and of course the scandals which beset them
Just before we arrive the restaurant this writer is strongly 'advised' by Toyin that she is a man, for the purpose of this lunch.
Finally, we arrive. The first taxi arrived a while before us.
'When we leave it won't look so far again!' quips Aketi who is already set to tuck into pounded yam.
Lagos Corner, as restaurants go, is a simple, clean restaurant on a regular street with other stores. It's been operating for five years.
On the counter is a game of ayo and on the tables, some reading materials on Nigeria and some local Nigerians of note.
The owners are glad to see us and engage us in conversation as the King of Juju music, Sunny Ade, croons in the background. CD. Of course.
'A sese fa fifty tiwa ni!' (We have just rocked our own 50th!) one of them boasts, of the previous week's Golden Jubilee Independence Anniversary celebrations.
They launched a Yoruba Hall last year she says, and are in the process of building. Three communities in Greater Vancouver - Burnaby, Surrey and New Westminster BC - are eager to have them.
'Won ndu wa ni!' she grins as she invites us to Nigeria Day planned for two days time. More grease to the cat that got the cream
We waste no time with our orders. Pounded yam, eba; egusi, efo; fish, assorted meat.
The meals are served quickly and we get down to the business at hand with all the seriousness expected of (legal) war hardened veterans.
The SANs roll their morsels expertly in their fingers before dipping in stew and popping into their mouths just as skilfully.
Ori mi ja, ja fun mi, eda mi ye o ja, ja fun mi, King Sunny Ade isn't letting up. Neither are we.
I ask Kemi who's sitting next to me in his trademark bow tie and dapper suit whether the bow tie is getting in the way.
'I'm managing it; it's a delicate situation but I'm managing it!' he assures, not once breaking his rhythm.
Counsel exchange war stories about the bar and bench. Corrupt Judges, exemplary Judges, cases - especially election petitions. Oh, what exactly was discussed? Let's just say we'll keep it private
Then, unexpectedly, the talk gets well, colourful. Really colourful. The writer realises why she was earlier warned to pretend she was a man!
Moral of the tale: where two or more men are gathered, colourful talk lurks around the corner
We finish, Yusuf picks up the tab and we move towards the door, with Kemi almost forgetting his conference bag, so good was the meal.
One of the young men helping out in the restaurant, Patrick, offers to take us back to Vancouver. He has a big SUV but still it's a tight squeeze. He's having nothing of our suggestion that we take two taxis back, and insists on the honour. The most senior amongst us, Wole baba! (aka Chief Olanipekun, SAN), doesn't have the heart to continue refusing so at his prompting we pile in.
'There are six SANs in this vehicle, if anything happens to us, awon governors a ma sunkun ni!' (some governors will cry!) quips one of the SANs (who shall remain nameless ). Many election petitions are riding on the backs of some of them
The mountains form a spectacular backdrop as we head back into the city. One of the SANs compares them to the hills of Ibadan. Weeell, we ponder his comparison, maybe not quite.
Patrick has lived in Vancouver for 20 years but visits Nigeria regularly. Talk goes to Nigeria. Naturally.
'If only Abuja were like this!' exclaims one of us.
'Na poverty and maintenance dey kill us there!' Patrick says with passion.
He points out that some of the the neon lights in the sign at Murtala Muhammed Airport have been dead for years and each time he returns home, it's still the same - not replaced!
'If I see pothole for dis road and call dem, I fit give dem two weeks to repair am. Dat na taxpayer money in action!' says Patrick, obviously frustrated about the state of things in Nigeria.
There's an expansion of the expressway going on, with minimum disturbance to other road users. Each side is being expanded by three lanes. They will be done in three months from start to finish our new guide informs us. Patrick who stays at VGC when he comes home expresses his frustration at the pace of work on the Lekki-Epe expressway.
We observe that back home, on a road with similar expansion works, traffic would have been stood on its head with impatient drivers immediately driving against traffic, horns blaring, curses spewing, abuse flying and tempers flaring!
'In fact' says Yusuf, 'it may not even be the driver who wants to flout traffic laws, it will be the owner shouting at the driver, "tabi ori e buru? Se oo ri'pe mo nkanju ni?" (Are you OK in the head, you unfortunate fellow? can't you see I'm in a hurry?), and urging him to drive against traffic!'
We all laugh as we realise that he's spot on.
Patrick's earlier hunch that our cab drivers probably took the longest route they could turns out correct as we reach Vancouver in record time. Cabbies! Same everywhere!
It's hard not to notice some disagreeable looking folks on the road. We are on East Hastings Road. Druggies, crackheads, the homeless and the down-and-out-on-their-luck. They would give our Area Boys back home a run for their money! They hardly, Patrick assures, stray into West Hastings where we are headed.
And there's a Police car on top of a bridge, waiting to nab traffic offenders. They have a target to meet on a regular basis and step up surveillance when their targets are due.
'The difference is that in Nigeria, the money will go to the Policeman's pockets!' we note. Here, the local government authorities get the fines and utilise it for the common good. And here, you are nabbed for a real traffic offence, not a contrived one.
We ask Patrick for recommendations on night life in Vancouver.
Toyin recounts a hilarious experience. Out with a group of friends the night before, they had stopped to ask a gentleman outside a certain risqué club whether it had been any good. He had muttered that he hadn't bothered to go in, and quickly walked away.
'But I'd recognised him as a Supreme Court Justice of one of the countries, who'd participated at one of the sessions I'd attended that day and he had obviously recognised me as well, though he pretended not to!' said Toyin.
'When we got to the door, we found out they were asking for ID - which explained why our Judge quickly left, flying his collar to disguise his identity!' he laughed.
'O gbe si reverse ni!' (He put it in reverse gear!) recounted Toyin, as we all burst out laughing.
We tumble out of the car as we reach the Convention Centre and Patrick refuses any payment as we thank him.
With the pounded yam, eba, egusi, et al sitting pretty in our tummies, it's doubtful if we have any serious plans to participate in any more sessions that day

Original_Search_Title: First Lady Donates Relief Materials to Kuje Prison
Headline: Nigeria: First Lady Donates Relief Materials to Kuje Prison
Body_Text:
First lady Dame Patience Goodluck Jonathan has donated relief materials to the Medium Security Prison, Kuje, as part of her activities lined up to mark Nigeria's 50th Independence Anniversary celebrations.
The items include bags of rice, beans, and sugar, cartons of detergent, beverages, toiletries, and tubers of yam.
Presenting the items yesterday, the first lady who was represented by Wife of Vice President, Hajiya Amina Namadi Sambo, said the visit was important because she has been championing the cause of the less-privileged in the society.
She said government placed more emphasis in reforming the prison system, adding that she was impressed for the efforts made by prisons authorities to engage inmates in skill acquisition while others have the opportunity to enrol in the university to make them better citizens after serving jail terms.
Speaking on behalf of the inmates, Mr Emmanuel Auta, a former staff of Aso Savings and Loans Limited, who was sentenced to 10 years imprisonment for engaging in 419 called on President Jonathan to consider their plight and grant presidential amnesty to deserving prisoners.
According to him, "Since the establishment of Kuje Prison, we have not had any presidential amnesty but it has happened in other prisons in Nigeria, but we are convinced that our several years in tears and heartbreak will be wiped away through your visit.
"We are appealing to you - as the mother of Nigeria - to speak to President Jonathan to look into our predicament and grant amnesty to those who deserve it, so that we can join our families and society again.
"And you shall consecrate the 50th year and proclaim liberty throughout the land and to all the inhabitants. With this, we are sure that this is our year of freedom."
He noted that 37 inmates had sat for Senior School Certificate Examination (SSCE) conducted this year in the prison while the prison was chosen as one of the centres for the National Open University of Nigeria (NOUN).
"Most of us who are not good in academic work have leant vocational trades like, tailoring, shoe making, soap making etc through the efforts of Comptroller General of Prison but we need the support of individuals to enable us get more materials for our industry," he said.
In her response, the first lady urged the inmates to take advantage of the facilities and enhance their skills, noting that the amnesty issue will be given due consideration.
In his address, the chairman of Kuje area council, Hon Danladi Etsu Zhin, noted that as a grassroots leader, he would ensure the sustainability of government programmers and policies, adding that his administration accorded priority attention to the welfare of inmates.
He promised to donate additional 10 bags of rice and tubers of yam to compliment the first lady's gesture.

Original_Search_Title: Jonathan's Special Assistant, Nwite, is Dead
Headline: Nigeria: Jonathan's Special Assistant, Nwite, is Dead
Body_Text:
The Senior Special Assistant to the President on Political Matters, Senator Polycarp Ogbonna Nwite, is dead. He died at the National Hospital in the early hours of yesterday after an illness.
According to a press release signed by his media adviser, Mr. Edward Dibiana, Senator Nwite's first son, Mr. Michael Nwite disclosed that his father passed on at the National Hospital, Abuja where he was receiving treatment in the early hours of yesterday due to complications from a medical surgery he underwent at Columbia Asia Hospital, Bengalore, India in April.
Nwite who resumed work two months ago after recuperating in his village, Odageri Edda, in Ebonyi State travelled back to his village three weeks ago for the new yam festival and was said to have slumped hours after his arrival, and was subsequently rushed back to Abuja where he was admitted at the private wing of the National Hospital before his demise.
A frontline politician in the South East, Nwite was Chairman, Senate Committee on National Security and Intelligence, when he was in the Senate. In 2008 he was appointed the Nigerian High Commissioner to Bostwana by late President Umaru Yar'Adua.
He was recalled to Nigeria in 2009 and made the Senior Special Assistant to the President (Political Matters), a position he retained under the current government until he passed on. He is survived by six children.

Original_Search_Title: Senator Polycarp Nwite is Dead
Headline: Nigeria: Senator Polycarp Nwite is Dead
Body_Text:
Lagos — Senator Polycarp Ogbonna Nwite, senior special assistant to the President on political matters is dead.
According to a press release signed by his media adviser, Mr. Edward Dibiana, Senator Nwite's first son, Mr. Michael Nwite said his late father passed on at the National Hospital, Abuja on Wednesday, where he was receiving treatment due to the complications that arose after treatment at Columbia Asia Hospital, Bangalore, India in April to restore his failing sight.
Nwite who resumed work two months ago after recuperating in his village, Odageri Edda, in Ebonyi State travelled back to his village three weeks ago for the New Yam festival and was said to have slumped hours after his arrival. He was subsequently rushed back to Abuja and was admitted at the private wing of the National Hospital.
A frontline politician and industrialist, Senator Nwite was an activist, who at the height of the regime of the late General Sani Abacha was one of the arrowheads of the pro-democracy group, National Democratic Coalition (NADECO), which insisted on the validation of the June 12, 1993 presidential election result which was voided by Gen. Ibrahim Babangida's regime.
For his audacity, Nwite was arrested and detained with some of his colleague- senators such as Asiwaju Bola Tinubu, former governor of Lagos State, Ameh Ebute and late Mark Nwulu, on allegations of being involved in the spate of bombings that rocked parts of the country then.
Twice, he contested the governorship election in Ebonyi state and lost.
He was chairman, Senate committee on national security and intelligence, while he was a senator. In 2008 he was appointed the Nigerian high commissioner to Botswana by former President, the late Umaru Yar'Adua. He was, however, recalled in 2009 and was made senior special assistant to the President, a position he retained under President Goodluck Jonathan until he passed on.
As a true believer in the principles of democracy, Senator Nwite had at different fora canvassed for internal democracy in the parties, supremacy of the rule of law and violence-free politics. He is survived by six children.

Original_Search_Title: Govs - Still Strong, Yet Divided
Headline: Nigeria: Govs - Still Strong, Yet Divided
Body_Text:
Lagos — Twenty-eight governors elected on the platform of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) were physically present last Saturday at the President Goodluck Jonathan's declaration rally. But out of the 28, only the support of twenty four governors can be relied on by the president for his presidential aspiration.
Governors of Adamawa, Kebbi, Jigawa, Sokoto, Katsina , Niger and Zamfara were not categorical where they would pitch their tents when the time comes . But with the solid supports from more than two-thirds of the governors, Jonathan can as well go to sleep with his eyes closed till October 18 and 19 2010 when the presidential primaries is expected to take place. In fact, the national ratification convention which comes up on October 23 would be a mere formality. This is because the governors as the leaders of the party in their states are the political rallying points and they would determine where the pendulum swings, as they say, "The governors have the yams and the knive.
Though in some states, there might be some political forces that could go against the position of the governors, but certainly, it would be almost impossible to defeat the position of the governors. In the South-west geo-political zone, for example, there are fears that there might be oppositions to the position adopted by the zone in Ibadan last month, but that fear was allayed, when Governor Olagunsoye Oyinlola told the rally at the Eagles Square that the collective position of the zone on who to support is binding on his state.
According to Oyinlola, "We in Osun State stand on the collective decision of the Southwest States to support the presidential aspiration of President Goodluck Jonathan. We shall support Goodluck/Sambo." By implication, this is a reaffirmation of the position taken in Ibadan and therefore nothing has changed. It is therefore expected that the zone, against all odds will give the President at least more than eighty percent of the delegate's votes.
Other governors of the zone supported his view. For example, the Ekiti Governor, Eng. Segun Oni in his comment said that the rally was the beginning of what he called the Nigerian Project, which must not be allowed to fail. According to him, "What Nigeria needs at this moment is a team work that would move the country forward to the Promised Land.
Governor Alao Akala of Oyo, while giving assurance of his state's support for the presidential aspiration of Jonathan, coined a new name for the President, "Jonathan Ebele Goodluck Azikiwe (JEGA).
On the same line of support, State governor, Gbenga Daniel said, "President Goodluck Jonathan is our President today. He would be our President tomorrow. From what you have seen, the people have spoken and they have spoken well. I stand here to affirm the stand the decision taken by the South-west in that in true federation we stand. The Jonathan/Sambo ticket is a symbol of true federation what they connote is true federation".
However, the areas of doubt are the North-west and North-east zones. The north-west governors did not address the issue of support for Jonathan. The zonal campaign chairman for the President, Governor Shehu Shema of katsina state in his speech only preached unity and oneness of Nigeria. According to him, "Be it north, east, south and west, we all belong to one family in the PDP. Be it northwest, northeast, north central or south-south, southeast or southwest, what we are looking for is service delivery based on equity, fairness and justice.
This is the time for a new era in Nigeria and we must come together as a nation to make the necessary progress." Governor Sule Lamido of Jigawa state known to be among northern governors opposing the aspiration of Jonathan, just said, "PDP is a family. There is nothing as a northern candidate or a southern candidate, the president that would emerge is a candidate of the Nigerian state, whosoever emerge would be presented to Nigerians as candidate of PDP because the party is a united family."
However, according to the Director General of Jonathan Presidential Campaign Organisation, Senator Dalhatu Tafida, the president would be able to get over 50 percent of the delegates, but certainly not the two third predicted by Senator Aniete Okon.
In the North central, it may not be outright victory for Jonathan because of the position of some of the governors. Kwara state will no doubt vote for their own, Governor Bukola Saraki and Niger State will vote for General Ibrahim Babangida.
Governor Babangida Aliyu of Niger state was one of the governors that attended the September 15 presidential declaration of General Ibrahim Babangida and therefore, his presence raised some doubts, and he responded thus in his speech, "I came here on Wednesday (referring to his presence when he attended the IBB rally). People will be wondering what I am doing here. Politics is all about dialogue, negotiation. I have been mandated to negotiate. Nigeria is for all of us. We must negotiate and dialogue to produce the next President. We believe in the politics of unity. We don't want the Cameroon treatment."
For Benue state, the position of the state is clear. According to Governor Gabriel Suswam who was also the North central states coordinator of the President Campaign Organization. He assured President Jonathan of the support of Benue state for his presidential aspiration. Suswam commended his colleagues for their mass turn out at the Declaration rally. Thereafter, he said that the presidential aspiration of Jonathan is historical because it marks the contribution of the major ethnic tribe in the sustained unity and progress of Nigeria as a sovereign nation.
However, the position of the Southeast and South south governors are clear, Governor Theodore Orji, who recently decamped from the All Progressive Grand Alliance (APGA) to the PDP in his speech at the rally sang Christian songs, he described Jonathan as a winner and as such, "a winner will always win irrespective of the odds" He sang to the admiration of all and the Eagle square was electrified by his song.
For Timi Sylva, who was tagged as one of the anti Jonathan registered his presence very well, Sylva commended his colleagues for the support so far given to Jonathan, concluding his brief comments with a prayer that the glory of God will continue to shine on all those that are supporting the presidential aspiration of Jonathan, stating, "Because you have done this, your life will never be the same."
The Rivers state governor, Rotimi Amaechi declared support of the state for the President. He said that the presidential aspiration of President Jonathan is an aspiration of both the state and Bayelsa state and therefore has all the support of Rivers state. His Delta state colleague, Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan appealed to Nigerians and particular all Deltans to register en-masse so as to enable them vote for President Jonathan at the appropriate time. He assured of the Delta state support for the presidential aspiration of Jonathan.
Sullivan Chime of Enugu was the next governor to speak. He said that the state is 100 percent a PDP state and therefore the state has endorsed the presidential aspiration of the President. He appealed to his colleagues to ensure that Jonathan is elected the Presidential candidate of the party and eventually elected the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria by next year. According to Chime, "for good governance and good infrastructure, please vote Jonathan."
The coordinating chairman for the Southeast states, Governor Martin Elechi described the rally as a defining moment in the annals of Nigerian as he has never seen such a mammoth crowd, where Nigerians came out en-mass to call on one man to rule them.
According to him, "never in the history of this country have I seen where Nigerians speak in with one voice calling on one man to govern them. I have never seen such a clamour. President Jonathan is a gift from God to Nigerians. President Jonathan will not annul any election, nor would he discriminate against Nigerians on the basis of tribe or religion. He has a listening ear."

Original_Search_Title: Four Years Later - Then And Now
Headline: Nigeria: Four Years Later - Then And Now
Body_Text:
Lagos — When I walked into the office of the then ThisDay Editor, Segun Adeniyi and told him amongst other things, that I wanted to be a writer for the paper, even I was not sure what I would be writing on; or how the writing would turn out if and when I got my request granted. Surprisingly, he did not laugh at me for thinking I could just wake up and get a page in a national daily. He asked what my interests were; and after our conversation, he said he felt my style was more suited to the 'Sunday Glitterati'. He directed me to the then Glitterati Editor, Moses Jolayemi whom I had never met before until that day.
I had never done any sort of proper writing whatsoever before then. Neither did I have any form of training as to how to do a good article. So when he asked me to send in a few copies of what I would be writing on within a few days, I became weak in the knees mostly because I did not expect everything to happen so fast. I expected to be told to come back later after they must have thought about it. You know the usual employers' rhetoric. I was so nervous at that point, that I was tempted to get a random article online, tweak it a little bit and then send in. I thank God that I was smarter than that.
I sent in an article titled 'LOVE OR MONEY' with a number of other topics I was going to be writing on and surprisingly, the editor not only liked them, but also said he would be running the article that week. At that point, I think I sent an SMS to everybody I knew reminding them that Reuben Abati might have his job threatened very soon by me.
Most people were excited for me; some others did not believe me especially my friends who till today still ask me to tell them who really writes my articles for me. Yeah, they think I'm not very coherent. I am obviously that Prophet God talked about, that is never respected in his own home, which is why I decided to constantly use them as topics in my article back then, as pay back for their lack of faith in me. The power of the pen
Well, on the 10th of September 2006, my very first ThisDay article was published and I must have read it about 30 times on that Sunday alone. Besides the fact that I could not understand where I found all those words from, it was fulfilling to finally be doing something I loved to do; even if as an amateur. The reviews came in, and as expected (since they were mostly from friends and family), they were mostly positive, praising me for a job well done. It was only months later, when my writing had improved that they told me how obviously inexperienced that first article was. I still laugh when I read that piece now. You can almost see how nervous I was through my sentences.
It has been exactly four years since I started writing this column even if sadly, I have not been very regular with my writing this year, as I am currently a schoolboy again. Through these years, I have written about the most random things from my President through my cheating friends and my love for yams, to now writing about writing. But without a doubt, my most recurring issues have been about Nigeria. I once heard that it is really easy to be a writer in Nigeria because of how dramatic our polity is. There is always something happening in Aso Rock or in some corner of the country. Nigeria is never short of stories. But there is also the theory that it is quite difficult to be a writer in Nigeria too because the constantly unfolding drama that we call our country, has way too many issues that it becomes difficult to pick one thing to focus on.
My relationship with Nigeria over these last four years has changed rapidly. I started off as an incurable optimist, constantly preaching the 'Nigeria WILL be great' gospel. I was sure that things would turn around soon and we would start living in paradise at some point. But most writers/columnists will tell you that the one thing that they get from doing what they do is having almost a first hand idea of what the situation really is. Writing everyday and seeing nothing change naturally becomes frustrating. That of course, threw me straight to becoming a pessimist within a few months. I was sure we were doomed and constantly saw nothing but a dying country.
Okay, let me do a quick run through on why it is very easy to join the pessimistic train. When I started writing in 2006, a lot of the issues we were having then, are the same issues we are still having today. An election year was approaching and as it is today, most Nigerians at the time, either did not know whom the candidates were, or did not feel the need to vote any of the candidates. It was (and still is today) a not so impressive line up of candidates.
As at this time four years ago, we had resigned ourselves to the fact that President Obasanjo would not deliver on his promise of constant power supply seven years after he had taken over as Head of State. The power infrastructure round the country was still shockingly bad and after billions had been (supposedly) spent in trying to get things to work, we were left with nothing. Today, nothing has changed. We had a President Yar'Adua who said electricity was a priority, setting all sorts of megawatts targets per year. Then his Vice President took over and still mentioned electricity as a priority. Well, you and I know the rest of the story. Even if there might have been a slight improvement (emphasis on the 'slight') in the last couple of months, we know we are still very far from being a nation of power.
Four years ago, we were already sick and tired of complaining about roads like the Benin-Ore expressway and how many lives keep getting lost there. President Obasanjo, who changed and reshuffled ministers probably every other month, had failed to address the simple issue of providing Nigerians with good roads to transport themselves. All we kept hearing was that N300b had been budgeted for road repairs within that period. Well, four years and a crying Minister later, we are still at the same spot; terrible roads, which result in endless traffic gridlocks and accidents that leave Nigerians, burnt to death like pieces of wood.
Four years ago, there was a painful trend of people getting robbed by (men posing as) policemen. Drivers would see the trademark police checkpoint torchlight from a distance telling them to park for what should be routine checks, only to get there and be robbed and sometimes shot at. Checkpoints were the new death zones. If people were not complaining that they were being robbed or shot at by the armed robbers in police uniform (or the policemen who became robbers), innocent Nigerians were being killed at checkpoints for refusing to give the policemen N20 bribes. Nothing much has changed today. Bus drivers and okada riders are still reportedly being shot at for the same reasons and sadly some of these policemen have gone scot free after the cases went cold.
Four years ago, the Super Eagles had failed to qualify for the World Cup in Germany. Nigerians were livid and had asked for all sorts of drastic measures to be taken. Coaches were sacked, the NFF was in disarray, players were tagged as unpatriotic, and everyone talked about the need to start then to prepare for the next World Cup with a four years head start. Well, do not scratch your head if that story sounds exactly familiar today. We are just a country that seems to be at a standstill.
I could go on with my sad examples but thankfully, I have gone from being a pessimist to being a realist today. I know my country has huge problems. I also know that these problems will not go away on their own, neither will we become great without some serious effort. I see no need to keep harping on the negatives when that has never helped anyone. Thankfully, four years after I started writing, we still have civilian rule going. It means we still have a chance to get things right.
The 2011 election timetable is out and a lot of people have cried foul because they think it is unrealistic. Seeing that the INEC boss is not about to budge, we obviously will have to make the best of the situation, which is to go out, register and vote. As much as I do not expect the most peaceful/free and fair elections yet, I do believe that things can be done differently this time. If people do stand up to vote and be counted, there is no reason why 2011 should be the same as 2007.
We have lived through the last four years as if someone pressed the pause button. Now we have an opportunity to not only press play for the next four years, but even fast-forward. Our destiny is in our own hands and that is the truth. Whether you belong to the 'Nigeria will be great' side, or 'Nigeria is a failed state' side of the divide, we must all accept that this is our opportunity to do something right for once. While it is easy to say, "nothing will change", there is no reason to believe that it cannot be done. Nothing will change only if we decide to approach 2011 the way we approached 2007. The time to act is now
A big thank you to my editor Gabriella Osamor, for putting up with me for three out of my four years of writing so far; especially in this erratic year. Here is to four more years of even better writing
ANSWERS ANYONE?
How many business days in a year do we lose to public holidays in Nigeria?

Original_Search_Title: APGA Commences Voter Education
Headline: Nigeria: APGA Commences Voter Education
Body_Text:
Onitsha — National Chairman of All Progressive Grand Alliance, APGA, Chief Victor Umeh, has embarked on voter education ahead of the January 2011 general elections.
Umeh, who spoke on the occasion of the new yam festival of Ekwulobia in Aguata local government area of Anambra State, asked the people of the community to ensure that they turned out en masse for the November voter registration by the Independent National Electoral Commission.
He told them that it was the only way their vote would count in the 2011 general election and appealed to them to vote APGA which, according to him, is poised to sweep the poll come January next year.
" Ndigbo should not be divided in the 2011 election. We in APGA, our own party, are prepared to sweep the poll in the 2011 general elections and the only way we can achieve that is by fielding persons of credible character.
"We are desperate to have 80 per cent victory by having quality candidates for the election. What is important is victory.
"We want people who will go there and bring something down to the people, somebody who will use his or her constituency funds for democracy dividends for the people," he said.
However, there was a mild drama when Umeh who wooed the member representing Aguata 11 constituency in Anambra State House of Assembly, Prince Ebere Ezechukwu, to join APGA, turned down the offer on the ground that his party, the Peoples Democratic Party, was still the party to beat in the forthcoming general elections.
Ezechukwu, who among others were admitted into the Igwe's council, Igwe Emmanuel Onyeneke, said he was not prepared to dump his party now that a new vibrant and virile leadership, led by Dr Okwesilieze Nwodo, had been put in place for the PDP.

Original_Search_Title: Frenzy As Sallah Festivities Kickoff
Headline: Nigeria: Frenzy As Sallah Festivities Kickoff
Body_Text:
Abdulaziz Abdulaziz, Ahmed Tukur — In these last few days of Ramadan, the excitement of the Sallah celebration is palpable nationwide. Amir Kabir, 6, expects to get a lot of "Goron Sallah", which is a token usually in form of new notes. "I would use the money to buy sweets, sunglasses and balloons." Young Kabir says.
Expectedly, the volume of economic activities has increased. LEADERSHIP SUNDAY reporter in Niger state reveals frenzy in commercial activities with people buying and selling essential items especially food items in bulk in preparation for Sallah.
Other states are not left out in the Sallah preparations. In Kano, our correspondent who went round reports that most markets, especially those that deal in clothing materials are choked up. In markets such as the famous Kantin Kwari, Sabon Gari and Kofar Wambai, thousands of buyers cause intimidating human traffic.
In high demand among shoppers, especially women are Atamfa, Shadda and lace fabrics as well as already-made clothes for children. Shoes and caps are also major sought after articles in the market. Streets where these types of clothing materials are sold are too congested that people move shoulder to shoulder. The price of some clothing has gone up. Five yards of the popular guinea brocades which used to cost between N1500 to N2000 now sell for as high as N3000 to N4000.
From the ever busy Sahad Stores to Area 1 and 3 shopping complex and UTC complex all in the Federal Capital Territory, shoppers can be seen snapping up items at a feverish pace in preparation for Eid El Fitir festivities.
At UTC complex in Area 10, Abuja, "Kojo", as he is popularly called is indeed happy. His personalized handmade cards are selling fast. He says orders for his bespoke cards have tripled. "Recently, a customer of mine who works with one of these banks, came in and placed an order of 65 Eid El Fitir greeting cards. That is double the number of cards I make in two months." Usually, his cards sell for N2500 - N3000, but as Eid El Fitir approaches, Kojo, in typical opportunistic fashion now sells them for N4000 a piece.
Prices of essential items, especially food stuffs have relatively remained the same in Abuja. At Sahad stores, the clothing section is brimming with customers snapping up clothes ranging from Guinea brocades, English wax, super wax and super exclusive, children clothes, shoes, bags. The food section is also buzzing with customers stocking up food items in preparation for Eid El Fitir feasting.
Hordes of traders from neighbouring states as well as neighbouring countries such as Niger, Cameroon, Chad and Benin, are seen making brisk business. Tailors are among top beneficiaries of this period with most of them working day and night to finish up the loads of fabrics expected to be turned into splendid garments.
Fashion designers, makeshift tailors as well as boutiques are witnessing a large volume of customers. Some of them attributing this to the fact that the salaries of civil servants were paid few days before Eid El Fitir. "Sallahs that fall in between salary days and middle of the month are always hectic as the volume of work is usually high," a tailor tells LEADERSHIP SUNDAY.
In Fagge, a predominant tailoring settlement, shops attached to houses are the common tailoring workshops with the tailors resorting to generators for their work as they seldom get electricity. The quarters, strategically located close to Kwari textiles market, is a hub of tailoring business in Kano with its chaotic nature mostly due to sounds of generators and blaring music from the tailors' workshops.
Hajia Maryam however feels patronage can be better. She is disappointed with the patronage she has gotten so far. "Business is not very good this Sallah" she tells LEADERSHIP SUNDAY. Maryam blames the economic situation in the country. However, she says she has to work overnight to meet the demands of her clients so that she completes their clothes before the celebration.
The prices of some food stuff have gone up since the beginning of Ramadan; it is expected to be the same with Sallah just a few days away. In Niger state, for instance a bag of "royal foreign" rice which costs between N7500 and N8000 now sells for N9000. A basket of tomatoes costing N1500 is now sold for N3000 while five tubers of yam which cost N500 now sells for N1000.
Also, the prices of livestock's and diaries have gone up. Chicken previously sold for N700 - N800 now sells for about N1000. A cow which sold for N70,000 in the popular Mariga cattle market in Kontagora area of the state now costs between N100,000 to N110,000. This has invariably affected the cost of beef - a kilo of beef is now N1000 instead of the usual N800.
In Kano, food sellers (rice, pasta, millet, etc) and dealers in soft drinks and ice blocks are also recording significant sales in the days leading to Sallah. The Kwanar Singer market, famous for food stuffs and assorted soft drinks, is agog with Ramadan sales.
A Field Day For Thieves
As traders and buyers negotiate through their purchases, thieves are all over the markets ready to pounce on unsuspecting traders. Several cases of stealing are reported everyday in those congested markets.
Market thieves employ ingenious tactics to perpetuate their crime. Popular is the pick-pocket technique while more daring ones grab handbags forcefully and run away with it. Those caught are given a beating of their lives before they are handed over to police or market authorities.
Traffic Jam
Vehicular movement within several towns, especially Kano metropolis has increasingly become difficult as traffic jams on almost all the major roads constitute a nightmare of sorts to motorists. Notorious for traffic congestion are Murtala Mohammed Way by Sabon Gari/Singer markets, Ibrahim Taiwo Road, IBB Road and Asibiti/Sabon Titi road. The traffic congestion, our reporter reveals is attributable to inflow of traders from other towns, states and neighbouring countries.
In Minna, some drivers interviewed at the central motor park say there will be no hike in transport fair because there is no fuel scarcity. Some of the drivers also say the low number of travelers cannot give rise to increased transport fairs. "During the Eid El Fitir, traffic is usually within Minna," a driver says.
Devotion
The month of Ramadan has great signifance in Islam, as it is a practical embodiment of submission and servitude to Allah. It develops and strengthens ones powers of self-control and promotes closeness to Allah.
The month of Ramadan which is considered as one of the Pillars of Islam usually comes with festive of prayers by Muslims and the performance of lesser hajj by those who can afford it.
A cleric, Alfa Tajudeen Aziz Afolabi, tells LEADERSHIP SUNDAY, "Fasting is an obligation for all Muslims and an opportunity to show kindness and do good to the less privileged amongst us in the society... I enjoin Muslims to maintain all the virtues imbibed during the Ramadan".
In the last five days, Muslim faithful have intensified worship and devotion as required in the last ten days of the Holy Month of Ramadan. These last days are believed to be the most sacred of the holy month, therefore, believers step up efforts in worship and prayers to court the pity of Allah, the almighty.
Most Juma'at and community mosques now conduct midnight prayers, "Tahajjud", with faithful trooping in large numbers for the congregational prayers as a way of further seeking closeness to God and seeking God's interventions on personal problems. Some believers retire to mosques where they would spend the last ten days of the month and only come out on Sallah day. While in the mosque, such devotees read passages from the Holy Qur'an, exhaustibly and pray all the time to seek closeness to almighty Allah. This practice is called, Ittiqaf. Many mosques in Kano provide facilities for such devotees.
During Eid El Fitr, an act of giving to the needy known as zakkat -el- Fitr is encouraged. In this case, alms in forms of grains are distributed to the needy and poor; based on the amount of persons one feeds in his home. The grains are usually measured with a cup referred to as "Mudunnabi" (The Prophet's Measure); this is one of the deeds a Muslim is expected to perform and is usually done on the eve or on Sallah. This act is usually one of the activities that mark the end of the Ramadan month.
In Jos the Plateau state capital, Bayo Alabira, a resident and parent is not very enthusiastic on the coming festivities. "It will be a low key celebration for me and my family" he tells LEADERSHIP SUNDAY. "After buying new clothes for my wife and children, I have little left to buy food stuffs and other necessities for the celebration". However, he says he would continue to remain prayerful and give alms to the needy.
Children are equally poised to enjoy the Sallah break. Aisha Abubakar, 9, tells our reporter: "I expect new clothes and shoes for the celebration. I can't wait to wear them and show them to my friends". She adds that "we also get to go the zoo and visit our relatives".
In Lafia, the Nassarawa state capital, the Eid Sallah ground along Shendam road is wearing a new look. It has been cleared of bushes in preparation for Eid El Fitir celebrations. In Keffi and other Local Government Areas of the state, traditional title holders are seen busy preparing their horses for the typical horse rides on Sallah Day.
With the Nassarawa state government yet to pay salaries, traders are complaining of low patronage. However few government establishments have commenced the table payment of junior staff.
An Abuja resident, Abdulsalami Badamasi, tells our reporter, "Ramadan is the most significant month in "Muslim-dom". It is the time in which the Holy Qura'an was revealed to the Prophet. It is a period of sharing, giving, warmth and spiritual repose." To him, "Such prayerful moments are what in my own opinion can chaperone Nigeria safely through one of the most exciting and promising elections in the country's history, whether Christian or Muslim."
Abdulsalami sadly will not be spending his holiday with his family in Kano as he wishes. "The financial commitment can be a burden. It is still one long haul of a month after Fitir before pay day. I simply cannot afford it".
He is not too enthusiastic with the thought of spending his holiday in Abuja: "Looking at the part of Nigeria where I come from-Kano, these kinds of festivities are very bland in Abuja. The same old parks, restaurants. Everything has a tinge of commercial undertone. In my part of the country, people engage in such festivity with glee and commitment. Every Sallah festivity lasts for a week in which the Emir makes a final Durbar (traditional Horse race to the palace) activity on the seventh day. The tars in Abuja city can break the strongest horse legs," he chuckles.
The Niger state government is partnering the Emirate councils to organise the annual Sallah durbar for Minna and Suleja.
On the likelihood of hike in food prices, Abdulsalami says, "Judging Nigeria's callousness in business, with a festivity like this, prices of things will most definitely shoot up."
In Kaduna state, the state government this year introduced free Ramadan feeding. Governor Patrick Ibrahim Yakowa, a Christian, while launching the food outreach, said he recognised the month of Ramadan as a "special month". "The Ramadan feeding is another way of helping some of our Muslims brothers and this will continue as long as we are in government. This is not limited to Ramadan. We have set-up a committee to look into the conduct of Hajj exercise with a view to bringing succor to our intending pilgrims," he said.
Sheikh Ahmed Gumi's house arrest in Saudi Arabia our reporter reveals has dampened the glamour of this year Ramadan "Tafsir" in Kaduna state. Gumi, a Kaduna based preacher, has been in detention in Saudi Arabia since February 2010 because of alleged links with Farouk Umar Mutallab.
With Nigerians groaning under austere economic times, a tradition known as Kakawa is gaining popularity. People contribute money to buy a cow, which is in turn shared according to one's contribution, so as to get meat to celebrate the Sallah.
The approaching festivities have however thrown up the likelihood of a scarcity of cash since banking operations will be interrupted. Holidays are expected to last four days from Thursday to Sunday.

Original_Search_Title: Igbo Community Celebrates New Yam Festival
Headline: Nigeria: Igbo Community Celebrates New Yam Festival
Body_Text:
Regional Editor — Igbo community in Lagos State and the Ohanaeze - socio-cultural group - have celebrated this year's New Yam festival at Badagry.
Host of the annual event, Eze Ndigbo of Badagry, Eze Michael Uchegbu, also launched his book, Igbo Language - Book Four - at the occasion.
At the ceremony, which was attended by many dignitaries including Chief Martin Nwaodika, general secretary, Igbo Speaking Community in Lagos; High Chief Abel Arukwe, the Obi Ndigbo of Okokomaiko; High Chief John Okara, the Obi of Ndigbo of Igbo Elerin, Uchegbu lamented that most Igbo children no longer speak or understand their language.
To reverse the trend, the monarch urged all Igbo parents to speak Igbo to their children at home, take the children to the village during holidays, teach Igbo language in nursery, primary and secondary schools, buy and read Igbo books and watch Igbo films.
He also recommended the children to visit Internet Igbo language websites.
Besides, he pointed out: "The day we allow Igbo language and culture to die, we cease to exist as Igbo people.
"Therefore all hands must be on deck to support the preservation and promotion of Igbo language and culture. My message to day is a promise, a pledge and a commitment by all of us, home and abroad. Anyi ga arusi oru ike iji mee ka asusu Igbo na omen a ala Igbo ghara ifu efu na elu uwa.
"We shall work harder than ever to ensure that Igbo language, Igbo culture, the way of life of the Igbo man does not disappear from the face of the earth."
Uchegbu also urged all Nigerians to vote in the forthcoming general election, while calling on EFCC, ICPC and all other security agencies to broaden and deepen the war against corruption.

Original_Search_Title: N87 Billion to Elect Treasury Looters? (2)
Headline: Nigeria: N87 Billion to Elect Treasury Looters? (2)
Body_Text:
"The Senate President is reported to be earning N250m quarterly or N83.3m per month while his deputy earns N50m monthly.
The senate has allocated N1.02bn as a quarterly allowance to its principal officers, known collectively as Senate leadership. Each of the other principal officers earns N78m every three months or N25m per month.
"This tragic state of affairs is unsustainable. Those in this feeding frenzy are endangering our democracy."
However, the curious twist in the above reports is the response of the National Assembly, who did not refute the allegations as incorrect, but helplessly pointed fingers at whom they called the founder or progenitor of the abuse of public trust!
In a report titled "OBASANJO'S GOVERNMENT PROMOTED CORRUPTION" on pg 6 in the Punch edition of 6/8/2010, "Members of the House of Representatives under the aegis of the Minority Caucus on Thursday accused the administration of former President Olusegun Obasanjo of promoting corruption.
They spoke against backdrop of media reports on Thursday, in which Obasanjo was credited as saying that the members of the National Assembly padded budgets and in some cases budgeted for ministries and agencies which they did not ask for.
"He was also credited as saying that with the connivance of bureaucrats such funds were usually withdrawn from budgets for projects that did not benefit the people."
But the leader of the minority caucus in the House, Alhaji Mohammed Ndume, described Obasanjo's comments as "unfair and uncharitable, arguing that lawmakers would not have bothered if the former president had said that Nigerian politicians were corrupt," They accused Obasanjo's government of doling out N50m to each member of the National Assembly to support his failed bid for a third term in office.
Alhaji Ndume further noted that ..., "the National Assembly has 80 percent People Democratic Party members in the Senate and in House of Representatives and Obasanjo is the father and godfather of PDP. "If that is so, then it means if the children are corrupt, then the father and grandfather are also corrupt! "We all know the level of corruption in this country. It was during his eight-year rule that corruption moved from a low level to a higher level."
Whao! Fellow Nigerians, are we talking of criminal offences here and not just an unemployed hungry and deprived Nigerian stealing a tuber of yam to keep body and soul together?
Well, if anyone believes that this level of sleaze is only peculiar to the Federal Legislature, then he should do a rethink! State Legislatures, Local Government Councils have learnt to play the game of follow your leader very well! No wonder election into public office at all levels have often become a 'do or die' matter to 'serve'! No wonder, a once unemployed rascal with connections acquires a N20m Hummer Jeep as soon as he is selected by his godfather as a local government chairman!
Well, we might ask if the Legislatures by themselves determine outrageous and vexatious emoluments, the answer is of course, no! The Revenue Mobilisation and Fiscal Committee is constitutionally saddled with this responsibility, but any hope that this committee's decisions are above board were jettisoned by horrid allegations of corruption leveled against this committee when its members paid a courtesy call on Governor Amechi recently in Port Harcourt!
The Rivers state government challenged the Committee Members to do their jobs transparently and should refrain from battering the prompt payments of states allocations with kickbacks or indeed predicating the size of allocations and location of oil wells and related revenues on the 'friendliness' of state governors. Amechi declared that states should never be made to go cap in hand to Abuja every month to collect their allocations!
Well, from the above, it is clear we are in deep doodle! Where do the principles of equity, justice transparency, 'one man one vote' come in this mess?
Any hope that our democracy is on course has been finally shattered by former Governor Donald Duke's recent expose on how INEC is compromised by incumbent Governors. Donald Duke has cautioned against any optimism in the electoral process, even if the electoral commission is headed by a saint!
Duke's expose makes interesting reading; in serious countries, the baby-face ex governor would be invited for debriefing by anti corruption and other law enforcement agencies! Duke has brazenly told us how he won his elections or was he narrating experiences which he garnered from the grapevine about what transpired in states other than Cross River?
In view of Duke's expose, we may just have kissed the over N87bn hurriedly passed by the National Assembly goodbye! How Jega hopes to order and receive 150,000 or so units of data capture equipment and other imported equipments within three months will be a big challenge, especially as such equipment are not off the shelf and may require 6 - 8 weeks or more to manufacture and consolidate for shipment with another 3 - 4 weeks leadtime before arrival port clearing and distribution. This would be an uphill task therefore, to complete a new voters' register and organize elections in four weeks before the January election; all these portend failure, but Nigerians are eternal optimists!
Even if all goes well and by some miracle the logistics are possible, and an excellent free and fair elections take place, are we likely to have credible leaders who truly wish to serve? The answer will still be a capital no! You can use best election practices to choose a leader amongst "Ali Baba's forty thieves", but what you will get may be adjudged to be free and fair, but you will end up with a THIEF as victor!
The political space in our country today promotes the survival of thieves and opportunists because the motivations as enshrined in the 1999 constitution are self-serving and unjust and oppressive. Only a truly federal constitution will give birth to a new birth in our country and not the fraudulently imposed 1999 version that will govern the 2011 elections.

Original_Search_Title: Ramadan - Fruits, Food Stuff Prices Soar in FCT
Headline: Nigeria: Ramadan - Fruits, Food Stuff Prices Soar in FCT
Body_Text:
Prices of fruits and foodstuffs have continued to rise in the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) as the Holy month of Ramadan progresses steadily.Residents yesterday complained of the increased prices and wondered why such hike comes up during Ramadan periods.
A market survey by our reporters showed an increase of prices on fruits, such as oranges, pineapples, water melon and banana.Prices of food stuffs like rice, beans, millet and guinea corn were also increased. Chairman of Dutse-Alhaji Fruit Market Association, Alhaji Isa Abdullah, said the price increase is as a result of high cost of purchases from wholesalers, adding that market seasons at times determine changes in the prices of commodities.
At Bwari Main Market, a bag of Irish potatoes which was previously sold for N7,500, now sells for N10,000, while a basket of vegetables sells for N13,500 as against N13,000. Similarly, a bag of rice at Mpape market which sold for N6,800 is now N7,400.
Prices of other items such as beverages, crates of egg, tubers of yam, vegetable oil and fruits, have also increased significantly due to the high demand of goods occasioned by the Ramadan fast. Our reporters however observed that items like fruits and beverages are on high demand in most markets visited.

Original_Search_Title: Fleet of Neighbourhood Markets Enlivens Ondo Towns
Headline: Nigeria: Fleet of Neighbourhood Markets Enlivens Ondo Towns
Body_Text:
A new wave of commercial endeavour seems to be sweeping across Ondo State, courtesy of a government initiative aimed at transforming the age-long form of market enterprise. For reasons ranging from restoring hitherto abandoned commercial spots, relocation from accident-prone locations to ensuring a modern and aesthetically-pleasing market environment, urban and semi-urban centres are being rebranded with a fleet of ultra-modern shopping facilities.
Tagged "Caring Heart Neighbourhood Market," the structures are being built in several locations in Akure, the capital city, as well as the headquarters of the numerous regions/local councils in the state. For instance, that for the Akoko regional community (that comprises the towns of Isua, Oka, Akungba, Ikare and Oke-Agbe) is located in Ikare, the traditional regional capital. Other major towns like Owo, Ondo and Okitipupa are also benefiters.
In fact, the Ikare project and indeed its location was informed by the need to relocate traders from their former site that is at the receiving end of a steep and apparently dangerous slope. Forty persons have this year reportedly been killed at the spot (Oke Alabojuto), which has served as the main township market since 1952.
The governor had earlier in the year visited the scene of an accident and vowed to relocate the market elsewhere and embark on the beautification of the entire stretch of the old market as well as carry out rehabilitation works on the notorious Alabojuto hill.
Located on a land area of 1.789 hectares, the market is being built by direct labour and is designed to have 700 (open and lock up) shops and an expansive warehouse with facilities like toilets, parking slots, police and fire posts, boreholes and health centre. It is expected to be completed in a couple of weeks at the cost of N200 million.
"We want to make the market a regional one that will attract businessmen and traders from within and outside the state," said a source close to the government, adding that the site (Alapata District, Akoko North East Local Government) was carefully chosen such that the people of Ikare would have easy access to the market.
Residents are thrilled by the development, going by their submission. Deji of Ilepa, Chief Omowaye Disu, said, "We are very happy. This is the first time in the history of the community to benefit from this sort of project. We will never forget Governor Segun Mimiko for this gesture."
Principal of Agboriki Comprehensive High School, Jimoh Akadiri, was likewise thankful. "There have been lots of deaths at the former market as vehicles fail to negotiate the slope and roll into the market and kill innocent people. This has come as a surprise to us and we thank the governor," he stated.
It was gathered that genuine traders at the old market will be given first consideration upon completion of the project and that no political considerations would come to play when the units are being allocated.
At the site of Cash Hold Road outlet of the project (rehabilitation of the Isinkan Market) in Akure, Mimiko's Special Adviser on Project Monitoring and Direct Labour Team II member, Kunle Ademeso, said that the market has 140 lock-up and 82 open shops, adding that the market has two boreholes, eight toilets, an administration block, sorting-bay for yam sellers and a car park.
While saying that the Direct Labour Team II is in charge of renovation markets at Isolo, Odopetu and Okelisa, he disclosed that the idea was to take traders off the street to ensure not only their safety but also address traffic congestion in the metropolis.
Commending the authorities, the Iralepo of Isinkan, Oba Joseph Olu Ojo, said, "My sincere appreciation to the governor for reconstructing the Isinkan Market, as the traders will now enjoy decent shelter. I know all conveniences will be in the market like the other ones you have wisely put in place.
"However, we need a health centre, improved medical services as well renovation of roads in the neighbourhood."
Apart from what looks like a massive urban renewal scheme to, among other reasons, aesthetically transform the state, government has commenced some road improvement projects such the dualisation of both the Oyemeku-Adesida-Oba-Ile-Airport Road and the Arakale Road.
Government spokesman, Kolawole Olabisi, commented on the cost of the Ikare stalls and maintenance programme put in place for the preservation of the markets, pointing out that the traders might not pay more than N25 for the stalls. He described the amount as minute compared to the facilities provided.

Original_Search_Title: Bulumkutu - Dilemma of Sacked Maiduguri Suburb
Headline: Nigeria: Bulumkutu - Dilemma of Sacked Maiduguri Suburb
Body_Text:
Maiduguri — When a low-income earning citizen in a country like Nigeria falls into the hands of a merciless landlord his troubles can best be imagined. For a citizen who happens to be tenant to two of such landlords, his condition can be piteous.
To Malam Mato, a Maiduguri based petty trader and father of four this was the reality that he had to contend with recently. He was not only confronted with a landlord who spared nothing including forceful eviction to get him pay his rent, but had to contend with another unwholesome factor that did not demand rent or any other fee to get him and family booted out, but the annual Maiduguri flood.
Until two weeks ago, Mato, his wife and children were happily residing in the heart of Bulumkutu, a neighbourhood of Maiduguri, the Borno State capital. Their relative peace was however shattered when a debilitating flood resulting from a torrential rainfall sacked them from their shanty household, a rented apartment.
Today this petty trader who makes small time business as a middleman for yam dealers and retailers is not only homeless, but also has to pay through his nose to get ferried on a canoe through the flooded roads to his place of trade. To add it up, Mato, a devout Muslim will be observing the worst of Ramadan fast in his 45 years of existence this year alone, as his wife and children had to go back to his father-in-law for temporary accommodation.
"This is the most embarrassing moment of my life. I cannot house my family during this holy month of Ramadan. I have to wait until my in-laws cook before I eat, and most painfully I lost most of my belongings in the flood...I have nothing except what I am putting on now...", said Mato almost in tears. Residents of Maiduguri in the past two decades have attested to the fact that Bulumkutu, a densely populated suburb has always been a waterlogged area. They also confirm that flood has gradually become part of the residents' life.
Flood in Bulumkutu has remained a perpetual incidence for a very long time. And at each round when the sky empties its drains upon the people of Borno, lives are sometimes lost, properties destroyed and disease like cholera takes its toll on the endangered residents.
Yahaya Haske, a Maiduguri-based mechanic told our reporter that "every year we witness families temporarily vacating their houses for three to four months to allow the flood dry off before returning back. Some years back a certain family lost two of its kids to a midnight flood that took every one unawares."
Mato also told our reporter that his case, as bad as it appeared, was better than some other residents. "There are many others that are worse off, many who almost lost their lives, many who have migrated back to their home states as a result of this rainfall," he remarked.
In this year's flood, over 50 households have been sacked by the raging water, which seek it course through people's homes and business outfits in want of drainages that could ease it out.
Most parts of Maiduguri metropolis were flooded in the past weeks, with even major roads becoming pools as motorists were seen splashing their ways through stagnant water that made commuting a headache to all.
Maiduguri had not witnessed a heavy season of downpour like this year's rains, many argue. With rain falling almost on daily basis now, many residents within and outside the state capital, especially those living in mud houses, continue to live in apprehension over possible collapse of their houses.
What particularly makes this year's rainfall quite different is the fact that canoes and mini boats have now taken over the conventional means of transportation in some areas where the streets have been covered by water. This has been the situation in Tashan-Kano park area where the renown yam market had been cut off by flood water, making it extremely difficult for traders to move in their truck of yams or buyers to go in and make purchases, until smart business minds immediately introduced canoes to ferry people in an out of the place.
Our reporter was one of the passengers to one of the canoes, after paying the sum of N20 return fee on the journey that lasted about seven minutes across stagnant water. While being ferried across to the yam market, one of the passengers, Yunusa Chiroma told our reporter that in his 12 years of trading at the Maiduguri yam marketers he had never witnessed such flood like this. "This is very strange to all of us here. Of course we do witness ponds of stagnant water on the roads making it difficult to pass through, but not a flood that suddenly turns our roads into rivers which canoes now ply to get us transported," he added.
The young man, in his late 20s, said the flood might be as a result of the lack of standard roads and drainage in the area, even as he hopes government should intervene, stressing "perhaps we will be salvaged from this extreme difficulty that has affected our business now."
Garba Alaji, a 17-year old operator of one of the boats making brisk business at the Yam Market, disclosed that in each trip he ferries eight passengers at the rate of N10 each. "I can't really say how many trips I make in a day, but certainly we don't rest at all, we go to and fro. Sometimes I make nearly N3000, sometimes N2,500 per day. The people appreciate it and it is a good business," he enthused. Across the water, our reporter met with Kachalla Ali one of the leading yam sellers inside the market. He revealed that he has been doing business in the market for "over 20 years now." According to him the flood had badly affected the yam business because "our trucks that usually come through the main route into the market now have to pass through Bulumkutu and we off load the yams behind the market. We then begin to negotiate on how to transport them on carts into the market, which makes us spend more. And at the close of business we pay N10 to canoe boys to ferry us out." He called on the state government to as matter of concern for poor citizens come to their assistance by ensuring construction of roads and drainage through the area to avoid future occurrence.
Incidentally, Borno State government has spent billions of naira laying township roads and drainages across the state capital and outside in the past seven years. These roads and drainages have in some places served their purpose. Many have raised concern on why Bulumkutu suburb, an area that has been known to be prone to flood, has not benefitted from the drainage and road constructions.
To avert the cases of flood in Maiduguri town, the state government had recently released over N100million for the evacuation of blocked drainage through a committee headed by the immediate past Head of Service, Engineer Ali Gambo Mainao, who was two weeks ago made commissioner in the governor Ali Sheriff's cabinet. The project is on-going as hundreds of youths are seen on daily basis removing slabs and evacuating dirt from the thickly blocked gutters in all nooks and crannies of the metropolis.
However, some critics strongly feel that such annual exercise of drainage evacuation will continue to be a drainpipe through which government funds are misused. They blame improper construction of drainage without an outlet for Maiduguri flood.
"We know the government has tried to beautify Maiduguri with roads and drainages, but the drainages are not well planned. They don't have outlets to a major river or carnal that could ease out the waters when rain falls. Just last week, the entire Lagos Street was flooded for hours after the rains. This was something that had never happened before and I am sure it is all because of the poor drainage system we have," said Umar Habib, a business man in Maiduguri.
Traders at Maiduguri yam market lament that they have made several pleas to the chairman of the council to come to their aid to see that they have at least accessible roads to their places of business. "But up till now nothing has been done. Though we have never been able to take our cries to the state government, at least he (Governor Sheriff) passes through this area and he also passes through Bulumkutu area to the airport. He sees how houses are submerged in water for months," said one of the yam traders' union leaders who would not want his name on print.
The new caretaker chairman of Maiduguri Metropolitan Council (MMC), Ali Umar, who was sworn in last week, said he would make sure he presented the case to the state government for necessary action, even as he noted that his new council was not unaware of the plight of Bulunkutu residents and yam marketers. "Their case is quite a pathetic one and I believe the state government has the area in mind because the township roads and drainage project is still ongoing, and no place will be left out. It is just that for now there is nothing one can do with the situation until the rains are over," said the MMC caretaker committee chairman. One thing that is of concern about the stagnant pools of water, which by extension constitute a major threat to the health of the people now, is the likely spread of diseases like cholera currently killing people in the Borno State today.
Many children especially the local Almajiris have turned the stagnant waters into swimming pools of sort as they were seen playing inside and unavoidably drinking from it. Health experts are more concerned about the situation because most of the submerged houses have pit toilets which contents are thrown out into the flood water, thus exposing many lives to water borne diseases.
Before the rains cease and the waters dry up, those sacked from their homes by the flood will continue to remain outside, and those whose business routes have turned to streams will still have to make do with canoes as alternative means of transport, while public health remains endangered

Original_Search_Title: 6 Die, 17 Missing As Flood Ravages Akwa Ibom Island
Headline: Nigeria: 6 Die, 17 Missing As Flood Ravages Akwa Ibom Island
Body_Text:
Uyo — AN unprecedented flood in the history of Akwa Ibom State has swept through Itak Abasi fishing community in Ibeno Local Government Area of the state leaving in its trail six persons dead and 17 others missing.
No fewer than 40 fishingboats were also swept into the Atlantic Ocean by the flood. The villagers who described the incident as a tsunami, said the flood swept more than three quarters of their once lively community into the ocean.
Governor Akpabio who visited the island, weekend, expressed sadness over the disaster, assuring that he would take care of the victims and ensure that they were relocated in a safe settlement.
The governor also directed the Local Government Council to gazette names of all victims affected and to forward the list to his office.
Akpabio who was touched by the plight of the victims, asked the council Chairman to arrange a make shift shelter for the people who had been suddenly rendered homeless as the agony of those who lost their loved ones lingered.
However, Vanguard learnt that Akwa Ibom State Government might not be able to address what appeared to be its worst flood disaster alone, except the Federal Government and the other agencies collaborated with it.
Executive Director, Projects, of the Niger Delta Development Commission, NDDC, Arc. Esoetok Etteh who hails from the area told Vanguard that the 15, 000 strong citizens of the settlement needed help as the flood had wrecked havoc on them.
Some of the residents told Vanguard that they were worried because the flood had washed away nearly all their fishing materials and had taken over their only primary school and health centre.
Vanguard was told that the attention of ExxonMobil, a U.S. international oil company was drawn to the impending danger, but that the company was allegedly insensitive to their plight. Vanguard also learnt that in 2002, Hon. Nduese Essien, the current Minister, Housing and Urban Development had advised the people of Ibeno to channel the matter to NDDC and the National Assembly through him.
In 2008, the Senate Committee on Environment visited the area with Secretary to the Akwa Ibom State Government, Mr. Umana Okon Umana, and recommended what should have been done in the area.
Vanguard investigation revealed that NDDC had allegedly awarded the contract to reclaim the land, said to be receding at about four metres yearly to a certain firm at the contract value of N6 billion, but three years after, nothing had been done even when the said firm had loaded shoreline protection equipment that had long been rotting at Upenkang village in Ibeno.
Councilor representing Ward II of the local council, Patrick Usenekong, who was evacuating some of the people trapped by the flood, told Vanguard that some of the people trapped by the flood now lived on top of their houses or on roof tops.
Usenekong said: "We haven't seen anything like this in a decade. The last this happened was 25 years ago."
At press time NDDC had donated relief materials worth about N500,000 comprising 700 tubers of yam, 60 bags of rice, 60 gallons of groundnut oil and other food items.
NEMA also donated materials worth N5 million including 300 mattresses, 300 bags of rice, 300 buckets for carrying water, 30 bags of beans, while ExxonMobil was said to have only made promises of support.

Original_Search_Title: N87 Billion to Elect Treasury Looters?
Headline: Nigeria: N87 Billion to Elect Treasury Looters?
Body_Text:
Democracy is popularly defined as a system of government designed by the people for the people. The adoption of this style as best practice of governance by several nation states is supported by the far reaching economic and social progress made by those countries with strong democratic principles and structures.
In this regard, the superior prosperity of the United States of America is seen as the result of its strong democratic structure, while the comparative poverty in North Korea, for example, is contrasted with the relative prosperity of its more democratic Southern neighbour.
Now, democracy invariably implies the adoption of the doctrine of one man one vote as the state is characterized by formal equality of rights and privileges for all citizens. The system also expects eligible citizens to exercise their right to vote or be voted for.
Thus, the principle of democracy would be breached wherever eligible citizens are denied their vote whether as a result of negligence or deliberate government manipulation.
However, the above conditionalities of voting rights must first be predicated on a formal agreement by groups of people who have freely expressed their mutual desires to live together and be governed within an accepted framework of rules and procedures generally defined as a Constitution!
The vital ingredients for a successful Constitution must also include individual freedom of speech, equity and justice! Thus, a social contract, which creates a master/slave relationship or 'monkey de work, baboon de chop' syndrome is unlikely to sustain an enduringly stable community or nation.
It is for this reason that although hundreds of nation states may claim to be democratically inclined, a country such as ours also professes democracy but regrettably, the practice is inequitable and unjust because the motivation for electoral office and leadership is driven by primitive self interest!
This is the critical difference between the continuous progress in social welfare of the so-called economically advanced countries and the retrogressive and poverty stricken state of most of those countries, which are endlessly developing!
A fine example of inequality and injustice that continue to deform our progress is evident in the size and value of remuneration or emolument packages enjoyed by those who are elected or selected to make our laws or administer our welfare! Nigerians have indeed been kept in the dark these last ten years or so on the burden of cost of operating our democracy, even when it is clear that such secrecy is against the grain of transparency and accountability expected in a true democracy.
However, the cat was let out of the bag recently when former President Obasanjo addressed the Niger State Senior Civil Servants at a retreat on Wednesday, 4/8/2010; Uncle Shege's admonition was that "we should look closely at the Legislature so that they can be more open and transparent in the way they do their work. Nobody knows in detail how much it costs us to maintain a senator. I believe it is more than N250m".
Well, the gentleman's guestimate must be respected to be fairly accurate; afterall, he was our country's president for eight years. In any event, he remains a critical member of the ruling party and may still have fair estimates of what his 'boys' are earning in the field.
In any event, Obasanjo's guestimate appears to be corroborated by Professor Itse Sagay, a respectable and accomplished constitutional scholar in a lecture titled "Legislating for the common good: contemporary issues and perspective" Sagay's observation are weighty and serious, especially in a country which United Nations Development indicators put amongst the poorest nations in the world, with over 70% of population i.e. over 100 million people living below the poverty line with incomes of less than $2/day!
"Sagay noted that In 2009, a senator earned N240m in salaries and allowances, while his House of Representatives counterpart earned N203.76m. In other words, a senator earned about $1.7m and the members of the House earned $1.45m annually. By contrast, a US senator earned $174,000 and in the UK, a parliamentarian earned about 64,000 pounds, annually.
