"In 2009, the federal legislature received N102.8bn comprising N11.8bn as salaries and N90.96bn (non taxable) as allowances. Is the taxpayer getting value for this colossal sum in the current democratic dispensation?
Should five per cent of Nigeria's annual budget be spent on 109 senators and 360 House of Representatives members? In other words, should 469 Nigerians gulp five percent of our budget leaving the remaining 150m of us to N1,0000 each?
"Barack Obama, the President of the richest country in the world earns $400,000 annually. The British Prime Minister, Mr. David Cameron earns  Â£190,000 annually, while a Nigerian senator earns $1.7m. It s absurd. It is as someone has called it; a feeding frenzy.
"The Senate President is reported to be earning N250m quarterly or N83.3m per month while his deputy earns N50m monthly. The senate has allocated N1.02bn as a quarterly allowance to its principal officers, known collectively as Senate leadership. Each of the other principal officers earns N78m every three months or N25m per month.
"This tragic state of affairs is unsustainable. Those in this feeding frenzy are endangering our democracy."
However, the curious twist in the above reports is the response of the National Assembly, who did not refute the allegations as incorrect, but helplessly pointed fingers at whom they called the founder or progenitor of the abuse of public trust!
In a report titled "OBASANJO'S GOVERNMENT PROMOTED CORRUPTION" on pg 6 in the Punch edition of 6/8/2010, "Members of the House of Representatives under the aegis of the Minority Caucus on Thursday accused the administration of former President Olusegun Obasanjo of promoting corruption.
They spoke against backdrop of media reports on Thursday, in which Obasanjo was credited as saying that the members of the National Assembly padded budgets and in some cases budgeted for ministries and agencies which they did not ask for.
"He was also credited as saying that with the connivance of bureaucrats such funds were usually withdrawn from budgets for projects that did not benefit the people."
But the leader of the minority caucus in the House, Alhaji Mohammed Ndume, described Obasanjo's comments as "unfair and uncharitable, arguing that lawmakers would not have bothered if the former president had said that Nigerian politicians were corrupt," They accused Obasanjo's government of doling out N50m to each member of the National Assembly to support his failed bid for a third term in office.
Alhaji Ndume further noted that ..., "the National Assembly has 80 percent People Democratic Party members in the Senate and in House of Representatives and Obasanjo is the father and godfather of PDP.
"If that is so, then it means if the children are corrupt, then the father and grandfather are also corrupt! "We all know the level of corruption in this country. It was during his eight-year rule that corruption moved from a low level to a higher level."
Whao! Fellow Nigerians, are we talking of criminal offences here and not just an unemployed hungry and deprived Nigerian stealing a tuber of yam to keep body and soul together? Well, if anyone believes that this level of sleaze is only peculiar to the Federal Legislature, then he should do a rethink! State Legislatures, Local Government Councils have learnt to play the game of follow your leader very well! No wonder election into public office at all levels have often become a 'do or die' matter to 'serve'! No wonder, a once unemployed rascal with connections acquires a N20m Hummer Jeep as soon as he is selected by his godfather as a local government chairman!
Well, we might ask if the Legislatures by themselves determine outrageous and vexatious emoluments, the answer is of course, no! The Revenue Mobilisation and Fiscal Committee is constitutionally saddled with this responsibility, but any hope that this committee's decisions are above board were jettisoned by horrid allegations of corruption leveled against this committee when its members paid a courtesy call on Governor Amechi recently in Port Harcourt!
The Rivers state government challenged the Committee Members to do their jobs transparently and should refrain from battering the prompt payments of states allocations with kickbacks or indeed predicating the size of allocations and location of oil wells and related revenues on the 'friendliness' of state governors. Amechi declared that states should never be made to go cap in hand to Abuja every month to collect their allocations!
Well, from the above, it is clear we are in deep doodle! Where do the principles of equity, justice transparency, 'one man one vote' come in this mess?
Any hope that our democracy is on course has been finally shattered by former Governor Donald Duke's recent expose on how INEC is compromised by incumbent Governors.
Donald Duke has cautioned against any optimism in the electoral process, even if the electoral commission is headed by a saint! Duke's expose makes interesting reading; in serious countries, the baby-face ex governor would be invited for debriefing by anti corruption and other law enforcement agencies! Duke has brazenly told us how he won his elections or was he narrating experiences which he garnered from the grapevine about what transpired in states other than Cross River?
In view of Duke's expose, we may just have kissed the over N87bn hurriedly passed by the National Assembly goodbye! How Jega hopes to order and receive 150,000 or so units of data capture equipment and other imported equipments within three months will be a big challenge, especially as such equipment are not off the shelf and may require 6 - 8 weeks or more to manufacture and consolidate for shipment with another 3 - 4 weeks leadtime before arrival port clearing and distribution.
This would be an uphill task therefore, to complete a new voters' register and organize elections in four weeks before the January election; all these portend failure, but Nigerians are eternal optimists!
Even if all goes well and by some miracle the logistics are possible, and an excellent free and fair elections take place, are we likely to have credible leaders who truly wish to serve? The answer will still be a capital no! You can use best election practices to choose a leader amongst "Ali Baba's forty thieves", but what you will get may be adjudged to be free and fair, but you will end up with a THIEF as victor!
The political space in our country today promotes the survival of thieves and opportunists because the motivations as enshrined in the 1999 constitution are self-serving and unjust and oppressive. Only a truly federal constitution will give birth to a new birth in our country and not the fraudulently imposed 1999 version that will govern the 2011 elections.

Original_Search_Title: Jega May Not Be Our Messiah - Okwu
Headline: Nigeria: Jega May Not Be Our Messiah - Okwu
Body_Text:
Chief Maxi Okwu is factional National Chairman of Conference of Nigeria Political Parties (CNPP), and presidential candidate of the Citizens Popular Party (CPP), in the 2007 race. A lawyer and vocal politician, Okwu, in this interview with Correspondent, AUGUSTINE MADU-WEST speaks on a number of burning national issues, including the controversy being generated by the speculated President Goodluck Jonathan's ambition to contest next year's presidential election.Excerpts:
The 2011 election has been fixed tentatively for January, next year, how feasible is this, with all the talk about Constitution Amendment and Electoral Reform?
Well, if the National Assembly as pronounced by the Senate President, David Mark gazettes the amendment of the bill they gave to the states and nobody challenges it in the court of law, that is the Constitution and everybody is bound. The former plan "B" put up by the former INEC Chairman, Prof. Maurice Iwu comes into play. We have no choice about it; it is a set piece. I have my personal quarrel with the fallback position of the National Assembly on this Constitution amendment process. They have decided that they will pick and choose all those sections that have crossed 24 states and adopt them, any section that fails; that is rejected. I think it is wrong.
I stand to be corrected and I hope it is challenged in the court of law. I believe that if you look at the condition of the Constitution amendment, a bill will be presented, you don't adopt a bill in parts, the bill must cross 24 states approval before it becomes the Constitution amendment. So, there are some place here which is contestable and this pick and choose sections that have crossed or did not cross, I doubt it personally. Like I said, I stand to be corrected.
What about the issue of Electoral Reform; especially as it affects the Justice Uwais Report. Some say it has to be adopted totally?
No, we have gone beyond that. Whoever is saying that does not know what he or she is talking. The Uwais recommendations were a mere recommendation, and the government that put up the Uwais panel decided to now present some portions of the suggestions as a bill to the National Assembly. The father of the process, late President Umaru Yar'Adua, sent six bills to the National Assembly, where two died immediately. So, you cannot suddenly adopt Uwais Report. You must bring it in bill form and move to National Assembly.
Yar'Adua did that with six bills and this is what is happening now. You can't go outside that, unless you want to introduce a private member bill, which nobody has done. What unfortunately happened is that Yar'Adua lost interest in the process when his government stabilised followed the Supreme Court endorsement. Did you see him lobby the National Assembly the way the American President, Barack Obama lobbied for his Health Reforms bill, where he practically called all Senators, all members of Congress, went for lunch first with the Republicans. Yar'Adua just sat cool and allowed the National Assembly to do what they liked with it.
So, the position now is that certain portions of that Uwais Report have been adopted, with many turned down. The kernel was thrown away; the mode of appointment of election management body, at the federal and state levels and a number of other critical recommendations were jettisoned.
So, the watered-down version we now have in this amendment process, which is now an improvement of guidelines for election, like time for petitions to be exhausted before inauguration or swearing in, and a few other cosmetic amendments.
But, be that as it may, I never believed in this process. I am even surprised that the National Assembly was able to achieve it. I'm surprised. I have always said that beyond the laws; the laws are okay, it is the officers of the electoral process that must reform the elective management body, and of course the electorate. These are the people who need reforms.
You are a lawyer, and you know some have faulted the process by which the new INEC Chairman Professor Attahiru Jega was appointed. Any quarrel with that?
You can't go outside an existing law. I'm a lawyer like you said, and a stickler to the law. The current laws of the land are the process through which he emerged. The man has startling credentials. His pedigree is super. But that is not enough. On the date of election whenever it is, Prof. Jega will not be in Achulo ward 3 of Enugu State, where I come from. I know that it is those men in the polling units, the presiding officers, and Resident Electoral Commissioner in Enugu that will determine the play. Jega will not be there.
So, he can do all his motions in Abuja, give all sorts of promises, but if his REC fails him, because he has no control over the RECs; they were appointed differently, that is it. Some of the RECs are PDP card-carrying members, especially in the South East where a protest has been raised and President Jonathan promised to look into it and effect a change. But, who knows. I don't know if he will be among the ones Jega will swear in, if such REC is sworn in, take it that PDP has won in Enugu State, because he will prepare a set piece and if after that, say a week to the election he is re-assigned to another state, the new REC cannot change the set piece. That is the problem.
What is then the way forward?
We must be proactive. There must be competition. The question now is where is the challenge to PDP; election challenge to PDP? It does not exist. I have not seen it yet. I have tried ceaselessly; maybe because I am not a minister or ex-governor, people were not listening to me. I think I failed, all the people who have been gum-chewing around in the name of mega party, I think, I have been vindicated. When I told you last time we spoke that it is going to be a mega flop, because that is not the way to go, I recall with nostalgia, the progressive governors forum put together by Chief Obafemi Awolowo in 1979-1983, it gave nightmare to the then National Party of Nigeria and by the time we were bracing the tape in 1980, the Progressive Peoples Alliance (PPA) was about to challenge the NPN until the Federal Government used federal might to settle that precept, using the FEDECO. How would non-PDP governors follow PDP governors in the so called governors forum? At Enugu the other day, could you imaging APGA governors attending PDP rally for the National Chairman, Dr. Okwerilieze Nwodo. I begin to ask myself, what is happening.
I don't see PDP governors attending an APGA rally. That shows that these governors are all scared of the Federal Government because the Federal Government holds the yam and the knife and this is one of the distortions in our federal system, which we must address. There is a structural imbalance in our federal system. Why would President Goodluck Jonathan summon South East governors? It is not right. I cannot imagine Tafawa Balewa summoning Michael Okpara, or Awolowo or Akintola or even Osadebe. It couldn't have happened.
dsBut now, the Federal Government sneezes everybody catches cold. This, to me, is the consequence of prolonged military rule and distortion of federalism. These are the problems. So, we must address this issue. The reason why everybody is rushing to Abuja, relocating to Abuja to be issued a piece of paper, which determines his fate in Achi my community, it should not be so, if you make the zones, regions powerful as it happened in the 60's. This won't happen.
Do you see this happening in our time?
This is the only way to go. It will take time. But we will get there definitely. We cannot continue this way.
You talked about the opposition to PDP not being there, but recently in a chat with the ANPP's Elders Committee Chairman, Alhaji Bashir Tofa, who is saddled with the responsibility of seeing to the coming together of opposition parties to secure a strong platform that will challenge the PDP, he said his contact committee has made appreciable efforts in that direction?
I doubt if that is true, especially from the ANPP angle. In fact, it is one of the greatest disasters of our democratic engagement since 1999; the failure of the ANPP to provide an alternative platform. Recall that by 1999, ANPP had about 16 states, was strong in the National Assembly and was the next in line to the PDP. If ANPP had held through to that achievement, it would have been the rallying force for the other side. What do you do? Unfortunately for the party, most of its national chairmen have been PDP people on sabbatical to the ANPP; from late Mohammed Waziri to Don Etiebet to the current chairman, Chief Edwin Ume-Ezeoke, who has been more PDP than PDP. That is the problem. That is a disaster.
They crashed with 13 governors and one of the remaining three, his stay in ANPP is doubtful. I won't mention his name. He is virtually PDP. What some of us are doing ought to have been initiated by the ANPP. So, I don't know where Alhaji Bashir Tofa got his confidence from. I don't know who they talked to, because they did not talk to me, and think I am relevant in this process. So, there is no evidence to show that he has been able to bring one or two parties together. Is it Action Congress (AC) for example or APGA? So, who are they talking with?
Well, let us see how it unfolds. So, as far as I am concerned, the ANPP has not satisfied its historical role in this process and has failed the nation.
The way things are going, do you have any fear that we may end up a one-party state?
Yes, with the sort of movement back to PDP. In any case, all the top shots of PDP who left the party have not been able to stay outside PDP. Orji Uzor Kalu, former Vice President Atiku Abubakar, and others. So, I don't know. It worries me seriously and I have tried to do all within my powers to see whether we could package a progressive alternative to PDP. AC has tried to do its best, but it has a problem.
It has already been stigmatised as a Yoruba movement, and outside the Yoruba land the AC is dead. It has gone back to the way of AD. I am looking at APGA to pick up its historical mission in the South East and expand. It has been bugged for about six years with litigations. With the recent pronouncement of the Supreme Court, let me hope that sanity will prevail. Some of us are from APGA. It is a viable option, at least from the South East. Let's look at it. Nothing is closed. But honestly, in terms of a national opposition to PDP, it does not exist.
So where do we go from here?
We take it one by one. Let us first of all achieve contentment of the PDP in certain areas. There are some good movement from the South East. APGA can checkmate PDP in the South East. I believe again that the ANPP could retain Kano and maybe Borno. If that happens, it will wage the Tsunami to PDP. Secondly, I am hoping that the PDP implodes or explodes. It has to if democracy must grow in Nigeria. PDP is ubiquitous. If President Jonathan secures the party's ticket, which I am sure he must pick, PDP will implode and that will mark the beginning of democracy in Nigeria.
This takes us to the controversy the PDP zoning in generating. The issue is almost assuming a national dimension. Is it supposed to be so?
Well, you are correct; it is an internal PDP arrangement, packaged at the time to satisfy certain agitations we found ourselves in 1999. The reason why it became a national issue, is because PDP is so overbearing. It is the dominant party. You have to concede that fact. It is the dominant party in the country today. I am not a PDP member, so its agreement does not bind me. But, as a political observer, participant, and commentator, I believe that the death of President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua is a game changer. And the zoning as practiced or as adopted has been overtaken by unexpected events. The fact of the ascendency to the presidency of Goodluck Jonathan has changed the picture. Anybody who fails to take that into account, is deceiving himself; because, assuming we are to agree that it is still in the North, any northerner who goes in there will be entitled to claim his eight years.
So, I'm recommending as a patriot, that they allow Jonathan to collect the ticket to complete the joint mandate; if the Nigerian people agree, between himself and Yar'Adua. That is my recommendation to PDP. I am not their member. We will face them at the polls and we could defeat them if we play our cards well. But I am advising the PDP to stop all this sabre-rattling between the North and the South South.
I see today everybody is adopting President Jonathan because he is in power. But recall a couple of months back he couldn't even visit the Villa form Aguda House within the same compound. Now that he has assumed power, everybody loves him. It's sycophancy. So, let PDP allow Jonathan to secure the nomination, which ought to have gone to Umaru Yar'Adua.
He must see himself as the transitional government that will carry every Nigerian along, every shade of opinion along for four years, so that we now have a level playing field for 2015; because the amount of money in the hands of these public office holders, in the Senate, House of Representatives, ministers, governors is overwhelming. It will require a lot of Goliath/David efforts to overturn them. It is possible but definitely, they have the arsenal.
Anti-Jonathan groups have argued that what he needed do is to utilise his limited time to carry out some developments in the country and quit. Is this not a plausible argument?
Ideally, yes. But, let's be realistic. The man has tested power, he won't throw it away. With the current guideline for election, January, as agued by the amendment, the earliest time you may have for national convention to emerge is September. What can he achieve in terms real development project between now and September? As far as I am concerned, it is impossible and if he pretends he can do it, he is lying. What I think he should do is to maintain peace, unity and good governance. Maximum, he can seek election. I support it. He should go ahead and seek it. But to think he will be able to turn Nigeria around overnight it is a lie.
We have uninterrupted democracy for 11 years, what has been the gain?
No. We have not had 11 years of democracy. We have had 11 years of civil rule. Democracy requires two important ingredients, choice competition and rule of law. There is no choice competition in our system yet. Go through the country, whether it is PPA, APGA, ANPP, PDP or AC, any state controlled by any of these parties wins 100 per cent local government elections. At the federal level, they are now calling Jega the Messiah, he may not be, because there is a structural imbalance on the line where he will meet his waterloo.
Again, the parties outside the PDP, there is no immediate viable alternative to PDP. It does not exist. I am in the opposition and I know it doesn't exist. People lack choice and competition. There have been some pretence to respect the rule of law, but it is not there yet. Look at the issue of Bakassi, government just took a stand outside the legal positions as established by the courts in the land. Another case is that of Iwu.
Agreed, he was unpopular. But I state clearly that the President, Goodluck Jonathan asking him to proceed on leave a month plus to his tenure date of departure is illegal. For you to remove a commissioner or chairman of INEC, you must have two-thirds majority in the Senate. So, we don't have democracy in the country. What we have is civil rule. Let us hope we will move from here to another point.
How do you access the National Assembly?
Total failure! We have spent billions on these fat cats as of today. From 2000 to date, if you look at the enormous resources, in terms of recurrent, in terms of overhead, in terms of capital vote to the National Assembly, what have we gathered in returns; not up to 10 bills passed into law. We are not getting value for our money from the National Assembly. So, we must address that issue.
The National Assembly is costing this nation too much. Agreed, it's an important part of the three arms of government, but we have to find a way of making it part-time and reduce the cost of the National Assembly. You can see legislators going about in five, eight-car convoys, not to talk about their principle officers. I recall in the Second Republic, my father was a Senator of the Federal Republic; he did not have a car, no convoy, or police escort, and these guys are costing us too much money. Let us reduce the cost.
How do we achieve this?
First of all, to make sure no member gets official car, no member gets residence at our expense and ensure that members are only paid sitting allowances. Remove the current situation where members parade army of aides, or let it be at their own personal expense. They should stop paying them severance or hardship allowance. Nigeria is practically bleeding servicing public office holders.
What is your concern about 2011 race, any?
My worry is that Jega might try to do his best but he must be assisted by his field officers. I was in Anambra for the election in February and the picture was that there were six viable contenders, that could match each other eye ball for eye ball, and polling booth for polling booth and the governor, chief executive of the state did not use his incumbency power by making the field uncomfortable for his competitors. That is the magic. If there can be competition to the PDP, forget about laws or reforms; matching them booth by booth, issue for issue; but for now, it does not exist. I don't know whether within the small time left, we in the opposition can package it. I want to believe we can, because honestly, I feel so bad about it and I have been crying over this issue for so long. I will not relent. But let us see how it goes.
Your message?
Well, we must make sure democracy survives as there is no alternative to democracy. If we don't get it during out time, our children won't. That is why we are fighting for it. Let us continue the struggle. Some of us have refused to surrender or decamp to PDP, because we believe we must leave a legacy for our children and children's children.

Original_Search_Title: Zoil Saves More Lives on the Volta Lake
Headline: Ghana: Zoil Saves More Lives on the Volta Lake
Body_Text:
Accra — Zoil Lifeguards stationed at Dambai in the Kete Krachi District of the Volta Region have once again averted an accident on the Volta Lake by saving the lives of 51 passengers together with their goods on last Wednesday.
The incident occurred when 51 passengers travelling on a boat with the name "Mpaebo" from Dambai to Ingarre all in Kete Krachi District capsized due to a heavy storm, but the timely intervention of the lifeguards saved the situation. Since the inception of the rescue facility, Zoil lifeguards have been rescuing passengers on the Volta Lake in the Eastern, Volta and Northern Regions.
The Guards in recent times have rescued passengers in similar accidents in Akateng in the Eastern Region and Katare in the Kpandai District of the Northern Region when a boat on the lake capsized on 14th July 2010 and close to 200 lives were saved as well as goods.
The users of the lake have at each instance expressed their appreciation to Zoil Services Limited for their innovation and the Government of the Republic of Ghana for supporting such an initiative.
On their first rescue mission on February 22, 2010 the guards rescued a driver, two mates and a truck load of yams, gari and beans from the Volta Lake at Dambai in the northern part of the Volta Region as the crowd which has been attracted to the scene hailed the guards and the successful operation.
The Volta Lake Transport Enhancement Project was initiated by Zoil Services Limited and the Transportation Ministry to ensure the safety of passengers on the lake.

Original_Search_Title: Sesame Square Premieres
Headline: Nigeria: Sesame Square Premieres
Body_Text:
Lagos — Sesame Square, the Nigerian version of the popular children television puppet show, Sesame Street, is to premiere later this year.
Sesame Workshop, the nonprofit educational organisation behind Sesame Street, and Nigerian co-producer, Ileke Media, according to a statement, recently kicked off production of Sesame Square in Nigeria with a furry, friendly face, Zobi, recently named by the people of Nigeria via a mobile vote.
Sesame Square will be hosted by Kami, a girl muppet who is HIV-positive and has golden fur and a taste for adventure and knowledge, and Zobi, a furry blue boy muppet who is energetic and finds ways to make even the simplest task fun and exciting.
Together, Kami and Zobi will welcome viewers to each episode, and will be seen in several segments throughout the half-hour including "Word of the Day" segments filmed in cities across the country and featuring Nigerian children interacting with the Muppets of Sesame Square. Each half-hour block will also present the following Sesame Street formats known to children worldwide.
In response to joining Sesame Square , Zobi said: "I am so happy to be here with my good friend, Kami, and I can't wait to meet everyone! I'm so excited about telling all of you all about my favorite things - and especially about my obsession with yams! Isn't everyone obsessed with yams?"
Nigeria will soon have the opportunity to find out all about Zobi and his yam obsession when the programme premieres later this year.
Sesame Square is funded by the American people through the United States Agency for International Development (USAID) to create the series and supporting outreach materials, and conduct research to evaluate the programme's impact.
Sesame Workshop is the nonprofit educational organization that revolutionized children's television programming with the landmark Sesame Street.
The Workshop produces local Sesame Street programs, seen in over 140 countries, and other acclaimed shows to help bridge the literacy gap including The Electric Company.
Beyond television, the Workshop produces content for multiple media platforms on a wide range of issues including literacy, health and military deployment. Initiatives meet specific needs to help young children and families develop critical skills, acquire healthy habits and build emotional strength to prepare them for lifelong learning.

Original_Search_Title: Orji - the Meaning of Integrity
Headline: Nigeria: Orji - the Meaning of Integrity
Body_Text:
There is something in Abia State Governor, Chief Theodore Orji that seems to distinguish him from many of his colleagues or indeed many politicians I know.
He appears to me one of the few, who still ask what I believe is a vital question that must prick the mind of every mortal being - what will people say?
To me, the essence of this vital question is substantially embedded in the bowels of morality, an ingredient, so fundamental but completely scarce in our everyday affair today, to our eternal peril.
This has resulted in a nauseating and shameful recourse to and glorification of deceit, betrayal, half truths and complete lies, if not impunity in everything we do.
Just listen to the arguments of all the contributors to the deafening ding zoning has become. How many of those talking are actually speaking the truth as they know it?
What we have is an army of barefaced apologists and unscrupulous liars, whose verbose arguments have tried continuously, though unsuccessfully, to drown the tiny voice of reason tugging at our national conscience.
Who in this country does not know that it is complete lie to say the PDP did not zone its positions? But, our leaders, amongst them, old men and women, whose attitude to life ought to be a signpost for the upcoming generations, would want everybody to believe otherwise.
It would have been different to argue that the dynamics have changed and new realities have come into play that would necessitate a deviation from the original template. But, no! They would prefer to lie about it and railroad everybody into singing the same mantra, all for personal gains, mainly filthy lucre.
Because President Goodluck Jonathan, who they are campaigning for, not because they believe in him, represents to them the face of government, the man with the knife and yam, who gives to whom he pleases, they must, to get a slice, do anything including sacrificing truth on the slaughter slab.
This is why I was completely disconcerted last Thursday, when the news broke that Orji was on his way to joining the PDP the next morning.
Now, I confess that even though I have scant regard for many of his counterparts, my sentiments toward Orji are unabashedly positive. He is one of the governors that have not disappointed me since he came into the scene.
Though my position is not a verdict on his performance in office, because I believe that should be left to those who elected him, it is simply based on the principle of working from the known to the unknown. I have severally run him through my own character evaluation device and have not found him wanting.
Ohanaeze Ndigbo, the pan-Igbo socio-cultural organisation, would have become history today, were it not for the governor. At a time when his colleagues in the South East abandoned the organisation, not because they did not believe in its principles, but because they were afraid of Obasanjo, Orji stood resolutely behind the organisation, not minding the odds.
In fact, for three years now, he is the only governor you would find at every Igbo Day celebration organised by the body every September 29. Others, would find one excuse or the other to stay away, in order to maintain their assumed good standing with outsiders, who would rather the organisation and the goals it pursues, died permanently.
That he could dare remain different and uphold his belief in these demonstrably practical terms, to me, defines character, more than a thousand self-effusive words by his contemporaries, who speak from both sides of their mouths on any given issue; not out of conviction but to advance personal goals.
Just before his defection to APGA, he was still resolute that it was PPA or nothing. In fact, he took out time to lecture me on the import of maintaining principles and clear focus in politics, when I spoke to him about it. That was until the party itself forced him out, by its wacky and completely asinine posturing a few days after.
So, even though he was aware of the dangers of running in his former party, with his deputy practically taunting him about it, he was still determinedly mindful about the implication of jumping ship. That again, to me, defines character.
I have since come back to Lagos satisfied, having witnessed his encounter with the PDP firsthand. It is not because I am sure he is not going into the PDP eventually, but because in the thick of all the hullaballoo, the governor appears to still remember the son of whom he is.
His response to the PDP blitz, last Friday, assured me that he is not in a hurry to take an action that would rubbish him and what he stands for at the end of the day.
I have known the new PDP National Chairman, Dr. Okwesilieze Nwodo, since he was a governor, close to two decades ago, as a man that has his way with words. I'm sure a less assertive character would have capitulated instantly to his sheer power of speech.
That Orji didn't take the plunge as many who were present at the event must have envisaged, I must confess, actually made my day. Instead of swallowing the bait firsthand, he touched on the fundamental issue I had expected him to - Integrity. "There are a lot of things involved in this (request). The first one is integrity," Orji had told his visitors.
I'm sure he would have got more than a thousand telephone calls from his friends immediately the story broke, who would have told him that he was about to commit political suicide, because, apart from the fact that moving into another party so soon after joining APGA would have done great damage to his reputation, the PDP is, actually bad business in the South East, if truth be told.
Now he has decided to look before he leaps, he would have many days more to ruminate on all available options. For instance, during this period of reflections and consultations, he would be told, if he has forgotten that the choice Nwodo is offering; "mainstream" politics and unity under PDP, is actually where the zone is hurrying out from. Did it bring any gain apart from emphasising the fact of its irrelevance in the scheme of things in the country? Wouldn't it be better to try unity under a regional platform, which would guarantee the region's identity and pride even if nothing else, since it has been proven that head or tail it would still lose out in terms of recognition at the centre - a case of taking its destiny in its hand?
I may be imagining things. But I thought Abia PDP stakeholders at the event were either keeping straight faces or practically flinching at the glowing tributes of Nwodo towards the governor, which indicated to me that they had their own plans. That is for those who were there. What about those absent, known to be Orji's sworn political enemies? How would PDP factor them in? Where would he go if the PDP brings him out and denies him the ticket for a second term?
Yes, we hear that he is being prodded by President Goodluck Jonathan to join the PDP bandwagon to beef up the numbers at the party's coming national convention, to give him the 2011 presidential ticket in a planned South East - South South alliance. But what are the guarantees for his own political goals, beyond being a good man?
In APGA, not only would this ticket be assured, he would be running on a popular platform. But in PDP, the reverse would be the case. He would simply be swimming against the tide. How much is the President willing to help?
For instance, Abia's poor state of finances today, is traced substantially, to the Federal Government removing it from those that got debt forgiveness under former President Olusegun Obasanjo, because of the issues he had with Orji's predecessor, Orji Uzor Kalu.
I would expect that the first place to start is for the Federal Government to take over this debt burden and free the state from this bondage, so it would have some funds to tackle some of its serious infrastructural problems.
How much weapon would it put into Orji's hand if it decides, for instance to pave some major roads in the state, such as Aba-Port-Harcourt Road or makes its presence felt in other ways in practical terms, by which the governor would have something to point at to remain in people's hearts?
Politics is all about discussions, persuasion and agreement. Nothing in it is static. That is why Abdel Baset al-Megrahi, the convicted Libyan Lockebie bomber is a free man today, because in politics, just like in international relations, there is always a deal.
I don't think Orji should take this plunge. I pray that he doesn't. But, since I don't have all the facts, I would want to see practical signs that it would be for good. Else, everything else is phantom. He would have sacrificed all he has and given his enemies room to have the last laugh; which will be a tragedy.

Original_Search_Title: LIRS Boss Tasks Financial Sector Workers On Productivity
Headline: Nigeria: LIRS Boss Tasks Financial Sector Workers On Productivity
Body_Text:
The Chairman, Lagos State Board of Internal Revenue Services (LIRS), Mr. Babatunde Fowler has called on operators in the nation's financial sector to adopt a healthy lifestyle, as this will help enhance their productivity.
Speaking at a health seminar organised for workers in Lagos, Fowler noted that it is important that workers are educated on health management issues, as this will help them deliver on set targets and contribute meaningfully in the achievement of the objectives of their various organisations.
He said that a healthy workforce will deliver optimum result and urged both private and government agencies to promote initiatives tailored towards healthy living among workforce.
The guest speaker, a physician from George Kerry Life Foundation, Dr Maltida Kerry enjoined LIRS workers to adopt a healthy lifestyle in order to guard against cardiovascular diseases such as hypertension, diabetes, heart diseases and stroke
Kerry pointed out that stroke in particular was quite dangerous, described it as sudden death of the brain due to lack of oxygen.
According to her, stroke leads to inability to smile, talk and raise arms just as it caused difficulty with balance and sudden severe headache, , adding that the disease is caused by the blockage of blood flow or rupture of an artery to the brain
On the issue of diabetes, Kerry noted that the disease was chronic, which is difficult to cure but could be managed by keeping blood sugar levels as close to normal as possible
"Diabetic patients should reduce starchy foods such as potatoes, yams, garri and white bread. They should rather go for multi-grain, whole grain and whole wheat breads. Legumes (beans, peas), nuts, chicken, fish, fish oils, eggs, avocadoes, olives, fruits and vegetables," she explained
She urged them to avoid sodas and manufactured juices, adding that they should limit their consumption of sweet and chocolates.
"They should also control their blood pressure and cholesterol. Exercise is also very important and people who are obese should try to lose weight. Sex is also a very good form of exercise because a session of sex burns out 200 calories which is equivalent to 15 minutes on a treadmill."
Some of the workers who expressed their view opined that the seminar was worthwhile as it opened their eyes to preventable diseases that cause early death.

Original_Search_Title: How Sincere Are Prison Decongestion Programmes?
Headline: Nigeria: How Sincere Are Prison Decongestion Programmes?
Body_Text:
Lagos — In spite of huge expenditures and high-level government interventions, Nigerian prisons remain overpopulated. This has led to suggestions in some quarters that decongestion programmes are failing because the intentions behind them are not sincere.
Prisons all over the world are set up by law to ensure restraint and custody of individuals accused of or convicted for violating the criminal law of the state. In Nigeria, like in many other places, the prison system dates back to the colonial era and it is modelled after the British system. It is a system that lays emphasis on punishment and deterrence.
Its noble goals are to reform those who pass through its gates and to protect the society. It also has the duty to keep, in safe custody, persons legally sentenced to jail and identify the causes of their inherent anti-social behaviour, treat and reform them to become law-abiding citizens.
The prison also has the responsibility of training inmates in trades that will make them useful to themselves and the society at large. But many observers have argued that this is not the case in Nigeria. They contend that instead of reforming the inmates, the prison system hardens them; subjecting them to horrible, degrading punishments, sometimes far beyond the level of the crimes committed, leaving them at the end of the day, physically and psychologically drained, unwanted, unloved and abandoned in an uncaring environment.
Over the years, the deplorable conditions in the country's prisons have remained worrisome and no serious attempt has been made by successive governments to improve the plight of detainees and other inmates. Records have it that the Kirikiri Maximum Security Prison for instance is overcrowded by 250 percent. The prison, which was built for 956 inmates, is occupied by over 2,600 inmates. To make this worse, majority of these inmates are awaiting trial.
Prison overcrowding is a major concern of the Nigerian criminal justice system hence the agitation to decongest them. There is a significant population of remand prisoners in all prisons in the country. A greater part of awaiting trial detainees in the nation's prisons are held under the holding charge and many have spent up to ten years waiting to be tried. According to a recent report by Amnesty International, more than three of every five prison inmates in Nigeria have not been convicted of any offence; instead they wait years for their trial in appalling conditions.
Reports obtained by THISDAY reveal that the total prison population in the country is 46,000, of which some 30,000 are awaiting trial and more than 800 are on death row, including women and juveniles. Only a very few of the inmates, according to the reports, can afford a lawyer, while the government funded Legal Aid Council has inadequate number of lawyers to effectively provide the needed legal aid to the inmates.
Many analysts have argued that the reasons for prison congestion are the reckless disregard of Section 35 of the 1999 Constitution and the indiscretion of judges and magistrates handling some offences. While the section states that "a person shall be tried within two months from the date of his detention," judges and magistrates most often give stringent bail conditions on some simple criminal offences.
For instance, recently, the Edo State Chief Judge, Justice Cromwell Idahosa, warned Magistrates in the state to desist from giving stringent bail conditions on simple criminal offences and unlawful detention. He handed down this warning when he concluded his annual visit to prison formations across the state in Auchi, Estako West local government council.
Idahosa was surprised when he learnt about the case of one Mrs. Mofe Adebanjo who allegedly stole coco-yam and palm kernels and was granted bail by an Uzebba Magistrate Court in the sum of N300, 000 with two sureties who are to be gainfully employed civil servants and house owners.
He directed that 15 inmates awaiting trial should be discharged if the Officer-in-Charge of legal and Director for Public Prosecution in the Ministry of Justice could not produce their case files after three years. He also freed three others and apologized to one of them, Mr Apeji Austine, who was discovered to have been unlawfully detained since October 2008 for conspiracy, while the main suspect has been freed.
Other factors responsible for this monumental injustice are lack of adequate funding, slow dispensation of justice and the large number of awaiting trial inmates, congestion in courts as a result of slow judicial process as well as inadequate structures to accommodate inmates.
The latest sorry state of affairs in the nation's prisons was revealed recently by the new Minister of Interior, Capt. Emmanuel Iheanacho, who while on a visit to Kuje Prison, Abuja, expressed shock at what he saw. The minister, who was confronted with a myriad of woes in the prison by the inmates, apologised to them on behalf of the government. One of the inmates, who summoned up courage, told him that some of his colleagues have been awaiting trial for over ten years. The inmate blamed the country's judicial system for their woes, adding that "the condition of bail is too much, it is unfair. The judicial system is not doing very well."
Iheanacho, whose ministry oversees the Nigeria Prisons Service (NPS), was touched by the plight of the prisoners. He promised to liaise with the Ministry of Justice to find a lasting solution to the congestion in the prisons. He also promised that before his 12-13 month tenure ends, the issue of overcrowding in the Nigerian prisons would become history. He promised better conditions for prisoners both while and after serving their term, noting that his main objective on the challenges facing the prisons is to find ways to ensure that inmates use the time they spend in confinement to improve their chances of becoming law-abiding, well adjusted, and contributing members of society after serving their sentences.
"I want to say sorry to you guys. I will hold discussions with my colleague in the ministry of justice to chart the way forward. The fundamental challenge is in the cell; if you take an analysis of people who are under encapsulation, the majority of them are not convicts. Having 30,000 people waiting for trial is unacceptable. We have to find quickest administrative way to solve this problem," he said.
On his part, the Attorney General and Minister of Justice, Mr. Mohammed Adoke, (SAN), constituted a nine-member committee headed by the Solicitor General/Permanent Secretary of the Federal Ministry of Justice to revisit the issue of prisons congestion across the country. A statement credited to Adoke's Personal Assistant on Media and Publicity, Mr. Onyema Omenuwa, said: "The minister is particularly bothered by the controversies that have dogged the programme since it was introduced in 2005 and as such, upon assuming office last week, resolved to immediately set the necessary machinery in motion to reform the programme, considering that the anomalies the programme was set up to address are yet to be eliminated."
Observers are wondering what the previous committees set up by successive Attorneys-General of the Federation achieved with the different committees they set up during their tenures. They allege that instead of proposing how to reform the administration of justice and addressing the root causes of the problem, the AGFs decided to play politics of settlement.
The observers express concern that even the current AGF's committee would neither solve the problems of prison congestion nor ameliorate it. They suggest that what should be done is to fasten justice delivery and reform the justice administration sector. They stress the need to fast-track cases and stop stringent bail conditions for simple offences.
One observer, who spoke to THISDAY on condition of anonymity recently advised that the enormous resources expended on past prison decongestion programmes should be investigated and culprits brought to book. He expressed surprise that some people could be benefiting from keeping awaiting trial inmates in jail indefinitely only to reap from setting up frequent committees. He submitted that the country's correctional facilities would not live up to their name if policy-makers keep playing politics with prison reform or show more interest in the money they make out of the process.
The observer wondered why it is always the Federal Ministry of Justice that sets up committees to decongest the prisons when, in actual fact, the Nigeria Prisons Service (NPS) is not a parastatal under it but the Ministry of Interior.
"Come to think of it, is the Nigeria Prisons Service (NPS) under the Federal Ministry of Justice or under the Ministry of Interior? How come each time it is the Ministry of Justice that sets up committees to decongest the prisons? A lot of people do not know why we have problems with the prisons. Is it not because the Federal Ministry of Justice cannot put in place a good and proper reform in justice administration?"
According to him, "that prison decongestion has been politicised is no longer in doubt; it has become a way of settling friends and political associates at the detriment of the prisons. We are all eye witnesses to the bogus and meaningless decongestion exercises of former AGFs. In spite of the whopping sum put into that programme, the money ended up in the pockets of friends and political associates with no job done. Some who got part of the largesse pretended to be doing prison decongestion projects without any aim or purpose. The enormous resources used in past prison decongestion programmes must be investigated and culprits brought to book and punished. The time to put an end to politics of prison decongestion is now."

Original_Search_Title: Between Octopus Paul And Nigerian Juju Men And Pastors
Headline: Nigeria: Between Octopus Paul And Nigerian Juju Men And Pastors
Body_Text:
One of the celebrities thrown up by the just concluded World Cup tournament in South Africa is the German Octopus Paul. The two-year-old psychic cephalopod achieved global fame for correctly predicting all of Germany's World Cup matches, including their two defeats by Spain and Serbia. It also successfully tipped Spain to win the World Cup - predictions that reportedly led to the mollusc receiving death threats from Dutch fans as it did from German supporters in the two occasions it successfully predicted German defeats. Surprisingly, the animal announced its retirement just a day after the conclusion of the World Cup.
There are a number of lessons from Octopus Paul.
One, belief in divination and other paranormal experiences is not an exclusively African thing, as is sometimes wrongly presented in the Western media. In addition to Octopus Paul, there are TV stations in Europe that are dedicated to broadcasting occult practices and paranormal experiences. Wives of some prominent politicians in the West have also been rumoured to be avid customers of those who claim to possess supernatural powers. In many African countries, even though the belief in occult and paranormal phenomena is pervasive, very few people want to be openly identified with them - for fear of being thought primitive. Will the open resort to divination in the West during the World Cup (as the case of Octopus showed) remove the stigma attached to such practices in Africa?
Two, the method used by Octopus Paul was transparent: it got the choice of picking food from two different transparent containers lowered into his tank - one with a German flag on it and one with the opponent's flag. The container Paul opens first is regarded as his pick. Another creature that achieved a celebrity status during the World Cup, though less celebrated than Octopus Paul, was a parakeet, Mani, who in far away Singapore was said to have correctly predicted the results of the last five but one matches in the World Cup tournament. Mani's method was also transparent: the 13-year-old parakeet would grab a card in his beak bearing the flag of the country it was predicting to win.
It is tempting to speculate on what would have happened if Octopus Paul was owned by a Nigerian, and the animal correctly predicted the outcome of one or two matches during the World Cup. It is likely that the owner would paint his face and eye lashes with the weirdest chalk around, build a mysterious grove for the creature (and if possible let decomposing corpses litter the pathway to the shrine) and spend the better part of an hour chanting incantations whenever any customer showed up. Of course he would only enter the shrine with his back, and tell every customer that the octopus was a gift from his great grand father or from a benevolent Water Mermaid. Were Octopus Paul owned by a Nigerian, we would by now have had priests and worshippers of the poor creature! Additionally, unlike Octopus Paul whose psychic power was apparently limited to predicting football matches, the Nigerian owner would claim the octopus would predict any event, heal any disease and infirmity and even tell you the person 'blocking' your success in life.
Three, despite the successes of Octopus Paul, it is unlikely to convince most Europeans that the alleged psychic abilities of the mollusc is reliable. For instance though he had a 100 percent success rate at the World Cup, of the six predictions he made during the 2008 European championship, he got only four correct. Similarly Mani wrongly picked the Netherlands to defeat Spain at the finals of the World Cup. For many, Europeans, yes, Octopus Paul has a high success rate, but he is unreliable.
Unlike Europeans, in Nigeria, the mere fact that the octopus achieved 100 percent success rate at the World Cup will mean that whatever he says (or is contrived to have said), will henceforth be taken as gospel truth - not mere prediction with a reasonable chance of error. How many diviners and pastors have created eternal enmity in families and communities by fingering people who are probably innocent, as the cause of other people's misfortunes? By forcing practitioners (including pastors who claim healing and miracle powers) to make their methods more transparent to encourage public interrogation of such claims, the people will be better served, and abuses and bogus claims checked.
Four, anything that has a name probably exists in one form or the other so I am not totally discountenancing the existence of esoteric phenomena and occult practices. But there is something that does not seem right the way these claims are bandied around in Nigeria. Tales of occult practices - of people who could make your manhood disappear simply by shaking your hands, of women who could use 'love potion' to ensnare you into marrying them or to do their wishes, of people turning into yam tubers simply from wearing Okada helmets - are a daily staple instilling fears, even paranoia in the hearts of many. Often opportunities to raise questions about the supposed powers of these 'forces' are missed. For instance even though many people tremble when some shrines like Okija are mentioned, we do know that people like Dr Chris Ngige, former Governor of Anambra State, and Governor Theodore Orji of Abia State who apparently swore an oath of allegiance to their godfathers at the shrine fell out with these god fathers without anything apparently happening to them. Don't we deserve an explanation from the administrators or priests of the Okija shrine - to help other clients hedge their risks? In the same vein, the curse famously placed by the Oba of Benin on kidnappers in the state does not seem to have stopped the menace of the hoodlums in the state. Doesn't the public deserve to ask the Oba some questions about this curse, and why it doesn't seem to have worked? This point is important because there are many people who consult oracles and accept whatever they are thought to have said as gospel truth - without any question on the method they used to arrive at their conclusions or the level of error in their judgments. There remain to this day illiterate and poor villagers who are forced to swear to deities and to accept whatever judgment or punishment they purportedly hand out - even though they do not understand how such forces work. The same is also true of people who rely on the jurisprudence of their pastors as intermediaries between them and God.
Five, though Octopus Paul has retired, the celebrity status it acquired during the World Cup is likely to spur further research on the psychic abilities of molluscs. There will certainly be books on the invertebrate and its psychic powers. How many books have been written about such phenomena in our country to inform or encourage debate? But come to think of it: what is really the difference between what the Asians proudly package and market as yoga and some of our traditional religious practices? Is there any difference between those Asians market as 'gurus', 'earth masters', 'holy men' and our 'babalawos', 'dibias' and 'juju men'?
We can only tap into the positive aspects of psychic phenomena and occult practices by removing the stigmas that are attached to them and encouraging public scrutiny of claims.

Original_Search_Title: Gadi's Return And Bauchi Politics
Headline: Nigeria: Gadi's Return And Bauchi Politics
Body_Text:
Bauchi — Time and time again, the power and role of deputy governors have been questioned by their bosses across the federation, and it would seem there would be no end to the humiliations that some of them have to endure.
Garba Gadi's case remains one other instance which has made political analysts take proper interest in the politics surrounding the office of deputies to state chief executives. In Bauchi State, where Gadi is struggling to clear his name and maintain his principled stance against political prostitution, the fight appears quite daunting for the victim of power play.
Joint Ticket Under ANPP
It all started in 2007, when Isa Yuguda was denied ticket by the People Democratic Party (PDP) to contest the governorship election in Bauchi State, a development that made him to defect to All Nigerian Peoples Party (ANPP), where he then picked Alhaji Garba Gadi as his running mate and both won the election on the platform of the new party.
The victory, which has been hailed in some quarters as one of the nation's bests in recent times, due to the massive support it enjoyed, some say 90 per cent in favour of the victorious ticket, is now a source of sour grapes, one, which is not only adding to the ugly side of Nigerian democracy, but promoting the dangers strewn along its way.
Barely two years in office, Yuguda, had decided to return to PDP in June 2009, a party he belonged when he was Minster for Aviation and Minister of State for Transport under President Olusegun Obasanjo's administration, before contesting for Governor.
According to Yuguda, when he concluded arrangement for his defection, he had no problem with the PDP leadership but the immediate past Governor of the state, Adamu Muazu, who used his influence as the then governor to deny him PDP ticket.
Unfortunately, his deputy, Garba Gadi, who was his running mate, was not convinced about the move and vowed to remain in ANPP that brought him to power. Yuguda, undaunted, moved along, nonetheless, alongside 17 legislators from the State House of Assembly, including the Speaker.
The development caused a lot of controversy in the polity, with the ANPP protesting profusely. Its National Chairman, Edwin Ume-Ezeoke headed straight to court to seek the relinquishing of office by the defectors, while his then PDP counterpart, Prince Vincent Ogbulafor applauding it.
The Stand Off
But, if Gadi had believed that his decision to take a different route from his boss was going to be without consequences, he was mistaking, as he soon found out. Few months after the mass return to the PDP, the State House of Assembly commenced his impeachment on grounds of gross misconduct.
Following the notice of his impeachment subsequently served on him by the House, the State Chief Judge, Justice Suleiman Darazo, inaugurated a seven member impeachment panel to investigate the allegations.
The House in their submission listed four offences, which included allegations that during the 2008 Hajj operations, he claimed double estacodes in respect of a single trip he made as the head of the state's delegation and that when he was serving as a Commissioner for Land and Survey, about N2.2 million kept under his custody was found to be missing.
The House also alleged that the deputy governor sponsored a local football tournament in his name using public funds estimated to be about N500,000.000 as well as accused him of awarding contract over the amount he was empower to.
While inaugurating the panel, Darazo, urged members of the committee to thoroughly investigate the offence because, according to him, it was a constitutional matter which members must take time to establish.
Immediately after the inauguration, the committee commenced sitting and gave an assurance that all the parties involved in the matter would be given fair hearing.
But the defence team led by Abubakar Malami (SAN) opposed the constitution of the membership of the Panel headed by Abdul Babayo Malle.
He argued that the panel was not properly constituted, considering that the provisions of the constitution, with regards to such exercises, was not duly followed, just as he questioned the inclusion of two members of the panel whom he claimed were card-carrying members of a duly registered political party in the country.
He based his argument on the constitutional provision that members of the panel must be "people of unquestionable character and proven integrity." Other requirements were that the panel members should not hold membership of any political party and should not be civil servants who are in the employment of the state government. Declaring that two of the members did not have the mentioned qualities and so were not qualified to be members of the panel, he called for their disqualification.
The lawyer, further argued that for there to be fairness and justice in the whole exercise the accuser and accused should be made to come face-to-face with each other to enable them ask each other some salient but pertinent questions that would shed more light on the matter because arguing that one of the members of the panel was once a deputy governorship candidate of one of the registered political parties while the other one contested for the members of the House of Representatives for Darazo\Ganjuwa Federal Constituency of the State.
Based on this the panel was dissolved and another one was inaugurated.
According to then Speaker, Babayo Garba, the first panel constituted by the state Chief Judge was dissolved by the House because there was a lapse in the procedure for constituting the committee.
"That was the reason we had to advise the Chief Judge to correct the mistake so as not to allow room for subverting the process and did not do that to snub the respected jurist." Consequent upon the dropping of one of the panelists, the members had their report, which found the deputy governor guilty, which enabled the House to bring down the hammer.
But not after the deputy governor had protested his innocence most vehemently. As he was doing so, he was also reaffirming his determination to remain in ANPP, in spite what he stood to lose. In fact, he claimed to have received calls and messages from some prominent personalities who advised him to resign so as to get his full benefits that included N100 million, an advice he claimed to have rejected.
"It would be most dishonorable for me to resign now when a panel has been set up for investigation. People have been visiting and calling advising me that I should resign, an option which they claim is more honorable. But to my mind that will be most dishonorable because if somebody has accused you of misdeeds and then you resign because of that you are just confirming that you are guilty. I believe I am not guilty.
"I will never be found wanting; I have served as a civil servant for 32 years and I have never in anyway been indicted; so, I have no fear, whatever anybody may have said to indict me; after all, I never had intention of contesting for the election but God made it and I believe that same God will fight for me."
The Impeachment
But all these failed to persuade his traducers. On August 13, 2009, the deed was concluded as he was impeached by the Assembly.
The Majority Leader of the House, Dayyabu Chiroma, who read the report for its adoption, reminded the members that the submission of the panel was not debatable as enshrined in section 188 of the Constitution and was duly seconded by the deputy Majority Leader, Auwal Mohammed Jatau.
Gamawa then called for voting through the raising of hands by members, because voice votes were not advisable in such matters.
At the end of the counting, 25 members supported the adoption of the report which translated into removal in accordance with the Constitution, while only five members opposed with one member abstaining. The House has 31 members.
Chiroma, had told the House that Gadi was found guilty of all the allegations against him with the exception of one which the House could not substantiate with any written concrete evidence and so submitted that the action of the Deputy Governor was in line with his office and so cannot be penalised for taking such an action.
According to the report: "No matter the explanation of the deputy governor that certain approvals were done due to exigencies it could not change the fact that there was outright negligence and disregard of government laid-down financial policies, the result of which normally creates nonchalance, disregard for some norms and practice of civil service by some government officials down the ladder. Therefore, the action of the deputy governor on these approvals was found by us to be ultra vires and overreached his powers in deliberate disregard for government's policy. Therefore, the allegations are proved."
Based on this, he was impeached. The next day Gamawa, was sworn in as the Deputy Governor.
But the deputy governor's lawyers, described the exercise as unconstitutional, and argued that the new panel did not listen to the deputy governor's view before submitting their report.
Usman, his lead counsel, stated that the court, which was presided over by Justice Mohammed Zango ordered the removal of one of the members of the panel, Ibrahim Abdulahi, who he said was not qualified to be a member of the panel having been an active politician who contested for the post of a deputy governor in the last general election under the platform of Democratic People Party (DPP) and he had not resigned his membership of the party.
Usman stated further that the panel was supposed to serve his client with a notice and allow him to respond before taking any further action, adding that the deputy governor had travelled out of the state because he was on leave and would resume on August 17.
His words: "My client applied for 10 days leave, which was approved by the Executive Governor, Isa Yuguda, so the panel was supposed to hear from my client who will resume office next week to respond, because constitutionally the notice was supposed to be served on him personally and not through any medium.
On the next action to be taken by his client, Usman stated: "I can only advise my client to appeal if he ask me because he is the one aggrieved so if he tells me to appeal, I will do that.
"I believe every rightful person will go on appeal because it is stigmatisation. He has been accused of committing criminal offence. So, any responsible person will want to go upward and clear his name."
Usman expressed optimism that his client would succeed.
It was perhaps based on this assurance that Gadi went ahead to challenge his impeachment.
His dogged stance and action to pursue his case have since paid off in a most dramatic way. It did barely 10 months after the impeachment, when the state High court ordered his re- instatement on the June 25, 2010. The court presided over by Justice Haruna Tsamani at state High court 4 declared that his removal by the House based on the recommendation or report of the panel when the matter was still pending in court was unconstitutional.
He stated further that the removal of the plaintiff, based on the recommendation of the defendant, when the plaintiff was not given a fair hearing, was unconstitutional and therefore null and void.
The judge did not end in reversing the action but had harsh words for the House, which he delivered thus: "It is a shameful thing that the law making body should breach the law of fair hearing by not giving the deputy Governor, Garuba Gadi ample opportunity to defend himself before his removal in office."
Judicial Reinstatement
The court ordered his immediate reinstatement and that all his financial emoluments from the date of his removal from office till his re instatement be paid.
But, despite this order the entire development was to be steeped into a huge drama. On Monday, June 27, Gadi had gone to resume office in line with the judgement. He had arrived his office at about 7am in a brown Toyota Camry salon car with his security men and was allowed into the premises by the security men at the gate.
But the drama began when he could not get access into the main office of the Deputy Governor as it was under lock.
He then decided to stay at the waiting hall pending when he would gain entrance into the office he once occupied before his ordeal began.
Because no other person was allowed in, including reporters, he had to speak to the anxious journalists across the fence of the office. And his words were clear: "I am speaking as the deputy governor of Bauchi State; as you can see, I have resumed my duties as the deputy governor because the court gave me judgment. I am right now in the office and will do my duties as provided in the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria. Yes, my security has been restored because the state police command has given me security cover."
But the situation was to change the next day when he was barred from entering the same office on the alleged orders of the state Commissioner of Police Danlami Yaradua, who later denied issuing it. "I did not tell anybody to stop him from entering the office and if any policemen did, he did that on his own."
It was actually learnt that the subsequent action by a palpably embarrassed government of the state was that an appeal was already in court and that should put the matter in abeyance until the court decided further. But Gadi's counsel, Ado Dauda disagreed, saying the position of the court was that mere appeal on a matter could not serve as a stay of execution.
In his words: "That pronouncement of the court takes immediate effect and you cannot in law stay an execution of that judgment, so the issue of filing a motion on notice for stay - we could say they are exercising their right, but it is not automatic that once they file the motion, it takes effect as to stay the execution of the order of the court. That is the position of the law.
"So to bar the reinstated deputy governor from entering his office is an act of the contempt of the valid order of the court. It is going to attract some punitive measures by the court, and we have already applied to the court for an order to punish them for that contemptuous act, the process is being processed now in court."
However, counsel to the state government, Muktar Abubakar Usman, said he had served the impeached Gadi with the notice of appeal and motion on notice praying for execution of the judgment.
He said: "In law, by virtue of a Supreme Court judgment, if it is decided that a litigant filed an appeal against a judgment and also filed a motion for stay of execution, whether at the lower court or at the court of appeal, as we did in our own case, the judgment cannot be executed, the filing of the motion of stay of execution is an automatic stay of execution of the judgment, pending the determination of that motion for stay of execution, and if the motion of stay of execution is determined in favour of the applicant, the order will now last pending the determination of the appeal filed.
"Now the impeached deputy governor, who now intruded into his office actually did what we call criminal trespass because there is already a stay of execution by virtue of the motion filed and served on him."
The Stalemate
However, the same court was to resolve the issue when the case came before it on July 2. There, Tsanmani struck out the motion for stay of execution.
While reaffirming his position for the immediate reinstatement of the deputy governor, he said the Attorney General of the State, Mustapha Suleiman, had earlier on applied for the withdrawal of the motion filed before the Court on June 25, 2010.
Though he did not give any reason for the withdrawal of the suit, the Court went ahead to grant the application and thereby struck out the case with a N10, 000 cost to the respondents whose lawyers led by Mamman Mike Osuma (SAN) had earlier on opposed the application for withdrawal because, according to him, they had already filed a counter motion before the court.
But another twist was to come into the matter when both Gadi and Gamawa were asked to stay away from the office pending the final determination of all the cases instituted.
Yar'adua, explained that the decision for the directive was based on the need to have peace in the state, pointing out that if the logjam was allowed to continue, it might lead to breach of the peace.
He said the decision was not taken by the "It was a collective decision taken by the security operatives in the state, including the police, the military, the SSS, and the NSCDC in the presence of all the contending parties at a meeting held at the state headquarters of the SSS. It was done so that there will be peace in the state that is why the office is locked."
Next day, the same police were denying giving such a directive against the backdrop of a barrage of condemnations from a groundswell of Nigerians, particularly the civil rights groups and opposition political parties, who saw it as not only an affront and infraction on the law but a needless interpretation of a relevant court order by parties not empowered to do so. It is actually believed that the entire bizarre episode was already embarrassing the presidency, which gave directives to all the parties, particularly the police to do the right thing.
That may have led to Gadi finally getting access to his office last week. Yar'Adua, had explained that the command received directive from the legal department of Force Headquarters, instructing the police to restore full security details to the beleaguered deputy governor pending the determination of the case on stay of execution.
Incidentally, the other Gamawa, retains his security and office as well.
Thus there is at the moment some people still see a political stalemate in the state, because Gadi may as well wear the toga of deputy governor, while Gamawa does the major work, since the governor holds the proverbial yam and knife and decides who to give a piece at the end of the day.
But to observers, the perks of office may not be what Gadi is after, but the politics of the state after the imbroglio.
Indeed, many analysts believe the larger picture is the 2011 governorship election, where he could be a beautiful bride for opponents of Yuguda, who had in recent times been facing a lot of onslaughts from his opponents.
Indeed, they believe that what is playing out in the state is a case of the bird dancing on the road having the drummers somewhere in the bush. That the deputy governor has carried out the fight so far, is believed to be a function of the tremendous support he is getting from those who want Yuguda out of power. Therefore, what he loses now in terms of money and power, he is likely to gain in future by his doggedness. That is if the opposition is able to upturn the governor. If the impending court decision comes in his favour, then he will have succeeded in upholding what he believes in.
Conversely, if Yuguda is able to make it back to government house by next year, his erstwhile friend and deputy, would have lost it all politically, were he to apply the usual Nigerian measure, except he adores his moral successes.
To many, it is not known what the entire development would translate into especially now that the PDP is under a new chairman, Dr. Okwesilieze Nwodo.

Original_Search_Title: Edo Chief Judge Warns Against Stringent Bail Condition
Headline: Nigeria: Edo Chief Judge Warns Against Stringent Bail Condition
Body_Text:
Beni — Magistrate in Edo State have been warned to desist from giving stringent bail conditions on simple criminal offences and unlawful detention.
Edo State Chief Judge, Justice Cromwell Idahosa, handed down this warning when he concluded his annual visit to prison formations across the state in Auchi, Estako West Local Govern-ment council.
Idahosa was surprised when he learnt about the case of one Mrs Mofe Adebanjo who allegedly stole coco-yam and palm kernels.
The Uzebba Magistrate Court granted her bail with an option of N300,000 fine with two surety who are to be gainfully employed civil servants, house owner, and two passports each.
The CJ who visited the New Auchi 168 bed Medium Security Prison noted that some magistrate's courts located in rural communities were strict with their bail conditions on simple offences and unlawful detention.
Idahosa directed that 15 inmates awaiting trial shoud be discharged if the Officer-in-Charge of legal and Director for Public Prosecution in the Ministry of Justice could not produce their case files after three years.
He also freed three others and apologized to one of them, Mr Apeji Austine, who was discovered to have been unlawfully detained since Oct 2008 for conspiracy, while the main suspect has been freed.

Original_Search_Title: The Return of Audu Ogbeh
Headline: Nigeria: The Return of Audu Ogbeh
Body_Text:
No, it is not the Audu Ogbeh you know that has returned. The name of the one I have in mind is Okwesilieze Nwodo. He did something on July 7 which threw me back into the past, into the arms of Audu Ogbeh. What both of them have in common is the PDP, the ruling Peoples Democratic Party of Nigeria, which they have both led.
Nwodo was secretary of the party in 1999 and I was in Jos when the primaries to pick the flag bearer for the party were held at the stadium there. Nwodo says the fairness of those days are back during his tenure. But for reasons we do not need here, because they will interfere with what is so important that we had to drag in Audu Ogbeh as metaphor, I would concentrate on what Nwodo has done this time around and what Ogbeh did.
