Expectedly, oil majors including Shell with headquarters in The Netherlands shunned the peace parley. That didn't stop The Friend of the Earth (Milieu Defensie) from reaffirming its commitment to prosecute Shell Nigeria for environmental degradation in Niger Delta.
A spokesman of the non-governmental organization, Mr. Geert Ritsema said his group and Nigeria's Environmental Rights Action (ERA) are collaborating to bring Shell to justice.
Ritsema said the suit before a Dutch judge focused on the failure of Shell to respond speedily to oil spillage menace in Niger Delta, gas flaring and demand for compensation for victims of oil spillages.
Shell Nigeria has been severally criticized for its poor handling of oil issues in Niger Delta.
MOSOP, for instance, believes that the oil major cannot be an innocent party in the hanging of its leader and celebrated playwright, Ken Saro Wiwa by late General Sani Abacha's junta in 1995. Since then, Shell had been declared persona non grata in Ogoni land, Rivers State.
HOSPITALITY AND MUSIC
Big misses of The Hague parley, notwithstanding, participants from Nigeria savoured African culinary, courtesy of Comrade and Mrs. Ofehe. It was a big deal dumping 'Oyibo food' for Nigerian dishes. Ask Dokubo-Asari, INC President, Keyamo and others, it was quite refreshing feasting on pounded yam, ogbono and other soup in Rotterdam.
Biting cold and 25 kilometre drive from The Hague to Rotterdam are no hitches for the conferees to savour the Nigerian delicacies. Mother's pot indeed.
In a dinner to end the conference, rastaman, Stereoman Ekwe was in his best musical element dishing out local Nigerian songs to the delight of the participants.
that night (february 26) at crown plaza hotels
The Hague, Ofehe deployed his talent to complement Ekwe's music. The activist was graceful in churning out rib-cracking jokes to the delight of guests.
EPILOGUE
The conference over, it is the burning desire of the Niger Delta stakeholders that Nigerian government and the international community would for once summon the political will to arrest the drift in the oil-rich. Dokubo-Asari's alternative to peaceful dialogue - arms struggle- has dire consequence for the oppressed and the oppressor.
That option is no tea party. Hope the political elite and oil companies would allow commonsense to prevail in tackling Niger Delta question.

Original_Search_Title: I Was in South Africa
Headline: Nigeria/South Africa: I Was in South Africa
Body_Text:
So what? Yes, so what?
A friend in Jo'burg saw me battling with this column and said " So what is special about being in South Africa?", aware that I have passed this way a couple of times.
I explained to him that this time was obviously different. Let me start this way. As host of the official World Cup countdown programme in Nigeria, it would have been terrible to see me on the streets of Abuja or Lagos when the world, South Africa especially was counting down to the World Cup.
It was therefore the view of the programme owners and consultants that I proceed to South Africa.
On Sunday night, I quickly packed a bag, jumped into a plane and after five hours of smooth flight, found myself alighting at the impressive Oliver. R. Thambo International Airport in Jo'burg by nine O'clock in the morning.
My host and the show producer in Nigeria Lilian Williams (A South African) was waiting. Remind me to tell you about Lilian later because the person who invented the phrase "Dynamites come in little packages" must have met Lilian.
I was shepherded to this beautiful guest house called Flame Lily Inn in a serene and heavenly environment in Jo' burg. (Also remind me to contact the NFF and suggest how they can use this place for their guests and board members who may not want to distract the Eagles!). Lilian drops me off, suggests I catch some sleep and that she will be back in five hours to brief me about my mission to South Africa and so on.
I get into the room, jump into the shower and was about to start dreaming when I heard a knock on my door. Lilian. She said it was five hours already. Was I fine? I said "yes" a lie. Do I mind driving out with her to town to purchase a World Cup ball and other World Cup bits that will enhance our shoot? I said I did not mind (a lie)
We drive out, and I come alive. Savour Joburg, exciting and busy shops, as she fills me in on the four days ahead of me. We pick what we want.
I nearly emptied my purse on some very interesting Christian literature...... At about six pm, as we drive back she suggests dinner and goes on about baked potatoes and whatever..... ( I lack the courage to ask her whether there was anything near pounded yam and vegetable soup). Something is brought, we eat, pay and as I was getting ready for a night's rest, a call comes through. Lilian is excited, and goes on and on....then I hear " ....fine by me, but I will have to clear with him....."
She hangs up, faces me and says. "Paul, Stephan (our cameraman) wants to know whether we can shoot on the famous Nelson Mandela Bridge this night! "I say it is ok (true ) by now I was ready for anything. When Lilian briefed me, she had said I needed to sleep early so that we could leave Jo'burg by 6am , four hours drive by car, to be able to meet the countdown ceremony in Bloomfontein, where Nigeria will play Greece on June 17 ...now they want to do a shoot on the Mandela bridge by 8pm. Was I expected to say no?
We get to the bridge, with the stories of crime and robbery ringing in my ears. We set up, do some shoots and drive back. No crime, nothing. I do not know whether I slept..... because about thirty minutes later, Lilian was knocking on my door, saying it was 6am and that she was set for Bloom.
On our way we stop to take some shots, tell Nigerians how convenient it could be to drive down, after the match against Argentina. Then the mistake....as I mention that I saw some "beautiful cows" dotting the magnificent country side. Lilian then goes looking for cows.
Those we shoot, she is not too impressed...finally we see "beautiful cows" and Stephan jumps down, rolling away, only for Lilian to try and persuade the cows to kick the World Cup Ball (Jabulani) we were using!
Bloomfontein is fun. The countdown at the Mimosa Mall ends with the Diski dance. Celtics player Prince Olomu is our guest. Very popular here as he takes us round the field and the dressing room. You can feel the excitement in my voice as I sit where perhaps, Osaze will sit at the impressive renovated Gauteng where I first cut my teeth as a CAF media officer in 1996.
We run into the Switzerland ambassador in South Africa, His Excellency Rudolf Baerfuss, come to inspect the stadium and I tell him on camera that I wish for a rematch to avenge the U-17 defeat. He says it will not be as sweet as beating us at home!
At the zoo we go looking for the mascot, Zakumi, who incidentally is a leopard. Could not find him, but we find a group of excited Nigerians in downtown Bloomfontein, they cannot wait for the arrival of fellow Nigerians. I talk to them even as Ngozi says whatever food Nigerians need will be available.
Before we know it, it is 7.30pm, time to grab something to eat and face the four hour journey back home. Then the rain.....heavy rain that slows us down...we practically crawl into Jo'burg, minutes after midnight.
I was supposed to sleep, yet another knock on my door. Lilian. Our flight to Durban is for 8am and we must be at the airport by 6.30. I manage to wake, grab a cup of coffee and off we go. The one hour flight to Durban is smooth, traffic slows our journey to the stadium.
We arrive as finishing touches are being put, ready to host the South Africa-Namibia friendly match. The South African Vice President and the FIFA President are in town, including 100 journalists from all over the world, brought in to symbolically count down the World Cup.
Our guide is very uncomfortable. She says we are late. We should shoot quick, we should not step on the grass and that since the police and security agents have swept the stadium people were not supposed to be allowed in. Lilian and Stephan are not impressed. They take their time, guaranteeing exclusive pictures of the 70,000 seater Moses Mabhida Stadium,where Nigeria will play her last group match against South Korea, 20:30 hrs on June 22.
We then walk round the stadium, run into a South African lady with a Nigerian jersey. I talk to her, only for Lilian to get this crazy idea of me doing a link in the sky car 160 metres above the stadium. I told her I had no head for heights. That I avoid buildings over five storeys high.....they will not hear. The arch incidentally is the most important feature of the stadium.
Then the disgrace. A 22-year-old girl manning the car tells me "....it is fine don't be afraid". I said I was not, jumped in and with my head spinning and my legs wobbling, the car plies the famous arch of the Wembley like stadium taking me to my death in the hands of South Africans......I survived it and have pictures to show.
We then realize we had a date with the Deputy Mayor at the City Hall, we needed to visit the much talked about Ushaka Marine World to see dolphins, seals, crocodiles......and the beautiful beach, only to discover that we were running late for our flight back by 6pm. Lilian then made frantic calls, trying to reschedule our flight in case we missed, just as Stephan desperately guided the rented car through traffic with the expert use of the GPS.
We make the flight, thank God for it as we stop for burgers for dinner (God I am famished) before boarding the flight. After landing and wading through traffic, we get in some minutes after 10pm as Lilian announces that there will be no rush for the Jo'burg shoot the next day and that call time was 9am.
I thought I was grateful. 8am, Stephan was in my Guest house, 8:30 Lilian came in and asked whether we were ready. I struggle into my pants and join them as we take off to Ellis Park where Eagles first and most important match against Greece will hold on June 12.
My guest is Idah Peterside, a good copy. We run through the stadium, relive his playing days and emotions even as we pray for a decent result against the Argies. We get someone to tell us the history of the popular surface that hosted the opening and closing final matches of the Confederation Cup.
We then proceed to town, the art related Mary Fitzerald Square, to talk to fans, sample their opinion on the match, shoot popular landmarks, had a wonderful experience at Museum Africa and the flea market,as rain, again tampered with our progress. Regretably, we miss the appointment with the Nigerian Ambassador General Buba Marwa.
If I thought shooting in Jo'burg was going to be easy, I was mistaken. Idah Peterside invites me for Football Africa programme by 9pm.
I will take time out to document my experience on this World Cup that has taken Africa by storm. Everywhere you go to, flags, balls, colour, pomp, countdown signs, sponsors and partners struggling to outdo each other especially at the airports and stadium vicinity.You can cut the fever with a knife.
South Africans count on Nigeria and other African countries to do well at the World Cup. They want to know how good the new coach is. We are condemned to win. All about my experience and adventure you can catch on my countdown programme on NTA, AIT and other network stations. Am I glad to be back?
Still on Finbarrs
Permit me acknowledge a text by Fenibo Jacks (class of '71) who said my list of Finbarrs greats was not complete without Emilio John, Gerald Ibe, Bernard Senaya, and Godwin Odiye. He also put in a word for late principal Tony Omera. Up School.

Original_Search_Title: Cattle, Food Dealers Embark On Nationwide Strike Monday
Headline: Nigeria: Cattle, Food Dealers Embark On Nationwide Strike Monday
Body_Text:
Enugu — All the umbrella unions of food and cattle dealers in Northern Nigeria have jointly declared a one week nationwide strike, starting from Monday, to protest alleged multiple taxation, extortion and other injustices meted out to them in the southern part of the country.
Prominent among the unions are Amalgamated Cattle Dealers of Nigeria, Kuri Development Association, Miyate Allah Cattle Dealers of Nigeria, Cattle Dealers Association of Nigeria and Fruits and Vegetable Merchants Association.
Speaking at a press conference in Enugu on Friday, spokesperson of the aggrieved unions, Abdulahi Shutalim, said that their members do buy goods from the northern part of Nigeria where they pay all the expected revenue and yet they are subjected to extortion and multiple taxation when transporting the products to the southern part of the country.
Shutalim who is the National Secretary of Amalgamated Cattle Dealers Association, announced that all the Northern cattle and food unions have jointly and unanimously resolved to go on a one week strike from Monday March 8, 2010, to express their grievances.
The strike, according to him, would affect supply of cattle, tomato, beans, rice, vegetables, onions, beans, chickens, maize, carrots, goats, yams and other food items, supplied to the South from the North.
Shutalim stated: "We do buy goods in Northern Nigeria and take it to the South South, South West and South East and we do pay our veterinary at the place we bought as well as state and local government revenue.

Original_Search_Title: Fasting Eliminates Waste - Experts
Headline: Nigeria: Fasting Eliminates Waste - Experts
Body_Text:
Lagos — The two major religions in the world, Islamic and Christianity at one time or the other observe fasting periods, which according to most religious leaders is a time set aside to abstain from food and water to seek the face of their creator while some argue that without fasting some prayer points may not be answered by God.
However medical experts have also revealed that fasting exercise is good for the body even as they said fasting allows the digestive system to rest and that it is also one of the quickest methods of eliminating waste. To them the kidneys, liver, lymphatic system, lungs, skin and colon do a great job but over time toxins build up in the body system and overwhelm the body's ability to remove them, "so with fasting there is quick elimination of these toxins which had built up over time and also enhancing of the body's healing process."
When fasting a number of changes occur in the body, including reduce of body toxins, improved immune function, cell growth and elimination, increased tissue oxygenation, release of chemicals stored in fat and other tissues, it also increases mental alertness. The body notices a feeling of increased energy during this period because energy is no longer focused on digesting food and as a result, healing action in the body is enhanced" say experts."
They further stated that when fasting, the body experiences a lot of healthy actions and effects which include, an increased sense of well being, clearer thinking, flushing of toxins, clearer eyes, renewed energy, healing with greater speed, purification of the blood, loss of excess weight and water, cleaner breath.
Apart from the benefits, of allowing the organs to rest, the experts also observed fasting helps slow the ageing process that occurs in our cells as a result of the damaging oxidation process from free radicals in our food.
Experts' cautioned those who engage in long fasting exercise such as 'seven days dry' to take water starting from the third day to avoid health problems.
On ideal ways of breaking fasting, experts advised that people should always take water, or tea, and after one hour they can now take vegetables or light foods but not solid foods such as bread, pounded yam as experts said can be injurious to the health of an individual at such period.
They also said people should avoid using fruits to break their fasting as most of this fruits like oranges can be acidic, and can cause harm to the body at such period but rather experts said it is preferable and more healthier to take fruits after the person must have finished eating.

Original_Search_Title: No Threat Was Used On Exopa Boss
Headline: Ghana: No Threat Was Used On Exopa Boss
Body_Text:
AN officer of the Narcotics Control Board (NACOB), Mr. Anthony Smith, has indicated that no threat whatsoever was used on the Chief Executive of Exopa Modeling Agency, Ibrahim Sima, who is standing trial at an Accra Fast Track High Court (FTC), before he confessed to the crime allegedly committed.
Mr. Smith, who was in charge of a team of NACOB officials detailed at the airport on September 7, 2009, noted that the accused person voluntarily confessed to putting substances suspected to be cocaine into four tubers of yams, which he intended to send to Germany.
The witness further asserted that the accused person was not harassed or intimidated into talking, adding that he made the confession in the presence of other persons, including an independent person.
The witness was giving evidence in a cross-examination conducted by James Agalga, counsel for the accused person, yesterday, before the court presided over by Justice Charles Quist.
Ibrahim Sima, 39 years, is facing two counts of attempting to export unlawful substances, and possession of unlawful substances suspected to be cocaine.
Sima, who was scheduled to travel to Germany, was arrested at the Kotoka International Airport on the night of Monday, 7th September 2009, during departure formalities, with tubers of yam containing substances suspected to be cocaine.
He had pleaded not guilty, and is currently on remand.
According to Mr. Smith, after the accused person was arrested, he was taken to his residence in Tema, a suburb of Accra, but no traces of cocaine were found in the room during a search.
The witness further indicated that the search party went into the kitchen and found a bread knife, which was identified by the accused person as what he used in cutting the tubers of yam.
Mr. Smith noted that the search party, of which he was a member, found some species of yam akin to those seen in the accused person's bag at the time of arrest, adding that glue, a gun and several other personal items were also found in the room.
The accused person indicated that his gun was licensed, the witness asserted, adding that the knife, yam species, and glue were sent to the NACOB headquarters.
He mentioned that the bag of the accused person had been deposited at the NACOB headquarters, as Mr. Smith denied that the bag was forcibly opened by him and his operatives.
Mr. Smith espoused that accused person's bag was padlocked, emphasising that it was the accused who opened his bag when he was asked to do so.
The case has been adjourned to March 11, 2010.

Original_Search_Title: Bayelsa Refurbishes Five Ferry Boats
Headline: Nigeria: Bayelsa Refurbishes Five Ferry Boats
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — A new ferry boat scheme has been launched in Bayelsa State to ease water transportation in the state.
Performing the ceremony in Yenagoa, Governor Timipre Sylva, said the scheme, a Public Private Partnership initiative marked the commencement of a vibrant marine transportation system in the state.
Inaugurating five of the refurbished boats, the Governor said that the government will improve the marine transportation despite the massive road projects to link many riverine communities in the state.
The Governor announced plans to acquire more ferry boats to ease the transportation problem of residents and movement of goods to and from riverine areas.
Governor Sylva commended the Ministry of Works and Transport for reviving the government-owned transport scheme at an affordable fare to the people.
He also announced plans by the government to create new 10,000 jobs in the civil service as a result of the new openings after the completion of the staff Biometric verification exercise, which identified some ghost workers in the system.
"People have been saying that I have sacked a lot of Bayelsans from work. But we cannot continue like this, where some people cheat on the system by having multiple employment. The biometric has opened up new opportunities and in a couple of months, we will employ more people. That is the gain of the exercise", Sylva stated.
The governor also said plans were on to alleviate poverty in the state.
" One woman was said to have complained that even though there has been improvement in the power situation but nothing to cook with the electricity. We have spoken to some of our brother governors, particularly Benue State on the supply of food items to Bayelsa State.
We have discovered that the cost of food is high in the state because of the transportation difficulties, but our arrangement with those states would be to get yams and other food items at a cheaper rates and we will flood the market with them to enable our people buy at affordable rates", he stated.
In an address of welcome, the Commissioner for Works and Transport, Mr. Emmanuel Frank_Opigo, said the private partner would be expected to refurbish the twelve boats and a barge acquired by the previous administration in 2003 with a six months trial period.
He stated that the available ferry boats, whose capacities had been boosted with higher horsepower engines, would be deployed to some designated routes in the three senatorial districts.
Also speaking, the Director, Public Private Partnership Bureau, Mr. Aiye Dikuro, said the scheme was purely a partnership arrangement with no heavy financial burden on the government.

Original_Search_Title: Support Madam Turai for President
Headline: Nigeria: Support Madam Turai for President
Body_Text:
I need to appeal to God to change the rules. You can't imagine how many marriages have crashed because a husband was unable to perform his conjugal duties. Things are changing and I think God should change the rules to make us happier. Nigeria has been doing the impossible and only the ungrateful would not acknowledge it. We have run a headless country for over two months and now we are running one outside the rules and but for the league of malcontents led by a grey-bearded killjoy and friends nobody is complaining. Nothing has changed since November 23. Rather than rock the boat, we should all be jostling for recognition in the Guinness Book of Records. That is where Soyinka, Falana and Bakare should direct their protests, not Abuja, Lagos, New York and London. The National Assembly should be probing the source of their air tickets and visa instead of imposing on us an acting president.
Political office is personal and nobody should think for a season that because they queued in the sun and the rain to vote, they still have rights. From the day that Iwu issued a certificate of return till he declares another pending election, nobody has rights or privileges. I challenge any common voter to provide evidence that he has been consulted since by his governor, chairman or representatives were sworn in?
So, I find it quite insulting that some people want Mr. President to transmit a letter just because he's gone on medical evacuation. How many of them have given notice that they are going to be sick? As governor of Katsina State, Mr. President was known to have disappeared for six consecutive months, without making a national headline. The state continued to run and economically too, because money, which should have been used to fuel official cars were saved. Ask the Lagos State House of Assembly how much Aktion Governor Fashola spends on fuel in a month and you get my drift.
Nigerians should be grateful, after all, having served Katsina dutifully for eight long and uninterrupted years, Mr. President was looking forward to his term on campus where lecturers naturally decide when to shut down or teach when Obasanjo went there to recruit him. Before then Madam Turaya was looking forward to the coveted second honeymoon since all the children have grown and married. Mr President is a private person who gave up his natural right to four wives to cling unto one and only Madam Turaya can declare him incapacitated. It is she who marries a man that knows when he is incapacitated and until so declared Oga is up and doing.
This is the trouble when you join I-too-know people with us northern feudal serfs. No wonder they want three republics in one. But I can't see any sense in making somebody acting president when he is not an actor?
I am totally disavowed to this acting thing. If not that the Saudi is yet to deliver my free ticket, I would have emigrated to Jeddah on the day Saint Michael Aondoakaa was removed as justice minister in favour of one pounded-yam eating SAN. The entire acting thing is illegal, ultra vires, null and void and those who succumbed to it will suffer when Madam recovers the mandate. Until Iwu pronounces a vacancy, this country is safely in the hands of the Yar'aduas and if anyone thinks otherwise, they are wrong. The Yar'adua's, like Modu Sheriff have the right to an annual vacation to the moon provided Nigeria sells enough crude to pay Branson the fare.
The idea that governors supported the coup is also a crash demonstration of the lack of the interpretation of the law in the absence of Aondoakaa. Who is the Governors' Forum? Are they legal? If so, what is their quorum? Did they form a quorum? Did the super governors of Bauchi and Kebbi vote in support of their decision? If it can be proved that they did not vote, then there was no decision. The votes of those two equal two thirds of thirty-six in this matter. Those in doubt can ask Aondoakaa how five nations make the rules at the UN Security Council. And if, as some bad belles are clamouring, the NASS impeaches Mr. Presidedent, to the extent that Katsina legislators did not say 'yeah' that resolution shall be null and void and of no effect anywhere in this country for the next 18 months. If our foreign reserves deplete, should we blame governors who do not live in Abuja?
Who has the power to declare Oga incapacitated? Only Madam. Who should be the natural successor in case Madam declares Oga incapacitated? Who has read the mind of Oga except Madam? The answer lies with Madam Turaya. Who has seen Oga since he left Nigeria? Only Madam? Who has spoken to him day, night and morning? Only Madam? Who could interpret his gestures? Who could read his mind? Only Madam. Who is his natural (not imposed) running mate? Only Madam. Therefore, the equation where all the answers to all questions are one, let us abide by the answer and support Madam Turaya, sorry, I mean Turai Yar'adua as President.

Original_Search_Title: Time to Rethink Fuel Subsidy
Headline: Nigeria: Time to Rethink Fuel Subsidy
Body_Text:
Lagos — Yes, you heard me right: the time has come to rethink fuel subsidy. All my life I have argued in favour of fuel subsidy, but now I am shifting my position. My pro-subsidy argument has always been based on two critical factors - the level of poverty in the land and the need to enjoy the benefits of being oil-rich. High fuel prices impact directly on the cost of living, which is never good news for tens of millions of poor Nigerians.
Because our transportation system is fuelled mainly by petrol, every slight increase in fuel prices has a ripple effect on the economy. Also, I recently conducted a research on oil-producing countries and discovered that many, if not most, subsidise the pump prices of fuel. It makes sense that if you have something in abundance, you should enjoy the advantage of lower prices. I shouldn't buy yam in Gboko at the same price with Lagos.
But I am now rethinking my position. In fact, I now believe more in subsidy. We need it badly. There is, however, a slight adjustment in my viewpoint. The problem with fuel subsidy in Nigeria is that it empowers mainly the fat cats in the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), the petroleum ministry and the import/supply chain. Trillions of naira have gone down the drain in the last three years through fuel subsidy. We keep buying petrol at black market price. So where's the subsidy? Why doesn't the state punish these fat cats? The system is polluted down the line. The amount of money involved is incredible - even an angel would be tempted. To go into it and come out with your reputation unstained is equal to becoming a saint.
One racket turns you into a billionaire; the next one makes you a multi-billionaire; and the next one makes you a billionaire in dollars. Some people have bought private jets at our expense. Some people have bought up streets and cities in Europe, Middle East and America through the subsidy racket. In plain English, therefore, only a few chaps - perhaps 2000 or so persons - have been enjoying fuel subsidy on our behalf while we see hell at the fuel pumps. And that is the point, exactly. In good times, you only get to buy fuel at N65 in Lagos, Abuja and Port Harcourt. Yet trillions of naira have been paid to marketers in the name of subsidy. The money is deducted from every state and every local government in Nigeria.
So if my council is entitled to N100 million allocation every month, N8 million or more will be deducted to fund subsidy - but, tragically, my people never get to buy the product at N65 per litre! That is what Fela would call "double wahala for deadi body". In one instance, we are losing part of our allocation to fuel subsidy. In another instance, we are buying fuel at N80 per litre! Now does that make sense? I think not. While the 36 states, 774 councils and the Federal Capital Territory contributed over N1.3 trillion to the subsidy fund between 2006 and last year, only a few areas enjoyed the benefit of paying the subsidised price.
For instance, Akwa Ibom State was the highest contributor to the subsidy fund (2006-2009) with N122.37 billion but petrol sells for between N85-N90 per litre in the state. Rivers State contributed N112.80 billion but buys at about N100. Delta State, with N108 billion contribution, buys petrol in the range of N80-N100. In another irony, Lagos, the biggest consumer of petrol, contributed only N37 billion to the fund, the same as Katsina State which consumes far, far less. That is unfair. But, still, I believe more than ever before in fuel subsidy.
I totally agree that the trillions of naira pumped into the ineffective subsidy fund in the last three years could have benefited the economy more. I mean, Akwa Ibom could have made better use of the N122 billion it contributed to the fund. Even if it had spent just 50 per cent of it on the local economy, the people of the state would have felt the impact - in form of roads, schools, etc. I fully agree that with the subsidy regime, the deregulation of the downstream sector of the oil industry will be a pipedream. Investors will naturally shy away from a market where prices are fixed by government. Even if they are given assurances that their costs will be reimbursed in part by the government through subsidy, the natural tendency is to be very cautious. After all, government has always failed woefully to reimburse fuel importers. This contributed in no little way to the banking crisis.
If I have argued like this, why do I then say I still believe in subsidy? Am I confused or am I confusing issues? No. This is my "revised" proposition. Deregulate the downstream sector. Stop paying subsidy to importers and marketers. Instead, government should pay subsidy directly to junior and medium-level workers. A new item should be introduced to their pay slips. It should be called "fuel subsidy". Even if it is N1500 per month, it will be far more effective and cost far less to implement. No NNPC is involved. No marketer is involved. No importer is involved. The workers can see it. They can touch the money directly. Really, subsidy is very funny in a sense. Why should a bank MD buy fuel at N65 per litre, thereby enjoying the same subsidy with his cleaner who comes to work everyday aboard a bus that also buys fuel at N65? To be fair, does the MD need fuel subsidy?
My "pay packet" proposition, however, will seem to cover only government workers. What of workers in the private sector? Should government make money available to their employers so that it can be passed into their salaries as "fuel subsidy"? My answer is no. These employers will just start behaving like marketers. They will have so many ghost workers on their payrolls in order to make outlandish subsidy claims. And some may not even pass the payments to genuine workers. I suggest, instead, that private sector workers should enjoy personal income tax reliefs, which should be equivalent to the fuel subsidy in the pay packet of government workers. But there is still a loophole in my proposition - what about self-employed workers? Are they not entitled to some subsidy too? Well, they are. The only thing I can think of now is also by way of income tax reliefs. Government should also make mass transit infrastructure more readily available. The rail transport system in Kaduna State is working - so why can't it work in other parts of Nigeria? More buses on the road through government support (BRT is working in Lagos) will also be beneficial, most especially to those who do not own cars.
The major reason I support deregulation is that it has the potential of creating a new economy for Nigeria - akin to the deregulation of the telecoms industry. With more investors invading the downstream sector, I can see an explosion in the petrochemical industry. There are about 3000 by-products from crude oil which we are currently not tapping because we only drill crude, export it and then stupidly import the finished products. Just imagine the thousands of new jobs that will be created by the new downstream economy. Engineers will be employed, as well as accountants, technicians and cleaners; not forgetting graduates of business admin and mass communication. Insurers and bankers will get more business. The opportunities are limitless.
This is how I conclude: I support fuel subsidy but I also support deregulation. A fraction (maybe 10-20 per cent) of the trillions of naira being burnt on subsidy should be paid directly to government workers as fuel subsidy (it's called cash transfer); tax incentives should be given to non-government workers to the equivalent of the subsidy enjoyed by the public sector workers; transportation infrastructure should be rapidly improved for the benefit of all; massive private investment in refineries and petrochemical industries should be encouraged with every incentive possible. The fat cats should go and look for another racket elsewhere.

Original_Search_Title: Valentine Day - Inri Church's Example
Headline: Nigeria: Valentine Day - Inri Church's Example
Body_Text:
Lagos — Agape is disinterested love. . . . Agape does not begin by discriminating between worthy and unworthy people, or any qualities people possess. It begins by loving others for their sakes. . . . Therefore, agape makes no distinction between friend and enemy; it is directed toward both".- Martin Luther King, Jr.
As the echoes of love vibrate across the world in celebration of the Valentine Day, it is no longer news that selfish love other than agape has been the motivating factor behind the celebration of this event.
The type of love that many profess today is so discriminatory and self serving. Groups and organizations are not left out, this is why so many find it difficult to show love to those who do not belong to their group. Many will today look out for those that are worthy of their love while so many others will use the occasion to indulge in activities other than agape which God has shown to humanity when He gave His only begotten son Jesus Christ.
But toeing the step of Christ who ministered the gospel of true love to all that crossed his part, the INRI Evangelical Spiritual Church, Lagos has been moving outside its walls to confront the difficult issues facing the poor in our society, using February as the month to reach out in a special way to these people with a practical message of love. Through this approach, it has been making positive impacts and contributions to the society. From its location at the INRI Prosperity Estate at Oke Afa area of Lagos, the church has reached out to thousands of people both Christians and Muslims with various forms of empowerment packages.
This year, the church has lined up programmes of activities starting from February 1 to culminate today, February 14, to incidentally coincided with the birthday celebration of the founder of the church, Primate Babatunde Elijah Ayodele. Through such activities, lives of less privileged members of the society have been touched. Widows, orphans and vulnerable children have been reached with this touch of love.
In the spirit of the Valentine celebration this year, the leadership of the church last Monday paid visits to homes of some widows and orphans and vulnerable children at Mushin, Ilasamaja, Isolo and Ejigbo areas of Lagos. These visits witnessed the distribution of bags of rice, tubers of yam and cash gifts to these categories of people.
Similarly, the church also extended such visitations to schools, hospitals and police stations last Tuesday.
Within the week, the church also organized series of lectures; book exhibitions; drama; photo exhibitions and lectures entitled 'The church in the re branding process-An agent of transparency, accountability and probity in governance and 'Harnessing the potentials of religion for the socio-economic and political growth of our nation' respectively. There were also empowerment for widows and scholarship for the educational advancement of the youths within the week.
The church will today roll out the drums to celebrate the birthday of the founder. Other activities lined up for the celebration include permanent appreciation to God by the INRI Church and distribution of JIGI INRI.
It has also looked after the needy and since alleviating needs is one of the key ways in which Christians shape the society and the world, INRI has not looked back in its involvement in active charity work aimed at shaping the society.
The man behind the vision
Since the strength of any caring church is hinged on the vision of its founder, this is probably why the INRI under the leadership of Primate Ayodele has become a typical house of refuge where the down trodden in the society have found shelter and could also heave a sigh of relief.
In an era when many church founders and leaders are busy touring Europe, America and Asia with passion as if traveling abroad would enhance their anointing; when so many leaders see overseas trip as a way of enhancing their profiles. Having been so engrossed with such trips, they left their flock at home. Nowadays, it is often impossible for bishops to pay visits to the poor members of their congregation unlike what the early missionaries were noted for. But Ayodele stood out among the pack. During the week, he toured Lagos suburbs where he interacted with the aged. Some of them who are lonely got television sets in addition to cash gifts and food items. It was all cheers in every house hold he stepped into as his presence alone renewed the strength of these senior citizens.
As the saying of the popular American human rights activist, the late Martin Luther King (Jnr) goes that if a man hasn't discovered something that he will die for, he isn't fit to live, Ayodele has said it over and over again that he was ready to sell all that he had in order to assist those who are suffering in the society.
He said that he is passionate about helping the helpless members of the society especially orphans and vulnerable children.
According to him, if these children could receive the needed care and attention from the society, they could become useful members of society with some of them becoming political leaders, industrialists, pastors and other professionals.
The clergy man has be partnering with some families, through which, he takes care of the clothing, feeding and school fees of these orphans while the families offer accommodation and the right environment these children need for their development. He had told reporters during one of his recent empowerment programmes in Lagos that "We have about 40 orphans and we used the opportunity to thank their guardians, today we are going to empower about 20 people while another 20 will be given micro credit. The other 10 will receive clothing materials because some of the widows' children do not have enough clothes and can hardly feed. We give them money, raw food and other materials to assist and support them.
"These are what we are celebrating. We don't believe that birthday must be celebrated with dancing and other merriments. My joy is to see the widows and orphans happy and this occasion offered me such an opportunity to celebrate with these categories of people who are members of our society. Let us continue to play our roles to see that our country Nigeria moves forward", he said.
A

Original_Search_Title: 100-Year-Old Retired Professor Who is Not Yet Tired
Headline: Nigeria: 100-Year-Old Retired Professor Who is Not Yet Tired
Body_Text:
Lagos — Professor Emeritus, Cornellius Olaleye Taiwo, the 100-year-old Professor of Education and the substansive King of the Oru, Ijebu, in Ogun State sat comfortably inside his expansive, well-furnished sitting-room at Oduduwa Street, GRA, Ikeja, Lagos looking quite younger than the 100 years he attained last October when he celebrated his centenary birthday. He was born in October 23, 1910 at Oru, Ijebu in Ogun State, he told Saturday Champion.
Then like a bolt out of the blues, a young, tall, lanky, charcoal-skinned man came inside the sitting-room, and as if he wanted to do "press-up", prostrated full length on the ash-coloured rug in front of the centenarian professor, whose school mates had all gone the way of their forebears.
"First of all, let me say it's a privilege and honour to see you not just because you are a professor, but because God has blessed you with such a long life to enable you reach 100 years," confessed the young visitor when he got up and had a handshake with the old educationist. "It is not all the time one has the opportunity to meet face-to-face with a man so blessed by God with good health and old age," the guest said further. Adding: "I pray that God will give me the opportunity to live as long as you, daddy."
This is the kind of reverence people have for the old man. He simply smiled, motioned his guest to get up, shook hand with him and said confidently, "May God grant your prayer," to which the excited visitor responded with a loud "A-a-a-m-e-n-n-n!"
Now, having dismissed the visitor, Prof. Taiwo began, as his not-too-sharp memory could permit (remember what age has done to man!), to recall the interesting tale of his long, studious life - a life dedicated to serving mankind and God.
Reminded that he looks rather younger than the 100 years God has enabled him to attain, Prof. Taiwo smiled, exposing a set of clean, strong, snow-white teeth. "Really?," he asked, expressing pleasant surprise. "I was born at Oru Ijebu on the 23rd of October 1910," he reminded the interviewer and then laughed quite heartily, "Heh! Heh!! Heh!!!... And at my last birthday I was 100 years old... Heh! Heh!! Heh!!! Yes. We thank God for it. Yes."
Later on, he attended the Yaba College of Technology Lagos; Trinity College, Cambridge; University of Cambridge; University of London Institute of Education, among other notable institutions of higher learning.
In one of his early biographies, Learning Thrills: Memoirs of an Educationist, published in London, Prof. Taiwo wrote, "I have some recollection of when I started school. It was after World War 1, probably in 1921. The school was St. Luke's School, Oru, Ijebu, a two-teacher school. It was a typical small CMS school attached to St. Luke's Church, Oru."
Just about two weeks ago, the University of Lagos awarded to the grand old educator who in his hey days once worked for the citadel of learning. "Well,... Emmm... Eeeeh... I have worked in the University of Lagos for some time," he told Saturday Champion. "We had the College of Education where I was in charge and I thank God. Many of those who passed through it, did well and they are happy to have done well. So that pleased me," he confessed cheerfully. "I felt very happy being honoured by the University of Lagos," he said as his eyes sparkled in excitement.
Taking one on a long reflective journey into the past, from the 1920s when he began his primary education to his secondary school years; all this period he was ahead of his class, smiling home each time with prizes he won for being the most outstanding student academically. If there is any aspect of his life he could forget, not the one that has to do with his superb brilliance at school.
"My mother said when I was five years old, she decided that I should attend the church school at Oru and she took me there and they gave me admission," recalled Prof. Taiwo amiably.
"It was there I read Prima 1, Prima 2, Prima 3 and when I read Prima 3, I got promoted to standard 1. Now the standard 1 school in the place is a significant one. That was at Osagun United School, Oru," he said as he observed in his professorial manner the effect of his speech to sink in the consciousness of his interviewer.
"So when they promoted me to Standard 1, I had no option. So I started attending from Standard 1. At the end of the year in Standard 1, I got the first prize. The second year, I was promoted to Standard 3 but at the end of the year again, I got the first prize," the old professor recalled jocosely as he laughed, "Heh! Heh!! Heh!!! Heh!!!... You see, that was a surprise to me and to my parents, too because standard 1, you can't omit it; a whole class. Standard 2, you can't omit that. But in this case, I was promoted to standard 3."
He continued his lively, historical narration: "The way it went, when I told my father, my father said, yes, the Head Master knew what he was doing; to promote you to standard 3, must have some meanings but tomorrow morning, I shall bring you Reader 3 and Reader 2 which you had never seen before; I will bring you a copy and so you must read and read and read.. I said thank you sir, thank you daddy. The following morning I went to school and I found myself sitting with Standard 3 students. At the end of that year, I got the first prize to go to Standard 4. At the end of Standard 4, I got the first prize to go the standard 5... Heh! Heh!! Heh!!! So I felt happy, happy and happy."
For those who want to live long, the first secret to attain longevity is to heed your parents advice from time to time, according to the great, grand old intellectual, who said point-blank: "My advice will be, first of all, you should listen to your parents when they advice you from time to time. You should also learn to learn; that is go to school or to some place like that and you grow up like that, following your parents' good advice... your days will be long, for sure."
But the old man does not feel very happy each time he realizes that all his classmates at Saint Andrew's College, Oyo in 1928 had all passed on. In those days, they were 41 students in class who were being taught to become teachers but they could not finish until 1941 when they qualified and obtained their certificates.
"Well, today, 40 have left... they have left oooooh! They've gone home. Some of them were older than I but nevertheless, some of them lived well and therefore they lived a healthy life. Until today, in fact, I don't have a single one of them still alive," he said.
Not just Prof. Taiwo's classmates that have all passed away like the wind, his parents also had all kicked the bucket. "My parents... oooooooh! Ooooooooh!! They have gone," he screamed. Adding: "No. No. They didn't live up to 100 years like me. But there's no doubt that they lived long, too, only that they did not live up to 100 years."
He recalled vividly: "My father was a trader and lay reader in the church. Then at a point he had to move to Ilesha because some friends suggested that such relocation would benefit his trading business (woven clothes). When my father accepted the suggestion, we all - my father, my mother and I moved to Ilesha." He added: "When we got to Ilesha, immediately, my father realized that the suggestion and advice were good; that at Ilesha, you could go round to some places and sell the woven clothes here and there because people loved woven agbada in those days."
Prof. Taiwo does not pretend being in love with beans and Golden Morn, a food one may think is chiefly for kids. The old professor relishes in taking beans and Golden Morn in the morning and in the evening respectively. But in the afternoon, he is always convinced by his nurse and children to see the need in eating something that will give him energy, like starchy food, say, either pounded yam or eba (garri). "But in the morning and evening, it's always Golden Morn and beans," Saturday Champion was informed by an insider.
Being a die-hard extrovert, Prof. Taiwo, regardless of his age, does not like staying indoor for too long and that is the main reason he honours virtually all invitations extended to him.
"One thing with him is that he loves to go out. He honours nearly all invitations. He doesn't like staying at home for too long. Every morning, he's always asking me, do we have any outing today? If I say no, he will tell me to check my file to make sure we have nothing to miss," revealed Prof. Taiwo's Personal Assistant who also doubles as his nurse. "He loves shaving. He loves clean shaving as you can see from his appearance," he said. Adding: "He listens to any kind of music. He loves dancing. He dances a lot."
The old educationist disclosed to Saturday Champion that his happiest moment in life was during his wedding with his only wife, Susana or Funke. Sadly, the wife passed on long ago. "Happiest moment?... Eh...emmm.... Happiest moment?" he repeated as if he wanted to be sure that he heard you well. "Eeeh! I think I will say my wedding day because so many people, friends from my St. Andrew's College, Oyo... they came and CMS Grammar School, Lagos where I was teaching, old boys, they came, then from the town itself, people came and it was a very significant day," he said happily.
Are there certain things Prof. Taiwo must have observed or avoided to enable him attain the enviable age of 100 years, an age many have dreamt of reaching but only few could make? The old, great, grand father confessed that he has never smoked since he was born in October 23, 1910; he is not in the habit of womanizing; detests drinking alcohol and takes good care of his health as well as avoids being stressed or tensed up by trivialities of this wicked wide world. "I don't smoke. I've never smoked since I was born. Drinking? I don't drink alcohol. Soft drink? Yes. I take soft drink," he said gently as he released yet another round of his peculiar hearty laughter: "Heh! Heh!! Heh!!! Heh!!!..Omo mi(my son), thank you for coming."

Original_Search_Title: Sima Goes to Court
Headline: Ghana: Sima Goes to Court
Body_Text:
Embattled former boss of the Exopa Modeling Agency, Ibrahim Sima would make another court appearance on Thursday.
Sima, who is standing trial for attempting to smuggle substances believed to be cocaine outside the country, is making an appearance after similar ones since he was arrested on September 7th, 2009 at the Kotoka International Airport by officials of the Narcotic Controls Board (NACOB).
The last time Sima was in court was February 1, when an official of the NACOB denied that the accused was being monitored before his arrest.
Mr. Anthony Smith of the Narcotics Control Board (NACOB), who was answering questions under cross examination led by defence counsel, Mr. James Agalga, said it was only when a test conducted on a traveller proves positive for a substance that he or she is monitored.
He admitted that NACOB officials had earlier on escorted Sima to the 37 Military Hospital for an ex-ray which proved negative.
Before his arrest, Sima had succeeded in lifting modeling in Ghana to lofty heights and built for himself a very large following of young guys and ladies who found his Exopa Burg and Modeling Agency, a perfect breeding ground for a career in modeling.
He had successfully organized the Exopa Fashion week which brought together an international array of celebrities including Ghanaian-born Hollywood actor, Kodjo Borris and was preparing to host another major event, when news of his arrest hit town.
Luck eluded Sima when a team of NACOB officials arrested him at the Kotoka International Airport on his way to Frankfurt, Germany, on September 7 last year while going through departure formalities for allegedly concealing five kilograms of drugs suspected to be cocaine in tubers of yam.
Officials of the Narcotics Control Board (NACOB) opened his luggage and found tubers of yam cut and joined. When the yams were cut open, the drugs were found concealed in them. Sima denied ownership of the products at the time claiming he was asked to give it to someone in Germany for a fee of USD 3, 200.
Sima is facing charges of attempting to export unlawful substance and possessing unlawful substance without lawful excuse.So far, the 40 year old has pleaded not guilty.

Original_Search_Title: Season of Ultimatum
Headline: Nigeria: Season of Ultimatum
Body_Text:
It is the season of ultimatums and every Nigerian of voting age knows a thing or two about ultimatums. Soldiers, our ex-rulers are best at giving it. They want anything done with immediate effect and they are equipped with the gun, the jackboots and the koboko to get it done.
If you think am lying, fly past Mogadishu Barracks without using the pedestrian bridge and without a military uniform and you will know the truth. So it is little wonder that persuasion hardly gets anything done, issue an ultimatum if you have the mandate and see how quick things change.
Only last week, our sleeping petroleum minister woke from his slumber, rode to the penthouse of NNPC Towers, gave an ultimatum and phew, an eastern wind blew off the long queues ensnaring foreign paparazzi for weeks since Christmas. The aftershock was felt in Lagos and to those of you think that just because pilgrims pass through your airport, you can call it international, you better think twice. As far as petroleum products are concerned if it works in Lagos and Abuja, the rest of the country can blame their crown heads.
A wicked friend of mine says that Rilwanu Lukman did that just to escape the impending Jonathan shake-up and make Nigeria keep paying for his flight ticket to his lucrative consultancy jobs in Vienna and other parts of the world. To a man who did not get a share, the booty is called haram! Rilwanu, carry go! The problem with ultimatums is that you need the coercive force of a soldier or a Lukman. It is said that Barkindo, the NNPC fat cat (no pun intended) is the man's right hand man.
For instance, if residents of the FCT have the force, they would have given Aliero an ultimatum - to ask the contractor building the overhead bridge to nowhere along Mogadishu Barracks to shift down a little and save residents of Kugbo from the danger of being overrun by speeding cars as they cross the road. Kugbo has real residents but Sunrise Hills belongs so far to rats and snakes. In real democracies, residents vote governments to deal with the rats...under PDP, I can't vouch for that order of things.
And that is not the only ultimatum we would have given. Take for instance, an ultimatum to the same Aliero to remove the car-wreckers he and the management of Oceanic Bank have erected on public highways in Abuja; or the traffic lights that have turned driving into a nightmare every 500 metres. But we do not have power to give such ultimatum and so, we clap and shout when he told us that the illegal fee he has been charging us since July is now reversed. What we do not know is whether we could sue him for making us lose plots and money etc etc during the period.
Before you accuse me of picking on Aliero, wouldn't you rather that you had power to issue an ultimatum on Rilwan Babalola not to appear either on tv or in any medium until he can at least guarantee electricity to Nigerians four days out of seven, for starters? I mean, the gangling young man is as reliable as NEPA/PHCN. Even when Umoru gave him an ultimatum to generate 6000 megawatts, he gave the impression that he had complied, but now he is telling us that he has only 2000 megawatts left.
The rest has been stolen by the Emir of Borgu! Doesn't that make you wish you had an ultimatum to give to pericarditis? I mean, we would have ultimately asked Umoru to return from Saudi and at least change the faces of the non-performing ministers that have been littering the political space since he last changed them? I mean, not that we should expect real change, but at least maybe they can change the faces so that somebody else's brother or sister can be called honourable before the true meaning of honour.
I love ultimatums. I mean, we could have given an ultimatum to Michael Aondoakaa to pack up and go until he has seen a legal dentist. Now I hear that a group from the south have given a seven-day ultimatum to David Mark to either make sure that their son is made acting President or...Well, I didn't truly know what they would have done in the event that their ultimatum is not met. But heh, doesn't it just make you love Nigeria? I mean, I woke up this morning and warned my wife to make sure that I have a steaming plate of pounded yam with egusi soup and bushmeat every other day even if she is suffering from acute fever. Now, that is an ultimatum I can enforce, except that who knows, the NCWS or any other NGO may be out there reading this. Shall we also give an ultimatum to Dr. Goodluck be a performing acting President or else...

Original_Search_Title: Essumeja Youth Want New Chief Installed
Headline: Ghana: Essumeja Youth Want New Chief Installed
Body_Text:
Kumasi — The youth of Essumeja in the Bekwai Municipality are seething with anger, following the lukewarm attitude of the Queenmother, Nana Adwoa Obro Pokuaa Nyankopon, and kingmakers of the traditional area to install a new chief.
They are furious, because the absence of a chief, since 2003, has brought development to a standstill.
Citizens of Essumeja have frowned on the apathy and conduct of the Queenmother towards the missing stool, and expressed grave disappointment in her.
Essumeja, which is the Benkum wing of Asanteman, has been without a substantive chief since Odeneho Oduro Numapau III died in 2003.
Mr. Kofi Ofori, the Chairman of the Essumeja Youth Association, expressing concern in a chat with The Chronicle, attributed the situation to the standing chieftaincy dispute, bordering on the eligibility of Nana Pokuaa Nyankonpon as the Queenmother, brought against her before the Kumasi Traditional Council by the Aduana Abusuapanin of Essumeja, Barima Osei, and for which a five-member Judicial Committee, headed by the Achirensuahene, Agyewodin Adu Gyamfi Ampem, was appointed since May 2008.
Other members of the Committee are Nana Owusu Ankoma, Seikwahene, Kyeame Boakye Yam, Nana Sabronum and Nana Adu Baah Ansah, Yamfohene.
The Queenmother is said to be disrespectful to stool elders, and seldom meets the Essumejaman.
As a result of the dispute, the Queenmother's nomination to replace Nana Oduro Numapau III, who died in 2003, has been rejected by the kingmakers, causing a stalemate in the enstoolment of a chief for the town.
The Queenmother does not feel obliged to exhaust her prerogative to nominate another person for consideration by the kingmakers, hence the protracted dispute. The Committee is yet to present its report to the Kumasi Traditional Council, after extensive findings for the determination of the dispute, to set the stage for potential claimants to stake their claim to the Essumeja stool.
They include Amo Gyebi (USA), George Kwarteng (Switzerland), Kwaku Ofori, and one Osei Kwadwo.
Even though the Asantehene, Otumfuo Osei Tutu II, has since December 2008 directed that Stool Elders of Essumeja take immediate steps to retrieve the Black Stool of the Aduana royal family, the acting President of the Essumeja Traditional Council, Nana Ntim Banahene, the Sanaahene, Nana Owusu Koko, the Ankobeahene, Nana Kwadwo Nyarko, and the Gyasehene, Nana Twum Barima Appau, have made no efforts to that effect.
On February 24, 2009, Otumfuo Osei Tutu II directed Nana Adwoa Pokuaa Nyankopon, who allegedly disregarded counsel by stool elders and family and traveled shortly after the Black Stool was reported missing, to appear before the Kumasi Traditional Council and answer charges without fail, or face the consequences.
Mr. Ofori has therefore called for the early intervention of Otumfuo Osei Tutu II to resolve the matter.
According to the youth leader, the absence of a chief was also affecting the role of the Essumeja stool in relation with the Asanteman Council, particularly with the loss of the Black Stool of the Aduana Royal family.
The Chairman of the Youth Association also hinted that the youth intend to pour onto the streets soon, in protest against the delayed determination of the dispute.
Meanwhile, Kumasi Traditional Council sources have indicated that chieftaincy disputes at Essumeja, Kumawu and Fomena, are among those penciled for determination as soon as the Council resumes sitting this year, to pave the way for the celebration of Odwira Festival of Asanteman, which cannot be organised without the Black Stool of Essumeja.

Original_Search_Title: Renewed Clashes Render Many Homeless
Headline: Nigeria: Renewed Clashes Render Many Homeless
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Ahead of the planned council poll in Ebonyi state in a few months time, supporters of two chieftains of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Ughariugo-Umuoghara in Ezza North local government area of the state engaged in a violent clash leading to the burning down of over 32 houses and destruction of property worth millions of naira.
As a result of the crisis which took place weekend, more than 100 persons have been rendered homeless; many sustained various degrees of injuries but no dearth was recorded.
Two PDP chieftains in the community namely; the Coordinator of Ezza West Development Center, Chief John Williams Nwode and the member representing Ezza North East in the State House of Assembly, Hon. Patrick Mgbebu are reportedly at the center of the conflict as they are said to be locked in a supremacy battle over their political ambitions as well as the control of proceeds of mineral exploitation in the area.
Our reporter gathered that although no life was lost, but about 19 persons received injuries and are now receiving treatment in hospitals.
Other property destroyed during the incident includes motorcycles, bicycles, yam bans, animals and household property.
One of the victims, a girl who did not want to be named told our reporter that the crisis took many of the villagers unawares, saying, "I don't know what the problem was but people started packing their belongings and before we could do something, some group of boys have come and started putting fire on our houses."
She stated that many people sustained injuries including machete cuts, while "many goats and yam barns have been burnt down, you can see for yourself", she said.
Another victim explained how some people have rendered his household homeless; "I lost everything including my clothes and money I only managed to save my documents, as I was helpless watching my property being destroyed by fire," he added.
Confirming the incident the Chairman of Ezza North Local Government Area, Barrister Ikeuwa Omebeh said that he was shocked when he got the information describing the incident as terrible.
He announced that the police have been invited and instructed to arrest those behind the incident adding that he has set up five-man committee to look into the mater maintaining that those involved in the crisis will face the law.

Original_Search_Title: FG Targets $10 Billion From Non Oil Export
Headline: Nigeria: FG Targets $10 Billion From Non Oil Export
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Chief Executive Officer of the Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC), Mr. David Adulugba yesterday in Abuja said the country's Non Oil export is expected to generate $10 billion as earnings this year.
He pointed out that the council is working hard to meet its set target. By the ongoing repositioning, he said the council will therefore contribute significantly to the nation's Gross Domestic Product (GDP). He made this statement against the $1.8billion it realized from the Non Oil Export in 2009. The CEO said he is optimistic that the $10 billion is achievable, based on the increasing earning from the non oil export in the last three years. He said, "towards this end, we have initiated several relevant projects, strengthened our partnership and collaborate with other stakeholders as well as facilitated opportunities for our exporters with the attendant benefits of penetrating the international market with our locally made goods."
He said more attention will be given to product development, human capital development and cooperation with multi- lateral institutions, sensitization workshop and the establishment of public-private partnership. The council's CEO made this disclosure yesterday during an interactive session with the media.
Adulugba stressed that the Council's quest to increase its baskets of exportable products from Nigeria remain the it's cardinal point towards promoting the non oil export for sustainable economic growth.
He said recently the council in collaboration with the Raw Materials Research and Development Council, Cross Rivers State Forestry Commission and the National Sugar Development Council jointly organised a stakeholders forum to identify and promote Thaumatin ,a natural sweetener which is highly demanded in the international market. The Council according to him is collaborating with other states, besides Nassarawa State Government for yam export to the United Kingdom. He said the pilot scheme last year resulted in the exportation of 73.5 metric tonnes of yams worth 30,000 Pounds.
The CEO said it is unfortunate that Nigeria in the past has not fully exploited its service export which is worth $2.5 trillion. He said, "Export often appears invisible to policy makers, its huge potential in increasing export turnover from Nigeria and ushering a favourable regime of foreign exchange earnings cannot be overemphasized." In other parts of the world, Adulugba said, this sector plays a vital role in facilitating all aspects of economic activities in a country as a vibrant services sector. Adulugba added that the Council based on the premise of recognition of the sector as a quick win product is collaborating with the Commonwealth Secretariat (COMSEC) of London in developing a strategy to create the requisite protocol for the development of services for export from Nigeria. This according to him will compliment the government effort at diversifying the economy from over dependence on oil especially in the wake of dwindling oil revenue and the global economic downturn.
He added that plans are underway to reposition the movie industry since it is the second largest in the world. He said this has become necessary because of the high potentials and the multiplier effect in terms of wealth creation and job opportunities particularly for the country's teeming youths and graduates. He said one of the strategies of the Council is to collaborate with the World Bank, National Video and Film Censor Board, Film Institute in Jos and the Nigeria Copyright Commission and some notable NollyWood key players.
Adulugba said the creation of the Solid Minerals Development Ministry will create an enabling environment for the Local Miners to improve on their export potentials. He said the Council has put in place various incentives to attract large scale processors, especially the foreign investors interested in the sector. He said the infrastructural facilities being built in Jos will encourage the small scale illegal miners to formalize their practice. The center according to him will help in the sorting, grinding and enabling quality control of the product to ease transaction and engender competitiveness.
He noted with regret that Nigerian entrepreneurs have not benefited from the African Growth and Opportunities Act (AGOA) established by the United States of America some years back. The reasons for this according to him is because of the non competitive business environment.

Original_Search_Title: Sights, Sounds of Calabar Carnival - Victor Uwaifo And Wife
Headline: Nigeria: Sights, Sounds of Calabar Carnival - Victor Uwaifo And Wife
Body_Text:
Lagos — Memories of 2009 edition of Calabar carnival will continue to linger in the minds of many who witnessed or participated in the show for a long time. The last edition appeared to have upped the ante of the festival and raised the standard for subsequent 32 days of street party fiesta in the Canaan City.
Though, the carnival commenced on November 30, with a colourful opening ceremony presided over by the state governor, Liyel Imoke, it reached its peak between December 26 to 28, when the city of Calabar was overtaken by tourists, foreigners and various industry players, who gathered to catch a glimpse of the famed carnival.
The event gradually peaked from December 26, when children from various background converged on the Millennium Park venue, where they took off their carnival procession. The display by kids proved that with the right attitude children can become an effective tool for cultural renaissance in the country. Clad in various flowing attires, they displayed rare passion for culture as they made their way through the carnival train.
However, if the children carnival succeeded in igniting fun and excitement in the minds of many, the parade on December 27, was simply elegant in every sense of the word. It arguably marked the Calabar Carnival as the biggest street party in the continent.
On that eventful day, the Millennium Park venue of the carnival take-off had come under siege of sort, with various participating troupes engulfed by the euphoria of the moment. The passion on the faces of those gearing to commence the carnival procession appeared most infectious as they exchanged banters in a crowd that can be best described as colourful and exciting.
But there was a little snag. With December 27, falling on Sunday, the carnival did not take off at 8a.m as anticipated because of church service; hence it was slated for 2p.m. It eventually took off at about 3pm. Defying the scorching sun, the participants and guests proudly waited in anticipation for the carnival to take off. At about 3pm, Imoke, in company of Governors Rotimi Amaechi of Rivers State and Timipre Sylva of Bayelsa State, declared the carnival open amidst the screams of excitement that confirmed that it was, indeed, the people's celebration.
As the theme of the carnival, Land of Our Birth: Our People, Our Heritage, implied, the people proved that the carnival was certainly theirs as they left nothing to chance and savoured every bit of the moment.
The procession commenced, with various floats competing to out do one another as people of various social standing mingled without inhibitions in the spirit of the atmosphere. It was evident that the carnival had reached a feverish pitch and the people set to savour life to the fullest. Various trucks loaded with musical equipment, and power generating sets supplied the melody that kept the carnival fire burning as the people trudged on in the spirit of the fiesta. They were a total of five competing bands, comprising Freedom, Bayside, Seagull, Passion 4 and Masta Blasta.
Freedom Band was the first to commence the carnival procession replete with unique costumes and dresses. The first groups in the freedom band consisted of beautiful ladies who were clad in black leggings and gold breaded pants and bra as they dazzled the gathering. They danced glowingly like angels; with Afro hair do to complement. They were followed by a set of green and yellow dressed girls and boys, who complemented the first set of girls with different dance steps and body movements.
Freedom band appeared made up of various groups who put their best to out do the performances of one another. Another group, garbed in Zulu attire consisting of sword bearing men also added to the fun and excitement in the air. The members were followed by another Afro-wearing troupe, comprising males and females, who complemented other troupes in the freedom band. But the entrance of a troupe garbed in Hausa attire added a nationalistic tinge to the whole carnival. Even cripples were not left out as they rode on wheel-driven platform to the admiration of many.
Freedom band comprised various troupes, who brought their artistry to bear on the carnival.
Freedom Band left the scene for Masta Blasta Band, with the first troupe in the band attired in pharaoh's attire depicting the image of the famed Egyptians. They were followed by charcoal cuddling young men, who added colour to the fun on offer. Another troupe in the Masta Blasta band was the Golden Revelers made up of some dainty dressed men and women.
However, Masta Blasta band added a new innovation to the carnival with Durbar, which succeeded in adding colour and fun to the entire carnival, thus giving it a national colouration.
Following Masta Blasta was Seagull Band with various troupes, including Adventure of Hebrew, Sojourn in Ghana, Our Pains and Tears, Uruan Experience, The Adventure of the missionaries, Efik Kingship and some foreigners adorned in Hebrew attire. Seagull band was led by Senator Florence Ita Giwa and had notable Nigerian movie stars like Patience Ozokwor, Rita Dominic, Kalu Ikeagu, Shan George and Lillian Bach.
Seagull was made of up very colourful troupes, with an Edo attired troupe depicting the rich cultural heritage of the Edo people. They were followed by other troupes from Ile-Ife, Sokoto Caliphate, all proudly showcasing their various cultural heritages. Some other troupes carried Yam image, depicting the new yam festival, which left many in the gathering applauding the rich African heritage on display.
However, the frenzy of excitement that characterized the carnival appeared to have reached a boiling point when Nollywood stars, Ikeagu and Lillian Bach appeared as Queen and King of Seagull band. Their entry left many in the gathering wowed, with others struggling to catch a glimpse of the stars and many cameras clicked to take a shot of the duo. Ikeagu was followed by a float tagged Destination Cross River, which displayed the rich tourism potential of the state.
Seagulls were followed by Bayside Band, which had the former State governor, Donald Duke, Minister of Environment, John Odey and other prominent Nigerians. Bayside entered in a trailer float, with various members dancing at the top as it moved in revelry of sort. The first troupe in Bayside were garbed in a Roman attire as they made their way through the carnival route and displayed a lot of dance acrobatics and free style that they garnered applauds from the gathering . Bayside was made up young men and women, who displayed energy and razzmatazz to the admiration of all and sundry.
Another spectacular scene was the sight of women of the Community Urban Development Authority, who picked dirt as they glory in the carnival procession, hence everywhere looked neat irrespective of litter.
Bayside was followed by Passion 4, which made their entry in style, and peopled by the youths, full of energy and vigour. They added colour and zest to the whole carnival setting with their performance. Passion 4 presented a cultural re-awakening of sort as the youths displayed their love for cultural heritage. They came in several troupes, with various messages, including Plains of Pleasure, Bechere (Correctness), Slippery Slopes of Knowledge and Wisdom.
However, the carnival reached its zenith when former Governor Donald Duke appeared in a motorcycle in company with his wife and elicited wide spread excitement and applauds from the people. It was evident that the people still held him in high esteem for the innovation of the carnival, which many agreed has affected the life of the average man on the streets of Cross River State.
Passion 4 brought vigour to bear on the carnival, entertaining the gathering with titillating dance steps and displayed rare energy that eclipsed the fun and excitement on offer.
Aside the five competing bands, they were other non-contesting bands, including Diaspora Band, Nigerian Navy Band, which proved their musical skills with their exciting performance that held many captive dancing without inhibitions. Other bands included Oceanic Bank, Amber Tinapa, District 9140 of Rotary Club, Christ Embassy, who presented colourful floats and added colour to the carnival.
Though the carnival procession lasted into the early hours of the next day, it did not deter the people from dancing and celebrating with the various carnival trains all through the 12km carnival route.
However, on December 28, Calabar Rocks held at the U.J. Esuene Stadium, where Wyclef Jean led the pack of other local artistes, including D'Banj, Julius Agwu, Sound Sultan, Weird Mc and Bigiano to entertain the people.
The stadium was saturated with human traffic and the excitement in the air was most infectious as screams of excitement pervaded the entire stadium.
At the end of the day, the judges released their results, which according to opinions met the expectation of the people. Passion 4 and Seagull emerged joint winners for band of the year, while Seagull emerged band of the year in the junior carnival category.
However, the success story 2009 edition of Calabar carnival cannot be complete without a mention of the pivotal role of the Nigeria Tourism Development Corporation (NTDC). The apex tourism agency besides collating data at the carnival supported it with 40 chemically treated mobile toilets, 50 dustbins and several branded materials.

Original_Search_Title: Police Probe Sylvia Owori
Headline: Uganda: Police Probe Sylvia Owori
Body_Text:
Kampala — POLICE are investigating over 10 prominent individuals, including fashion guru Sylvia Owori, over wetland destruction in Kampala City.
This follows complaints from the National Environment Management Authority (NEMA) that encroachers were reclaiming wetlands that shield Lake Victoria from pollution.
NEMA has also ordered some individuals and companies to restore the wetlands. Among these is a flower exporter Rosebud, accused of destroying a critical Lake Victoria wetland at Lutembe.
The wetlands targeted by encroachers include Kinawataka, Kawuku and Kyetinda, which filter waste Victoria. They also absorb rain water and avert floods.
The Lutembe wetland is recognised as a Ramsar site (a wetland of international importance).
Owori is allegedly reclaiming land in Kawuku wetland by dumping marram. The Police arrested a worker at the site recently and took a statement from him.
NEMA chief Dr. Aryamanya Mugisha has also asked the CID to summon Owori, the watchdog said in a statement.
Naboth Kusima, who heads the CID's environment desk, said a file was opened on Owori and that she would be summoned.
She was also ordered to restore the wetland.
Others being probed are Motel Adad belonging to Salango Sebunza of Lubowa-Zana, Ntinda wetland near Minister's Village, construction of Precious College in Kinawataka and Salango Kyekubya of Salaama-Munyonyo, who is allegedly selling plots in a wetland.
