The CID are also investigating Life Resurrection Centre over dumping marram in Nsambya wetland.
Enos Sebuyira, Michael Kaya and Robert Kirumira, on their part, are accused of encroaching on Kalungu-Kawuku wetland.
William Luwemba Apuuli, an undersecretary in the energy ministry, is also under investigation for alleged encroachment on Kawuku wetland. Some of his workers have been arrested, NEMA said.
He also appeared at NEMA's offices and was ordered to restore the wetland. "The lake is shrinking and encroachment on the wetlands exposes it to the danger of drying up," Naome Karekaho, NEMA's spokesperson, said.
Accordingly, NEMA has deployed spies to report any dumping in the wetlands.
Rosebud, which belongs to Sudhir Ruparelia, a property mogul, is accused of fencing off a permanent wetland with a chain-link.
NEMA has ordered him to submit an environmental audit.
Aryamanya Mugisha told The New Vision in an interview that the encroachers dump marram at the weekend, during long holidays or at night. He said they are rich individuals who know the environment law.
"These are not yam growers," Mugisha said. "The encroachers are powerful individuals who know the importance of wetlands."

Original_Search_Title: Potassium Bromate - Deadly in Bread Baking
Headline: Nigeria: Potassium Bromate - Deadly in Bread Baking
Body_Text:
Olayinka Olawale — WITH increasing cases of kidney damage in Nigeria, researchers discovered that potassium bromate - a flour improver used in baking bread and other confectionaries - could be a contributor.
Further research by the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration Control (NAFDAC), confirmed that potassium bromate was indeed, a deadly chemical.
NAFDAC banned the chemical from being used in Nigeria and even sealed up some bakeries that flouted its directives. This was in 2007.
Major bakeries in Nigeria have to boldly print "free of potassium bromate" on their bread wrappers to allay the fear of the bread eating public.
However, two years after the bromate war led by then NAFDAC director general and incumbent Minister of Information and Communications, Prof. Dora Akunyili, the deadly chemical is staging a comeback.
Many even believed it never left most bakers' mixing bowls and tables. But, that they were merely more discreet in its usage.
This has prompted NAFDAC and its current director general, Dr. Paul Orhii, to launch the second war against potassium bromate.
Potassium bromate is an oxidising agent that had been used as a food additive for decades, mainly in bread baking. That was until its harmful effects were discovered.
Medical research revealed that when potassium bromate is consumed, it is toxic to the kidney and can damage it. It is also linked to cancer, nervous system disorder and renal damage.
Researchers added that potassium bromate, usually in form of white crystals or powder, causes permanent deafness in animals and man.
Many were alarmed that such huge destruction could be mixed into an "adopted" staple food - bread.
At the peak of the campaign in 2007, many Nigerians abandoned bread for the traditional breakfast of yam and beans or pap plus akara and beans porridge.
Orhii has renewed NAFDAC's call that bakers should stop using potassium bromate in the production of bread and bakery products in the country because of the life-threatening dangers posed by the chemical.
At a recent one-day workshop on "Food Safety in Bread Products" in Lagos, he lamented that despite the bromate ban, some bakers were still using it in baking bread.
"Potassium bromate is carcinogenic and can, eventually, cause serious health problems like kidney failure and cancer."
Orhii noted that NAFDAC was mandated to safeguard the health of the nation and would not relent in its efforts.
He said the workshop was to remind the Master Bakers Association of Nigeria (MBAN) of NAFDAC requirements and to warn the unscrupulous bread producers.
Orhii added that any product manufactured under poor hygienic condition and not registered by the agency, was hazardous to health and unfit for consumption.
"Prior to the commencement of bread registration with NAFDAC in 2007, the condition of our bakeries were very appalling and with no good hygienic practices (GHP) in place.
"The review of routine inspections carried out during this period revealed the need for proper monitoring of bread production and disallow the use of potassium bromate in bread production," he said.
Orhii added that NAFDAC realised the various challenges ahead, both within it and on the side of stakeholders, and decide to make registration mandatory and free in 2007.
"Many bakeries are in the habit of not maintaining GHP after their product had been registered by the agency. That is why we carry out routine inspection and surveillance," he said.
He said some bakeries found to be producing under very poor hygienic conditions or using potassium bromate, were fined appropriately during the routine inspections.
According to Orhii, some bakeries were promptly closed down and suspects arrested in order to forestall further production of bread that were unfit for human consumption.
Mrs. Adebola Kumolu-Johnson, Deputy Director, chemical Monitoring Division Narcotics and Controlled Substance Directorate, NAFDAC, Lagos, said, people were exposed to health risks by consuming potassium bromate in bread and other baked products.
She said the carcinogenic effect and health risks associated with potassium bromate in dough making, has necessitated it ban in several countries, including Nigeria.
According to Kumolu-Johnson, a chemical or chemical substance is banned when its use poses a risk to human health or environment.
"It is not banned to punish manufacturers, reduce their profit or send them out of business", she said, adding: "there is always an alternative or substitute to a banned chemical."
Kumolu-Johnson in a paper entitled "Health Implication: Use of Potassium Bromate in Bread and Bakery Products" at the workshop, explained that, it was to educate the bakers about the health risks of the consumption of the chemical.
She said it was also to inform on other forms of oxidizing agents or bread improvers that have been approved for use in bread making.
"NAFDAC, as the regulatory body is concerned and is ensuring that potassium bromate as a banned substance is not used in bread and bread products.
Sadly, this substance is still being used as many bakers have developed unscrupulous ways of concealing it in their bakeries and using it during the dough making process.
"The chemical works by strengthening the dough, making it more elastic and as a result, increasing the loaf volume, improving the texture and enhancing the keeping quality of the loaf."
Kumolu - Johnson explained that, though, potassium bromate was meant to "bake off" in the process of bread making, it was found to leave a deadly residue in ready-to-eat baked products.
She said apart from the health risks from its consumption in bread and other products, individuals exposed to the chemical, including the bakery workers, were in danger.
"It potential health effects from direct exposure include inhalation, which causes irritation to the respiratory tract; chronic exposure, which can cause kidney damage and skin contact, which causes itching, redness and pain.
Other include eye contact which may result in eye damage and aggravation of pre-existing condition."
Mr. Chris Ofuani, Deputy Director, Food Registration, NADFDAC, Lagos, said the commencement of registration of bakeries by the agency in 2007 was to adapt to international best practices, forbidding the use of potassium bromate.
Ofuani said the essence was to encourage bakers, irrespective of size or location, to identify with the objective of NAFDAC's management that banned the use of potassium bromate.
In his paper entitled "Bread Packaging and Registration," he said, the need for good hygienic practices to be introduced to all bakers, even at the grassroots, also influenced the process.
"The process of registration is a series of activities which aids the agency to determine whether a product should be given marketing authorisation, if the product should be sold, distributed, advertised, manufactured, imported, exported and used.
"It is designed to ensure that products are safe, wholesome, efficacious and suitable for intended use. The process also ensures that regulated products are of good quality and conform to national and international standards.
Ofuani said by March 2010, products registered in April 2008, would be due for renewal and urged all bread manufacturers to register their products with NAFDAC.
Alhaji Lateef Oguntoyinbo, Chairman, Lagos State Master Bakers Association, said members were ready to support NAFDAC to achieve success.
Oguntoyinbo said in Lagos, there was about 95 per cent compliance among its members and that it would soon be 100 per cent.
"Bakers are not murderers; we know the health implications of potassium bromate and we are trying hard to stop our members from using it. "There is no law that is completely perfect. But, we will try to comply 100 per cent.
"I cannot say that we don't have people who still use potassium bromate in bread production; but we will continue to enlighten them on the health risks," he said.
Oguntoyinbo also implored government to ensure that the chemical was not imported into the country and to impose a heavy sanction on any agency or person selling it to bakers.
As the master baker said, if the poison is not available in Nigeria, no matter its fascination to some members, it would be impossible for them to purchase and use.
Fortunately, NAFDAC is in control of the importation of such chemicals.
The agency should liaise with border agencies to stop potassium bromate from entering Nigeria, obviously through smuggling. NAN Features.

Original_Search_Title: The Revamped Jolly Bar
Headline: Ethiopia: The Revamped Jolly Bar
Body_Text:
Jolly Bar & Grill, formerly a landmark in Arada, located by the Victory monument in Arat Kilo, reopened, on Friday, December 17, 2010, after a hiatus of almost three years, during which it was closed for renovations.
Originally opened in 1959, Jolly was a bar, with Solomon Bakery next door, owned by Abeselom Yehdego. It stopped serving alcohol in the 1980s, and it remained a café serving tea, coffee, and cakes.
"It was a regular meeting place for a group of people at the university when I was a student, and we used to have coffee there and talk and talk," a former customer of the bar told Fortune. "Very few people used to drink beer 45 years ago."
He could not remember much else about the place that has undergone quite a facelift over the past three years.
"The renovation was complicated to do," Yehdego Abeselom, a.k.a. Bobby, 31, the son of Abeselom and manager of Jolly, told Fortune. "It took two years, and I had them redo it many times until I was happy with the way the place looked."
The venue has been expanded to encompass both the former café and bakery and has two outside areas; in the front and to the side of the section geared to become a café in future.
"We are starting like this, and at the moment are only open for dinner," said Will Morkunas, executive chef. "I am hoping that we will be ready to start serving lunch right after New Year."
This will eventually expand to include serving breakfast, pastries, and offer catering services, according to the US chef who purportedly gave up a job on the Hollywood strip to be a part of the project.
"I hired Will because of both his great food and his personality," Bobby told Fortune. "He is not just a chef, but also a social butterfly. I want to teach people here different aspects of the industry, and he is someone who can whip them into quality."
In homage to this, the one wall of the open kitchen, where the chefs can be seen preparing the food wearing white chef jackets and caps, dons a picture of the bossy head chef from the movie, "Ratatouille," about the Parisian rat that could cook.
Will had complete carte blanche in creating the menu, a work in progress that the cheff planned to change every week.
"It includes things I like to cook, and favourites from previous menus," he said. "There are limitations regarding available ingredients here, but we use mostly locally produced organic food. Very few items, like vinegar, oil, seafood, and fish are imported."
He likes local food, and has some fusion dishes, which he plans to expand, such as fried injera (which crisps very well, according to the chef) with guacamole, an avocado based dip which originated in Mexico.
For now, the menu is divided into four sections, each a course to a four-course meal, and the dishes remain more on the traditional side, including American favourites like mashed potatoes and hamburgers.
The first course features Salad of the Caesar (65 Br) and seasonal (45 Br) varieties. It includes mostly bitter green leaves a bit too big to be bite sized, shaved root vegetables like the crisp and sweet radish and carrot, with the usual suspects tomato and cucumber, served with the tangy, sweet vinegary house dressing.
For the second course, choose between French onion soup (75 Br) or mini crab cakes (120 Br). The main course offerings include four hearty options: Chicken breast, served with potatoes and cabbage (150 Br); parpardelle (large, broad, flat, and thick noodles), served with vegetables and parmesan cheese (120 Br); as well as steak, zucchini (a popularly cultivated summer squash), onion rings, and mashed potatoes (180 Br).
The Jolly Burger (65 Br) comprises a thick beef burger patty with melted cheese serves on the one toasted half of a bun with the tomato, onions, and sauce on the other half. Add extra cheese (7.30 Br), vegetables (4.50 Br), or meat (9.50 Br). It is big enough to make eating with one's hands awkward, an act that seems out of place in the establishment anyway.
The chef's tasting menu (having one dish from each course for 320 Br) and chef's choice (simply ordering one's meat preference and "be surprised" for 250 Br) are in line with the new approach and complete rebranding targeting an upmarket crowd.
"Before, when my father gave the management over to a relative, he said, 'Do what you want, but do not sell alcohol,'" Bobby, who studied film and business in the US, recounted. "Eventually it was in shambles, and I thought, 'We have to resurrect this place.'"
The concierge at the entrance attests to this change, as does the American pop background music.
"I made a big investment," said Bobby, who declined to disclose the total amount. "We wanted to make a big comeback. It cost a pretty penny."
It appears as if hardly any expense was spared in the renovations. The decorative pillars sport black and white photographs from olden days, when Arada was just developing. More walls are decorated with old LPs framed with the disc and cover of artists like Madonna, Michael Jackson, and the Rolling Stones.
Even the water closets, to which access is gained through saloon-like swinging doors, are beautiful with modern glass basins standing up on the counters and shiny, large wood framed mirrors. Like the cartoon chef hung in the kitchen, the bathroom likewise has a joke or two, donning a sign above the toilet of acceptable positions in which to use said apparatus.
The bar top features pictures of female pole dancers in silhouette with coloured backlights, and under the counter are convenient hooks to hang one's bag on. Not a bad posse in which to enjoy the cocktails, totalling 21, and ranging between 95 Br and 38 Br apiece.
"We want to be known for making the best cocktails in town," Bobby said.
Despite his father ceasing to serve alcohol in the 1980s, when he saw the new restaurant, he said, "You have climbed Mount Everest," according to Bobby.
While Jolly may not yet have reached the summit, it appears to be aiming that high, and it has other innovations installed. For instance, there is no cashier, and every waiter uses the software for the billing herself.
Service also seems to be a big concern and when a waiter dropped something, two others literally ran to immediately clean it up. The tables are fully laid with wine and water glasses, forks, knives and spoons, as well as cloth serviettes folded in the shape of a boat. Little lamps on the tables mirror the light in the fake fireplace, above which Christmas stockings are hung to add some holiday cheer, in addition to the appearance of warmth.
As hardly any celebration can be complete without dessert, on offer are sorbet in orange, guava, grapefruit-champagne, or mango flavours (60 Br); strawberry semifreddo, which means "half cold" in Italian and is a class of semi frozen desserts, like ice-cream cakes, custards, and certain fruit tarts with the texture of frozen mousse (70 Br); or Grand Marnier (a liqueur made from a blend of true cognacs and distilled essence of bitter orange) scented chocolate tartlets (85 Br).
It is pricey, on top of which a 10pc service charge, and 15pc VAT is added. A soft drink costs 12 Br and a local beer 30 Br.
This is the new Jolly, but the manager did not do away with the old completely. He had a painting commissioned, copy of Leonardo da Vinci's "Last Supper," featuring what Bobby calls great figures in Ethiopian history seated in Jolly. It includes Emperor Haile Selassie, Haile Gebrselassie, Teddy Afro, Aster Aweke Aweke, Mulatu Astatke, Bob Marley, and two philosophers having cake and tea.
By contrast, Jolly is far from serving its last supper; it is only just beginning.

Original_Search_Title: Chasing the Dots - the Promise, Baked Potato; Delivered, Chips
Headline: Namibia: Chasing the Dots - the Promise, Baked Potato; Delivered, Chips
Body_Text:
OH dear, here we go again, another election, one lot will clearly win and the others will bitch and moan. Inane propaganda using the full lexical suite of political excrement will flow like stale urine through the addled brains of the Namibian population; neither the putrid smell or the nature of this meaningless drivel will penetrate the minds of the lemmings.
But it will be peaceful, declared a free and fair erection and life will go on unchanged! Democracy will be declared fit and healthy. Subservience will intensify.
The rich will get richer and fatter!
But who to vote for? I have not been canvassed and have received one "manifesto" (from Swapo, Windhoek East), which is little more than the standard bunch of "bullet points" from Swapo's manifesto with no explanation why I should vote for the candidate. It got me wondering how we could change things so that this potentially rich and beautiful land of ours could escape from its ideological logjam and the seemingly endless tales of violence, baby dumping and similar that pervade our media.
I have no idea of the candidates in my constituency, let alone what they stand for!
How the hell can I make a rational vote without information and some form of personal track record?
But I am not going to protest by abstaining, that is wrong. Equally I get pissed off with parties who abdicate their responsibilities by failing to go to work or boycott the election process.
Sure I would like all the legal matters in the Courts to be concluded but that is no excuse for not voting. Perhaps I should give all the candidates a chance and vote for all of them!
It was while looking at the menu at my usual steakhouse, having been spurred by an urge for a good steak, I began thinking.
A menu quite clearly tells you what you are going to get, in my case a 200g sirloin, onion rings and a baked potato / garlic butter; and it shows the price.
A sort of manifesto promise?
However experience tells me to order carefully and make sure the waitress takes down the order correctly; I know things will go wrong!
Sometimes they try to give me a rump steak, not a sirloin, sometimes it is far from medium rare having stood on the counter on the hot plate for 10 minutes, sometimes the potato is so cold the butter does not melt and once the onion rings were just the lumpy scratchings from the end of a batch. But I got an excellent steak, good onion rings apart from a few off cuts, but then there was no baked potato!
The very sweet little waitress explained there were no baked potatoes and I must have chips. Or she could take it away for 10 minutes (Namibian time?) and then bring it all back with a potato! Explaining the steak would be ruined, the onion rings less than crisp, fell on deaf ears.
The menus promise (or manifesto) to give me a balanced meal was broken. I tipped off the chips that were soaking up the juices and enjoyed what I had! And yes, after a pleasant chat with the floor manager, a few unfortunate tears from the waitress, I got a discount.
But the real problem was I did not get what was promised, only a part. Like manifestos, they only work when the whole package comes together; less is a breach of promise!
Perhaps political manifestos should either deliver or the voter gets a refund!

Original_Search_Title: Pineapple, Simsimprices Rise
Headline: Uganda: Pineapple, Simsimprices Rise
Body_Text:
As the pineapple season closes, the market has witnessed scarcity, which subsequently has impacted on prices. Traders say pineapple supplies from Arua are reducing and they are only relying on Luwero supplies, which obviously cannot satisfy demand.
In markets around Kampala including: Nakasero, St Balikudembe, Nakawa and Wandegeya, traders anticipate prices to rise further due to intermittent supplies yet Christmas is around the corner, which usually attracts high demand for fruit items.
According to traders in markets like Kalerwe and Owino, supplies have already run out and the only available pineapples are sold expensively.
Prices range between Shs3,000 and Shs3,500 up from Shs2,300 just a few weeks ago.
Simsim prices have also been rising. In a couple of weeks prices have gone up according to market information. Ms Annette Nakabiitto, a fruit vendor at Owino Market, says the supply of Simsim has reduced in the last few weeks, which has forced prices of the commodity to rise.
At Owino Market, prices have increased from Shs3,000 per kilogramme to Shs3,400. Usually when there is enough supply in the market a kilogramme of simsim retails at Shs2,500.
According to Ms Nakabiitto, supply will remain low throughout this year.
However, sweet potatoes are in plenty and have seen a drop in prices. A big piece is selling at Shs100 for wholesalers and Shs170 for retailers.

Original_Search_Title: Do Job Seekers Have Any Right to Privacy?
Headline: Uganda: Do Job Seekers Have Any Right to Privacy?
Body_Text:
Due to the increasing number of candidates for positions, employers are now looking for employability. This means they are looking for people with directly applicable and relevant skills. It is no longer shocking that for a graduate job, a PhD holder is attending the interview. Fresh graduates leave universities with heaps of advice such as: prepare yourself to demonstrate not only your 'head' knowledge but also its application at interview; give examples of group work, projects, programmes you can use, languages you can speak, etc.
Indeed to be able to stand out from the crowd, all job seekers are told to market and sell themselves as a 'ready to go' package, thus not someone who will need a lengthy probation period. But what are the realities out there? While many people do apply; the concluding clause in many job adverts is "only successful candidates will be contacted" hence all unsuccessful candidates will never get a response.
Many job seekers spend the little hard-earned money sending application letters and photocopies of laminated academic certificates, however, research shows that hardly do such people get any responses from potential employers. The obvious imbalance in supply and demand for labour has also resulted in the hiring companies feeling that they are in control and can demand whatever they want and offer whatever money they feel like; with people so desperate to get the jobs offered at any cost. Even the terms like project manager, business analyst seem to have taken on new meanings -- in other words they appear to be looking for people with many tasks that would warrant wearing many "hats".... no wonder everybody seems to refer to himself as a "something... consultant".
In a typical Ugandan style where much of our packaging is either in polythene bags or office waste paper, a friend of mine recently stumbled on the photocopy of his transcript which he had attached to an application for some job simply being used to wrap samosa and roasted sweet potatoes on the street.
This incident and many others related, have left me pondering as to whether job seekers have any right to privacy of their personal information, regardless of being in a desperate state at times? What about safe protection of their documents in a country infiltrated with forgeries of academic papers? By employers accepting to receive documents seeking employment; they are obliged to safely keep the documents.
Alternatively, employers should always request the job seekers to collect their documents or send back their documents with at least a comment as to why the job seeker was not an ideal candidate. This will enable the candidate make necessary adjustments that might make him/her a better competitor in future. The next time you hand in your application; ask yourself where your documents might end up?
Mr Kapyata is a lawyer

Original_Search_Title: Rector Harps On Science And Tech
Headline: Nigeria: Rector Harps On Science And Tech
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Rector of the Federal Polytechnic Offa, Dr. Mufutau Olatinwo has described science and technology education as a necessary ingredient for the acquisition of entrepreneurial skills and for national development.
Olatinwo, who made this known at the 4th National Conference of the School of Applied Science and Technology, with the theme, 'Science and Technology Education: Bedrock for Entrepreneurial Skill Acquisition and National Development', held at the polytechnic, said all efforts must be made to sustain the stride so as to move the nation forward.
"The process of equipping entrepreneurs with necessary skills should be through science and technology education, entrepreneurial training and development."
According to him, science and technology education could offer useful suggestions on how the country could benefit from applied sciences, frameworks, adding that it would also assist policy makers in taking steps that could rejuvenate and reposition the economy within the context of applied sciences skills applications.
The Rector also noted that the polytechnic was known internationally for its giant strides in sweet potato-for-bread and other confectioneries research. "At a convenient point on this research efforts, employment and investment opportunities will start to manifest, which will be to the benefit of the immediate communities and other Nigerians."
Also in his keynote address, Vice-Chancellor of Fountain University, Osogbo, Prof. Husseni Oloyede said higher institutions were critical determinants of a nation's development.
"Science and technology are closely related to the extent that one depends on the other for its development. The concept of science and technology relates to the totality of activities in a nation, which results to innovation", he said.
He added that science and technology help to reduce poverty and enhance international competitiveness.
He however expressed concern that science and technology development in the country was yet to attain its enviable status due to a number of challenges, which include the issues of funding, power thus, among others, saying that for students to acquire entrepreneurial skills, such skills should be reflected in the curriculum of various disciplines.

Original_Search_Title: Nothing to Show for Freedom Struggle
Headline: Kenya: Nothing to Show for Freedom Struggle
Body_Text:
At 74, Ann Njoki Gaichinga has seen much of what life has to offer; three years as a freedom fighter in the forests, two years as a detainee and the rest of her life in perpetual poverty.
At the Nyayo National Stadium on Tuesday, she sat sadly in the stands, dressed in her Sunday best, with a matching blue jacket, but without the cheer that befits the occasion.
She had been invited, and had a white card with a blue mark in one corner, but the officers at the gate to the VIP stand told her she was late and could not be allowed in.
She ambled over to a place on the terrace next to some chiefs in uniform, and from there cast her steady gaze at the dais where the leaders sat.
Ms Gaichinga spoke to the Nation of her sadness, not only at being kept away from the dais, but for the failure to have gained anything from her years as a freedom fighter.
"That freedom you talk about has no meaning for me. I do not like to talk about those days because it sounds like mockery, like someone who killed your brother but keeps telling you about it," she said when asked of her days as a freedom fighter.
She eventually opened up.
Ms Gaichinga was in Nairobi in 1952 when the British colonisers declared a state of emergency as the Mau Mau fighters stepped up the war on the colonialists.
She was living with her father, who worked at Firestone Tyre Factory, and used his pass to travel back home to Mugoiri in Murang'a.
"There was a lot of harassment by the white men and the homeguards, so I pretended to go to school to fool them," she said.
The pretence of learning was during the day but at night, she and several other girls would gather near the forest, where they would prepare roast sweet potatoes for the fighters.
They would throw the homeguards and chiefs offguard with their good looks and lead them into an ambush, where the fighters collected the guns and killed the collaborators.
Like many of her colleagues, she was captured during an unsuccessful raid, and was shot in the feet in the process, before being transported to detention camps.
She was jailed at Nyakianga, Kangema, Kamaguta, Murang'a and served her last years at Koimbi.
But when she returned to her village, there was hardly any land for her father's family; her parents had died, brothers jailed. The land was less than two acres for those who remained.
She married a man who feared her because of her past and battered her endlessly.
"Somebody had told him I was a fighter capable of killing."
She had her seventh child with him in 1978 and eventually left his home.
She rented a house in Maragua Town and settled there as a businesswoman dealing in avocados. She still buys and sells the fruit to an exporter.
"We were fighting for land, but you see, even if I were to die here today, I would have nothing to my name. Have you ever heard of anybody who fought for land and lives in a rented house?" she asked, her gaze set at the empty seats in a section of the VIP area and an entire stand on the President's left.
There were also 5,000 students from 24 secondary schools in Nairobi, dressed in red, black, white and green T-shirts and arranged in the stands to create a massive Kenyan flag.
Then there was the choir, said to be largely composed of staff from State House, the President's residence.
"The First Lady's State House Choir was started at the beginning of the year. It is an initiative of Her Excellency the First Lady, who saw the need to give staff working in various departments at State House a chance to share in the creative music experience," read the programme for on Tuesday.
It also has staff from the Office of the President, the Permanent Presidential Music Commission and has co-opted members from various Nairobi churches, making up a choir of 120 people.

Original_Search_Title: Child-Centred Approach Vital in Art
Headline: Zimbabwe: Child-Centred Approach Vital in Art
Body_Text:
Harare — THIRTEEN-YEAR-OLD Tawanda Maponga (not real name) of Mbare was born with HIV and he is aware of his status.
Tawanda and his mother Sheila rented a small dark room in Mbare and life was normal as his mother made sure he took his daily dosage of paediatric ARVs on time.
Sadly, his mother who was also HIV positive, is one of the people who died in the horrific Mhunga bus accident along the Harare to Masvingo highway last year.
"Soon after my mother's death, my aunt Mai Munya from Chitungwiza offered to stay with me since there was no one to pay rent for our lodgings.
"My aunt and all other relatives did not know that I am HIV positive since my mother did not tell them when I was tested. Only her friend, known as Achihera, knew our status and that we were both on anti-retroviral therapy.
"At the funeral, Achihera only told my aunt to take good care of me since I was sickly but did not tell her I was on ARVs," said Tawanda during an interview with The Herald.
The little boy said they stuck to a strict diet when his mother was alive, and he knew what to eat and what to avoid.
"My mother made sure I ate whole wheat bread, unrefined maize meal, avoid fatty and all processed foods.
"I had serious problems as my aunt fed me with junk food ranging from cakes, sweets, potato chips thinking she was pampering me.
"I was not comfortable taking my ARV's in her presence since she did not know my status. I did not want her to ask me too many questions so I took them every time I bathed in the morning," explained Tawanda.
He said his aunt knew about the ARV's when they ran out.
"I cried all day and my aunt got worried and asked what the problem was.
"I told her that I was afraid to die as the pills my mother used to give me to stay alive were finished.
"She did not understand and asked me to show her the empty containers. My aunt took the empty ARV packaging to the hospitals where they confirmed her worst fears," added Tawanda.
Tawanda is one of the thousands of opharned and vulnerable children on ART who face numerous challenges because of improper handover between guardians when the person who knows the child status dies.
According to Hospice and Palliative Care Association of Zimbabwe, clinical and technical advisor Mrs Eunice Garanganga, there is a lot of movement of OVC's among relatives when parents or guardians die thus compromising pediatric ART delivery services.
"Different people may take children to hospital but fail to update each other on the child's progress. "There is a lot of movement of OVC's amongst relatives when parents or guardians die thus compromising pediatric ART delivery services. "Orphans usually do not have permanent primary care givers and the new primary care giver might not see the need to keep appointment as a priority," she told journalists at a recent Media breakfast meeting on ART for children.
"No disclosure to the child resulting in child not knowing why he or she is taking tablets thus affects dedication.
"For those children on ART, monitoring progress and adherence is still weak because of inadequate laboratory testing. Generally, children have problems accessing second line treatment. Cases of sharing drugs with relatives affect adherence to ART for children," she added.
She pointed out that children in boarding schools faced challenges of lack of teachers with skills to continue supporting them and providing information on ART.
"Limited understanding of needs of adolescents living with HIV and lack of adolescent friendly reproductive health services is also a major cause for concern," added Mrs Garanganga.
She said children were not being tested because of religious beliefs and parents feared having their children tested.
"As a result, in most cases children's HIV status is not known and therefore ART services are not provided even when the child may already be infected," she added.
She revealed that there is general lack of knowledge on ART services available to children in some areas. There is also limited knowledge or information on automatic eligibility of children on ART as a result some children are not enjoying ART services, which could be readily available to them.
"Of the few institutions offering pediatric ART, there is reported lack of or CD4 machines not working consistently due to maintenance problems," she said.
While ART has ushered a new era in the management of HIV and Aids, children's ART is not widely available in Zimbabwe and neither is it being delivered in the most effective way to benefit all the children who need it.
"The current situation of Children's ART is marred with numerous challenges. During the meetings, it was highlighted that in 2009, only 20 percent of the children between 5- 17 years of age living with HIV and Aids are currently on ART.
"Children's formulations are still in limited supplies and the public health system, the main channel for providing children's ART is currently fragile therefore compromising accessibility of pediatric ART," she explained.
She said the health system is generally understaffed despite the efforts in recent years of training health professionals.
"The supply chain management still faces a lot of inadequacy with frequent stock outs of drugs in some areas and drugs expiring because of low uptake in other areas.
"Reports also indicated that general ART services are still not readily available in outlying and peripheral areas being central in core developed areas," she added.
Adherence in children's ART was identified as one of the major challenges in providing effective ART.
"There are inadequate diagnostic services for children below 18 months.
"Transport to the nearest service providers was reported to be unaffordable on regular basis for most families and this has compromised adherence treatment as medication is not collected on a regular basis.
Lack of proper child counselling skills among service providers and the limited information dissemination of pamphlets and other information that include policy documents to children were major problems.
"Information given should be the same at all levels. This will improve on quality of care.
"Faith healing, where some churches would tell the child to stop taking the ARV's because they have been healed by prayers affects uptake and adherence to ART.
"It was also noted with great concern that children are not consulted when tested for their HIV status or when put on ART thus compromising their rights and also the current policy on testing of minors overrides issues of accessibility and human rights. "Minors cannot be tested without consent from parents or guardians under this current policy though it is under review.
"Children, generally participate very little in programmes that affect them. Service providers and parents or guardians plan for and implement programmes for the children," she said.
Head of the Aids and Tuberculosis Unit in the Ministry of Health and Child Welfare, Dr Owen Mugurungi said of the 105 740 children living with HIV and Aids in Zimbabwe, only 25 000 were on treatment.
Dr Mugurungi said the number of children in urgent need for ART was 35 189.
Of the 117 institutions giving ARV's, 70 were pediatric ART sites.
He said 95 percent of children who were HIV positive acquired the virus from their mothers, an indication that the prevention of mother to child transmission programmes were not working optimally.
There was a need for holistic and coordinated support strategies that focus on children in this era of rapid scale of treatment for children.
Service providers from the community to the tertiary level of the health delivery system need to be adequately equipped through training, the provision of adequate resources such as drugs, machines and other support services to provide quality service to the children.
Providing a child friendly environment that responds and addresses the physical, psychological, social and mental need of children should be a prerequisite.
A family and child-centred approach that involves the child and the parents, or caregivers addresses information, education, and communication issues about the care of the infected child from HIV pre and post testing accent and consent as well as ongoing care and support.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Impress Over Donations for Landslides Victims
Headline: Uganda: Bududa Donations Reach Shs15 Billion
Body_Text:
The value of donations raised by the government, aid agencies, religious groups, companies and individuals in response to the devastating landslides in Bududa District last month reached around Shs15bn, Daily Monitor calculated yesterday.
There is no central figure yet collated for the donations given in both cash and kind, but this is an estimate based on conversations with a variety of stakeholders this week.
"I am profoundly impressed by the local, national and international effort," said Mr Musa Ecweru, Minister of State for Disaster Preparedness. "Even the local poor have offered things like a bowl of sweet potatoes."
By far the largest amount of money has come from the government itself, which made Shs10bn available for the relief efforts for flood and landslide victims in various districts throughout the country. Parliament approved the amount, in response to a Shs27bn Emergency Preparedness and Response Budget request from the Office of the Prime Minister (OPM).
Other big donors include the Red Cross (Shs2.2bn) and the United Nations (Shs2bn).
Details on how this money has been spent is currently being collated and will take time to process, said Mr Bigirimana, Permanent Secretary of the OPM.
But Mr Martin Owor, Commissioner for Disaster Preparedness, gave Daily Monitor preliminary information collated by the OPM and the Bududa District Disaster Management Committee, which showed that in total 148.3 tonnes of maize flour, 30.2 tonnes of rice and 62.5 tonnes of beans have been distributed, along with 970 tarpaulins, 40 tents, 12,624 blankets, 29,867 bars of soap, 1,733 mosquito nets, and over 400 bags of clothes, amongst other items.
An assortment of well wishers contributed to the Red Cross' Shs2bn fund, including foreign governments, banks, telecommunication companies, food manufacturers, media houses, religious organizations, sports clubs and artistic groups.
The money is being distributed across several districts affected by mudslides and flooding. Emergency expenditure has bought essentials such as saucepans, cups, plates, jerry cans, soap, tents, water treatment chemicals, as well as generators, latrines and washing shelters.
Catherine Ntabadde, Red Cross Senior Public Relations Officer, said, "The response to this appeal has been overwhelming." But donations are yet to reach the Shs5bn Red Cross appeal, with a shortfall of Shs2.8bn.
The UN agencies including WHO, WFP and UNICEF have together spent around $1m (Shs2bn).
Tim Pitt, the Head of OHCHR said, "We believe the disaster response was largely within the capacity of the government and civil society of Uganda," he said. "This is good news for Uganda."
World Vision gave over Shs40m, which paid for psychosocial services and child protection services.
Oxfam gave around Â£82,000 (Shs250m) of which one third has already been spent. They aim to shift their focus from emergency sanitation provision to long term disaster risk reduction strategies, said Ben Pickering.

Original_Search_Title: Gourmet Treats for Zuma and Friends
Headline: Namibia: Gourmet Treats for Zuma and Friends
Body_Text:
Government will spare no expense when it rolls out the proverbial red carpet for its high-profile international guests this weekend, during which champagne costing N$1 000 a bottle will be served at a State banquet.
The dignitaries will include Zimbabwean President Robert Mugabe and his South African counterpart Jacob Zuma.
According to the official programme for the weekend's festivities, at least 15 heads of state or their representatives are expected to arrive in Windhoek between 14h00 and 17h00 on Saturday afternoon.
It is unknown whether these invited guests will make a brief turn at the gospel concert at the Independence Arena at 18h30 before heading towards the flagship function - a sit-down gala dinner at the prestigious Safari Hotel.
President-elect Hifikepunye Pohamba and his wife Penehupifo will be the hosts at this event, which promises to be nothing short of lavish.
It is understood that about 800 people will be treated to fillet of silver angelfish and fish terrine served with a prawn skewer on a bed of lettuce. This starter will be rounded off with a Mary Rose sauce.
All the stops are pulled out with the main course of this finely crafted menu. Guests will be able to treat their palates to fillets of beef and oryx, accompanied by chicken cordon bleu on a bed of mashed potatoes served with a carrot and green bean bundle.
Should there still be space on their plates, the delegation can enjoy a phyllo pastry basket with creamy mushroom spinach and cranberry sauce.
The Namibian understands that Pohamba and his guests will have the option to quench their thirst with very expensive French champagne.
Those dignitaries with a sweet tooth will conclude the evening on a high note: delectable Austrian dessert served with berry coulis and garnished with segments of fresh fruit, before heading to bed to be ready for an almost 10-hour programme on Sunday during which President Pohamba, the Prime Minister and the Deputy Prime Minister will be sworn in.

Original_Search_Title: Food and Travel - Nostalgia Trip to L'escargot
Headline: Zimbabwe: Food and Travel - Nostalgia Trip to L'escargot
Body_Text:
Returning to the once hugely popular L'Escargot at Courteney Hotel in The Avenues, is like bumping into an old friend who's been overseas several years. Whereas the former chum has gained or lost weight, hair is thinner (or missing) and the odd frown lines add or diminish facial character, take a nostalgic trip down memory lane and L'Escargot -- at first sight at least -- hasn't changed in a third of a century.
Well, some of the staff are unchanged, the menu's identical, except prices; cooking's very similar. Thirty-odd years ago, the still fondly remembered, well-respected maitre d'hôtel, Henry Banda would have been meeting, greeting and seating, with a trademark smile and total discretion over who dined with whom.
In those days it could take three weeks to get a table on more popular nights and most lunch times. It was easily among the nation's top five restaurants. They probably sold more Irish coffees on any one night than all the capital's other outlets did in a week!
Sadly Henry finally retired to his native Malawi. (It was Nyasaland when he arrived in this city!) but didn't put his feet up long. Of venerable age and firmly one of the old school in the hospitality sector, he was soon called to the great dining room in the sky.
But his name lives on in L'Escargot's hardwood covered menus with the distinctive snail carving on the front. Tournedos Henry Banda, featuring the most deliciously tender well hung export quality fillet steak and topped with young asparagus and Hollandaise sauce is $10, coming with a choice of starch and pleasantly steamed vegetables.
I wasn't in the mood for steak, no matter how succulent when I called unannounced on Friday. In the good old-bad old days it was an exercise in sheer futility to simply rock-up at the distinctively designed -- also unchanged -- rustic brick restaurant without a confirmed booking.
I was in that end of The Avenues and wanted to avoid a mother of a storm building up and rolling in rapidly from the east. Later I was to learn we had an 86mm lashing in Eastlea; Ha-ha-ha-rare was to become a gridlocked, bad-tempered city of impatient fifth-rate anarchist drivers.
Chaos reigned throughout the municipal area; many traffic lights failed and -- as usual -- there was no sign of the ZRP taking charge, trying to sort out snarl ups.
Its "colonial" predecessor boasted, with much justification, being a force to be reckoned with. Currently, no matter what the circumstances, when the solids hit the fan, the Zimbabwe Republic Police is usually a force to be sensibly reckoned without!
The barman served me an icily cold Pilsener, apologising they only had "bombers", so-called "quarts", holding 750ml of lager. I needed no apology, especially as it was just $2.
A table of 15 early-door punters from a "talkfest" at the hotel left as I arrived and, including me, there were possibly another 12 customers on what, for many folk, was month-end pay day Friday. Clearly the situation has changed from having to wait three weeks for a table! I ordered, with confidence, splendid minestrone ($2), the recipe for which goes back to the days when Atilio Vigoriti ran the hotel with Nick Mandeya (they're now down-sized at Adrienne's, Belgravia).
It was delicious, served with a too-tempting basket of toast, soft white bread and crunchy bread sticks with butter. Chef's salad, also $2, was a generous bowl of grand ingredients including more-ish purple olives and soft cheese.
I wanted fillets of Kariba bream at $5, a local take on the traditional worldwide Friday favourite fish and chips, but tilapia wasn't "on". I like trout, but it was double the cost.
I usually have my Nyanga (or, more recently, Scots) trout grilled or baked whole, sometimes stuffed, with head, eyes and tail on; by now crispy skin intact. I was talked into trying the fish dish "split", ie butter-flied: decapitated, de-boned, eyeless, tailless and denuded of skin. The mother of my children, who never liked supper staring at her, would approve.
There was nothing wrong with the cooking, presentation or taste, but on my next trip down memory lane, I'll revert to ordering the fish in the old-fashioned way. I'd saved half the salad to go with a dish which came with sliced boiled potatoes, then greens and (oddly) chips followed. My Irish pals would approve!
It seems churlish to criticise a sweet which was "on the house", but fruit salad comprised only apple and banana. It was sharp and pleasant, but seriously bland-looking. Most diners-out "eat with their eyes" and as the nation's fruit counters burst with red apples and oranges, water and other types of ultra delicious melon, plums, nectarines, green, black and red grapes, mangos, guavas, strawberries, raspberries, blueberries, bilberries and chuck berries, among much other colourful natural goodness, it was disappointing not to have at least a splash of brightness in the bowl....with some vanilla ice-cream.
The King and Queen of Spain were guests at "old" L'Escargot and if Their Majesties were to return today, they wouldn't be disappointed in the a la carte restaurant now run by GM John Siyachitema, who has returned from the US.
Look out for eponymous snails as a starter: L'Escargot de Bourgogne at $5 for half a dozen, artichoke hearts cooked in garlic butter ($5) and a really retro proper prawn cocktail, $10.
Kingklip fillet is $20, queen prawns $12, kings $15; quails en croute with pepper sauce $10. Pepper sauce is perhaps the authentic taste of L'Escargot which memory recalls and an excellent man-sized fillet, dramatically flambéed at your table, smothered in a black peppercorn-rich sauce is $15.
My soup, salad, grilled trout and two "quarts" of Pilsener came to $18.
L'Escargot is a residential hotel and opens for lunch and supper daily. Smart-casual dress.

Original_Search_Title: Magistrate Remanded over Bribery
Headline: Uganda: Magistrate Remanded over Bribery
Body_Text:
Kabale — Kisoro Grade One magistrate David Cheptuke Kaye was on Wednesday remanded to Ndorwa Prison in Kabale District after he pleaded not guilty to charges of soliciting a bribe.
Court presided over by Grade One magistrate Jude Okumu, remanded the accused untill March 3 when his case shall be ready for mention.
The State prosecutor, Mr Thomas Okoth, told court that investigations into the matter were still going on, adding that once they are complete the case against Mr Kaye will be taken to the Anti-Corruption Court in Kampala for adequate handling.
Mr Cheptuke was arrested on Tuesday after he allegedly received a bribe of Shs100,000 from John Bosco Nyundo, who wanted bail for his relatives Scovia Busingye and Denis Musekura. The two were being charged in Kisoro magistrate's court over allegedly destroying the sweet potato gardens of Rev. Innocent Nshizirungu of Nyakabande Sub-county in Kisoro.

Original_Search_Title: Major Reap as January Inflation Drops Two Points
Headline: Tanzania: Major Reap as January Inflation Drops Two Points
Body_Text:
Dar Es Salaam — WITH the run towards a target of single digit inflation by June this year gathering momentum, Bank of Tanzania (BoT) Governor Benno Ndulu has a reason to smile.
National Bureau of Statistic's latest monthly consumer price index report shows that inflation fell from 12.2 per cent last December to 10.9 per cent last month.
Professor Ndulu is targeting an inflation rate of seven per cent by June this year, according to reports by the Central Bank. The target failed last year due to increasing prices for essentials that kept inflation rate hovering at 14-15 per cent against the five per cent target.
Professor Ndulu said high rise in prices on global fuel prices, depreciation of the shilling and falling commodity prices was due to global economic recession. But, last month's inflation rate gives Professor Ndulu reasons to stay on course and attain the ambitious target.
However, the latest inflation figure remains high as compared to January 2009's rate of 8.6 per cent when the incumbent governor, a former World Bank director, clocked one year in office. Professor Ndulu took over at the BoT helm in January 2007.
NBS said food inflation rate decreased to 11.3 percent in the year ended January 2010 from 14.5 percent in the previous month.
"The monthly headline inflation rate for the month of January 2010 rose by 1.7 per cent. The increase under consideration was highly attributed to the increase of fuel prices by 4.8 per cent and food prices by 1.9 per cent," reads the NBS report.
Fuel items that contributed to the price increase include kerosene and charcoal. Food items that contributed to price rise were cereals, sweet potatoes, cooking bananas, vegetables, fish, beans, cowpeas, sugar, groundnuts and coconut mature, the report stated.
Non food inflation rate has increased by 1.2% in January 2010. Other non food items that had increased prices include furniture, household equipments and education materials.
The report stated that from January 2002, the value of 100/-, which is the official purchasing power, has been decreasing, declining to 55/- and 58 cents in January 2010.
The National Consumer Price Index covers prices collected in 20 towns in Tanzania Mainland for 207 items dominated by food items.

Original_Search_Title: Kisoro District Magistrate Arrested over Bribe
Headline: Uganda: Kisoro District Magistrate Arrested over Bribe
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Kisoro Grade One Magistrate was yesterday arrested andâ-‚locked up at Kisoro Police Station for several hours over allegations of taking a bribe.
David Cheptuke Kaye was picked up by detectives from the office of the Inspector General of Government (IGG) at the court chambers around 9:30am as he was allegedly receiving sh100,000 from John Bosco Nyundo.
Nyundo said he had been sent by his relatives to bribe Kaye so that he could release them on bail.
In an interview at the Police station, Nyundo said on January 29, he and his relatives, Denis Musekura, Scovia Busiingye and another whose name he did not give, were asked by the magistrate to pay sh300,000 to be granted bail, but later disagreed with him when they insisted on getting a receipt for the money.
The three had been charged with malicious damage of property. They were accused by the Rev. Innocent Nshizirungu of Nyakabande parish of destroying beans and sweet potatoes in his gardens on January 12.
"It was then that I contacted the IGG officials who have now arrested him," Nyundo said.
Kaye admitted having received the sh100,000 but said it was part payment of a loan he gave Nyundo last month when the latter had a patient in Mutorele Hospital.
"He (Nyondo) is being used by people who do not want me to be in this district," he said.
Kaye added that the complainant, who holds a diploma in law, had been pestering him for a job as a court clerk but he had advised him to wait for the Public Service Commission to advertise.
The district criminal investigations officer, Fred Lumala, said Kaye would be transferred to Kampala for prosecution.
Last year, the inspector of courts summonedâ-‚Kaye over complaints of sexual harassment and asked him to explain the allegations, according to a letter which The New Vision saw.

Original_Search_Title: Babylon Residents Make the Best of a Deplorable Situation
Headline: Namibia: Babylon Residents Make the Best of a Deplorable Situation
Body_Text:
Windhoek — As heavy clouds cover the skyline of Windhoek, promising another day of rain, Babylon residents are already up and going.
Shebeens are already open for business and while some patrons are already indulging in traditionally brewed beer at nine in the morning, others a busy going about the business of doing laundry as well as other daily chores.
Scores of children are playing in the streets even those in school uniforms find themselves playing in pools of muddy water.
The informal settlement, which is one of the fastest growing settlements in Windhoek, is home to a number of people who come to the capital in search for a better life. But every so often, life in the city turns out to be worse than what they left behind in their places of origin.
But instead of taking it lying down, many residents are working hard to make ends meet.
Take Damien Kashihalwa for example. He completed a training course in carpentry, woodwork and bricklaying at the Katutura Youth Enterprise Centre (KAYEC) last year. Despite his efforts to find work, he is still unemployed.
Kashihalwa decided to put his training to use and has started to make chairs which he sells at N$100 per chair.
He only started selling chairs recently but has already made a bed, a couple of chairs and is planning on making cupboards for his own use.
"I want to make business, but currently it is a bit difficult because we do not have electricity in our houses and I cannot always use generators. I have to go to the Okahandja Park market whenever I want to produce something, this is a bit challenging. I would appreciate it if we could get electricity for our houses," Kashihalwa says.
Kashihalwa is not the only one who took up starting a small business to make ends meet.
As one walks through Babylon you notice how many people sell one thing or the other. Anything goes, from recharge vouchers, chicken legs and fish to 'kapana'.
It however seems as though, selling alcohol remains one of the most popular ventures.
Each and everyday, Martha Sizula, a mother of five, walks to the Okahandja Park market to sell potatoes, sweets, onions and packets of chips.
Sometimes she only makes N$10 a day, depending on customers.
"It is better than nothing, if I can make enough to buy bread for my children to take to school, it is enough. We survive on the money I make by selling this stuff. I do it for my children," she says.
With a little encouragement, investment and hard work, Sizula's business and that many other residents of the capital's informal settlements can become flourishing, viable businesses.

Original_Search_Title: Children - a Crucial National Asset
Headline: Zimbabwe: Children - a Crucial National Asset
Body_Text:
Harare — NATIONWIDE, Monday and Tuesday were purely glorious days.
There was so much hustling and bustling as the 2010 school calendar got underway on on January 12.
Monday, was the day for pupils in boarding. Those black trunks with all sorts of inscriptions on them, and the shear amount of luggage brought back memories of yesteryears, although we never carried that much stuff.
That goes to show the fallacies about the good old days!
But the array of school uniforms, hairstyles observed at one of the bus stops was lovely: a real rainbow. This is a plus for the schools systems that made it mandatory for pupils to wear uniforms when going to and from school.
The crowning moment was Tuesday morning when the new entrants into the education systems, both Grade Zeroes and Grade Ones who could not hide their excitement, confidently walked to their respective schools in their crisp uniforms, feeling so grown up and so knowledgeable and responsible.
But how very disappointing that the rude awakening that confronted some of them on that first day was the unavailability of teachers as reported in the media.
But when the teachers' grievances are eventually resolved, they will confront the real one, and in some cases weekly, mum and dad will have to put aside some money to replace that pair of shoes, socks, hat and sometimes school jersey that are either lost or forgotten or just given away to a new-found-friend. This comes with the territory called growing pains!
On Tuesday, the little boys and girls confidently marched on like soldiers, satchels on their backs, hands in their pockets, but just walking on, with no idea whatsoever, where the school was. Some of the mothers who were accompanying them could not keep pace with those self-assured steps.
What a marvel it was to watch, when it was also very evident that in less than two hours a good number of these bundles of joy and energy would be fed up with whatever they will be doing in whatever room, and with whatever stranger who will be telling them lots of do's and don'ts.
The likelihood is that the poor teacher had so many coming up to him or her sulking and saying: "I want to go back to my Mamma," or "I am hungry".
But children being children, it is also not surprising that by the end of that first day, they had already connected with each other, and playing as if they had always known one another -- a symbolic feature of that age of innocence that slowly gets eroded with time.
Mrs Stella Mlauzi of Mufakose who was accompanying her Grade One daughter made this interesting remark, "For Grade Ones, real school starts in June.
You will literally scream your lungs out as it will almost be impossible to get these very enthusiastic faces you see now out of bed." But our hearts go out to all the gallant men and women who have the unenviable task of workmanship.
What a job, just being handed over someone's child, and being given that enormous national responsibility to mould those little beings so that they progress on and on until they become the President of the Republic of Zimbabwe; inventor; nurse; doctor; engineer; soldier; police officer, etc.
But above all, doing so until those little beings eventually realise that they are Zimbabwe's most critical assets who have a major role to make this great nation a major player in the global arena.
That Grade One teacher has also been trained to realise that his or her duty is not only fashioning someone for onward transmission to the next level in the school system, but that what he or she is doing is laying the foundations of nation building.
For these tiny beings will be the mothers and fathers of the future, who will set up family structures based on the initial values that might start with asking the child to loudly tell others his or her name is, to enable peaceful co-existence in the classroom, and beyond.
The same with those who are enrolling for Form One. Most of them still have to get placements since Grade Seven results were only released last week.
Every child going back to school is moving one level up, and this is where the real issues are, for various changes take place: physical, psychological, and emotional and, in some cases even spiritual.
Takudzwa Nyamayaro of Highfield who started Grade Seven on Tuesday could not contain his excitement as he exclaimed: "Yeah, gore rino ndisu tiri panyanga." (Yeah, this year we are in charge.)
Adults take many things for granted. Ambitions and the power game start at an early age. I also wondered what Takudzwa's class would be like by year end. Would they really be in charge, and in charge of what?
But it is not a misnomer to state that this academic year is starting at a fairly good note. Well I thought so, until I saw the news report about teacher absenteeism.
Optimism abounded across the board because this is 2010, a year declared by many as a year of new beginnings, breakthroughs and achievements.
The major driver to the hopes and expectations is the relative improvement in the economic situation after the introduction of multiple currencies at the beginning of last year.
This has brought about some improvements, although much more still needs to be done.
It is a development that many believe will resuscitate the ailing education system, which has not been spared by the economic challenges caused mainly by the illegal sanctions.
Adults are products of past visionaries who saw that the legacy they can bequeath on Zimbabwe is high standard education system. This is our heritage, and that legacy must be passed on.
A sound education system is the flagship of any successful nation, and this is what The Arena desires for Zimbabwe.Children are also the nation's priced possessions, its untapped assets, and, in order for Zimbabwe to be what it desires to be, these assets must be taken care of in a responsible manner.
Their future must be shaped with wisdom, for they are the future that will also craft an even better tomorrow -- standing their own in an increasingly competitive and sometimes hostile environment. As the Latin phrase "in loco parentis" implies, teachers and the whole school system, act in the place or role of parents. They do not replace the family structure, but complement and in some cases supplement it.
As we all get back to restoring that vanishing glory, a major question is: what really happens from the time when a child starts school up to the time when he or she is called a problem child? To use a biblical term, why do some parents lose their first love, the love that observers always witness when a child enters the school system the first time?
Why is the momentum not maintained from the time parents make that painful decision on which school to send their child, up to the time he or she graduates with a "cum laude" in their field of study?
Why does interest centre so much on purchase of uniforms, books and payment of school fees, and not the person for which all these are being done? Is this abdication of parental responsibility? If so, why?
By end of January, how many parents (both parents) will confidently claim that they know the names of their children's class teachers; the school headmasters and deputies; the chairpersons of the School Development Associations; the syllabus; the school's code of conduct and grievance procedure, etc?
And, how many parents by end of first term will know their children's friends and where they reside; and, why their son or daughter chose to be friends with them?
But above all, how many parents would be able to confidently articulate the behaviour change in their children, apart from class performance? As it is, how many parents know what stuff their children watch or listen to be it on radio, television, the Internet, mobile phones, and all the gizmos that are a major attraction to this touch button generation?
The Arena raises these concerns because we have made observations over a long period, and noted that some children are using a lot of these things without parental guidance.
Part of the teacher education curriculum includes psychology, sociology and philosophy of education. These equip teachers and school heads to understand and comprehend the various behaviour patterns they come across, and sometimes how best to deal with them.
Parenting is different.
It is an on-the-job training scenario.
At the end of the first term, how many parents will be able to identify these behaviour changes and take appropriate corrective measures that are in sync with the family's value and those of the school?
If year-in, year-out a Grade One teacher is able to mould and pass on products that will become a high achiever, how about parents?
While we are not stereotyping parents or schools, we pose the question, "are you watching the children?" We ask because of the gravity of problems emanating from our schools, ranging from delinquency to problems associated with spiritual issues.
Most of these problems have always been blamed on peer pressure, and in some cases, school authorities have come under severe criticism because of the in loco parentis aspect.
But what role do parents play in this systemic breakdown of their children's lives and the general educational standards? How are parents contributing in the destruction of these national assets?
We raise these questions because for years, the media have reported cases of strange goings-on at some schools across the nation. These are probably a fraction of the true national picture as institutions work very hard to keep things under wraps.
Last November when The Herald reported about strange activities of a spiritual nature at one of the country's best rated schools in Harare, Eaglesvale, it shocked everybody, because nobody could believe that mass hysteria could also attack pupils at such an ivy league school.
Just before Eaglesvale, there was another case of "deadly rings" at an unnamed school, which caused so much harm among the families and children concerned. There was also another case of mass hysteria at an unnamed primary boarding school in Masvingo. And if trends are followed, the mass hysteria is usually at girls' schools. What is the secret behind this?
Radio Zimbabwe presenter Tilda Karidzamimba has delved into these issues with the hope that the nation comes up with viable solutions that would bring back normalcy in our schools and families as well.
It has been so easy to apportion blame on goblins and other spiritual elements, but how many cases of drug abuse (with fancy drugs like Ecstasy) and alcohol abuse are going on in the homes and schools, without the parents' knowledge? Then, there is a new craze that has hit urban-based parents. That addiction is called "private schools" and "extra lessons", with many unscrupulous and bogus dealers ripping off a majority of these unsuspecting parents.
Last week, someone wrote to The Herald complaining that the so-called private schools were ripping them off.
The question is, notwithstanding the challenges faced in the past few years, why are people opting for these bogus institutions, when Government and tax payers invested so much in educational infrastructures in the past three decades?
Who is teaching pupils in these bogus when the education sector like other sectors has been seriously affected by the brain drain of teachers?
But as The Arena writes, in most high density suburbs where there is hardly space for recreational facilities, the rate at which the so-called private schools or colleges are mushrooming is amazing, and parents have no qualms at all enrolling their children in those places.
Where are these private colleges housing these pupils and who in Government is granting these permits? Why are they being accredited when they do not meet the minimum standards, and when Government schools beckon for more children?
And, why are some churches working against Government initiatives, for they are leasing their church buildings to these bogus educators? There is a marked difference between the Shona sayings: "Chakabaya chikatyokera" and "Mbambaira ikangoera yagonya, yotoda moto kuitwasanudza".
The first one is a pointer to a deep-seated problem that is beyond redemption. And the second one acknowledges that problems are like a sweet potato, which is normally twisted.
However, it takes some effort to straighten it through burning it up. This is exactly where we are: we are like sweet potatoes that need to be fired up.
Investing in the future is everybody's business. It is time that schools start alumni, and past students invest in the development of those schools.
With high fliers scattered all over the globe, why should the Zimbabwean education system continue to suffer to the point of near collapse?
In 2010, why should kids who enter school doors where most of us passed through be made to feel that after all, the first day at school is a non event?

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Hits Single Digit
Headline: Zambia: Inflation Hits Single Digit
Body_Text:
ZAMBIA's annual rate of inflation has dropped to single digit brackets as at December 2009 after maintaining double digits brackets throughout the ended year.
The annual rate of inflation, as measured by the Consumer Price Index (CPI), declined by 1.6 percentage points from 11.5 per cent in November 2009 to 9.9 per cent in December 2009.
Central Statistical Office (CSO) director, Efreda Chulu said the decline of 1.6 percentage points in the annual rate in December 2009 was as a result of decreases in some food prices.
Releasing the latest economic statistics for the month of December 2009 in Lusaka yesterday, Ms Chulu said price increases recorded between November and December 2009 were lower compared to price increases between November and December 2008.
Of the total 9.9 per cent annual inflation in December 2009, food products accounted for 4.0 percentage points while non-food products in the CPI collectively accounted for a total of 5.9 percentage points.
"Contributing most to the decline in food were decreases in the cost of meat, dried kapenta, preserved meat, sugar, other fresh vegetables and fresh fruits.
However, increases were recorded in the cost of mealie meal, maize grain, cereal products, cooking oil, tomatoes, rape, dried beans, sweet potatoes, salt and other processed food products," she said.
On national average prices of selected products, a comparison of retail prices between November 2009 and December 2009 shows that the national average price of a one kilogramme of dried kapenta (Siavonga) reduced by 2.9 per cent from K49,990 to K48, 526.
The national average price of one kilogramme of cucumber reduced by 12.5 per cent from K4,179 to K3,655.
However, the national average price of a 25 kilogramme bag of white roller meal increased by 3.1 per cent, from K46,289 to K47,736 while the average price of a 20 litre tin of maize grain increased by 6.1 per cent from K24,325 to K25,806.
During the month of November, Zambia recorded a trade surplus valued at K332.9 billion.
The country's major export product in November 2009 was from the intermediate goods category accounting for 72.7 per cent, comprising mainly copper cathodes and sections of refined copper, plates, wire and sheets of refined copper and articles of cobalt.
Ms Chulu said the CSO revised the poverty lines of the Living Conditions Monitoring survey of 1996, 1998, 2004 and 2006.
She said the 2006 poverty results showed that poverty had declined from 68.1 per cent in 1996 to 59.3 per cent in 2006.

Original_Search_Title: Minister of Finance Announces Procedures for National Resources Development
Headline: Sudan: Minister of Finance Announces Procedures for National Resources Development
Body_Text:
The Minister of Finance and National Economy, Ali Mahmoud, has announced a package of measures targeting the development of the national resources in the general budget for the year 2011
Top of that measures are to contain of the sparing of national resources, shrink in collection of fees, combating all forms of taxand customs evasion, controlling the parallel market and stabilizing imbalances in the supply and demand which affect the basic needs of consumers, particularly the necessary goods such as sugar, wheat and sorghum
Addressing the Conference of the Legal and Procedural Mechanisms on Control of the Public Money, which was organized by the federal Centre for Research and Capacity Building at the Friendship Hall Wednesday, the minister renewed his ministry's commitment to the control of financial performance and implementation of the declared policies in the new budget for reducing the government expenditure
He affirmed the importance of mobilizing the official and people's efforts as well as the efforts of private sector, civil society organizations and all institutions in combating financial corruption and reducing the embezzlement of public money in its all forms
The minister pledged to boost the implementation of the conference recommendations, expecting the outcome of the conference to contribute to help the ministry complete the financial and institutional reform operations, besides restructuring of the general budget, increasing the financial management efficiency, realization of transparency, enhancin the monitoring tools and mechanisms and expanding the government financial statistics on the federal budget level, alongside implementation of the state resources' planning system with the aim to control expenditure and to upgrade the performance efficiency to consolidate the government efforts aiming to complete the electronic government system and the network linking between the financial and conventional organs

Original_Search_Title: The German Warship FGs Koeln Leaves EU Navfor After More Than Two Months On Operation
Headline: Somalia: The German Warship FGs Koeln Leaves EU Navfor After More Than Two Months On Operation
Body_Text:
On 24 November, FGS KÃ-LN conducted her last day of operations as a part of EU NAVFOR, after 74 days of counter-piracy activities in the Gulf of Aden and the Somali Basin.
EU NAVFOR Force Commander, Rear-Admiral Philippe Coindreau, expressed his deep satisfaction to the warship for the contribution given to EU NAVFOR Operation.
FGS KÃ-LN conducted a variety of tasks whilst deployed. Of particular note was the disruption she conducted on 4 October, enabling her to foil an attack against a merchant vessel after she received her Mayday call. She also safely escorted the MV VICTORIA, chartered by the World Food Programme, enabling the delivery of 3,881 tons of food (maize, salt, oil, corn-soya blend, and sorghum) to Somalia.
"You have undoubtedly a part of the good results we obtain in the counter-piracy mission led by the European Union" said Rear-Admiral Philippe Coindreau, EU NAVFOR Force Commander, in his farewell message to the warship.
The warship has 216 crewmembers and has been operating as an EU NAVFOR unit since 13 September 2010.
EU NAVFOR Somalia - Operation ATALANTA's main tasks are to escort merchant vessels carrying humanitarian aid of the World Food Programme (WFP) and vessels of African Union Mission in Somalia, AMISOM and to protect vulnerable vessels in the Gulf of Aden and Indian Ocean and to deter and disrupt piracy. In addition, EU NAVFOR monitors fishing activity off the coast of Somalia.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Corp Makes Strides in Attaining Medium Term Goal
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Corp Makes Strides in Attaining Medium Term Goal
Body_Text:
ZIMBABWE Stock Exchange-listed blue-chip counter, Delta Corporation has made significant strides towards attaining its medium term goal of achieving annual output of 7,3 million hector litres through various capital expenditure projects.
The alcohol and beverage manufacturer has over the past six months managed to launch the 350 000 hectolitres per year PET line and also completed work on its beer packaging line in Bulawayo.
For the six months ended September 30 the group recorded a 50,5 percent growth in revenues on the comparative half-year period to US$214,5 million while half- year to half-year revenues were 14 percent up.
An analysis of the local bourse shows that Delta is the second biggest company after telecommunications operator, Econet Wireless in terms of revenue.
