During the period under review, Delta's operating income was US$27,2 million of which US$1,2 million accounted for finance charges and a loss from their associate Afdis of US$366 000 was deducted.
Some US$1,3 million income arising from the acquisition of Schweppes was added to give the group profit before tax of US$27 million.
The income tax expense for the group for the period amounted to US$6,5 million, which saw the group post profit from continuing operations.
Delta closed the period under review with net asset value of US$183,2 million with total assets standing at US$298,1 million.
The group then capped it all by declaring a half-cent dividend.
During the same period, the group also recorded marked market share growth across all beverages on the back of improved product supply matched by strong demand for lagers and soft drinks achieving market share of 97 percent and 90 percent respectively.
Capital expenditure for the period amounted to US$35,2 million half of which was in new returnable containers.
The group's gross borrowings amounted to US$23 million at an average cost of 10 percent with tenures ranging from 90 to 360 days.
Half-year total beverage production amounted to 2,8 million hectolitres driven by a 48 percent and 63 percent surge in lagers and sparkling beverages.
Traditional beers volumes were four percent down, consequently volume mix closed at 18 percent.
The group launched its PET range and the energy drink Burn over the period under review and on the beers side Golden Pilsner was re-launched and Castle Light was launched in the market.
The associate Schweppes Zimbabwe Limited with a market share of 65-70 percent achieved 65 percent volumes growth in the nine months to September 30 and is forecast to post US$5,5 million earnings before interest and taxes for its full year to December.
Afdis on the other hand was in a loss position.
However, a restructuring exercise that cut the head count by 35 percent and wage bill by 45 percent coupled with the positive change to the customs excise regimes in the mid-term fiscal policy is expected to see the associate return to profitability in 2011.
The group declared a dividend of US0,50c per share, which come up to US$5,9 million translating to a four times dividend cover.
These dividends are payable out of the profits of the company for the year ended March 31.
Going forward, lager volumes for the month of October were 54 percent ahead of prior year and three percent ahead of plan while the under supply for the market in the southern region has been reduced with the coming on board of the Belmont production line.
A good recovery in volumes driven by the one litre Shake-Shake product line slowed down the sorghum beer volumes decline.
Sparkling beverages volumes were 41 percent ahead of prior year and ahead of plan while operating margins improved to 19,2 percent.
In the outlook period the group forecast to achieve beverages volumes of six million hectolitres and operating in a market share of between 90 percent and 97 percent.
They are further targeting earning before interest and tax of US$74 million and management believes they are generating sufficient cash to fund dividends to shareholders.

Original_Search_Title: Massive Recovery at Rufaro Marketing
Headline: Zimbabwe: Massive Recovery at Rufaro Marketing
Body_Text:
Rufaro Marketing, the liquor division of the City of Harare, says turnover shot up by 78 percent during the 11 months ending July 31 2010 to US$3,7 million in response to a string of measures implemented by the entertainment group's new board, which included a reshuffle of top management, cost containment and the introduction of new products lines.
The liquor group was on the threshold of collapse only 12 months ago.
But the Toderai Chigumadzi-led board, which came into office in April 2009 was buoyant this week that in spite of the sorry state of Rufaro's properties some of which are in an advanced state of dilapidation, the worst was over.
Harare itself is now under the stewardship of Muchadeyi Masunda, one of the country's best corporate minds.
An ambitious project to increase the group's footprint in Zimbabwe and across the region will kick off soon, The Financial Gazette understands.
A recent report by the Rufaro Marketing board obtained by The Financial Gazette this week indicated that the turnover increase to US$3,7 million had given management the impetus to double revenue in the next 12 months.
Sorghum volumes surged by 79 percent during the review period to 5,7 million litres from the 3,1 million litres reported during the prior comparative period in 2009. Lager volumes increased by 87 percent to 1,2 million litres from 653 000 litres.
The growth in volumes and turnover was also driven by the reopening of several outlets that were closed at the height of the domestic recession in 2008, together with the reestablishment of supplier relations, increased product ranges and staff training programmes.
Rufaro Marketing reopened one beer utility during the review period, bringing the total to 51.
By March 2010 the company had 11 neighbourhood taverns but two more were added during the review period, in addition to three night clubs and 20 bottle stores, details of the financial report indicated.
Rufaro Marketing purchased 29 generators, 82 television sets, 60 decoders, four vehicles and seven refrigerators during the period.
It repaired 28 cold rooms as part of the revival of the liquor company, once the envy of competitors in the beverages distribution market, but had been extensively run-down by the effects of Zimbabwe's devastating economic crisis and poor management.
Mass advertisement programmes are expected as the battle to recapture Rufaro Marketing's market share kicks off, while a programme to bring top-of-the-range entertainment, such as performing groups and popular disc jockeys (DJs), is under consideration.
The terrain has changed since the dollarisation of Zimbabwe's economy last year, just about the time Rufaro Marketing drifted into a coma.
New and aggressive players have entered the liquor market with attractive packages in a market where intense competition from imported products is the new threat.
And with a tattered image, and a significantly subdued consumer confidence in its offerings, Rufaro Marketing will have to do more than just bringing top DJs and musicians to its outlets, but work on the overall outlook of the rundown properties, most of which are in dire need for an urgent facelift.
Chairman of the City of Harare's business committee Wellington Chikombo told The Financial Gazette this week the company had been further crippled by the leasing of Rufaro Brewery to Delta Beverages in 2008.
"We toured Go Beer and Ingwebu in Gweru and Bulawayo respectively to see how they structured their management.
That is when we decided to rebrand, restructure and revitalise to improve operations," Chikombo said
"However, our major challenge is the sprouting of bottle stores in the city.
"Most of our clients are shunning our outlets, we have appealed to government not to renew licenses for bottle stores," he added."The main idea is to make Rufaro Marketing a cash cow for the city so as to ease rate payers' burden.
Rates are so high but we cannot blame the City of Harare, right now they have no other means of generating revenue," he said.
Rufaro Marketing had 144 beer outlets throughout Harare but only 89 are operating.
The company's infrastructure is now dilapidated with play centres and recreational facilities that it used to support now an eyesore.
Some of the beerhalls have since been leased to other companies while revellers are shunning the outlets due to their state.

Original_Search_Title: Beenie Man Comes to Garwe
Headline: Zimbabwe: Beenie Man Comes to Garwe
Body_Text:
Harare — First it was dancehall icon Sizzla, then came Brick and Lace, R&B star Akon with Sean Paul and just last Sunday it was none other than the King of Dancehall, Beenie Man.
After a hectic night at the Old Hararians Sports Club where he did his "thang" to a house brimming with fans and revellers, Beenie Man's next port of call was Garwe Restaurant in Eastlea.
Garwe Restaurant is an exclusive eatery in the serene Eastlea suburb off Robert Mugabe Road, a place which has become synonymous with an afternoon or evening retreat for the discerning individual or executive. The menu is dominated by mainly traditional dishes some of which you can no longer find at home.
Food is prepared using firewood and there is variety: Sadza remhunga (sorghum meal), rezviyo (rapoko) or the usual maize meal with muriwo wetsine (black jack relish) or une dovi (peanut butter) or even rabbit meat, you name it, it's all on offer.
The visibly exhausted performer, who arrived with his entourage just after 3pm, definitely liked the place and certainly the peace as he took time to talk to patrons and staff as he waited to be served.
"I feel good to be here and it's great to be here in Zimbabwe. It has been my dream to come here . . . see, I even have a video of Bob Marley when he came here. So for me, this is a great time of my life," said Beenie Man.
Garwe opened some six years back and has proved to be the place to be. One good reason for that is this is a place you can go to and relax and have your meal in cool and natural surroundings.
As you walk into the yard, one striking feature about this place is the all green environs which are a reminder of a bushy adventure rather than a restaurant in an upmarket area.
The main features at Garwe are its stark reference to the traditional African lifestyle: From the grass thatched gazebos to the traditional cartes du jour on offer and the decor. You are even invited to use your hands to eat your sadza.
However, the place has become a stop-over for almost all the big names that visit the country and the capital despite the fact that alcohol is not yet on the menu. (You can choose to bring your own though). And Beenie Man is just one of them.
Managing director and proprietor, Mandy Mvukwe, said the joint is also ideal for corporate events or private functions.
"People can come to hold their corporate events here. Besides our main gazebo, we have another one with a capacity of 60 people," said Mvukwe. "I can tell you that our busiest days are Thursdays and Fridays when people starting winding down in their offices."
A basic meal goes for US$10, but you will certainly eat your feel without regrets.

Original_Search_Title: Sabmiller Resumes Reporting Delta Results
Headline: Zimbabwe: Sabmiller Resumes Reporting Delta Results
Body_Text:
Harare — SABMiller PLC on Monday announced that it has restarted reporting the results of its Zimbabwe associate, Delta Corporation Limited with effect from April 1. SABMiller had stopped including Zimbabwe results in 2006 because of the country's deteriorating economy.
It has resumed reporting due to "the effective 'dollarisation' of the economy in 2009, the end of hyperinflation and the stabilisation of the Zimbabwean economy."
"All credit goes to the Delta management team, whose efforts in keeping the business running in the last few years have been little short of heroic," said Mark Bowman, managing director of SABMiller Africa.
"Despite extreme hardship and untold obstacles, they have continued to supply Zimbabwean consumers with quality products and to secure the livelihoods of many thousands of people, either directly employed by the business or dependent on it for a living," he added.
The business contributed total beverage volumes in the six months to September 30, of 1,0 million hectolitres, including lager volumes of 0,3 million hectolitres.
Delta, in which SABMiller Africa now holds a 36 percent interest, had previously been a significant contributor to SABMiller's Africa operations.
While not included in SABMiller's results, in the year ended March 31, Delta's sales volumes (based on SABMiller Africa's 36 percent interest) were 0,4 million hectolitres of lager, 0,3 million hectolitres of soft drinks and 1,1 million hectolitres of other alcoholic beverages.
Its key lager brands include Castle Lager, Eagle, Lion Lager, Carling Black Label, Golden Pilsener and Bohlinger's.
Its soft drinks portfolio includes a range of Coca-Cola brands and it also manufactures Chibuku, the market leader in the traditional sorghum beer category.
Joe Mutizwa, chief executive of delta, said managing the company over the last eight years has been the business equivalent of white water rafting.
"The challenges have been large, varied and relentless. The demands have been many; -- requiring perpetual vigilance and constant adaptation. The greatest reward, for me, has been to assist young managers to take charge of the destiny of the company in the middle of a blinding macro-economic sandstorm. Our motto was: 'If it is to be, it is up to us!' My team lived up to this motto and now we are enjoying a remarkable turnaround. It has been well worth it! We are now better placed to face new and different challenges going forward."
SABMiller, the world's largest brewer, said on Monday that sales improved in the first half of its financial year thanks largely to strong gains in developing countries. In a trading update for the six months ending September 30, the company said lager volumes rose one percent and soft drink volumes gained two percent.
Double-digit gains in lager volumes in Asia and Africa offset declines in Europe and the Americas in the six months ending September 30. SABMiller did not report earnings figures, but said they had improved because of the strength of key currencies against the US dollar.
SABMiller is one of the world's largest brewers with brewing interests and distribution agreements across six continents.
The group's wide portfolio of brands includes premium international beers such as Pilsner Urquell, Peroni Nastro Azzurro, Miller Genuine Draft and Grolsch, as well as leading brands such as Aguila, Castle, Miller Lite, Snow and Tyskie. SABMiller is also one of the world's largest bottlers of Coca-Cola products.
In the year ended March 31 2010, the group reported US$3 803 million adjusted pre-tax profit and group revenue of US$26 350 million. SABMiller plc is listed on the London and Johannesburg stock exchanges.

Original_Search_Title: Secrecy is No Use to Public Companies
Headline: South Africa: Secrecy is No Use to Public Companies
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — WOULD you invest in a company that has made a loss in three of the past five years, whose shares are so illiquid it is virtually impossible to sell them, and whose CEO hasn't been heard or seen in public for quite some time?
I am sure most investors would agree that their money would be far better served stashed under a mattress. It therefore amazes me that there are still dozens of companies that fit the above profile and yet continue to shell out hundreds of thousands of rand maintaining a JSE listing without deriving the benefits.
One such company is Don Hotels, which reported its financial results for the year to end-June earlier this month.
Despite a 164% rise in revenue to R171m -- boosted by a 51% stake in iKapa Tours & Travel acquired last year -- the group slumped to a headline loss of R9,1m, the second in as many years. While the company released a detailed commentary on the results attributing the loss to the downturn in the hotel sector -- the group's core business -- CEO Thabiso Tlelai was away and not available to answer some of the more tricky questions regarding the company's performance.
Another recent example is maize and sorghum beer producer Awethu Breweries, which earlier this month reported a R3,1m loss for the year to June and announced that auditors Logista-Johannesburg had drawn attention to the fact that its liabilities at year-end exceeded its total assets by more than R5m.
Awethu said the slump in revenue from R17,7m to R3,1m came in a year of "extremely tough" trading conditions that also saw its mill close for a period. Yet CEO Tony Ford was nowhere to be found, investors having again to rely on the Stock Exchange News Service (Sens) announcement for details.
Attempts to contact the company were unsuccessful, with its listed telephone number leading to a message that the number did not exist. I have no doubt there are times when CEOs of listed companies wished their companies were in private hands, that there were no pesky stakeholders -- analysts, shareholders, journalists -- sticking their noses in.
And, yes, I admit, there is no real obligation to entertain probing questions from journalists or analysts, which can be a big distraction from the day-to-day operations of any company. But let me tell you why managing your public profile, soothing investor fears and answering questions from journalists matters so much.
One may assume that most CEOs go to the equity markets to raise cheaper capital or perhaps provide a form of ownership in growing companies for management or employees. Whatever the case, they are asking investors to choose their company among hundreds to invest their cash.
However, in exchange for that trust, investors will want to know that their investment is safe and that over time it will grow. It is for this simple reason that any company's most important asset is its reputation.
No one is going to invest in a company whose listed telephone number does not exist. Few are going to take up a rights offer in a company where the only communication they have with the company is a Sens news release twice a year. There will be no demand for your shares if there are no clear prospects for growth.
Reputation is vital and CEOs need to find effective ways of communicating regularly with the investor community. It is for this reason the media becomes a proxy for investors who want to know about the companies they invest in, asking the probing questions they cannot.
Speaking to the media is probably the best way to communicate with the investor community about new projects, growth prospects and build trust in your company. Without the media, your reputation is hollow, one-dimensional and untested.
Without a solid reputation, it will be difficult for groups such as Don and Awethu to tap investors for further funds or encourage more interest in the group's shares in future.
My advice to these groups: delist. It will save you money, avoid scornful columns such as these and give you full control of your business. You will also save investors frustration and disappointment.
Baumann is an assistant editor.

Original_Search_Title: Auditors Query Awethu's Viability
Headline: South Africa: Auditors Query Awethu's Viability
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Reporting a R3,1m loss for the year to June, Awethu said auditors Logista-Johannesburg had drawn attention to the fact that its liabilities at year-end exceeded its total assets by more than R5m.
MAIZE and sorghum beer producer Awethu Breweries said yesterday that its own auditors had questioned its ability to continue as a going concern.
Reporting a R3,1m loss for the year to June, Awethu said auditors Logista-Johannesburg had drawn attention to the fact that its liabilities at year-end exceeded its total assets by more than R5m.
"These conditions ... indicate the existence of a material uncertainty which may cast significant doubt on the company's ability to continue as a going concern," it said in a JSE statement, quoting the auditor's report.
Awethu shares, which have traded on the JSE since 1997, were unchanged at 5c yesterday. As of a year ago, more than half the shares in issue were owned by CE Tony Ford, Bloomberg data shows. The shares were as high as 11c a year ago.
Attempts to contact Awethu yesterday were unsuccessful. The company's reported telephone number led to a message that the number did not exist.
Awethu said the slump in revenue from R17,7m to R3,1m came in a year of "extremely tough" trading conditions that also saw its mill close for a period.
The company posted a loss per share of 3,66c, down from a profit of 0,93c a year earlier. The year to June 2011 was expected to be better and the company was likely to benefit from the R6,5m sale of its brewery in Carletonville and other moveable brewery assets, it said.

Original_Search_Title: Rombo Success Story in Social Services
Headline: Tanzania: Rombo Success Story in Social Services
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam — There is something fascinating when one looks at the number of pupils registered to begin standard one in Rombo district this year, compared to those enrolled in 2005. A report by Rombo District Commissioner's Office indicates that pupils enrolled in 2005 were 68,459 compared to 57,040 enrolled this year. This is a rare situation that could happen in other parts of the country which normally record increment of pupils' school enrolment every year.
Family planning campaigns that have been conducted in all 20 wards of the district account for the decrease. Most families have resolved to have maximum of three children "to be able to bring them up well." The government is striving to attain the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) before 2015 which is the deadline. Areas given priority include reduction of abject poverty, improvement of people's health and good supply of water.
Rombo district is far ahead in the country in implementing the 2005 CCM manifesto that insisted that there should be at least one dispensary in each ward. The number of dispensaries increased from 20 in 2005 to 35 this year. According to Rombo District Commissioner Mr Peter Toima, health centres have increased from four in 2005 to five in 2010, thus,
rendering good services to the local residents.
Mr Toima says that motherchild care has been improved as expectant mothers receive help from health officers, thus, reducing maternal deaths from 104 per 100,000 mothers in 2005, to 53 per 100, 000 mothers in 2010. Julieth Kimaryo, a mother of two living at Mamsera village in the district says that her children have not suffered from malaria for the past two years, thanks to a programme enhanced my the government to distribute free treated mosquito nets to women and children under five years.
Over 74,242 mosquito nets have been distributed in the district for the past three years. Rombo has also made impressive strides in reducing number of new HIV cases in the district from three per cent in 2005 to two per cent this year. Different programmes are being conducted by different stakeholders, including non-governmental organisations. The number of people accessing ARVs has also increased from 19 in 2005 to 1923 this year. On water supply in Rombo district the district has made great strides in making sure that more people get safe and clean drinking water and within a distance of half a kilometre.
Over 75 per cent of the residents get clean and safe water. The 10 remaining villages will soon get water, because scoping study and design stages are currently under the implementation of a consultant - NORPLAN (Tanzania) limited. Grand water project in Rombo, the East Kilimanjaro Water Supply Project phase two (2005-2009), expected to cost 10bn/-, is currently being implemented by Germany government through Kfw. Rombo district also boast of having well maintained roads infrastructure.
Among the 534 kilometres, 72 kilometres are tarmac while 350 are at gravell level. Once the Marangu-Tarakea road project is completed, it will provide the area with the first tarmac road in history. For many years the road had been more often rendered impassable by torrential rains or land slides, which are common in the area. Resindents of Rombo have also started benefitting from the East African Community (EAC) Common Market which took off last June, thus, making people benefit from 'clean' trans-border trade.
The market has done away with 400 'Panya routes' which existed across the Kenya-Tanzania border, prior to the signing of the Common Market agreement. I visited Loitoktok, Tarakea, Taveta and Holili border towns and witnessed how people are engaging themselves in legal businesses without much interference from customs officials. "I used to sell maize to Kenya through Panya routes but thanks to the Common Market that I can now freely conduct my business here (Holili) without interference, says Lucas Mkenda, a maize vendor at Holili township.
Kenyans sell meat to the Tanzanian side and in turn buy grain like maize, beans, millet and sorghum at much affordable prices from their Tanzanian counterparts. Women who were most often seen smuggling kerosene from Kenya to the Tanzanian side are no longer there. Instead they buy the commodity using the well established market at border posts. Rombo DC admits that there were about 400 'Panya routes' in the district before the common market became operational, "but all of them have vanished."
He says that about 100,000 jobs have been created in Rombo after the establishment of the common market, through outsourcing, as enterprises take advantage of the market. The market, which is made up of Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Burundi and Rwanda, paves the way for free movement of labour, services, capital and goods among the greater EAC member states. Traders do not suffer when moving their products across the countries as some of the restrictions are now relaxed, says Mr Toima.
The cost of doing business has been reduced, following the development of efficient communication infrastructure across the borders in Rombo district. "People freely visit or cross into Kenya and vice versa for social, academic and business-related purposes" says the Rombo DC. "Now, consumers in Rombo get access to a variety of cheaper and better quality products and services, thanks to the competitive environment", he says.
"From July 1, business people have the right to establish trades in other EAC states, work permits are issued within specific time periods and many service providers supply their services in other partner countries and receive equal treatment to that of domestic providers," says Mr Toima. If the trend continues, people in Rombo are set to improve their socio-economic lives, thus, decrease their influx to urban areas like Moshi town, Dar es Salaam, Arusha and Mwanza.

Original_Search_Title: Over 400 Illegal Routes Vanish Due to Common Market
Headline: East Africa: Over 400 'Panya Routes' Vanish in Rombo
Body_Text:

Kenyans sell meat to the Tanzanian side and in turn buy grain like maize, beans, millet and sorghum at much affordable prices from their Tanzanian counterparts. Women who were most often seen smuggling kerosene from Kenya to the Tanzanian side are no longer there and instead they buy the commodity using the well established market at border posts.
Rombo District Commissioner, Mr Peter Toima admits that there were about 400 'Panya routes' in Rombo district before the common market became operational, but all of them have vanished.
He says that about 100,000 jobs have been created in Rombo after the establishment of the common market, through outsourcing, as enterprises take advantage of the market. The market, which is made up of Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Burundi and Rwanda, paves for free movement of labour, services, capital and goods.
Traders don't suffer when moving their products across the countries as some of the restrictions are now relaxed, says Mr Toima. The cost of doing business has reduced, following the development of efficient communication infrastructure across the borders in Rombo district.
