And it is all for free, thanks to the support from a British charity raising funds for donkey welfare.
Lamu district has 5,000 donkeys, with the island itself having 2,000 animals.
When we visited the centre during the Lamu Cultural Festival, we found a two-month old foal with its mother in a stable, which would stand for an intensive care unit.
The young donkey had a deep cut on the leg. To prevent it from causing trouble in the stable or aggravating the injury, its mother had to give it company.In another stable was a donkey, which could barely walk due to severe injuries sustained while transporting goods on the island.
On the holding ground, which could stand for hospital wards, there were 17 donkeys undergoing treatment for various ailments and injuries. The animals are kept here until full recovery.
Fed with hay
The treatment and healing process could take a few days or months, depending on the severity of the disease or injury.
The sick donkeys are fed on hay, maize husks and sometimes goodies such as carrots and bananas.
On some occasions, the attendants and even the owners take the animals to the seashores for washing to reduce chances of contracting skin disease.
And just as it happens with human beings, some owners abandon their sick or injured animals at the sanctuary.
The islanders can identify their animals by their face or through branding.
Some animals had wounds, eye problems, abdominal problems and ravaged skins.
Most of the injuries are inflicted by owners either by overloading the carts or whipping them with rods.
The donkeys are dewormed twice a year, while their hooves are trimmed every three-to-six months.
This helps the animals walk properly since they carry heavy goods as well as serve as ambulances.
At any given time, the sanctuary is manned by at least three attendants.
According to the centre's manager, Mr Abdalla Hadi Rifai, the facility was established in 1987 through the support of a British tourist, Dr Elizabeth Svendsen.
Ms Svendsen was on holiday in 1985 when she came across sick and neglected donkeys on the island.
The tourist was moved by the appalling state of the animals and decided to create a centre to cater for their basic welfare and also educate the residents on animal rights.
The project tied in well with her experience as she was running another donkey sanctuary in Devon, England.
Ms Svendsen raises funds for the management of the centre, salaries and other basic needs. The kitty also benefits from donations by tourists.
The sanctuary has a surgeon, a manager and nine workers who care for the sick donkeys.
Since the island has no motorised transport, the residents rely on the animals to move goods. This makes the donkey one of the most sought after animals on he islands, and also the most expensive.
A donkey goes for between Sh15,000 and Sh18,000. It takes nine months for a donkey to deliver. Both male and female donkeys carry goods from the age of two and half years.
There are two breeds of donkeys in Lamu, the common Somali species and the white coloured Bahrain species.
In order to prevent cruelty against the donkeys, the sanctuary workers educate the islanders on the need to care for their animals.
"Some of the donkeys brought here for treatment have body injuries and cuts after the owners beat them up when they turned wild," Mr Rifai said.
"We normally hold public meetings and visit schools to educate the locals on the need to look after their donkeys. The animals are the pillar of transport on the island," he added.
Studies elsewhere show cruelty to animals is caused by lack of knowledge on the rights of the animals and the benefits of keeping them healthy.
In areas like Mwea and Lari, the situation is made worse by alcoholism and drug abuse among owners.
Animal welfare groups are now using the economic benefits of keeping donkeys healthy to educate owners on animal rights.
Part of heritage
The National Museums of Kenya director-general, Dr Idle Farah, said that the donkeys are part of the culture and tradition of Lamu people.
"It is important for the locals to take care of the animals since they are part of their culture. Lamu is not complete without the donkeys," he added.
Dr Farah thanked the British charity for putting up the sanctuary, which has helped improve the quality of life for donkeys on the island.
In 2001, Lamu was listed as a world heritage site by Unesco.

Original_Search_Title: Mityana South Gets Three MP Contenders
Headline: Uganda: Mityana South Gets Three MP Contenders
Body_Text:
THERE was tension at Ssaza ground in Mityana town during the nomination of NRM's Henry Kamya Makumbi, and an independent candidate, Ssozi Kaddu Mukasa, for the Mityana South parliamentary seat.
Makumbi, who has been the Mityana town LC3 chairperson, was nominated first. Makumbi was escorted by a number of traders and district party leaders.
His supporters from Ssekanyonyi, Namungo and Busimbi sub-counties arrived on lorries and pick-up trucks, wearing dry banana leaves and singing his praises.
Makumbi later addressed his supporters at the taxi park.
Incumbent Ssozi Kaddu Mukasa was escorted by former Ssingo county chief Stanley Kijjambu and several supporters. Thereafter, he held rallies at Ssekanyonyi, Mpirigwa and Namungo.
The Anti-riot Police kept patrolling the town to ensure that supporters did not clash.
Another independent candidate, Edward Ssebombo, was also cleared to run.

Original_Search_Title: Every Woman is a Designer -  Kayode Sofola
Headline: Nigeria: Every Woman is a Designer -  Kayode Sofola
Body_Text:
He breathes the law the way regular folk breathe air. He has done so all his life. His father, Kehinde Sofola (who passed away a few years ago) was one of those who defined the practice of law in Nigeria during those definitive years of the sixties and seventies. Choosing the same profession as such a father is likely to have one or two effects: getting overshadowed or becoming rebellious. The Senior Advocate of Nigeria escaped both; and has clearly and patiently proved his mettle without the proverbial chip on the shoulder.
For a "silver spoon kid", he is of a firmly conservative disposition. This is written all over him, from the Cartier frames to the Saville Row suits, He also endorses character as the ultimate style, naming Nobel Laureate Wole Soyinka and the late Tai Solarin as examples.
A particularly noticeable practitioner in the sea of black and white, Kayode Sofola boasts an easy, almost effortless elegance of style that can be easily missed by the unpractised eye, on account of its simplicity.
But then, the world is his playground, meaning he can speak with authority about traffic in Italy or security on a Caribbean island. He rates the Nigerian judiciary in contemporary times high, in spite of "bad eggs". Though he thinks we have seen better, he explains today's judiciary is beneficiary of good precedence from the first generation and is definitely not on the level of some banana republics
Conservative and publicity shunning, he is all about family, and refers constantly to his closest relatives: his father, his lovely wife, and his grandson, whose photograph he prominently displays.
On his elegant Ikoyi premises, he takes an easy trip down memory lane with Morenike Taire, and chats about the things which make him tick.
Your decision to read and practice law, how far was it influenced by your father?
He was clever enough not to ask me to read law. But he took me to court when he was doing some really fantastic cases.
Can you recall any such case?
There was a case in Ijebu Ode. Politicians trumped up a witness to the box to testify that he saw them at the night of the incident, with the permission of the judge. They killed some policemen. They came back in as they had been coached . I also witnessed the case for the first minimum wage. Despite that he was very careful not to tell me to read law.
Were you like a rebel?
The earlier part was very tough. He was a strict disciplinarian. All my propensities to be mischievous, rascally and all of that, were very rigorously curtailed at that time. As a lawyer I was doing submissions in which I had to do thorough research by myself. People think that I'm just regurgitating what I had been told in the office. I remember this justice; I'd had a submission and he just reacted immediately and said: "tell your father I disagree with him!" I had no idea what I was going to say in court.
I was going to come to that. I was going to ask, were there times when you were disadvantaged?
We come from a culture where people identify you with the clients you represent. Sometimes you get to court and you find a judge totally contrary. I don't mind. I know I've had advantages for being his son, I must accept the other side.
Do you have any recollection of the famous Awo trial, the one in which he was convicted?
I won't say I was personally caught up with the particular episode. Anybody who was in Nigeria knew the impact. There were more substantial cases.
You have had the distinct opportunity of seeing the practice of law as an insider in two generations. Can you make comparisons?
I think the previous generation were more likely to behave with a sense of propriety, more ethical, more exposed. Having said that, there are very distinguished professionals in this generation. Our judiciary is one of the best . We have people we can hold up
What is your idea of a good vacation?
A chance to walk around with no care in the world. Be incognito. You keep jumping up and down in Nigeria, you almost actually have no use of your legs. London and Paris. I was taken up by Cancun. It has a lot of very beautiful places, they have security.
Are you a particular dresser?
Yeah, I'm my wife's tailor's dummy. She sells fabrics. I'm of the view that every Nigerian woman is a designer. You will tell the sewing mistress or the tailor how to fashion out your garment. Really creative.
What other designers do you like?
Very simple. On the last day of my vacation, that's when I do my shopping. If I see something I like it, I wear it and it hangs well on me, end of shopping. The top end of the business; the conservatives, Saville Row, you have those who are developed like Knightsbridge. Places you walk into you know that this is the type of place you find the kind of suit or the kind of shirts...
Do you do bespoke?
The bigger shops now have segments where the big designers are located. It used to be just Harrods before but all the major shops, even Marks and Spencer's have designer stands. If you see the type of style that is your style, you will say to yourself, "I'm done!"
Are you particular about designers?
It doesn't matter although at the end of the day the better ones have clothes that hang a little bit better. Hugo Boss, and so on. It's just that a practicing lawyer will deal with a lot of caution.
Do you have a personal style?
You get to a stage where your clothes no longer define you, you get bigger than what you're wearing. Wole Soyinka does not wear the most expensive clothes, but he's Wole Soyinka. Tai Solarin would go around in khakis, yet he is Tai Solarin.

Original_Search_Title: Kasese NRM, FDC Fans Clash
Headline: Uganda: Kasese NRM, FDC Fans Clash
Body_Text:
SUPPORTERS of the National Resistance Movement (NRM) clashed with those of the Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) in Kasese district after the former tried to disrupt Dr. Kizza Besigye's rally at Mubuku.
When Besigye arrived for the rally on Wednesday, a handful of NRM supporters donning yellow T-shirts heckled him. They had tied strands of dry banana leaves around the venue.
As Besigye began addressing the rally, the NRM fans booed him, chanting: "No change, Pakalast" repeatedly.
The FDC supporters collected the banana leaves and set them on fire. A stand-off ensued and Besigye ordered his supporters to ignore their opponents.
Police guards formed a ring around Besigye until they were reinforced by a team from the district.
Karim Bashir, the in-charge of the political and electoral desk, said: "Our role is to ensure a peaceful campaign for all candidates. We shall investigate the incident and take action. We condemn all acts of hooliganism."
Thereafter, Besigye continued with his campaign programme in the district. He addressed rallies at Maliba, Kyaya, Bugoye, Karusandara, Kanamba, Kisinga, Kyarumba, Kajengwe, Bwera/Mponde and Kasese town.
He accused President Yoweri Museveni of weakening traditional leaders. He said the Rwenzururu kingdom was a raw deal, just like that of Buganda.
"Buganda Kingdom was restored first, but the Kabaka is not allowed to freely tour his kingdom. You should be on the look-out because President Museveni and defense minister Dr. Crispus Kiyonga were opposed to the restoration of the Rwenzururu monarchy," Besigye said.
He promised to build an airport in Kasese when elected president: "Kasese is endowed with mineral deposits and tourist attractions which are key to the development of the nation. It is, therefore, necessary to have a modern airport built in the district," he said.
Besigye said as president, he would adequately fund the health sector so that health workers do not flee the country for greener pastures.
"I am a medical doctor so I know what I am talking about. Overlooking the health sector by this regime must stop. Doctors and health workers must be respected."

Original_Search_Title: Elections Becoming Discredited
Headline: Namibia: Political Perspective
Body_Text:
WHAT should be an exhilarating experience, which offers the opportunity for vibrant debate and exchange of views as Namibians get ready to exercise their democratic choices, has instead become a messy, confused and even discredited process.
And yes, I'm talking about our elections, national, regional or local, which are rapidly becoming, if not a joke, then something that people just don't take seriously anymore. It is so sad that only 20 years after Independence, the euphoria has already given way to disillusionment.
SOME will argue with me to say that there will still undoubtedly be a huge turnout among particularly the Swapo support base and that this in turn would indicate that all is not lost, but I'd beg to differ. Thousands in the country won't fail their party at the polls because this unquestioning loyalty has been inculcated in them for decades, but those who are really concerned about our democracy's discontent will find it hard to get excited about the dearth of leadership at all levels and the problems around most of the processes.
I don't necessarily quarrel with the predictable outcome (the people's choice, warts and all, should be given some measure of respect) but where I do have deep reservations is about the lack of depth of our democracy. It is really just a facade and without deeper meaning. And this - along with the rot that accompanies it in terms of generally deteriorating standards and lack of professionalism - is the real tragedy.
How can we hold our heads up high and celebrate the triumph of democracy when our country is becoming a free-for-all? Where corruption runs riot and poverty incrementally increases? Where our existing leadership is so disappointingly bereft? When the party-political youth leadership are either unable to intelligently articulate what they stand for, or spew rhetoric without real comprehension? Where the politics of conformism means that individual characteristics and belief systems must be subverted (if they are there at all!) because 'the party' demands sheeplike adherence and obedient clones rather than people who can make a difference?
When will we ever see a diverse group of candidates, in whatever election and regardless of the political entity to which they belong, with intelligence and charisma and the will to change things? Not in the near future, because if you stand out in the crowd, you risk being hypothetically clubbed to death for daring to be different!
And then there's the general rot. The banana republic-like symptoms which are becoming too prolific to ignore. The theft of 42 laptops from election headquarters - a place which, during elections, should be more closely guarded than State House! The request by the Swapo Youth League to give employees a half day off today to enable them to go and vote. Such nonsense. The polling stations are open until 9 at night and all day Saturday, and we have to waste more valuable economic time to sweeten the process for the electorate!
The continued celebration of mediocrity. I could hardly believe my eyes when I watched the Head of State in a party-paid address on NBC TV on Wednesday night! Was this the leader of the 'mighty' Swapo? A man who monotonously read from a prepared text looking down at the feet of his viewers? You want someone to come across as though they believe in what they say, even if you don't agree with them; who can look the viewer in the eye and speak with confidence and charisma and conviction! "Vote Swapo for peace," he affirmed, in a country where women and children are being brutalised and slain on an almost daily basis. This is not peace. This is war!
I don't give a darn about party-political hype. I just wish that in Namibia we could come up with some people of whichever persuasions, who are going to try to change things for the better rather than celebrate their non-existent achievements. We must stop the pretence that all is well, when our unemployment rate is criminally high; where the youth are getting a second-rate education and then find themselves on the streets; where our women and children are being defiled; and the graft and looting are fast becoming rampant.
What Namibian wouldn't want to get out there as soon as polling stations open and vote when there are real choices and good candidates from whichever political fold? A highly charged and motivated electorate doesn't need the offer of public holidays and time off to cast their votes. They wouldn't miss the opportunity. But, unfortunately, we're not there yet.
Some may see this column as unnecessarily cynical. But allow me to say in my defence that I want the best for my country, and this isn't it!

Original_Search_Title: The GPA Principals And History's Departure Point Lost
Headline: Zimbabwe: The GPA Principals And History's Departure Point Lost
Body_Text:
ACCORDING to media reports, in the aftermath of the Sadc ceremony to open that organisation's new headquarters in mid-November, the assumption that there would be a side meeting of the regional body's Troika to directly address the Zimbabwean crisis turned out not to be true.
What has since emerged is that President Jacob Zuma is due in Zimbabwe by the weekend beginning Friday November 26 according to Reuters News Agency. Whether his meetings with the three political principals will be as brief as the last time he visited, it is hard to tell, but what is clear is that no matter the duration of his visit, this time Zuma has to ensure that there is progress or at least clarity on the way forward vis-à-vis the GPA.
But because Zuma is not a Zimbabwean leader, one cannot expect too much from him and our focus must no longer be on measuring his ability to deliver our country to a political Canaan.
Instead our focus at this critical juncture must be firmly set on how President Robert Mugabe, Prime Minister Morgan Tsvangirai and Deputy Prime Minister Arthur Mutambara demonstrate a full appreciation of the gravity of the issues at hand in the country. Especially when they meet Zuma.
In direct relation to the political stalemate in the country at present, the three leaders have neither demonstrated nor indicated a clear understanding of how with each passing day their differences real or perceived are now patently symptomatic of how the GPA has become a political ceasefire arrangement.
This, in a fashion similar to the much written temporary World War 1 Christmas Day ceasefire, where warring troops suspended fighting for a day and continued with killing each other on Boxing Day. In other words, nothing in relation to their political attitude to each other or to the country has changed.
The three leaders have failed to utilise time to depart from the past. Instead they have used it to restock on ammunition in order to fight another day in similar fashion to all the fights of the last 10 years.
This also essentially means that they have failed even in their endeavour to seek incremental change in the country, and that perhaps all they sought from each other was temporary respite, or to live to fight another day. And it is this attitude which neither puts the country first nor inspires confidence in the leadership capabilities of all of the three leaders.
But the country and its citizens have to face up to the reality of the tragic nature of the leadership of the inclusive government, and once again decry what has come to be called "African politics". And where this statement is made, this article may be considered naïve or simplistic.
The truth of the matter is that the political reality of our country standing as always on the precipice of disaster and doom because one party has done one thing or the other is setting the base for a banana republic where it is individuals and their increasingly personalised political parties that hold sway over the nation. Simply put, all of the three political parties made the GPA their bed and they must lie in it, albeit with all its discomforts.
And for these discomforts all of the three leaders are to blame. Perhaps one more than the others, but when the final analysis is made, the GPA is the responsibility of all of them. Mugabe must be informed that political brinkmanship is neither helpful to the country's present nor its future.
Seeking, as evidenced by his publicly recorded statements, to have an "arithmetic" condign election and attempt to get rid of the MDCs in government is neither realistic nor possible.
This is because even if he seeks to ram an election down our throats, the political psyche of the country will not digest it and he together with Zanu PF will be dogged by legitimacy problems nationally and in the international community. Such a development would only serve to increase the number of people seeking to leave the country, compound the economic crisis further and make this state a very unstable one.
Tsvangirai on the other hand cannot skirt his responsibilities in the GPA and the matter of how his political conduct is potentially negatively viewed by its guarantor, Sadc. Where he makes public statements, they should be consistent both with his party's principles as well as with the mantra of "necessary compromises" he has insisted on since September 2008. This together with a deliberate tackling of the perception that the MDC-T is working too closely with the West and a reduction of MDC-T policy ambiguity on the issue of sanctions would serve our country better.
Mutambara for all his attempts at bridging the political gap between his two more powerful colleagues, must function with a greater understanding of collective responsibility with his party as well as the inclusive government.

Original_Search_Title: Supporters of Besigye and Museveni Clash
Headline: Uganda: Supporters of Besigye and Museveni Clash
Body_Text:
Supporters of Kizza Besigye yesterday clashed with those of President Museveni in Kasese as the political battle for control of the district ensued.
While addressing a rally at Mubuku Sub-county in Busongora North, a group of 20 NRM youth draped in dry banana leaves arrived.
Dr Besigye's supporters then seized the dry banana leaves from the group and set them on fire. It took about 10 minutes for the police to restore calm between the two parties.
Freedom to assemble
Mr Monday Karim, the district police officer in-charge of electoral offences, told Daily Monitor that two NRM supporters were arrested for trying to disrupt Dr Besigye's rallies. He said they will be charged with violent conduct.
The IPC presidential candidate also tried to calm his supporters.
"Leave those sanja (dry banana leaves) people alone. They have failed to learn that we also have freedom to assemble."
Dr Besigye's party, the FDC, is fighting to consolidate its presence in this area, the only district in western Uganda where FDC has representatives in Parliament.
Dr Besigye, who carried out door-to-door campaigns in some villages and was set to address a rally in Kasese town later in the evening, was thanked for having advocated the restoration of the Rwenzururu Kingdom. He was accompanied by former army commander Maj. Gen. Mugisha Muntu and Busongora South MP Christopher Kibazanga.
Mr Kibazanga, who opted not to run for Parliament but for the district leadership position, said the NRM has resorted to organising "hooligans" because they are losing out to the opposition.

Original_Search_Title: Consensus Unusual
Headline: Nigeria: Consensus Unusual
Body_Text:
'Hyena flirts with the hen and the hen is happy not knowing that her end has come' - African proverb
What would have been the crowning glory of my journalism career would have been the birth of LEADERSHIP EDGE sometimes in 2008. I was its editor and I handled the assignment with uncommon passion.
I still live, breathe and savour the idea which still haunts me today. It was conceived by Mr. Sam Nda-Isaiah and went through incubation and labour in the hands of many midwives. What I considered a sizzling package was a pot-pourri of elitism - focusing on in-depth corporate briefs, informed analyses, leadership and statecraft; offers some useful tips on business, entrepreneurship, boardroom affairs, international intelligence and diplomacy was about to go to bed when Mr. Nda-Isaiah introduced a persona that he believes should feature in our compact list of 10 people the President Must Talk to To Save Nigeria. Her name is Jean Herskovits, an American.
Believe it or not, there are some foreigners who understand the social psychology of Nigeria more than most Nigerian leaders - living or dead! Herskovits has been interfacing with all Nigerian leaders since independence and was a sure go-between for North America and the country. She has lived and interacted with villagers and city folks in 35 of Nigeria's 36 states and saw Abuja frm 'its infancy onward'. Indeed, in his riveting memoirs, Diplomatic Soldering, Joseph Garba, described her as his 'teacher' on Nigerian affairs. Washington, I learnt, relies more on Jean than the CIA apparatchiks when it comes to honestly analysing Nigeria.
Herskovits' submission leaves me reeling in pains because I was reading her when the news of the Adamu Ciroma-led Northern Political Leaders Forum (NPLF) announced the choice of former Vice-President Atiku Abubakar as its consensus candidate. That sent a poisoned arrow straight into my marrow as this woman had two weeks earlier submitted that this nation can be great again if we discard divisive vices and embrace liberal democracy: "If Nigerians were disappointed in the 1980s and already made cynical by Ibrahim Babangida's 'transition without end', they were more discouraged - and far more cynical - by 2007". These are the reflections of 'a troubled Nigerian friend'.
As a concerned citizen, I was thinking 2011and how to regain our Nigerianess. I agree with Herskovits on many things, including the fact that the clamour for more states will never cease and the fears of the minorities will never be adequately assuaged by the infraction called Federal Character. I do not see hope in how some oligarchic imposition of Atiku Abubakar will impress a Middle Belter for instance, whose opportunities have been narrowed by the balkanisation and intensification of fears of domination. Ask the Igedes in Benue when they will produce a governor. It's like opening up the sore of ethnicism and rubbing salt on it. Since the criteria for selection was the exclusive preserve of the committee and as such oblique, are we not returning to the trenches when the ruling class must always be blue-blooded or a Fulani. Even the age of absolutism died 200 years ago in Europe where people rule by 'divine rights'. Now suzerainty belongs to the people. In spite of his blue-blood, Sardauna struggled all his life to harmonise the North.
Alhaji Shehu Shagari got his ticket twice on campaign anchored on "One Nigeria, One Nation under One God". Inflicting a Northern agenda will only remind us of the centrifugal and centripetal forces that stymied the collective march at independence. and the firing of 'the first bullet of the civil war' when 12 states were created. It's for the same reason of national cohesion and integration that I will never support the creation of any more states. Indeed, if it were possible
I am not a great fan of President Goodluck Jonathan, but what Ciroma and other presidential candidates from the North, including those already scheming to produce a South-Eastern candidate as Atiku's running mate or wanting to become his Chief of Staff, have done is to take us back to the fiefdom of those days. The new Nigeria we crave is not to be couched in that mould. The template is wrong. Like in Mathematics, if the constant variable is wrong, the conclusion cannot be right. You have just handed the banner of the PDP to Jonathan, whose spin doctors came out with a new slogan of him being the consensus candidate of Nigeria. Great! The NPLF can have Atiku as president of the North. The rest of the country will shop frm the remaining list of those who have the nation as their constituency: Nuhu Ribadu, Goodluck Jonathan, Pat Utomi, Muhammadu Buhari and the rest.
The oddities of the parlour party of the Northern elite are anachronistic, baleful and inimical to national unity. We do not have to go to 1966 and 67. To entrench sectional interests is to say Nigerians should relive the travails of another needless civil war - God forbid, even in election. Definitely not the way to go in this information age, where to engage the rest of the world and open avenue for national prosperity, tranquility and development, you take ethnicity, religion and region to the back seat. Imagine if America's Democratic Party had adopted consensus candidate, would there have been a President Barack Obama? We should copy good examples. Nigeria is not a banana republic.

Original_Search_Title: This Lawmaking Industry
Headline: Nigeria: This Lawmaking Industry
Body_Text:
At the height of the global financial crisis President Barack Obama of the United States lashed out at the bankers who continued to draw fat bonuses even while government was expected to bail out their organisations. Economic analysts with some social conscience have lately denounced the unconscionable wages and allowances of Chief Executive Officers (CEOs) of private organisations even in developed countries while their respective economies struggled with the worst recession in 70 years. Strategists of development even in the capitalist sense of the word are now concerned about the worsening inequality in society.
Reflecting on the American efforts at economic recovery recently, an American commentator remarked that it is only in 'banana republics' that government could afford widening social inequality without minding the ruinous social consequences. Even in a system of free enterprise, those conscious of the systemic implications are worried about excessive remuneration of private businessmen. Hence, it is often more than symbolic when public officers cut their own wages in response to difficult economic situations. It is a form of wage regulation by sheer force of example.
The situation is, however, different here, as members of the National Assembly have elected to increase their emoluments in a most provocative manner. The irony is too vivid to ignore: while in richer countries private businessmen are rebuked for outrageous bonuses and allowances, here in Nigeria, the home of the wildest peculiarities, it is the legislative arm of government at the centre that should be put on the spot for the bogus allowances received by the lawmakers.
To start with, it is curious that the subject of the salaries and allowances of the National Assembly members is not made an open issue in the spirit of transparency. It is inexplicable that those who are supposed to be representing the people and make laws for the common good would prefer that to put a veil on the matter of how much it is costing the nation for them to do their work. The National Assembly members scrupulously avoid discussing the issue. As a result, figures are bandied about on how much members of the National Assembly take home monthly and quarterly.
This newspaper reported on August 8 that: 'In 2009, the federal legislators received a total of N102.8 billion comprising N11.8 billion as salaries and N90.96 billion as non-taxable allowances.' We are talking of what fewer that 500 persons received in an economy in which workers go on strike to demand N18, 000 monthly minimum wage. It would amount to an understatement to call this trend a drain on the public treasury.
For clarity, the lawmakers deserve decent remuneration for their service to the country. They need good rewards. However, there is certainly no sense of proportion and equity in a system in which an elected officer earns 240 million a year as salaries and allowances while the system has to be disrupted to secure an annual wage of N196, 000 for the lowest paid worker. In fact, in the THISDAY story cited above, law scholar, Professor Itse Sagay, was reported to have put the matter in a sharper perspective like this: "the National Assembly members are the highest paid legislators in the world their salaries and allowances represent a cruel anomaly in the country's democratic governance.
The anomaly is a breach of public trust A senator earns N240 million ($1.7 million) in salaries and allowances while his House of Representatives counterpart earns about N204 million ($1.45 million) per annum'.
The lawyer compared this with the American senator who 'earns $174, 000 (and) a UK parliamentarian earns about $64, 000 per annum, which is very low compared to that of Nigerian lawmakers'.
Now, the point at issue is this: the earnings of the members of the National Assembly are economically illogical, socially insensitive and morally repugnant.
First, this increasingly underdeveloped economy cannot sustain this greed of the lawmakers. Each year that this National Assembly passes a budget it is followed up with the lamentation by analysts that a disproportionate percentage of the budget goes in to recurrent expenditure. The pay of the lawmakers has further ballooned this recurrent expenditure. It is understandable if the government is in deficit, financing defensible capital projects. But it is utterly indefensible that that a time may come when deficit budgeting may be required to fund the jumbo pay of a few hundreds of lawmakers.
Secondly, in a socio-economic environment defined by mass poverty and misery, it is outrageous that lawmakers could award themselves the sort of salaries and allowances we are talking about. As lawmakers they should be concerned about the widening gulf in our inequitable reward system. It is an issue of our socio-economic life. This should even inform the spirit of some of the legislations. Thirdly, it is morally untenable that those elected by the people don't feel responsible and accountable to the people from whom the mandate was supposed to have issued in the first place.
In a way the Abuja lawmakers behave as if they are operating in an industry totally immune to the economics, morality and the reality of the larger society. While some industries are collapsing and some factories are relocating to Ghana, it seems only the Abuja lawmaking industry is growing. How many small scale or medium scale industrialists make a net profit of N60 million in a quarter? Yet it is these small and medium scale industries that are supposed to boost employment required to ensure a job-led growth.
Without a steady supply of electricity and other negative factors of the business environment the industries are merely surviving and not booming. In contrast, to invest in the Abuja-based lawmaking industry all you need is to secure the ticket of a 'winning' party, and go ahead to 'win' the election by all means possible. If the results are challenged, you may need to fund lawyers who will defend you at the tribunal or at least keep you in the Senate or the House for some three years before the election is annulled. In three years you will have profited enough and be ready to go home with your earnings even if the election is annulled. The law does not say you should return your earnings even when the same law says your election is unknown to it. That is the logic of this lawmaking industry where the principal officers of the National Assembly are the captains of the industry.
How else can one describe them when the Senate President reportedly earns 250 million Naira per quarter; his deputy goes home with 150 million Naira; and each of the eight remaining principal officers is entitled to 78 million Naira at this same period? If our federal legislators are the highest paid in the world, then it would not be wrong to say that the legislations coming from their chambers are the best laws money can buy on earth.
Maybe the nation should have a rethink on the place of the lawmakers in the governance process. Some observers have suggested that we might not need more than part-time legislators as a way of warding off the huge expenses on lawmaking. Some veterans of the system such as Pa Anthony Enahoro still nurse the nostalgia of the parliamentary system in preference to this Nigeria's exorbitant presidential system.
Members of the National Assembly are wont to flaunt their oversight functions. With the recklessness displayed in this matter of salaries and allowances, it is time the people performed the oversight functions over the National Assembly itself. The people should assume the responsibility of making the lawmakers accountable and transparent. Lawmaking should not be another industry; being a member of the National Assembly is a privilege to render public service.

Original_Search_Title: Invest in SMEs - Minister Nsanzabaganwa
Headline: Rwanda: Invest in SMEs - Minister Nsanzabaganwa
Body_Text:
The Minister for Trade and Industry, Monique Nsanzabaganwa has called on the business community to invest in Small and Medium Enterprises (SME's) since they are drivers for the country's economic development.
She made the call during the cerebration to mark 'Africa's Industry Day' in Gasabo District, and event held at BN Producers, a Small and Medium Enterprise (SME) industry that deals in Mushroom and banana products, including flour and wine.
"The Business community should put more effort in the SMEs as it's the engine of the economy," Nsanzabaganwa said, adding that the government will back them up with funds and training will be provided.
The African Industry day is celebrated annually on November, 20. "We need our small industry to be able to compete with the regional industries by adding value to anything they produce which will bring sustainable development," the Minister pointed out.
Berthilde Niyibaho from BN Producers, urged the government to keep up the support rendered to SME's
"This (BN) is a small factory but with a huge potential to supply both the domestic market and the region. We ask the government to give us any support they can so as to add more value to what we produce," she said.
BN Producers started four years ago.

Original_Search_Title: Drainage Ignored, Says City Engineer
Headline: Uganda: Drainage Ignored, Says City Engineer
Body_Text:
KAMPALA City Council (KCC) officials are aware that making roads without proper drainage is among the biggest causes of potholes, but they are not about to change this. "What do you do if you want to construct a road of 10km and have funds for one kilometre?" says Michael Mudanye, an engineer working with KCC.
On other roads, it is a question of space. "Putting drainage channels on narrow roads could expose motorists to more danger," says Mudanye. "The authorities have to balance cost and the benefits that come with the roads."
The man in charge of city drainage, Edward Mukalazi, complains that construction takes priority over drainage. "Drainage does not attract a lot of attention yet it is one of the factors weakening the roads," says Mukalazi.
City engineer Joseph Musoke adds that due to increased construction, surface run-off has increased because there is hardly any soil for rain water to sink in. The run-off carries soil and garbage, which block existing drainage channels.
"It is important to re-construct the drainage pipes or drainage systems to accommodate the increased water run-off in the city," he said.
He also recommended increased awareness and enforcement of the law to curb the indiscriminate disposal of waste. "People throw waste like banana peels, mineral water bottles and plastic bags on the roads, which end up in the drainage," says Musoke.

Original_Search_Title: Fake Cash Recovered From Pump Attendant
Headline: Uganda: Fake Cash Recovered From Pump Attendant
Body_Text:
The Police in Kampala have recovered counterfeit notes totalling sh5m from the home of a city fuel station pump attendant.
The wads of notes in denominations of sh10,000 were picked from the home of Ibrahim Kasule in Kabalagala, a city suburb, at the weekend.
Kampala Metroplitan spokesperson Ibin Ssenkumbi yesterday told the press that a beneficiary of Kasule's generosity, named Masitula Nanfuka, led the detectives to his home.
According to Ssenkumbi, Nanfuka and her unidentified boyfriend saw the large amount of money when they paid Kasule a visit last week.
Nanfuka reportedly fell in love with their "rich" host.
Nanfuka later went to the Clock Tower to buy bananas worth sh35,000, only to be nabbed with the fake money.
"Nanfuka was surrounded by people and she told them that she picked it from Kasule's home," Ssenkumbi stated, adding that counterfeit notes worth sh160,000 were recovered from her.
Detectives later recovered more notes from Kalule's home in Kabalagala. The two were taken to Central Police Station, where they were paraded before the press with the fake money.
Kasule claimed that the money belonged to Mary Nassiwa, who the Police are now hunting for.
Ssenkumbi urged the public to be vigilant, saying there could a lot of fake money in circulation.
In a related development, Godfrey Wegulo, who reportedly poses as a soldier, was on Sunday evening arrested in the Old Taxi Park with sh40,000 in counterfeit notes.

Original_Search_Title: Now That the 2010 Elections Are Over
Headline: Tanzania: Now That the 2010 Elections Are Over
Body_Text:
NOW that Tanzanians have just come out of the general elections, it would not be a bad idea to give some useful advice to our politicians on how to address the challenges that lie ahead in the quest for reconciliation, unity, equality and greater democracy.
It will be a big mistake for all those who were elected in their capacities to engage in plutocracy. Under this exploitative system, it is said that the richest 1 percent of the population gobbles up 20 percent of the national pie or cake. I think it will be absurd to suggest that the politicians should travel to distant and dangerous countries to observe such rapacious inequality.
Those politicians who wish to see peace prevailing in the country should put the interest of their country and the people first. In the just ended elections, there has been growing frustration among the citizens who feel that the politicians, especially those who are representing them in their constituencies, most of them had failed to deliver.
That is why they did not vote them back into office. I do agree with those who think that the elements of banana republics are manifesting themselves in Tanzania. We now have it right here at home - and in the event that people will ignore the voice of those who are left to suffer because of the actions or inactions of some politicians, it might create more hostilities against them, especially their parties.
In my recent observation, I came to think of the Election Day turn- over. The statistics show that those who registered were over 19 million people but the number of those who exercised their constitutional right was shocking.
To many Tanzanians, this was a reflection of their resentment to the system. It is indicating that these people not only abstained from presidential voting but also shunned voting for the members of parliament and councilors.
When you come to think of it, that is why this resentment, the answer is clear. It is all about the rift between the haves and have-nots. It is about hopes that have not been met.
If we draw lessons from countries which are developed, for the United States, the richest one per cent of Americans now take home almost 24 per cent of income, up from almost 9 per cent in 1976.
As Timothy Noah of Slate noted in an excellent series on inequality, the United States now arguably has a more unequal distribution of wealth than traditional banana republics like Nicaragua, Venezuela and Guyana.
CEOs of the largest American companies earned an average of 42 times as much as the average worker in 1980, but 531 times as much in 2001. Perhaps the most astounding statistic is this: From 1980 to 2005, more than four-fifths of the total increase in American incomes went to the richest 1 percent.
So it is not different from what the majority of the people of Tanzania are facing. There is no statistics to show the level of disparity, but if you just look at the communities there is every indication that more people are living below the poverty line.
That's the backdrop for one of the first big post-election fights in Tanzania. The issue is how the politicians who have assumed their posts can deal with the question of equitable distribution of resources.
The country is endowed with vast treasures if well exploited for the interest of the people can bring drastic development. People who are living in rural areas are not demanding to be given a piece of moon. They just want to be treated with respect by the members of parliament.
The politicians have a tendency of not visiting their constituencies and only showing up when the elections are due. It is amazing to see even in this time and era, there are still circles of politicians who have no regrets to seek electorate votes even when it is crystal clear that they have not been able to bring a drop of change.
I think this time most of them were tagged and the public wrath ended their hegemony. Rural communities in other countries such as Islamic Republic of Iran enjoy all the amenities that their urban fellows have.
For example in Iran, the government has been able to bring basic utilities such as electricity, water and gas in every rural residence. The communities do not pay for such facilities since the government is subsidizing.
Imagine no family has to travel even a kilometer to access health facilities. I bet that Tanzania is also able to achieve this. But most politicians are not thinking outside the box. Many times it has been the president who is making all these gestures and efforts about improving the lives of local communities.
Politicians are just singing the chorus; they do not want to compose their own songs. Why other countries have politicians who are creative and ready to sacrifice for their constituencies and not in Tanzania. I think these politicians who occupy the posts have failed to empathize with their communities.
It would be a big mistake if the media after the elections will not make an agenda to follow-up on all the pledges and promises that these politicians were making in their campaigns. If the media does not take this opportunity to put the record straight about those politicians who are acting cunningly to exploit the masses, then history will indeed judge us for failing to defend the weak and oppressed.
In short, inequality leaves people on the lower rungs feeling like hamsters on a wheel spinning ever faster, without hope or escape, but it is man creation. Many political scientists believe that economic polarization also shatters our sense of national union and common purpose, fostering political polarization as well.
So in this postelection landscape, let's not aggravate income gaps that already would make a Latin American caudillo proud. To me, we are not far from reaching a 'banana republic' point, especially if the media does not act responsibly to expose corrupt elements in the system, where our inequality has become both economically unhealthy and morally repugnant.

Original_Search_Title: Tingatinga Piece Fetches Regional Record Price in Paris Auction
Headline: East Africa: Tingatinga Piece Fetches Regional Record Price in Paris Auction
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The world record price for an East African painting has been set at $51,079.
And the picture -- a Tingatinga piece by the late Rajabu Chiwaya -- was bought 10 years ago at the Gallery Watatu in Nairobi, for just...$6,000. A Tingatinga piece at the same gallery would now cost anything from $15,000-$25,000.
The picture that set the record, at an auction in Paris by Artcurial, is called Gold Spotted Leopard and Friend The Songbird, and was painted in the 1970s in enamels on a threaded rubber board. It measures 106cm by 82cm. If you can't manage the Watatu's price range, don't worry -- there are plenty of other places to buy.
The most obvious is the studios of the Tingatinga Arts Co-operative Society at Msanani in Dar. Or, if you are based in Nairobi, you need not go further than the Banana Hill Arts Centre, a 30-minute drive from the city centre. There, the gallery owner Shine Tani will show you a group of genuine, if small, Tingatinga pieces (each around 30cm by 30cm) at only Ksh3,000 ($40) each. These paintings -- of hippos, lions, birds and other delightfully innocent creatures -- are stacked on Tani's desk, not on the wall.
That honour goes to another Tanzanian artist, Haji Chilonga whose works are on display until the end of this month. They make for an interesting show. Chilonga is an artist with real talent but, as seen here, it is somewhat submerged by what for me is an irritating trend towards the sort of slickness seen in advertising work and magazine illustrations.
At times, Chilonga struggles to be seen through his own popularity and mannerisms. For example, a figure pulling the vegetable cart in one of the strongest pictures on show is shadowed by a series of curves -- like those speed stripes you see in cartoons -- while the legs, which should be straining with the effort of heaving the cart, are reduced to ciphers -- a couple of paint strokes merely to indicate their presence but not to support the weight of the figure they bear.
You can see it again in his painting of a group of people carrying what I take to be water containers on their heads. These are two dimensional indications (colourful and attractive patterns though they might be) rather than solid statements that describe weight and form.
This is very clever but not convincing. It is what is famously known as Marmite painting -- the quality is there and you either like it or you don't. I don't. It's too automatic for my taste (no sense of struggle) and rather too contrived.
However, in its technical ability, Chilonga's work is head and shoulders above a lot of the stuff seen on gallery walls, and it is easy to see how he gained his reputation.
When he relaxes and paints what is before him -- and the picture of a palm tree here, though still somewhat stylised, is a fair example -- he is rather good. He captures the palm's fountain-like spray of leaves perfectly while its cool, morning tones are enlivened by little touches of orange, signalling the rising sun and the day ahead.
Another successful picture, I thought was the one hanging near the front door of a herd of cattle dashing through the dust. He has caught the form of the beasts and the flurries of his paint give us something of their stamping energy, too. It's just that Chilonga seems to spend too much of his time trying to paint Chilongas... and his natural fluency becomes ever more rigid as style takes over.
Frank Whalley runs Lenga Juu, a fine arts and media consultancy based in Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: Try and Avoid Overexposure!
Headline: Kenya: Try and Avoid Overexposure!
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It always seems like a good idea but it never is. An artiste, like currency can lose value if over exposed. A couple of those come to mind like Jua Cali, Churchill, Daddy Owen to name just a few. Maybe its just me who feels like Churchill is losing his brilliant sense of humour.
The jokes sound the same and the Kikuyu, Luo jokes have lost their punch. Churchill has been doing a great job but maybe the overexposure is sucking him dry.
He brought out a side of comedy that wasn't common and he made his millions and is still making more. However, as it is with everything else under the blue sky, too much of something is bad.
Churchill is practically everywhere, which means that he is bound to repeat his jokes over and over again. His favourite are the tribal jokes, which are funny until you hear them a fourth and fifth time.
This comedian is on radio from Monday to Friday, he has his top rated talk show, Churchill Live, weekly and is hosting corporate parties almost on a daily basis.
Let's be honest, were he a lesser man, he would have recycled all the jokes he's ever done but he has somehow managed to stay on top.
But when you are at these engagements, you are bound to repeat a lot of your jokes and as our entertainment industry, is very small, the audiences will contain familiar faces.
This means, half of your audience will hear you recycle your jokes more then three times and that is when the jokes go stale.
It is the same route that was taken by another comedian, Nyambane. We loved him until we started anticipating every joke he had up his sleeve and he slowly faded from the limelight with his clown suit and banana-eating habits.
It is the same with musicians. Jua Cali, as loved as he is, got to the point where he had to take a back seat because we knew all his songs by heart and being the clever guy he is, he took a step back to regain his breath and creativity and if possible start a new style.
As for Churchill it is time he invested heavily on writers both for his show and himself because he cannot make the entire country laugh with the same jokes.
Even international stand-up comedians like Chris Rock, Jay Leno and Seinfeld have a dozen or more writers to ensure they stay fresh and relevant.
As a creative, you need to take a sabbatical from the public to strengthen your brand since the more scarce you are the more in demand you get and the higher premiums you can demand.
It's the same theory with money; it is scarce and durable, just ask Zimbabwe.

Original_Search_Title: Senate Under a Former General - What Lessons?
Headline: Nigeria: Senate Under a Former General - What Lessons?
Body_Text:
Abuja — Prior to and shortly after his emergence as president of the Senate, not many gave Senator Alechenu Bonaventure David Mark any chance of lasting more than six months on the obvious musical chair of the highest lawmaking chamber in Nigeria, the red- carpet-Senate.
This perception was based on different parameters, but rightly or wrongly, the antecedents of the retired military general - turned - politician from his military days to his journey into the murky waters of politics and subsequent election into the senate in 1999, were such that only a few could ever had thought he could spend six months on the seat of the president of the Senate.
His re-election into the Senate in 2007 was keenly contested, just as his emergence as senate president was equally keenly contested, to such an extent that both contests were turbulent for the retired military General.
His first breakthrough was the sudden realisation by the present director-general of his campaign, Comrade Abba Moro that there was really no reason to continue with the cabal that was out to stop the senator's third term bid for no good reason other than personal interesst.
Suffice to say that the battle did not end, it crossed over to the senate when the National Assembly was proclaimed by the late president, Umaru Musa Yar'Adua and the ruling PDP zoning of the office of the Senate president to the North-Central geo-political zone.
The likes of Senators Nuhu Aliyu (Niger), Abubakar Danso Sodangi (Nassarawa), Gbemisola Saraki (Kwara) and George Akume (Benue) contested against David Mark, although others withdrew, it was obvious that majority of the senators preferred David Mark, except his long time rival George Akume who felt as a political general, it was pertinent for him to fight to the finish.
Of course he (Georg Akume) had the support of the former governors who got elected into the senate in 2007, but as expected he lost the battle again.
The parameters that were used in arriving at the rather hasty conclusion that David Mark was likely not to make a good senate president became mere fallacies. Rather than divide the Senate to the advantage of only those that voted for him, he embraced and carried all along.
It was expected that he was going to trade tackles with the bloc of former governors, it was also expected that the Obasanjo and Yar'Adua factor would also work against him; but above all, it was expected that his perceived arrogance, from his military, would also be the final nail on his coffin, as was the case with the late Chuba Okadigbo.
This perception and fears that David Mark would make the worse Senate president became even more obvious when he told the world shortly after his emergence as senate president that the National Assembly would alter some of the provisions of the 1999 constitution.
To a lot of people, it was not just a tall promise but an impossible one, especially judging by the way the attempt to alter the provisions of the 1999 constitution under the leadership of former President Olusegun Obasanjo went and the role David Mark and a few others were alleged to have played, which saw the bath water thrown away with the baby.
His quirk of referring to his colleagues as "my bosses" did not make much meaning to some, who still believed that as a General he could only carry on pretences a short while; but for three and half years running the tone has not changed, which in it self exemplary.
Another fear was whether David Mark would actually avoid the commonest of the banana peels in the leadership of the National Assembly - touching monies meant for the senators, which had swept away most of the presiding officers of the National Assembly in the past.
Research has shown so far that one of the reasons he is still Senate president is because he blinds himself to the direction of funds meant for senators.
It was also expected that having a former General as head of parliament with the strongest weapon of "impeachment", working with a civilian government where indiscipline is the order of the day, the country was going to experience the most acrimonious relationship between legislature and the executive.
Instead, the Senate under David Mark is so far the only one that successfully altered the provisions of the 1999 constitution. It has witnessed the most cordial relationship with the executive, swept all the banana peels away and above all witnessed unexpected calm which has silenced the musical chair.
Rather than arrogance, the Senate president exhibits a very high sense of humor that thrills senators and takes away the stress of the day off them.
The doctrine of necessity introduced by the Senate president, which brought to an end the political logjam that was occasioned by the long absence of late President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua on account of ill health, where the then Vice president could not perform the functions of the president, stood him out and clearly defined his sagacity and patriotism.
It is important to note that the feeling in most quarters is that the National Assembly under the leadership of David Mark once again, rose to the occasion with maturity and exemplary leadership qualities.
The seeming resolve of the Senate leadership under David Mark not to tinker with the standing committees of the upper legislative chamber is also responsible for the stability, more so that chairmen of committees are allowed to run their budgets without undue interference.

Original_Search_Title: Group Alleges Plot to Kill Gov Akpabio
Headline: Nigeria: Group Alleges Plot to Kill Gov Akpabio
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE campaign organization of Governor Godswill Akpabio of Akwa Ibom State yesterday said it had uncovered plans by a group of people to assassinate the governor as well as kidnap members of his family.
According to a statement issued by the Godswill 2011 campaign organisation, signed by its director of media and publicity, Mr. Anietie Ekong and made available to Daily Champion in Lagos, those behind the plot, plan to carry it out within the country or during any of the governor's visits abroad.
The organization, against this backdrop, said it has requested for urgent security intervention to prevent the threat from being carried out.
It alleged that the plot to assassinate Governor Akpabio was hatched by some people working for a gubernatorial aspirant in Akwa Ibom State, noting
that on Sunday, October 24, 2010, a member of the group using a pseudonym, Imo Udoekaekpin, (ekot2010@gmail.com) and working for the said aspirant caused to be published in Ibom Forum, a Yahoo Group site allegedly bankrolled by the gubernatorial contender, calling for the murder of the governor and the kidnap of members of his family.
The statement quoted the Yahoo Group post as saying in reference to Governor Akpabio: "the governor will choke . when a gun is drawn on him. So why . whine like a baby instead of kidnapping or ordering the kidnapping of our governor, his wife, his children?!"
It further quoted the email as saying," so why waste time begging the governor . enable the kidnapping of her Excellency Unoma Akpabio; go beyond that and take in his baby son; find them in Banana Island, find them in London; let there be no safe place. Make our dear governor feel the pain .Till you are willing to waste even the Governor, misery will prevail."
The Akpabio campaign organization condemned the plot and called on Akwa Ibom people to avoid politicians who have shown desperation in the pursuit of their ambition through blackmail and threat. "Our investigation shows that Imo Udoekaekpin who claims to reside in Uyo is actually" somebody who resides in the USA "and is among those sponsored to undermine the Akwa Ibom state government.
"This plot signals an emerging culture of perilous politics alien to Akwa Ibom state and its people. It is very disturbing given the trend of viciousness now being demonstrated by a few recalcitrant Akwa Ibom citizens in the United States of America who we are told, are being sponsored to destabilize the administration of Chief Godswill Akpabio through the dissemination of falsehood and propaganda through the internet.
"We call upon all Akwa Ibom people to remain vigilant, avoid and dissociate themselves from desperate politicians and other persons who promote or practice the culture of violence and divisiveness. Rather, the people should fight to uproot such a culture with all lawful means available lest they lose generations and future glories of Akwa Ibom state to self-aggrandizing schemes by desperate opportunists.
The governor's campaign organization further linked the plot to a recent interview in a national daily in which a governorship aspirant called for the stoning of Governor Akpabio.
The organisation said, "what we are witnessing is a growing culture of thuggery, violence and brigandage which have been introduced to the politics of Akwa Ibom State by a self-confessed motor park tout because of his vaulting ambition."
It further said that the recent assertion by Governor Akpabio that incidents of kidnappings and political assassinations in Akwa Ibom State have political undertones in terms of sponsorship should be thoroughly investigated by security agencies since the opposition in the state believe that because the governor cannot be faulted on performance, the only way to get him out of the way was through the contrivance of a state of insecurity that would lead to the declaration of a state of emergency.
The organisation ,however, advised supporters of Governor Akpabio to remain calm and law abiding, assuring that
threats to the lives of the governor and members of his family have been reported to security agencies. .

Original_Search_Title: How to Unlock the Potential of Crafts Sector
Headline: Uganda: How to Unlock the Potential of Crafts Sector
Body_Text:
Kampala — HOLDING brand new bank notes totaling to sh70,000, Justine Nakate wears a prolonged smile as she leaves the cashier's desk at Uganda Crafts 2000. She has just sold 10 baskets to the company.
A group of over 30 women are tirelessly weaving more baskets and consulting each other such that none makes any design or size in deviation from what the company requires.
On the other side of the office, Betty Kinene, the proprietor, is busy too. Her aim is to beat the one month deadline to deliver over 1,000 baskets to One Thousand Villages, an American company that buys handicrafts from Africa.
The above scenario is a clear indication that Uganda's crafts industry has the capacity to bail thousands of people out of unemployment.
However, local players are concerned that despite their efforts to drive the handicraft business, the Government has not come in to support the sector.
"The crafts industry, if given the desired attention, has the capacity to empower nationals economically," Kinene says.
She adds that even though the industry is a source of income for many Ugandans, little has been done to exploit its full potential.
Uganda has a wide array of handicraft products, ranging from basketry, mats, ceramics, beads, pottery, hand textiles, toys, jewellery, bags, seasonal cards, ornaments, shoes, cutlery to sculptures.
Most of the products are made out of local materials like banana fibre, wood, soft stones, waste papers, clay and animal horns.
The crafts are produced from all regions of the country with limited differentiation based on culture, history and traditions.
The market is dominated by women, accounting for over 80%, with the local market supported by a sizeable number of tourists from overseas. Nakate, a mother of three, says she earns at least sh100,000 per week from her home-based handicraft business, especially when the exporting company that buys her stock has orders.
Her business costs are minimal too. She does not pay rent for the business premises and the materials used are free, apart from the weaving needles and paint.
Nakate gets time to do her domestic duties because she doesn't get away from home.
Lilian Ssemigga has been in the crafts business for six years now, and is an exporter, who periodically travels overseas for international exhibitions.
She operates a shop at the equator along Kampala-Masaka road and has experience in making a range of crafts from waste papers, sisals and banana fibre.
Ssemigga notes that local industrial players operate under vast constraints in Uganda.
Where is the missing link?
Basing on the information she exchanges with her counterparts in Kenya, Ssemigga says Kenya is far ahead because dealers in handicraft businesses predominantly depend on their government's support.
She adds that as individual Ugandans dig deeper into their pockets, Kenyan and Tanzanian governments have always had a hand in facilitating their counterparts to participate in international exhibitions.
"The government of Kenya makes sure that handicraft makers and buyers have continuous access to long term credit," she laments.
"They have regular access to local and foreign market information, among other incentives, but all these have for long remained dreams in Uganda despite efforts to lobby our government to follow suit."
Ssemigga adds that because governments elsewhere in East Africa support craft makers, Kenyans, for example, have resorted to buying Ugandan crafts like baskets and re-branding them to sell to overseas markets.
"If the Government helps us develop, it will enable more people have disposable income, which will in turn enhance the nation's purchasing power," she says.
Government's response
Ben Naturinda, the deputy executive director of the Uganda Export Promotions Board (UEPB), admits that the crafts industry has the potential in helping the Government fulfill its women's economic empowerment programmes.
He says plans were underway to help small-and-medium enterprises access foreign markets through collection and dissemination of data about the markets and sponsoring more exhibitors intending to participate in international trade fairs.
According to the 2008 to 2012 Uganda National Export Strategy document, the European Union market is worth about 20.8b euros. The US is the world's largest importer of handicrafts, gifts and decorative articles.
Naturinda says there was still a need to exploit the fair trade opportunities provided under bilateral trade agreements such as the European Union under Enterprise Bargaining Agreement and the US under the African Growth Opportunity Act that offer preferential treatment to Uganda's handicraft products.
Uganda exports small quantities of handicrafts, especially baskets and wickerware with statistics from UEPB indicating that crafts valued at $0.141m (about sh308m) were exported between 2004 and 2005.
The body does not have recent data about the industry's exports, but revenue was projected to have increased since the 2005 records.
Currently, more of the crafts are sold domestically and a few, exported to the US, Canada, Australia, Germany, theUK and Japan.
Website information available with Fibre to Fashion, an online publisher about fashion, indicates that Kenya considered the handicraft industry as a crucial component of the micro-and-small enterprise sector, giving reason why a lot of attention is given to the industry.

Original_Search_Title: Symbolism At the Presidential Candidate Nominations 2011
Headline: Uganda: Symbolism At the Presidential Candidate Nominations 2011
Body_Text:
Kampala — This time, the presidential candidates' nomination days - Oct.25 and Oct.26 - exceeded expectations.
The frenzy, excitement, and colour of the mammoth crowds on streets that had been perfectly cleared of traffic compensated for the loss in number of candidates who were whittled from the 40-something who picked forms to a manageable seven.
It was an event marked by symbolism. The angelic white uniform of the not so angelic Electoral Commission team; the Progressive People's Party candidate Bidandi Ssali's glowing orange-lit lanterns; and Uganda Peoples Congress candidate Olara Otunnu's vintage floral black and red shirt, were all chosen with a message in mind. Otunnu's shirt was so embroidered and so flowing-like that one wag said designing it was possibly what delayed his nomination.
Fashion-wise, however, the cream of the cream was Naomi Mao, aspiring First Lady of Democratic Party (DP) candidate Norbert Mao. Her little black dress hugged her not-so petite figure perfectly and her giraffe brown-on white clutch bag stole the night.
Naomi had rehearsed well. When her husband moved to shake hands with the presiding Electoral Commission staff, she deftly snuck into the photo op and received the certificate of registration with Mao.
Even veteran First Lady, Janet Museveni, not to mention wannabe Susan Bidandi Ssali, must have gone green with envy. When they had the chance, they stayed glued to their chairs.
Message-wise, however, the day belonged to Beti Kamya, candidate of new-kid on the block, the Uganda Federal Alliance (UFA). She had a single-powerful unique selling point: vote me and get a federal Uganda.
But she also threw in an appeal for the gender vote. Apparently, at a recent conference with Ugandan women activists, which was also attended by Liberian President Ellen Johnson Sirleaf, they resolved to pressure President Yoweri Museveni to appoint a woman vice-president. But why bother, she said, just vote for me and you get a woman president without depending on someone else's mercy.
This time Forum for Democratic (FDC) President Kizza Besigye, who is the joint Inter-Party Co-operation (IPC) candidate, went to the right barber. He looked dignified and mature, a far cry from the youthful back-bush cut he had in the 2006 election. More importantly he was seconded for nomination by Joseph Ssemogerere, a former Katikiro of Buganda Kingdom. Besigye wants the Buganda vote. Some of his supporters wore the FDC blue and the Inter-Party Cooperation (IPC) orange.
As usual, as his convoy snaked to Nakivubo Stadium in downtown Kampala city for his post-nomination rally, he had his skirmishes with the pro-Museveni police.
At the rally Besigye told the crowd: "Everybody knows that the NRM government has failed and this time we do not need to remind people about that Museveni's winning percentage has been going down at every election." He continued: "We will tell people what we will do when in office of the President and victory will be ours. We have been matching towards the frontline and the NRM is at the edge and this time they won't escape defeat."
The man to beat, President Yoweri Museveni, and his graceful First Lady were ordinary but they made up for lack of imagination with their choice of delegation. Museveni, 64, opted to combine the old with the new. He was proposed by bush-war veteran and NRM historical, Moses Kigongo and seconded by recent recruit to the NRM, former FDC MP Alex Onzima.
As early as 9 am, thousands of youthful NRM supporters thronged the city centre, Entebbe and Jinja Roads to cheer Museveni as he headed to the Mandela National Stadium Namboole to be nominated for the fourth time. Thousands more painted Kololo Airstrip yellow, the colour of the NRM campaign.
The crowds in yellow T-shirts and waving dry banana leaves (symbol of Museveni's eternal rule) were joined by the bodaboda motorcycle taxi riders also in yellow shirts and caps. "Tajakugenda, Tajakugenda, Tajakugenda, Oo Tajakugenda" they chanted meaning "Museveni will never lose".
At 10:07 am Museveni became the first candidate to be officially registered by the EC. He arrived at Namboole with 20 members of the party's Central Executive Committee who later turned up at Kololo in a yellow bus with inscriptions of the numerous districts where NRM boasts a presence and "NRM PakaLast" meaning "NRM forever".
When Museveni arrived at Kololo at 2:30 pm, the NRM fans were ecstatic. The ground was filled with the deafening blows of yellow whistles, vuvuzelas and joyful ululations. Never mind that to pull such crowds, the NRM paid the cheerleaders and fueled the bodaboda motorcycles. Some, as has become the norm, complained they had been diddled.
Before Museveni could take to the podium, NRM secretary General Amama Mbabazi invited Alex Onzima who seconded Museveni's nomination to explain why he did it. Onzima, right on cue, criticised his erstwhile colleagues in the Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) leadership.
The strategy became clear: Onzima and Kampala Mayor Ntege Sebagala, who formerly was of the Democratic Party, had "seen the light" by crossing to the NRM. Museveni is anxious to capture areas he has persistently lost in previous elections. Onzima promised to deliver the West Nile vote to Museveni and Sebagala promised the Buganda vote. The Presidential election is set for February 18, 2011.

Original_Search_Title: Artists' Union Shocked at Hip-Hop Brawl
Headline: Namibia: Artists' Union Shocked at Hip-Hop Brawl
Body_Text:
The president of the Oruuano Artists' Union, Banana Shekupe, has expressed shock and dismay at the violence that left an artist and his friend injured during Blacksheep's album launch in Windhoek two weeks ago.
The event landed hip-hop artist Jericho in hospital with stab wounds that were allegedly inflicted by GMP 411 editor Chris-Paul, also known as Krespo, and GMP dancers Dicksa and Dennis. Jericho was also hit by an oncoming vehicle, while his friend Anton Langbooi almost lost his middle finger in the brawl.
"This is very bad for the industry," said Shekupe, calling for more discipline in the music industry and saying that such behaviour is discouraging sponsors from supporting such events in the future.
"Artists are peaceful people. They work together. They are not aggressive," he said.
Shekupe called on the Police to protect artists at music shows.
"Artists' life are at stake," he said, pointing out that there is a lot of jealousy going around and that artists should avoid going to certain places.
"What is the future of artists if there is no protection?" asked Shekupe, stating that in other countries, they were better treated. He urged artists to consult the union if there are issues that need to be resolved.
"We don't want our artists to get killed," he said.
Last year, kwaito artist The Dogg's face was scarred in an incident at a Windhoek nightclub. His alleged attacker claimed he was acting in self-defence. Both men laid charges of assault against one another.
City Police spokesman Marx Hipandwa says a Police presence at music performances can't be guaranteed but the organisers of such events should follow the right procedures and apply for Police protection.
Hipandwa said it is impossible for the Police to be present at every event but if fights break out, they will intervene.

Original_Search_Title: Abuja's Night Markets
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja's Night Markets
Body_Text:
EDEGBE ODEMWINGIE and ONOKOGU KANAYO JUBAL went on an excursion around the budding night markets springing all over the Federal Capital territory.
Abuja night life is beginning to blossom. Presently, its claim to fame (at least at night) is its' hugely popular and thriving pepper soup and grill (poultry, fish or meat) garden bars. At a peep further, night markets are springing up in the most unlikely places. Empty spaces in the day get transformed into trading hubs at night. From food vendors offering sizzling delicacies, raunchy, electric music squares to sellers of rat poison and other conceivable sundry commodities, Abuja's suburbs and city centers are bustling with night markets. "Abuja streets come alive at night," David say. "The town is fast following in the footsteps of its elder nocturnal prodigy, Lagos".
From Berger junction, Nyanya/Marararba bridges to Kubwa Federal Housing junction, night markets are flourishing in Abuja. The buzz sets in when trading kicks off at 5pm and closes at around midnight.
On closer look, night markets are mostly situated around busy bus stops and junctions. The mobile nature and accessibility of night markets give the traders an edge over regular day-time markets.
Typically, night markets are dominated by the sale of food items and in terms of scale of operation, the volume of businesses handled by the sellers is generally small.
At Area 1, in Garki District, the night market bustles with vendors cashing in on passengers waiting for their long-haul buses to transport them to the various satellite towns surrounding the FCT. To Callistus, the night market in Area 1 is a convenient arrangement. "While I queue for my bus, I buy virtually everything I need". He further revelas to LEADERSHIP SUNDAY that it saves him the transport expenses of shopping in a regular day-time market which, in any case, is off his home route.
