In Area 1, the vendors do not operate in isolation. An authorised park is situated there. The park in question occupies three of the four-lane expressway that leads to Gudu district and other satellite towns surrounding Abuja. With vehicles zooming past at sometimes over 120km per hour at night, countless persons have lost their lives on this road.
Salami is not oblivious of the dangers of picking passengers in the middle of the express way, especially at night. "If I park down the road, I won't get passengers quickly, " he said. Salami picks passengers on his way home from work to Mararaba, a border town in Nassarawa state. "At least it bears the cost of fueling my car".
Last year, Comfort (not real name), a former fuel attendant at the Conoil Filling station nearby was knocked down while trying to cross the ever-busy express road. She survived, but not without permanent injuries to her back and waist. Comfort blames her accident on the often chaotic nature of the road (especially at night), but agrees she is lucky to be alive.
In Lugbe, the story is no different. From the Federal Housing junction, the night market begins to bustle. The inviting perishables on display are sure to catch the attention of would - be shoppers and passers - by alike. Vendors expertly arrange groceries ranging from Irish potatoes, baskets of tomatoes, atarugu (smaller peppers) and tartashe (the larger ones), onions, cabbages, lettuce, African Spinach and suya (grilled beef).
Further down the road, off the Julius Berger camp-out, the aroma of peppered meat and chicken flavoured with all sorts of spices caress the nose. Music blares from the juke boxes of commercial CD hawkers who have giant CD racks filled with the latest pirated music releases.
Caroline and her friends are dancing hysterically to the sound of Tuface Idibia's Implication at Onuche's music store. Meanwhile, the bread seller who shares space with the music seller is shouting herself hoarse trying to call attention to her loaves.
Over the noise, the Deraco Productions van is calling everyone to buy 'genuine' Nollywood movies. Come buy original Nollywood movies for just N150. Broda, sista, come buy am now." he said in Pidgin. "You no go see am again, na your last chance be dis." So much cacophony.
If Dr. Igwe Shaolin's animated performance could be intrepreted, one could swear the street doctor could cure all venereal diseases brought to him. "We have a cure for gonorrhea, syphilis, staphylococcus and anything wey dey worry you. Don't die in silence," he warned.
Shoe traders are seen shouting invitations to hurrying, mostly unconcerned and sometimes gullible customers, many of whom hesitate in front of piles of shoes, clothes and bags.
Banana and groundnut sellers sit side-by-side. The mallams in this cluster trade mainly in fruits.
The human traffic and market concentration around Lugbe is indeed intimidating . Perpetual, a shoe trader says she has been in Lugbe since 2006 , when the FCDA carried out demolition exercises in Karmo. "It was the FCDA demolitions carried out in various parts of Abuja that resulted in the heavy human population you see here". According to her, with limited space to trade in town, sellers had to take refuge in Lugbe were restrictions are limited. Business is sure to go uninterrupted as long as payments are made to the market touts and 'traffic police.'
On the part of the sellers, Okeh tells our reporter that with trading at night, most of his co- sellers are able to escape the daily harassment by the Abuja Environmental workers.
"We know when they (environmental workers) close for the day, so we usually start sales after that time", he said.
Asabe, a seamstress, roasts peppered chickens in the market. Over shouts of, "Wuse, Berger, Area 1, Area 10" from touts, she roasts chickens in the night market on the side to make extra income. "In Abuja, you can't rely on one source of income unless you want to be tossed into the street by your landlord," she said. "At least, I am not a prostitute."
For those who throng the place, what's the attraction? Nonye, a salesgirl in Wuse market, says goods are unbelievably cheap. "With N250, you can get a good bunch of bananas here. In Wuse, if you touch the bananas, they say N300, N500. The Irish potatoes are also cheaper here and so are the clothes and shoes, though the quality is lower."
The clothes section is flooded with Chinese imports. Etuk, a banker, who was trying to get a Barcelona FC jersey for himself and his girlfriend thinks the clothes in the market are fair-priced and of wearable quality. "I love this stuff, especially the jerseys. I have about four of them, even the swimming shorts with the clubs' names inscribed on them. Sometimes I go for a stroll and just enjoy the market bustle: buy roasted maize here, get coconut there, buy chilled sachet water there, you know? The life's down, but not out."
Although, these night markets are mostly situated around Abuja's suburbs and surrounding satellite towns, they are also sprouting around the city's business districts from the fast food booths in Wuse to the night markets in Areas 1 and 3.
It is hoped that these market don't develop into some of the trading monstrosities (remember Oshodi and Mushin?) that virtually crippled some areas in Lagos before the recent efforts by the current governor, Babatunde Raji Fashola (SAN). These night markets, as in the case of Oshodi, come with attendant opportunistic crime and street trading, which often times result in agonising gridlocks for motorists.
If night market operations are to be improved, improved lighting system and provision of other infrastructural facilities such as parking spaces, water, public conveniences and the re-organisation of these markets will go a long way in improving their general appeal.

Original_Search_Title: Braving the Nation's 'Buses of Death'
Headline: Tanzania: Braving the Nation's 'Buses of Death'
Body_Text:
Moshi, in the shadow of Kilimanjaro, is a pretty, community spirited town. There are the local Chagga, the Masai people, the first- and second-generation Indian expatriates running successful trades, the ready-to-go or worn-out mountain climbers, the foreign volunteers and the gap-year students. This mix is a big part of what gives Moshi its instantly likeable and friendly feeling.
The easiest way to get around Moshi is on foot. The town seems to revolve around one main clock tower roundabout and whichever exit you take will not leave you disappointed: you may come across a Hindu temple, a mosque, a Manchester United merchandise vendor or a banana seller.
When you need a break, stop off at one of the many quaint little coffee houses roasting fresh Arabica beans picked from the foothills. My friend Razan and I sat out in the sun, shielded by giant banana leaves, sipping a coffee, sharing jokes and memories with Charles and Salim, our former mountain guides, now our friends and local guides.
Bristol Cottages was a great location from which to explore Moshi Town in the two days I had. With the physical exhaustion of the climb lingering, it was delightful waking up in this bed and breakfast, being served a continental-style morning meal by friendly staff. The place is run by a lovely Indian family who, used to climbers passing through the town, offer a 24-hour laundry service (from sleeping bags to boots) and recommend local safari and tour operators.
For the quality of service and rooms, the price was more than reasonable - $25 (about Sh35,000) per night per double cottage room with air conditioning, including breakfast and free Wi-Fi. While Moshi doesn't need a lot of time to explore, it would be a shame to miss this town if you're in Tanzania.
Inter-city buses in Tanzania are unreliable, uncomfortable, incredibly slow and, most of all, dangerous. Having just looked up the statistics on Tanzania road traffic deaths, I'm relieved to have survived. Half-way through my first journey, I had re-named them the "buses of death". The ticketing system is bizarrely the one strand of order in what is otherwise a day of chaos - you may not be able to rely on reaching your destination in one piece but you can rely on your seat number.
It took Razan and I almost 10 hours to reach our next city, with no time to get off the bus for a toilet break in case we were left stranded (bus drivers are notorious for zooming off). We were on a paved road for an hour before the driver veered off onto a dirt track through a barren, dry desert, which was home to the odd wild baboon.
Despite booking on the most expensive bus company ($2-$3) for a single intercity journey), I have never felt more frightened in a vehicle. Dust, sand and dirt continued to layer our bodies and many of the locals began to cover their faces with scarves, as did I. The swerving and loud horns continued all day and there were times where I genuinely thought the bus would flip over. Razan and I were so scared that all we could do was laugh.
Singida, described by one local as the "forgotten part of Tanzania", was an experience with a very different flavour than Moshi. Razan and I had come to see the work of The Kanga Project, a charity for which we were fundraising, and visit the local women it would be directly benefiting. We jumped into the back of 4x4 the next morning and drove to the nearby village of Ilongero.
We spoke to local schoolchildren about their inspirations, stumbled upon local village political elections and were able to see where the bulk of our fundraising had been used - a solar-powered chicken incubator. Sustainable development and education were the key criteria for our fundraising and we left Ilongero that evening content in the knowledge that our efforts would benefit people and wondering how we could do more
It was time for another ride on the "bus of death". Again, the only thing right about the journey was our assigned seat numbers. Although this bus had windows, it felt more unsafe this time around. Once again, we were the only non-locals on the packed bus and as soon as the dirt road track began, we passed an overturned bus and an overturned truck in the space of an hour.
We still had 10 hours left. I contemplated texting our bus details back home in case something happened but was suddenly distracted. Razan was sitting in the aisle with her head back and eyes closed. I knew she was awake but I couldn't bear to tell her what was sitting about four centimetres in front of her face.
"Razan, please don't look in front of you." A local woman standing in the aisle was holding a chicken whose claws were swaying gradually towards Razan's face. Razan went into what can only be described as white-knuckle panic that lasted for the next two hours. During that time we heard the crow of roosters from the front of the bus and the luggage above, providing a welcome distraction to the dangerous driving.
Another unlikely but welcome distraction came about nine hours into the journey, when the bus broke down. At this point, we were more concerned about a toilet break and surviving the last hour of the highway than reaching our destination.
We finally pulled into the bus station in our next transit city, Arusha. We hadn't even dismounted the bus when we were flooded with offers from touts reaching into the bus windows offering to carry our bags or take us on a safari. We ran to the storage compartments and grabbed our backpacks just in time to make a quick getaway.
Although the buses in Tanzania left me slightly frazzled and in need of three consecutive showers, at $3 (Dh11) they are an unavoidable risk for intercity transport, unless you want to fork out at least $200 for a private car.
It's a shame we only had one night in Arusha, but we made the most of it and fortunately stumbled upon what appeared to be an eating institution in the town. Khan's BBQ on a Bonnet, a car spare parts dealership by night and BBQ by day, was one of the most memorable meals on my travels.
The concept may conjure up thoughts of your kebabs splashed with petrol or door handles in your salad, but for $3 per head, you can choose from a wide-ranging buffet of grilled meats, lentils and salads, washing it down with fresh passion fruit juice. Located on Mosque Street (walking distance from the bus station) in the Muslim district, it's essential to pay this place a visit. Being hugely popular, it's also a good place to meet fellow backpackers or climbers.
Eager for some rest, reflection and relaxation, I was ready for our next destination. I had been on the go for so long that five days on a beach as a final stop on my round-the-world tour sounded like a good way to wind down. Razan and I boarded our Precision Air flight ($125) from Arusha airport (tip: if you want a view of Kilimanjaro from the sky, request a seat when checking in). Flying out of mainland Tanzania, over the striking turquoise waters, we saw a lush green island in the distance - Zanzibar!
The author writes for The National, the leading English-language voice in the Middle East.

Original_Search_Title: A Prize for Winners and a Big Hand for the Unsung Heroes
Headline: Kenya: A Prize for Winners and a Big Hand for the Unsung Heroes
Body_Text:
Nairobi — I had every intention of writing an epic story celebrating the strength of the runners participating in the Standard Chartered Nairobi half-marathon race.
Their tremendous feat of athleticism is something less than one per cent of the society can confidently say they have accomplished; they deserve every piece of credit they receive  and they will receive a lot.
However, as I stood at the starting line on Sunday, surrounded by a mass of enthusiastic folks urging on the runners, my focus shifted from the athletes to a small, handmade sign toted by two boys on the sidelines. "Go Daddy!" it read, "Run with your heart!"
There is no doubt that what this man's heart needed most lay with the boys; that their small banner would give him more strength than his legs ever would to complete the race. For every runner at the starting line, there are at least five of their biggest fans actively taking photos off the course.
They reassure the nervous runners; they hold warm-up jackets and carry bags filled with energy gels, bananas, iPods and more.
These loved ones have gone through every step of training with their runners, adjusting their lives and schedules so their athlete can make it to the finish.
They are the strangers on the sidelines who wield raucous cowbells and scream your name at the top of their lungs regardless of how insane they might look.
They are the patient volunteers who pick up your water bottles after you toss them at their feet. The cheerleaders, the spectators, the volunteers. These are the unsung 'Mashujaa' (heroes)of the marathon.
Nonetheless, they must have cheered Michael Tiony to victory in the half marathon as Chemtah Rionutukei claimed the women's title.
After finishing second in this year's Family Bank half marathon in Eldoret, Tiony took home Sh150,000 in prize in 1:02.15, with Kiprugut Too (1:02.16) and Josphat Mutai (1:02.19) settling for second and third positions respectively.
"I'm so happy to have won the race. It was quite warm compared to the other times, but I managed to keep pace with the other runners to the end," an elated Tiony said.
Attracting about 14,000 participants, the annual event boasted a scenic race route, numerous street-side festivities and an exciting carnival for the young and old.
"I have been running in every edition of this marathon and I'm glad I finished yet again, despite my old age," said 69-year-old Ragot Omondi, after completing the 21-kilometre race.
Despite a number of entertaining sideshows from spectators - who had decided to 'do' the marathon along the streets - it was the run that formed the centrepiece of the eighth edition of the race.
Those who chose the more leisurely 5km route were not left out either as the youngsters and their not so athletic guardians tried outdo each other.
"Running with my friends from school was just too exciting. We enjoyed although I feel too tired. I will train for the 'big' race next time," said 10-year old Yasmin Omar.

Original_Search_Title: All Ankles Shook At Milege Concert
Headline: Uganda: All Ankles Shook At Milege Concert
Body_Text:
Kampala — One of the Milege Band guitarists in a hearty performance at the group's inaugural concert
Watching Milege play was like listening to a soothing serenade in the middle of a dripping night on Christmas Eve. It was last Friday night at the Kampala Serena Hotel as the jazz band treated its fans to their first public concert - the Afro-Jazz Concert.
Victoria Hall was full, which perhaps goes to show that this genre, which has been a rather abstruse one to many an average Ugandan, has finally registered - thanks to the corporate well-heeled.
Though new, Milege Band is a force to reckon with as far as Jazz and tranquil sounds are concerned. By the way, Milege means an ankle shaker in Luo. And these gifted artistes really used their name well to get our ankles shaking all through the show.
For about two hours, they delivered live mesmeric sounds that got revelers to their feet.
Backed up by the Uganda Heritage Roots, a team of dancers so well-versed with cultural dance and acrobatic gigs, the Milege show produced an amalgam of crazy entertainment (crazy in a good way).
The crowd, which constituted mostly whites, cheered on as the band belted out the crowd's favourite song, Nankasa, a traditional one from central Uganda, featuring gorgeous women on stage twisting their waists like they were on an electric current. You would think had heard President Museveni's hit single You want Another Rap?
Agwara was also another cultural sound from West Nile among the Lugbara, Kakwa, Alur and Madi. The song that talks about a lazy girl who enjoys eating than working got the 'adungus' sounding in unison to a backdrop of acrobatic flips.
Watching the group's two mellifluous vocalists; Vivian Ocen and Gloria Mbabazi, reminded me of musicals like Glee and Spectacular, where singing seems so easy like sipping on a glass of juice on a hot summer holiday in the Bahamas. Guitarists Francis Banana and Paul Owembabazi made me think Nickelback.
The drummers and the two pianists were exceptional. Ironically, the song called Intro was meant to crown off the show at around 10.30pm. But the crowd still 'wanted another rap', which they got in Migration, another sensational piece of Jazz that took us to the end. When Milege releases their album soon, I will be one of the people queuing to grab it un-pirated. It's just as well they are based nearby in Ntinda.

Original_Search_Title: Drama As Arik Airline Hostess Walks Out on Judge
Headline: Nigeria: Drama As Arik Airline Hostess Walks Out on Judge
Body_Text:
Lagos — Arik Airline hostess, Mrs. Adeola Adeosun, nearly caused pandemonium in Alimosho Grade, B Customary Court on Monday as she walked out on the judge in anger.
The light complexioned young woman had insinuated that the court gave her picture to a female journalist who used it to illustrate her story in one of the national dailies not Daily Champion.
She said that she submitted the said picture to the court but was shocked when she saw it published.
Her action enraged the president who ordered a police attached to the court to arrest her for contempt of court.
The petitioner's parents were in court and witnessed the whole scenario. They waded into the matter and pleaded on her behalf for forgiveness.
Earlier, the court had dissolved the marriage between Adeola and her husband, Oyewole Adeosun.
The petitioner had alleged that her husband had formed the habit of beating her at the slightest provocation as well as indulging in gambling and living irresponsibly.
The mother of one, who dragged her estranged husband before the court, also said during the evidence-in-chief that she had to pack out of her matrimonial home two years ago when she observed that the respondent had lost his job.
Although Oyewole informed the court that his wife had on three occasions slapped him, her love was still waxing stronger in his heart, adding that all efforts made by the members of his family towards amicable settlement proved abortive as his wife remained adamant.
With the evidence before the court, the president, Mrs. Abigail Olatunji, said it was clear that the marriage between the duo had broken down irretrievably due to incompatibility.
She, therefore, dissolved the marriage immediately, and advised both parties to approach a Family Court for proper explanation on the custody of the only issue of the marriage (Adelere Adeosun).
Meanwhile, an 18-year-old man has been arraigned before the court for purportedly dumping a dead goat on the express way.
The man, a resident of Ipaja Ayobo, who pleaded guilty to the charge, though informed the court that his goat ate a banana peel suspected to have been poisoned by his neighbors.
He was consequently ordered to pay N5, 000 cash or be remanded at Kirikiri prison for 30 days.

Original_Search_Title: Yanga, Simba Battle for Top Spot
Headline: Tanzania: Yanga, Simba Battle for Top Spot
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam — PREMIER League giants Simba and Young Africans will be praying for each other's slip-up when they take on Kagera Sugar and African Lyon respectively.
There are five matches on card today, with games pitting Toto African vs. Majimaji; Mtibwa Sugar vs. AFC and Polisi Dodoma up against Azam FC; however, the interests of most soccer fans will be on the giants' fixtures.
Defending champions, Simba, moved level on points with Yanga at the top of the table following back to back victories over Mtibwa Sugar and JKT Ruvu.
Meanwhile, Yanga's goalless draws against JKT Ruvu and Azam meant that the Jangwani street outfit squandered the four-point lead.
Thus, after nine rounds of action, the Dar es Salaam giants tie on 21 points which suggests that the Mainland football season again would be another two-horse race between the traditional archrivals.
Yanga face a test of character against African Lyon, and Simba's prayers will be for another slip up for the league leaders, so that they can take advantage to go up on the table. It is the same story for Simba who visit Kagera Sugar at the Kaitaba Stadium.
The Msimbazi Street faithful will keep their fingers crossed that in the end the results in Kagera play on their favour and possibly they reopen gap at the top.
Yanga, currently leading the table courtesy of their goal difference advantage, cannot afford to drop more points as that would obstruct their title ambitions, and expectations will be to get back to winning ways.
Ghanaian midfielder Ernest Boakye and defender Bakari Mbegu will likely miss the encounter at the Jamhuri Stadium, in Morogoro. The duo were expelled from the team's camp earlier this week and have remained in Dar es Salaam.
Boakye has been at the centre of Yanga's achievements this season, dominating the engine room, and has played in all his team's matches expect last Sunday's clash with Azam.
Godfrey Bonny is expected to fill the void as he did against Azam. Simba go into the Kaitaba Stadium vying to maintain their winning form, but this could be one of those banana skin tests for Patrick Phiri's men.
Their hosts are arguably in good form and would be determined to take good results at home. Azam will be on road to face Polisi Dodoma in a match that can make or break their season.
The city tycoons looked capable of challenging the bipolar hegemony of Simba and Yanga before the start of the season thanks largely to a well assembled squad, but after almost half of the 22-match marathon, they trail their rivals by massive seven points.
Itamar Amorim's side needs to win at Polisi' backyard to keep their title dreams alive. Toto African could have failed to seize the home ground advantage after being held to a 1-1 draw by visiting African Lyon last time around, but Choke Abeid would expect that his troops to avoid same mistake when they welcome Majimaji at the CCM Kirumba Stadium in Mwanza on Wednesday.
Mtibwa Sugar bounced back from a 4-0 demolition at home to Azam FC, with a 1-0 win over Ruvu Shooting 1-0 and will be all out to collect maximum points and possibly a handsome victory over lowly AFC to amend for the humiliating home defeat.
AFC's stuttering form continued with a barren draw against Polisi Tanzania at their backyard - Sheikh Amri Abeid Memorial Stadium last Saturday. Another defeat will only pay their way back to the lower division. The Arusha-based outfit remained rooted at the bottom of the table with five points from nine outings.

Original_Search_Title: Laughter is Indeed Good for Your Health
Headline: Nigeria: Laughter is Indeed Good for Your Health
Body_Text:
Kill and dry! Buy your original rat poison. Get rid of rats from your home. Don't share your apartment with rats because they don't pay rent," came the coarse voice of a young man, his wares, various brands of rat poison, hanging from his neck.
"Wonderful things happen in Lagos, no wonder it is nicknamed 'Eko for show,'" commented Felicia, one of the commuters in this 18-seater passenger bus. Another commuter by name Sam asked her to recommend the best rat poison to use for the destructive pests that had virtually taken over his home.
Said Sam seemingly innocently: "Felicia, please which rat poison would you recommend for these troublesome rats in my home. I need the most effective of the lot," whereupon Felicia retorted good-naturedly: "Why did you pick on me? Am I the only person here?
And do I look like a rat poison seller?" This caused ripples of laughter from other commuters.
"I know why I asked you. You are a good businesswoman who deals in any lucrative ware," replied Sam. "Thanks for the vote of confidence," Felicia replied sarcastically.
Said Yinka: "You did well to ask, Sam, because Lagos rats and mice are like no other in the universe.
It was in Lagos that I saw rats as big as rabbits, swimming comfortably. I was flabbergasted. The two big rats jumped inside the overflowing gutter, like professional divers. Initially, I was happy that they won't come out alive but to my astonishment, they not only came out alive, they seemed to have had a good swim!"
"That pales into insignificance when compared to what I witnessed in Ajegunle, Lagos. One evening, I was just strolling when I saw a rat trading words with a cat," said Okey.
At this point, the laughter ceased momentarily and Comfort said: "Haba Okey! Please say something plausible. "This is no joke," Okey maintained. "As Nigerians would say, I saw it with my two naked eyes. I saw the cat first closely watching the rat from some distance away; trying to catch it for dinner. Suddenly, the rat sensing danger, turned its head and saw the cat. Immediately, it stood on its hindfeet and was making some funny noise while gesticulating as if daring the cat to come and catch it."
Said Ali: "It might be true. Animals are becoming wiser. After all, during the just-concluded Commonwealth Games in India, monkeys were used as security agents although stories had it that if you have a banana to spare, you will escape the monkey's ugly hands."
"Security agents indeed! But, what could have given the rat such confidence? asked Joe laughing
"I think the adage that says familiarity breeds contempt is apt here. Perhaps, they have been living together for so long that the rat began to forget that it is a rat but sees itself as a cat," answered Okey.
"That may be correct. I remember some years back in Zaria, a lot of rats had invaded our house because we had a lot of grains in the house so they were really having a field day.
It was like the first settlers went back to where ever they had come from to invite the entire clan," narrated Hassan.
"It got so bad that even in broad daylight, you will see about three to five rats dancing on the dining table while people are sitting close by. My aunt got so angry that she bought a kitten.
As soon as the kitten came in and began to meow in its tiny voice, the rats scurried into hiding, terrified. We thought it was over until the third day, some came out as usual and when the cat began to meow again, they stopped for a second, observed the kitten and then continued to play, ignoring the poor kitten completely because they must have reasoned that they were all of the same size," everyone was roaring with laughter.
Continued Hassan: "A week later, the kitten pounced on one and killed and ate it. From that day, the rats disappeared knowing it was no longer business as usual."
"Even Lagos mosquitoes are of a different make. Fans and air-conditioners scare away normal mosquitoes but Lagos mosquitoes seem to love the cool air. The just perch on the victim and take their sweet time and suck your blood while you sleep soundly," noted Ben.
"Who says laughter is not a good medicine? We've all forgotten we have been in this gridlock for over three hours," Ngozi reminded all.

Original_Search_Title: It's a Carnival As Supporters Make a Statement
Headline: Uganda: It's a Carnival As Supporters Make a Statement
Body_Text:
The capital was caught up in a frenzy of activity as bus and taxi loads of people descended onto the city from near and far to rally around their candidate.
Trumpets and whistles were blown creating a cacophony unlike any other with motorcycle riders whizzing between a seething mass of pedestrian and vehicle traffic as the election season got officially underway on the city streets.
Bystanders were treated to some drama earlier in the day as the convoys of NRM candidate Yoweri Museveni and FDC leader Kizza Besigye met at the Spear Motors junction in Nakawa.
Mr Museveni was returning from the Mandela National Stadium Namboole following nomination, while Dr Besigye was heading there. A war of words erupted between supporters from both camps. The police acted fast, creating an emergency route for Dr Besigye's group so that Mr Museveni's convoy could pass.
Muhoozi appears
First Son, Lt Col. Muhoozi Kainerugaba emerged out of his car and took a glimpse at the action. On seeing him, some Besigye supporters shouted: "Tell your father that we shall get him out of the Entebbe State House."
The candidates soon embarked on an all-out effort to woo voters, addressing post nomination rallies at pre-arranged venues the capital.
A grand spectacle was staged at the Kololo Independence grounds where incumbent Museveni converged with ruling party supporters to launch his campaign for a fourth term in office. They came in droves from around the country, donning yellow t-shirts with portraits of Mr Museveni and the symbolic and much-maligned dry banana leaves (bisanja).
Supporters were treated to free food and drinks as the airstrip was left littered with garbage. Dr Besigye, who is gunning for the presidency at the third attempt as flag bearer of the opposition cooperation, IPC, hosted his supporters, many of them having also arrived from upcountry at the Nakivubo Stadium in downtown Kampala.
Jiggy MPs
There was no seat left vacant in this stadium that seats 25,000 spectators while the entire grass pitch was also filled with people. Besides local musician Ronald Mayinja sending the crowd in frenzy with his Tuli kubukenke (we are on tenterhooks) song, there was more amusement on the menu when MC Ibrahim Ssemujju Nganda asked the 17 Opposition MPs present to dance for the audience.
The sight of the mainly burly MPs doing paka chini left many in the crowd in stitches. DP flagbearer Nobert Mao addressed a rally in Kawempe on the outer reaches north of the city.
Similarly, other first-time contestants Beti Kamya of the Uganda Federal Alliance and Jeberi Bidandi Ssali were introducing themselves to the people.

Original_Search_Title: 14-Seater Matatus On Way Out in City
Headline: Kenya: 14-Seater Matatus On Way Out in City
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The matatu industry in Nairobi is set for major changes with the announcement that the common 14-seaters will be phased out.
Transport minister Amos Kimunya told a meeting of industry players there would be no registration of the 14-seater matatus starting January next year.
Instead, the current route operators will be encouraged to form societies through which they can buy and run bigger buses, which carry upwards of 25 passengers.
Both the Matatu Owners Association (MOA) and the Matatu Welfare Association (MWA) welcomed the decision as being in the right direction but needed the right kind of support from the government.
"We are fully in support of the idea. The ministry has been speaking with us the stakeholders and we think it's the right way to go," said Mr Simon Kimutai, who heads the owners' association, said after the minister's announcement on Tuesday.
Mr Dickson Mbugua, who heads the welfare association, was more cautious in his support of the move, which has been in the pipeline for some time.
"It is a good programme, but it needs the proper support from the government. There has to be time to transform," he said.
The move, according to Mr Mbugua, would need to be complemented by "soft credit facilities" driven by the government to enable both the societies and individuals to access cheap loans to purchase the bigger vehicles.
But matatu crews who spoke to the Nation at the Khoja Mosque roundabout said they would suffer the most as they would lose jobs once the small matatus were eventually removed from the city centre.
"Two months is a very short notice. Some of the matatus on the routes are new and the owners are yet to pay back the loans they took to buy them," said Mr Joseph Njihia, a matatu driver. It costs an average Sh1.2 million to buy an imported used van and it normally took between 18 and 24 months for the owner to recoup the amount.
Other matatu crews said they would eventually lose their jobs since the buses would need less manpower and carry more passengers than the matatus.
City routes are considered the most lucrative because they are short, most of the roads are good and the morning and evening rush hours guarantee good returns.
The government's move is line with the Integrated National Transport Policy, which outlines the intention to eventually remove the 14-seater matatus from the city centre.
The policy has been approved by the Cabinet but is yet to be taken to Parliament for approval setting the stage for full implementation by the Transport ministry.
The Matatu Welfare Association estimates that there are 15,000 matatus on about 50 routes in Nairobi, and about 80 per cent of them -- 12,000 -- are the ubiquitous 14-seaters.
Overall, there are about 80,000 registered Public Service Vehicles in Kenya, with 60 per cent of these -- 48,000 -- operating in urban centres. Mr Mbugua said it would take about six years for the 14-seaters to naturally phase themselves out of Nairobi.
The routes that are likely to suffer once the programme is implemented are Kikuyu, Kawangware, Lang'ata, Madaraka, South B and C, Mwiki, Highridge, Githurai 44, Dandora, Baba Dogo, Westlands, Kiambu, Banana, Industrial Area and Embakasi.

Original_Search_Title: Okereke-Onyiuke and Politics of Stock Market
Headline: Nigeria: Okereke-Onyiuke and Politics of Stock Market
Body_Text:
The activities coming from the Nigerian Stock Exchange in the last two months is everything but a disgrace to all of us. More so, shortly after the global economic recession, capital markets of other nations are picking up, but the Nigerian capital market operators decided to toy with the lives of millions of our citizens whose investments are tied to the stock exchange.
It still baffles the writer just like it did to other Nigerians and stakeholders in the Nigerian Stock Exchange, how over 150 mobile policemen, armed to the teeth, invaded the headquarters of the Nigerian Stock Exchange only to be followed shortly with an announcement on the Channel Television of the sacking of the director-general of the exchange.
Gestapo-style, armed policemen barricaded the entire premises and subjected staff to embarrassment before they were allowed entry into the building. It becomes more of a puzzle without a riddle considering the fact that the director-general had earlier in January this year given notice of her retirement after 35 years of service to the nation. The question here is, what is it that the Security and Exchange Commission (SEC), the supervisory agency, discover about the DG of the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE) now that they did not find out all these years?
That the director-general of the NSE has only less than two weeks before proceeding on her terminal leave only for her to be sacked makes it more sinister and indeed questionable. Apart from this, no tangible reason was adduced for the sack; neither was the woman given the opportunity to pack her personal belongings from the office she had occupied for several years. Rather, her office was thrown open by security operatives who ransacked and turn things upside down while searching for only God knows what.
We are not just in a democratic dispensation, but under a government that claims to believe in the rule of law. Even a condemned criminal or any other prisoner has the right to be present while his/her office or residence is being searched and that is the practice all over the world. Despite all these, SEC has not come up with any charges or indictment against the erstwhile director-general except that it appointed a replacement.
The whole incident as far as Nigerians are concerned, SEC is like the proverbial bird dancing on the road while the drum beaters are in the bush playing the music. SEC is just playing out the script written out elsewhere. There are so many questions that need answers, at least, to calm down Nigerians who have invested heavily on the stock market, especially now that it is recovering from a recession. Could the sack of the DG of NSE be a fallout of powerplay with the lingering court cases of its president with AP shareholders? Was the DG of NSE position also nullified by the court alongside that of the president of the Nigerian Stock Exchange? What role did the supervisory agency, SEC, play to avert or manage the crisis rocking the NSE? Who actually deployed the armed mobile policemen to the buildings of the NSE and with whose permission?
Both the authorities and the Lagos Police Command and the Force Headquarters in Abuja have all denied knowledge of the deployment. All right, since no one claimed to be responsible for the deployment of the armed policemen to NSE premises, why are they still there up util this moment? Could the sack of the director-general of NSE be linked to the alleged illegal sharing of N1.2billion by the council members? If yes, what punitive measures have been meted to other council members or is the former DG the only scapegoat? The questions are too many and will continue to come without answers to it. It might be pertinent to state here that the Nigerian Stock Exchange clocked 50 years a week before Nigeria's Independence Day anniversary and this should have been well celebrated, instead what Nigerians and stakeholders got was crises galore in return.
What a shame! In as much as the writer does not personally know the erstwhile director-general of the stock exchange or holding forte for her, the treatment meted to her is uncalled for, considering the role she played in taking the stock exchange to the enviable position it is occupying today. Before now, many Nigerians did not know anything about stock market or shares but through her tireless efforts, the stock market came to limelight. Through her hardwork, many Nigerians are rich and better off today, including her present persecutors and detractors.
Our leaders and policy makers at all levels should rise above pedestrian issues and begin to reward hardwork and honesty, especially to those who served this country selflessly. This will encourage and boost the morale of others to do better. Prof. Okereke-Onyiuke is one of the patriotic citizens of this country, having served under many presidents of this country, starting from the days of Gen. Sani Abacha up to the present democratic government of Dr. Goodluck Jonathan. She deserves to retire peacefully and honourably with celebrations; otherwise let her persecutors come up their charges now or keep silent forever. This is a democratic Nigeria, not a banana republic.
Dr. Pizzarow Onyewuchi is the coordinator of Vanguards for Democracy, Fairness and Equity

Original_Search_Title: There's This Country, Then the Other, And Another
Headline: Uganda: There's This Country, Then the Other, And Another
Body_Text:
Last week over 6,000 Ugandans flooded Nairobi to watch The Cranes play Kenya's national side, Harambee Stars, in a 2012 Nations Cup qualifier.
That is the largest number of Ugandans to cross into Kenya for any such event in the record books. But there is something else. Just five years ago, perhaps only 1,000 would have followed The Cranes to any match outside Uganda.
To explain this newfound love for things Ugandan, it is worth noting that it has been possible partly because Ugandans are doing well in the world. Though football administration in Uganda remains in a mess, this year The Cranes have overcome several odds to shine.
As I write this, at the Commonwealth Games, Uganda has bagged two gold medals. That places us 16th out of 36. However, we are the fourth highest ranked African country, after South Africa, Nigeria, and Kenya. You might sneer at the list of countries in the Games, but truth is that there were times when we used to hug the bottom of that list.
Now if we go back 15 years, the story would have been totally different. verything about Uganda was about President Yoweri Museveni, his membership of the "new breed" of African leaders, the HIV-Aids story, and the economic turn-around. Today pretty much the same characters are in the news, but they are not reformists. They are power-hungry constitution-breaking kleptocrats, brutal task masters with illegal militias that beat down protestors; election thieves who have done the impossible - have made the expression "mega corruption" meaningless.
We have noted this trend before; the rise of an alternative society and a narrative that is not hostage to the state and its bankrupt politics. But in the last 10 years, this alternative universe has grown sharply.
There was a lot of interesting theatre, and a few academic and scholarly works, but let us stay with the creative arts. There was the world-acclaimed The Abyssinian Chronicles by Moses Isegawa. However, it would be opportunistic to claim Isegawa as ours. He is too much of a global citizen for that.
The first lively and major effort outside the feminism-fuelled Femrite School of literature was Doreen Baingana's Tropical Fish: Stories Out of Entebbe, which won The Commonwealth Prize for the Best First Book in the Africa region, in addition to a host of other gongs. Then in 2007 Monica Arac de Nyeko's intriguing little book Jambula Tree won the Caine Prize.
In June, three Ugandans did it in style, sweeping the three top BBC awards in African Performance Play Writing Competition, bundling out Nigerians for whom the prize had almost become a birthright.
Deborah Asiimwe's Will Smith Look Alike was the overall winner.? Kenneth Atwine's Kitu Kidogo tied for second with Julia Childs' The Coffin Factory.
Then Angella Emurwon booked third place with The Cow Needs A Wife. What all these things make possible, is that now you can write a decent book on Ugandan society and not have to give space to its politicians and politics to complete the story.
Equally delightful is that as state politics becomes a criminal enterprise and rots away, some things remain pure and give one a pure sense of place. Recently I had the chance to listen to Christ the King Choir. We are all getting on, so a few of them have put on the additional kilo here and there and show a few wrinkles under the eyes, but their voices are still heavenly. No, they have actually gotten better. After all these years, that was beautiful to hear.
I am always impatient for the trip to Tororo when I get back. There are two mandatory stops. The first is Namawojolo for roasted Gonja (sweet banana). I often buy more than I eventually eat. But eating the Wojolo Gonja is just part of it. The other ritual is just smelling its sweetness.
The second stop is this place near Kibimba (I keep forgetting its name, however many times my brother reminds me). Anyhow, the place is famous for its roast maize. Now, I have eaten roasted maize in America, all over Europe and Africa, and I swear that place has the sweetest maize on this fair planet.
And there are other things that are simply too majestic to be grabbed and banked in a foreign bank account by the kleptocrats - the River Nile, and Lake Victoria. I think if we didn't have them, it would have been possible to return to Uganda after a long absence and struggle to find familiar landmarks.
After all, the story is told that in parts of the north, for example, ancient trees were cut down, centuries old anthills cleared away, and graves removed to disorient the people who had fled their lands for the internally displaced peoples' camps at the height of the war between Joseph Kony's Lord's Resistance Army rebels and the UPDF, when they returned home.
Now on October 9, the 48th anniversary of our independence, President Yoweri Museveni showed rare magnanimity. He honoured former president and archrival Milton Obote for his contribution to Uganda's independence. Still, it was notable that he could only work with something from Uganda's past. The promise of Uganda's future is now possibly well out of his reach.

Original_Search_Title: Kist Scoops Prize in Italy
Headline: Rwanda: Kist Scoops Prize in Italy
Body_Text:
Kigali — Kigali Institute of Science and Technology (KIST), last week, received an award dubbed "First Forward Prize" in recognition to the institute's efforts to promote Rwanda's architectural designs using local resources.
The award was a result of a competition held at the University of Architecture of Venice (IUAV) in Italy, which had attracted many universities from all over the world.
KIST won the prestigious prize after it entered into a partnership with the Italian university last year, and the two institutions are collaborating in conducting joint research and project development on the use of local and available materials.
"We had the best exhibition for local materials for architectural design and we were recognized as the best university in promoting this," said Prof. Abraham Atta Ogwu, the Rector of KIST, said in an interview with The New Times.
"We showcased the construction of prototype building system with 80% natural materials such as banana stems, bamboo and coffee wood," he said.
Ogwu stressed that Rwanda has a lot of local materials such as clay soil, banana fibre and bamboo which are wasted and can be used for construction, which he said can even go a long way in reducing the cost of construction.
However, Ogwu said the country still lacks skilled architects and the newly established faculty of architecture and design has only 61 students.

Original_Search_Title: Republican Party Disbands
Headline: Namibia: Republican Party Disbands
Body_Text:
BY next Friday, the Republican Party (RP) will be something of the past.
Henk Mudge, RP president, announced over the weekend that he would resign from the National Assembly on October 14, and the RP would be de-registered the next day.
Mudge said on Saturday evening at a dinner hosted in Windhoek that he hoped all RP members would throw their weight behind the Rally for Democracy and Progress (RDP).
Although it was originally expected that the long-awaited merger between the two opposition parties would only materialise within the next year or two, Mudge said the upcoming local and regional government elections necessitated a faster decision.
According to him, an earlier decision to have RP candidates standing for the RDP in the November elections would have brought about confusion.
As a result, Mudge said, he proposed at the RP extraordinary congress on Saturday that the party be dissolved.
He said: "I can tell you it wasn't [an] easy [decision]. Fortunately, we look forward to a bigger challenge."
For his part, RDP president Hidipo Hamutenya said the table was set for an opportunity to forge a strong political counterforce to Swapo.
"Namibia deserves a better alternative. We cannot continue this rule of Swapo. Much is rotten in the house of Namibia because of the political arrogance of Swapo."
Referring to the pending election court challenge, Hamutenya claimed they had a watertight case against Swapo. He charged: "We have discovered multiple types of ballot boxes. We will fight our adversary. We are holding them at ransom."
Hamutenya further alleged that the ruling party "makes sure they misuse public resources to sustain themselves in power and is stifling the media".
Mudge, during his address, also accused Swapo of having forgotten about the plight of "the grassroots level".
He said after visiting especially remote villages he gets "so upset that I feel I want to drive back to Windhoek and want to grab the President [Hifikepunye Pohamba] by the neck to show him what is happening to the grassroots level people."
Mudge said Namibians "want a unified opposition that can challenge Swapo and we decided we will set the example".
According to him, Swapo got away with election rigging in the past because they knew that political parties usually has little money left after an election and could therefore not challenge the results in court.
"This time we are not going to lose and let them get away with it. I am quite sure that we will have a re-election. We must. This is not a banana republic. We will take them to court for everything they do wrong."

Original_Search_Title: Baby Stolen From Fort Portal Hospital
Headline: Uganda: Baby Stolen From Fort Portal Hospital
Body_Text:
Kampala — A three-weeks-old baby girl was stolen from its mother in Fort Portal Hospital. The incident happened hardly a month after a day-old baby was stolen from its mother at the same hospital. But unlike the first incident where the culprit is yet to be found, the police in Fort Portal have arrested a 21-year-old woman for allegedly stealing Mr Thomas and Medias Sangano's baby.
Ms Sangano was still recovering from a caesarian operation. The suspect, who was admitted on Thursday to the same hospital with stomach complications, was arrested on Saturday while travelling with the baby to Bugangali Sub-county in Rukungiri District.
According to the police, the suspect allegedly offered Ms Sangano money to buy bananas from a nearby canteen. She later boarded a motorcycle and disappeared with the baby at around 8pm on Friday. The parents of the missing baby reported the matter to police who tracked and arrested the suspect.
Confessions
According to the police, upon interrogation, the suspect confessed that she had stolen the baby because she was taking it to her husband. The Kabarole deputy officer in-charge of criminal investigations, Mr Gideon Kabau, said the suspect will be charged with child theft.
Three weeks ago, Ms Rosette Komuhangi, a resident of Nyabitusa village Busingye parish in Nkoma Sub-county Kamwenge district gave birth at Fort Portal hospital but her baby was allegedly stolen by a lady who posed as her attendant.
The mother of the stolen child said she would sue the hospital on grounds that the nurses were responsible for the missing baby because it was stolen when she was still unconscious.

Original_Search_Title: The Home of Cocoa has Very Little Chocolate
Headline: Ghana: The Home of Cocoa has Very Little Chocolate
Body_Text:
There are few countries in Africa where one finds the most famous local produce or mineral readily available or affordable.
In South Africa, you don't get to see gold easily. In Kenya, very few people can afford a cup of quality coffee; in fact coffee houses are exclusive and the consolation for most people is tea.
In Sierra Leone, famed for its high quality diamonds, most citizens only see the precious stone in pictures.
Ethiopia and Uganda are the few exceptions. In Ethiopia, home-roasted coffee is a way of life as is matoke (bananas) in Uganda.
It is therefore not a surprise that in Ghana's capital Accra, a country known for cocoa, getting a cheaper and better quality chocolate is almost impossible.
There are more chocolates on supermarket shelves in Nairobi than there are in Accra.
Though a coastal city with a climate comparable to Mombasa, Accra can only be described as humble.
Starting from the small and unattractive Kotokoto Airport - the hustle and bustle associated with airport taxis in most African cities hardly exists.
Accra is far from a modern city, but it is orderly.
The public transport system is a mixture of old rickety vehicles - similar to the Fords that dominated Nairobi's Industrial Area route in the 1970s - 80s - saloon taxis and modern buses used under the city's Rapid Transport System.
But Ghana's cedi currency is strong, exchanging at 1.4 to the US dollar. In Kenya, it goes for Sh56 at current rates.
In 2007, Ghana devalued its unit by 100, leading to a confusion that still lingers.
It is not strange to ask for a product's price and be told it goes for 10,000 cedi, yet it costs 100.
Speaking of money, if one wants tidy returns from the money market, Ghana is the place to invest in now.
Interest rates for one month fixed deposits is nine per cent and goes up to 14 per cent for three month fixed deposit.
Ghana is receiving its first payment for oil this month, which is exciting the people and government.
Debate now revolves around the new wealth and how the proceeds will be put to use.
One of the suggestions is to channel the money into funding the National Health Insurance Scheme (NHIS), which is similar to Kenya's National Health Insurance Fund (NHIF).
The oil revenue is expected to hit Sh80 billion every year.
There are concerns that the US has warmed up to Ghana to get priority access to oil.
President Barack Obama has visited Ghana and the country receives funding under the Millennium Challenge Account, a US scheme to reward Third World countries that embrace democratic governance and the rule of law.
Ghana has formed the Millennium Development Authority, an agency that implements projects financed by the kitty.
Just like many countries in Africa, Ghana has recovered from a turbulent past dominated by coups between the 1960s to 80s.
Penalty headache
On a light note, Ghana is yet to recover from the World Cup penalty miss by famed striker Asamoah Gyan at the extravaganza in South Africa.
If he scored in the extra time penalty against Uruguay, Ghana would have been the first African country to reach the semis in the history of the tournament.
"On that day, people moaned across the country. The city was deserted for two days. You would think it was the days of the military coups when people stayed indoors," said David Abugri, a retired government driver who now operates a car hire business in the city said.

Original_Search_Title: Indians Celebrate Traditional Onam Day
Headline: Uganda: Indians Celebrate Traditional Onam Day
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Indian Malayalees community from Kerala state in India celebrated the traditional Onam day in Jinja on Saturday. The day, which is celebrated annually, is in memory of King Mahabali, their former state and religious leader.
The celebrations were attended by the internal affairs minister, Kirunda Kivejinja, and over 200 Indian families in Uganda at Gymkhana Club in Jinja. The function started with the lighting of a traditional lamp called Nilavilakhe amid chants and songs.
Indian traditional dances and serving of the 25 traditional dishes on banana leaves followed. The dishes included rice, Thoran, Avial, Upperi, Sharkarapureti and various types of Payassam (sweet dishes).
The event organising committee chairperson, Chacko Babu, who is also the chairman of World Malayalee Council in Africa, appealed to the members to fight corruption and betrayal, things he said, Mahabali fought during his reign.
Kivejinja urged the Malayalees community to work closely with other Indian communities in Jinja in partnership with Ugandans to foster development and to help the needy.
He commended the Indian community for upholding their culture as exhibited during the function. The group's treasurer, Ajith Prasad, commended the Ugandan Government under President Yoweri Museveni for recalling Asians who had been chased away in 1972 by the late President Idi Amin.

Original_Search_Title: Coups And Assassins Are Again Knocking On the Door
Headline: Uganda: Coups And Assassins Are Again Knocking On the Door
Body_Text:
When it was reported in January that the American Congress had directed the Obama Administration to 'tightly' watch Uganda's preparation for the 2011 elections, there was a lot of excitement among the opposition.
US Secretary of State Hillary Clinton, was required to keep the lawmakers up to speed on the electoral process starting in March, detailing a closer scrutiny of candidates' security during campaigns, independence of the Electoral Commission, the hygiene of the voter register, media freedom and citizens' right to political assembly etc.
Mr Olara Otunnu, a presidential candidate on the Uganda Peoples Congress ticket, while appearing on Straight Talk Africa, described the directive as a "milestone because for the first time the Museveni regime is being held to the same electoral standards as other governments." He added: "...up until now, the regime has enjoyed scandalous exceptionalism, particularly concerning democracy, human rights and corruption. This is the beginning of the end of that impunity."
It appeared like an audacious follow-up to the statement in Obama's inaugural speech in which he promised to extend the frontiers of freedom. Similarly, in a speech delivered in Ghana last year, Obama identified promotion of democracy as one of the key pillars of Washington's renewed engagement with Africa.
One cannot tell what Otunnu felt when the same Clinton praised President Museveni recently amidst 'concerns' about the independence and impartiality of the Electoral Commission, freedom of assembly and the media, etc. What is obvious is that America's mode of dealing with 'troublesome democracies' has not changed much. There will always be general statements that sound sweet and a few actions like withholding aid or travel bans that look good. But practically, the weight of matters concerning regime change lie with those that bear the load of a dictatorial regime - the citizens.
With the increasing electoral pressures that the Obama administration is facing at home; 'small matters' like the democracy of banana republics will continuously be relegated to the status of 'any other business' on the agenda. African countries like Uganda will be left to their own devices when it comes to determining who leads them -as the case should be.
Trouble is that the conventional way through the ballot is becoming increasingly irrelevant. Elections are now an expensive shambles intended to keep those in power where they are, a position that they misuse to abuse that power to disempower citizens. Despite the fact that economies are growing at an average of five per cent for the entire continent, the gap between the rich and poor is widening.
Since the leaders can cheat their way to leadership, they are not accountable to anyone. They are therefore under no obligation to deliver services. Gradually, when the remnants of pretences in the electoral process as a method for appointing and changing regimes fizzle out, we shall revert to the bad old ways.
Already, Mauritania (August 2005), Madagascar (March 2009), Central African Republic, Guinea Bissau, Principe and Sao Tome, Ivory Coast have, in the post-'wind of change' era, demonstrated a willingness to change government by force of arms, confounding those who claimed that Coup d'etat were 'a thing of the past.'
It would not be wishful thinking to assert that now more than ever, assassins and coup plotters are warming up. For if they ever swung into action, they will find the open arms of discontented and frustrated people who will view them as an alternative to the tragic comedy that dictators have made out of electoral democracy.
Mr Sengoba is a commentator on political and social issues

Original_Search_Title: KCC, Reform Garbage Management
Headline: Uganda: KCC, Reform Garbage Management
Body_Text:
Kampala — The revelation that the Kiteezi garbage landfill is slowly poisoning the population with lead and stifling children's brains is alarming. The surrounding communities have always complained of the smell from the site and the fact that spring water in the area has become unpalatable.
This new research finding shows that their plight is worse than they had imagined. The mess could have been avoided by sticking to the basic good practices in municipal garbage management.
More than three-quarters of the garbage in Kampala is organic material that can be converted into manure or used for generating electricity. The problem is there is no garbage separation system. Banana peelings mixed with polythene is no good for manure, and it would be terribly expensive to separate them at the landfill.
KCC should therefore implement a garbage collection system that ensures separation of organic from non-organic waste at household level. If this approach is implemented, KCC will have much less garbage to deal with at Kiteezi. Their garbage collection efficiency would also improve, unlike the present situation where KCC is able to collect only 40% of the 1,500 tonnes of waste generated in the city.
One of the main problems with KCC, however, is that they run a garbage management system without the involvement of the people who generate garbage. KCC should embark on a sensitisation and mobilisation campaign on garbage separation and utilization.

Original_Search_Title: Odemwingie Targets Europe With West Brom
Headline: Nigeria: Odemwingie Targets Europe With West Brom
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ambitious West Brom striker Peter Osaze Odemwingie has urged the Baggies to target a spot in Europe after their memorable derby victory over Birmingham. Odemwingie, who grabbed his second Albion goal in Saturday's 3-1 win, wants the club to aim high after he helped inspire a comeback win against Blues to move Albion into the top half of the early Premier League table.
While admitting a place in the Europa League is a long shot for a newly-promoted club, the Nigeria international insisted his own experience in helping Lille qualify for Europe proves the value of setting ambitious targets.
He said: "There is strength in the squad and we can definitely look forward to staying in the Premier League.
"We emphasise playing good football and I think that will attract more supporters to the club.
"The ambition is definitely to stay in the Premier League, although I haven't heard anyone talking about us making the Europa League.
"I haven't heard anyone say that, but inside me I will keep the hope. When I went to Lille they weren't expecting anything, but we ended up finishing second.
"So why not? If things go well we can end up finishing in the top seven."
The Â£2million signing scored to put Albion 2-1 up on Saturday, after applying the pressure that forced Scott Dann's own goal to draw the Baggies level.
And the 29-year-old also walked away with the sponsors' Man of the Match champagne, following an eye-catching all-round display.
It came on the day Baggies fans unfurled an anti-racist banner in support of the striker, whose departure from Lokomotiv Moscow brought an apparently racist message from supporters of the Russian club.
Odemwingie made a point of celebrating with supporters at the Birmingham Road End - the location of the banner - at the final whistle.
He said: "I went to thank the fans for the effort they put in to encourage me, to get behind me and to make me feel at home.
"They gave me a lot of support and got behind me, so I want to thank them. It's a big thing for me to be here, because everything is nice and positive. I think it's a blessing for me to be here. I don't doubt that this place is right for me.
"All those little doubts that you sometimes have are gone."
â-‚Odemwingie's treatment at the hands of Russian racists during his time with Lokomotiv Moscow was a driving force behind his decision to seek sanctuary with West Brom.
The sick banners depicting bananas and the words "Thank You WBA" paraded by his old fans, following his Â£1.5million move to The Hawthorns in August, merely confirmed he made the right choice.
The retaliatory banner, proclaiming "Thanks Lokomotiv", which is tucked away in the corner of the Birmingham Road End of The Hawthorns, was flying highest after Nigerian international Odemwingie inspired Albion to their first win over the Blues in five years.
The dark days of abuse in the shadow of the Kremlin seem a million miles away for a striker who is quickly becoming the driving force behind Roberto Di Matteo's bid to stop the yo-yo existence of the last eight years at the club.
"To be honest, I didn't want it to be an issue because there were many sides to the story," he said. "It wasn't just racism, although there was that. Lokomotiv are going through a crisis as a club and they tried for a while to blame it on me because Nigeria qualified for the World Cup and Russia didn't.
â-‚Meanwhile Odemwingieâ-‚ has been warned by Albion boss Roberto Di Matteo he must maintain his form throughout the Premier League season.
The head coach has seen his Â£2million signing score twice in four games to help the club clinch two Hawthorns victories. But Di Matteo is refusing to get carried away with the 29-year-old's performances.
He said: "Peter has had a great impact but let's wait and see. It is a long season. Let us hope he can sustain it. "Apart from the fact he scored on Saturday, I am pleased about the fact he is a good professional.
"He trains well, is a happy chap around the dressing room and training ground, and he works very hard for the team.
"He is an easy character. He has always got a smile on his face and gets on with the majority of the team. He is happy to play and train. He is a good personality.
"There is something nice and positive about him."
Di Matteo saw Albion fans show their support for Odemwingie with an anti-racist banner on Saturday following a message displayed by fans of his former club, Lokomotiv Moscow.
The Baggies boss said: "He wants to brush it under the carpet as soon as possible.
"He appreciated what the fans did but he wants to get on with his football."

Original_Search_Title: Genocide Survivor Accounts the Horror He Went Through
Headline: Rwanda: Genocide Survivor Accounts the Horror He Went Through
Body_Text:
Arusha — A survivor of massacres at Nyange Parish in Kivumu Commune, Kibuye prefecture (Western Rwanda), claimed Monday that Tutsis who sought refuge at the church lost hopes of living when their Mayor Grégoire Ndahimana, refused to protect them from attacks launched by Hutus.
"We sought refuge at the church believing the building is protected by God and we will be saved. Hutus started attacking us. We complained to Ndahimana when he visited the church on April 14, 1994, but he replied that since Inyenzis (Tutsis) have started the war, Hutus will respond," the survivor, who is fourth prosecution witness, said.
Led by trial attorney Segun Jegede, a Nigerian, the witness code named CBN to protect his identity for security reasons was testifying before the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR) when he claimed further that they were very much worried of their life following remarks given by Ndahimana.
"We were afraid. We understood the government had abandoned us. We were disheartened," the witness told Trial Chamber III presided over by Cameroon Judge Florence Rita Arrey.
According to him, when the attacks intensified on April 15, 1994 he decided to camouflage himself by putting on banana leaves on his clothes like attackers and dodged through them to a nearby bush towards Gitarama prefecture (Central Rwanda), leaving behind his family members, who were all killed at the church.
The trial continues Tuesday. Ndahimana is charged with genocide or complicity in genocide, in the alternative and extermination as crimes against humanity. It is alleged that Ndahimana was responsible for killing or causing serious bodily or mental harm to members of Tutsi population between April 6 and 20, 1994.

Original_Search_Title: YABATEH Students Win U.S.$90,000 World Bank Project
Headline: Nigeria: YABATEH Students Win U.S.$90,000 World Bank Project
Body_Text:
Lagos — Twenty students of Yaba College of Technology have won the sum of US$90,000 from the World Bank STEP B Project under the Innovators of Tomorrow Awards (IOT) project.
The awardees, who are final year students of the college, emerged from the departments of Computer Science, Electrical/ Electronics Engineering, Food Technology, Estate Management, Industrial Management and Mechanical Engineering.
According to the release by the institution, the money won after competing with other institutions in Nigeria is to be expended on their final year projects.
Some of the projects to be carried out are production of edible ant tumor soup thickener using soil, sawdust, paper, waste, banana leaves, corncobs and logs of wood, utilisation of sawdust and corncob for the production of edible protein among others.
The awardees were given a special training on capacity building by the World Bank on the 15th of September, 2010 in Abuja .
Meanwhile, the college will be holding an international research conference and exhibition on sustainable utilization of the environment for greater productivity.
The event, being organized through the college Biological Science Department in the School of Science, has invited foreign experts to discuss the theme of the conference cutting across universities, polytechnics and corporate bodies of both public and private sectors.
The conference slated for 5th to 6th of October, 2010, has among other discussants, Dr. L.O. Chukwu, an associate professor/head of department, Marine Science, University of Lagos, Dr. Chuma Okoro of the department of Biological Science and Biotechnology, Caleb University and Mr. Uche Agbanusi, the National President, Nigeria Environmental Society.

Original_Search_Title: ISO Retirees Cry Out for Benefits After Ten Years
Headline: Uganda: ISO Retirees Cry Out for Benefits After Ten Years
Body_Text:
"This is very unfair," laments Mr Isaac Mulumba, a former Internal Security Organisation operative who was retired on March 31, 2003 with 62 other officers. "ISO has maliciously, willfully, deliberately kept submitting the wrong figures so that we suffer. I don't know what they have against us."
The retired operatives have sought redress from different authorities for what they describe as failure to pay their full terminal benefits upon retirement to no avail so far.
They have written to President Yoweri Museveni with little success. Then they took the government to the High Court and won in 2004. But even that has not yielded the results that they had hoped for yet. They do not know who to turn to now.
Documents obtained by Sunday Monitor show that the operatives received communication from their Director of Resource Management on March 21, 2003 that they would be retired by the end of that month.
Not formally retired?
However, according to a letter written by the group to President Museveni and dated August 19, 2003, they were not formally retired by the end of April and neither ex-gratia nor transport allowance was paid to them as provided for in the organisations (terms and conditions of service) regulations of 2000.
"On the 12 May, 2003, we were summoned by Mr Fred Kyakulaga (Deputy Director Resource Management) who gave us cheques ranging from Shs2 million to Shs5 million and he verbally retired us. There was no formal communication retiring us or explaining the payment and how it was computed. The majority of us were subsequently scrapped from the payroll and a few were retained under unclear circumstances," said the letter, signed by the group's chairman, Mr Fred Wairugala.
Arguing that they feel aggrieved because they "were just thrown out on the streets as if we are criminals," the group wrote to the Minister of State for Security on May 14, 2003, and the ISO Director General on June 10, 2003.
The ISO Director General then responded to each of them individually retiring them formally but, according to the letter, did not mention their entitlements. It was at that point that the former operatives decided to sue the government.
In a case filed before High Court Justice Yorokamu Bamwine, the two parties agreed to an out of court settlement that the retired ISO operatives would be paid their entitlements totalling Shs1.2 billion.
Subsequently, the Solicitor General wrote to the Permanent Secretary in the Office of the President. The September 22, 2004 letter, which was signed by Mr Joseph Matsiko on behalf of the Solicitor General, noted: "We entered into tripartite negotiations with the plaintiffs and the Internal Security Organisation. The outcome of those negotiations was that the Internal Security Organisation conceded that they indeed owed the plaintiffs a total of Shs1,174,080,574."
"This settlement was recorded in Court and to-date the Internal Security Organisation has paid a sum of only Shs86,555,954. The plaintiffs have filed an application for the order of mandamus seeking immediate payment," wrote Mr Matsiko in the two-page letter copied to the Secretary to the Treasury. "The purpose of this letter is to urge you to effect the payment of the balance of Shs1,029,581,609 as a matter of priority to avoid a Mandamus Order."
The Director of Logistics and Finance in ISO, Lt. Frank Banana, who was in charge of the Human Resource Directorate when the 63 officers were retired, declined to comment on the issue, saying, "Those are matters which are in courts which I am not competent to comment about."
Shy to comment
When he was reminded that the matter was no longer before court after the two parties reached a resolution, Lt. Banana said, "Don't take me into details." He added that only Security Minister, Amama Mbabazi, who is currently at the week-long delegates' conference of the NRM party, where he is also Secretary General, can offer any further comment on the matter.
As the 63 retirees wait for their fate with uncertainty, Mr Mulumba claims some officials within the government are stalling the payment process on purpose. He said: "We are being cheated by more than two thirds of what is due to us. They are refusing to pay us by impunity. We said we cannot accept that because it is not just. All those are delaying tactics so that they wait until we die."

Original_Search_Title: Who Qualifies to Be a National Hero?
Headline: Zimbabwe: Who Qualifies to Be a National Hero?
Body_Text:
Harare — YES, the effective impetus for dismantling the structures of white-ruled Rhodesia and achieving genuine liberation in the country came principally from the intensification of the armed struggle.
But this is no reason to imprison the discussion and criteria used to select our national heroes within the narrow framework of guerilla activity. Neither should disagreements between individuals and political parties and within political parties over the direction in which the country is moving come into the equation.
Zimbabwe is much bigger than individuals and political parties. Independence came about as a result of sacrifices by Zimbabweans regardless of race, tribe and political party affiliation. The picture is indeed a complex one. And this fact should be in the forefront of our minds as we endeavour to debate and discuss the whole issue of who qualifies or does not for national hero status.
For starters, the armed struggle was a logical culmination of various forms of resistance that had gone on before to the ruling white minority in the then Rhodesia.
There has been a great diversity of responses and resistances to colonial experience in this country since 1890 and even before that. This immense diversity of African responses to colonial domination and subsequent contributions to the development of post independent Zimbabwe needs to be taken into account when we debate who qualifies to lie at the National Heroes acre.
There has indeed been resistance by the people of Zimbabwe throughout the history of the country. The only difference being that it took different forms, strategies and tactics depending on the period being talked about.
Let us not forget that for years we thought that Britain had the power and the will to dislodge successive Rhodesian governments from power. Alas no. it was only after the realisation that the British government had neither the power nor the will to do so when the armed struggle became the only viable and effective option. But protests were there throughout before the era of the armed struggle.
It has to be said that many of the young men and women who left the country in the 70s to join the armed struggle did so in the name of Bishop Abel Muzorewa and his African National Council (ANC). Yes, he later signed the discredited internal settlement agreement with Ian Smith to usher in the short-lived Zimbabwe-Rhodesia but the question is: Did that make him a total outcast? Was Bishop Muzorewa a collaborator with the racist Smith regime in the mould of Chief Jeremiah Chirau and paramount Chief Khayisa Ndiweni? Certainly not.
Could we not have acknowledged and recognised the earlier works of Bishop Muzorewa perhaps not as a national hero but in other ways? So does Reverend Ndabaningi Sithole, Reverend Canaan Banana, James Chikerema and indeed Gibson Sibanda not deserve national hero status? Are we so consumed by hatred that we fail to see the trees for the woods?
What has happened to the vast web of generosity and love that characterise us Africans in Zimbabwe and Africa in general? Certainly, the gentlemen above in their different ways were missionaries for Zimbabwe in both their lives and works. We, who are myopic, did not see as clearly but history will relate.
There is too much reliance on the armed struggle and political connections to Zanu-Pf in the selection of our heroes. There are some heroes currently interred at the National Heroes Acre who do not represent the finest aspects of the struggle for Zimbabwe both pre and post independence. There are others -- known national heroes -- who are not there but should, to all intents and purposes, be there.
It is common knowledge that there are other people both living and dead who joined the armed struggle not for genuine reasons of wanting to liberate the country but for dubious reasons. Some were dragged into the war of independence kicking and screaming. Others were running away from the long arm of the law. Again, history will relate.
I think that it is very important that we do not distort history. Because of the shortness of colonial rule (1890-1980), the resistors in the 1890s (primary resistance stage), the 1920s and 1940s (secondary resistance stage),the 50s and 60s (tertiary resistance or majority rule stage ) and the 70s (post tertiary or armed struggle stage) lived and are living to tell the tale in these 30 years of our independence.
What I am emphasising here is the need for an accurate and honest approach to our history regardless of the political party one may belong to.
