We do not want to see a situation, where for example, an unquestionable freedom fighter fails to make it to the National Heroes Acre just because he has parted company with Zanu-Pf or he or she has decided to belong to a political party of his or her choice. God forbid!
In any event, it is in the nature of democratic political parties to have disagreements. We need to be tolerant of other people's views and opinions because we think differently and come from different backgrounds. If we have the same opinions in an organisation, then that organisation might as well be dead. It must be dissolved because it is not serving any useful purpose and indeed has no business to exist.
The point I want to make here very strongly is that we find comfort and nothing else in people we agree with but growth in those we disagree with. That is my bottom line.
In conclusion, the point must be emphasised that the period before the war of liberation witnessed an enormous diversity of African responses to colonial experience. And we are currently seeing the same diversity in the 30 years of our independence in terms of correcting our democratic deficits and shortcomings.
All this must be taken on board when deciding who should be declared a national hero. And no political party should have the sole mandate of the decisions but the mandate should be given to an independent national body.
Coupled with an independent body, there should be a well-established criteria and procedure taking into account the complexity of our history both before 1980 and beyond.

Original_Search_Title: Ogun Assembly Factions Seek President's Support
Headline: Nigeria: Ogun Assembly Factions Seek President's Support
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — AS details of the meeting of the estranged members of Ogun State House of Assembly begin to emerge, the two sides in the House of Assembly have been giving different account of what transpired with each side claiming to have gotten the nod of the President.
Nine legislators had at dawn on Monday announced the suspension of Speaker Tunji Egbetokun and the appointment of Mr. Emmanuel Soyemi Coker in his stead. Apart from announcing the appointment of other Principal Officers, the legislators suspended 14 other members belonging to the Group of 15 legislators led by Egbetokun.
The legislators also took other far reaching decisions including approval for accessing the contentious N100 Billion Bond.
But the Egbetokun camp cried foul prompting the Presidency to order the sealing off of the House and summoning them to Abuja.
Egbetokun's Deputy Mr. Remmy Hassan had on Thursday said that President Goodluck Jonathan had given recognition to his group because in Jonathan's view the action of the nine lawmakers was illegal hence it could not stand and that as such, the House would hold its plenary session on Tuesday.
But the Deputy Speaker of the Coker faction, Mr. Ayo Odugbesan denied that President had given recognition to the Egbetokun's group saying "we held a meeting with the President who told us that anyone has an issue with the present situation [crisis] should go to court.
"He [President] also gave the Governor [Daniel] and senator Jubril Martins Kuye 24 hours to settle the issue of factions within the Party's State Executive and give him the feedback within 24 hours.
"President Jonathan has no power to re instate Egbetokun; it is only the judiciary that has such powers. He has ordered that the Assembly should remain shut until the issue is resolved"
But Remmy Hassan accused of Odugbesan of being economical with the truth saying "the President met with us [G15] on Wednesday and told us that he was in Ruwanda when he heard that nine members of the House had met to remove Speaker Egbetokun and suspended 14 others
"The President told us that when he heard the news he described is as a monumental embarrassment to Nigeria because Nigeria is not a banana republic where anything goes. He told us at that meeting that he would meet the other group [G11]"
According to Hassan, after that meeting, the president scheduled a meeting to be attended by the two groups for Thursday to iron things out.
"But the Governor [Daniel] attended the meeting without presenting any legislator from the other group which he Daniel is supporting.
Hassan said that contrary to claims by Odugbesan and Coker that the President had seen reason with them,
Jonathan told them at that meeting that he never made such statement, "in fact when we told him about such claim, what he told us was that it is giving a dog a bad name to hang it and insisted that whoever made such statement should go and refute it.
Hassan said that what the President told them at the Thursday meeting was that he hoped issue would be resolved amicably but added that but if it would turn rancorous then there is nothing stopping the government from getting the Supreme Court to give pronouncement on it [dispute].

Original_Search_Title: Price of Bananas Up in Hoima
Headline: Uganda: Price of Bananas Up in Hoima
Body_Text:
Kampala — The prices of foodstuffs in Hoima municipality have gone up. Beatrice Kabasomi, a trader in Hoima Central Market, said a small bunch of matooke costs sh7,000, from sh3,500.
A big bunch is at sh15,000 from sh11,000. A tin of tomatoes is sh15,000, from sh7,000.
The increase has not affected sales, according to Kabasomi.
However, Godfrey Bagamba, a vegetable seller, said the price of vegetables was stable.
Beef prices have remained stable with a kilo at sh4,000 and sh6,000 for goat meat.
The prices of cereals, particularly maize flour, which is in plenty, have reduced to sh600 a kilo from sh800. Rice is at sh1,600 a kilogramme.

Original_Search_Title: Taraba 2011 - No Longer At Ease for Suntai
Headline: Nigeria: Taraba 2011 - No Longer At Ease for Suntai
Body_Text:
Taraba — The race to the Taraba State government House where Governor Danbaba Danfulani Suntai is presently on the driver's seat has begun, and on the racetrack are three persons including the incumbent. Interestingly, the three contenders, Danbaba Suntai, Senator Joel Danlami Ikenya and Senator Abdulazeez Ibrahim are all of the ruling People's Democratic Party (PDP), making it more of a family affair.
However, the battle in Taraba which should be an in house matter since there is so far no aspirant from any other political party, promises to be fierce, intriguing and great as the aspirants appear to be 'up in arms' to crush one another, using fair and foul means as they move about, selling their best sides to the public.
For the incumbent governor, he appears to have all it takes to beat. If not for anything, he has the state resources at his disposal. Suntai emerged as PDP candidate in 2007 on a platter of gold. His emergence followed the disqualification of Danladi Baido who won the PDP primary election.
When Baido, the then anointed government candidate was rejected by PDP's national leadership under President Olusegun Obasanjo, the lot fell on Suntai who was then the Secretary to State Government (SSG) in governor Jolly Nyame's administration. He was subsequently picked by Nyame to replace Baido.
Besides the huge state resources at his disposal, Suntai is said to have improved upon his political status in 2007 as he succeeded in wooing the aggrieved members of the PDP who left the party for another party after the 2007 election back to the PDP fold.
This, according to Daily Trust investigation, has doused the inter-party opposition against the government and the PDP, and by implication reduced the political contest in the state to an intra-PDP affair.
Another thing that is likely to work for Suntai, according to his Chief Press Secretary, Alhaji Hassan Mijinyawa, is his ability to deliver dividends of democracy to the people of the state in a way they never had.
He believes that Suntai has touched lives in diverse positive ways. He said Suntai in three years had opened up Jalingo, the state capital with township roads, construction of high profile bridge across river Lamurde as well as construction of 82 kilometre road in Karim Lamido and others.
Above all, he said his principal initiated and built university for the state, adding that the Suntai administration has been able to inject sense of responsibility in public service, discourage sharp practices and promote religious harmony in the state.
Be that as it may, Suntai's critics said he has achieved nothing in three and half years, insisting that the achievement recorded in the state was a far cry compared to the amount of money he took from the federation account within the period.
It was gathered that what gave credence to non-performance foray against Suntai was largely informed by the governor himself. He was said to have appraised himself about a year ago and admitted that he had not done well. One of Suntai's opponents, Senator Joel Ikenya said Suntai failed the state woefully because he came to power without a programme.
But what appears to be the major headache of Suntai, according to sources, is his frosty relationship with his political mentor, Rev Jolly Nyame, on whose back he rode to power in 2007.
Suntai and Nyame fell apart shortly after the former assumed office in May 2007. Though relationship between the two men is known to be cold, it is not clear whether Nyame is giving support to any of the other aspirants that are challenging the governor. In any case, analysts say if he decides to work against Suntai, it would spell doom for the ambition of the governor.
But reacting to the negative effect of the failing relationship of the two men on the re-election bid of the governor, his media aide Mijinyawa said Suntai has worked to earn himself victory at the polls with or without Nyame.
He added, however, that Suntai "holds Nyame in high esteem but issues of politicking is a different ball game."
Daily Trust also gathered that what is likely to be a banana peel for the governor is the fact that members of his cabinet have resigned to contest various elective positions ranging from local government chairmanship to senatorial positions.
It was gathered that Suntai is already at crossroads on who to support as any attempt to back any of them against others would likely have a boomeranging effect on his own ambition.
Senator Abdulazeez Ibrahim, another strong contender is not new in the business having run for the position in 2003 on the platform of the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP), and in 2007 under the PDP, when he lost gallantly to Danladi Baido at the primary. Believed to be a grassroots man, Ibrahim is already moving from ward to ward, mobilizing support for his aspiration. It was reliably gathered that Ibrahim who hails from the central zone like Suntai, is returning to the trenches with enough financial muscles to prosecute his ambition.
But what may likely work against him, according to findings, is his religion, as the political direction of the state is partially shaped by the faith an individual professes.
Joel Danlami Ikenya, a serving senator from Taraba south is another person in the race. Unlike Ibrahim, Ikenya is coming out for governorship the first time.
Since 1999, Ikenya has been in the legislature; first as member of the State House of Assembly between 1999 and 2007. After that, he won election to the House of Representatives [2003 to 2007], and now, he is in the Senate. With more than ten years of legislative experience, he says he is now prepared to end Suntai's reign in the state.
He told Daily Trust his decision to run for governor in 2011 was informed by the desire to change the fortunes of the state.
He said Suntai had confessed that he was a failure; therefore he had no business seeking re-election. He said the governor was no longer fit to govern the state.
But even with his over ten years of legislative clout, analysts believe he has no strong political structure that can translate his aspiration to reality.
He claimed, however, not to be only close to the former governor, Rev Jolly Nyame, but that he was Nyame himself. But sources had it that he had no serious backing of any of the political leaders in the state.
Ultimately, some of the issues that would dominate political discourse, and are likely to determine the political direction of the 19 -year old state that prides itself as 'Nature's gift to the Nation' include economy, peace and security, ethnicity and religion.
Observers are of the view that any of the contestants that could handle or play up some of the issues better at certain levels is likely to have good finishing.
For instance, the state has a centralized economy base which revolves around the government or those in government, hence, if anyone is in government it is farewell to poverty, while the majority on the other side of the divide are not just living in squalor but are gradually resigning to fate, seeing poverty as a way of life.
According to analysts, the alarming poverty rate in the state was a function of ineptitude of the party leadership in the state has produced since its creation 19 years ago.
They believe successful governments in the state had failed to, or lacked capacity to lay foundation for sustainable infrastructural development, resulting in backwardness and attendant poverty crisis.
Based on this circumstance, poverty eradication becomes a campaign mantra, particularly for those calling for change or paradigm shift in governance.
Apart from poverty as campaign gimmick, there is religious harmony, peace and security. Issues of zoning and ethnicity are also amplified, depending on where the campaign takes place.
But in the final analysis, political leaders in the state would ultimately determine who takes the day. Investigation revealed that though Rev Jolly Nyame has had it rough in the hands of critics, he still, to a large extent, determines who gets what and how in the state. It was gathered that some independent moves have begun by some stakeholders, using one respected bishop to wade into the face-off between Suntai and Nyame.
As of now, sources say elder statesman, General Theophilus Danjuma (rtd), has refused to meddle into the local politics in the state. However, the source said if Danjuma chooses to identify with any of the aspirants, the person will certainly have the best outing. For now, Suntai is said to be in Danjuma's good books as his foundation now partners with the state government in certain areas of development. What is not clear, however, is whether he will back the governor's re-election bid.

Original_Search_Title: How Women Adapt to Ramadhan
Headline: Tanzania: How Women Adapt to Ramadhan
Body_Text:
It's 4pm at Mnazi Mmoja bus stop and the sun is still shining. It is vividly clear that there are more women struggling to squeeze themselves into the already congested buses and most of them are in veils.
It doesn't take long for one to know the cause for such a hassle. Of course, it is Ramadan, the Muslim holy month of fasting and it is at this time that, women are rushing home in order to prepare food in time for their families to break the fast.
By all means, they have to get home earlier. For many families, Iftaar is not like any other normal meal that can be prepared in a jiffy; it is a collection of several well-cooked meals.
In other words, as Ramadhan knocks the door, Muslim women are the first ones to readjust their lifestyles in order to assert their positions as caring and loving mothers and wives to their families.
A radio presenter
Geah Habib, a female radio presenter at Clouds FM, when I met her, she was quick to say, "I hope your interview won't take long. I hate eating meals that have been prepared by a housemaid during Ramadhan. Therefore, come what may, I have to get home earlier and prepare Iftar."
But that is not the only readjustment Geah has made so far. She has also changed even the general content of her popular Leo Tena programme.
Her gossip programme discusses conflicting issues in a typical lower class society. It's in the same programme where trendy Taarab music is being played.
Busy as she is, yet she still has time to rush home and prepare something special for her husband.
Even in such a trendy world where more women are becoming breadwinners, Geah still finds it inappropriate not to prepare Iftar for her family.
"Our families need appetising meals because they are fasting. I can give all excuses in other months not to cook but not this month," she says.
A lecturer
Imane Duwe is a lecturer at St. Augustine University. To her, Ramadhan can only be difficult to families whose members don't share responsibilities. Although she prepares meals for her family, she also thinks that, it is not purely a woman's role.
To make things simple, she has prepared a month long timetable of possible meals to be served. Everyday, she discusses it with others to see if there is any change in preference.
"If there is anything missing at home, my husband would always pass at the market and tops up the stock," she says.
Imane is one of those who will do anything to make sure that Iftar is served in time.
"We cook at least three meals per day. I discourage cooking too much food because the excess only ends up in the dustbin. Our religion discourages extravagance of any kind," she says.
Nevertheless, she is thankful to her long time house help for being familiar with the family's cooking style.
"I can let her cook and everyone will just be okay with her meals," she says.
Her husband also supports during this month.
"He prepares some meals and leaves behind a note indicating what is left. When I get home I finish the rest."
However, Imane finds weekends appropriate for her to prepare the family's favourite delicacies since she has all the time.
A businesswoman
For unmarried women like Zuhura Juma, she has to prepare her own food whether she is back in time or late.
"I get home at around 5 to 6 pm because of the traffic jam. I just prepare something simple such as spaghetti or even bread with tea," she says.
Married to a Muslim
Anna Pascal is a Christian woman married to a Muslim. To her, things are a bit difficult since she has to strike a balance between her banking job and Ramadhan.
"It's a bit hectic, but Alhamdullilah everything is okay because fasting is not completely a new thing to me," she says.
She gets off work at 5pm. Since she is in Mwanza, traffic jam is not her major concern. To make sure that her husband finds everything set on the table, Anna buys a stock that would last for at least a week on weekends.
"Once everything is in the house, preparation becomes easy. If I want to prepare anything that takes longer, I normally direct my house girl to start preparing some few things in advance. Otherwise, whenever I know that I would be late, I let her prepare everything."
Unlike in other Ramadhan, Anna this time is concerned about the increase of food prices since she also has the task of setting up the budget.
"Much as the prices have gone up, but women always fare well when it comes to negotiating. They are familiar with the prices of coconuts, rice, spaghetti, potatoes, bananas and other stuff," she says.
Client advisor
Moza Musa, 25, is a client advisor at Access Bank. She hasn't seen herself changing much this month because she still lives with her family.
"I don't feel that pressure of rushing home to prepare Iftar. I just make sure that I get home earlier and enjoy the meal with my family. If I happen to be home earlier, then I help them cook."
On weekends however, while everyone else is exhausted, Moza does the cooking.
Meanwhile, she can't help thinking how her lifestyle would change once she gets married.
"I know I will manage when the right time comes," she says, with a radiant smile.
A business woman
Jasmini Nassor, 31, has her small business at Kariakoo in Dar es Salaam.
Her day begins at 6am when she opens her store. During this month, she has to close it at 3.30pm in order to get home and start her chores in time.
She looked a bit exhausted when I saw her at Mnazi Mmoja bus stop. She walked back and forth, looking desperately in every side for a daladala.
When I asked her how she has managed to adjust herself, she says, "It's quite difficult but I have no other option. I have to make sure that everything is prepared well in advance so that we can sit and enjoy as a family."
However, sometimes she gets home late and finds her children to have already prepared something.
"They have grown up," she says in an unsettled tone as she looks for a sign of any coming daladala, determined this time not to miss it.
"I still need to get back and break the fast with them. I also need to supervise them too," she says, while avoiding more of my questions.
All in all, during Ramadhan, the last thing a woman would do is failing her family by not taking her traditional roles seriously.

Original_Search_Title: Banda Tells Off Diplomats Pushing for Regime Change
Headline: Zambia: Banda Tells Off Diplomats Pushing for Regime Change
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Zambian President Rupiah Banda has accused some western diplomats and donors of pushing for a regime change and told them to "pack and go".
This is the second time in barely two months that there has been a feud between the 73-year-old president and western donors, who contribute a substantial amount of funds to various sectors in Zambia. It has raised fears that some donor countries might further withdraw aid to the southern African country in protest.
Several western donors have accused the Banda administration of lacking seriousness in the fight against corruption and frozen funding to the health and road construction sectors after revelations of graft in the two fields.
This followed the acquittal of former president Frederick Chiluba - a Banda ally - on corruption charges last year. This week, the government refused to appeal against a Zambian court's rejection of a 2007 judgment in a British court that found Mr Chiluba and his close aides guilty of the theft of public funds amounting to $46 million (Sh3.7 billion) during his rule between 1991 and 2001.
"This is not a banana republic; it doesn't belong to anybody" said President Banda on Thursday before leaving for his homeland of Chipata - about 500km east of Lusaka.
"If somebody is fed up with us, they should just pack their bags and go where they come from. We are an independent state and I think that they should give us a chance to follow the laws which they left behind."
He warned foreigners to stop interfering in Zambia's affairs.
President Banda said some Zambians should be ashamed for openly being "agents" of foreign countries.
He also dismissed the diplomats and other foreign donors' accusation that he and his administration were not keen to fight corruption.
"Of course you know we are fighting against corruption. There are a lot of people in court; many of our generals are in court, we have never interfered with that. Why should just one case become a major issue?" he asked.
"They (diplomats) know we have an election next year, so they are sticking their noses into our business to try and influence the election; that is colonialism."

Original_Search_Title: Revisiting the Woes of Senator Anyanwu's Sacked Committee
Headline: Nigeria: Revisiting the Woes of Senator Anyanwu's Sacked Committee
Body_Text:
Abuja — The recent sacking of the Senate Committee on Communication has suddenly evoked questions that border on interest and high complicity.
Today Senator Sylvester Anyawu who chaired the ill fated committee continues to rue his misfortune, but was he the culprit or a victim of a major conspiracy? Uchenna Awom in this analysis prods some musings that are very suggestive and could ignite further questions.
Senator Sylvester Anyanwu may be ruing his current misfortune in the Senate, of being the first in this tenure whose position as chairman of a standing committee was terminated instantly and on the floor and also had his committee; the Committee on Communication' dissolved. Up till now the committee has not been reconstituted, but the echoes of the crisis continue to reverberate ostensibly over insinuations of a likely travesty of justice in the circumstance.
It is no longer news that the committee was sacked by the Senate President, Senator David Mark, over some seeming complicity in the screening of the Chairman and members of the board of the National Communication Commission (NCC). Mark premised his reason for doing so on "attempt by the committee to ridicule the upper chamber, by telling it lies of a screening that never took place.
Consequently, the Senate suspended order 17 of its rules to carry out an open screening of the nominees on the spot in the plenary as against the committee screening. They were also cleared and confirmed. Among them are; Mr Peter Igoh as chairman; Dr Eugene Juwah as Executive Vice Chairman and Chief Executive Officer; Mr Okechukwu Itanyi as Executive Commissioner and Alhaji Mohammed Bintube as Non-Executive Commissioner.
Curiously, the senate relied on the same report written and signed by a substantial number of committee members, to screen and clear the nominees, thus raising questions as to the real motive behind the allegations of lies and eventual sacking of the Chairman and his committee members. Observers of the unfortunate episode are beginning to stitch some ominous conjectures, which may not be far from the truth. The question is if actually the committee did not screen the nominees, why accept their report? Again one of the allegations was that the committee was not availed with security report of the nominees and as such had to carry out their functions without the very important security disclosures.
In the same vein, why did Senator Argungu choose the auspicious moment in the chamber after his chairman has concluded presentation of his report to raise objections and tell the Senators that a committee of which he is a member failed to screen the nominees, without availing himself the opportunities provided by his supposed closeness to Senator Anyanwu to prevail on him not to present the report on the floor that day since in his estimation the nominees were not screened.
It is plausible, to say that the Senate President, could have asked Senator Argungu and other members of the committee who denied being part of the process questions, as to the real reasons why they failed to screen the nominees if was true that they were not screened? Could there have been a missing link in the whole saga?
It was expected that Senator Mark could have deferred to legislative privileges and exhaust all avenues in difference to the existing camaraderie to prod the minds of those who raised objections to the report, which was eventually used to clear the nominees. Do we say that there was more to the saga than meet the eye?
Already there are growing musings that Senator Anyanwu who has largely remained ominously quiet over the saga may have been a fall guy to a larger plot that led to a clash of interest over who and who must constitute the board of the NCC.
Investigations suggest that the hurried screening and eventual clearance of the nominees' moments after the crisis on the floor was to foreclose an imminent backlash. Sources disclosed that what actually happened was that Anyanwu was proverbially caught between the devil and the deep blue sea. 'He was a victim of a hire wire politicking and clash of interest in business, politics and ethnic consideration. What happened was that Senator Argungu may have innocently played a role that ended up achieving the unintended. That was to say that other interests capitalized on his hard stance to actualize their motive, not necessarily to humiliate Senator Anyanwu.
Though it was said that Anyanwu exhibited crass naivety on the floor and even behind closed door session, by giddily accepting culpability; 'may be in the believe that could be spared in the circumstance. Anyanwu was simply made the sucker, because he strongly believes in his innocence, but you know that in situations like these, you play a smart game otherwise your career would be blown to pieces. That was actually what happened. Yet it could be mitigated the day the Senator become courageous enough to tell the world what actually transpired".
The truth according to the source was that the interest in the NCC suddenly became the Achilles hills of some smaller elements in the power game, like Senator Anyanwu. The interest traverses from the top to the bottom. And of course the major truth was that those already holding sway at the NCC was banking on the disruption of the screening and clearance process so that they could stay on knowing that the Senate was set to embark on a long recess. That was on that side and then there were the internal interest within the Senate that not only hastened the clearance but erroneously put Senator Anyanwu on the political slammer.
POSTSCRIPT
The committee, which was chaired by Senator Sylvester Anyanwu (PDP, Imo) had submitted a report of the purported screening of members of the Governing Board of the Nigeria Communications Commission (NCC) to the Senate for consideration.
Debate was to be halted mid-way when a member of the panel, Senator Umar Argungu (PDP, Kebbi), raised a point of order in which he disclosed to a shocked Senate that no screening took place contrary to the report presented by Senator Anyanwu.
Argungun in his submission wondered how the committee chairman came about the report being presented to the Senate.
To corroborate the disclosure of Argungu, another member of the committee, Senator Tanko Ayuba (PDP-Kebbi) who signed the report along six other committee members, said that he signed the report of the screening only "this morning but was not aware of any screening been done by the committee".
"This is the time to own up. I signed this morning but didn't attend any screening," he said.
Obviously embarrassed by the revelations, the Vice Chairman of the committee, Senator Joseph Akaagerger (PDP, Benue) appealed to the Senate, to dissolve into a closed door session to resolve the issue. But the appeal was rebuffed by Senate President David Mark.
Senator Mark emphatically told him that; "There will be no close session. The committee has put itself in an embarrassing situation. I am sad by this. One committee should not ridicule the Senate.
"I am truly disappointed in the committee. None of the committee members can exonerate himself and all of you appended your signatures. I am short of words but I insist that we will not go into close session. This committee stands dissolved".
A confused Anyanwu, who pleaded against the dissolution of the committee, said: "we feel sad but we can still correct our short-coming".
However, after much pressure from the Deputy Senate President, Senator. Ike Ekweremadu and Sen. Mohammed Maaji Maina, Deputy Chief Whip, Mark agreed to go into a closed door session.
But after a closed door session that lasted about 45 minutes, the Senate President announced the disbandment of the committee, adding that the members of the governing board of the NCC who were at the Senate gallery to witness their confirmation, would be screened openly by the Senate.
Anyanwu had in the report stated that the committee screened the members on July 27 and urged the Senate to confirm the nominees. He had also affirmed that the reports of the police and security agencies also cleared the nominees.
However, a copy of the report made available to newsmen showed that only five of the 11-member committee signed the document that cleared the nominees.
The report also indicates that the Clerk of the committee, Mr Kolawole Kayode, signed for Senators Yisa Braimoh (PDP, Edo) and Odion Ugbesia (PDP, Edo).
DENIALS
Senator Anyanwu has remained quiet, he continued to rue his fate, but the silver lining is the concomitant questions. Observers are now saying that what had happened was a carefully choreographed act, not actually targeted at Senator Anyanwu, but was rather meant to achieve a predetermined goal which also hurt Argungun's game plan. At the end both those who denied the report and those who accepted it lost for higher interest, though they might have benefitted for performing their legislative functions. The end game and perhaps the overall gain is the discovery of the certain banana peels on the way of virile oversight function of the parliament. In that case Anyanwu's cries can still be mitigated.

Original_Search_Title: Unelected Toadies Mock Our Democracy
Headline: South Africa: Unelected Toadies Mock Our Democracy
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — WHEN an avowed communist lashes out at the media as being a "threat to democracy", it is not just hypocritical and laughable, but deception. This calls for a review of the very "democracy" that is being referred to - lest Soviet-speak prevails.
The communist minister, Blade Nzimande, holds a parliamentary seat and a Cabinet post, not because his party won in the elections - it had no chance of winning. Now unelected toadies - including all MPs, the president and other ministers - are talking about democracy and have been rubber-stamping the curbs and imposing their inimical will on society. Yet they demand accountability from others - without being either elected or accountable themselves.
Nzimande, moreover, exercises power and enjoys privileges because he belongs to a party that formed an alliance with the African National Congress (ANC) based solely on their common reaction to apartheid. This alliance - spawned in a fascist environment and nurtured in conspiracy - lacks ideological content and relevance, and has degenerated into the cronyism of spoils. It has no genuine political objective - the lifestyles of these comrades in corruption mock any expression of real politics.
Further, Nzimande owes his position less to his red star and more to his ancestors for being from the tribe of the country's paramount chief. Nzimande, like most of his "struggle" tribesmen and women, was sidestepped during the time of former president Thabo Mbeki. As a verkrampte protege of the communist motherland - the Soviet Union - Thabo Mbeki learned how to manipulate ethnicity, race and sinister objectives behind ideological facades and beguiling phrases.
Had there been constituency elections - still the best method for ensuring representative democracy and responsible government - Nzimande would not be defending anything. But an aborted democratic promise has enabled communists to head departments and determine policy.
Nzimande now moulds the hearts and minds of people whose majority proclaim the great religions once denied by communists. This absurdity (together with arrogance, corruption and incompetence) is the main ingredient of the regime - and comprises the "democracy" that democrats and patriots must continue to attack.
Fortunately for Nzimande and his unelectable comrades, the constitution is on their side. It has facilitated them getting into legislatures by giving parties the exclusive power to choose MPs. It has allowed foisted people to pass legislation, sit in judgment about the rest of the body politic and direct SA's destiny. And it has thus provided for the evolution of a banana republic.
At present, Parliament is an imposition on the country and it houses people and programmes that are not in accord with democracy or the interests of the people. But a new spirit is stirring - one that questions the entire package. Hence, tribal Bolsheviks are worried lest their lack of democratic content and their fraudulent presence in the national legislatures ends their participation. Nzimande and his comrades are worried about their precarious positions. Instead of being sucked into anxieties about the relationship between the government/ruling party and media on one side, and both of these and democracy on the other, the fundamentals of the constitution should be revamped. The constitution has allowed: the dangers of nonelected people ruling a country; tyrannical tendencies and corruption owing to a lack of accountability; and communists pronouncing on democracy and deciding national priorities in essential fields.
After a decade of independence, Americans realised that their founding constitution, the Articles of Confederation, was troublesome. With courage, insight and experience, they changed it. A more lasting constitution and dynamic union ensued. The late Frederik van Zyl Slabbert was asked to evaluate SA's electoral system. Worthy recommendations were made for constituency-based elections. Kempton Park 1994 needs a local Philadelphia 1789 - otherwise conflicts and disintegration will continue.
Mabogoane is a freelance writer.

Original_Search_Title: 100 Agents Moved in ISO Shake-up
Headline: Uganda: 100 Agents Moved in ISO Shake-up
Body_Text:
Kampala — The new Director General of the Internal Security Organisation, Lt. Ronnie Balya, has reshuffled senior officers at the intelligence outfit. Lt. Balya announced the changes in an August 20 memo to all ISO staff. He has reshuffled all 10 directors, three regional intelligence officers and 34 district Internal Security Officers.
The changes
In the new changes, Lt. Don Mugimba is the new deputy director general and also heads planning, protocol, legal affairs, internal counter-intelligence and audit. Others moved are Lt. Herbert Muramagi, who has been the director of operations and now heads the directorate of security. His new office has four departments of counter-intelligence, organised crimes, defence and counter-terrorism. The operations office is no more.
Lt. Frank Banana has been transferred from the human resource directorate to logistics and finance. He has been replaced by Lt. Melkizedeki Were who has been transferred from finance. Lt Hannington Kakura has been moved from intelligence and analysis to head the electoral affairs directorate. He has been replaced by Lt. Fred Lubega who formerly headed the secretariat at the head office.
Lt. Charles Tumuhairwe is the head of the directorate of regional affairs, a docket which includes border security, joint permanent commissions and regional security bodies. Other new appointments are Lt. David Rusa (inspectorate), Lt. Fred Kyakulaga (political intelligence), Lt. Kibrai Ambako (socio-economic affairs) and Lt. Charles Oluka (technical services).
The regional officers who have been transferred include three long serving operatives; Lt. Emmy Musinguzi, who moves from headquarter to south western region and Lt. Charles Tumusiime, who has been transferred from West Nile to northern Uganda. He will be based in Gulu. Lt. Vincent Gahima is the new RISO of the West Nile region after serving for years in mid-western.
Lt. Balya said in the statement that the changes were meant to strengthen the operations of internal security. "I have made the following redeployments /transfers to strengthen the existing mechanisms in order to enhance focus on the strategic objectives of the organisation," the memo reads. The latest changes come two weeks after his predecessor Dr Amos Mukumbi was sacked and redeployed by President Museveni as his special advisor on security matters.

Original_Search_Title: Mayor Survives Lynching
Headline: Uganda: Mayor Survives Lynching
Body_Text:
Fort Portal mayor Edison Asaba Ruyonga on Monday survived being lynched by angry supporters of area MP Steven Kaliba, who accused him of backing the lawmaker's opponent.
Mr Kaliba is running against Mr Alex Ruhunda to become NRM flag bearer for the municipality parliamentary seat.
Business in the town was paralysed for more than an hour, as hundreds of Mr Kaliba's supporters, clad in banana leaves, holding tree branches and sticks, stormed the mayor's supermarket.
Businessmen and boda boda riders carried Mr Kaliba's portrait and demanded to know why the mayor was allegedly de-campaigning the MP. However, Mr Ruyonga was not at the supermarket at the time of the chaos.
His wife Jane Asaba, who was at the supermarket, ran for safety as the mob demanded to know her husband's whereabouts. They later cut several tree branches and dumped them in front of the shopping facility. Noise enveloped the town as the MP's supporters, chanted 'big size'.
Police intervened
Mr Kaliba had earlier during a rally, promised to bring development to the area. The situation was later calmed down by the police.
When contacted yesterday, Mr Ruyonga said he was at the District Police Commander's office registering a case against the MP.
Meanwhile, announcements have been running on local radios calling Mr Kaliba's supporters to clean the town they littered.

Original_Search_Title: Zain Rewards Trader in Millionaire Promo
Headline: Nigeria: Zain Rewards Trader in Millionaire Promo
Body_Text:
Lagos — The final batch of winners in the just concluded Zain's Wake up a Millionaire offer has been rewarded with their cash prizes. At a prize presentation held at the Zain Headquarters in Banana Island, Ikoyi, Lagos, 21 winners received their cheques from officials of the Telecoms operator.
The winners who received their winnings at the prize presentation included a Scrap dealer, Isiaka Abubakar; a retiree, Sunday Abiodun; a trader, James Odediran and a Lagos resident, Kehinde Ekunola. Other winners at the ceremony were Uche Nwankwo, Chidi Igwe, Michael Oniye, Samuel Ekwo, Uche Nwankwo and Aholue Daniel, amongst others. All the winners were presented with cheques of two million naira each.
Speaking at the occasion, Isiaka Abubakar, a scrap dealer, commended Zain Nigeria for fulfilling its pledge to its customers by rewarding winners in the promo, describing his cash prize as a life-line that will go a long way to change his world. He urged the mobile operator to continue to excite customers with offers that will change their lives, revealing that he will invest his new found fortune in his trade.
Also speaking, James Odediran, a Lagos-based trader showered encomiums on zain for empowering him with financial resources to further his education. He said his dream has always been to further his education and his winning has opened a vast window of opportunity to realise the dream.
The just concluded Zain's 'Wake up a Millionaire', is an interactive and rewarding SMS Service which provided customers an opportunity to become millionaires daily. With the offer, Zain elevated over 100 Nigerians into the millionaire rank as well as rewarded many others with laptops, mobile phones, and generators, amongst other prizes through the marketing initiative.
Winners were selected daily and monthly throughout the duration of the offer. Winners in the Daily Draw received N2, 000,000.00 each while winners in the Monthly Draw were rewarded with N10, 000,000.00.
The prize presentation was attended by Director, Commercial Planning and Strategy, Zain Nigeria, Godfrey Efeurhobo; Manager, Marketing Communications, Zain Nigeria, Nkiru Olumide-Ojo; Public Relations Manager, Zain Nigeria, Emmanuel Otokhine; and Channel Branding Manager, Zain Nigeria, Mrs. Adejumobi Mofe-Damijo, among others.

Original_Search_Title: ISO Boss Forced Out of Office
Headline: Uganda: ISO Boss Forced Out of Office
Body_Text:
The Director General of the Internal Security Organisation (ISO), Dr Amos Mukumbi, was yesterday forced to hand over the leadership of the intelligence outfit to his deputy, Lt. Ronnie Balya.
President Museveni last week fired Dr Mukumbi, who has been at the helm of ISO for the last five years. He had refused to hand over to Lt. Balya, saying he had a confidential file auditing his deputy's performance as well as those of some other senior directors.
Dr Mukumbi was sacked following an audit of the July 11 terror attacks in Kampala which placed most blame on ISO's failures. More than 74 people were killed and scores injured in the twin bomb attacks at Lugogo and Kabalagala, both Kampala suburbs.
But even before the bomb attacks, President Museveni was reportedly furious with ISO after it failed to detect the magnitude of the September 2009 riots in which 27 people were killed after demonstrators took to Kampala's streets, protesting the government's refusal to let Kabaka Mutebi visit Kayunga District.
At that point, the President temporarily handed over political intelligence information gathering, especially the Buganda docket, to the External Security Organisation--which reportedly looked more organised
Litany of faults
But sources indicate that upon further scrutiny of ISO's operations, the President faulted Dr Mukumbi who had removed the counter-intelligence docket from Lt. Balya, a veteran covert operations specialist.
The President was also reportedly furious that government programmes like NAADS and UPE have been abused yet ISO has structures meant to monitor them. Security Minister Amama Mbabazi and the Coordinator of Security Agencies, Gen. David Tinyefuza, yesterday supervised Dr Mukumbi's exit.
At the function where only two government media houses--the Uganda Broadcasting Corporation and The New Vision were allowed--Mr Mbabazi announced that Dr Mukumbi had been appointed special presidential adviser on security matters.
Daily Monitor barred
Daily Monitor was barred from covering the ceremony. Dr Mukumbi replaced Col. Elly Kayanja in 2005 as the boss of government's civilian intelligence agency responsible for providing national security intelligence to Uganda's policy makers.
The agency's role is to also engage in covert operations on government programmes and security at the request of the President. Dr Mukumbi, a veteran intelligence operative who joined ISO 24 years ago, bows out on a low after poor rating of his performance despite Mr Mbabazi's attempt to flower his departure punctuated by tense moments.
Before an afternoon stage-managed handover ceremony in front of the government media, Dr Mukumbi was ordered to pass the baton before all ISO directors including Lt. Hannington Kakura (collection), Lt. Frank Banana (human resource), Lt. Barungi (office of the DG), Lt Kiiza (IT/technical), Lt. Herbert Muramagi (operations) and Lt. Lubega (Secretariat).
Others present were ISO Deputy Director in charge of West Nile, Taban Amin, the Undersecretary at State House, Hajj Kakande and all regional security officers. The agency was founded in 1986 and took over the duties first performed by Military Intelligence.
The first Director General was Maj. Gen. Jim Muhwezi, who led it until 1996, before Brig. Henry Tumukunde took charge up to 2003. ISO has trained intelligence officers recruited from all over the country. Upon recruitment, the operatives are trained at the Institute of Intelligence and Security Studies at Makajo.

Original_Search_Title: Secrecy Law Threatens Democratic Credentials
Headline: South Africa: Secrecy Law Threatens Democratic Credentials
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — ONE would be hard pressed to find a government that would say it likes the press or enjoys the kind of scrutiny the press subjects it to. Yet it is equally difficult to find a democracy that is attempting to emasculate the media in the manner in which the South African government is doing with the Protection of Information Bill and the punitive media tribunal it wants to introduce. And although SA is not alone in fighting the free flow of information, it stands out as a country that is sliding backwards at a fast pace in its bid to make public information its own private property.
Consider some of the defining features of authoritarian tendencies in media law, which London-based author and academic Tim Crook writes about in his latest book, Comparative Media Law and Ethics.
A jurisdiction that leans towards authoritarianism practis es "absolute or near total secrecy and suppression of all information relating to intelligence, police". Its "free- speech rights are qualified by rights of national security".
It doesn't believe in "guaranteeing public access to information collected and stored by government/state bodies".
It is a country that imposes "criminal liability on private citizens and journalists who received state information where the classification of confidentiality and secrecy is broad and all-encompassing".
And there's more. Its "ruling elite" buys into the media market "to silence and discourage critical reporting". It's a place where "heavy penalties" are handed down to journalists who "try to protect their sources".
It is also a country that grants "wide powers for state agencies to tap communications of journalists and collect surveillance evidence for the purposes of identifying their sources" and practices "overt and covert ... attacks on journalists working for media institutions that publish critical coverage".
If all that sounds familiar, it's because it reads as if it was lifted chapter and verse from the latest government proposals.
The sad feature of Crook's work, however, is that while SA sits firmly in the libertarian camp today, it will find itself lumped on the authoritarian side of the fence tomorrow if the government succeeds in clamping down on freedom of expression.
The other alarming fact, though, is that the South African proposals are the best kept secret in the international arena of media law experts, anticensorship groups or institutions that protect the purveyors of information themselves. When the draft bill was circulated last week among experts in Canada, Israel, the UK and the US, their reactions were not surprising.
"The proposed bill is draconian," says Mibi Moser, a renowned media lawyer in Israel, where limitations on the media are already stringent because of the country's geopolitics and the constant tension between the military and the executive. In comparing the South African bill and current Israeli legislation, he highlights SA's broad definition of "state information", as well as the reasons for not disclosing such information as enormous causes for concern.
Israeli law puts the emphasis on the right of the Israeli citizen to have access to public information, he points out. But the emphasis in the South African instance is the reverse "and it is frightening". But "the most arousing cause for concern", in his appraisal, are the proposed "severe penalties for obtaining, collecting, capturing or copying all kinds of 'state information'."
It's a view echoed by Prof Klaus Pohle, a media law specialist at Canada's Carleton University. "This is something you might expect in a totalitarian banana republic," he argues. However, it's the arbitrarily defined terms of "public good" and "national interest" that unsettle Pohle most. While Pohle concedes that most western democracies -- Canada included -- have legislation that errs on the side of national security, which is often poorly defined, it is never "as ill defined as (SA's) description of the national interest".
"Among the enumeration of what is meant by the national interest is the public good", which is "vague and subjective.... This section should be the most worrying since it can include anything," he says. "Any criticism of public policy, governmental activity or even private commercial activity may be seen as an attack on the public good."
Imagine trying to debate the previous South African government's so-called AIDS denialism under the proposed legislation, he suggests. It just wouldn't wash because it would be undoubtedly seen as an attack on the public good. But would silence then have been helpful? Surely not.
Or take the example of the Gulf of Mexico oil spill. Had it happened in SA, "one could easily assume that assigning blame, however much warranted, may not serve the public good because it might disturb relations between the developer and the state" under the proposed bill, he points out.
"It might even be illegal to discuss critically the contents of this very bill," he says. Or the arrest of Sunday Times journalist Mzilikazi wa Afrika, for that matter. Not to mention corruption on the part of public servants and government officials. Or the lack of service delivery. The list goes on.
But the question that rolled off most tongues was why SA would want to go down this route at this time.
The answer is quite simple. Our political elite is feeling very insecure and threatened, not by a lack of security, but by what the free flow of information might do to their ever- closing political system, and so has decided to drag the young democracy in the direction of such countries as China, Saudi Arabia, Venezuela and Russia.
Joel Simon of the New York-based Committee to Protect Journalists says governments everywhere are becoming more sophisticated about the strategies they use to control the media.
"Increasingly, legal pressure, advertising boycotts, punitive tax raids, libel suits and other less visible means are replacing imprisonment and state-sanctioned violence," he says.
This is true of China, where a journalist is more likely to be fired than jailed for critical reporting, he says. "And in nominally more open countries, such as Russia, where the Kremlin has used tax raids to take over critical media outlets."
(In SA, it seems, the preferred practice is still arrest.)
Simon balks at the proposed media tribunal, which he sees as a pathetic attempt to control criticism and dissent.
Yet if the litmus test of real political authority and power is the ability to withstand criticism, then why doesn't "the African National Congress (ANC) muster the maturity and the courage that is required to lead without resorting to these kinds of measures?" Crooks asks. "Don't they see that they would also amass a considerable amount of power in the world if they did?"
Clearly not. Instead, the ANC is about to dispose of all the political dignity it has left.
For the past few years, SA has found itself languishing among the flawed democracies of the world in the index of democracies compiled by The Economist's Intelligence Unit (EIU), scoring just shy of what is required to join the world's full democracies.
It was hoped that this year SA would crawl up into the category of the world's best.
"But if these media proposals become practice, SA will be downgraded, not upgraded," says Pratibha Thaker, regional director of the EIU's Africa department.
Forde is a freelance writer.

Original_Search_Title: Voters Impressed by Decoration at Sites
Headline: Rwanda: Voters Impressed by Decoration at Sites
Body_Text:
Kigali — Voters were yesterday impressed by the decorations at all the polling centres across the country where they thronged in the wee hours of the morning to exercise their constitutional right to vote for the Presidential candidate of their choice.
Most of the polling stations were decorated in blue, yellow and green, the colours that make up the national flag while others used traditional decorations using locally fabricated materials like banana fibre.
The voting rooms were also well carpeted and at some of the centres, there was music calling upon Rwandans to freely vote to express their rights and democracy.
"It is my first time to vote in such a good atmosphere at a polling station, well decorated, no chaos and everybody happy with the environment," said Innocent Nkusi, one of the voters at Muhima polling station in Nyarugenge District.
Voters began heading to polling stations as early as 4a.m, despite the fact that the sites were not due to open until 6a.m, and by midday the process had ended at most of the centres.
At some polling stations visited by The New Times, chairs had been organized for the voters to rest as they waited for their turn to cast their ballot.
"We voted comfortably here, we even have chairs to sit on. To me, this exercise has been wonderful. I have never witnessed it anywhere in Africa," said Peter Kabanda another voter at one of the polling stations in Kigali City.

Original_Search_Title: Imbalu Targets 40,000 Candidates
Headline: Uganda: Imbalu Targets 40,000 Candidates
Body_Text:
Kampala — Over 2,000 candidates from the Bamasaaba community in Uganda and western Kenya have been circumcised since the grand launch in Mbale on Friday.
One of the organisers and Manafwa district chairman, Peke Walimbwa, said over 40,000 boys will have undergone the ritual by the end of the season in December.
Three candidates were circumcised at the grand launch, witnessed by President Yoweri Museveni, at Mutoto cultural grounds.
At the fete, over 300 boys put up a colourful performance, dancing to the local music and dance known as kadodi, before they faced the knife. Enoch Mufumula, 18, has been waiting for this day since childhood.
"I feel good because I have finished the debt of the Bamasaaba. I am now a man and I can marry anytime," said Mufumula, a Primary Six pupil. The boy-turned-man, however, added that he will continue with his studies.
Mufumula disclosed that during the circumcision, he felt a lot of pain. "But once determined, you can complete the process without fear. I was told that pain is part of making one a man."
Three surgeons attended to Mufumula during the ritual. Without any gloves, the first surgeon poured dust from burnt bricks into his fingers and some onto the foreskin.
The dust was meant to improve his grip as he grabbed the foreskin and pulled it. He dug his sharp thumb-nail deeper into the foreskin and pulled further and then marked the point where the second surgeon cut the foreskin with a sharp knife.The third surgeon cut off the inner skin, letting out a lot of blood.
The surgeon then poured raw eggs on the wound. The eggs coat around the wound to stop the bleeding. The entire ritual is done without anesthesia.
"We do not use gloves during circumcision. I wash my hands very clean with herbs and soap to ensure proper hygiene. We don't use gloves because they are slippery and may cause accidents like cutting off the head of the penis," said Hajj Yusuf Wamboga, a traditional surgeon.
However, Dr. Paul Musani of Mbale Hospital said lack of protective gear may expose the candidate or the surgeon to infections such as HIV/AIDS.
He added that the surgeons must maintain personal hygiene, wash hands, cut their nails and sterilise the knives using hot water.
"Each candidate is entitled to a knife, which is not supposed to be used on another," he said.
After circumcision, Mufumula was wrapped in a bed-sheet. The wound was not dressed because the surgeons believe that if it is dressed in bandages immediately, pain sets in.
According to Wamboga, the wound is dressed three days after the circumcision. He said the candidate has to go to hospital to get the wound dressed and get antibiotics to prevent infections.
In the meantime, Mufumula was given some herbs to reduce the pain. He was also given millet porridge and sweet bananas. His father, John Waniaye was very excited. "My boy has become a man and was brave throughout the ceremony. He made the family proud," he remarked.
Waniaye explained that Mufumula did not bath the night before because culture demands that before bathing, the family slaughters a goat to appease the gods. Bathing is done after three days.
"Without the ritual, the boy may run mad. We are still looking for a goat, after which, he will bath in the camp with hot water to wash away the bad spirits."
Like other freshly-circumcised youth, Mufumula has to sleep on dry banana leaves to prevent blood from spilling onto his mattress and bed-sheets. The Bamasaaba believe that it is bad omen for blood to stain the beddings.
"So his beddings will be dry banana leaves for the three weeks he will be in the camp. The healing takes about three weeks," Waniaye explained.
The candidates sleep in the kneeling position to avoid hurting themselves.
The circumcision season always brings life to the quiet mountainous region of Bugisu.
This year's opening ceremony brought most activities to a halt. Businesses, schools and most offices were closed to allow workers attend the ceremony.
With the dancing and drinking during day and night, casual sex ruled the ceremony as the youth forgot about HIV/AIDS. There were also reports of defilement and rape.

Original_Search_Title: KCC FC Rainbow Appears
Headline: Uganda: KCC FC Rainbow Appears
Body_Text:
Kampala — As a rainbow appears in the sky following a heavy downpour, a brand new board of directors, a rainbow executive, was ushered in last Sunday by a KCC FC electorate that gathered at City hall to open a new chapter in the club's illustrious history.
The contrast between KCC's new board and the one SC Villa unveiled last week, was unmistakable. There were no men in grey suits, no VIP ambiance to match the Sheraton ball room or video projectors at City hall. That's a preserve of the bourgeois.
The new board chairman, Bakka Musujja, KCC's finance director, spotted a checked jacket and light yellow shirt. Nothing special. A reminder that from inception, KCC FC was meant to be a common man's club.
Kasasiro boys, is how the club is fondly referred to. And where you had high sounding businessmen, lawyers and executives dominating the Villa high table, KCC had budding politicians with enough skill to charm a fly... Jaberi Bidandi Ssali "mister," Haji Nasser Sebaggala, Rose Namayanja.
Bidandi, the first deputy city mayor in mid 1960's, Sebaggala the current mayor and Namayanja, his deputy.
It isn't KCC until you hear the chants and trade mark choruses and so the elections were inundated with the endearments; kangende gye ngenda City yange enkyaye mama, Namayanja sang as the fans replied; nedda nedda olimba city council ekwagala.
It was followed by that sweet rhythmic three time clapper KCC! KCC ! KCC as the fans got caught in a frenzy.
The dominant yellow in the hall, smiles and laughter all combined to bring the sun down and brighten the mood as spirits got lifted instantly. Nostalgia. It was the spirit of a rainbow. This club has always been preserved by hope and there was hope again.
Among those present, Musujja named Mrs Justine Kasule, William Blick, Peter Kibazo, Kabuye and Jimmy Kiwanuka as executive members (read board directors).
Finally, KCC has a brand new leadership, as fresh as a ripe banana. Who ever knew this could happen? New faces? That's a landmark achievement. By end of next week, the other newly appointed directors will be made public to complete the cast and KCC's version of a new dawn will be launched. Villa, watch this space.
Bidandi preached a message of comradeship while Sebaggala insisted on success and good public relations. At the same time, Musujja appealed to fans to return and join his new team to restore lost glory and make the football club the pride of the city again.
At that point I was filled with a nostalgic feeling as it suddenly dawned on me that there is a real possibility that this city will be yellow again. It's what Ugandan football has been waiting for. The yellow rainbow. The KCC renaissance.

Original_Search_Title: Two Things I Fear Most - Discovery of Oil Deposits and HIV/Aids Cure
Headline: Tanzania: Two Things I Fear Most - Discovery of Oil Deposits and HIV/Aids Cure
Body_Text:
Call me dispensable; I'll not quarrel with that! Call me a realist ... I'll not quarrel with that either. But, call me a prophet, soothsayer, doomsayer, crystal ball gazer, palm-reader, phrenologist and suchlike occultists/con-tricksters? Nor, Sir (or Madam, as the case may be); I draw the line on that one...
I'll attempt an explanation... Press reports in June 2010 had it that more petroleum deposits had been discovered in Tanzania. One memorable newspaper carried the headline 'Tanzania's oil prospects get even brighter!'
A positive headline under most normal circumstances, one would be forgiven to surmise. And, a positive intro... 'A recently-concluded geological assessment in Tanzania's southern offshore deep oil exploration project has shown there is a potential of over a billion barrels of oil... or seven trillion cubic feet of natural gas...' (The Citizen: June 29, 2010).
That piece of good news seems to have interested the likes of Wall Street Journal who published the reports - no doubt for the Capitalist world to take note. This should no doubt have investment vultures up in the air and circling, slowly circling above Tanzanian thermals ready to pounce on the country with deals which are indecently lopsided in their favour as a matter of course.
It will, of course, take years and billions in investments before the oil can start flowing - and/or the gas gusting out of the bowels of 'Mother Earth' ready to line the pockets of everyone else except ordinary Tanzanians. Talk of poor people in a rich country upon whose resources they have no say, no gain!
We all know what the 1997 mining policy and 1998 mining legislation did to our finite precious minerals. Not only are communities in foreigner-owned mining localities 'cribbed, cabined and confined' (Shakespeare's Macbeth pardon) by wanton, exploitative mining activities.
They were hounded out of house, home and livelihood to give way to foreign interests in the only country they knew and loved!
But, we never seem to learn ...
For me and the lot of Tanzanians, discovery of mineral resources, including petroleum, sets shivers up and down our collective national spine. It is nothing to write home about - let alone dance and sing in the streets in elation.
They can do that in Wall Street, in Canada, in Australia and in South Africa, where capitalist interests have Tanzania's mining industry in a hammerlock from which even a self-professed Government Of the People, By the People and For the People cannot free it.
On the opposite spectrum of discovery which I fear for and about is that of a cure (?) for the dreaded HIV/Aids scourge. For a full three seconds or so, I was elated when I read about this several weeks ago.
Then it dawned upon me faster than greased lightning that this was going to prove the 'wrong' development. Inherently, it may be a good thing. But, to me, that is a negative development at this point in time.
Look at it this way ...
Within hours of the discovery being published, I was taken aback by the uninhibited excitement shown by the people I talked to about it. 'God is great,' they bubbled. 'We are tired of eating a banana in its skin...!' See the point? Get the (ugly) pic?
After a generation of HIV/Aids education that was slowly and painfully acquired regarding safe/protected sex, people are now going to go at it full blast. This is in the misguided belief that there now is a cure for the malady... And that, all they need to do after unprotected sex is to take a shower (pardon, President What's-his-Name), go to Mangi's Chemist Shop, pay for a course of 'HIV/Aids abracadabra' and, presto... Whew!
Not every scientific discovery is a blessing... Pass the mahog - and Cheers!

Original_Search_Title: Okakarara Students Build Kitchen for Kindergarten
Headline: Namibia: Okakarara Students Build Kitchen for Kindergarten
Body_Text:
Windhoek — A group of 10 students from Okakarara Secondary School recently built a kitchen for the Divundu Kindergarten as part of a community project of Impala Tours.
In addition to building the kitchen, the students also grew some banana trees, lettuce, tomatoes, beans and other vegetables at the kindergarten.
After the successful completion of their charity mission, the students were rewarded with a boat cruise on the Okavango River and game drives through the nearby Mahango Game Reserve.
Andre von Flotow, owner of Impala Tours and Car Hire said he was grateful to the students and other partners who made it possible for them to assist the Divindu Kindergarten.
"It was absolutely great to accompany a group of children that were more than willing to support others in Namibia. I would also like to thank Waterberg Wilderness Lodge, Coastal Car Hire and NACOBTA, for their immeasurable support - without them we would not have achieved so much, in such short time," Flotow said.

Original_Search_Title: RPF Rocks Ngoma
Headline: Rwanda: RPF Rocks Ngoma
Body_Text:
Ngoma — The RPF presidential candidate, Paul Kagame, yesterday continued his campaign in the Eastern Province addressing over 80,000 people at Kabare School in Ngoma district where he was warmly welcomed by the jubilant crowd.
The candidate arrived in Ngoma at around 4 p.m. amidst thunderous applause and loud music. He took a few minutes to greet the excited supporters.
Shortly after he was introduced to the crowd by his campaign manager, Fidele Ndayisaba, Kagame told the residents that the plan remains the same and clear; building lasting peace, development and democracy.
He told the surging crowd that voting RPF 100% is itself democracy because it is the choice of Rwandans.
Speaking on his second rally of the day after Kirehe, Kagame told Ngoma residents that democracy is priceless and is not given as aid, but it is the people of a country who decide to give themselves democracy, not foreigners and intruders.
Backed by the massive support from the crowd, Kagame noted that democracy cannot be imported into the country but rather it's the citizens who define which kind of democracy they want, to which the crowd replied in unison "we want you".
He added that any such choice will be made by Rwandans come August 9 and that the day will determine the destiny of the country. Fronting his party to continue with the landmark achievements, Kagame said that Rwandans will not regret the choice they will have made in voting RPF.
Asking the resident what their choice would be between RPF politics which are pro-development and politics of hate, the mammoth crowd thunderously responded, with clenched fists "RPF, RPF".
He called on the residents to continue improving their standards of living through hard work and remain with hope and dignity as Rwandans. He promised to continue delivering, once voted into power, and never to stray from the path that he has already set for the country.
A leading entrepreneur in the district, Steven Mugisha, gave a testimony on how he increased his income from Rwf 40,000, he earned as a driver. Today, he owns a business worth Rwf50m and 3 cars.
Christine Murebwayire, a widow and a member of COOPROVIBA, a banana wine producing cooperative, also testified on how she was inspired by President Kagame's call to be self reliant.
She formed a cooperative with 16 other women to grow bananas and produce wine for export. They started with Rwf50,000 and today are worth Rwf50m and they want to set up a Rwf130m factory to process banana wine. They have improved their living standards.
Kagame is expected to address two other rallies in Nyagatare and Gatsibo districts today.

Original_Search_Title: Will It Be Second Time Lucky for Government?
Headline: Kenya: Will It Be Second Time Lucky for Government?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya first held a national referendum on the constitution in November 2005.
The campaign split the then Narc government into two, with then Roads minister Raila Odinga leading the Orange team comprising politicians from the Liberal Democratic Party against the document and President Kibaki pushing for its adoption.
The Orange team was supported by the then official opposition party Kanu and retired President Moi.
They argued that the document vested too much power in the presidency.
They accused the Parliamentary Select Committee on Constitutional Review, then chaired by Nyaribari Chache MP Simeon Nyachae, of watering down the Bomas draft at a retreat in Kilifi.
The Orange camp carried the day by garnering 3.5 million votes, against the Banana's 2.5 million.
The results saw President Kibaki suspend his entire Cabinet, save for Vice-President Moody Awori and Attorney-General Amos Wako.
He later sacked Mr Odinga and five other Cabinet ministers who had opposed the document.

Original_Search_Title: Local Producers Connected to Markets
Headline: Tanzania: Local Producers Connected to Markets
Body_Text:
A network has been established to link Tanzanian producers with developed world's buyers.
The facility's board chairman, Mr Deo Kafwa, said during the inauguration of the Tanzania Network for Fair Trade (Tanfat) at the weekend in Dar es Salaam that the aim was to undercut middlemen who had been exploiting producers.
"Tanfat wants groups of Tanzanian producers to sell their products directly to purchasers in the developed world. In the end, they will be able to sustain their businesses and improve product qualities and quantities as the markets demand," he said.
Twenty-five producer groups have joined Tanfat.
The fair trade (FT) initiative is not new in international business. It is an organised social movement and market-based approach to help producers in developing countries obtain better trading conditions and promote sustainability.
The movement advocates the payment of a higher price to producers as well as social and environmental standards. It focuses on exports from developing countries to developed countries. The transactions mainly involve handicrafts, coffee, cocoa, sugar, tea, bananas, honey, cotton, wines, fresh fruits, chocolate, flowers and gold.
FT certified sales worth $4.08 billion in 2008. In June 2008, the Fair-Trade Labelling Organisations International estimated that over 7.5 million producers and their families were benefiting from FT-funded infrastructure, technical assistance and community development projects.
However, Mr Kafwa said Tanzania was not one of the beneficiaries.
"Our starting point therefore should be to build capacity for producers to produce high-quality products and to be able to negotiate with buyers," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Legendary Simona Abdallah for Arusha Cultural Diversity Festival
Headline: Tanzania: Legendary Simona Abdallah for Arusha Cultural Diversity Festival
Body_Text:
Arusha — World famous Simona Abdallah, Denmark -based, female musician will kick off her maiden performance in Tanzania at a concert to be held in Arusha in September.
Simona is a lady singer who also plays the 'Darbuka,' this a traditional Goblet drum (Chalice drum or Doumbek) used mostly in music originating in the Middle East.
The goblet shaped Arabian Darbuka is normally regarded to be a music instrument to be played by 'males' only, but Simona is the first lady musician to break the tradition with an internationally acclaimed success.
Ms Abdallah will be performing on September 11 at an open-air, entrance-free, multi-cultural concert which is to be staged at the National History Museum (Boma) and Via-via grounds for four days. Ms Miriam Byekwaso the Arusha festival organizer The 'Arusha International Cultural Diversity Festival' is the northern zone's answer to Zanzibar's 'Sauti-za-Busara!'
The event starts from September 6 but Ms Abdallah is set to display her remarkable talents on Saturday, September 11, according to the event organizer Ms Miriam Byekwaso.
"The Alliance Françoise in Tanzania is sponsoring Simona's program here," said Miriam adding that other foreign embassies represented in the country are supporting the event in one way or another.
Confirming her participation in the AICD festival Ms Simona Abdallah stated via email that after performing at the 'Cultural Diversity' festival in Arusha that Saturday she would also play one show at the Alliance Française in Dar Es Salaam on the following Wednesday, 15th of September before connecting to Kenya and Uganda.
"The flight is booked for my solo tour in Tanzania which may also cover Uganda and Kenya. I am so excited," Simona said adding that she will however first perform in France (Vence and Grenoble) and Croatia (Zagreb) with the wonderful star, Natacha Atlas (Belgian singer), then at Mela festival in Oslo en-route here.
While in Arusha, Simona, famous for her internationally acclaimed hit single, 'Reach Out,' is expected to climb onto the Arusha stage from 6.00pm that Saturday evening of September 11. Born in 1979 in Germany, from Palestinian parents, Simona plays Arabic percussion, but specializes on the Darbuka, a tradition music device always considered to be a masculine instrument.
Simona who is a self taught musician has been playing instruments since the tender age of 15. One of her goals is to record an album that fuses western music, rock, house and pop with the Middle Eastern rhythm
The Arusha International Cultural Diversity Festival will be staged on hybrid platforms, as one act performs on the National Natural History Museum arena, another will be making scene at the Via-via podium located on the other side of the venue.
Artists from Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, Malawi and Burundi as well as some international acts from overseas are expected to participate in the three-day event.
"We are torn between bringing Lady Jaydee and Banana Zorro both of whom play with real bands, so one of them will blaze the event here," said Miriam adding that when it comes to music acts, live performances are to be mostly considered.
Planned to take place at both the Arusha Natural History Museum (Boma) arena and on the adjacent Via-Via grounds from the 9th to 13th of September, the AICD festival will combine all; Cultural dances, Modern music, hip-hop, theatrical performances (drama) drawing, painting and sculpturing as well as yes disco.
More events apparently are going to be added to the program as time goes by, local artists will be given priority as the lineup expand to include performers from Denmark, Kenya, Uganda and Burundi including the popular female disco jokey from Uganda DJ Rachel Kungu. A 100 piece Maasai troupe from Oldonyosambu will kick off the show by parading around town before making the curtain-raiser dance at the venue.
Famous female sculptor Ms Mwandale Mwanyekwa from Bagamoyo together with the Arts and Craft trio, 'Big Mama Arts' organization will appear at the event as well as Mama Charlotte Arts of Maji-ya-Chai not to mention performances by Ms Ngoma Troupe based at TCDC Usa River.
The event will from this year henceforth be running under the 'Urithi Platform for Cultural Diversity' in Tanzania.

Original_Search_Title: 26 Hospitalised Over Possible Poisoning
Headline: Rwanda: 26 Hospitalised Over Possible Poisoning
Body_Text:
Rwamagana — At least 26 people are hospitalised at Rwamagana Hospital after drinking a suspected poisoned banana juice locally known as 'Umutobe.'
The victims, including seven men, three children and 16 women, were admitted on Monday. Medics said five are in critical condition.
The victims, all residents of Bibare Village in Kigabiro Sector, Rwamagana District, were discovered ill, on Monday morning, by their neighbours.
Emmanuel Nyirimanzi, a village local leader in charge of security told The New Times yesterday that they learnt with shock that three families were suffering with severe stomach pains.
He added that the victims were members of an extended family and immediate neighbours.
"We woke up in the morning, and all seemed okay, but we were surprised to be informed that some people were about to die. I later came to know that they drunk banana juice that could have been laced with poison," he said.
Blandine Nyirabizi, 20, one of those admitted said they suspect one of their neighbours to be behind the suspected poisoning.
"You see the container in which they prepared the juice was left outside the house for a night, to dry after washing. Someone must have taken advantage of that to contaminate it," she said.
Dr. Herman Kalyama, who is attending to the patients, said that all of them have similar conditions; diarrhoea and vomiting.
However, he said, majority of them were responding well to treatment. The results of their tests had not been released to confirm poisoning, he said.
"We have carried blood samples for testing but in the meantime, we can't discharge any of them," he said.

Original_Search_Title: The Priest VS the Legislator
Headline: Nigeria: The Priest VS the Legislator
Body_Text:
