Mr Mthethwa, who is also the chairman of the interministerial committee on xenophobia, said earlier this month that SA was not a "banana republic" and had the capacity to deal with the threats of violence. "By and large, these are criminal acts," he said.
Western Cape police spokesman Frederick van Wyk said yesterday that police had been deployed in several townships in the province.
He said although there were reports of violence and looting in some townships, the situation was under control. "Two shacks were burned in separate areas and sporadic incidents of looting and threats have been made against foreign nationals," he said.
Seven men aged between 19 and 30 were arrested in Nyanga township for public violence.

Original_Search_Title: Foreigners Leave SA As 'Vuvuzela' Blare Dies Down
Headline: South Africa: Foreigners Leave SA As 'Vuvuzela' Blare Dies Down
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — James Phiri is a disturbed man. The taxi driver operating in Johannesburg is a Zimbabwean who has been working in South Africa for the last 15 years.
Mr Phiri has made a fortune from the Fifa World Cup that ended here on Sunday with the final game between Spain and The Netherlands. But despite the boom in business, his future could not have been any bleaker, thanks to renewed fears of xenophobic attacks against foreigners.
Security has been great during the tournament, with the special "fast track" World Cup courts expediting justice swiftly and sternly, deterring criminals. With well-known hoodlums picked up and placed in police custody, the notorious streets of Hillbrow in Johannesburg couldn't have been safer.
But the fresh threats of xenophobic attacks, similar to those witnessed a couple of years back, are almost real now, with many foreigners packing their bags and leaving. "We have been receiving text messages telling us that there are free buses to take us back to Zimbabwe," Mr Phiri said.
"This is unfortunate because we have contributed a great deal to the South African economy. The loaf of bread I buy each morning and the money I use to buy phone airtime shouldn't be taken for granted," he said. On Saturday, Johannesburg Daily Sun reported that foreigners were already being hounded out of some areas like the township of Ramaphosa, east of Johannesburg.
"Just get out of town" screamed the tabloid's front page headline to a story that claimed residents of Ramaphosa had given foreigners an ultimatum to leave. "We met as residents and agreed that we will not assault foreigners, but we want them to go," the newspaper quoted a street vendor, Mr Mthethwa Nongwaza, saying.
Anti-foreigner riots flared up in 2008 when locals claimed foreigners were taking up their jobs. Official figures indicate that 62 people were killed in the attacks. "They are taking our jobs. They accept cheap labour rates. While we South Africans complain that the money is too little, they work for anything," Mr Nongwaza is quoted as saying.
The xenophobic attacks are mainly concentrated in the high-density townships and lower class residential areas where many Zimbabweans, Malawians, Mozambicans and Nigerians live. There is a considerable number of Kenyans here too, and despite assurances by Police minister Nathi Mthethwa that security will be beefed up, tension remains high in the Rainbow Nation.
An increased security presence, including army and air force patrols, has been noted in areas considered vulnerable, with some Somali traders fortifying their shops for fear of fresh attacks.
Banana republic
The minister is, however, quick to deny any involvement by the military. "We have law enforcement, which has to do with civilians, and the police will be deployed as and when this is needed," he told a press briefing on Wednesday. The South African authorities were at pains to assure foreigners that their country "is not a banana republic" and that fears of xenophobic attacks must be dismissed.
Ramaphosa was in the news in 2008 when a Mozambican, Ernesto Alfabeto Nhamauve was set ablaze, with images of the dying, burning man featuring prominently in the international press. There was a hint of post-World Cup attacks on Wednesday when about 50 locals looted a shop belonging to a Somali in the township of Khayelitsha, Cape Town, just after the city drew the curtain on its World Cup programme with a final match at the Green Point Stadium.
"Be ready for Xenophobia Two from Monday!" screamed a drunken Nigerian at a downtown bar in Hillbrow in the early hours of Sunday, indicating the fear that is rife here. The 2008 attacks were a sobering experience and many here hope that the success of the Fifa World Cup will not be blotted by renewed attacks on foreigners.

Original_Search_Title: Police Ready to Act on Xenophobic Threats, Says Minister
Headline: South Africa: State 'Prepared' for Xenophobic Attacks
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — THE government announced its readiness to deal with any outbreak of xenophobic violence yesterday, and claimed reports that scores of foreigners were leaving SA in fear for their lives were baseless.
Reports had said foreigners were leaving over rumours of attacks targeting foreign nationals after the Soccer World Cup ends on Sunday.
Chairman of the interministerial committee on xenophobia Police Minister Nathi Mthethwa said SA was not a "banana republic" and had the capacity to deal with the threats of violence. "By and large, these are criminal acts," he said during a media conference yesterday.
Mr Mthethwa dismissed talk of xenophobic attacks as the latest attempt to rob SA of the opportunity to celebrate its achievement in successfully hosting the World Cup.
The trend created negative perceptions such as that SA would not complete infrastructure projects in time for the World Cup and that the country would not be able to host a successful tournament because of crime and terrorist attacks.
"The goalposts keep shifting," said Mr Mthethwa, in an assertion reminiscent of "third force" theories after the xenophobic attacks in 2008 in which 62 people were killed.
Home Affairs Deputy Minister Malusi Gigaba said there had not been reports of people leaving SA for fear of violence. "We are taking these rumours seriously just because we don't want them to end up being a self-fulfilling prophecy."
Mr Mthethwa said an investigation had found that the scores of foreigners leaving Cape Town in the past few days were doing so because they were seasonal workers and not because they feared attacks.
Among those blamed for the rumours was the media. In some instances school children were being targeted "to become the bearers of the bad news", said Mr Mthethwa.
However, the fast-track policing and sentencing approach used during the World Cup would be applied to those accused of xenophobic attacks, he said.
He urged communities to "blow the whistle against any criminals that are disguised behind xenophobia".
Mr Mthethwa said the government was looking at intervening to ease tensions among local and foreign traders, often the cause of attacks. The intervention could come through bylaws to regulate an environment that often gave foreigners an unfair business advantage.
He denied reports that the army had been deployed to potential hotspots. "We have law enforcement which has to do with civilians and the police will be deployed as and when that is needed," he said.

Original_Search_Title: A Nation of Bad Losers?
Headline: Nigeria: A Nation of Bad Losers?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Your Excellency Sir, let us visit and re-visit the World Cup. Firstly Sir, we aware of the hypothesis about the ones paying the piper needing to have the chance - once in a while - to decide what tune is to be played. We at Planet Sport never doubted that this is important. In fact, we believe more than government could ever do that governments should be more involved in the decision making processes of sports than they currently are, as it would stem the rate at which funds tend to be misapplied in the sector. It is this our belief that had made us to be the champions of having sitting governors made chairmen/presidents of sports associations/federations.
So, if you had cared to ask us - and when I say us, I am referring to the corps of sportswriters and sports editors - you would have found that 99 percent of us are in favour of reining in the body that calls itself the Nigeria Football Federation; and at least 75 percent of us would have told you the set of officials that make up the board are just jobbers and have neither vision for, nor mission, in the sport.
Again, if we had been taken into confidence, we would have let you know the repercussions of having to embark on the course of action that you have. Yours is not a junta; it is an elected government. As such, it cannot afford to be found making the kind of decisions that leaves the nation to get classed with either banana kingdoms or pariah nations.
Also, have you called us in to ask for our opinion on how to solve the problem; we would have let you know how easy it is. And I believe a step in the right direction was taken at the weekend with the sacking of the leadership of the house in the president, Sani Lulu Abdullahi and First Vice-president Amanze Uchegbulam.
Actually, what we would have advised is that since the election into the board was only weeks away, the government should just have selected its men for a new board and put into place an election committee of its own choosing. Presto, you have a new board full of candidates of government's choosing. Very simple. And all of these acrimonies, threats and bad blood would have been avoided. It would have been the astute usage of one stone to kill two birds; the third method and preferred manner of wreaking victory, according to all the gospels on the art of war, particularly that propagated by Sun Tzu in the procurement of victory without fighting a battle.
Leaders of Nigeria should stop putting this country in the class of the world's lesser nations with decisions that do not befit the class of noblesse oblige nations. Our Leaders should be seen doing the things, and making the kind of decisions, those called 'World Powers' do.
Mr. President, do you think the likes of Angela Merkel of Germany, David Cameron of Great Britain, Barak Obama of the United States, Nicolas Sarkozy of France would have made this kind of intolerant decision of taking their nations out of a global comity for the "flimsy" excuse of inadequate performance?
These are the kind of countries that Nigeria's leadership should be emulating in acts and style of governance. Unfortunately for us in the past, our leaders have been doing everything but what leaders of the modern world would be found doing. Take for instance, Vice-chancellor of Germany, Merkel, was in South Africa at the weekend as a guest of the president of South Africa, David Zuma, for the Germany-Argentina quarter-final match. Do you think if it were Germany that were at the end of the humiliation that Argentina got, Merkel would have walked out on Zuma and declared war on Argentina or pulled her country out of FIFA?
Nigeria's decision is the height of intolerance and it is the reflection of our home grown politics, and social and tribal leanings. We are a nation that has yet to learn the art of humility, either in victory or defeat. This is why every election in the country always ends up in rancour. We are pathological bad losers.
Let's look at it this way, Your Excellency, in case you do not believe we are a nation of bad losers.
In football, we do not have a bigger reputation than France or England. Yet these two countries have gone home from South Africa in manners that smirk of disgrace. In the case of France, former world champions, they left at the same time that Nigeria did and with statistics similar to that of Nigeria; one draw two defeats. In their case, they only managed to score one goal, while Nigeria actually managed three. But neither France nor England had done anything rash.
Argentina, despite their good run in the series, have ended their World Cup adventure with the worse defeat they had ever suffered in their lives at the Finals. But the president of their country has not been threatening fire or brimstone. Don't you see how this our mis-decision shock-a-thon has now turned the villains of our World Cup campaign to angels? The players must be thumbing their noses at us and saying, look at France, look at Argentina now, look at England. It is only Nigeria that wants to commit suicide.
In fact, Your Excellency Sir, the only thing that separates us from the Iraq of Saddam Hussein's era when national team footballers are flogged in public, or locked up inside spiked barrel for failing to win matches is that we do not have these boys at home here in Nigeria to haunt, otherwise, we probably would have had them flogged too, or locked up in some Gashua cells.
Another annoying part in this decision is that we daily see more nation-degrading performances being put up by politicians. We see members of the House (of Representatives) go up in arms and fight battles on the floor of the House in front of camera. Yet, no one has said they do not deserve to exist. No presidential order has come to scrap them. But in football, we managed to lose two matches, and the future is scrapped for over one million players, over 30 million Nigerians who depend on the dollars repatriated yearly by players in diaspora.
Or what was our dear revered Governor Rotimi Amaechi thinking when he was conspiring this mis-advice? Did he put all the issues involved on the burner to see how they pan out under heat? Did he realise that without us playing football that no new players would be produced that would be good enough for the top clubs of the world to notice? Did he realise that even if we come back after two years that we would not have the quality players to make a good national team for the next ten years?
But Your Excellency Sir, these are not the most annoying in the issues of this ban.
After Governor Amaechi managed to wreck the single most important object of love of Nigerians, we thought he should have known he owes Nigerians an explanation; but instead, what does he do? He ran back to his primary constituency - politics - to take refuge, and pretend he does not know that a sector he knows next to nothing about, but which he had dabbled into and sent to the gallows, is now about to suffer the hanging sentence that awaits it as a results of his sinister decisions.
Last weekend, instead of Governor Amaechi calling sportswriters around to rationalise his presidential mis-advise shock-a-thon, he pretended that nothing was amiss in football land and ran to his political constituency - via an executive briefing session for political editors - for neutralising publicity. In the United States, spin doctors called this 'damage control'.
In case anyone thinks there is nothing wrong with wanting to talk to his original constituency, which he had abandoned for so long in pursuit of his sporting jobbery, such people should remember that this governor; by the time he had done with the game - our game, which we hold so dear to our hearts - it was in tatters and left for dead at the graveyard.
So, for single-handedly arranging the demise of football in Nigeria, anyone with any iota of commonsense would have thought the least this our very own footballing 'Dr. Kevorkian' owe us is an explanation as to why the game deserved the mercy killing he has supervised for it. After all, for every splendid or asinine decision his task force made in the days that it was the super star of the game in the country; he had had the decency to bring sportswriters around to explain the rationale. So, why now is he loathe to do what he had been doing when the going was smooth and all yummy?

Original_Search_Title: Nwodo's Emergence Puts Anambra PDP Gladiators Guessing
Headline: Nigeria: Nwodo's Emergence Puts Anambra PDP Gladiators Guessing
Body_Text:
Onitsha — Dr. Okwesilieze Nwodo, the new national chairman of Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) has a herculean task not only to restore sanity to already battered and abused party, factionalized along various interests but also to bring the much talked but lacking internal democracy within the party.
Nwodo, former Governor of Old Anambra State and the founding Secretary of the party, himself has assured that he would restore sanity and internal democracy to the party.
In as much as the Ukehe Prince would want to reposition the party, keen observers of PDP politics have been expressing doubt as to how he would succeed in this ardous task, given varied and complex interplay of political forces in PDP.
But even if Nwodo tames all other political forces within PDP, many, you may call them pessimists or realists would swear that only one state and that is Anambra, would pose a hard nut for him to crack.
In fact, this school of thought will want Nwodo to be cautious of Anambra State PDP crises and different dramatic personae involved in the crises. They were eager to point out to Nwodo and his aides to note that former chairmen of the party, notably, Chief Audu Ogbeh and his immediate predecessor, Eze Vincent Ogbulafor, both had their fingers burnt in Anambra PDP crises. It is believed that if Nwodo try as much as possible to avoid the banana peel that is Anambra PDP crises, or be as fair and just as possible, he will have fewer headaches in his tenure.
However, that is the dilemma or even the litmus test a lot of political observers of Anambra PDP crisis said Dr. Nwodo will be facing in few weeks or months to come. If he wants to sanitize the party and brought in internal democracy, can he succeed in Anambra chapter which has become a problem child of the entire party, given the peculiar nature of Anambra State politics and the penchant by key actors to hijack the party structure for their selfish ends?
In a state where their mercantile tradition has been brought into politics, governance and even civil service, which is typified in PDP, can Nwodo delicately succeed in resolving the crises without roughing the feathers or stepping on toes?
This is given the manner of his emergence as the national chairman of the party and the caliber and characteristics of the prominent members of the party in the state. People who believe that the party does not belong to all but to few of them as their own share of Nigeria's commonwealth in which they commercialize every position and appointments.
Former Speaker of old Anambra state, Chief Ifanyichikwu Enechukwu, inhis analysis of PDP crises, said that the problem they had in the state PDP was an attempt by some people to balkanize the party for their personal interests.
This in effect has resulted in the emergence of several factions in the party; while some others who do not have the capacity and resources to get involved in the rigours and intrigues left the party out of frustration. Some also left the party to get back at the people who have the structure and ultimately, the party becomes the loser.
This glaringly came to fore when over forty- five aspirants contested for the party primaries of the 2009 governorship election under the platform of the party. In fact, it became practically impossible for various hierarchies of the party to conduct successful primary, leading to the picking of Prof. Chukwuma Soludo as the flag- bearer of the party.
But instead of backing him as the party's choice, whether right or wrong, he got more strident opposition from his own party; with some pulling out to contest in other parties against their own candidate.
Prominent among them were Chief Andy Uba, who left for Labour Party (LP), Prince Nicolas Ukachukwu who joined Hope Democratic Party (HDP) and Uche Ekwunife who is strongly believed to have been backed by Senator Annie Okonkwo moved to Peoples Progressives Alliance (PPA).
Andy's younger brother, Chief Chris Uba although did not move out of the party, was said to have thrown his wait behind the incumbent governor, Mr. Peter Obi of All Progressive Grand Alliance (APGA). All these though staunch members of PDP worked assiduously to ensure that PDP did not emerge winner in that election.
If Nwodo does not move fast to resolve the crisis within Anambra PDP, similar sabotage, back-stabbing, intrigues and open opposition of party's candidates may be the order of the day in 2011 election. And that is why Nwodo should stand firm on his promise to sanitize and democratize the party.
How the new chairman will bring all these key players under one roof and one round table to resolve all the nutty issues will be the major task for Nwodo in Anambra State PDP and a lot of people are closely monitoring his steps and how he will arrive at acceptable and amicable resolution of Anambra PDP crises.
His major set-back however, is President Jonathan Goodluck's connection with some key players in the crises. It is clear that the emergence of Dr. Nwodo was facilitated and made possible by the President and Commander-In-Chief, Dr. Jonathan Goodluck himself. For inspite the opposition from certain quaters in South East even from his home state, Enugu which resulted in the emergence of candidates against him, the President was said to have remained resolute on Nwodo, which eventually paved the way for him.
Former President Olusegun Obasanjo is generally believed to be President Jonathan's mentor and he tends to defer to him in many issues to avoid a collision course with Obasanjo. The leader of one of the faction in Anambra PDP, Dr. Andy Uba, former Senior Special Assistant on Domestic Matters, to Obasanjo when he was President, is an arch loyalist of his former boss. The former President has never hidden his interest to foist Andy Uba on Anambra people. Andy Uba believed rightly or wrongly that PDP structure in the state belongs to him having being the governorship candidate of the party in 2006. His major grouse against Ogbulafor and Soludo was according to sources, was that they denied him what was rightly belonged to him by refusing to give him the ticket of PDP in 2009.
It is already speculated in the state that with the relationship his boss has with Jonathan and the support given to Nwodo during the his emergence as the National Chairman of PDP, he would be influenced by President Jonathan and his mentor Obasanjo to hand over the structure of the party to Uba.
This is because, many believe that the emergence of Jonathan is an extension of Obasanjo or his third term agenda. Jonathan like Uba are Obasanjo's boys and it is said that it is their caucus that brought Nwodo. Therefore, he would be handicapped to refuse to toe their order.
Chief Chris Uba, a member of PDP Board of Trustee (BOT), is the younger brother of Andy Uba, but for sometime now his political interest is believed to be antithetical to that of Andy, thus leading to sharp disagreement, which made each of them to pursue their respective political agenda, independent of each other. But blood, pundits in the state said is always ticker than water and when the chips are down, the Uba brothers will come together to resolve their differences so as to wrestle the structure of PDP from others to ultimately recapture power in the state. In fact, many believe that the so called differences between Andy and Chris is a political gimmick, insisting that both of them have the same agenda, especially with President Obasanjo as their common godfather.
If the PDP structure is eventually given to the Ubas as many of their supporters are already speculating in the state, then where will the party consign gladiators like Senator Okonkwo, Prince Ukachukwu, Prof. Soludo and of course, Dr. Alex Ekwueme, former Vice-President and ex-BOT chairman among many other party stalwarts and their supporters in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Priests Warned Over Child Abuse
Headline: Kenya: Priests Warned Over Child Abuse
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Catholic priests who molest children will be surrendered to the police even as they are punished by the church.
This was the no-nonsense message delivered by Nairobi Archbishop John Cardinal Njue on Wednesday when he announced new rules in the face of complaints of misbehaviour by priests.
The guidelines require each diocese to draw up child protection policies against priests who sexually molest them. At a celebration of Holy Mass to end "The Year of the Priests" at Nairobi's Holy Family Basilica, he said:
"The policies will have direct involvement and collaboration with civil authorities in criminal cases such as rape and paedophilia."
"A cleric accused of any moral impropriety of a sexual nature, especially the abuse of minors, will attract penalties as stipulated by the Church law, not excluding dismissal from the clerical state," Cardinal Njue said.
He, however, added: "The misdeeds of a cleric should not be used as an excuse to attack the entire church."
"As a church, we cannot tolerate any act that harms and violates the rights, the dignity and integrity of any person perpetuated by any member of the clergy," he said.
The measures come at a time when cases of rape and child sexual abuse by the priests are rife nationally. Claims of sodomy in the US are also common.
In Kisii, a priest has been accused of defiling a schoolgirl only minutes after giving her bananas and oranges for her sick mother in hospital and praying for her.
Archbishop Njue also urged clerics unable to live the life of celibacy to leave the priesthood, "and in fairness acknowledge that it was one's personal choice".
The copies of the regulations were later handed to the bishops of the church. On the proposed constitution, Cardinal Njue said there was still time to amend contentious issues.
"There is time for dialogue, but not dialogue to buy time before the referendum," he said. Cardinal Njue told the congregation that the issues of abortion, gay marriages and kadhi courts need to be sorted out.

Original_Search_Title: Kifumbira - the Slum Where Kakama Met His Death
Headline: Uganda: Kifumbira - the Slum Where Kakama Met His Death
Body_Text:
Kampala — For almost two weeks, Sven and Naomi Karekaho were hurting after their son, Kham Kakama, mysteriously disappeared. Kakama was kidnapped from home in Bugolobi on June 8.
The search for the one-and-a-half-year-old boy ended on a sad note when his decomposing body was found 12 days later next to a garbage skip, about 3km from Kamwokya market.
One of the suspects in the murder, Godwin Tumusiime, led the Police to Kifumbira in Kamwokya, a Kampala suburb, where they found the body surrounded by burnt cables and banana peels.
The Police are puzzled why the kidnappers murdered the boy and then asked for a sh30m ransom from his parents.
Kifumbira
A small path along a filthy water channel leads to the shanty densely populated slum of Kifumbira where one of the suspects, Brian Sajjabi Jr, lived in a rented single-room house.
Tumusiime, who was arrested in Ishaka, Bushenyi district, led the Police to Sajjabi's room in Kifumbira Kisenyi II at the border of Kamwokya and Kyebando, a suburb in Kawempe division.
Sajjabi's room is behind the Government-aided Kampala City Council Primary School.
George Kivumbi, a resident of the slum, says its name (Kifumbira) is derived from the large number of Bafumbira from south-western Uganda, who settled in the area decades ago. The place is now inhabited by all tribes.
Among them are Bakonjo, Bakiga and Baganda. Most inhabitants get attracted to the area because of the low cost of living. Residents pay between sh20,000 and sh60,000 per month for a one-room house. The money also caters for power.
Food is also sold cheaply. For example, a plate of matooke, rice and beef costs sh1,000. Chapatti with beans, popularly known as kikomando, goes for sh500.
Most of the houses are makeshift and small corridors separate them. Many of the houses are in poor condition with leaking roofs. There are stinking pit latrines next to houses. The place is also littered with garbage and it floods during the rainy season. Some residents, especially women, have turned their rental rooms into bars and staggering drunkards are a common sight in the area.
Most of the residents are market vendors, casual labourers or hawkers. Many of them sell charcoal, and at some stalls, women seem unbothered as flies hover over dry fish and other foodstuffs sold near garbage heaps.
A big water channel marks the border of Kawempe and Kampala Central divisions.
Residents dump garbage and human waste in the channel and the rain washes the litter down into Kyebando swamp where Kakama's body was recovered.
Poor security
Kawempe Division resident district commissioner Edward Ssekabanja says there are about 360,000 people in the slum, part of which is in Kawempe Division and the other part in Kampala Central Division.
He says the poor security in the area is due to laxity by local leaders. Everybody minds their own business and loud music blares from joints selling local brew.
Ssekabanja adds: "Residents do not care what their neighbours do until trouble comes."
Kifumbira is notorious for selling local brew, marijuana smoking and petty crime, says a detective, who used to work at Kira Road Police Station.
Local officials say the crime rate had gone down due to vigilance of security organs and local leaders, but it has started rising again due to laxity.
The defence secretary, Christopher Mugisha, notes that cases of house-breaking and theft are resurfacing in the area.
Who is Sajjabi?
Sajjabi's landlord, Said Muhoozi, describes him as a reserved person who hardly had friends. He says Sajjabi once had a stay-in girlfriend, but the two separated.
He adds that he had been looking for Sajjabi to pay rent arrears amounting to sh60,000.
Mugisha says Sajjabi told him he was a student at Makerere University.
"I thought he was a student who wanted to save money. Many students rent cheap houses instead of hostels to make ends meet," Mugisha notes.
Solomon Mukonjo, the pastor of the church where Sajjabi prayed, said he suspended him from the church when he got complaints from a parent that he had impregnated her daughter and eloped with her.
He disclosed that contrary to what the residents said, Sajjabi had never been a minister in the church.
Mukonjo added that Sajjabi was stopped from playing the piano in church because he was not a permanent member.
When the suspects were arrested, irate residents of Kifumbira wanted to lynch them, but the Police intervened.
The Police recovered over 10 simcards of different mobile telecommunications networks in Sajjabi's house.
The Police are still investigating the murder.

Original_Search_Title: Jonathan's Take On Presidency Should Be Performance-Driven
Headline: Nigeria: Jonathan's Take On Presidency Should Be Performance-Driven
Body_Text:
Lagos — Former Abia State PDP Chairman, Reverend Benson Ezem is campaigning for Jonathan's presidential contest in 2011 but insists the agitation must be driven by performance. He also canvasses for a participatory democracy that should throw up the right candidates in the country.
How would you assess Jonathan's administration so far?
I want to believe it is rather too early to appraise President Goodluck Jonathan's achievements in office after just a few months. However, unfolding events and certain positive decisions taken by his administration are making the citizens feel the presence of a government. There are so many actions going on now in the presidency. And the administration seems to have chosen its areas of priority, namely: electoral reform, power, war against corruption and the Niger Delta.
Although the government has still not jettisoned the 7-point agenda of late Yar'Adua presidency, current facts point to the direction that areas of priorities have been identified. You can check out the issue of the appointment of the INEC Chairman. The president has in different fora both at national and international levels said that his administration would give Nigerians a credible, fair and free election. Let us believe him on this because he has demonstrated it by the appointment of Professor Attahiru Jega.
I believe he is focused on this area of giving us a credible election. It has been my opinion that no nation can survive, sustain and maintain national cohesion without credible and accepted elections. This has been the cause of lack of confidence in government by the people. Because most of our so-called representatives are not elected by the people, they are not the true reflection of the people's aspirations and there is therefore this lack of sincerity of purpose. It is only through credible election that we can unlock the full potentials of the Nigerian people.
What are your expectations of the President and what do you think he should be doing to win the admiration of the electorate?
In the first place he has earned the admiration of the people. The president, given his humane disposition, has already earned the love and admiration of the Nigerian people. Where he will shoot himself on the foot is when he falls below the expectation of the people. Now, everybody is in full support of his presidency.
The Nigerian people want him to succeed. So he has to do all he can to write his name in gold. I think a credible election and improved power are all we desire now. Also the fight against corruption must not be underrated. Our nation cannot move forward when millions of our petro-dollars are being stolen daily by our leaders. We still have so many corrupt persons in authority and until such people are fished out and made to face the law, I doubt if the people will believe any crusade against corruption. In as much as the president himself has vowed to stamp out corruption, we demand and desire that he watches those people that come to him.
The argument is on-going as to whether Jonathan should contest the Presidential election of 2011;
what do you think?
This should not be a source of worry to us, rather we should ask ourselves what the constitution says on this. Does he have a right to contest as a sitting President? What does the party constitution say? In the exercise of his fundamental human rights, the president is free to contest. President Jonathan possesses the requisite constitutional requirement to contest. It is, as a matter of fact his inalienable human right. This has only become an issue because of the so called zoning arrangement in my party. The North is saying it's its turn, while the South is saying the same thing. I think the people that conceived this idea of zoning did it to correct the imbalance in the polity occasioned by the long military presence in the country with the leaders that emerged from that arrangement coming largely from the North. But our democracy is advancing and we should allow the best candidates to also emerge.
Do you share the popular view that there is much insincerity in the preparation of the budget at the executive and legislative levels?
Nigerians are very wasteful; unaccountability is fast becoming part of us. Our leaders are not accountable.
Corruption can only be minimised if we collectively decide to be responsible leaders; we must know the character and pedigree of the people we are putting into office from the councilors, Local Government Chairmanmen to our National Assembly members. A situation where you have inexperienced and corrupt people coming into office, people who have not had any credible working experience being imposed on the electorate by their godfathers, who want to fester their moneybags through contracts and outright looting of the treasury is not acceptable.
There is need to create the right enabling environment for the electorate to elect honourable representatives, people of their choice that will be held accountable by them if they are perceived to derail in their promise to deliver the democratic dividends, in their manifesto. What we see now is people who get elected with nothing and within one year they are buying houses and cars and generally living in opulent circumstances, forgetting their constituencies.
With regard to the attitude of legislators, regarding constituency projects as an extension of their banana peels, how should this matter be approached?
The legislators should concern themselves with their legislative functions and allow due process in the award and execution of the constituency projects. Effective monitoring by the legislature of these projects and their constituencies will ensure that the projects are delivered on time and in accordance to project specifications.
And what about their jumbo loans, do you think it is justifiable especially since this is the taxpayers' money; relate this to what obtains in civilized democracies such as Britain and the USA and the clamour for part time legislators instead of this very costly full time legislative work?
Infact the legislative assignment should not be full time, even as it stands today, most of our legislators treat it as part time. The amount of money that is spent on running government is too much when compared with other countries. We as a country have a duty to make things work. We are spending so much on frivolities, while the infrastructure is suffering.
As a former state Chairman of the PDP in Abia State, what was the experience like and how does the larger constituency of the party stand to benefit from that experience?
When I was the State Chairman of PDP in Abia State, I ensured by the grace of God and the unrestricted permission of my leader, Chief Onyema Ugochukwu that the right thing was done. He insisted on my doing the right thing without interference that no party officer was imposed. There was participatory democracy and all leaders were asked to go back to their wards no matter their positions or ranking to elect the party officers. This was strictly adhered to and it gave rise to the EXCO today in Abia State of which Chief Ndidi Okereke is the Chairman. Some of the local government Chairmanmen and the state executive, I've not met before were sworn into office through this democratic process. Things can really work well in Nigeria if we as leaders decide to do the right thing.
As we proceed to the 2011 elections, what are the issues on ground?
The electorate is tired of fake and unfulfilled promises from the aspirants who eventually get elected. The 2011 elections should be approached differently; aspirants especially at the gubernatorial level should be able to outline their programme of action so they can be held accountable for non-performance. They should have a firsthand knowledge of the developmental needs in their states from wards to local governments and the state in general. Let us know how your governance is going to bring about a change, a transformation and how it is going to impact on the leadership, the women and youths.
What are you going to do to improve the poverty indices of your state? A time has come for the electorate to demand for quality and transparent leadership from their leaders. The leaders should be held responsible and accountable; a leader should show compassion, he should be involved in the life of the people. The release of funds monthly to states should be reconsidered because it has not translated to a meaningful development of most states.

Original_Search_Title: Firm Earns U.S.$30,000 in Furniture Fair
Headline: Angola: Firm Earns U.S.$30,000 in Furniture Fair
Body_Text:
Luanda — The Angolan artesanal company MPK won about USD 30,000 in the Fair of Furniture, Decoration, Illumination and Textile-home, held from on July 24-27 in Luanda, said the official of the firm, Maria de Fátima Santos.
Maria de Fátima, who was speaking on the sidelines of the fair closing ceremony, said that her firm exhibited some exotic materials and work with natural products, such as Bamboo, banana leafs, coconut palm fibre and natural coco.
During the four days event, he explained, the firm showed over 20 decoration artefacts and established contacts with individualities involved with hotels issues.

Original_Search_Title: World Cup Teaches Spirit of Tolerance
Headline: South Africa: World Cup Teaches Spirit of Tolerance
Body_Text:
Polokwane — If the entertainment of the World Cup is supposed to be about football, this year it has almost been drowned by music, dance and art, not counting the self perpetuating controversy of the sound of the vuvuzela.
Immediately after South Africa was chosen to host the tournament six years ago famous local musicians who would have been expected to light up the opening ceremony met their end in quick succession, sometimes quite dramatically. As we mourned Brenda Fassie, Miriam Makeba, Jabu Kanyile, Lebo Mathosa, Tiny, TK and more recently Busi Mhlongo it was never in doubt that new talent would rise to the occasion.
The opening ceremony was peppered with performances and recordings of an impressive array of emerging and seasoned artistes. During the choreographic display I relished the heart warming popular 'Shibobo' that was released by TKZ to fire up Bafana Bafana during the 2002 World Cup. To further heighten my excitement as a resident of Limpopo,a large group of shy blanket-clad women dressed in Ndebele, xiTsonga and tshiVenda ethnic wear sashayed around a steaming calabash as they swept the floor to Sipho (hotstix) Mabuse's nostalgic early 90s quick beat Shangaan rendition.
Those three ethnicities were the most despised when I first arrived in South Africa, being relegated to foreign intrusion from Zimbabwe and Mozambique. The world community warmly embraced South Africa by entrusting us with the responsibility of hosting the important game.
As that goodwill was consummated when the world along with its celebrities came to our door this June and dwelt among us, the mainstream South African citizenry acknowledged its less glamorous kin from the north. Not to be left out the school children danced the specially contrived soccer moves of the 'diski', while colourfully dressed youth did gymnastic calisthenics.
The numerous blunders in defence have resulted in many spectacular goals being scored during the soccer matches, attesting to the enduring need to practice and refine one's skills.
If only the playmates we kicked banana fibre balls with had the grace to refrain from the incessant repertoire of rude tautological reprimands every time I lost a ball to an opponent, perhaps I too would have stayed in it long enough to hone the never-tested football acumen that is now only good for a TV coach. How many would-be Ugandan soccer stars were browbeaten in this manner into seeking less stringently critiqued enterprises we will never know.
Today we tolerate the mishaps and enjoy the game, celebrating passionately with total strangers, who we fall over, hug and kiss without reservation whenever the ball finds the back of the net.
Secretly I am ambivalent over the emphatic triumph of Ghana over the USA. My hopes as an African have been resurrected by that win, which has brought pride and exuberance back into the games for the ordinary nonchalant man on the streets of our beloved mother continent.

Original_Search_Title: Family Cries Out for Justice Over Girl Raped By Catholic Priest
Headline: Kenya: Family Cries Out for Justice Over Girl Raped By Catholic Priest
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A Catholic priest has been accused of raping a schoolgirl only minutes after giving her bananas and oranges for her sick mother in hospital and praying for her.
The priest, who was serving at Gekano Parish in Kisii Diocese in November last year when the incident reportedly occurred, then tried to swear the 16-year-old girl to secrecy with a promise of education.
A senior administrator in the diocese, also a priest, confirmed that a complaint had reached the bishop's office over the matter, and said investigations were ongoing.
But the girl's family suspects a cover-up plot, citing the fact that the accused priest has since been moved from the diocese and the conduct of another priest who later visited their home to collect the clothes worn by the girl at the time she was allegedly raped.
The family has also accused the police of inaction even though they reported the matter to the Keroka police station.
The Class Seven girl is said to have gone to the priest's house to return the plastic bag in which she carried the fruit to her mother who was at St Elizabeth Gekano health centre about 70 metres from where he lived.
The woman had been admitted with malaria on November 23 last year, and her daughter was taking care of her.
The priest visited the sick woman to pray with her between 8 a.m. and 9 a.m. the following day.
"After praying he asked me if I had taken any food, but I said that I had no appetite. He asked me if I would take some bananas and oranges.
"He said that he had some fruits in his house which he wished to give to me. I accepted. But the priest instructed my daughter to make sure that she returned the plastic bag," the mother told the Sunday Nation this week.
"Within a short time the girl had brought a bunch of bananas and two oranges. I ate one and gave one to the nurse," she said.
The mother of six said that after removing the fruit her daughter returned the bag to the priest's house as instructed.
"She stayed there for long, and I started getting worried where she was. She was supposed to go home and prepare lunch for her siblings, and yet the keys for the house were on my bed," the mother said.
"She did not come back and instead went straight home. In the evening I decided to leave hospital and go home and asked her why she returned without taking the keys, but she kept quiet."
She said the following day her daughter opened up and told her what had happened.
"While in the house he asked me why my parents were always fighting, but I kept quiet," the girls said. "He then asked me to allow him touch my breast, but I declined again as I got frightened."
Warned her
She said the priest forced himself on her, tore her clothes and then defiled her. The girl said the priest then promised to assist her to pursue her education and warned her not to disclose to any person what happened as people would laugh at him.
Veronica Ogato, a nurse at the health centre, said she, too, suspected that something bad had happened. She recalled that when the priest came she was attending to the mother of the victim.
"He came in, greeted us all and prayed, then he left with the girl," she said. The nurse said she grew worried when the girl stayed for long in the priest's house as the mother was complaining.
"I later saw the girl seated next to the fence of the church in tattered clothes, using her sweater to cover her torn clothes. I sensed that something must have gone wrong," Ms Ogato said.
"She looked depressed and stressed, but I did not tell the sick mother," she said. "At 4 p.m. I passed by her parents' place and asked to be allowed to talk to her. I came with her to my house and persuaded her to tell me what happened. She opened up and narrated to me what had happened."
She said the girl told her that a "bad man" had torn her clothes off and defiled her. The priest then gave her Sh100 as a bribe to silence, her but she refused to take it.
"As a nurse, the following day I decided to give her an injection so that she did not conceive as it was three days after her monthly period," Ms Ogato said.
The family said the girl at one time even attempted suicide after her schoolmates began to tease her. The parents moved her to a different school. The family has also become the laughing stock of the village, mocked by villagers who accuse them of "exchanging their daughter for a bunch of bananas and two oranges". The mother said that a day after the rape, the suspected priest visited her home, prayed for her and gave her the sacrament.
On November 26, the girl's mother claimed that Father Zachary Bikeri, the priest in charge of Gekano Parish, visited their home to purportedly ask for forgiveness over the incident on behalf of the priest.
"We prayed together, and after prayers he had a written statement which he asked my husband, daughter and me to append our signature to. The statement said the church was going to act on the allegations, and so we obliged.
"He then took the clothes that my daughter had worn when the incident occurred to take to the bishop so appropriate action could be taken against the priest," the mother said.
Delayed to report
She said they believed that justice would be done and that was why they did not report the matter to police.
The girl's mother said when she threatened to raise the alarm, her daughter's clothes were returned to her on April 12, but they had been cleaned.
The Sunday Nation made several attempts to obtain a comment by phone from the accused priest on June 11 but without success. "I am on the road and will call you back ... I will call you back please," he said and then switched off his phone.
Fr Bikeri told the Sunday Nation that he could only talk to the girl's father.
"As you can see I am attending to another visitor, and you will have to wait or else you come another day," Fr Bikeri said and then went back to his house.
Father Jeremiah Nyakundi, the administrator who is third in line in the Kisii Catholic Diocese, admitted that the complaint had reached the bishop's office.
"We received the complaint about the defilement in December last year, and it was that time that the priest was moved in order to allow for investigations to go on," Fr Nyakundi said.
However, Father Nyakundi said only the Kisii Catholic Diocese Bishop Joseph Mairura was in a position to tell how far the investigations had gone.
"What I can confirm is that the priest is not in Kisii diocese as he was moved, and it is my hope that the case will be completed, but at the moment it is still an allegation," Fr Nyakundi said.
The bishop was out of office when we tried to contact him. The victim's father says he unsuccessfully sought a meeting with Bishop Mairura in January.
"On January 18, 2010, I went to see him but he did not meet me. I was told that he was busy, and I had to leave," the father said. Keroka police boss Ramson Lolmodoni said they did not know about the incident.
"I have tried to search for the case in our occurrence books, but I am unable to get it; even if he had reported, it was too late as we could not act," Mr Lolmodoni said.
The girl's father is bitter about the leadership of the church which he accuses of abandoning his daughter and protecting the priest.
"She was tormented, and we have not been able to get her professional counselling services due to lack of resources. This is the question I have been asking the leadership of the church: why are they doing this to us?" he said.
On March 11 this year, the victim's father went to local chief Jane Matara to report the incident. She advised him to report it to the police.
He said he went to Keroka police station on March 29 to report the matter, but the area OCS told him that it was too late to act on the case. "He (the accused priest) has been calling us and promising that he would pay for the education of our daughter in a boarding school provided we did not report the matter."
"And at the same time, we were to tell the bishop that all the allegations against him were lies so that he could compensate us, which we have declined," the victim's father said.

Original_Search_Title: Ngoma District to Privatise New Market
Headline: Rwanda: Ngoma District to Privatise New Market
Body_Text:
Ngoma — District authorities have finalised plans to privatise certain components of the newly constructed multi-million market in Kibungo town.
Ngoma Mayor, François Niyotwagira, confirmed the new move, in an interview with The New Times on Thursday.
He said that the district sunk in Rwf 471 million to complete the new market.
"It is a modern market that we want to use to improve the income of the district and its people. We are yet to open it officially because we intend to privatise most of its components for to ensure its proper management," he said.
The Mayor noted that the market is expected to boost the town's economy in many ways.
Charles Ntageruka, the Vice Mayor in charge of Economic Affairs, reiterated the importance of privatising the management of the market, saying that the district made the decision to leave some of the market's day to day management to those capable of doing so.
He said the district's long term objective is to maximise revenues, and that the new market would help them achieve their objective.
"We shall mainly privatise the VIP section which is actually more of a shopping arcade The other part can be managed by the district," he said.
With the completion of construction works, traders are eagerly waiting for its opening.
"I look forward to selling my bananas from there," said Jean Claude Ndahayo.
" I salute the district's leadership for making this happen,".

Original_Search_Title: Kidnapped Baby Killed,Three Arrested
Headline: Uganda: Kidnapped Baby Killed,Three Arrested
Body_Text:
Kampala — The one-and-half-year-old boy kidnapped from Bugolobi, an upscale Kampala suburb, three weeks ago, was yesterday found killed in a swamp in a Kamwokya slum.
Three people are being held in connection with the kidnap and murder of the boy, a son to Sven Karekaho of the Uganda Revenue Authority and Naome Karekaho, the spokesperson of the environment watchdog, NEMA.
The family was too devastated by the murder.
The decomposing body of Khan Kakama was discovered around 5:00pm in Kifumbira Kisenyi II at the border of Kamwokya and Kyebando in Kampala central division.
The body, dressed in a blue sports pair of shorts and a cream T-shirt was wrapped in polythene sack and dumped next to a garbage dump, 3km from Kamwokya market.
The body, which had a deep cut wound on the head, lay around a heap of burnt cables and banana peels in a wet thicket surrounded by latrines.
The Police said the private parts were chopped off.
A somber mood hang over the area as curious residents, including many children, watched in shock as the Police retrieved the body.
Police chief Maj. Kale Kayihura oversaw the exercise led by the commandant of the newly-formed special investigation unit, Hillary Odoch. The deputy head of CID, Moses Sakira, was also present.
Police snifter dogs kept the charging crowd at bay. The parents were not at the scene.
Kakama got lost on June 8 between 10:00am and 12:00pm when his parents were away at work.
Speaking to journalists at the scene, Kayihura said: "It has been a long search. I appreciate the efforts of the Police, as well as the other security operatives in this search."
Kayihura said two people had been arrested in connection with the murder, at moment being considered to be child sacrifice.
Earlier, the Police had arrested a house-help who allegedly handed over the boy to the kidnappers near the family home.
"Early this morning, we got the key suspect in Bushenyi. He gave us the hopes that the boy is still alive. He told us the boy is somewhere in Kampala. He took them to where the body was dumped," said Kayihura.
He only identified the suspects as 'Godwin' and 'Junior Brian' who is a resident of the area where the body was found. But the Police believe the case involved a racket of criminals whose motive has not been ascertained.
Kayihura said Odoch will head the investigations.
However, The New Vision learnt from Police sources that initially the kidnappers asked for sh30m which was handed over to them at a remote place in Bushenyi. After receiving the cash, the kidnappers went silent.
The security kept tracking them in Bushenyi and in the process received information that led to arrest of two people.
One of the suspects after receiving the ransom money went on a spending spree, raising suspicion in the community.
"He was picked up in Bushenyi where he was buying land and had bought a boda-boda bike. He admitted to kidnapping the baby but left him with his friend in Kyebando adjacent to the Northern Bypass. He admitted to having kept the child but he cried so much that he put a kaveera on his head to silence him. He then strangled him and threw the body in a sack in the swamp nearby. This was the day after the kidnap," Police sources said.
At time of the boy's kidnap, there were two house-helps. One of them, 21-year-old Molly Nabaasa, allegedly left home with the child claiming she was going to buy airtime. Nabaasa returned shortly after without the boy, claiming he had been kidnapped. But residents suspected the kidnappers and Nabaasa knew each other.
The second house-help informed the parents about the disappearance of the boy.
The kidnappers reportedly made contacts with the family asking for a ransom.

Original_Search_Title: The Shooting War is Yet to Be Signalled But the Dirty Fighting Has Already Begun
Headline: Tanzania: The Shooting War is Yet to Be Signalled But the Dirty Fighting Has Already Begun
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A hunting season of sorts has opened in Tanzania, and the hunters and the hunted are equally busy.
It's about election time, that moment every five years when the politicos go out and to search for that magic wand of the modern era, the vote.
It's the vote that takes one to the civic council, parliament or Ikulu, elevating one into prominence, power and glory.
It's the vote that confers on one the ability to make decisions affecting the lives of millions of people without necessarily fearing sanctions in cases of failure.
It's the vote that makes sages of dunces and magnates of paupers. The African vote, depend on it, is pure magic.
So it is sought with the single-mindedness and perseverance of the Argonauts in search of the Golden Fleece, and in this search no quarter is given.
Even though the national electoral authorities want us to believe that campaigns have not started - they are supposed to blow a whistle or something - already the long knives have been unsheathed and first blood has been drawn. The race is on.
As the hunters criss-cross the country, they are cajoling and promising when they are not threatening and punishing, depending on the mood of the moment and the balance of power in any given context.
We are already hearing complaints voiced by incumbents against unnamed interlopers conspiring and acting "to take my constituency," as if it were some chasse-garde, a private domain.
This is the time we are bound to hear politicians promise bridges where there are no rivers - these can also be provided - and blankets for sweltering Dar es Salaam.
Most will base themselves on what they call their party manifesto, usually a document containing a long wish list that must await some rich auntie to implement.
Little wonder then that a former president, straight after he was elected, declared his party manifesto impossible to implement.
Politics is about the only growth industry in the country, seeing as it employs more people than can be found in factories; literally anybody who deems himself unemployable can jump into the fray, either as an aspirant or as a "supporter," meaning cheerleader, heckler, fixer or bouncer.
Sangomas and soothsayers will not be left behind either.
At the very top, the presidential race, matters look disconcertingly tepid, because no one expects Jakaya Kikwete to face any serious challenge from within CCM or the opposition, although known protagonists are already shadowboxing for a shot at the office he will be vacating five years hence. The succession contest has commenced in earnest.
That contest, engaged mainly through proxies, will provide a central plank in the ruling party primaries, as 2015 presidential wannabes seek to cut their presumed adversaries' feet from under them well before the actual race begins.
The irony is that there will be grimmer fights within the ruling party than between it and the opposition.
The opposition, after all, can hardly muster enough candidates to stand in every constituency, let alone every ward.
The place to look for a real fight - and this will be mostly dirty - is within the CCM itself, a ponderous behemoth that is slowly crumbling under its own weight, shorn of any philosophical or ideological pretensions, kept on its feet only by control of government resources and the feebleness of an opposition that seems keen on prolonging its infancy.
Although the shooting war is yet to be signalled, there is no scarcity of banana skins being slipped under foes' feet, including heavyweights sponsoring candidates, even opposition ones, to stand against party colleagues, just to cut them down to size.
These internecine machinations call to mind the words of Aeschylus, the Greek historian and philosopher:
So in the Libyan fable it is told that once an eagle, stricken with a dart, said, when he saw the fashion of the shaft, with our own feathers, not by others' hands, are we now smitten.
Jenerali Ulimwengu, chairman of the board of the Raia Mwema newspaper, is a political commentator and civil society activist based in Dar es Salaam.

Original_Search_Title: 2011 - the Odds Against Babangida (2)
Headline: Nigeria: 2011 - the Odds Against Babangida (2)
Body_Text:
One of General Babangida's recent blunders is his alleged bashing of Nigerian youths. When confronted with a question as to why his generation would still love to be around the corridors of power instead of allowing the young generation of Nigerians to paddle the ship of state, Babangida was said to have responded that the youth could not lead because they lacked the requisite education to provide the kind of leadership the nation needed. Also, Babangida was recently quoted as saying that the victim of his political rascality and brigandage, Chief M.K.O Abiola, winner of the June 12, 1993 presidential election should be immortalized. This was an election which was annulled by IBB that eventually led to Abiola being arrested for demanding for his mandate and killed in detention,.IBB advocated merely that a democratic institution be named after Abiola.
Nigerians are, indeed, hard put to appreciate Babangida's hyperbole. Did you lure your friend to his untimely death in order to claim his mandate through the backdoor and ask for his immortalization?. But the Babangida's come - back bid is said to be predicated on exploiting Nigeria's massive poverty, the people's tendency to suffer amnesia and our inordinate generosity of spirit. Perhaps the richest aspirant that will mount the hustling, the retired infantry general is most likely to draw hugely from the goodwill he cultivated via his now infamous settlement syndrome and the army of wealthy "Babangida boys" which is reported to have stashed millions of dollars abroad. Besides, with the sad exit of such formidable contenders as General Shehu Musa Yar'Adua (who died in detention in December 1997), Babangida feels that there is no Northerner who can give him a fight in the political arena. It is on this score that he is busy bank rolling nocturnal meetings of Northern politicians to press for the PDP to respect an amorphous zonal arrangement for the presidency.
Yet, aside from his cunning disposition, the gap -toothed general has sturdy arms. He is said to be counting on his huge financial resources said to be in the region of billions in hard currency, to see him through a presidential election. He has also painlessly and effortlessly allegedly concluded plans to buy the media to manage his battered image. With such an outlay and a generous budget against the backdrop of massive poverty among Nigerians, Babangida hopes to distribute his largesse (a tendency he is adept at) to secure the presidency. Nonetheless, Nigerians harbour no fears about IBB's enormous stolen wealth, as they are no fools. Nigerians who are generally chafed at the sorry state in which the nation is today, no thanks to the evil promptings of Babangida and his cohorts, know how to pay him back.
In spite of Babangida's misrepresentation that Nigerian youth lack the training to offer inspiring leadership to the nation, they are educated enough to know that Babangida has nothing to offer. Although Nigeria has been reduced to a banana republic in which subsidized illiteracy is now part of an elaborate power game, Nigerians will no long accept barely educated murderers to toy once more with the collective destiny of their country. In the wake of Babangida's recent lampooning of Nigerian youthss, one radio announcer, perhaps an angry youth, was reported to have retorted: "since the past 17 years Babangida stepped aside, how many degrees has he earned apart from honorary doctor of debauchery? Having pocketed what could have been used to educate the youth, how did IBB expect the Nigerian youth to have acquired enviable education?. Yar'Adua entered Aso Rock with a Master's degree and Jonathan with a Ph.D. IBB must know that a standard has already been set" The radio announcer said it all.
Someone should inform Babangida that, from the United States to Great Britain, from France to Germany, brains and age are the unassailable clinchers these days as far as elections are concerned. His sundry excesses will haunt him like ghouls in a nightmare. So also shall the ghosts of Chief Abiola and General Yar' Adua who have now been reduced to dust by the venomous conspiracy of the "Evil Genius" and his late Khalifa. He should reflect on the fate of Pol Pot, Suharto, Papa Doc, Baby Doc, Mobutu, etcetera and realize that the end of history is the other side of mid-night. Many Nigerians are even saying that Babangida should not be allowed to participate in a process he subverted several times over. And they wonder what moral platform will Babangida mount to advertise his candidature.
But if he insists on running, since as they say, all manner of knives are invited to an elephant's funeral, let him come. He shall meet desperate Nigerian youth - members of the dangerous class he has created - with their daggers, bows and arrows. He shall meet pro-democracy activists who are ready to vote for him with stones. It was this group that fought him to a stand still and prompted his unwilling flight. He shall meet with the prognosis of guerrilla journalists who chased him out of Aso Rock and they will tell him that the presidency is no longer for foxes and annullers. Above all, Babangida should know that power wears out one who does not have it. Such is the intractable end of the history of an opportunist whose cunning and guile have triggered Nigeria's agonizing trip to doom.
Concluded

Original_Search_Title: Bad Weather Keeps Vegetable and Fruits Prices High
Headline: Uganda: Bad Weather Keeps Vegetable and Fruits Prices High
Body_Text:
Kampala — Although inflation has been dropping to single digit figures in the last few months, there seems to be no reflection of the same dip on the price of food items.
The current 4.4 per cent inflation has not translated to low food prices but has instead seen such prices rise due to scarcity, which is blamed on unfavorable weather conditions. Prices of fresh vegetables, fruits, bananas, and some cereals have risen.
According to Mr Christopher Musinguzi, a Nakasero Market vendor, prices have continued to rise in the second quarter of 2010 compared to the first quarter of the 2010. Fruit prices have increased with popular Markets like Nakasero, Kalerwe, Owino among others experiencing low supplies.
A pineapple or a kilogramme of passion fruits goes for as high as Shs1,500 and Shs3,800 respectively. With supply of fresh products dropping there has not been enough to supply increasing demand.
According to Mr Vincent Muhoozi, a price analyst based at Nakasero Market, there is a limited supply of fruits and vegetables, which has contributed to the increase in price, as a result of low production and market supply.
Mr Muhoozi said: "The main suppliers of fruits and vegetables are from Kabale, but at the moment they are not supplying because such supplies are out of season and traders have been forced to import from neighbouring countries, which translates into increased prices."

Original_Search_Title: Time for  New Law Now, Says Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: Time for  New Law Now, Says Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki has said the proposed constitution is the best and the time for Kenyans to have a new law is now.
He reminded Kenyans that he had been at the forefront of the constitutional making process since joining politics was convinced that the current set of laws was best for the country.
"We have amended the current constitution several times. It's now time we had a new one. But even as we press on with this, some of you think that the current draft was just picked from the roadside," said President Kibaki in an apparent reference to those opposed to it.
"Even though we know that it is not perfect, it is still good for this country. It will accelerate development and expand education opportunities for all the way it did to such countries like China and Singapore."
He was speaking Kyumbi trading centre, where the Yes team made a stopover on its way to a rally in Machakos Town, Eastern province Friday.
Olive branch
And with only 45 days remaining before the August 4 referendum, the leaders, who included Prime Minister Raila Odinga and Vice President Kalonzo Musyoka urged Kenyans to vote for the proposed constitution as it would unveil the "new face of Kenya".
Even as they drummed up support for the document, they extended an olive branch to those opposed to the document to join them and pass the new law for the sake of the country.
"Let us talk to those opposed to this draft...I am sure that at the end, we shall all be together as one nation," said President Kibaki at the centre - just a few metres from where his entourage had an accident as he campaigned for the Presidency prior to the 2002 general elections.
"It is a high time we be united for Kenyans to move forward."
Accompanying the President were Cabinet ministers: Anyang Nyong'o, George Saitoti, Charity Ngilu, Mutula Kilonzo, Mohammed Kuti, Otieno Kajwang', Franklin Bett and MPs Wavinya Ndeti and Johnstone Muthama.
Curiously, Machakos Town MP Victor Munyaka, who is in the No camp, was present
The leaders opened the Maruba Dam that cost the government Sh380 million to rehabilitate.
Thing of the past
During various stopovers at Mlolongo, Athi River and Kyumbi trading Centre, the leaders exuded confidence that they will carry the day at the referendum.
Mr Odinga told residents that once the proposed constitution was passed, corruption and the culture of impunity will be a thing of the past.
At Kyumbi, the PM recounted how President Kibaki's accident during campaigns for the 2002 general election turned into "a blessing for Kenyans".
"Our captain was injured just a few metres from here during the 2002 campaigns but despite this, it did not dampen our spirits. The game had to go on and you all know that we prevailed," said Mr Odinga.
"That is why we decided to construct a dual carriage road all the way from Mombasa to here. I can assure you that this region and the country as well will witness more development projects once the draft law is passed. More factories will dot this area," he said.
He said the yet-to-be constructed economic zones will create more employment opportunities for millions.
He clarified that abortion is not permitted under the proposed constitution as had been alleged by those opposed to the document.
Better life
Vice President Kalonzo Musyoka, on his part, said the time for change was now and that Kenyans should not be left behind.
"We are therefore asking you to vote for the draft law and you will see the face of Kenya change," he said.
"Let us not let down our future generations. I can assure you, this document will bring about a better life for you. It is time to build the nation," said the VP at Mlolongo.
He said the 'Yes' side was assured of victory especially after the alliance of both the 'orange and banana' sides.
"I can assure Kenyans that this document is good for them. They should indeed not be threatened by it," he said.
Mrs Ngilu said those opposed to the new law were insincere and were going contrary to the wishes of Kenyans.
In the run up to the 2005 referendum, Mr Odinga led the 'Orange' side that opposed what was dubbed the 'Wako draft' while President Kibaki led the 'Banana' side that was championing for the document.

Original_Search_Title: Abounding Eyesores in Monrovia Need Cure
Headline: Liberia: Abounding Eyesores in Monrovia Need Cure
Body_Text:
Being a fact that Liberians, wherever they find themselves take pride in boasting that our nation is the oldest independent republic in Africa, and liberally citing 163 years of independence, time has caught up with us to show tangible fruits of our independence.
Independence goes with responsibilities and obligations that demand implementation. Being so, this article begins reflections on Monrovia as the capital city of Africa's oldest republic - Liberia.
This small capital, Monrovia, which beats in size Banjul, capital of the smallest country on continental Africa, becomes congested during daytime only because most people who occupy the city by daytime are dwellers of Monrovia's suburbs.
A typical example of this is seeing someone freshly disembarking from a bus at the foot of Gabriel Johnson-Tucker Bridge and frantically asking a taxicab, "In town, in town?," only to be heading for Broad St. This writer gets baffled hearing people standing at the intersection of Carey and Johnson streets asking taxicabs, "In town?", clinching a notion that has dwarfed their Monrovia to mean Broad street and two-thirds of Randall St.
For six days a week, the long queues of multitude that begin gathering after 3 pm at Waterside, Johnson Street and its entire slope toward Johnson-Tucker bridge, McDonald Street, both entrances of the University of Liberia, and along both sides of Broad Street for vehicles gesticulating their directions is proof that most people seen in the city during daytime dwell outside Monrovia but only go there to either do businesses or hustle for something.
Most of them rise up by 4 am from the suburbs for the commercial district of Waterside while some go to jam Broad and a segment of Randall streets hoping to satisfy these interests.
And perhaps, because most of these suburban dwellers know no other cites, they often ask to go "in town" without calling "Monrovia", understandably meaning Broad Street in Monrovia. It must not also be surprising that so many of them lack knowledge of names of the ten crisscrossing streets in Monrovia, which they rush to 6 days a week.
Backtracking to the genesis of Monrovia depicts that earlier arriving settlers relocated from Providence Island onto Cape Mesurado perhaps with a focus to form a commonwealth and not build a city laid out with room for expansion. In fact, only those residing on Front, Ashmun and Snapper Hill Broad Streets up to 1960 were considered Monrovians until after the Israelis constructed the new Executive Mansion in 1964 before the city limit spread to City Hall.
According to historical accounts, Speaker Richard A. Henries, who resided on Carey Street opposite present-day Holiday Inn hotel, later dragged Monrovia's boundary to Oldest Congo town (near Police Zone 3) after moving to his new home there.
On Sundays, most of those attending churches commute from outside the city limit and the streets are sparsely populated with people from outside Liberia renting houses mainly on Front (Sao Boso), Ashmun, Broad Gurley, Benson Streets etc.
Ellen Sandemanie, last Commissioner of the Commonwealth District of Monrovia, extended beautification to newly Capitol Bypass in 197O and that followed elected Mayor Edward A. David's efforts to mount street names at intercessions, engage in garbage collection and city transport system, all aborted by rebel conflict that began 24 December 1989 and ended in August 2003.
Today the dilapidated E. J. Roye building, uncompleted Central Bank of Liberia edifice, unfinished home of NH&SB, the Executive Pavilion, expected to undergo renovation, and numerous untidy vacant lots on many streets are eyesores that need prompt cure to facelift Monrovia's skyline especially after Chinese contractors finish the Vai town bridge by the end of 2011 linking the city with Bushrod Island attracting more and more taxicabs to commute all streets with ease.
Continuously permitting dilapidated buildings and zinc shacks to deface Monrovia with freshly paved streets amounts to allowing more eyesores that need cures. And the challenge is for City Hall to demand owners of untidy vacant lots to develop them while, henceforth, making sure that all building plans for the city are approved before implementation in order to be certain that only certain types of buildings are erected in certain areas.
Seeing how residents have turned the already narrow sidewalks of many streets into, living rooms, kitchens, makeshift garages, playgrounds for their children, selling places and even laundry places with dirty water rolling down blocking access to pedestrians in Monrovia is a painful sight.
This writer wonders why the continuous heaps of garbage in marketplaces and streets littered with polyethylene bags appear to mean nothing to Monrovia residents and commuters from the suburbs. No one ever stops to think: When will we ever have a clean city? Now the streets of Monrovia will be paved glittering but people keep keeping them dirty. Imagine three persons eating bananas on a speeding motorbike recently dropped the peelings on Warren Street by CDB King elementary just as people do after eating bananas in a village.
Oh yes! Another disgusting eyesore is how many vehicles remain parked for hours on major streets like Broad, Randall and Benson streets simple because their operators worry about paying no parking fees though such vehicles make the streets narrower and embarrass persons disembarking from vehicles at stopping points.
"The streets were bad," we complained. Now the streets are getting better, what are we doing to keep them clean and make Monrovia wholesome? Let's make curing the abounding eyesores in Monrovia everybody's conscientious business.

Original_Search_Title: Police Pursuit of Gang Ends with Eight Dead
Headline: Kenya: Police Pursuit of Gang Ends with Eight Dead
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Eight people were killed on Wednesday as police engaged gangsters in a gunfight on the Nairobi-- Naivasha highway.
Among the casualties in the deadly high speed drama, that started in Naivasha and ended in Ruaka on the outskirts of Nairobi, was a policeman and a prison warder. Others were a motorist, a bystander and four men described by police as gangsters.
Police said three of the gunmen killed were prison warders, and further described them as commanders of the gang that engaged them in an exchange of fire. However, prison officials identified only Mr Julius Wafula Sirengo, one of those killed, as their own adding that he was not part of the gang.
While confirming Mr Sirengo's killing, the officer in charge of the Naivasha prison, Mr Patrick Mwenda, said the other two were not his men as claimed by police. The chilling encounter started at 8.30 am in Naivasha where the police officer attached to the Highway Patrol Unit was gunned down three kilometres from the town, as he teamed up with colleagues to chase a car carrying the gunmen who were speeding away.
The chase, according to police, ensued after the gangsters' vehicle sped off when police flagged it down. At Ruaka, Mr John Mwangi, 25, was shot dead as he watched the exchange of fire from the balcony of a four-storey building. Two kilometres away at Kwa Munyinyi stage, Mr Edmund Wanyama's body lay in a pick-up truck, having been shot as he rode in the vehicle with two workmates.
