The other four men were shot by police as they tried to get out of their vehicle which was cornered at Ruaka, by a different team of officers attached to the Nairobi-based Special Crime Prevention Unit (SCPU). Three AK-47 rifles, including that snatched from the dead officer in Naivasha were recovered after the shooting.
Two accomplices escaped with two other rifles. According to Mr Mwenda, the warder killed in Naivasha had been arrested earlier by police."The DCIO in Naivasha called me in the morning. He told me he was interrogating one of my men. I confirmed he was a member of staff. After 9 am I was called by the station commander who told me to identify the officer's body at the mortuary," Mr Mwenda told the Nation.
The other two, he added were not his men. Though the warder was not on duty, he said it was unlikely that he was involved in the gun fight. Concerning the others yet to be identified, Prisons boss Isaiah Osugo said: "If they were prison warders, they did not acquire the arms from the prisons. We do not use AK-47 rifles. If it is established they are warders from other prisons, then we cannot defend criminals. I am also sorry we lost an officer in Naivasha."
When the gangsters' car failed to stop after being flagged down by officers from the Highway policemen, with the driver accelerating past the officers, they then gave chase in their car, a Nissan X-Trail with a blaring siren, police said. According to Naivasha police, the car was way ahead of the police when it veered off the road and landed in a ditch, forcing the occupants to alight and take cover in the bush.
Unaware that the gunmen were nearby, the police car stopped at the scene and the officers alighted. It was then the gangsters emerged and sprayed the officers with bullets killing one of them and snatching his AK-47 rifle, then they drove off in the police car, with its siren blaring.Naivasha police commander Ernest Oponyo said: "The police gave chase and caught up with the fleeing gangsters at the Naivasha turn-off."
At Kimende, on the way to Nairobi, the gunmen used the police vehicle to block another motorist and seized his car. The getaway car's registration number plate was circulated on the police communication network after the owner alerted Naivasha police who were on their trail.
Some gunmen continued riding in the police car which they used to clear traffic as they escaped. But they abandoned the police car at Kinungi, squeezed into the other, made a U-turn, drove for a few kilometres before taking the road to Ruaka. They passed the pick-up in which Mr Wanyama was.
"I did not see any vehicle in front or behind us. I just heard a bang and thought it was a tyre burst. But it continued and I realised we were being shot at," said Mr Godwin Juma, who was driving. Mr Moses Kilunga, who was also riding with them added: "I realised Juma was losing control of the vehicle and I stretched my hand to hold the steering, and Wanyama, was seated at the middle. It stopped and seven men came running and ordered us out. I think they were police because they left us after we identified ourselves." The three were employees of Nakuru wholesalers and were travelling to Banana for a welding job.
At Ruaka, Mr Denis Njogu, 28, mourned his brother who was killed on the balcony. "There were gunshots and everybody took cover. My brother (Mwangi), was a caretaker of the building. He was standing on the fourth floor watching. I knew it was him, after I recognised the clothes he wore as the body hung from the rails of the balcony."
Additional reporting by Casper Waithaka

Original_Search_Title: The Political Storm That Never Was
Headline: Zimbabwe: The Political Storm That Never Was
Body_Text:
Harare — ON Wednesday last week, Prime Minister Morgan Tsvangirai's office attempted but failed to cause a political storm through an ill-informed statement issued by Minister Gorden Moyo charging that Cde George Charamba, the permanent secretary in the Ministry of Media, Information and Publicity should be disciplined by GPA principals for allegedly undermining the Prime Minister's authority over a non-existent Bilateral Investment Protection and Promotion Agreement between Zimbabwe and South Korea.
There are three reasons why the attempted political storm failed. First, it is not easy to generate a political storm over something that does not exist, as there is no procedurally and legally signed BIPPA between Zimbabwe and South Korea.
Second, the Prime Minister's office needs to learn one or two things about timing when it comes to news making because by Wednesday last week, Zimbabweans and South Koreans were, along with the rest of the world, already preoccupied with the start of the 2010 Soccer World Cup finals south of the Limpopo and could not be bothered with an alleged storm over a fictitious BIPPA.
Third, and most important, the attempted political storm was doomed to fail because it was based on the following four MDC-T falsehoods:
The persistent and hopeless MDC-T lie that Prime Minister Tsvangirai is the head of the Government of the Republic of Zimbabwe;
The ridiculous MDC-T presumption, which is of course false, that the very same architects, supporters and beneficiaries of the illegal and evil Western economic sanctions against Zimbabwe which have hurt ordinary people the most can also be the champions of direct foreign investment through instruments such as the purported BIPPA between Zimbabwe and South Korea;
The MDC-T nonsense, which is worse than a falsehood, that a cabinet minister can use a power of attorney to delegate his or her powers and functions to another cabinet minister; and;
The naive MDC-T belief, which falsely assumes that politicians, particularly the so-called GPA principals, have the constitutional power to discipline civil servants.
An examination of each of these falsehoods should show beyond any doubt why Minister Gorden Moyo's outburst against Charamba was exactly the kind of political rubbish that has come to be expected from him. Here's why.
For all the propaganda battering our country gets from its detractors whose main gripe is that they do not want to see the legacy of our liberation struggle taking permanent root, the fact remains that Zimbabwe has one of the most professional bureaucracies, including the army and police, you can get around the world. This is why our bureaucrats and officers are among the most sought-after by the international community.
A major reason for this is the well entrenched administrative practice in our bureaucracy which distinguishes between politics and administration such that our civil servants, including permanent secretaries, are employed and only disciplined not by politicians but by the Public Service Commission which is an independent, professional and constitutional body not run by politicians.
Gorden Moyo's outburst that Charamba should be disciplined by politicians, one wonders by what means short of a public flogging, belies not only his ignorance of Zimbabwe's system of governance but also exposes the MDC-T's lie about its alleged commitment to good governance which more than anything else requires a clear separation between politics and administration.
Charamba is an administrator and not a politician and yet the unacceptable threats of jungle discipline against him are coming from dangerous politicians in the Prime Minister's office with vested interests in Tsvangirai's blundering. Surely, that cannot be an example of good governance.
Parenthetically, it is infantile for the Prime Minister's office to imagine that referring Charamba to the so-called political principals as defined in the GPA is a big deal.
We are a constitutional democracy and we do not want to become a country of GPA or ABC political principals. More than that, we in Zanu-PF know our political principals by their names and led by President Robert Mugabe they include Vice Presidents Joice Mujuru and John Nkomo among others we have known for years.
Otherwise, on the matter at hand, the public record will show that one the MDC-T's GPA afterthoughts is its political dislike and personal hatred of Charamba as permanent secretary in the media, information and publicity ministry. Gorden Moyo's outburst had more to do with that than a non-existent BIPPA.
Indeed, if Charamba had a case to answer over the BIPPA issue, the competent authority to handle the matter would be the PSC but Gorden Moyo's outburst did not even suggest that he knows there's something called the PSC although he mumbled some mumbo jumbo about "ethics of the civil service" in his claim that Charamba was "unethical" when clarified the true position about the alleged BIPPA between Zimbabwe and South Korea. The clear but absurd implication was that it is unethical to correct Tsvangirai.
One thing should be very clear. While politicians can prevaricate or even lie for all they want, civil servants have a professional duty to tell things as they are. As things stand, what Charamba said about the alleged BIPPA between Zimbabwe and South Korea, which he had a duty to clarify as the permanent secretary responsible for information, is the factual, administrative and lawful position.
To call that position "unethical" or "irrational" as have done the Prime Minister's office and its unthinking media hacks is hogwash.
The South Koreans and serious prospective investors understand that something unprocedural and illegal cannot be ethical or rational.
Then there is the claim by Gorden Moyo that the alleged BIPPA between Zimbabwe and South Korea, which was unprocedurally and illegally signed by the Minister of Science and Technology, Heneri Dzinotyiwei, is valid because the minister responsible for signing BIPPAs, Elton Mangoma who is in charge of Economic Planning and Investment, "officially" gave Dzinotyiwei "a power of attorney".
Now this is pathetic. Does the Prime Minister's office really expect to be taken seriously by asserting that a cabinet minister can delegate his or her powers and functions to another minister by dint of some power of attorney of all things? That is the stuff of banana republics or parallel governments.
For the record, it should be known and understood that in Zimbabwe the executive functions and powers of cabinet ministers are assigned to ministers by the Head of State and Government and the Commander in Chief of the Zimbabwe Defence Forces and through Acts of Parliament and that is President Mugabe.
When there is a temporary need to reassign the powers and functions of a particular minister to another, it is done by the President, not some power of attorney, through a notice in the Government Gazette.
As such, if Minister Dzinotyiwei had lawful authority to sign a BIPPA with South Korea on behalf of the Republic of Zimbabwe as claimed by the Prime Minister's office through Gorden Moyo, then it should produce or point to the relevant Government Gazette with the relevant notice. That would settle the matter but such a notice is as non-existent as the alleged BIPPA!
Then there is the amazing claim, touted by MDC-T media hacks, that the alleged BIPPA is an example of Prime Minister Tsvangirai's efforts to bring foreign direct investment to our country. Well, one needs to sit down to deal with this one because it is breathtakingly ridiculous.
For the avoidance of doubt nobody is opposed to having foreign direct investment through whatever channel including any effort by Prime Minister Tsvangirai as long as that is done lawfully through the transparent and accountable systems of the State.
But we now know that the MDC-T does not believe in transparency and accountability, which it treats as relevant only where Tsvangirai is personally involved.
For example this has come through the MDC-T's opposition to the Marange diamond mining activities of Mbada and Canadile despite the fact that the two companies are in lawful partnership with ZMDC who are lawful owners of the mining rights in the Marange diamond fields.
The MDC-T's perverted view of transparency is akin to that of a naughty pastor who solemnises a couple's marriage during the day only to show up in their bedroom at night demanding that they consummate the marriage with him watching it all in the name of transparency.
If Tsvangirai wants to bring investment to Zimbabwe, which would be most welcome, he should do so openly, procedurally and lawfully in the letter and spirit of good governance and he must allow the various arms of government outside his office, such as media, information and publicity, to do their work without poking his nose in their business.
That is the simple but important point which some overzealous MDC-T media hacks have conveniently missed in their apparent desperation to market their tired copy to the dwindling MDC-T constituency.
But there is also a more fundamental point here. It is very difficult if not impossible for any fair minded person to believe the notion that those like Prime Minister Tsvangirai who are architects or supporters or beneficiaries of the prevailing evil and illegal Western economic sanctions can also be champions of direct foreign investment in our country through BIPPAs or whatever channel. Surely, that is a contradiction.
If Prime Minister Tsvangirai and his office truly want foreign direct investment in Zimbabwe then the first and major thing they must do is to unequivocally and persistently seek the removal of the evil and illegal Western economic sanctions before pursuing any BIPPA, especially with Western aligned countries such as South Korea.
Even fools must know that the reason why we do not have foreign direct investment from Western countries and their allies at the moment is not because we do not have BIPPAs with countries such as South Korea but because we have evil and illegal economic sanctions.
Anyone who does not understand this will never ever understand anything.
Finally, the Prime Minister's office and indeed the MDC-T and its founders and funders must appreciate that the real reason behind the failed furore over the false Zimbabwe-South Korea BIPPA is too apparent to hide.
The issue is not about the alleged BIPPA and it is not about Charamba either but about Tsvangirai's long standing yet hopeless effort since February 13, 2009 to pose as the head of the Government of the Republic of Zimbabwe.
Despite the fact that everyone, including all MDC-T cabinet ministers and indeed Tsvangirai himself, knows that Zimbabwe's Head of State and Government and the Commander in Chief of the Defence Forces is President Mugabe, there has been a continuous and thus relentless propaganda campaign to present Tsvangirai as the head of government in Zimbabwe when there's no factual, legal, constitutional or even GPA basis for that.
The mess over the alleged Zimbabwe-South Korea BIPPA is nothing but an outrageous propaganda stunt. We are now supposed to think that, as the alleged head of government Tsvangirai went to South Korea to secure foreign direct investment from that country to help recover our economy when we all know that he actually went there to receive a dubious honorary degree with no national meaning or purpose beyond his personal interest.
The message to Tsvangirai, his founders and funders, cronies and media hacks should now be very clear: for the sake of all of us, the MDC-T's propaganda that Tsvangirai is the head of government has gone too far and enough should be enough such that the time has now come for Tsvangirai to get real outside propaganda games.
Charamba is doing a sterling job for his country as a professional, Tsvangirai and Gorden Moyo should do theirs as politicians.

Original_Search_Title: How We Broke Into Homes Through Ceilings -Suspects
Headline: Nigeria: How We Broke Into Homes Through Ceilings -Suspects
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nemesis has caught up with Chukwuemeka Ani and Ejike Eze, two members of a gang which specialises in raiding homes when their occupants are away in Ibafo, Ifo Local Government Area of Ogun State.
The suspects had invaded the Glory Estate in Ibafo on Friday, May 21, when luck ran out on them. This was shortly after they had successfully raided two homes and were about to take their leave.
They met their waterloo when members of the Landlords Association of the estate, who sighted them, grouped together and effected their arrest.
Musa Daura, Ogun State police commissioner, while parading the duo, alongside other suspects at the command headquarters, Eleweran, Abeokuta, on June 3, confirmed the arrest and said the suspects had been handed over to detectives at the State Criminal Investigation Department (SCID), for further probe into the matter.
Other members of the gang who took part in the attack on the estate, he said, are still at large, while detectives have launched a manhunt for them.
According to him, the suspects belong to a gang which had been giving residents of the estate sleepless nights in recent times.
The commissioner disclosed that N2,500 a DSTV decoder, jewelries and beads as well as a Nivea Body Spray were recovered from the two suspects.
In a chat with Daily Independent, Ani and Eze admitted raiding two homes within the estate when their occupants were away.
"I stay with a friend, Okezie at Akinyode, Ojodu, Banana Estate, Lagos. I once worked at building sites in Ojodu and its environs when I initially came from my village (Eha-Amufu, Enugu State) few years ago. But when I could no longer lay hands on any other jobs, I ventured into breaking into homes. A friend (names withheld) introduced me into it and he is now at large. And two months ago, I came in contact with this young man, Eze, when he came to see his elder brother in our area.
"One day, I was strolling along our street and met him. I spoke Igbo to him and he was able to respond. And that was how we suddenly became friends. I introduced him into the gang. On the very day in question, we went to the estate and gained entry through the ceilings, by breaking the asbestos. We did not meet anybody at the homes and we carted away some valuables from their wardrobes. But as we were about leave the estate, we did not know that members of the community had sighted us and had laid ambush for us until we were apprehended and beaten up. From there, we were handed over to the officers and men at the Ibafo Division," narrated the 23-year-old suspect.
Eighteen-year-old Eze, corroborated Ani's confessions.
According to the Agwu, Enugu State-born suspect, "immediately, we were through and we were about to leave, we scaled the fence. But unknown to us some people who we later found out to be members of the Landlords Association in the community had laid ambush for us and we were arrested. We were handed over to the police at Ibafo and subsequently brought to this place (SCID, Eleweran, Abeokuta)."
Daura, assured that the two suspects would be charged to court as soon as investigations are concluded on the matter.

Original_Search_Title: Going Bananas As Art Gallery Comes of Age
Headline: Kenya: Going Bananas As Art Gallery Comes of Age
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Back where it all began, in that cluster of villages to the west of Nairobi, the Banana Hill Arts Centre is celebrating its coming of age - its 18th birthday.
Painter Shine Tani and his wife Rahab, who own the gallery, have made merry with an exhibition called Soko Poa (Nice Market), presenting some 40 or so pictures in the main hall this month.
In addition, there are around 700 paintings and drawings in racks around the walls plus, I am told, some 2,000 pictures hung or stored in the back rooms.
And you could afford one. Prices start at just Ksh500 (about $6.5) for a little acrylic painting of a woman fetching water by new artist George Mungai, to Ksh280,000 ($3,590) for a large oil called Cancelled Concert by Shine Tani himself.
Scattered over Tani's desk - it's that sort of place; casual to a fault - are a few unframed Tinga Tinga pictures from Tanzania, and four or five paintings by the Ugandan Ismael Kateregga; small oils of elephants and zebras left over from his recent exhibition there.
On the walls, I was struck by two things: The continued excellence of Martin Kamuya and the fact that Julius Kimemia, if he wants to progress, must break free from the powerful influence of the man who taught him, Peter Kibunja.
Kibunja showed the way with paintings of people with round faces and huge soulful eyes and his prolific pupil Kimemia has dutifully followed with a lot more of the same.
He strikes me as a promising painter who will do well to escape from his master's shadow by finding a subject he can call his own.
A couple of Kibunjas - one of them being Church on Sunday - show why he gained his reputation for wry social comment, cunningly disguised as naivety, that delights with its freshness.
Kamuyu is one of those little known artists admired by his peers and you can see why: His two paintings are cleanly executed, imaginative, subtly coloured and celebrate a certain freedom of spirit.
Of the two pictures shown (and why not more?), I preferred Night Out, a witty take on a hen party, while I felt Cost Sharing with its eight fractured picture planes was trying a little too hard.
In a back room, I saw a group of pictures by Francis Kahuri, now approaching his seventies and becoming the Grand Old Man of Kenyan art. If you like pastiches of Picasso, you'll like Kahuri too.
The Banana Hill gallery is accessible and always welcoming - but I do wish some of the pictures were rather more considered and relied on skill as much as charm.
Still, the owners are to be congratulated. Running a gallery for 18 years and counting is no easy business - and if you want further proof of that, look no further than the current state of the RaMoMA in Nairobi's Parklands, which lays fair claim to being East Africa's finest private gallery and museum.A beacon for art lovers and a showcase for the brightest and best in local talent, it is going through what might be politely called a rough patch. Think BP.
Some of the pictures seen there recently would have been better suited to a gift shop, labels were missing, walls needed repainting, the floors could have done with a sweep... it was so uncharacteristically gloomy and dull.The only bright spot remained that section of the ground floor given over to visiting children where the work was cheerful and there was a powerful sense of enthusiasm and good fun.
In the past couple of weeks at RaMoMA, six of the 20 or so staff have been handed dismissal letters, citing "financial constraints" and at least three of the board of trustees have resigned.
They were followed through the door by the recently appointed general manager, Rahab Nderu, who herself resigned shortly after supervising the staff sackings.
The good news is that although many galleries seem to go through these convulsions from time to time - rather as bands suffer from "musical differences" - they tend to recover just as quickly.
Frank Whalley runs Lenga Juu, a fine arts and media consultancy based in Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: Forget the Racism and Humdrum Football, Hope the Cup Rescripts Continent's Role
Headline: Africa: Forget the Racism and Humdrum Football, Hope the Cup Rescripts Africa's Global Role
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Hooray, its here! After years of anticipation, months of hype, and periodic injections of veiled racism, Africa's World Cup is up and running.
Like a billion other hapless footie junkies, I am procrastinating on more important tasks because I can't miss Cote d' Ivoire vs Portugal, or even the Serbia-Australia tie.
But this time around I am bunkering down for the planet's premier sports competition with certain reservations. To begin with, there is the realisation that the majority of the games are really not that great.
For sure, every World Cup produces a couple of classics, and they often feature less fashionable teams.
In USA '94, for example, Bulgaria's unshaven party animals overcame Mexico in an epic second-round battle, then put Germany to the sword.
My favorite was Columbia vs Saudi Arabia, a fantastic match with a brilliant goal that no one watched because both sides were already eliminated.
Still, the overall quality of Cup football rarely matches that of top-drawer club competition; too many ties are duds.
Ukraine and Poland's lugubrious second-round contest in Germany is a case in point. That Shevchenko's blatant dive in their final group match against Tunisia put Ukraine through made it even more painful to watch.
This is the second problem. The Fifa World Cup is the world's worst officiated sports event. One game does change everything (a la the ESPN ad), especially when a referee's bad decision is the deciding factor.
Examples of spurious whistles, harsh red cards, gratuitous penalties and rigged outcomes from past World Cups are legion.
Fifa officiating sucks and there's a pecking order to boot. The absurd red card shown to Samuel Eto'o in a build-up match against Paraguay feeds the impression Cameroon is the most penalized-per minutes played World Cup team.
The theory that it all evens out over the long run will be put to the test this time around, as will the unwritten rule favoring the host team's progress.
South Korea's 2002 victories over Italy and Spain testify to the latter, like the performance of host Spain in 1982.
Spain won once in five games, scoring only four goals. Two came from controversial penalties, a foul two metres outside the box seeing them through to the second round.
Bafana Bafana vs Thierry Henry's France will provide the settings for evaluating both of these hypotheses on African soil.
Ideally, this should not be a concern - which brings us to the difficulties good referees face.Unlike cricket and many other sports, there is no formal acknowledgement of the "spirit of the game."
The Brazilian defender, Roberto Carlos, candidly admitted as much - winning justifies cheating - and there are yet deeper sentiments behind the histrionics, poor sportsmanship, and controversy marring the competition.
After Geoffrey Hurst's controversial strike in the 1966 England-Germany final, the referee conferred with his lineman and awarded the goal; the commentator rued "that one is going to be talked about for years to come." The debate continues, but rarely mentions the Russian linesman's comment after the match: "Consider it payback for Stalingrad."
The "human element" Michel Platini cites in defence of opposition to video replay subsumes football as "war by other means." Diegito's mano de Dios becomes payback for the Malvinas conflict, and there are contrasting variations on the theme.
Mussolini exploited the Cup to promote his Fascist regime in 1936; a beleaguered Argentinean dictatorship did the same in 1978.
West Germany's improbable defeat of the invincible Hungarians in 1954, a side senior pundits rate as the best national team ever assembled, announced to the world, "We are back."
It came to be seen as a catalyst for the vanquished country's post-war economic miracle.
These examples illustrate how the World Cup entered the narrative of international affairs, transforming the footie into a more universal trope.
We accept the flawed officiating, humdrum football, and unrequited expectations of the heroic and dramatic because every four years the competition generates a new script.
So what will the script be this time around; will Africa shine?
Hard to say, the continent's initial conditions make unpredictability a fact of life. Pre-Cup developments, beginning with Togo's tragic transit through Cabinda before the Cup of Nations in Angola, appear to conform.
The dampening effect of botched ticket allocation, global economic malaise, terrorist threats, and the unusually high number of injuries claiming the Cup's marquee players have followed suite.
So has the Jabulani ball. Unveiled as "an energetic unit combined with perfect roundness," the new ball is erratic in flight and difficult for players to control.
Internal criticism of the government's financial expenditures may have prompted the bizarre ads emphasiSing the long-term angle appearing on Supersport.
Set in the distant milieu of 2034, African narrators reminisce fondly about the great event--against back-to-the-present backdrops of grimy urban flats and quaint rural shacks.
Right now, it's the cynics that I remember. South Africa, the real "energetic unit" in this show, has put its house (and new stadiums) in order, shouldering the risk in the wake of a global financial crisis.
Fifa, in contrast, will rake-off some 94 per cent of the $3.3 billion generated by television and marketing rights - while poor ticket sales in Europe reduce host revenues.
We should also remember that Seph Blatter's support for African football is bound up with the votes of Africa's national federations.
Hence the final concern: The scrum outside the Nigeria-North Korea warm-up match reminds us Fifa is capable of passing the buck if anything goes wrong.
Africa's World Cup is not about financial returns, or even a breakthrough performance by African teams.
This script begins with a level playing field where Samuel Eto'o doesn't have to worry about monkey calls and bananas. Let's hope the unpredictability remains on the pitch.
Paul Goldsmith is a researcher based in Meru, Kenya.

Original_Search_Title: Housing - FG Repositions Ministry for Efficient Delivery
Headline: Nigeria: Housing - FG Repositions Ministry for Efficient Delivery
Body_Text:
Lagos — The federal government has restated its determination to reposition the Ministry of Lands, Housing and Urban Development. Speaking in Lagos at the weekend, the Permanent Secretary in the ministry, Engr. Anthony Ozodinobi said as part of the repositioning efforts, the ministry has been charged by the administration with the responsibility of implementing government policy and housing and urban development plan, as contained in the government white paper on Urban Development and Housing Policy off year 2002.
Ozodinobi, who was in the state on a three day official visit to inspect and take stock of all federal government landed properties scattered around the state, with a view to evaluating and seeing how they can be useful to the public, said that the visit became necessary to be able to know how to plan on the available resources. Among the places earmarked for inspection are; Satellite Town, Olodi Apapa; Tolu Village in Ajegunle; Mechanical Yard at Iponri and Banana Island. Others are Onitiri - Orike in Yaba; St Gregory Road, Legico Office Building in Victoria Island, among other places.
Speaking further on measures being put in place to achieve the targeted aim of the ministry, the permanent secretary disclosed that government will promote computerization and human resources development of lands registries throughout the country with special attention to the development of a coordinated and comprehensive registry for all lands belonging to the Federal Government and all its agencies, adding that the ministry would network all land registries in the country into a National Depository.
To this end, he further added that the ministry will collaborate with the Federal Ministry of Industries, the Nigerian Building and Road Research Institute, and the committee of Bankers in promoting growth and development of small and medium scales industries in the building materials sub-sector 'in all parts of the federation, emphasizing the determination to set standards, regulate, supervise and monitor the development of all categories of building materials and housing types for both rural and urban areas.
On regulation in the sector, he stated that policy and set standards would be formulated so as to coordinate the activities of other agencies of government in the area of housing and supervise the federal agencies under it. The agencies include; Federal Mortgage Bank of Nigeria, Federal Housing Authority, National Housing Fund and Urban Development Bank Limited.
Speaking on how the ministry will provide security and prevent likely occurrence of fire disaster, he reaffirmed the plan to review existing legislations on Fire Service and Fire Safety delivery to ensure consonance with the goals of national industrialization and economic development.
His words: "To create an enabling environment that would enable people own personal properties, lands must not only be made available but must also be secured, accessible and save. All these are in line with the Land Agenda of the Federal Government.

Original_Search_Title: South Africa Goes Bananas As World Cup Kicks Off
Headline: South Africa/Zimbabwe: South Africa Goes Bananas As World Cup Kicks Off
Body_Text:
TODAY the global spotlight will be on South Africa as the Fifa World Cup, the first ever on the continent, kicks off at the 95 000-seater Soccer City stadium in Johannesburg with hosts Bafana Bafana taking on Mexico in a match which promises to be a historic cutthroat encounter.
The 94 700-seater (to be exact) Soccer City stadium will be an electric sea of green and gold/yellow as "Bafanatics" jump and cheer their team to the rafters. There will be soccer fans, celebrities and powerful figures in politics and business to witness the African spectacle.
As the four-week, 32-team tournament opens today with Bafana Bafana -- a longshot to bookmakers but passionately supported by locals -- taking on Mexico, football fans will be in for a treat until July 11 when the curtain comes down on the tournament.
But the opening match and indeed the World Cup itself will be more than football. The event will give Africa, South Africa in particular, an unprecedented chance to showcase its culture, beauty and opportunities like never before. Perceptions, stereotypes and attitudes will be changed or reinforced in the process.
Of course there is always the downside to this, dangers of crime, prostitutes flooding South Africa and the poor in shanties not realising much benefit from the event.
But so far South Africa -- and by extension Africa -- is proving sceptics wrong. The tournament is bringing the best out of Africans in their collective diversity.
South Africans as hosts are leading the way. Other Africans have been very helpful in the build-up to the event. They supported the tournament all the way and helped to market it in manner which showed that with organisation and collective will the continent can do much better.
Overall South Africans, assisted by other Africans and the international community, have been impressive. They worked hard to build world-class infrastructure -- stadia, airports, roads and hotels -- to make the event an African success.
The people of Mzansi, "Bafanatics" in particular, were also not disappointing in rallying behind their team.
In colourful scenes never seen before in South Africa, vuvuzelas across the country blew in unison as the clock struck 12 noon on Wednesday in a mass display of support for Bafana Bafana. The country just went bananas! It was incredible and reminiscent of scenes which characterised the release of Nelson Mandela in 1990.
Tens of thousands of people poured on to the streets of Johannesburg to greet Bafana's appearance on an open-top bus with an ear-splitting cacophony of cheers and blasts on vuvuzela horns. It was a terrific show of passion and gameness by South Africans to receive guests and embrace the spectacular event.
Soccer's stars and their supporters from around the world have converged on South Africa and the country -- with all its flaws and all its wonders -- has seized the global spotlight. Let the games begin and soccer fans enjoy the beautiful sport in peace and harmony.
Football being what it is you never know which teams are going to do well in this tournament unless you are clairvoyant. Some of us would love African countries to go all the way but realistically speaking my last four teams, provided they don't meet along the way, are Brazil, Spain, Argentina and England. I'm going with Brazil, but charity begins at home and I say Viva Bafana Bafana, siyashosholoza! (pushing forward powerfully and fast). Ayesaba amagwala! (The cowards are afraid.)

Original_Search_Title: Dimeji Bankole On Trial
Headline: Nigeria: Dimeji Bankole On Trial
Body_Text:
The late flamboyant Senate President, Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, it was, who imported the word banana peels, into Nigeria's political lexicon to illustrate the intrigues in the upper chamber legislature.
The phrase was specifically coined to describe the land mine that had been laid against successive Senate leadership that had seen its officials in hazy exit from the headship of the National Assembly.
Today, the coinage is not only creeping into, but has, indeed, become very prevalent in the Green Chamber, as the House of Representative is often described. Former Speaker of the House, Patricia Etteh, who got a dose of the poisonous peels, became its first casualty. While some blamed the incident that led to her exit from the leadership of the House on her political naïveté, others believed that her political voyage was imbued with deceptive visage.
However, her successor, Demeji Bankole, though, not quite on the same path, has consistently been roughened by those who see him as romancing the dreaded peels. In the past few weeks, the House has been put in the public glare of allegations of financial impropriety. Some lawmakers had complained over Bankole's style of leadership and the speculations that he has been fingered in a number of corruption cases since he assumed the mantle of leadership in the House.
For instance, the car scandal, which the House buried hastily, has been resurrected. Festus Keyamo, a Lagos-based activist lawyer had alleged connivance within the House last year over the supply of cars that were allegedly not exactly what were ordered by Peugeot Automobile Nigeria (PAN). He had also alleged scam in the payment of the Value Added Tax (VAT), involved in the purchase of the vehicles.
Keyamo had pointedly accused the House leadership of untoward actions in the transaction. But the legislators were not moved by his allegations.
In the current rumbles, however, the former chairman, House Committee on Information and National Orientation, Dino Melaye, has given the Speaker an ultimatum to resign or be forced to quit the stage. He was also said to have vowed to make the House ungovernable if Bankole fails to resign voluntarily.
But, chairman, House Committee on Media and Public Affairs, Eseme Eyiboh responded by dismissing the threat as uncalled for since, according to him, the House had at all times displayed its preparedness to address the grievances of its members whenever they arose.
For Yusuf Ali, a Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN), the history of crisis in the legislature has shown that no one can predict anything. "One may find that overnight anything can happen. I do not want to speculate anything like they are alleging on the Speaker. Until we have provable facts on what happened or did not happen in any of their committees, it would be wrong for anyone to join in the speculation. But, if the House feels it has enough evidence to impeach its speaker, guided by laid down rules, so be it. All I know is that Nigerians are watching the drama with keen interest," Ali said.
The conflict in the hallowed chamber of the lower legislature had, last week, degenerated to a shouting match between Bankole and Independence Ogunewe, one of his antagonists.
Bankole, in his early 40's, holds a degree in Economics from the University of Reading, England. He obtained another in military Technique from Oxford University also in the United Kingdom before proceeding to Harvard University in the United States of America for a course in Public Finance Management. He was elected into the House in 2003 and was re-elected in 2007. He hails from Abeokuta, Ogun State.

Original_Search_Title: For Wabara, Justice Delayed is Not Justice Denied
Headline: Nigeria: For Wabara, Justice Delayed is Not Justice Denied
Body_Text:
Former President Olusegun Obasanjo must be sulking over the ruling of the Court of Appeal on Tuesday last week. A heavy burden has also been lifted off the shoulders of the former Senate President, Adolphous Wabara; a humiliating burden which he has carried with dignity since March 22, 2005. That was the day Obasanjo, in a nationwide broadcast, accused, judged and presented Wabara to the world as a criminal. He said Wabara, and others, shared a N55 million bribe from the former Minister of Education, Fabian Osuji, before approving a budgetary allocation for the ministry. Wabara denied.
It is of little interest now whether the allegations were true or false. The onus of proof was on Obasanjo. He failed. But the manner he and his EFCC collaborators treated the issue was extremely disgusting and appalling. As Senate correspondent, I was never one of Wabara's fans. Not that we hated each other; but we had different jobs to do. I maintained a weekly column in The Punch which he never liked. And he was not the only senator that hated the column. No apologies. What I liked most about Wabara, and I still do, are his deep sense of humour and poetic speeches.
He assumed office as President of the Senate with huge experience as a two-term senator and a former House member. His background in the secret service was an added advantage. The expectation therefore was that he would settle down, employ his repertoire of experience and make a success of the assignment. He tried to do just that. But he soon discovered that the Presidency of the Senate was the most sought-after office by South-East politicians. Those who were not in the Senate wanted their godsons to occupy the seat while the South-East senators themselves fought dirty in a bid to unseat him.
Unfortunately, Wabara, either by design or mistake, kept stepping on series of the legendary banana peels placed on his path by his brothers. To worsen his fate, he came to power at a time scandals associated with bribery in the Senate had turned the respected upper legislative chamber into a den of thieves in the imagination of certain misguided and misinformed members of the society. Both the innocents and the thieves were counted together. All you needed to do to discredit a senator was to say that he took a bribe. No one would ask further questions. The victim of the unruly mischief would then live the rest of his life with the humiliating tag. It was either Francis Arthur Nzeribe was beating drums about sharing money to senators not to impeach Obasanjo; or Wabara was accused of awarding contracts on Christmas Day. In most of the rumours, it became difficult to separate facts from fiction and blackmail from truth. And curiously, most of the allegations about Wabara were bandied by the South-East senators.
It was under this atmosphere that the bribe-for-budget scandal surfaced. And it made news because Wabara was fingered. It was a juicy piece of scandal. It was played up daily in the media while the public savoured it with relish. What we wrote were facts. No one knew the truth yet. It was the duty of the accusers to present the truth in a court of law. Chris Adighije, a senator, confessed that Osuji gave the money and that Wabara had a share though he did not ask for it. Wabara denied. Obasanjo deployed EFCC to investigate and submit the report directly to him. It was unprecedented and against the EFCC Act. When the EFCC could not do a great job, Obasanjo's aides, always eager to rubbish the National Assembly, scripted a national broadcast. All those mentioned in the scandal were pronounced guilty in the broadcast, including the respected Senator John Azuta-Mbata.
Obasanjo told Nigerians that his action was based on an "interim oral report" by the EFCC. In other words, Nuhu Ribadu could have just walked in and said, "Mr. President, I think these people collected the money." And that was enough for the President to mount a broadcast on a scandal which five years later, still roams in the realm of an allegation. He said that the money was recovered and would be used as evidence in court. Yet, five years later, the Court of Appeal lambasted him for prosecuting a criminal case without any evidence. So, where is the money? Immediately after Ribadu was used by Obasanjo to achieve that aim, which ended the strong speculations that Wabara was set to contest for the Presidency in 2003, a move that would have clashed with Obasanjo's third term bid, the matter was transferred to the ICPC. Why was the EFCC brought in, in the first place?
The Punch said in its editorial: "But despite the wide endorsement of Obasanjo's renewed fight against corruption, however, the President must learn to respect the rule of law by making sure that the due process is followed before pronouncing a culprit guilty." Bolaji Adebiyi, a senior colleague wrote, "what, for instance is the purpose of a national broadcast over a report of a preliminary investigation into a matter in which many of the accused persons are still pleading their innocence....? If the President has on the basis of preliminary report....condemned the suspects, what justice awaits them at the court?"
On April 5, 2005, Wabara resigned as Senate President, "to preserve the sanctity of that office and to be able to attend to those allegations made against my person. The pursuit of which I trust I shall be vindicated." On Tuesday, June 1, 2010, he was vindicated by the Court of Appeal. The case of corruption against him could not be sustained with evidence. It was dismissed. Doesn't Obasanjo owe Wabara and others apology?

Original_Search_Title: Employment - How Museveni is Failing the Private Sector
Headline: Uganda: Employment - How Museveni is Failing the Private Sector
Body_Text:
Throughout its 70-year history, France's Third Republic (1870 to 1940) stumbled from crisis to crisis, from dissolved parliaments and poor military strategies and planning to the appointment of a mentally ill president.
But the most ridiculous of all were arguably the macro-economic policy applications, which featured inter alia, employing people to build structures...and then paying the same people to break the same structures down. Reason? Government was under pressure to create employment.
Last week, Objection! submitted that President Museveni's directive to lower the retirement age to create room for fresh graduates is evidence that he is losing the plot and running out of solutions to our unemployment problem. Ridiculous directive, but a solution that France's Third Republic would be proud of. I argued last week that the government should focus on exciting the private sector and enable it to create jobs. Question now is where has Museveni gone wrong in handling the private sector?
The starting point should be that on the whole, the President has made many good decisions when one considers his approach to macro-economic policy. Uganda has a bigger economy and more opportunities than it had 30 years ago. But it also has a bigger population and therefore more problems to contend with; not to mention a growing opposition that threatens the President's stay in power, something now driving him into panic mode. This should explain some of the untenable policy soundbytes now emanating from the President under the guise of economic panacea. The Museveni administration has allowed the private sector to exist but has not been prudent in fostering its growth.
The first problem is the obvious one of corruption. In a banana republic like Uganda, the government is by far the biggest spender; which means that the private sector leans a lot on government for its subsistence. But when it is only a few people in government that call the shots and award all the tenders and contracts to themselves and friends and family it means that the rest of the private sector that is not 'connected' is stifled and out-competed by their well-connected counterparts. The private sector in a capitalist entity thrives on healthy competition. When you kill the competition through corruption, you effectively kill the private sector.
Secondly, access to credit is now easier, but the terms and conditions on which credit is accessed are simply ridiculous for both individuals and institutions. In such a sector, the government must impose favourable terms on credit providers or else the entrepreneurial up-starts cannot grow, because the financial institutions simply enforce foreclosure provisions and swallow them up.
One should also consider the undue proliferation of districts and lower local government entities. This is increasing the size of government, so the cost of administration is staggering. This trend is not only costing the taxpayer loads of money, but it has unwittingly entrenched a paradigm shift: people have stopped thinking entrepreneurially and are now focusing on getting district status as the primary modus of economic development.
Let's look at the nepotism in government. Just consider how selectively the President applies his interventions to collapsing businesses. Why should some businesses be allowed to fail, while others are always cushioned whenever they are on the verge of collapse? Consider also the advantage it gives such enterprises - reaping where they did not sow; and the impact on their competitors, unable to break even because a select few are able to produce at lower cost and offer products at tricky prices. This is a critical structural disincentive to private sector growth.
Mr Tegulle is a social/ political commentator

Original_Search_Title: Watermelons Are the Third Force in Campaigns for New Constitution
Headline: Kenya: Watermelons Are the Third Force in Campaigns for New Constitution
Body_Text:
It is two months to the referendum and the battle lines are drawn. Most voters appear to have made their choice even without reading the proposed constitution or benefiting from civic education. That is worrying, but not quite as disturbing as those who are clearly bent on sabotaging the process from within.
The malicious attempt to insert the words "national security" into Section 24 (i)(d); the bizarre ruling of the High Court with regard to the kadhi courts in the current Constitution and, finally, the reluctance to release adequate funds for civic education all point to a determined, concentrated and sinister plan to destroy the process from within the walls of government.
Failure to satisfactorily explain any of these activities just confirms that powerful forces are at work to ensure that the proposed constitution is rejected.
During the transition from the apartheid regime to a democratically elected government in South Africa, the ANC cited many examples of organised violence and sabotage that indicated that a Third Force was determined to deny the public the right to elect a government of their choice.
It is hardly unreasonable, then, to suggest that a Third Force representing powerful economic and political interests is determined to deny Kenyans their transition from poverty, dictatorship and impunity to an era of accountability, justice and prosperity.
We are at a transitional moment that offers hope to the majority but represents a huge threat to the beneficiaries of graft, illegal allocations of land and elitist politics.
This time around we do not have oranges and bananas pitted against each other. But do we have lots of watermelons who are green on the outside but red all the way through. They are Yes by day but No by night and they are a clever and devious bunch.
Their tactics are much more subtle and calculating because they have access to government machinery and, apparently, are awash with funds. So we should expect to see more attempts to muddy the waters and cause confusion before voting day.
That said, the only real response to such threats is a comprehensive civic education programme that allows all Kenyans to make an informed choice. But let's be honest and admit we have a poor record of tolerant listening to one another's point of view.
Two weeks ago, I attended a civic education forum in which one educator made genuine and convincing points on why he was dissatisfied with the document's proposals on majimbo and counties.
Since many present were in the Yes camp, they paid little attention to his input. That saddened me.
Proper civic education must inform and challenge positions and prejudices and teach us to respect and not ridicule the other's point of view.
Similarly, I do not see any value in churches assembling their personnel just to tell them why they should vote No because of a limited number of issues.
Surely it would be correct to also invite a member of the Committee of Experts and give them an opportunity to explain and defend the document that they are responsible for.
Civic education is not about selling your opinions but honestly and openly answering questions, addressing fears, responding to prejudices and respecting the decisions of each individual. Why are we all so afraid of that type of civic education?
The decision of politicians to campaign for and against the constitution before civic education had even begun shows utter contempt for the ability of Kenyans to read, discern and decide for themselves.
In fact if politicians dominate the process, don't be surprised that Kenyans become suspicious that the proposed constitution is a political trap and then proceed to reject it. Politicians should remember that Kenyans have grave reservations about most of them.
Likewise, the limited role of civil society organisations in civic education to date is very disappointing. Their contribution has not gone beyond successive press conferences in luxury hotels.
Surely it is time we found CSOs having their own public forums aimed at educating, informing and challenging our citizens.
I would want to see them going door-to-door in the slums, visiting bars and football games, work places and shopping centres, and doing that voluntarily and without media attention. Alas!
Yes, this would have been the perfect moment for a new political movement to emerge but potential leaders have become careerists unable to concretise their ideas in a community setting.
So our energy will go towards monitoring and containing the watermelons rather than getting enthusiastic about a new movement and a new constitutional dispensation.
Still that, and the World Cup, will preoccupy the most vigilant of us for the next few weeks.

Original_Search_Title: U.S.$27 Million Property - Otudeko Files Fresh Suit Against Zain
Headline: Nigeria: U.S.$27 Million Property - Otudeko Files Fresh Suit Against Zain
Body_Text:
Lagos — Mr. Oba Otudeko, Chairman, First Bank of Nigeria Plc, and his firm, Broad Communications, have re-filed a suit before a Federal High Court sitting in Lagos, against the directors of Zain, Nigeria's mobile telecommunication firm, challenging the directors' decision to lease a $27million property in Banana Island, Ikoyi, Lagos for use as its (Zain) headquarters.
Trial judge in the matter is Justice Okechukwu Okeke. The plaintiffs are arguing that the leasehold was extremely over-valued at $27million for a five-year tenure, and that the development is not in the interest of the GSM firm and its shareholders.
Other defendants in the suit are Mr. Gamaliel Onosode, Mr. Moez Daya, Mr. Sam Deeb, Mr. Haitham Al-Khaled, Mr. Salah Al-Fouzan, Ms. Tsega Gebreyes and Mr. Paul Usoro.
According to the plaintiffs, the decision of the board of directors was unfair and prejudicial, as the directors failed to exercise reasonable care and diligence in the discharge of their duties when they entered into the leasehold transaction concerning the 5,040sq meters property.
They are praying the court to direct the defendants to reverse the leasehold entered into in the name of Zain and are also urging the court to take a judicial notice of the fact that the defendants had treated the report of the committee with impunity and went ahead to execute the lease and made payment despite the negative findings on the suitability of the property made by the committee.
The plaintiffs are further seeking to protect their interests, as well as that of the company itself, from the abuse of controlling powers of majority shareholders, following Zain's decision to lease the disputed property for use as its headquarters.
The court had on May 17, struck out the suit on technical grounds, which necessitated the plaintiffs re-filing it.

Original_Search_Title: RPC, Cleric Among Power Cut 'Victims'
Headline: Tanzania: RPC, Cleric Among Power Cut 'Victims'
Body_Text:
A special operation to collect billions of shillings in unpaid bills by the Tanzania Electric Supply Company (Tanesco) entered its third day yesterday, where a regional police commander and a reverend a were among its first casualties.
However, the technicians failed to disconnect power from Ukonga Prisons, which has reportedly accumulated massive power bills because it is also using electricity to run a brick-making factory. The Ukonga Prisons power network has also been illegally connected to a nearby mosque and a handful of shops within the area.
In one of the operations that focused on residential areas in Dar es Salaam, Tanesco revenue protection officer Mposheleye Mwasenga identified a Mr. Liberatus Barlow, a senior police officer who is also regional police commander for Tabora, as being one whose houses were affected during the exercise.
He said the house, with residential number UK/MMD/163 at Ukonga which belongs to Mr. Barlow, was disconnected from Tanesco's grid after it was established that the residents had accumulated bills amounting Sh4 million.
The huge bill became the tip of an iceberg when further inspection revealed that a house with residential numbers UK/MMD/265, situated at Ukonga Banana, owned by Reverend Clement Mganda of the Winners Chapel had unpaid bills running to the tune of Sh19million.
On Monday, the newly-appointed Tanesco managing director, Mr William Mhando, unveiled the operation which is aimed at collecting Sh300million the utility firm is owed by its customers who include individuals, government and private institutions.
Out of the Sh300billion in debts, government institutions, including the Tanzania People's Defence Forces (TPDF) and the Dar es Salaam Water and Sewerage Authority (Dawasa), owe the power utility firm a total of Sh58.5billion, according to Mr Mhando. Private institutions and individuals owe the company Sh241.5billion,
He added that customers who have accumulated debts were due for power disconnection beginning two months ago. In surprising revelations, while briefing journalists during the operation, Mr Mwasenga said a Sh19million debt was not documented in Tanesco's computers at its headquarters along Morogoro road.
"It is likely that someone (a Tanesco employee) might lose his job if it will be established that the Sh19million debt is not in our system and that it is not attached to any payment account," warned the utility firm's revenue protection officer.
He said when Tanesco technicians popped in the house owned by the Tabora RPC, an elderly woman knelt in front of them, pleading to be spared from disconnection. When the technicians arrived at the house, a houseboy started phoning a Deo Lyimo and a Mama Julie, who were identified as residents who happen to be the RPC's younger brother and his wife, respectively.
Surprisingly, Lyimo and Mama Julie failed to produce payment receipts and Lyimo allegedly asked the Tanesco technicians not to disconnect electricity because he had instructed his houseboy to give them 'something'. The Tanesco technicians reportedly refused the offer insisting that if he wanted to clear the bills he should go straight to Tanesco offices.
Tanesco communications manager, Badra Masoud, said the operation will continue and it will sometimes be carried out at night in collaboration with the police so as to net people connected to the grid illegally.

Original_Search_Title: TJI Ends SUG Week in Grand Style
Headline: Nigeria: TJI Ends SUG Week in Grand Style
Body_Text:
The Rector, Times Journalism Institute, an arm of the Daily Times of Nigeria, Mr Ndubuisi Ugbede has urged the management of the school to paddle the newly admitted students to enable their success in the journalism profession as he also advised the students to go about their studies with seriousness in order to enable them go through the school and not the other way round.
Ugbede said this last week during the schools 45th student union governments (SUG) week, held at the school premises in Lagos, with the theme "The role of journalists in sustaining democracy".
In the course of the event, the one time Sunday Times Editor and a senior lecturer in the institute, Mr Dele Odebiyi, described culture is a reflection of the totality of the personality of the people, it talks about the way we behave, our mode of dressing is part of our culture, how we think, our reactions when thins happen.
Above all is the language of the people, you get to know where a person is essentially from the language the person speaks, Nigeria as a country have about 353 languages so therefore Nigeria those not have just one culture. we have the Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa, Ibibio's and the rest of them, we have things that reflect the cultural ability, if you link it to what is called democracy in this country, then the theme of this week should be journalists and our democracy" Odebiyi said.
He also noted that, culture concerns all of us. Therefore, we should look into other peopes culture just as we do to ours.
Furthermore, Nigeria is a country where we have about 353 languages, it is very difficult to define such a country as having only one culture as it is important and paramount to those who posses them.
According to him, the average Nigerian child has neglected his/her culture by inviting the English man's way of life.
Where is the culture of story telling in our lives today, the traditional education in Yoruba land, the first contact that the child makes in content of the culture is through story telling and I know that every culture has the element of story telling. Through story telling, we are able to impact cultural imperative to the younger ones."
"In the past when we practice so much of our culture, if someone wants to sell bananas, along the road were people walk through, all they do was to bring lives of bananas, spread them at the junction, put three four bananas as the case may be, how they want to sell them together as a bunch, and put's one kobo on the leave, the intention is, any body who wanted to buy some will see that one kobo as the mark of how much each bunch is sold for, even when the seller is not around, people drop one kobo for one bunch of banana they pick, at the end of the day, may be about 6:pm the owner of the bananas comes back, he still gets back to find the complete amount of the bananas which he left for sale, and also the one kobo he kept as the price tag for the bananas. People never thought of stealing, but tody, it's the other way round" he said.
The event eventually came to an end with a pageantry display which emerged the new campus queen of the institute. The new queen Miss Rebecca did not fail to expressed her excitement stating how overwhelmed she felt, haven emerged the winner of the 2010 campus pageantry.
"I feel so happy, and will continue from where the past queen stopped, I will bring out by best and with the aid of other students and executives of the school to make Times Journalism Institute great academic environ for the students." She said.

Original_Search_Title: Bello, a Rebel With a Cause
Headline: Nigeria: Bello, a Rebel With a Cause
Body_Text:
MR. Bukhari Bello, presently the subject of speculations for the chairmanship of the Independent National Electoral Commission, INEC, has a history of rebellion to the very system that has upheld electoral fraud in Nigeria.
Before he was appointed and subsequently dismissed as Executive Secretary of the National Human Rights Commission, NHRC, Buhari had set himself on the positive side of history with his quiet but determined opposition to the ploys of anti-democratic forces to sabotage the conduct of the June 12, 1993 presidential election.
As Director of Legal Services of the then National Electoral Commission, NEC, Buhari was a quiet but resolute force in checking the perceived moves of then Attorney General of the Federation Clement Akmagbo and those allegedly aligned with him to frustrate the conduct of the election.
Bello's proclivity to democratic tenets, however, became pronounced during the Obasanjo civilian era when he turned the Human Rights Commission from being an apologist of the ruling government into a critical articulator analyst of government human rights violations.
A year into his second five year tenure at the NHRC in 2006, Buhari was unceremoniously dismissed from his job for the commission's sharp criticism of the detention of the broadcast journalist Gbenga Aruleba by the federal authorities.
He was returned to the Ministry of Justice as a bureaucrat and had remained largely out of the public eye until the recent speculations of his appointment as Chairman of INEC, an organization where he left a legacy of astuteness.
Professor Humphrey Nwosu, the chairman of the NEC in his book on the election published in 2008 spoke effusively of Mr. Bello's steadfastness against the antics of the anti-June 12 strategists.
Recounting how he found Buhari a useful ally after he was reportedly shut out by then President Ibrahim Babangida in the midst of the bedlam just before June 12, 1993, Nwosu in his book titled "Laying the foundation For Nigeria's Democracy: My Account of June 12, 1993 Presidential election and Its Annulment", said:
"As I was unable to contact the President, I decided to visit the residence of the Attorney General of the Federation. On my arrival, he appeared surprised, as he was not expecting me. He was conferring with Barrister Philip Umeadi, SAN, who was the same counsel for Abimbola Davis and his 'Association for Better Nigeria'.
"Immediately Barrister Umeadi, an experienced Senior Advocate of Nigeria who knew me very well, saw me, he uttered, 'Humphrey, I do not want to do anything with NEC,' and left me alone with the Attorney-General of the Federation. He entered one of the rooms in the Attorney-General's residence," he said.
Transition to civil rule programme
Nwosu said Akpamgbo assured him that the elections would hold, saying: "He informed me that the Director of Legal Services, NEC, Buhari Bello, had just left his residence that he directed him (Bukhari Bello) to assemble and bring to him all the relevant decrees relating to the Transition to Civil Rule programme.
He explained further that he would use all these documents in a meeting of National Defence and Security Council and other principal officers of government scheduled for 10.00 a.m. that morning. He claimed that the purpose of the meeting was to find a solution to the court order restraining NEC from conducting June 12, 1993 presidential election.
He asked me to return to my office that after the meeting, he would inform me to go ahead with the scheduled election.
"As I left his residence, I betrayed no emotions. However, I was surprised to learn from him that the meeting of the highest ruling body of the country (NDSC) would start within an hour to deliberate on the presidential election scheduled for the next day and that I, the Chief Electoral Officer of the federation, was not invited."
Nwosu further narrated suspicions by Bello of a plot by Akpamgbo against the election who he accused of employing diversionary schemes with the instruction to him ( Bello ) to assemble the decrees governing the transition.
"He also went further to express his shock and dismay in meeting Chief Philip Umeadi, the lawyer of ABN that morning who was apparently putting up with the Attorney-General of the Federation."
Nwosu book said he convinced Bello for the two of them to bump into the meeting of the NDSC holding later that morning.
Explaining how Akpamgbo opposed the holding of the election at the meeting, Nwosu said: "Our suspicion about the position of the Attorney-General was not misplaced for he took an opposing view from that of NEC. He stated that NEC should rather obey the Abuja High Court order and postpone the election.
This was contrary to what he told Bello and I in his residence. Besides, he claimed then I was not a lawyer. He stated that we should obey the High Court order and later appeal against the decision of the High Court.
He said the only way out of over-ruling the High Court was enactment of a new decree. Bello, the Director of Legal Services in NEC took him on. He respectfully reminded him about the Court of Appeal ruling on the matter in Komolafe versus Omole."
Nwosu asserted that Bello alleged mischief on the part of those against the election as he said the order was filed on May 18, 1993 and only served on the commission on June 8 and a ruling given by the now late Justice Ikpeme in the night of June 10, 1993.
"The President after weighing the arguments on both sides of the divide was convinced that NEC position was quite tenable and that the Abuja High Court order would not stop us from conducting the June 12 presidential election.
We were about sharing a smile of victory when we heard shuffling of papers and legs. And as we looked up, we also heard the collective voice of the military colleagues of Mr. President saying: 'We are not banana republic. No one should tell us what to do. Postpone the election at least for one week to prove that we are sovereign nation.'
"We were surprised and wondered what could have given rise to the new development. On our inquiry, the Senior Security Officer sitting close to me, shoved a piece of paper containing a release from a Director of Information in United States of America embassy. It reads: 'We are awaiting the Federal Government's reaction to the high court decision.
However, any postponement of tomorrow's election is unacceptable to the US Government.'
"The statement by the Director of Information of the US embassy agitated the President's military colleagues and made them to demand that the Presidential election should be postponed even for one week in order to assert Nigeria 's Sovereignty.
"At this point in time, I looked up to the President and our eyes met. He sought my view on the release from the US embassy, which had made his colleagues agitated and demand the postponement of the election. I was very forthright in expressing my views," Nwosu writes as he said he condemned the statement but equally advised that the release should be ignored.
"Our advice might have pleased the President and displeased some of his colleagues who did not want the election to take place. They wondered why he should listen to a civilian official who was not even a member of the ruling council."

Original_Search_Title: 'Declare Kaujeua a Hero'
Headline: Namibia: 'Declare Kaujeua a Hero'
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Jackson Kaujeua's death has once again brought to the fore public debate about who qualifies to be a hero or heroine in an independent Namibia.
Kaujeua' death last Thursday follows that of another eminent Namibian from the security sector, Commissioner Ruben Ashipala, who died last month. Ashipala was not declared a national hero despite his extraordinary exploits during the liberation struggle and calls to confer him hero status.
This too led to the perception, in some quarters, that only politicians are declared heroes once they are departed.
In the case of Kaujeua, artists want to see him declared a national hero and buried at the Heroes' Acre, because of the enormous role he played politically using culture and music.
"He might not have been a politician but he is a hero. Being a hero is not only sitting in Parliament. If they don't declare him a hero, then they don't know what a hero is, he deserves to be at the Heroes' Acre, no doubt about that," said Ndilimani vocalist, Castro Ileni.
"That guy was a legend, each of us here took a leaf out of Jackson's book of life.
His contribution is big; he did not fight with guns, and he did not fight through politics but used language as a means of his contribution. His music reminded us about freedom. It was what inspired freedom and that is just as important as the role played by politicians," said Allen Jonathan, also known by his stage name Swartbaster.
Founder member of Ndilimani, Banana Shekupe, concurs with Jonathan and Ileni: "I call on all comrades in Swapo and leaders of Swapo to declare him a hero."
A Cabinet ad hoc committee in 2007 under the chairpersonship of then Deputy Prime Minister, Libertina Amathila, to develop criteria for the determination of national heroes/heroines for burial at the Heroes' Acre, defined a hero as a legendary figure (person) of great strength or ability, admired for achievements and qualities, who showed exceptional courage, perseverance and charisma in performing extraordinary deeds for the benefit of the nation/society.
Such deeds can be political, military, economic and social excellence or great service to the state and nation. The committee was composed of then Minister of Information and Broadcasting, Netumbo Nandi-Ndaitwah, Minister of Defence, Charles Namoloh, Minister of Safety and Security, Peter Sheehama, Director General of the National Central Intelligence Agency, Lukas Hangula, and Ngarikutuke Tjiriange, who, at the time of his appointment, served on the committee as Secretary General of the SWAPO Party.
The committee said the status of hero/heroine could also be bestowed upon an institution, place or equipment. This includes among others, a person distinguished by extraordinary bravery; a person who is goal-oriented and does not give up until the goal is achieved; a person who stays level-headed in a crisis; who does more than what is expected by others; who is willing to risk personal safety to help others; who is consistent, persistent and of an impeccable character; who is doing what is right, irrespective of personal consequences; and a person who is prepared to serve and sacrifice far greater than his/her self-interest.
The committee determined that certain benefits should accompany the decoration of hero/heroines of Namibia. Such benefits can be monetary awards or status in society and the benefits should be defined and regulated by an Act of Parliament.
The committee further found that the Heroes' Acre is not a cemetery and that its value should be maintained. Only people who have been conferred the two highest honours shall be entitled to be buried at the Heroes' Acre.
The committee also advised that the law should provide for beneficiaries to lose their awards if they commit dishonourable acts after they have been decorated.
It (the committee) deliberated on the naming of monuments after living people and recommended a moratorium on naming monuments, buildings, equipment and streets after living people. The committee felt that such naming should be done posthumously.
Meanwhile, Kaujeua will be buried at Ovitoto at the weekend as per his wish, according to family sources.

Original_Search_Title: It is Wrong for Parties to Jettision Zoning --PPA Chieftain
Headline: Nigeria: It is Wrong for Parties to Jettision Zoning --PPA Chieftain
Body_Text:
Ozumba — In your view, so far how have we fared democratically?
In the first instance, I must say it has been good and bad. The fact that we have succeeded in having civilians hand over power to civilians in this country is a very good development. Again the fact that we have succeeded in managing the Augean crisis ignited by the ill-health of the late president, Alhaji Umaru Yar'Adua, though with a lot of labour, we handed over to the incumbent President, Dr. Goodluck Jonathan is another plus. These are sure signs that democracy is taking roots in the country.
But, basically, if we look at the three arms of government we'll find out that more often than not our attention has been focused more on the executive without looking at how far the legislature and the judiciary had performed.
I will say that the legislature has been largely negative. Terribly poor. It has failed to intellectually articulate laws and bills that will really advance this country? I will want to draw our attention to the electoral Reform bill that has been there for quite some time now. Nigerians are not impressed with the way it has been dragged up and down and manipulated. From this action we find out in them people who have vested interest and therefore not open to the experiment of core democratic principles.
It is a sad development because we are looking forward to the legislature to come up with the electoral reforms that will put us on the map of countries with sound democratic institutions. So I must say that it has not been too good for the legislature.
Again, if we also look at the judiciary, we will find out that in some cases they have come up with judgments that really excited Nigerians. One of the biggest days of my life was the day Chief Bode George was jailed for corruption. For me it was hallmark of judicial activism.
But then, the way cases are lingering, in our courts, for instance the Imo state governorship tussle which is still hanging in the courts, when it should have been dispensed of a long time ago, is a sad commentary.
You see when you have spent three years in the office and at the end of the day the judgment does not favour you or even if it goes in your favour, you will find out that you have disoriented many issues. I must stress that justice delayed is justice denied. So the delay in handling issues that come up over courts is a big draw back.
Besides in some cases you find out that some of the judges have been guilty of gross incompetence or have been corrupted, so to say, or intimidated into towing lines that cannot be judicially defended. Nevertheless, I still trust that democracy is our best option.
A number of the challenges you have raised justify the clamour for electoral reforms. There is however, a controversy on the adoption of justice Uwais report. Should it be adopted fully or in amended form what is your opinion on this issue?
You see, the legislature can always add value to any bill or recommendations brought to it. The value it adds must be in consonance with democratic principles and the expectations of the civilized world. Core issues that must be addressed include, that one man must have one vote, the issue of rigging and other electoral fraud in all ramifications and of course intimidation and manipulation of electoral laws, as well as, systems must be addressed.
It is painful that Ghana is today being ranked as a civilized social formation when Nigeria is still within the rank of undeveloped social formations. Why?
It is a function of its electoral system. That is the simplest answer. In Ghana we saw a situation where an incumbent president was defeated and he conceded. But in Nigeria even local government chairmen are not easy to get out of the office and that is the habit of fraud.
If you are in office, and you know that you can manipulate the system (the electoral process) to perpetuate yourself in power, the tendency is that such a system will be corrupt. But if you know that at the end of the day you will be accountable and the electoral process must throw a verdict of guilt on you by throwing you out of power, the tendency will be for you to be sure you are more pro-people in your orientation, attitude and governance.
Regrettably, a lot of local government officials are anti-people and they are all building structures of deceit to manipulate the system in their favour so that they can remain in office to perpetuate injustice on the people.
