Do you subscribe to the view that a positive electoral reform/system starts with the appointment of the helmsman. How would you want this issue settled in Nigeria?
I believe in strong institutions and not persons. If you put the best of persons into a weak institution you will also have a negative result. The process of appointing the helmsman either at the Independent National Electoral commission (INEC) or Independent state electoral commission (ISEC) level. I, agree is very crucial. But it is not all about it. It must not be a case of garbage in, garbage out. A man with integrity, principles, self-convictions can change a system. It is not always you put in a man to deliver you, even in very corrupt systems.
But I feel that it is not proper for the president or the governor to appoint an umpire over himself. So that system, I agree should be considered, but it is not the be all and end all of it. It is just one.
What is your view on zoning and internal democracy within the political party?
I come from the Progressive Peoples Alliance (PPA). I am the Imo state chairman of PPA. We strongly believe in the principles of internal democracy.
We strongly believe in the principles of internal democracy. You see in Anambra State, despite the fact that we had our opinion, but when a woman emerged as the governorship candidate, we went ahead with her. At least we made one point that somebody can win nomination and be allowed to run.
On the zoning issue, in a social polity like ours the tendency for people to feel marginalized is quite there and you must integrate them by giving them their due. You also know that zoning, at times produces mediocrity. It might not give you the very best you deserve. Against this background, I think there should be a blend of this zoning and the ability to deliver services.
In PPA we look at zoning but at a point, where we find somebody who is very capable of flying our flag. And moving us forward, we might opt for that person. If somebody coming from a particular zone is an electoral liability what do you do? You go for the other person. But when you have somebody who is qualified coming from a particular zone that feels it's their turn to be favoured, of course, natural justice demands that it should be given to such a person.
Do you see anything wrong with President, Dr. Goodluck Jonathan Contesting the 2011 Presidential elections?
There are two issues there. In my personal opinion, I want Dr. Goodluck Jonathan not to push his luck beyond goodluck. I will prefer a situation whereby he sets the standard of decency, intently and trust by putting everything in to complete this term and charging the affairs of this country for ever. I will want people to beg Dr. Jonathan to continue. I wouldn't want him to deploy all the arsenals he has now to try to start manipulating the system to make sure he comes on board as President again. That will derail him and that might be the limit for goodluck. Nigeria is not a banana republic. If there are agreed issues let the issues be agreed.
But then, if extensions ought to be made, let it be made by the people not by sycophants. The Newspapers are already agog with calls for him to run. These are the people who would lead him to Golgotha. So he has to apply wisdom in what he is doing.
Let's look inwards into the PPA. With the exit of His Excellency. Dr. Ikedi Ohakim, what is the situation in the Imo State PPA?
The truth is that the exit of His Excellency is only a temporary set back. This is because in a political party there are those who are there simply for pecuniary benefits in the system and once the man they are looking forward to quits, the tendency is for them to quit. So his exit was of good riddance because all along, Chief Ohakim did not support the PPA. He just saw it as a vehicle that took him to that office and he did not do anything to empower the party. I prefer to be the son of a man I can mention as my father than the "bastard son of somebody that is the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria."
After his exit we took stock and we gained a lot of sympathy from the peoplr. People saw this people who are staying back in the party as people of integrity because they are not bothered about contracts and other patronages from the government. People saw them as people who are committed to the advancement of democracy.
Of a truth people love PPA. They see it as their own party. The Action congress (AC) they see as the party of the Yoruba's.
The PDP they see as belonging to the conservative North. So they say that the PPA belongs to the Ibo man and that is to a large extent true. It is therefore not surprising that they have embraced it as their party.
Every where I have been to on this our harmonization/sensitization tour, it has been out pouring of support love and goodwill to the PPA.
Look, if any election is free and fair in this state today, tomorrow without manipulation I assure you PPA will emerge victorious.

Original_Search_Title: Kidnap - Orji Vows to Deal With Sponsors
Headline: Nigeria: Kidnap - Orji Vows to Deal With Sponsors
Body_Text:
Umuahia — Governor Theodore Orji of Abia State has vowed to deal with prominent politicians from the state alleged to be sponsoring kidnappers to terrorise citizens and visitors to the state.
This came against the backdrop of the release of three Chinese expatriate workers kidnapped by hoodlums in Abia State, a week after they were abducted. The governor said investigations conducted by the government had shown that prominent politicians from the state, residing outside it, were deeply involved in sponsoring hoodlums to terrorise residents and visitors to the state.
According to him, the government is already verifying the evidences contained in the investigations. He assured that once the facts were substantiated, it would be made open and the personalities involved would be laid bare.
Governor Orji vowed that no person fingered in the report would be spared as kidnapping had dented the good image of Abia.
Amnesty expires
He explained that the amnesty programme initiated by his administration for repentant kidnappers was not a sign of weakness, even as he reminded those still engaged in the crime that the deadline for them to repent and surrender their arms was yesterday.
"But where they prove stubborn and feel that they have arms and can continue to harrass people and disturb the peace of Abia State, the government will also show that it is here to make sure that lives are secured," Orji warned.
The kidnapped expatriate workers, who are staff of NIFEST Steel Rolling Mill, Aba, were brought to Government House, Umuahia, yesterday, at the instance of Governor Orji, who expressed his sympathy with kidnap victims and assured them that they would not encounter such experience in the state anymore.
In his remarks, the General Manager of NIFEST Steel Rolling Mill, Mr. Charles Ogbonna, said the release of the Chinese was due to the initiative of the state government in proclaiming amnesty to the kidnappers and other criminals in the state.
"Actually, if not for this initiative, I do not know what could have happened to these men," Ogbonna said, noting that they were confused after the kidnappers informed them that they were holding the expatriate workers.
"The situation was worsened because the three Chinese do not speak and do not understand English language, which made communication with their captors very difficult. Their captors took to the bush and seized their laptops, money while they were fed with few fingers of bananas," he added.
The situation, it was learnt, made the staff of the steel mill to be enveloped in confusion until last Wednesday when the state government got involved in discussing with the kidnappers through the special adviser to the governor on security, Col. Ezichi Kalu (rtd).

Original_Search_Title: Kwali Chairman Calls for Investment On Children's Education
Headline: Nigeria: Kwali Chairman Calls for Investment On Children's Education
Body_Text:
The Kwali Area Council Chairman, Joseph Shazin, Thursday urged parents as well as stakeholders in the council to invest morally or financially in their children's education.
Shazin, who spoke during the children's day celebration at Kwali, assured that his administration will not waste time in providing all the necessary educational facilities, scholarships as well as payment of school fees to pupils and students, adding that the council is already liaising with relevant departments on education matters.
The chairman warned that his administration would not tolerate child abused by parents and explained that hawking of sachet water, bananas, oranges on the streets by children are negative tendency in a society.
During the march past, Government Secondary School, Kwali, emerged first in the senior category, while Government Day Secondary School (GDSS) came second..
In the other category, Junior Secondary School, Yangoji came first, while Kwali Central School, Kwali, came second with Ambassador Global Academy coming third.
At Abaji, over 30 schools participated during the Children's Day celebration that was made colourful by drama displays presents by students.
Council Chairman, Alhaji Yahaya Musa Mohammad, who was represented by his vice, expressed satisfaction at the level of preparation for the event and called on the students to face their books in order to be great leaders of the country.
At the end of the march past, Government Girls Secondary School Abaji took first in the senior category, while Junior Secondary School, Yaba and LEA Primary School, Nuku came first in the junior as well as primary schools categories respectively.

Original_Search_Title: A Slow Fuse Burning
Headline: East Africa: A Slow Fuse Burning
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Only the week before I bemoaned the fact that many artists when changing style in the name of developing their talent seem to take one step forward and two steps back.
But last week I came across one artist who has proved to be an exception to this gloomy rule: Larissa Hoops.
Hoops, still only 29, is perhaps best known for her deconstruction of breakfast tables... each picture consisting of six or so engaging small paintings of a table cloth (usually a colourful khanga), a bunch of bananas, a newspaper and a coffee pot and sugar bowl which, when hung together, form one inviting spread.
Two such groupings -- each proving that the whole is greater than the sum of its parts -- are in her current exhibition at Le Rustique restaurant on Nairobi's General Mathenge Road.
But it is her latest work that caught my eye this time. First, there is a series of super-realistic street scenes and, secondly and more importantly, there is a group of collages that I believe might well make this artist's name.
The street scenes are a useful showcase of Hoops's ability. Out she goes with her camera, often using gigantic billboards as a backdrop, then it's back to the studio for a few self indulgent days seeing just how accurately she can get it all down. The answer seems to be, very accurately, indeed.
The passers-by would recognise themselves, I'm sure of that, and Viceroy and Zain, among other leading Kenyan brands, should be paying her royalties for the puff.
But it is Hoops's collages that are outstanding. There are four on show -- all of room interiors, one of a kitchen, one of a salon and two of the same sitting room, made two years apart.
Each is meticulously constructed with paper, photographs, cloth, and paint. And this painstaking effort is amply rewarded with the creation, quite simply, of some of the finest work I have seen by any artist recently.
There is in them an echo of the 1956 collage of Richard Hamilton called Just what is it that makes today's homes so different, so appealing? That picture, also a room interior, with its Charles Atlas muscleman flexing his biceps next to a bikini beauty (below), was one of the precursors of Pop Art.
Hoops's pictures are rather less frantic, more spacious and certainly less eclectic.
This quietness gives them a long fuse that smokes away as you view them until realisation arrives with a flash.
The two sitting room pictures, called Tea and Toast and Tea and Toast 2 deal with nothing less than the state of the nation.
They take the post-election violence as their starting point yet, unlike most artists and photographers who stop at the murder and the flames, they take us forward to today, to a more prosperous and settled time when, with the violence behind us, we hope for a better and fairer future.
Tea and Toast was made in February 2008 and records the height of the violence. It shows a very ordinary sitting room with a cup of tea and two toasted jam sandwiches on the table.
In a bookcase to the left are newspapers (the Saturday Nation has the headline Ray of Hope) and books that wittily include Matthew Kneale's short stories Small Crimes in an Age of Abundance.
Through the window, beyond the neat white picket fence, can be seen absolute mayhem. People are engulfed by fire, a car is a burnt-out wreck, the neighbourhood is ablaze. The second picture Tea and Toast 2, was made in February this year.
Superficially, it is the same room. But a careful look reveals a number of changes (rather like those paired pictures in children's puzzle books where you are invited to spot the differences.) Prosperity has arrived. There is now a tablecloth, for instance, and the old battered TV has been replaced by a smart plasma Sony with Playstation controls.
The toasted sandwich on the table looks as though it could be ham instead of jam and a book has been added to the shelves: Starting Your Own Business. The headline in the Nation states: Kenya marches to a new dawn. Clearly it is a time of confidence, of renewed certainty in a future worth investing in. The fuse is still slowly burning -- and then comes the flash.
Look through the window and all is calm. Peace reigns. The burnt-out shell of the car is still there but it is being used as a display table by a vegetable seller. Elsewhere people quietly go about their business beneath a clear sky, unafraid. These are important pictures and deserve to find a home in a major Kenyan collection. They are incisive and relevant to the nation and must stay together.
Whoever does end up the owner, they can be confident they have bought a piece of Kenya's history, as well as two very fine pictures, indeed.
Frank Whalley runs Lenga Juu, a fine arts and media consultancy based in Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: 'History Books Distort the Zimbabwe Story'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'History Books Distort the Zimbabwe Story'
Body_Text:
A scholarly friend who observes these things remarked to me recently that we must be the only country in the world with four sets of O'level text books all written by the same authors. That made me think. Our young people's ignorance of history which I have lived through took me further.
We all know that everyone who tells history rewrites it. That is a simple statement of the fact that none of us is free from bias. If we recognise that, and try to correct it, that is no bad thing. When the bias is deliberately built in to serve some sectional interests (which usually means the present rulers), then you have a dangerous situation.
For example, the mantra repeated frequently on ZBC every April 18: "Independence day means the day we celebrate Cde Mugabe's victory over the British," raises more questions than it answers. Pardon me if I give a little more theory before I come back to "facts" like this.
All officially-taught history is to some extent a myth. That doesn't mean a lie, though it might be. By "myth" I mean a story we tell ourselves to explain this strange world we live in and to define our place in it. The stories grandmothers used to tell their grandchildren are myths in this sense; some were fables and some were history she had heard or experienced. All were offering an explanation and a justification of how we lived. But history is built on facts. You may be able to hide facts or destroy evidence so that we lose the knowledge of past facts, but they still affect us. If we can't give a fairly accurate account of how we got where we are, we have lost a lot of guidance about how to get where we want to go from here. We have lost an understanding of ourselves.
Those frequent rewritings of our official history tell us that important facts and people are being airbrushed out of memory and out of our national myth, just as official photos of Soviet leaders had to be airbrushed afresh after each of Stalin's purges.
I don't find those O'level texts any more pleasant to read than the opinion columns in our state press, but my impression is that the official national history taught in schools (and they don't teach anybody else's history, which might provide a little balance) is being boiled down to that simple statement about a victory over the British.
Try asking your children or grandchildren "Who was the first President of Zimbabwe?" or "Who founded Zanu?" and, very often, you will get the same incorrect answer to both questions. Well, losers like Canaan Banana and Ndabaningi Sithole do get airbrushed out of history. Predecessors of present rulers are often allowed to fade away. That megalomaniac Qin Shi Huang, who called himself the "First Emperor" of China, tried to have all books written before his time destroyed, so that history would begin with him, as if he had come down from heaven and shaped China single-handed.
Yes Qin Shi Huang did unite the various kingdoms China had split into during the "Warring States Period", but tradition said there had been unified governments before that. He is called the builder of the Great Wall, but those kingdoms had all built walls to defend themselves against raids by the non-Chinese "barbarians". He just started the job of joining those walls to make one Great Wall. Plenty of other rulers have tried to create myths of their own uniqueness and how irreplaceable they are. We see examples in our own national/tribal/party myth.
Mysterious deaths can always be given new explanations. Some English people still argue about whether "the wicked Richard Crookback" murdered his nephews in the Tower of London so that he could become king or whether, as King Richard III, he was a much-maligned man who showed promise of being a great king -- and all that happened in 1483!
So we could see the arguments about Chitepo, Tongogara, Parirenyatwa and others outlive us. If they don't just become non-persons in the shadow or the blinding light of one Great Leader.
An interesting feature of our myth is that not only predecessors and rivals get the airbrush treatment. Enemies don't disappear entirely, but they remain as shadows. A shadow is another name, among some peoples, for a ghost.
Enemies who get this treatment remain to haunt us, and because they haunt us, the Great Leader who once defeated them is still needed to defend us against them again.
Anyone over 20 years of age must have noticed the big inaccuracy in that statement about "Mugabe's victory over the British".
Ian Smith has been airbrushed out of the picture. Cecil Rhodes isn't mentioned in the whole story at all. One manipulated the British government and the other rebelled against it, but it is more convenient to attribute all their evil deeds to "the British". After all, if our Great Leader was to tell us that he was defending us against Rhodes's efforts to recolonise us, someone would surely say "But isn't he dead?" Likewise with Smith. Our Leader can't make Tony Blair the bogeyman now that man has moved to a different job. Ranting about Gordon Brown didn't sound so convincing -- a gentle, inarticulate man and no media star, so even ZTV couldn't make him look worth hating.
Now the spin doctors face a period of short-term British leadership. They can't rewrite everything every time Britain gets a new Prime Minister, so "the British" make the best permanent enemy.
Even if someone was to pull the plug out of their foggy, inhospitable little island and sink it, a few of them would survive. They would still be around as long as our Great Leader wants to rant against them.
And if, after airbrushing Joshua Nkomo out of the picture, he does the same to Mzilikazi and Lobengula, we'll have the makings of another Gukurahundi, but I trust the Ndebele people will still be around when most of today's big names are forgotten.
The myth is being simplified into something like the one the rulers in George Orwell's 1984 aimed for; a boot grinding a face, forever. History, any history we can remember, assures us that however hard dictators try, they can't make life quite as bad as that. One way to stop anything like that happening here would be for grandparents to tell the young the history they have lived through, full of names they remember, names that may not appear in school history books. Names like Burombo, Savanhu, Chikerema, Nyandoro, Mzingeli, Muzorewa . . . And so on, heroes or villains.
Children should learn that nobody is pure hero or pure villain. Children in Mbare should be taught to ask where the names of their streets came from. The answers would fill volumes. But I remind any young readers that "every time an old person dies, a library goes up in flames". Don't let that happen to your grandparents or the old man sitting in the sun on the corner. Collect their stories now.

Original_Search_Title: Bongo Flava - Newcomers Ruling the Roost
Headline: Tanzania: Bongo Flava - Newcomers Ruling the Roost
Body_Text:
Style and elegance took centre stage as celebrities rubbed shoulders at the Diamond Jubilee Hall, Dar es Salaam, at the red-carpet Kili Music Awards ceremony last weekend.
The 11th edition of the annual awards, which had a total of 22 categories, featured the bigwigs of the local entertainment industry as the scene turned into a 'who is who?'
The parking yard opposite the hall told it all, from the small Toyota Starlets to the fuel guzzling Range Rovers.
This was just the place to be to catch them all, big and small as they basked in the limelight with Jamaican-American star Sean Kingston in their midst.
The show hosted by Jokate Mwegelo, had a mix and match of them all, from Movie stars, former beauty queens, comedians, radio and TV presenters to soccer stars.
Towering Memphis Grizzles' star Hashim Thabeet was part of the distinguished audience dwarfing everyone with his athletic posture. Yanga boss Imani Madega was there, too, to catch the proceedings of the night.
To be fair the trick of an invite-only worked, as the organisers sieved the audience to perfection.
The Awards
This year's glory went to male artistes in all accounts, the awards, the performances and even fashion.
Up-and-coming Bongo Flava artiste Diamond aka Naseeb Abdul, was the toast of the night after he bagged three awards, a record for a rookie who only came to the big stage a year ago.
He took the Best Up-and-Coming artiste of the Year title , beating fellow newcomers Amini, Belle 9, Quick Racka and Barnaba.
He also went on to win the Song of the Year Award for his hit 'Kamwambie' and he was to cap it up with the Best RnB Song, ahead of AT's Nipigie featuring Stara Thomas.
The other big winner on the night were Mzee Yusuf and Jahazi Modern Taarab who have revolutionised Taarab music.
They bagged a treble: Best Composer, Best Taarab Album and Best Taarab Song, fending off competition from bands like Coast Modern Taarab, Five Star, East African Melody and New Zanzibar Stars.
The African Stars 'Twanga Pepeta' who gave a thrilling show snatched a brace Best Album of the Year (Kiswahili) and Best Kiswahili Single.
Lady Jay Dee was voted the Best Female Artiste, Burundi's Kidumu and Ugandan diva Juliana took the East African category (Haturudi Nyuma) and Joh Makini carried home the gong for the Best Hip hop Song (Steam Zimelipiwa) as Chid Benzi scooped the Hip hop Artiste of the Year award.
Putting dancehall in the arena, Bwana Misosi resurrected from obscurity to win the Best Ragga Song ('Mungu Yuko Bize'). The award was presented to him by the soft spoken Jamaican-American star, Sean Kingston.
Nipigie ,a song which has brought back Stara Thomas into the limelight did not disappoint as it was declared the Best Collabo of the Year, though she wasn't in the audience.
The happiest family without doubt was the Zorros, Banana won the Best Male Artiste award, his father Ally's contribution to the industry was honoured in the Hall of Fame.
The hugs and kisses told it all as Banana and his wife, sister Maunda Zorro and Zahir himself took to the stage to crown a family affair.
The institutional Hall of Fame Award went to Clouds FM. Led by Ruge Mutahaba they were not any different from what you hear on radio, as they took to the stage.
Other winners on the night were AY, for the Best Reggae Song (Leo remix featuring Kenyan diva Wahu), Halid Chokora, Lamar aka Niekamp Sjoens , Marlaw, and Cpwaa to complete the long list of winners on the night Performances
A warm performance by songbird Mwasiti set the mood for the night with well coordinate choreography by the Tanzania House of Talent (THT).
After a short break, Offside Trick came on stage to a thunderous applause as they went through raunchy motions of their song 'Bata'. They were followed by Mzee Yusuf who gave a thrilling account of Taarab's increasing popularity.
Banana and the B-Band sampled MJ's 'They Don't really Care about Us'. There is one thing that I don't understand, why does he keep using helmets at his performances , hopefully he will let us know soon.
Apart from hosting the show, Jokate switched roles to team up with AY to give a breathtaking performance of 'Kings and Queens' with such creativity that overshadowed all the other performances.
Speeches
Different guests who were asked to announce the winners made a meal out of the situation with very lengthy and sometimes very irrelevant speeches.
Some even sought personal recognition, Young Africans' boss Imani Madega made a scene when he chanted 'Yanga oye slogan', he was booed for that.
Magic FM presenter Dida who was called on to present the Best Taarab award became too wordy.
The award winners most whom were just not well prepared and some ended up mumbling inaudible things.
May be this will serve as a lesson in future to artistes to prepare their speeches in advance from the moment they are nominated.
Fashion
Being leggy by all standards seemed to be the night's statement for the fashion conscious females, judging from the many short dresses.
But some chose to go grand with flowing gowns.
Maunda Zorro had a paisley get-up that left tongues wagging, especially being in the presence of her father.
Former EATV presenter Maimartha Jesse caught eyes too when she stepped out in that other purple dress of hers, which I think I have seen on more than one occasion. There was also the oddly see-through spotted by Dida Wa Mchops.
Some just kept it modest. Nyosh El Sadat of FM Academia gave it a Congolese touch with a shiny black suit. Of those who graced the podium, Yanga Africans' mid-fielder Athuman Idd's was a definite fashion miss when he turned up in trainers with folded sleeves, Mmmh!
MC Jokate made a fashion statement of the night when she changed three times. First in some easy, chic dresses,I am told was designed by Ally Remtullah. She then slipped into an over flowing glamorous purple gown with a peep toe shoe.
The stage
The stage was a spectacle to behold with breathtaking lighting and décor and all the colours of the spectrum were well represented in the well demarcated seating arrangement.
Grips took turns to clear the stage immediately a performance.
Despite earlier hitches from the nomination days when big names like TID and Dully Sykes withdrew, it was a night to remember.
The show was one of the best in recent times. There was an obvious dominance of Bongo Flava.

Original_Search_Title: Mutsekwa in Protests Ban U-Turn
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mutsekwa in Protests Ban U-Turn
Body_Text:
HOME Affairs Minister Giles Mutsekwa yesterday made a U-turn on his ministry's ban on public protests until after the Fifa soccer World Cup, saying it was not government policy to forbid Zimbabweans from exercising their right to express themselves.
Mutsekwa told the Zimbabwe Independent that his ministry was not banning public protests but was appealing to Zimbabweans to demonstrate peacefully during that period.
However, sources in the Criminal Investigations Department confirmed that they had received a circular stating that demonstrations have been banned until after the World Cup in South Africa.
"Yes, we did receive such a circular and as far as we are concerned no demonstrations would be permitted until after the World Cup," said the official.
Asked to explain why an instruction had been issued to the police to ban public protests, Mutsekwa said he was going to look into the issue
as that goes against government policy.
"There is no way this government and myself would make an attempt to ban people's freedoms. As the inclusive government we promote people's liberties and we would not be involved in infringing those rights," he said.
"There is no banning of demonstrations. We made a promise to South Africa that we are going to come up with strategies of helping it stage a peaceful event. To that extent, what we intend to do is to make an appeal to the Zimbabwe population. If they want to engage in demonstrations, they should be peaceful."
This is in contrast to his statement to our sister publication, the Standard, when he said the ban on demonstrations was part of efforts to rebrand the country's image after almost a decade of chaos.
He was quoted saying: "That is true (banning of protests)...it's an arrangement to present Zimbabwe and the region as a whole as a safe destination. It's also a strategy to rebrand the country's image."
The MDC-T has condemned the decision to ban public demonstrations until after the World Cup.
"The decision is not only a violation of the people's basic rights but a confirmation that we have indisputably become a banana republic which is afraid of the people's constitutional right to express themselves," said the MDC.
"The freedoms of expression, movement and association are never threats to any government but provide an opportunity for the people to peacefully express themselves. Freedoms are never a liability but an asset in a true democracy."
The MDC called on the Co-Ministers of Home Affairs to reverse their decision.
"Dictatorships are averse to the people's rights to express themselves and this decision will seriously dent the credibility and image of the inclusive government," it said.
lMeanwhile, the MDC-T has condemned the appointment of former Media and Information Commission chairperson Tafataona Mahoso as head of the recently appointed Zimbabwe Media Commission secretariat, describing him as a "decorated media hangman" who has no place in a reform agenda.
"His (Mahoso) tenure as the chairperson of the now defunct Media and Information Commission is littered with graves of independent newspapers and radio stations. His legacy as a proponent of a shackled media industry speaks for itself. Zimbabweans urgently deserve media reform in fulfilment of the provisions of the Global Political Agreement and the national aspiration to democratise our national space," it said.
During his tenure as chairperson of MIC, Mahoso closed several newspapers.

Original_Search_Title: Can We Trust Goodluck Jonathan?
Headline: Nigeria: Can We Trust Goodluck Jonathan?
Body_Text:
Few days after providence placed him in charge of the affairs of the country, the Gooduck Jonathan presidency is gradually taking shape and showing what it will offer in the coming months. When he was on a cliff-hanger, as a sidelined vice president and as an unsure acting president, he was overly cautious, for obvious reason- any wrong decision could send him to the wrong side of the cliff.
He succeeded at that. Now that he's in control, slip-shod errors and excuses are no longer tenable. He is now solely responsible for his actions and will be held accountable for them; that is if we ordinary Nigerians ever bother to exercise our constitutional powers to make our leaders fall in line. If power translates into confidence, as people say, then I expect the president to have put behind him the stage frights he displayed during his interview with Christiane Amanpour of Cable News Network, CNN,by now. Perhaps he has. His deft political moves these days have already claimed two victims in the People's Democratic Party- Prince Vincent Ogbulafor, and Abdullahi Adamu, the national chairman, and the secretary of its Board of Trustees respectively, and more are in the offing.
Whether he is task-driven or self-centred, patriotic or not, a provincial president or president of all Nigeria, a leader that has nation-wide acceptability or narrow support base, the coming days will prove where his interests and strengths lie. However, in the mean time, some of his actions have elicited concern about his leadership style. They bother on policy inconsistency to arbitrariness and lack of guts and grit (there are speculations that he's torn between Obasanjo and Danjuma about whose overriding dictates he should listen to.)
Last Monday, a change of guard was effected at the Nigeria National Petroleum Corporation, NNPC. Austin Oniwon replaced Shehu Ladan as Group Managing Director, less than two months after Ladan took over. No one knows the high-wire politics that informed the change. But Ladan was appointed by the President himself. The questions now are whether Ladan was imposed on him in the first instance, who did and why? If he accepted the 'imposition" then, what happened that the president suddenly changed his mind? Don't we deserve explanations from government instead of relying on speculations? The case of the NNPC is an interesting one. In the three years of this administration, the corporation has had five GMDs. It is gradually becoming the proverbial banana peels on which whoever steps will slip disastrously. The scramble there is a manifest testimony that other sectors are dysfunctional. Everyone who has the 'connection' wants a piece of the pie that the NNPC represents and, in the end, give nothing in return. Such is the tragedy of the NNPC, the nation's cash cow, for which dirty wars are fought without any thought for the stability of the institution, the sustainability of policies and the survival of the nation.
Let's look at another burning issue. The confirmation of the ex-governor of Kaduna State, Architect Namadi Sambo, as vice president may have brought to an end speculations, permutations, analyses and horse-trading but the wounds inflicted may take long to heal. As with religion and ethnicity in this country, conspiracy theories are being spawned to underscore Jonathan's motive or sincerity. Some say it is a Christian Association of Nigeria, CAN, agenda to install a Christian governor in Kaduna for the first time. Others say Jonathan chose Sambo because of his bigger ambition of being president and, therefore, wanted a vice president who is less controversial, less ambitious and compliant. If the aspect that concerns religion is true and Muslims are sure of their facts, then why did they not prevail on Sambo to turn down the president's "magnanimity"? On the other hand, it shows the president's mastery of intrigues.
Another matter of national concern is the way the Jonathan government is playing games with the Independent National Electoral Commission, INEC. Within two weeks of Professor Maurice Iwu's departure from the commission, it has had two acting chairmen- Philip Umeadi and Solomon Shoyebi, and is expecting a substantive chairman. The wahala is a question of seniority between Umeadi and Shoyebi. Why is it so difficult for the Jonathan government to put its house in order without rubbishing the system and embarrassing the individuals involved, when a simple civil service check can determine the procedures and guidelines for appointments?
There is also the issue of whether he should run or not. Believing that good fortune has come his way, the president is now playing hard to understand. Except the hint he provided during the CNN interview (he said he might run as vice president or as president) it has been silence from him, yet his posters are everywhere, his body language shows he's interested and his Niger Delta brothers are no longer content with one of their own being president for one year; they are agitating for more: they want him to be president for the next four years and crown their dream agitation with another four, making a total of eight years.
In all these permutations, the president is yet to come out with any position. Mr President would do the nation a lot of good if he sets the records straight- what his heart desires and what political jobbers and hangers -on want him to do. In accordance with the Constitution of Nigeria, the president is free to aspire, but what of the moral questions involved, the trust broken, the feelings not assuaged? He should assert himself, instead of giving the impression that he is not presidential enough.

Original_Search_Title: Soccer World Sex Workers Fear Missing the Party
Headline: South Africa: Soccer World Sex Workers Fear Missing the Party
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — "It's great that the World Cup will be held here... I just wish we could have a bit of the pie," said Zandile, who works the streets of Sandton.
Like other Johannesburg prostitutes, Zandile dreamed of getting rich from World Cup fans.
Now she complains that foreigners will be scared off by fear of AIDS and crime and there will be no World Cup bonanza.
South Africa has the world's biggest HIV caseload, with 5,7 million cases, and foreign fans have been repeatedly warned in their home countries about the dangers of casual sex.
Last year, some officials warned that 40000 sex workers would invade this country from around Africa, but with the tournament only weeks away, the reality looks very different.
Zandile and her colleagues fear the refusal of authorities to create safe areas for prostitution during the tournament will make it nearly impossible to attract clients.
"Foreigners and tourists don't like to look for the girls on the streets," said Mudiwa, a sex worker from Zimbabwe.
"The government needs to create a safe space for us, so that the customers know where to find us. When you get into a car, you never know if you'll be able to see your child again."
Some politicians last year called for the creation of protected areas for prostitution during the World Cup, following examples of zones designated during the last edition of the tournament in Germany in 2006.
Instead, cities such as Cape Town have preferred to clean up the streets, following New York's zero tolerance approach to crime.
Advocacy groups also unsuccessfully urged the government to put a moratorium on prostitution-related arrests.
"There are so many logistical and political issues inherent in the World Cup that sex work is very low on the agenda...it's politically much more expedient to ignore the problem than to deal with it head on," said Marlise Richter, a researcher who collaborates with sex worker advocacy groups.
LOST OPPORTUNITY
Germany changed its criminal law around sex work ahead of the event, but in South Africa "that opportunity has been wasted", Richter said.
The host cities have few plans for how to protect sex workers or their clients, saying that with prostitution still illegal, they were limited in what they could do.
Sibongile Mazibuko, the head of the World Cup team for Johannesburg, said the city would make condoms available, but had no plans for how to deal with the issue otherwise.
"We can't give them shelter because we can't be part of a crime...we can't have a banana republic that creates laws for an event for one month," she said.
The scourge of AIDS is also a powerful deterrent.
"People see us as breeders of AIDS, and that kills the business for us," said Mpho, another Sandton street girl.
Due to its clandestine nature, the number of sex workers in South Africa, the continent's economic mecca, is difficult to ascertain, with many operating from city brothels or suburban homes and some at high-end apartments, catering almost exclusively for foreign clients.
Others, like Zandile, try their luck on the streets, despite constant fear of violence, abuse and arrest.
POLICE ABUSE
"Police will abuse us. They will take us to the police station and will ask for sex. Or they will say 'girls, let's talk...that's to see if we can talk money," she told Reuters.
Prostitutes, especially those working the streets, are prone to being sexually and physically attacked. Others, who are pimped by gangsters or organised sex syndicates, are often at the mercy of criminals who also supply drugs.
"Presently the industry is regulated by criminals and 2010 offers those criminals opportunities to get away with very serious crime on a larger scale," said Eric Harper, Director of the Sex Workers Education and Advocacy Task Force (Sweat).
It is unclear if demand for paid sex will rise significantly during the June 11-July 11 tournament.
Some new arrivals from Zimbabwe, Botswana and other neighbouring states have been spotted lining up along Johannesburg's streets, but nowhere near the tens of thousands of prostitutes predicted by some officials last year.
Abikanile said she was one of a dozen who had hitchhiked down from Malawi in hopes of making money from players and fans.
"I was told I could make in a week what I would earn in a year back home, so why not?" the 35-year-old housewife said.
Yet if last year's Confederations Cup, a rehearsal for the World Cup, is anything to go by, business might be slow, Zandile said.

Original_Search_Title: Ndere Introduces a Blend of Cultural and Modern Music
Headline: Uganda: Ndere Introduces a Blend of Cultural and Modern Music
Body_Text:
Ndere Centre is here to transform your Friday night entertainment experience, continuing in the tradition of providing cultural fun. At 8:50 pm last Friday, Steven Rwangyezi, proprietor of Ndere, introduced the start of the entertainment that would appeal to both our hip and retro sides.
The show started an hour late but what Rwangyezi had promised seemed worth the wait. First off, he introduced the idea behind the theme Friday night.
"Ndere Centre has resuscitated and rejuvenated Uganda's songs and dance, and taken them to the world," he said. Adding the rhetorical question, "How does the young person express himself?" He then went on to illustrate that the show was meant to bring the appreciation of culture to everyone including the younger audience.
This year Ndere Troupe marks 26 years in the industry, and Rwangyezi took a moment to reminisce about a time when, after three years of rehearsing, no one came to the show. It seemed like history was about to repeat itself when forty-five minutes later seats were still empty. The five members of Milege band performed anyway.
Soothing rhapsody after soothing rhapsody was churned out. When the soothing was beginning to get monotonous, they threw in a remix of the Luo lullaby Weyoweyo,-complete with some groovy beats that got some people's stamping their feet.
Francis Manana, lead guitarist and manager of the band, described their genre of music as Afro fusion jazz.
Vincent Binomugisha, a teacher of music who was part of the audience, appreciated the tone and harmonisation of the different instruments. Hon. Mike Sebalu, member of the East African Legislative Assembly and founding member of Ndere, described it as a soothing and unique approach to western music.
Later, Milege band seemed like a couple of smoothies to down patiently as you wait for the main course. Well, blame it on Oscar Kihika and friends who introduced us to two-saxophone-playing geniuses. But really Milege does not add that much to the jazz scene, and will have to up their game a little if they one day plan to be more than just curtain raisers.
Enter the real Ndere Troupe with an assortment of African instruments from the various sizes of the adingi, adungu, the flute and xylophone; to the heart stopping African drums synchronized with modern refrains of the saxophone and keyboard.
No doubt this is what the audience unleashed Shs10,000 for. Soloist Isha Nakato shook her waist seductively to the sounds as she sung a tune mixed with Runyoro and English- a rendition of Runyege, a Bunyoro courtship dance.
The girls were truly stunning, first in their black leggings and Zebra print wrappers, and later in the devilish blue and black attire. They danced around the floor in a way that brought back memories of the gothic figures in Michael Jackson's Thriller. And thrill the young village men in Runyege costume they did, bewitching them with their seductive dance moves.
But wow could these girls shake! Watching them, one felt their bodies were regularly massaged with bananas. The way they did the Beyonce Baby Boy stroke, with so much energy as they exited backwards, would make the diva herself very jealous. And then there was the Michael Jackson walk and the drunken act of the enamoured village boys...
Watching the performance was like having a combination of Shakira, Beyonce and a young ballet dancer in one lovely Ugandan package. It is no wonder they dizzied and dazed the local men until the wonderful symphony ended in a romantic conquest... and boy and girl lived happily ever after. The cheers from an initially almost empty auditorium were something of a surprise.
If this launch is anything to go by, Culture Meets the Modern Friday nights at Ndere Centre are very promising indeed. You just might want to drop the bang bang in the stuffy clubs for this refreshing experience.

Original_Search_Title: They're Mere Emblems
Headline: Kenya: They're Mere Emblems
Body_Text:
The announcement on Monday that the Interim Electoral Commission had settled on green and red as symbols for the "Yes" and "No" vote proponents ahead of the August 4 referendum marks another phase of the campaigns for the proposed constitution.
The symbols are colours that are crucial in our country's history. Both of them are engraved in our national flag, itself a national symbol of reverence.
But though we may like the colours in our flag, we can imagine that both sides -- in the name of political competition -- will tear into each other. As a result, the campaigners will deviate from the real issues.
Whereas each side is entitled to its campaign tricks, we urge the campaigners to stick to the issues that will enable wananchi to make the right choice regarding the proposed document.
It will be sad for Kenyans to reject or accept the proposed constitution just because their leaders convinced them that, somehow, one colour is better than the other.
Let all voters know that the colours were picked as symbols only. Any other belief attached to the colours is neither here nor there.
We feel obliged to offer this advice given the kind of hatred we witnessed during the 2005 referendum.
Then, leaders attributed all manner of bad qualities to the popular fruits, until they seemed to forget what the oranges and bananas were meant to represent.
Let us not have another referendum like the last.

Original_Search_Title: I'll Miss Everything But the Potholes of Kampala Roads
Headline: Uganda: I'll Miss Everything But the Potholes of Kampala Roads
Body_Text:
A guidebook to Uganda prepared me for roughly what to expect: friendly, welcoming people, beautiful scenery and lots of rice and beans. Three months later however, and the impression Uganda has left upon me runs much, much deeper.
I came as a volunteer for an NGO - Send a Cow Uganda and have been living with an inspiring Ugandan couple and their two adorable boys for the last three months. My weekdays follow much the same pattern as most other Kampala-dwellers. Come the weekends, however, and I go exploring!
Hopping from place to place during my free time has been much easier than expected. Whereas in the UK, tickets must be bought in advance for an allocated seat on an allocated bus, here, I jump on a boda to the taxi park whenever I feel like it, and grab a seat in a taxi to wherever I'm going! Admittedly, I'm pretty terrified for 95 per cent of the high-speed journey and so chatter incessantly to whichever unfortunate soul is sitting next to me, but upon reaching my destination, I'm always amazed at the value for money.
My first trip was to Jinja: that former industrial town with buckets of charm and breathtaking views. On a Saturday I visited the Bujagali Falls where I had been hoping to raft the Nile. It was not to be, however, as just five minutes into my visit and while sipping a soda overwhelmed by my stunning surroundings, some unkind insect bit me and alarmingly quickly I came out in a rather unpleasant allergic reaction.
Panicking slightly, I ran around frantically taking photos for half an hour before hotfooting it back to a pharmacy in Jinja. Despite this minor drama, however, the extraordinary natural beauty of the falls and the fierce power of the water will be forever imprinted in my memory. It truly is a sight to behold.
The next day I headed to the source of the Nile where I spent six wonderful hours just wandering around. I visited the stalls selling beads, watched the naughty monkeys steal bananas, took a boat ride to the point the lake becomes the river and saw monitor lizards, otters and kingfishers. I watched the fishermen catch my delicious lunch of tilapia, before taking a boat across to the park where I climbed to the brow of the hill. I was amazed that no one else had taken the effort to do the same because the view was fantastic. It is so unspoilt I almost felt I'd discovered it myself.
Given the success of this first jaunt, I was keen to plan my next trip and the Ssese Islands were my target. From ferry to ferry, it was brilliant and I implore anyone who hasn't already been to make it their priority! The islands are picture-postcard-perfect: palm trees, white sands and hardly a person in sight. I spent the days wandering around, reading on the beach and meeting other travellers and even took a nature walk through the fishing village, into the very wild forest and then to the top of the island to take in the stunning view.
In the evening, my fellow visitors and I sat around a campfire having a supper of fresh tilapia, watching the sun go down over the lake. At around midnight, we decided it was high time we ticked the "swimming in Lake Victoria" box and so, under a star-filled sky and with lightening flashing on the horizon, in we splashed. I will never, ever forget that moment.
My work has allowed me to see another side of Uganda, however, when visiting impoverished rural communities in Gulu and Amuru. At times it was hard to believe the tragic stories behind the smiling, open faces, but the barren vegetable patches and swollen bellies of undernourished children told another story. Meeting those people and seeing the results development projects can have stiffened my resolve to dedicate my career to working in this sector: good people just don't deserve to suffer like that.
So now, almost three months down the road and my time here in Uganda is drawing to a close, time for some parting thoughts. Living with a truly exceptional family enabled me to become immersed in Ugandan life and I feel I've come to know some of the wonderful quirks the guidebook omitted to mention, like the "square potholes".
I was intrigued that three months ago, when I stepped into Uganda, I found Ssemawata Road being cut into squares of potholes and three months later, the squares are enlarging each day. I am also intrigued by the obsession with Spanish TV soaps and the "disorganisation" brought by rain!
Uganda, for me, is a place where friends are family, where no inconvenience is too great and where the pace of life drifts, rather than rushes along; where the opportunities are endless, the potential is tremendous and the future is full of dazzling sunshine.
I love this country, its people and its nature and I cannot thank you all enough for welcoming me to share in your Pearl of Africa. I hope I'll be back before long.

Original_Search_Title: Diamond Becomes Toast of Kili Awards Night
Headline: Tanzania: Diamond Becomes Toast of Kili Awards Night
Body_Text:
Up-and-coming Bongo Flava artiste, Diamond aka Naseeb Abdul, was the toast of Kili awards night on Friday night in Dar es Salaam.
He bagged three awards, which were a record for an artiste that joined the big stage only a year ago.
Diamond, who was in attendance with his mother Sandra Sammy, picked awards for the Best Up and Coming artiste of the Year. He bit other newcomers in the same list like Amini, Belle 9, Quick Racka a.k.a Abott Charles and Barnaba (Elias).
The rising sensation also went on to pick the Song of the Year award for his hit 'Kamwambie.' To cap it all, he grabbed the Best R&B Song award ahead of AT's Nipigie of Stara Thomas.
The visibly emotional Diamond took to the stage with his mother amidst a thunderous applause from a star-studded audience.
He also qualified to represent Tanzania in the coming African song writing contest for the anti-malaria campaign to take place in Senegal next month.
Runners-up to Diamond's sensational collection was the African Stars Band that collected two awards for the Best Album Band with its Mwana Dar es Salaam song.
The band was also at hand to pick the Best Kiswahili Song award. However, what surprised most fans was the fact that the song was not earlier mentioned since the group had two songs in the same category.
Mzee Yusuph and his Jahazi Mordern Taarab also got two awards in the Best Taarab Song and Best Song writer/composer.
The Zorro family emerged winners after Banana Zorro was voted the Best Male Artiste of the Year, and his father Zahir Zorro's individual contributions to the industry were honoured in the Hall of Fame category.
In this category the efforts of Clouds FM were also recognized. Its team, headed by Ruge Mtahaba, was at hand to receive the award.
Lady Jay Dee was voted the Best Female Artiste while Kidumu and Juliana took the East African category and Joh Makini took the prize for the Best Hip hop song. On the other hand, Chid Benz scooped the Hip Hop Artiste of the Year award.
Other winners during the night were AY for the best reggae song and, after a long spell in oblivion, Bwana Misosi was resurrected as he won the Best Ragga Song award. The award was handed to him by the soft-spoken Jamaican-American star, Sean Kingston.
'Nipigie,' which has of late become very popular, did not disappoint the audience as it was declared the Best Collabo of the Year. It features AT and Stara Thomas.
Other awards went to Halid Chokora, Lamar a.k.a Niekamp Sjoens, Marlaw a.k.a Lawrence Marima and Cpwaa to complete the list of winners on the night.
And in a star-studded evening gala which witnessed scintillating performances, Mwasiti led the way as she took command of the stage with a team of THT dancers.
There were others who followed her footsteps with crowd pulling jigs that had Banana Zorro sensationally sampling Michael Jackson's beats of 'They Don't Really Care about Us.'

Original_Search_Title: Symbols for Referendum Out Next Week
Headline: Kenya: Symbols for Referendum Out Next Week
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The symbols that will represent the 'Yes' and 'No' vote at the referendum will be known next week.
Top officials of the Interim Independent Electoral Commission (IIEC) will meet in the Nairobi on Monday to select the symbols from a list of suggestions by Kenyans.
The commission will also announce the campaign period, which by law ends 24 hours to the August 4 referendum date announced earlier on Friday.
However, IIEC chairman Issack Hassan said his team would only settle for neutral symbols.
"No party symbols will be picked. The banana and orange symbols used in the last referendum are also out of the question," he said at a press conference at the commission's headquarters.
Kenyans have been asked to suggest appropriate symbols and the commission has set up an interactive website to enable that. It has also provided for postal delivery.
Mr Hassan said suggestions had even come from Kenyans in the diaspora. At an earlier function, he indicated that these symbols included wildlife and food.
The orange, which represented the triumphant 'No' vote at the last referendum, was later adopted by the Orange Democratic Movement, led by Prime Minister Raila Odinga.
Earlier, the chairman had said that any symbols, even while neutral, are likely to have meanings ascribed to them by the campaigners.
"We have an interesting menu, but at the end of the day, whatever we choose can be tortured and twisted," he said at a media breakfast hosted by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP).
UNDP has launched a programme through which it will collaborate with civil society groups to offer civic education on the proposed constitution.
Mr Hassan said the symbols are mandatory because they will enable the illiterate to identify the side they wish to vote for.
The campaign period is yet to be announced but each camp has lined up its forces for the referendum.
The 'Yes' camp, headed by President Kibaki and Mr Odinga, is set to launch its campaign on Saturday at Uhuru Park.
This comes a week after the 'No' camp, headed by the National Council of Churches of Kenya, launched its campaign at the same venue.
The National Cohesion and Integration Commission and other organisations have warned against the kind of divisions that arose in 2005.
These are seen to have marked the genesis of the violence that followed the 2007 General Election.
"History repeats itself and every time we forget, the price goes higher," said Mr Hassan, who was also a member of the 2005 Constitution of Kenya Review Commission.
The IIEC also issued writs for the July 12 by-elections in Matuga and 26 civic wards. The by-elections would have been held on the same day as the referendum as both the writs and the proposed constitution were published on the same day.
All other by-elections, such as the one for Juja, will be held after the referendum, said Mr Hassan.
He said August 4 was arrived at after consultations on logistics and planning with the police commissioner and the Education Ministry.
"More than 50 per cent of our polling stations are located in educational institutions and we hope they will have closed by then," said the chairman.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki and Raila Back New Constitution
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki and Raila Rally Their Parties Behind 'Yes' Campaign
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki and Prime Minister Raila Odinga will on Tuesday meet leaders of parties with MPs in Parliament in an attempt to rally them behind the 'Yes' campaign.
The meeting comes as the Committee of Experts launches a 30-day countrywide campaign to educate the public on the contents of the proposed constitution.
It was understood that the President and the PM, who are leading the 'Yes' team, had chosen to use leaders of parliamentary parties to bring on board MPs opposed to the proposed law.
A statement from the Presidential Press Service said the meeting with top leaders of 15 parties will start at 10.30am at the Kenyatta International Conference Centre.
Among the party leaders expected at KICC are those from the Orange Democratic Movement, Party of National Unity, ODM-Kenya, Kanu, Narc-Kenya, Safina, Narc, Ford Kenya, New Ford-K and Sisi Kwa Sisi.
The others are leaders of Chama Cha Uzalendo, Ford Asili, Kaddu, Kadu Asili and People's Democratic Party.
Joint secretariat
The meeting will discuss the modalities of setting up a joint secretariat that will coordinate the 'Yes' campaigns and how the parties will use their countrywide network to marshal support at the grassroots level.
The government is facing opposition from Christian churches, which have vowed to lead the 'No' campaign after talks to strike a deal on contentious issues in the new set of laws failed.
They launched the 'No' campaign at the weekend at Uhuru Park in Nairobi and on Monday, Catholic bishops were meeting in Karen to strategise for their campaign.
The Church is opposed to the section of Article 26 which empowers doctors to end a pregnancy if it endangers the woman's life or she needs emergency treatment.
Christian leaders are also opposed to the retention of kadhi's courts in the proposed constitution under Article 169 and 170, which limit their authority to disputes over personal status, marriage, divorce or inheritance, where all the parties are Muslims and agree to take the case to a kadhi.
They have been joined in the 'No' campaign by a team of ODM leaders from the Rift Valley led by Higher Education minister William Ruto.
Others in the 'No' campaign include Kaddu leader Cyrus Jirongo and Igembe South MP Mithika Linturi of Kanu.
The proposed constitution was published last Thursday.
However, before the real duel starts, the CoE has a 30-day period to conduct civic education with the help of non-state actors.
On Tuesday, the experts will launch the exercise at KICC in preparation for the final phase in the review process.
'The committee is expected to facilitate civic education on the proposed constitution for a period of 30 days (after its publication). The committee will launch various civic education materials tomorrow (Tuesday)," the committee said in a statement.
On Monday, the experts and the Mohammed Abdikadir-led Parliamentary Committee on the Constitution agreed that Kenyans would not use the Orange or Banana symbols during the referendum.
Registered symbols
They also resolved that none of the symbols currently registered by the country's 47 political parties would be used.
"We have gone through all the symbols registered by the 47 political parties. We have resolved that we shall not use any of these symbols during the referendum. We shall also not use the Banana or Orange," PSC vice-chairman Ababu Namwamba told journalists after the two-hour meeting.
The meeting was called to deliberate on the question to be asked during the referendum and to look at the symbols to be used.
The law governing the constitutional review requires that the IIEC consults with PSC on the symbols to be used and the question to be asked during the referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Nation Gears Up to Decide on New Constitution
Headline: Kenya: Referendum Question Out
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The question to which Kenyans will respond as they vote for or against the proposed constitution at the referendum will be known on Monday. The Interim Independent Electoral Commission and the Parliamentary Select Committee on constitution review meet on Monday to frame the question.
In 2005, Kenyans were asked; Are you for or against the ratification of the proposed new constitution? Supporters of the No vote, who were given the Orange symbol by the Electoral Commission of Kenya, garnered 3.5 million votes against the Yes supporters whose symbol was Banana who got 2.5 million votes.
This was a 58.12 per cent win for the No campaigners against the Yes campaigners' 41.8 per cent. On Sunday, IIEC chairman Ahmed Isaack Hassan said his team would meet with PSC at 2.30pm. Mr Hassan could not, however, say when the electoral body would set the date for the referendum.
The IIEC has effectively taken over the baton for the last phase on the road to a new constitutional dispensation which involves preparing the public for the referendum. The public vote has to take place within 90 days from the publication of the proposed constitution.
Seven days
Attorney-General Amos Wako published the proposed constitution last Thursday. Mr Hassan's team is required to frame the referendum question within seven days and have the voters' registers ready in 21 days. The Committee of Experts (CoE) will also have 30 days to conduct civic education and politicians will only be allowed to campaign afterwards.
Speaking at Kenyatta International Conference Centre when he published the proposed constitution, Mr Wako termed the campaigns for the 'Yes' and 'No' votes as premature and asked politicians to refrain from holding rallies until after civic education.
If Kenyans endorse the proposed constitution at the referendum which is expected not later than August 6, President Kibaki will be required to promulgate it within 14 days. Mr Hassan also said the electoral body will today release the final tally of voters following the end of the manual registration on Sunday.
The registration was extended by four days, giving a chance to millions of people who had missed the Wednesday deadline. The extension had been requested by organisations such as the Federation of Women Lawyers-Kenya chapter. Mr Hassan said an additional 1.5 million voters were expected to enter the voters' roll in the four extra days.By last Wednesday, before the extension, 11,771,068 voters had enrolled.
And as the focus turns to the referendum, MPs pushing for the rejection of the proposed constitution want the CoE barred from conducting civic education. The group claims that the Nzamba Kitonga-led committee would be crusading for the adoption of the document to justify its work.
MPs Kiema Kilonzo (Mutito), Charles Keter (Belgut), Isaac Ruto (Chepalungu) and Mithika Linturi (Igembe South) said the committee could not be trusted to impartially educate Kenyans on the contents of the proposed constitution. "For purposes of objectivity, it is important for a different neutral body to be given the task to conduct civic education ahead of the referendum," Mr Keter said on Sunday during a funeral in Nzambani, Kitui.
Mr Kilonzo said the matter had been complicated further by the endorsement of the proposed constitution by some foreign countries, which were also funding the enactment of a new laws under the Agenda Four reforms. In an apparent reference to the United States, the Mutito MP said the CoE could not take positions contrary to the ones taken by some of those funding the Agenda Four reforms.
The US Government, through Ambassador Michael Ranneberger, has endorsed the document. Elsewhere, CoE official Gad Awuonda asked lawyers to play an active role in civic education so that Kenyans can vote with their conscience during the referendum. However, Mr Lawrence Karanja, chairman of LSK Rift Valley branch, said lawyers needed financial backing to carry out civic education.
Reported by Peter Leftie, Mutua Kitavi and Simon Siele

Original_Search_Title: Polls Team Set to Choose Symbols
Headline: Kenya: Polls Team Set to Choose Symbols
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Now that the Proposed Constitution of Kenya is headed for the referendum, the remaining crucial phase of the review is the choice of symbols representing the two sides and how they will be used in the campaigns.
The question is: Which symbols will be used this time? In 2005, the 'Yes' was represented by banana while the 'No' side was represented by the orange.
But then, these simple fruits later assumed immense political life of their own to the extent that the 'Orange' symbol gave birth to two political parties.
Secondly, they generated negative epithets such as "Rotten Orange" and "Banana Republic" with the Orange side equating their rivals with a banana republic.
After publication of the draft, the Interim Independent Electoral Commission (IIEC) is to frame the question to be put to the referendum within seven days.
IIEC chairman Ahmed Issack Hamed confirmed to the Saturday Nation that the question would be framed next week.
The Constitution of Kenya Review Act, 2008, gives the IIEC the power to assign any symbol as was the case during the 2005 referendum.
But the question remains what kind of symbols will be appropriate at this time when the country is still undergoing national healing?
In the absence of a substantive referendum law, the IIEC is supposed to come up with the symbols representing the 'No' and the 'Yes' sides, just like the defunct Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) did.
In the current Constitution, presidential, parliamentary and civic candidates are allowed to pick their own symbols which are then registered by the polls agency.
Mr Gabriel Mukele, a former vice-chairman of the disbanded ECK, said that before they came up with the 'Orange' and 'Banana' symbols, they had to consult concerned parties before making a decision.
But it is the then chairman, Mr Samuel Kivuitu, who came up with the fruits.
The commissioners then tossed a coin to decide which fruit goes to which side.
Mr Hamed said in an interview that the commission has the final authority to come up with a symbol.
"But we are consulting to add value to any decision we will make ... we don't want symbols that will divide the country," he said.

Original_Search_Title: More Towns to Become Municipalities
Headline: Uganda: More Towns to Become Municipalities
Body_Text:
The Parliamentary Committee on Public Service and Local Government was yesterday a stage for passionate calls from senior members of Parliament seeking approval of their town councils into municipalities. Last year, MPs approved Mukono, Hoima and Kasese towns to municipality status.
The committee, will next week table its report before the whole House on the decision to have five more towns elevated.
Among those already recommended are Mukono, Hoima and Kasese while those seeking municipality status are Bushenyi, Masindi, Ntungamo, Busia and Iganga. "We were satisfied with what we saw in those towns," Ms Emma Boona (Mbarara), the chairperson of committee, said.
Ms Mary Karooro Okurut (Woman Bushenyi) put up a spirited fight for the elevation of Ishaka town to a municipality by presenting documentary evidence that the town has grown both in size and has a better infrastructure. "We are the leading area in the whole country in milk and banana supplies, and we have the largest teaching hospital in the region," she said.
Her Ntungamo counterpart, Ms Beatrice Rwakimari told the committee that Ntugamo town deserved the municipality status because among others, it exports flowers to international markets. According to the law, a town council can be elevated to a municipality, if it has a population of at least 100,000 people and all capacities of meeting its own service delivery. However, some MPs said the elevation of town councils to municipality status should be done by the Local Government ministry.

Original_Search_Title: Salute the Bravery and Vision of Founders
Headline: South Africa: Salute the Bravery and Vision of SA's Founders
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — THIS month is the centenary of the South African state, whose founders would have marvelled at their creation - the foremost in Africa and a world-class entity, which is now being undermined.
An inspired act of courage, vision and determination, it brought together diverse peoples when Europe's overarching multinational entities were disintegrating into separate nation states.
Former colonies, republics and kingdoms were forged into a unitary and variegated state, the first - and still the only modern state - founded by natives on a continent whose other states were created outside by foreigners.
The founders - Louis Botha, Barry Hertzog, and Jan Smuts - were war-seasoned generals, who had led a genuine anti-imperialist struggle in a true people's war. These great men laid the foundations and frameworks that have enabled the evolution of a complex and dynamic country with a thriving economy and vibrant society.
The generals had valiantly fought with their troops within the country, which stands in contrast to the history of another liberation movement - the African National Congress (ANC), which was led by a globe-trotting former lawyer who now, appropriately, has an airport named after him. He lacked military courage, unlike his legal partner, who trained and then returned to fight - but was betrayed and islanded.
The forces under the exilic former lawyer "fought" away from the country, hurling spears from afar, a tactic Shaka discarded with contempt as cowardice. The hurlers returned to stab an unsuspecting nation in the back, typical of cowards.
Without these founders - Botha, Smuts, Hertzog - and their successors, SA would not have existed and developed. Their outstanding statecraft, which has still to be emulated in sub-Saharan Africa, is now being destroyed by unpatriotic elements.
Even black and other movements owe their existence to the creation of the state by these men. Black people benefited from these inheritances without the commensurate initiatives of blood and sweat, hence their estrangement and non-appreciation.
Undoubtedly, racial inequity existed and full democracy was absent. But social, health and material provisions - the best in Africa - existed for black people. Long before 1994, blacks had voted directly, at least, for urban and rural councils and executives - izibonda and bungas. Now all races don't even vote for central and provincial legislators but for mere party representatives.
If apparent or real oppression and the absence of liberal democracy are criteria for condemnation, the worst is elsewhere. The US was founded by only the Virginia aristocracy; most whites were not enfranchised and blacks were enslaved; however, its history and founders are acknowledged.
Democracy never existed in the Soviet Union and there is still none in China. But everyone is indifferent to this, including the ANC, its proteges and friends now selling the economy to imperialistic Beijing as it monkeys around in international relations.
Racism raised its head elsewhere: Jim Crow and lynching for blacks in the US; from pogroms to the Holocaust for European Jewry. Providence and goodwill mercifully saved SA from terrible racist manifestations, but the current regime's evil racial programme is catastrophic.
The centenary of Africa's greatest state is eclipsed by a sports jamboree; Blatter overshadows Botha. A fake "patriotism" is being whipped up to opiate the masses. National challenges and historic events are ignored, much befitting a banana republic in crisis.
The early statesmen tackled a complicated state energetically and responsibly with creative initiatives, however controversial some were, unlike the current impotent and parasitic incumbents, who are short on substantive initiatives and solid responsibility.
This month, let us salute Botha, Hertzog and Smuts for their creative genius and their great legacy - as meanwhile, inveterate non-white elites and their non-black fellow travellers insidiously manipulate this highly centralised state in a manner that is unsuitable for such a diverse society.
Mabogoane is a freelance writer.

Original_Search_Title: Six Waragi Dealers Arrested in Kamwenge
Headline: Uganda: Six Waragi Dealers Arrested in Kamwenge
Body_Text:
Kamwenge — Police in Kamwenge are holding six people over illegal selling and drinking of waragi. They were picked up on Friday afternoon, according to the deputy Officer in Charge of Criminal Investigations, Mr James Musinguzi. He said all the suspects are from Kanyegaramire parish in Kamwenge Town Council. Two 20-litre jerrycans of crude waragi were also impounded and taken to Kamwenge central police station.
Mr Musinguzi said investigations are complete and the suspects will appear in court tomorrow to answer charges of enguli drinking and if proved guilty, they will be liable to one-year in jail or pay a fine of Shs4,000 according to Enguli Act (1964). The arrest follows a recent ban imposed on the sale and consumption of Alcohol in the district by its security team and council.
Now 22
Meanwhile, another person has died of suspected methanol-laced waragi bringing the number to 22 in the district. The deceased, Mzee Julius Tabalo hailed from Lyakahungu parish, Nkoma Sub-county, Kibaale County.
According to the police, Tabalo's body was found lying on a road side with a bottle of crude waragi next to him. At least 80 people were killed earlier in Kabale District after taking waragi. The waragi is made mainly from a special type of banana in many districts in western Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerian Women Are Loyal, Transparent...
Headline: Nigerian Women Are Loyal, Transparent...
Body_Text:
Lagos — Her smile and genteel comportment radiates warmth and simplicity. These are only just a few of the attributes that earned her the fitting sobriquet: the gentle lady of Alausa where she holds sway as deputy to Lagos State Governor, Babatunde Raji Fashola (SAN). Though she strives to toil in the background away from the glare of publicity or journalists' relentless inquiries, the demands of office constantly put her in the limelight.
In the last three years, Princess Adebisi Sarah Sosan's relationship with her boss has been devoid of rancour due, in most part, to her loyalty and the dedication she has shown to her duties. The effect is a working relationship defined by mutual respect. She was recently inducted into the Hall of Fame of African Leadership in Abuja. FUNKE OLAODE met Princess Sosan recently at the 60th birthday anniversary held in honour of ICAN President, Mrs. Adegite. Sosan was a guest speaker and drew resounding applause from the audience. She bares her mind on various issues including the role of women in the society
She was looking radiant in a lovely purple suit with matching jewelry. The occasion was at the 60th birthday celebration of Mrs. Elizabeth Adegite, the President of the Institute of Chartered Accountants of Nigeria. She was among the invited guest speakers. Princess Sarah Sosan, deputy Governor of Lagos State was a professional teacher before veering into politics. Despite the role of her office, she supervises the Ministry of Education where her impact across the state is enormous. Born in Lagos on February 11, 1956 to the family of Chief and Mrs. Durosinmi of Irewe town in Ojo Local Government Area of Badagry division. Her foray into politics was not accidental having been exposed to it early. Her father was a member of the defunct Action Group (AG) and Unity Party of Nigeria (UPN). She had always had passion for teaching. After her early education at the Unity Assembly Primary School Apapa and Secondary Education at the Awori-Ajeromi Secondary School, she commenced her sojourn in the teaching profession at the then Lagos State College of Education, Ijanikin (now Adeniran Ogunsanya College of Education) in 1980 where she obtained the National Certificate of Education (NCE). She later proceeded to the University of Lagos Akoka, where she obtained her Bachelor of Arts in English Education (B.A) Ed in 1988 and a Masters Degree in Adult Education (M.Ed) 1989. Her determination to keep abreast with technology led her in 2004, to proceed for an Advanced Diploma in Information Technology at the Lagos City Computer College. She's also commissioner of education in Lagos. As a Youth Corper, Bisi taught at the St. Leo's Secondary School Abeokuta, Ogun State as a classroom teacher while her sojourn in the Lagos State public service started in January 1989 when she was employed by the then Lagos State Post Primary Teaching Service Commission (PP- TESCOM) as a Master Grade II, a classroom teaching appointment.
Between 1990 and 1999, Mrs. Sosan left the four walls of the classroom and served as a Principal Education Officer at the Lagos State Ministry of Education and then moved to the defunct State Primary Education Board (SPEB) now Lagos State Universal Basic Education Board (LSUBEB) as an assistant Board Secretary.
