Later in year 2006 when the state Primary Education Board transformed to Lagos State Universal Basic Education Board, she was redeployed to the Department of Communication and Information Technology as the Head of Department (HOD). As the Head of Department, she was saddled with the responsibility of ensuring computer education and training was given the attention it deserved in Lagos State Public Primary Schools.
Her sojourn in the political world got a boost in 2007 when she was appointed to the position of deputy Governor by Babatunde Fashola. She is married to retired Navy Commander Seyi Sosan and blessed with children. Speaking on the role of professional woman and nation building, Princess Sosan said, "Women have a lot of role to play in nation building. They have a lot of role to play at home and society. If a woman is well taken care off, they get multiple values from that. It gives her the opportunity to prove herself as a mother, a leader, a loyalist and an organizer."
Breaking the glass ceiling by women sometimes requires extra efforts, should a woman limit her aspirations in terms of career and contribution to nation building? Princess Sosan has this to say. "A woman should work to the highest level of her career, to attain it through hard work, perseverance. There are a lot of challenges out there for women and you want to prove yourself that you can do it. So you have to persevere and be tolerant. Like I mentioned that women are always loyal and transparent. They have given talents by God to understand issues and to even look into issues and how to arrive at a compromise. Not a compromise to the detriment of values but to make things work out in the society. And that is why younger ladies out their should always see women who have gotten to the peak of their career as role models. See how the women in governance have shown brilliance. So with hardwork, you can get to the top without compromising your womanhood".
And what can be done to boost professionalism among women in Nigeria? "The most important thing is hardwork. And having the ability to prove that you can do it right. In any profession you are, you integrity matters and hard work matters. My dear it is very clear that the banana peel is always there for everybody, just be mindful and whoever you are matters as well that you should be above board. And inspite all the challenges you should be able to prove that at all time. There is a time for child bearing, do it in time, leave it there and continue with your career and you get to the top".
In the past, deputies have had bruises with their bosses that even led to impeachment. What has sustained Princess Sosan in the last three years in the administration of Governor Fashola? Gazing at the reporter, she picked her words like a teacher in the classroom. "Yes it is true that we have had issues concerning governors and their deputies. What has helped me is that I have a very good leader. I have a team leader; I have a leader that gives opportunity for each person according to their capacity and ability. This leader is always there for us to offer advice. Again, the support of my family, my husband and my children for their understanding that when mummy is out there, they continue to pray".
Advice to other women My advice is that there are challenges out there but they can overcome the challenges through trust in Almighty God, and being themselves. You don't have to do what others do and if you want to emulate others do it in a positive way and do it well so that you can excel. I have always said that you shouldn't allow the challenges of womanhood overwhelm you. You would always be confronted with the challenges in home front and the society. Don't listen to that, forge ahead and you look up to make it."

Original_Search_Title: Jakaya Kikwete Has a Point
Headline: Tanzania: Jakaya Kikwete Has a Point
Body_Text:
Arusha — The President of Tanzania, Jakaya Kikwete (JK) has challenged journalists to report or write not only about negative but also positive things that people do. He probably has a point.
The point is that journalists can take lead to influence the media, professional societies and other institutions to recognize significant performance achievements regularly. The aim is to influence the media to encourage members of society in Tanzania to consider reconstructing their basic value systems around performance rather than around money or gadgets as it is currently the case. Needless to mention, performance achievements are engines of economic development.
Currently, most members of society in Tanzania value peoples' worthiness in terms of amounts of money or the things that money can buy. For instance, you are somebody in many parts of Arusha only if you are perceived to have plenty of money or associated gadgets. It matters less how the money or gadgets are acquired.
Meanwhile, you are considered to be unsuccessful in society or even a fool if you do not use your talent, expertise, experience or position to enrich yourself. It does not matter if you make significant contributions to society in various ways.
Therefore, it can be deduced that the individual's focus on acquiring money and gadgets coupled with selfish attitudes contribute to corruption and poverty that exist in the country. It implies that, Tanzanians from all walks of life must contribute urgently to reconstruct realistic value systems for development.
It suggests that the mass media like newspapers, TVs, radios and magazines can do something in that regard. They can initiate a process to reward significant individual performances regularly in various sectors of development in the country.
The aim is to encourage members of society to acquire value systems that enhance economic development. Countries that reward individual performance achievements regularly use frameworks that carry the following titles: Lifetime Achievement Awards, Hall of Fame, Grammy, Emmy and others. Tanzanians can formulate rewarding frameworks and criteria that are appropriate to their local and national situations.
In particular, local journalists in Arusha can initiate a process to reward outstanding academicians that include those that are alive as well as those who have passed away. For example, local nominees for the reward in academic excellence would probably include the distinguished individuals like the late Professors Mainoya, Hirji and Rogers from the University of Dar es Salaam.
In education, teachers in Ilboru, Kaloleni and Arusha Day Secondary Schools deserve public recognition because their schools have excelled in National Examinations consistently over the years.
Journalists are also well placed to recognize distinguished local conservationists like Mr. Lota Melamari, the Director of Tanzania Wildlife Conservation Society. Mr. Melamari recently received an international conservation award for his effort to protect Lake Natron from investments that would destroy the ecosystem of the lake if allowed to operate. Needless to emphasize, local people were supposed to be first to acknowledge Melamari's contributions.
In athletics, the legendary Filbert Bayi and Juma Ikangaa deserve some lasting local recognition. Ignore the proposition if members of the society in Arusha have already awarded the athletes in public for their outstanding contributions to the sport.
Many local engineers deserve recognition. The nominees for the award in the architect category would probably include the distinguished local architect that designed the Naura Springs Hotel at Sanawari in Arusha municipality.
Journalists can also initiate a process to reward politicians who contribute say, to democracy significantly. Nominees could include Dr. Slaa and also other political leaders who play significant roles to enable members of public to realize their rights.
Mass media can reward journalists who show courage in their work. For instance, the media can award journalists who are courageous to reveal the truth despite the associated dangers in doing so.
Institutions other than mass media can also recognize individual performance achievements in public. For instance, the Arusha Chamber of Commerce is well placed to recognize distinguished business people in town. The Chamber may nominate its newly elected Chairman, Mr. Adolf Olomi, for innovation in the industry sector. Mr. Olomi is the owner and Director of the factory in Arusha that manufactures banana wine.
Miners can nominate the late Juuyawatu and Titus for their pioneer achievements in the mining sector. Both individuals are still household names in the mining industry in Arusha.
Associations of Bus Owners in Arusha would probably nominate owners of Dar Express to compete in the category of ground transport while Flying Organizations may consider owners of Precision Air for awards in the Air Transport category.
Other institutions can reward distinguished musicians, fine artists, business coaches, volunteers, community and social workers. Institutions can award poets and lobbyists for children's rights. Frameworks can be developed to reward individuals who protect animals' rights and the environment. Biodiversity conservation can be a category for rewards.
JK might support journalists who encourage individuals to excel. His support or otherwise would be a means to verify as to whether he meant what he has said about journalists.

Original_Search_Title: Soccer City's 74000-Seat Headache
Headline: South Africa: Soccer City's 74000-Seat Headache
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — APPREHENSIVE soccer bosses will be on the edge of their seats during two Nedbank Cup second-round matches this weekend, as the games could throw the best-laid plans of the Premier Soccer League (PSL) and the 2010 World Cup local organising committee into disarray.
Soccer City - which will be the centrepiece of the World Cup - will be officially unveiled to the world during the Nedbank Cup final on May 22, but the litany of upsets that has plagued the tournament could make it difficult to sell out the 94000-seat venue.
Talks are at an advanced stage with US, European and Asian broadcasters to televise the final to a global audience. But after the departure of crowd-pullers Kaizer Chiefs last weekend, soccer bosses will be crossing their fingers that Orlando Pirates and Mamelodi Sundowns do not follow the same route.
Insiders said selling the match as a spectacle had become more challenging as Chiefs are one of a few local clubs known in Europe. Most of Europe came to know about South African football when Eurosport secured the rights to televise a Soweto derby (Chiefs vs Orlando Pirates) live to more than 40 European countries in November 2007.
Pirates are still in the tournament but will have to avoid slipping on the banana skin that is giant-killing amateur side Als Puk Tawana at Orlando Stadium tomorrow night. Sundowns have their own problems as they tackle potentially tricky lower-tier upstarts Black Leopards at the same venue on Sunday.
PSL CEO Kjetil Siem conceded yesterday that he was nervous and could not even dare contemplate the possibilities ahead of a weekend that could present a headache.
"If you look at it from a commercial side of things, it (the possible departure of Downs and Pirates from the competition) is making me very nervous," the Norwegian said. "But if we look at it purely from a football perspective, we have to consider it as one of football's possibilities."
Unlike the other venues, Soccer City is yet to be tested and football governing body Fifa and the local organising committee had no alternative but to turn to the PSL in their search for a match big enough to officially open the stadium.
Siem said they were encouraged by the fact that more than 20000 tickets have already been sold - 74000 short of a sell-out crowd.
"The fans bought these tickets without knowing which teams would be in the final. That is something to be happy about. We would have been frantic if no one had bought tickets and the fans had simply waited for the identity of the two finalists before buying tickets.
"We hope the historic aura of the opening of the stadium will be enough to encourage fans to come regardless of who is in the final."
But Siem admitted the departure of Chiefs was regrettable because they would have drawn huge crowds after winning the Telkom Knockout earlier this month.
Siem said the biggest challenge facing the PSL was that Chiefs and Pirates had by far the biggest followings of any clubs in SA.
No other club comes close to them. Teams like Sundowns and Celtic attract decent crowds to their own home grounds but not outside of their strongholds.
"The fan base in other countries, on the other hand, is bigger than two clubs. You will have six or seven clubs comfortably pulling similar numbers," he said.
In other Nedbank Cup second- round matches this weekend, Wits host Thanda Royal Zulu at Bidvest Stadium tomorrow afternoon, and Winners Park entertain Black Aces at Seshego Stadium on Sunday.
n Chiefs goalkeeper Arthur Bartman and defender Dominic Isaacs completed a deserved double for the club when they walked away with two awards at the Telkom Knockout awards yesterday.
Bartman was acknowledged as the goalkeeper of the tournament after edging out Pirates' Senzo Meyiwa and Ajax Cape Town's Hans Vonk. Isaacs was recognised as the best defender of the competition after beating Chiefs team-mates Tshifiwa Mmbooi, Punch Masenamela and Thabang Rooi.
Both winners received R100000, but the losing finalists did not walk away empty-handed. Dipsy Selolwane, acclaimed as the tournament's best midfielder, also received R100000 for his effort.
The top goal-scorer award was not given as no player scored the minimum requirement of five goals during the tournament.

Original_Search_Title: Zanzibar's Malaria Success a Potential Banana Skin
Headline: Tanzania: Zanzibar's Malaria Success a Potential Banana Skin
Body_Text:
Zanzibar City — Efforts to combat malaria in Zanzibar have seen the prevalence rate come down from 35 percent before 2008 to below 1 percent, but health officials are worried the gains could be reversed.
"Despite the achievements in reducing malaria, a lack of funds for awareness[-raising], indoor residual spraying and surveillance, is a challenge. Also, we have a problem with people's resistance to behavioural change, particularly in keeping the environment clean and in the use of mosquito nets," said Mwinyi Msellem, head of the diagnostic unit at the Zanzibar Malaria Control Programme (ZMCP).
As malaria prevalence heads towards zero, the population is also losing its natural immunity to the disease, meaning that population screening will become increasingly important, said Msellem. Malaria prevalence was below one percent, according to the Roll Back Malaria Indicator Survey of 2007.
ZMCP interventions include case management through the treatment and training of health workers, and integrated vector control through the use of insecticide-treated bednets, environmental hygiene and indoor spraying. The programme hopes to cover 95 percent of the island's estimated 280,000 households in this way as against 60 percent at present.
The prevention of malaria in pregnancy has also been a key malaria control intervention although the use of prophylaxis medication by pregnant women is low at 40 percent against a target of 85. "Most pregnant women attend clinics when they are close to delivery so they just get the last dose," he said. Two doses are recommended.
Along with past interventions, emphasis is on new case monitoring and research. "When you get this kind of success you have to increase surveillance," he noted.
Early epidemic detection system
A Malaria Early Epidemic Detection System has also been established to monitor new cases at 52 of Zanzibar's 150 health facilities. "Weekly, they [the health centres] send text messages to our server indicating the number of patients, those tested for malaria, and the number diagnosed with malaria," said Msellem. Comparisons are then drawn against previous weekly reports.
"If an increase is noted, we have to investigate and check breeding grounds," he said. The health centres reported 1,671 confirmed malaria cases in 2009, of which 618 were children under five.
According to the Ministry of Health and Social Welfare, health centres have been showing 2-3 percent malaria prevalence, with no change noted since the short March-May rains.
Emerging challenges
Challenges are, however, emerging in disease monitoring, behaviour change and funding.
Among the problems is difficulty getting monitoring data from the health facilities, he said. Mobile phone technical errors sometimes also interfere with data collection.
"Each district also needs its own surveillance and response team and there is a need for more trained personnel," he said. "To set up this system you also need a lot of money."
Refusal to test and be treated for malaria has also been noted as cases decline, raising the risk of onward transmission or even death. This is because some people believe malaria has already been eliminated, said ZMCP.
Mariam Mussa, 34, a small trader and mother of three in Tunguu village south of Unguja, one of Zanzibar's constituent islands, said: "I thank God that the last time one of my children had malaria was in May [2009]. In the past we used to have malaria frequently."
"Situation remains fragile"
"Although malaria is down, the situation remains fragile. Sustainable commitment by the government, including having its own funds for the anti-malaria programme and awareness of the need to keep the environment clean, is important to control mosquitoes," Juma Muchi, a doctor, told IRIN.
The likelihood of donors and the government withdrawing support due to the recorded success is a major concern, according to ZMCP's Msellem. "We need to sustain control measures to avoid a resurgence... Malaria prevalence was reduced to 1-2 percent in the 1970s, and then people relaxed... If we do not have proper strategies and do not work together - yes there is a fear of sliding back."
"Maintaining the gains in fighting malaria is probably the biggest challenge facing Zanzibar now," said Asha Abdallah, the Minister of Employment, Youths, Children, and Women.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: It's an Insult to Call Me IBB's Boy Declares Gen. Shagaya
Headline: Nigeria: It's an Insult to Call Me IBB's Boy Declares Gen. Shagaya
Body_Text:
General John Shagaya is a serving Senator of the Federal Republic and Chairman of Northern Senators' Forum . In this interview, the Minister of Internal Affairs in the defunct IBB's regime speaks on a number of issues including IBB's return to politics, the Jos crisis and acting President Jonathan's trip to the United States.
Excerpts.
It started with OBJ. Now IBB wants his turn as a democraticPresident. They were military heads of state and have hijacked democracy. What do the generals want?
I will say that democracy needs generals like us. And if you ask me why, I will tell you there are three main reasons. The first reason is that the generals are Nigerian citizens and are of age to seek the mandate of Nigerians to participate in the process of electioneering of leadership at the various levels of leadership in this country.
The second reason is that the constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria states nowhere that any professional body is banned from seeking the mandate of the citizenry. So, there is nowhere in the constitution except a coup situation that forces anybody not to be elected because he was an ex-military officer.
The third reason which is very selfish is that anywhere in the world, a military officer is better equipped to understand leadership, human management, resource management, international relations and respect for nations of the world.
Hence, in countries like Britain, even among the royals, a prince and a princess must be exposed to some military training, for that prince and princess to qualify to become a King or a Queen.
In the US, from the attendance of independence in the 17th Century, that is 1776, or thereabout till date, they always prefer a retired military officer to rule their country, beginning from Washington to whoever. Amongst all the Presidents of the United States, I think only one did not serve in the military and that is Bill Clinton.
And this shows that there is a merit in a qualification that is attached to an individual that has acquired certain discipline through military training. So, these three major reasons qualify any one of us to qualify for the mandate from our people to participate responsibly and reasonably in politics.
The individual or individuals compete with all those who are qualified from the constituencies. It's the choice of the constituency to vote or not vote for the person. It's not mere fighting an imaginary enemy and playing to the gallery or using the media profession to fight ex-military or no ex-military officers. I will advise such people, the biggest of the critics of ex- military men to come out and seek the mandate of their constituencies, to qualify to be elected and lets see how many of them will be elected.
The years when these former military dictators were in government were not the best years for Nigeria. We know the military mismanaged Nigeria.
Well, Babangida has thrown a challenge to Nigerians who play to the gallery. He raised the question that Nigerians should compare his era, the developments of that period, the earnings of that period to developments in the country between 1999 - 2007. And then, you can say which one was more wasteful. He asked that question. And I think the media people should ask all those who are criticising him to find an answer.
It is not enough to say because John Shagaya is an ex-military man, therefore, he is not qualified to be in the Senate. If we compare the contributions on the floor of the Senate, research or whatever, the ex-military members are solid senators. I do know that Senator David Mark, the Senate President has been able to manage the upper chamber very well.
IBB
In the history of the senate of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, no ex-uniformed person has been found wanting. General Ike Nwachukwu as a Senator did well. David Mark, the Senate President has done so well to maintain stability in the Senate and for over three years, no banana peels have been thrown at him. It means there is something in his quality of leadership.
But among the civilians, when the leadership of the Senate went to the East, in eight years, there were six Senate Presidents. Compare, contrast and provide for me an answer.
You are just speaking like a military man that you are. Perhaps you can say the military are gifted in the art of manipulation.
Well, I don't think that is the right word to use because if you use that word, then you are abusing or insulting the sensibility of very many educated Nigerians. Are you saying that if you take away the percentage of military men in the population, the remaining 140 million Nigerians are idiots? You are insulting the sensibility of the people and it is not right for you to say what you said.
The outside world would laugh at us and would consider us so cheap that we would rather prefer mediocrity to quality representation. And so, wherever Nigerians find the quality of representation, so shall the person be elected. The question of whether the fellow was an ex-military man, or an accountant or a former Marine Navy is not the issue. If the individual is good, he is good.
You were the Minister of Internal Affairs in IBB's regime and so, your point is understandable.
I was the Minister of Internal Affairs under the administration of IBB. I modified that using the word administration because a regime is more draconian but the IBB regime was more democratic than even some democratic systems in Africa.
So, I will rather prefer you call it an administration because they were few military men in that government. The larger percentage, about eighty- something were civilian brothers and sisters. There were a lot of very learned people like Prince Bola Ajibola, who was brought in from the Hague. Chu Okongwu, a financial wizard was in the World Bank was also in that administration.
There were also Kalu Idika Kalu, Dr. Olikoye Ransome Kuti, from World Heath Organisation and many of them. If you say the military are manipulative, you are insulting the sensibility of these other people. These were the people who ran the IBB's administration.
They were not uniformed people, but they were brilliant Nigerians who were brought into Nigeria to come and run the system. So, this is 2010. Let the Nigerian media begin to drift away from seeing the people in government as they are.
We did that in the early 1959 and sixties because we were fighting a colonial master to win an independence. We won the independence and now, we should be speaking as Nigerians. We should be trying to find solutions as Nigerians.
You spoke so glowingly about the administration of IBB who was in power for eight years. What does he want again in Aso Rock? Did he forget something?
I think the question is, when it comes to voting, will you be able to work out a counter strategy to tell Nigerians that you have a better candidate than that fellow. And if you are able to sell your candidate, for God's sake, IBB will accept defeat because that will be the voice of Nigerians.
We all have a vote each. But if anybody emerges as a result of the collective vote of Nigerians, it will be wrong for anyone of us to sit down to complain. It has been like this from the beginning. Until one dies, as long as there is a calling for one to serve at whatever level and one has the strength and the disposition to serve, I think individual Nigerians, men and women, should for God's sake be allowed to serve.
Lets get away from such issues that are meaningless and the man has said, if you have anything against me, go to court and challenge me. And in any case, all political parties in their nomination forms have a portion that individuals would be disqualified if they are found wanting, if they've been indicted, if they've been jailed. So, Nigerians can go and swear to an affidavit, that XYZ, is unfit to rule us. And it is also my right and the right of any other candidate to also sue when his accusers have no proof of their allegations.
We have been recycling leaders from OBJ and a lot of Nigerians from past regimes are still in the system. What about the youths?
I beg you Chioma, a lot of African countries would love to have some of these our past leaders to come and head their countries. No matter what you think, no matter what you feel, there is nothing you can do about the voice of majority.
What is the beauty of democracy? It is the right to speak and that is why you and I are talking today. You have the right to interview me, you have the right to inform Nigerians because your profession allows you to. And also, I have the right to share this experience with you.
Isn't it time for certain people to leave the stage for the young generation instead of all these recycling in the system. Now, we are being told the young people have nothing to offer.
Why don't you encourage any young person you think have the quality and the vision to lead the country to come on board? Let him get to the media house to present himself, come out and ask Nigerians to vote for him. That will put to rest any question in that direction. Until Obama became the President of the United States, nobody thought that anybody of black origin, who 245-250 years ago would have been regarded as a slave would become the President of America. We must see ourselves in the light of progression, not retrogression.
And so, this question or challenges of who is qualified and who is not should not be discussed in our history. But we should be discussing who is the best material to handle a particular office in our country? That is what we should be discussing.
Is zoning still necessary in PDP?
I'm very incompetent to answer that question. I'm a very loyal party candidate and PDP card-carrying member and if my national chairman for the past 14 days have said that for the interest of Nigeria's stability, the zoning system will be maintained, I have no reason to quarrel with that. Don't forget, that is a party decision but not the constitution. So, anybody is free to go and run from another party.
Thank God, the Independent party is already added. So, if anybody wants to defeat PDP because of its zoning policy and that independent fellow thinks he can come from any part of Nigeria, for God's sake, such a person should come out to run.
So, can the Acting President Jonathan run for presidency on PDP platform?
I can't speak for him. I think you better ask him.
I understand the so-called zoning policy is an opinion of one man and it is not even approved by the National Working Committee (NWC) of PDP and therefore, was not generally approved.
Well, I can't join issues with your own thinking and feeling . It's better you ask those who are responsible for such pronouncements. So, I decline to answer that question.
Will the North have a consensus candidate under the platform of the PDP?
Once you begin to talk of consensus candidate, then, the polity may negate the democratic nature of allowing people to come out and vie and let Nigerians choose. If the policy of the party at the end of the day whether it is through primary, through whatever means, produces a candidate that could face many other candidates, he would go. Don't forget that Nigeria has 53 registered political parties, including the Independent Party.
Any of these parties could produce the President, why are we worried about the PDP ? Every Nigerian above the age of 40 years is qualified to run for the President of Nigeria. The coming out of one individual who thinks he is of age to run does not stop any other person from coming out to run. So, where is the issue?
Where is the problem? When people talk about a fellow like IBB the way they do, then it means such fellow possesses certain qualities and potentials that so clearly make it known to mediocres that they cannot challenge him. It clearly glorifies him .
And so, the challenge now involves the media to discuss the issue. Let Nigerians check through the 53 parties and pick a candidate to challenge the PDP candidate, to challenge an ex-military man, to challenge an ex-accountant or ex-whatever. In the senate, we have people from the media. Adeyemi is from the media and some others, like Chris Anyanwu.
I'm sure they are elected because they were good. We also have people like \professor Aminu, a former VC, a professor of Medicine, a former Minister, former ambassador and today a Senator. It means that there are some good in him that Adamawa people have seen. Why narrow it to a military man? That is very negative for the purpose of development. It's not progressive at all. I accept, there is an on-going debate on IBB's intention to return and I'm just contributing to the debate.
I hope it wouldn't be said, another 'IBB boy' has spoken?
Anybody who says that will be insulting my intellect because I'm a Senator of the Federal Republic of Nigeria. I am representing a senatorial district. My constitution allows me to speak on behalf of those who have mandated me. I am speaking with you, sharing my views and I am also sharing the views of the majority of the people who thought I was qualified in 2007 to be elected a senator.
I want to deviate from politics to enquire about Jos. What is the situation there now?
You are putting this question to somebody who is a member of the Presidential Committee that is looking into the crisis with a view to making a recommendation to the Federal government of Nigeria . Be it as it may, I will tell you that the situation in Jos is very much under control.
The committee has restrained itself from giving individual interviews on the subject matter. We do not want a situation where, until the report gets to the President, different comments would be made about the recommendations of the committee. So, I won't comment on that except that the issue of the crisis remains isolated to that one local government, that is, Jos North and partially Jos south . It has not involved the remaining 15 local governments in the state. So, the state government, the military and the police have done well enough to isolate the problem within these two localities.
So, what is your prediction? Would peace ever return to the local government in question?
There is hope that peace will return.
But in the recent time, like Monday or thereabout, there were reports of some killings still going on.
You see Chioma, everywhere in Nigeria, everyday, there were reported killings, killings that are worse than the one you are talking about.
I believe this matter is escalating...
No, it is not escalating. We can look at three weeks ago, the Director-General of NYSC, asked youth corp members deployed to Plateau to choose if they should be redeployed to other parts of the country. Unanimously, they said no, they want to serve in Plateau state and they are all serving in Plateau State and these boys and girls, numbering over a thousand come from the 36 states of the federation.
Now, we are in a civilian rule and we are meant to understand from the crisis in Jos that soldiers don't take orders from civilian governors. Is that the truth?
I don't know where you got that from. If you read your constitution, you will see that an executive governor of a state is the Chief Security Officer of the state. And where there is a situation of internal unrest and the military are ordered out of the barracks by the President of the Federal Republic, the governor chairs the security meeting of that state.
That military general subordinates himself to the state governor. I'm yet to know if there is a deviation anywhere in our country.
There were reported killings of a Pastor and his wife in Kano recently and policemen being stationed in Bauchi to forestall an outbreak of religious violence. The Libyan leader, Gadaffi suggested Nigeria be split along religious lines. Should Nigeria consider it?
There have been crisis in Ebonyi where communities woke up and started killing themselves for a piece of land. There have been fighting in the South-West, there have been fights in the Delta, there was the Boko Haram crisis. There have been all kinds of crises in the country and each of them had its own peculiarities.
The killings you talked about now happened in states outside where I am resident. I have no authority to comment on those. Bauchi has a governor. Kano has a governor and of course, we have the federal government outfit.
That strengthens the suggestion made by Gadaffi.
I don't want to join issues with Gadaffi. He is a head of government and a Senator of the Federal Republic of Nigeria like me who is experienced will not join issues with him except to sympathise with Gadaffi, that being a member of the African Union, he had chaired AU and so also have many past leaders of Nigeria. He knows that there have been no complainants from Nigeria about the issues he raised.
I wonder how a judge can sit down and pass judgement on an issue that has no complainant and respondent. He should be educated enough, having chaired AU, having visited Nigeria many times that Nigeria is a multi-religious nation. If he doesn't know, his ambassadors would have advised him on Nigeria. It is unfortunate. If truly he made those statements, it's unfortunate. But lets believe that he may have been misquoted.
But when we look at our existence as a nation, one would be tempted to agree that perhaps, that's what we really need here.
Maybe I need to take a few seconds to explain further what I mean. The community where I come from is Langtang, a local government that does not have up to one million people. We are about 900,000. And we have Christians, Muslims, non-believers and all other types of religions that are around the world has made Langtang a cosmopolitan town.
So, if you say now that you want to divide Nigeria along religious lines, are you now saying that my cousin who is a sheik in Jos will be taken to Sokoto and my brother who is a Christian, to England because Christianity came from the West? So, lets not go into it. I don't want to discuss that.
What's your opinion on acting President Jonathan's visit to United States?
I will imagine it would improve our diplomatic relationship with the outside world. Until few weeks ago, Nigeria had one common issue to discuss and that was the issue of young Muttallab, who on the 25th of December, attempted to blow up a plane. Nigeria, a peaceful nation we are, now was unfortunate to be one of those countries that the US would pay close attentions to on issues of terrorism.
Now, the issue went on and on and was discussed severally on different platforms in Nigeria. The media was the greatest contributor on that debate and of course, sending certain messages to the United States that indeed that action was not fair considering our role in the maintenance of peace in other countries of the world, global peace if I can put it that way. We went on peace-keeping in many countries of the world.
And of course, you know our role in ECOMOG, in African sub-region, Africa and the world at large, and we deserve to be treated better than to be likened to terrorists because of the incidence of one young person . So, the visit of the acting President to that country should tell Nigerians that a lot of good will is being extended to us, and that by the behaviour of one young kid, we should not be maltreated by way of international relations.
And so, many of us are quite happy at that trip and that the acting President was granted audience. Don't forget that in our last fifty years or so, it was not every head of government that was granted audience on visits to America. So for me , its a good omen.
Finally, what do you see when you look ahead of 2011 general elections?
I'm honestly reading the mood of the national assembly and the speed with which the national assembly took and considered the strong views of Nigerians and especially those that were expressed in some special reports like the Uwais report that suggested that certain areas of INEC's law need to be amended to give confidence to Nigerians to allow for the emergence of quality Nigerians, truly voted Nigerian people and if that law comes into operation, I want to believe that the elections of the year 2011 will be better than other elections.
Let us also not forget that if we look at our past history, from 1959, during regional elections, all sorts of criminalities were practised. But as we came along in history, we realised that all those have been minimised. So, as a developing nation, we are doing very well. We keep comparing ourselves with developed countries of the world forgetting that even though we made mistakes yesterday we could improve tomorrow.

Original_Search_Title: Forestry Body Questioned On Performance
Headline: Uganda: Forestry Body Questioned On Performance
Body_Text:
Kampala — FORESTY professionals have demanded a review of the performance of the National Forestry Authority (NFA) in a bid to save forestry resources that are being mismanaged.
"NFA's performance must be reviewed if the scarce forestry reserves are to be preserved," Banana Abwori, a professor from Makerere University's faculty of forestry and natural conservation, said recently.
Abwori was speaking at the annual conference on forestry organised by the Uganda Forestry Association at Makerere university in Kampala. John Kaboggoza, the former dean of the university's forestry faculty, said corrupt NFA officials at district level immensely contributed to the destruction of forestry in the country.
David Walugembe, the secretary general of the forestry association, said plans were underway to promote eucalyptus species based on specific criteria.

Original_Search_Title: Facing the New Frontiers - Accounting for People's Money
Headline: Liberia: Facing the New Frontiers - Accounting for People's Money
Body_Text:
On a tour of rural Liberia, she bought some bananas and peanuts along the road from a peasant woman. Then she demanded a receipt for the transaction from the woman as evidence of money spent. The woman, illiterate and not understanding what Finance Minister Dr. Antoinette Sayeh meant or wanted, looked in awe. She could not understand the transaction, for all her life, she has no idea of a receipt for selling her little goods to people who do not need evidence that they purchased LD5 peanuts or bananas.
Encounters with European colleagues tell the same story. For every transaction, however minute, whether it is buying a bottle of beer, they ask: 'can you tell him to give me a receipt, please?"
The rationale for demanding receipts is for accountability and transparency, without which conclusions of theft or fraud are reached.
Dr. Sayeh, like the European colleagues, emerge from different and distinct socio-economic settings, where documentary evidence for financial transactions is mandatory. If not, fraud is concluded, for there is no other mechanism through which to prove that the money was spent for the intended purpose.
Finance Minister Augustine Ngafuan's admission that this country, despite being independent since 1847, never had an accounting standard, summarizes the anarchy with regards to public finance. It was free for all. No one was accountable. People could steal state money, build buidings, and then rent them to the state, a factor that eplains while the government is the biggest tenant.
But when he suggested that there is no need for a structure such as an auditing commission to ensure accountability, with his use as the Bible-Thou shall no steal as being sufficient, it takes a different dimension. Man by nature is inpure. Thus the need for laws and structures to monitor his designs, just as God did with the Ten Commandments.
Here, the standards are different. For decades, public officials have grown accustomed to seeing public money as their inheritance, and therefore the need not to account, whether through receipts or other forms of documentation. Now, with new norms, there is anarchy and recriminations. Times are different. The new frontier is accountability and transparency, no longer the declared war against foreign ideology-communism or socialism-that rewarded the vain and the hyper-corrupt.
Following series of stories on the HIPC (Heavily Indebted Poor Countries) audits necessary for Liberia to qualify as a heavily indebted poor country and therefore apply for more loans to get indebted again, one former government official walked into our offices.
"If they come and audit the New Democrat now, you will be short..."
His point is that some of the expenditure placed under fraud is really not fraudulent. It is the absence of receipts and documentation. It is the carelessness in documenting transactions when public money is the case. Dr. Sayeh knew this. So she demanded receipt for her peanuts, since the money she was spending was state money, not hers. Others simply do not see the difference. For example, when the UN Panel of Experts described the Maritime Fund as 'a cash extraction box', Mr. Benoni Urey, then Maritime Commission, angrily retorted: 'it is our money '
The difficulty of accounting for very cent in a non-accountable society is clear. But then the former official was told that if this paper claimed to have certain equipment and facilities that auditors discovered it does not have, this is a different case.
The uniqueness of the current General Auditing Commission audits, as compared to the past, is their investigative nature and approach. The GAC goes beyond receipts and documents to plunge into field verifications of projects the government claims to have spent public money on that in fact do not exist. In pure and simple terms, this is stealing taxpayer's money and state resources.
But these are changing times. Minister Ngafuan warned the Auditor General to be careful with what he said are 'political machinations'because this is a political season. He said once one's name enters an audit report, it becomes a 'politcl hell'. He is right. The process of naming and shaming could be deterrence.
The audits are replete with false projects that the government says it completed. Few examples are noteworthy.

Original_Search_Title: Jonathan Orders Police to Bring Former Governor 'Dead or Alive'
Headline: Nigeria: Get Ibori Dead Or Alive, Jonathan Orders Onovo - I'm Not Guarded By Militants - Ibori
Body_Text:
Warri — Acting President Goodluck Jonathan has reportedly told Inspector General of Police, Ogbonna Onovo, to produce former Delta State Governor, James Ibori, dead or alive, because he is angry that Oghara youths prevented policemen from arresting Ibori on Tuesday.
In the police team were Deputy Inspector General of Police, Ekpoudom Ekpoudom; Assistant Inspector General of Police, Zone 6, Muhammed Abubakar; and the Delta State Police Commissioner.
The squad allegedly informed Jonathan that on their way to Oghara, "We sighted a road block mounted by militants, so we had to retreat to avoid bloodbath."
The source added: "When (Jonathan) got the news that Ibori used militants to evade arrest and that policemen had to withdrew to spare avoidable loss of lives, he was very angry and called (Onovo) personally and told him to get Ibori for the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) dead or alive and banged the phone."
It was also learnt that Onovo immediately called Ekpoudom and told him of the order.
"So what we are planning now is to lay siege to Oghara from the air, land, and water, and smoke out Ibori wherever he may be hiding, otherwise (Onovo) would be in trouble," a source at Force Headquarters added.
Jonathan reportedly wants Ibori in the EFCC net before the end of this week.
"But the police have never arrested anybody for the EFCC, so why is this case different? This fight can always be sorted out amicably without going this far, but we have to do our job," the source lamented.
"The Commander of the Joint Task Force (JTF) in the Delta region has been put on red alert to be on the look out for Ibori, who is allegedly somewhere in Oghara, his home town.
"Policemen have cordoned off all access roads to the village, but our fear is that what happened in Odi may repeat itself in Oghara, and that would be bad."
Residents of Oghara have started leaving their homes for fear of attack by government troops said to be combat ready.
Frances Tom, a motor mechanic in the town, said in a telephone interview that he has relocated his family to Warri because he fears the face off may degenerate into an inter-tribal war.
About 10 persons allegedly brutalised by policemen who stormed Oghara on Wednesday to arrest Ibori are lying critically ill in hospital in the town.
A team of security personnel, led by Ekpoudom descended on the serene town in seven Hilux pick up vans, and two Expert Peugeot vans, accompanied by two motorcycle riders.
It was learnt that the arrival of the team comprising Army, Navy, and police personnel caused a stir, but the residents were united against the bravado.
Residents accused Jonathan of acting out a script by ordering Onovo to arrest Ibori, in defiance of a court ruling that the status quo be maintained.
Besides, they accused Jonathan of using his office to trample on the fundamental human rights of a citizen, as his directive to Onovo smacks of injustice and is prejudicial.
One of the residents, Ejiro Okpomor, said: "Nowhere in a civilised world would you see what happened here in Oghara, except in a banana republic.
"It is reminiscent of the military era where brute force and official fiat were used to settle scores.
"The matter in question is before a court of competent jurisdiction, yet (Jonathan) ordered security agents to arrest someone. Is it not prejudicial?
"To me, the action is irresponsible. The question therefore is, where is the due process and rule of law that this administration says it is adhering to?"
Okpomor reiterated that the Constitution is very clear on criminal issues; that is, an accused person is presumed innocent until otherwise proven.
"Yet Jonathan has flagrantly set aside this provision and continue to behave like a bull in a china shop.
"As far as I know Edwin Clark is trying to whip up tribal sentiment. It is the same Clark, who benefited immensely from Ibori's government, who has turned around to whip up these spurious allegations.
Also on Wednesday, Ibori's media aide, Tony Eluemunor, condemned the police action and reiterated the call for all parties to respect the rule of law and allow the courts to determine the matter, being the only authority in Nigeria competent to settle disputes between parties.
"The government and indeed all authorities are subject to law and the jurisdiction of the courts to which disputes are constitutionally vested and decided," he said.
Ibori reiterated through Eluemunor in a statement that he neither sponsors nor makes use of militants.
He said State Security Service (SSS) and the EFCC have all been served with the ruling of the Asaba Federal High Court which ruled on Monday that the status quo be maintained.
No militant or any group engaged the security agents in any fight as Oghara people are peace loving and law abiding, Ibori insisted, expressing fears that the falsehood may be an excuse for the security agencies to visit more mayhem on defenceless civilians, especially the children and women of the town.
"Therefore," the statement added, "We categorically refute the unfounded allegation that militants disturbed the policemen who stormed Oghara on April 20, 2010.
"This sort of allegation is on its second life-circle; it would be recalled that when the earlier Ibori versus EFCC case opened in Kaduna in December 2007, the same wild allegation gained currency.
"It was brought up then at the court by the EFCC that was hell-bent on getting the court to deny Ibori bail.
After the Federal High Court, Asaba exonerated Ibori, the EFCC has come up again with yet unnamed charges but ample innuendoes, leading the press to mention billions of Naira worth of bank shares.
"Yet, it is on record that the EFCC has yet to make public the charge it has against Ibori. But what has reoccurred is the allegation about militants.
"This is an archaic repetition that should not be taken seriously by anyone, unless the government is confessing that the amnesty granted to former militants has failed."
The Conference of Nigerian Political Parties (CNPP) on Wednesday warned against political vendetta by Jonathan, and demanded the investigation of all corrupt former Governors and those named in the Halliburton scandal.
It stressed that the anti-graft crusade should be all-inclusive, especially since corrupt political office holders walk the street as free men.
CNPP Secretary Willy Ezugwu said Nigerians are watching the process and would react appropriately as soon as bias is discovered.
"Interestingly, we have seen parts of the Okiro Report as published in the papers, and now we want the publication of the full report," he demanded.
But Ezugwu admonished the anti-graft agencies to abide by the rule of law in their renewed crusade.

Original_Search_Title: Invocation of Sections 143, 144, Acid Test Before National Assembly, EXCOF
Headline: Nigeria: Invocation of Sections 143, 144, Acid Test Before National Assembly, EXCOF
Body_Text:
Lagos — The circumstance of Goodluck Jonathan's emergence as acting President was no doubt an extra-legal necessity which many agreed was used to satisfy the exigencies created by certain situations outside the contemplation of the constitution. Since the position is meant to be "during temporary absence of the President", not a few Nigerians expect the government to correct the incongruity by invocating relevant sections of the constitution at least to put the issue behind us. Some senior lawyers, who spoke to DAILY INDEPENDENT's Adam Adedimeji and Francis Famoroti want the National Assembly or the new Executive Council of the Federation (EXCOF) to invoke Sections 143 or 144 of the constitution. In fact, they want them invoked urgently.
SEVENTY-THREE days after the inauguration of Dr. Goodluck Jonathan as the acting President of of Nigeria, the clamour for the removal of the ailing President Umaru Yar'Adua on the grounds of incapacitation is growing thicker by the day.
The lawyers who expounded this argument in separate interviews with Daily Independent in Lagos say that the process would enable Jonathan to assume the office of the President in accordance with the provisions of Sections 143,144 and 145 of the Constitution.
Specifically, they said Section 145, which provides for acting President during temporary absence of the President did not envisage invisible presidency as it were in the present circumstance.
Due to the vacuum created by the invisibility of ailing President Yar'Adua since November 26, 2009, the Senate, relying on the "Doctrine of Necessity" had last February 9 passed a resolution making Jonathan the acting President and Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces.
The House of Representatives followed with a similar resolution and endorsed the decision of the upper legislative chamber.
But the action, which was a political solution to the logjam foisted on the country, had no clear constitutional nod.
The Senate's claim that it relied on section 145 of the constitution had strengthened the controversy, even as some analysts, especially lawyers contend that the process was unconstitutional, null and void.
These lawyers say the failure of the ailing President to comply with the provisions of section 145 before travelling to Saudi Arabia on health ground amounted to impeachable offence.
Section 145 prescribes that "Whenever the President transmits to the President of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of Representatives a written declaration that he is proceeding on vacation or that he is otherwise unable to discharge the functions of his office, until he transmits to them a written declaration to the contrary such functions shall be discharged by the Vice-President as Acting President.
Amidst the failure of the ailing President to transmit such declaration, the Senate ostensibly applied the doctrine of necessity to inaugurate Jonathan and in the process breached the provisions of section 145.
Although the Senate tried to justify the political option it took, this however polarised the National Assembly into a number of divides such as the pro-Yar'Adua group, pro-Jonathan and the National Interest Groups.
In the ensuing debate, Jonathan dissolved the Yar' Adua appointed EXCOF and re-constituted the council.
Some lawyers argue that time was ripe for the new EXCOF to invoke the provisions of section 144, and thereby declare ailing President Yar'Adua incapacitated in a bid to restore constitutional democracy in the country.
Mr. Gabriel Ojumah, Lagos Lawyer and Chairman, Citizens Right Watch
"We all know that the President, Alhaji Umaru Yar' Adua, is incapacitated. If the politicians and top public functionaries in the legislature and the executive love Nigeria and Nigerians, if they want the living standard of the people of Nigeria to be improved and if they love democracy, they should go ahead and do what Section 144 of the 1999 Constitution provides.
You know the section says the President or Vice President shall cease to hold office, if
by a resolution passed by two- third majority of all members of the Executive Council of the Federation it is declared that the President or Vice-President is incapable of discharging the functions of his office; and the declaration is verified, after such medical examination as may be necessary, by a medical panel established for that purpose.
Where the medical panel certifies in its report to the Senate President and the Speaker of the House that in its opinion the President or Vice-President is suffering from such infirmity of body or mind as renders him permanently incapable of discharging the functions of his office. Then, a notice thereof signed by the Senate President or the Speaker of the House shall be published in the Official Gazette of the Government.
The President or his deputy shall cease to hold office as from the date of publication of the notice of the medical report.
If therefore EXCOF cannot do what is constitutionally provided, it shows that they are part of the group causing corruption in the society. This is the right time for them to invoke the relevant provisions of the 1999 Constitution.
It is unfortunate we don't want to face reality. The reality on the ground is that President Yar' Adua is incapacitated. And since he is incapacitated, the machinery should be in motion by setting up a medical panel in line with section 144 of the constitution to determine his health so that we can do away with all the distractions particularly the cabal who wants to remain in power at all costs. "
Robert Omote, Graduate School, Faculty of Law, University of Benin
The decision to rush the ailing President home was occasioned by the desperation of members of his kitchen cabinet in connivance with a cabal to short-circuit the Vice-President ( as he was then) from acting. The present subterfuge jolt by the same cabal to return the same ailing President to the seat of power in their quest for relevance are political calculations to cause distractions in the country.
Section 144 of the 1999 Constitution with its innocuous provision circumscribe to the knitty-gritty of permanent incapacity of Mr. President or his vice. Section 144 (1) provides 'The President or Vice President shall cease to hold office if;
(a) By a resolution passed by two thirds majority of all the members of the executive council of the federation, it is declared that the President or Vice President is incapable of discharging the function of his office; and
(b) The declaration is verified, after such medical examination as may be necessary, by a medical panel established under subsection (4) of this section in its report to the President of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
2. Where the medical panel certifies in the report that in its opinion, the President or Vice President is suffering from such infirmity of body or mind as renders him permanently incapable of discharging his functions of his office, a notice thereof signed by the President of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of Representatives shall be published in the Official Gazette of the Government of the Federation.
3. The President or Vice President shall cease to hold office as from the date of publication of the notice of the medical report pursuant to subsection (2) of this section.
4. The medical panel to which this section relates shall be appointed by the President of the Senate, and shall comprise five medical practitioners in Nigeria;
(a) One of whom shall be the personal physician of the holder of the office concerned; and
(b) Four other medical practitioners who have in the opinion of the President of the Senate, attached a high degree of eminence in the field of medicine relative to the nature of the examination to be concluded in accordance with foregoing provisions.
Talking on the way forward, I think in view of the President' s incapacitation, there are some constitutional provisions to stabilize the country's body polity with recourse to:
S.306 (1)(2)(3) (4) of the 1999 Constitution. Section 306(1) provides thus " Save as otherwise provided in this section, any person who is appointed, selected or otherwise selected to any office established by this constitution may resign from that office by writing under his hand addressed to the authority or person by whom he was appointed, elected or selected.
Section 306(3) provides "The notice of resignation of the President and of the Vice President shall respectively be addressed to the President of the Senate
Alternatively, members of the National Assembly can invoke Section 143 (1) (b) (II) . Section 143(1) provides; The President or Vice President may be removed from office in accordance with the provisions of this section.
Section 143 (1) provides 'The President or Vice-President may be removed from office in accordance with the provisions of this section.
Section 143 (2) Whenever a notice of any allegation in writing signed by not less than one third of the members of the National Assembly.
Is presented to the President of the Senate
Stating that the holder of the office of the President or Vice-President is guilty of gross misconduct in the performance of the functions of his office, detailed particulars of which is specified "
Section 143 (11) provides thus "In this section-"gross misconduct" means a grave violation or breach of the provisions of this constitution or a misconduct of such nature as amounts in the pinion of the National Assembly to gross misconduct.
Recourse also can be taken from S. 144 (1) )( a) which provides thus;
The President or Vice President shall cease to hold office if-
By a resolution passed by tow-thirds majority of all the members of the executive council of the federation it is declared that the President or Vice President is incapable of discharging the functions of his office..
Obviously, there are challenges at the domestic and international fronts that will propel the acting President to be under stress. A trip for example outside the county will imply that there will be no one to preside over the affairs of the country. Nigeria is not a banana republic. It is imperative that the President be impeached to signal the death of the cabal's operatives to curtail every distraction both real and imaginary.
Ibukun Oremodu, Lagos-based Lawyer
The ailing President, Alhaji Umaru Yar'Adua should be impeached because notwithstanding the fact that he is unhealthy, the circumstances surrounding how he sneaked out of the country to Saudi Arabia in November last year and also returned home this year while being shielded from public glare amounts to gross misconduct.
In any event, our constitution did not contemplate the invisibility of the President. And by extension, the 1999 Constitution is greater than any individual no matter how highly placed and that is why I will suggest that the relevant provisions of the constitution be invoked against the ailing President.
The Executive Council of the Federation that has just been constituted by the acting President, Dr. Goodluck Jonathan should therefore initiate the constitutional process leading to Yar'Adua's impeachment and remove him from office by reason of his inability to continue to discharge the function of his office.
Interestingly, all the Yar'Adua's political structures are no longer in place. So, the EXCOF should invoke the provisions of section 144 of the 1999 Constitution and impeach him. Jonathan should be allowed to take over fully as the President along with a new deputy."
Norrison Ibinabo Quakers, Attorney At Law
Section 1(1) provides that the constitution is supreme and its provisions shall have binding force on all authorities and persons throughout the federation. Against this backdrop, it is safe to state that all that the National Assembly can do, since the provisions of Sections 144 and 145 have not been complied with by both the Executive Council of the Federation and the President respectively, the National Assembly can proceed to impeach the President for gross misconduct as defined under Section 143(11) of the Constitution, bearing in mind that the President has been out of office for more than 74 days without a formal notice, as envisaged under Section 145.
At this stage of our nationhood, national interest must override personal, ethnic, religious and other parochial considerations. Since the state of health of the President is unknown, the National Assembly should invoke the provision of Section 143(11) and allow Section 146(1) to kick in, which enables the Vice President to assume full presidential powers. The National Assembly's reliance on the BBC broadcast of Mr. President as qualifying for the letter or declaration is most absurd and bizarre. Nigeria is greater than any single individual or group. The National Assembly must allow the Constitution to take its course. Whilst it is conceded that the resolution is as a result of political expediency, which to a reasonable extent has moved the country forward, there is a Section of the Constitution, which can be invoked to change the political equation. I believe the Executive Council of the Federation as a matter of urgency, should exercise the power conferred on them in Section 144(1)(a) of the Constitution by declaring that the President is incapable of discharging the functions of his office, so that a substantive President and Vice President are confirmed and appointed respectively. Under this present scenario the provisions of Section 146 can be invoked easily.
Nurudeen Ogbara, former Chairman, Nigeria Bar Association, Ikorodu Branch
I expect the Executive Council of the Federation to immediately pass a resolution to the effect that the President is not fit and proper to discharge his functions in accordance with the provisions of the Constitution. But I think, they will not want to be a hurry to do that so that people will not read meaning into the dissolution of the past EXCOF and the constitution of the new one. With time, I expect the EXCOF will pass the resolution in line with the provisions of the constitution. We hope they will not disappoint us Nigerians."
Ademola Adewale, former 2nd Vice-Chairman, NBA, Ikeja Branch
If the unfolding scenario is clear, some people said to be members of pro-Yar'Adua group are threatening to challenge the constitution of EXCOF, it is time for the Attorney General of the Federation to advise EXCOF on Section 144 of the 1999 Constitution, to declare the ailing President incapacitated.
The recent unfolding scenario wherein the acting President has been disallowed from seeing President Yar'Adua is enough for the Attorney General of the Federation to advise the President or the EXCOF to initiate process to declare him incapacities. It is important to start the prosecution of the so-called cabal that has created this logjam in the affairs of the government of the Federation of Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: A Banana Republic
Headline: Nigeria: A Banana Republic
Body_Text:
I was looking for another word on Microsoft Encarta dictionaries when I stumbled upon the phrase "Banana Republic". I was attracted to it, clicked on it and read the one and half sentence definition. What I saw made me curious and I decided to read and reread it. It excited a line of thought in my head and I decided to copy it for more reflection.
Here is the definition of banana republic according to Microsoft Encarta 2009:"Small dictatorship. A small country with an unstable government and an economy dependent on the export of a single product or on outside financial help (disapproving)".A cursory look at the definition of banana republic would give the picture of Nigeria fitting the bill of banana republic only as it regards the phrase "dependent on the export of a single product or outside financial help.
However, a deeper study will show clearly to an intelligent observer that Nigeria is in every sense manifest, a banana republic. Let's take a deeper look, if you would. Our national dailies are replete with such phrase as "this country is ruled by a cabal".
A cabal simply means a group of conspirators and plotters, particularly one formed for political purposes, a clique, an exclusive group of people. The Cabal (1867-1873) who ruled England during that period was just five families out of the whole England. This seems to be the origin of this word. This fits a small dictatorship.
The cabal that calls the shots in Nigeria is just a small dictatorship. Ours is clearly a country with an unstable government and an economy dependent on the export of a single product and loans and grants. From independence to date, we have had the worst possible political history anyone can think about. Even in the last decade of democracy, the feeling of it is exactly as if there has been a war or some terrible political upheaval. If your argument is about the term; "a small", well, here is a country with the highest population in the whole continent of Africa, smaller in landmass only to one two other countries, but with no moral or political muscle to talk about Cameroun, Togo or Ghana.
There is an African proverb that says: "Height does not mean that my son has grown". So, Nigeria's physical size does not translate to anything meaningful in benefits to its citizenry. T herefore, Nigeria is small and mostly non-existent in the psyche of its people. This is not a matter of being unpatriotic. This is issue-based. By impact, there is no basis for the ordinary Nigerian to remember this country even in his or her dreams.
There is just no relationship or love lost between Nigeria and 'its people'. In this country, it is every man for himself. Abroad, Nigerians are treated like shit. In fact, an estimated 25 per cent of Nigerians abroad are not with Nigerian passport. They are either Ghanaians or Cameroonians, and if they could speak any French, they would claim to come from such places as Mali, Togo, Benin Republic or any other toy country than claim to be Nigerian.
The instability in the Nigerian government that qualifies Nigeria as a banana republic is not just the recent actionable medical fraud foisted on the country over President Yar'Adua's health. The maneuverings in People's Democratic Party, PDP that brought Olusegun Obasanjo to power, the elections from 1999 to date, Odi massacre, Boko Haram, Jos crisis, Niger Delta militancy, kidnappings, corruption, lack of electricity, intolerable criminality, to mention but a few, are clear cases of instability that promote or demote Nigeria to the unenviable position of a banana republic in every sense of the word.
In the last few years, the argument has been on as to whether Nigeria is a failed state or not. It is surprising that this is the source of argument. The indications are clear. A country that depends almost solely on oil exports for revenue, where in spite of several trillions of dollars in oil revenue do not have a single serviceable power station is clearly a failed state. A country where nearly all the manufacturing industries have closed for lack of sustainable power supply while Ghana is celebrating 20 years of stable electricity supply is a failed state.
A country where a minister's child would travel abroad to treat malaria is certainly a failed state. A country where the President celebrates regular departure from the country to obtain medical treatment abroad is certainly a failed state. A country where an incapacitated President cannot resign is a failed state. A country with about a hundred and fifty million people that does not have a functioning railway track is a failed state.
A country that has 923,768 square kilometres of landmass, most of it arable that imports food is a failed state. A country with some of the best brains on earth that produces only corrupt politicians is certainly a failed state. A country where godfathers buy up a state and make it their business empire and bring mayhem on the state to assert their control and yet nothing happens to them is a failed.
A country where the criminal elements go with police escorts and the populace are not protected but are left at the mercy of armed gangs is a failed state. A country where policemen and soldiers on duty have to depend on N20 extorted from motorists and motorcyclists to sustain their presence on their beats is a failed state. A country where armed policemen and soldiers run faster than and overtake unarmed civilians when armed gangs attack and never return for counter-attack is a failed state.
A country where $16 billion U.S. can be budgeted for electricity and the whole amount is stolen without as much as a candle to show for it is a failed state.A country where the national assembly committee investigating the power contract scam would be caught stealing millions of rural electricity project fund is a failed state.
A failed state, no matter the physical size, is a banana republic. Nigeria is a failed state; therefore, Nigeria is a banana republic. The enormous potential of Nigeria, however, makes her redeemable.
Let's pray now that we have Goodluck who also has patience - two powerful ingredients- we would be on the march to recovery from our present state of decline, to socio-economic growth, and political stability.

Original_Search_Title: Muzorewa - Cheated Out of History
Headline: Nigeria: Muzorewa - Cheated Out of History
Body_Text:
ABEL TENDEKAYI Muzorewa, retired Methodist Bishop walked away from history 32 years ago when he signed the Salisbury Accord. This ill-advised step consigned him to the dustbin of history. So when he passed away last Thursday, April 8, at 85, not a few might have asked, Muze who?
Yet he had played important roles in the struggle against White minority rule in Zimbabwe (then called Rhodesia). At a point in the liberation struggle when the nationalists were mainly in jail, underground or in exile, Muzorewa, rose to mobilise the populace and give them hope.
From the pulpit, he encouraged them not to despair, not to accept their situation and that apathy was no option. He told the African majority: "Don't sit down and cry, but cry while you're running".
To the Ian Smith White minority regime, the Church was essentially a political organisation, and Muzorewa its political leader who was organising resistance and sabotage from the sanctuary of the Church. But Muzorewa argued that the duty of religious leaders is to serve the people even if the law forbids it.
He told religious leaders that it is part of their Christian duty to oppose unjust laws. He declared: "If religion just means to go to church and pray, then it is a scandal".
Born on April 14, 1925, he was ordained in August 1953 and consecrated Bishop of Rhodesia in the United Methodist Church at Masera, Botswana in 1968.
Two years later, he was banned from entering the Tribal Trust Lands in the country where 75 per cent of his flock lived.
In the early 1970s, Muzorewa and Reverend Canaan Banana established the United African National Council (UANC). He did not see himself as a politician, rather, he believed that racism was ungodly and that it can be brought down by passive resistance.
Many Blacks joined his UANC and his national stature grew. His fall began with the breakdown of the negotiations in Geneva between the minority regime that had declared a Unilateral Declaration of Independence (UDI) from motherland England, and the local and exiled nationalists.
Fearing a bloody end to minority rule, and perhaps acting on the advice of Britain, the Smith regime decided to accede to the primary demand of majority rule based on 'One Man, One Vote'
Muzorewa, took this on the surface. Another leader who accepted this was Ndabanigi Sithole, the veteran guerrilla leader who had returned from exile.
Sithole, a father of nationalism in the country had on January 1, 1960 founded the National Democratic Party (NDP) to which all the main anti-colonial leaders, including Robert Gabriel Mugabe and Joshua Nkomo had belonged. A third signatory, was Jeremiah Chirau, a traditional chief who supported White minority rule.
This threesome and Smith signed the March 3, 1978 eight-point Salisbury Agreements. They provided for a new constitution which included a declaration of human rights, an independent judiciary and Public Service Board and dual citizenship which would enable the Whites retain their British citizenship.
Other agreements included police and security forces being free from political interference, guaranteed pension, 28 of the 100 seats in parliament reserved for Whites and the rest for Blacks, and that the constitution cannot be amended without the consent of the White bloc of parliament.
A Transitional Government made up of the four signatories was established with Smith remaining as Prime Minister while the chairmanship rotated.
There was also a ministerial council of 18 divided equally between the races. Amnesty was granted, and guerrillas wishing to join the security forces being free to do so. The execution of people for political or "terrorist" offences were halted, and 700 out of the official 950 political prisoners were freed.
The two main guerrilla movements, ZANU led by Mugabe and ZAPU the Nkomo -led one, were unbanned. Racial segregation in social arenas, including hotels and theatres were scrapped.
Smith was quite optimistic and said: "Our assessment is that once the agreement is made there will be gradual winding down(of the war) and that in time terrorism will wither away".
Muzorewa could not see that the agreement and the follow up actions were bound to fail because the two main exile-based nationalist movements, ZANU and ZAPU, and the supportive African countries were not party.
Secondly, there was no real transfer of power as the Whites remained in effective control of the armed forces, police, politics and the economy. In some cases, envoys sent out by Muzorewa and other internal leaders to parts of the country to explain the agreements and appeal for ceasefire, were seized and executed as traitors.
When the elections were held, Muzorewa had 67 per cent of the votes, winning 51 of the 72 Black seats, Sithole won 14 per cent and 12 seats, Kayisa Ndiweni from Ndebele won 11 per cent and nine seats while Chirau won no seats.
The British Conservative Party led by Margaret Thatcher said the elections were democratic and acceptable. The United States Senate by a vote of 75-19 declared the elections free and fair, and the dawn of majority rule. But the Jimmy Carter administration, Africa and the rest of the world rejected the elections and Muzorewa's government was not recognised internationally.
An all inclusive Lancaster House Agreements followed, and in the internationally supervised elections that brought true independence to Zimbabwe (on April 17, 1980) Mugabe won 63 per cent, including 57 of the 80 Black seats in parliament, Nkomo 23 per cent and 20 seats, and Muzorewa, the British and White favourite, won eight per cent and three seats.
With this, Muzorewa whose initial struggles can be likened to the heroic roles played in neighbouring South Africa by Bishop Desmond Tutu and the unforgettable Reverend Allan Boesak, faded away. His star dimmed, and the former nationalist hero went down in history mainly as a collaborator of the White minority regime.
In 1985 and 1996 he lost parliamentary elections. He was a simplistic and naive politician who could not decipher when he was being used by the minority White regime. That cost him his place in history.

Original_Search_Title: Ibori Declared Wanted
Headline: Nigeria: Ibori Declared Wanted
Body_Text:
Abuja — Almost five months after the Federal High Court, Asaba, Delta State, cleared him of all charges of corruption, another round of travails may have begun for former Governor James Onanefe Ibori.
Yesterday, the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) declared him wanted over a fresh N44 billion alleged fraud and other corruption charges.
But the former governor, in a swift reaction, said he was never at any time invited by the anti-graft agency.
He vowed to challenge what he called "political persecution" by EFCC.
