In the 2009 elections the BDP increased its popular vote by a paltry 1 per cent despite its massive use and misuse of state resources, an array of populist programmes and the 'Khama magic'. The reality is simply that the BDP has been in power far too long (close to 50 years) and if things were equal, naturally it should have lost elections and regroup in opposition like it happens in western democracies. This point is aptly captured by John Holm and Staffan Darnolf (1999) in their discussion of Botswana politics when they write that 'after a prolonged period of rule most ruling parties lose an election, regardless of their policy successes and probity'. The general marginalization of the opposition by the BDP by denying opposition parties state resources is also a factor.
Furthermore, the option of remaining in the BDP is even more risky if the BNF members can elect in July a new leadership that is forward-looking and credible. It could be dawning on the BNF members that under the current leadership their party has one leg in the grave and is headed for obscurity just like the Botswana Peoples Party (BPP). The BPP steadfastly rejected cooperation with other parties since the mid 1960s and it has paid a terrible price as a result. A different leadership could lead the BNF into a strategic and productive cooperation/partnership with the BCP and this would spell doom for both the Barathaphati and A-Team parliamentarians in the marginal constituencies, which are many. The Barathaphati should be aware that in the affairs of man nothing lasts for ever and the end could be nigh for BDP's incumbency.
Given this scenario, the second option for the Barataphati members is to leave the BDP as individuals and join different opposition parties. However, this option is fraught with uncertainties and difficulties. The option may be just as good as remaining in the BDP and enduring the pain and hopelessness in the party. Chances of the Baraphati being genuinely well received in another party are pretty slim. History of factions in both the BNF and BDP shows that it was precisely because of the influx of new members in those parties that hostile factions emerged to destabilize the parties. In the BNF it started in the mid 1980s when new members (some coming from the BDP) joined the party and tried to help modernize it. The old guard resisted this development and an awful lot of resentment against the newcomers became the order of the day. In the BDP the arrival of the likes of Mompati Merafhe, Festus Mogae and others in the late 1980s and early 1990s led to the A-Team and Barataphati rivalry and its attendant rancour that endures to this minute.
Therefore, Barataphati members joining either the BNF or BCP are likely to arouse bitterness that could stimulate instability in those parties. The Barataphati could be treated like illegal immigrants, and would occasionally be reminded of their BDP background where they were part of the machinery that infiltrated and marginalized the opposition for decades. Moreover, they would be branded opportunists and Johnny-come-latelys whose primary motive is to cream off parliamentary and council seats under the BNF or BCP tickets.
The third and most viable option for the Barataphati is to quit the BDP and form their own party strategically under the leadership of Gomolemo Motswaledi. The party should then form partnership with the opposition, preferably the BCP which seems to be the best led party in the land. When the BCP was formed in 1998, under similar circumstances being faced by the Barathaphi in recent months, it had very limited time to campaign for the 1999 elections. By contrast the Barataphati have all the time in the world to form a party and start preparing for the 2014 elections. Furthermore, unlike the BCP in 1998 when it broke away from the BNF with just the cream of the leadership but without much following, the Barathaphati boasts of a significant number of followers nationwide. The faction may even be having more people than the A-Team even though it is safe to say there is a rough parity between the two factions in this regard. Whereas it looks like the BCP needs ten more years to assume state power without joining forces with the BNF, the Barataphati would need less time to attain power if they decide to throw in their lot with the BCP.
Motswaledi demonstrated the strength of human spirit when he braced himself for 'crucifixion' by advocating equal treatment of members in the BDP, a party he has sacrificed so much for. Therefore, his sacrifice should not be in vain. His 'crucifixion' by the BDP strongmen has unwittingly made him a brand name that the Barataphati should amplify and use to soar to new heights of their political careers. The persecution of Motswaledi has actually made him a martyr to many BDP and non-BDP members. In short the Barataphati have things going for them.
A scenario that should be appreciated by both the BCP and the Barataphati is that in a number of African countries, ruling parties that ruled for ages only bit the dust after a significant number of their members got disillusioned and joined forces with the opposition.
Just on their own the opposition parties remain fragmented and hostile to each other, and ruling parties exploit this to remain in power until the cows come home. Therefore, the BCP should look at the Barataphati in this light.
It seems that God could be communicating with the Barataphati through recent private media revelations that a private multi-national corporation has for three decades being the power behind the throne and calling the shots in the BDP as it bankrolled the party's election campaigns and other undertakings. In this regard the Botswana situation has not been too different from what obtains in numerous African countries where such developments have reduced such countries to banana republics.
Given this situation and its possible consequences for the BDP, this is the right time for the Barataphati to form their own entity and work in alliance with a stable opposition party. It could be now or never for the Barataphati Young Turks. They should strike iron while it is still hot by responding to the 'calling' and grab a glorious opportunity. Otherwise they may live to regret it for the rest of their lives. When all is said and done there is no point in remaining in a party that has discarded the ideals for which they joined it, and which treats them like second class citizens. As the Barataphati have often lamented only one man has absolute control of their party, and those who express dissent and alternative opinion are ruthlessly dealt with in a manner not to be forgotten in a hurry.

Original_Search_Title: Comic Sides of Anambra 2010
Headline: Nigeria: Comic Sides of Anambra 2010
Body_Text:
Awka — The convoy, comprising state-of-the-art sport utility vehicles, spoke much on the status of the man being conveyed to the campaign ground.
Branded vehicles followed the train with pictures of a governorship candidate emblazoned on their sides. As the crowd of his supporters ran after him, the convoy made a detour to a traditional ruler's palace and every activity in the area would stand still. In apparent show of respect, other road users stopped and waved their hands to the august visitors.
The candidate would come down from one of the black jeeps along with his Director General of the Campaign Organisation and walk into the warm embrace of their host.
The meeting would not last more than ten minutes and the vehicles would pull out and head for the rally venue. The crowd would chant solidarity songs and tell the governorship candidate that "you are there, in fact it is unopposed, take us to government House Awka, in short go and prepare for your swearing-in in March 2010."
Before the candidate would make his speech, a solo speaking voice with a very high pitch would erupt and begin to sing those ballads that paint eulogising pictures of him. Down there among the crowd, self-acclaimed ward and local government coordinators were already drawing lists of members present at the occasion and running among the crowd of people in search of the "Director for Logistics."
As the guests took their leave, the crowd would go after them with the commercial motorcycle operators (Okada) displaying all they knew best in cycling. Of course, all the walls in the local government area were adorned with the colourful posters of the candidate, selling the impression that there was no other candidate known to the people besides the current guest.
Ironically, when the entire dust of the candidate's visit had settled, trouble immediately ensued. The candidate announced N500,000 for the party members and N1million for the rest of the crowd. But the self-acclaimed coordinators were telling a different story.
"No, I heard His Excellency say N1.500 million and not N750,000 for everybody. You better produce our money or nobody goes home with his arms and legs intact," one of the henchmen who felt shortchanged threatened.
But his opponent was not bothered. "You can go ahead and do whatever you like after all what stakes do you have in the campaign organisation? I told you it is N750,000 and nothing more. You know these people, they will announce millions before the crowd and when they are about to go they will give you what is in their minds," he maintained.
When the argument became stronger and tempers rising, the party chairman and campaign coordinator would call for a brief meeting in the office inviting few party stalwarts. Shortly after the meeting the man demanding full disclosure of the sum would come out, take some supporters and call the names of party members and at the end, a large number of party members would be announced as none members and that ended it.
Elsewhere, a member of a campaign organisation had been at the campaign office in Awka, waiting patiently for stakeholders meeting. He had been there for about four hours and not even an okada man dropped off a member, let alone a major stakeholder.
Angrily, he went home only to receive a phone call from a fellow member that the meeting was held in a hotel and some people that the Director General did not like the size of their nose were not allowed to attend.
Deepening the frustration of the man was the fact that motorcycles, cloths and face caps were distributed along with very fat envelopes. The man got angry and held a meeting with the affected people. The outcome of the meeting was that the posters given to them to paste and the billboards had finished and there was urgent need to print more. The group would by-pass the Director General talk terms with the Director of Publicity, the governorship candidate and over N10million would be released for printing and mounting of more billboards and posters. One week later, the group would go to their homes and bring out the posters and billboards hidden for two weeks and begin to paste them. Then four persons including the Director of Publicity would share the whooping sum of N10 million among themselves.
Away from the in-fighting in the respective political parties and the campaign organisations, the crowd of supporters, loyal and rented, are also part of this comic drama call the Anambra election campaigns.
It has become common to see a particular crowd appearing in every campaign rally held by candidates. Sometimes the same prayer singer that sang for one candidate would sing for another candidate.
In fact, a particular retired school principal was seen in the house of a governorship candidate and for more than two hours he was running down on another candidate and celebrating what he called the high qualities of the man he was in his house.
Of course he went home happy and accomplished, only to be seen that evening in the house of the same candidate he had earlier vilified.
This reporter also met him there and asked him what he had come to do. Expectedly, he quickly denied going to the first candidate's house and even denied his name. When he saw this reporter chatting with the candidate, he vanished within seconds in his old 504-saloon car.
But the 25 governorship candidates are not concerned by the day light deceits of their so-called supporters. The candidates have been neck deep in selling their manifestoes to the electorate in a most humorous manner. More interesting is the fact that the candidates have jettisoned the age long electoral violence that leaves sorrow, tears and blood as the regular trademark.
A look at their manifestoes and how they analyse them paints a picture of hilarious expository of politicking. Dr Chris Ngige of the Action Congress (AC), for instance, claims to have constructed more roads than any other governor and most of those infrastructures are his major trump card.
But Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) governorship candidate, Professor Chukwuma Soludo, would trivialise Ngige's achievement as pedestrian.
"My 'African Dubai Taiwan' is not what would be achieved with the meager state allocations from the Federal Allocations Commission (FAC) or with the pieces of revenue from Onitsha and Nnewi business commercial and industrial towns. This is not a question of building roads and making noise about it. We are talking about the amazing future of Anambra State and the hope that I see." Ngige would not wait for him to come down before retorting, "This Dubai Taiwan thing is funny. The man should make Anambra look like Asaba or Lagos before Dubai or Taiwan. Besides, Dubai has economic crisis and they owe a lot. Some consortiums of businessmen and conglomerates have come in to help Dubai. So somebody should tell our brother that he is dreaming. We still need these roads, electricity and other basic infrastructures to attract foreign donors. They do not give you anything for nothing."
Soludo would not let go. He would present his intimidating credentials of a first class degree holder, a professor, former economic adviser and former Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) governor. But Ngige would contend that one does not need to have a degree in econometrics to be a good administrator, adding that the minimum qualification is WAEC certificate attempted.
Governor Peter Obi must have the best advertisement copywriters compared to other candidates. Even before the election period, his billboards told the story more. In this election, tagged Change We Believe In, which obviously is against Iyom Uche Ekwunife of the Progressive Peoples Alliance (PPA) that christened hers as The Change We Need. Obi's billboards would present pictures of laughing children and a mother breastfeeding a child. He would also showcase a billboard of a prototype building complex standing beside an uncompleted project and it would read, "What Mr. Peter Obi is doing."
While the spaces for the billboards appeared to have been covered, the governor resorted to hanging small side billboards on stands of streetlights. Some of such billboards apparently cut the picture of Obi's as staff of one of the oil companies in Nigeria, as a yellow helmet is superimposed on his picture.
Ekwunife is a rare breed politician. She is a woman with the heart of a man. Iyom, as she is called, has indeed given Anambra women a new face in politics.
Dressed to her best, Ekwunife would visit stalls in every market in Anambra sit with the traders asking for their votes. Ekwunife's campaign, aside from being hinged on youth empowerment and development of rural communities, paints a picture of mother and child brand of politics. At a rally in Nnewi, Ekwunife exhibited motherhood. "It is only a mother that understands the plights of her children. I am not saying that the fathers do not have any role to play, but the mother is closer to the children and reads the lips and minds of her children better than the fathers. So I urge you all to vote for a mother because mama gives when hunger strikes. "I represent the symbol of motherhood and that is what it is. I have never failed you before and I cannot afford to fail you now that it matters most. Anambra has seen a lot of trouble and it takes a woman, a mother to restore peace and love in Anambra.
"Take a look at a man that lost his wife and you can see how devastated he can be, let the mother step into the government house and restore peace into her family," she said.
Soludo with his deep baritone voice cuts the picture of a schoolteacher addressing his students. He has a constant song that has become a common feature in most of his campaign rallies.
Soludo would mount the soapbox, put his tall red cap to shape and chant the first verse of his song: "Anambra has good luck, God loves us all." The song would later electrify the arena and the women on uniform would snatch the microphone from the master of ceremony and the chorus would linger for minutes.
While addressing the crowd of supporters, he would draw his running mate, Senator Emma Anosike, closer, embrace him, raise his hands and introduce him as a perfect match, apparently to put paid to insinuations that they had been at daggers-drawn.
He would not fail to tell the world his achievements as a CBN Governor, interjecting it with his policies on the banking sector that led to the recapitalisation of the commercial banks.
Soludo is not alone in this soapbox rendition of songs. Dr. Andy Uba of the Labour Party (LP) is equally a singer of songs. Uba's song is that of supplications to God. "God in heaven you saved me when my spirit is in trouble. I give you thanks. I come to you with good thanks. I come to you with good praise. I shall sing the spiritual song to show that you are great. Forgive me my sins oh God, so that I shall benefit from your grace. Remember your promised to carry this cross. You promised that you would be my own. So I am before you to partake in your blessing," he would chant. After the song, Uba would present a tractor and some motorcycles to the people.
Obi is not lost in the religious trend. Though he does not have any religious song attached to his campaign rallies, his executive council members and party faithful do have their own song. "He is Peter the rock, the rock of Anambra state, the rock of continuity," they chorus. The governor also hinges his campaign on the town union leaders who he believes would galvanize support for him at all times. He would attend morning masses, flaunt his chaplet to all who care to see, that he is close to God. He would take pictures with Reverend Fathers and Bishops and ensure they received wide publicity. He also has Dim Chukwuemeka Odumegwu Ojukwu as his joker. One of his billboards has Ojukwu raising his hands. But the advertisement copywriters are seen by observers as making a fundamental mistake by presenting what they called Ojukwu's last wish that Anambra should vote for Obi. "Is Ojukwu dying today? Are they telling us that the man is ready to die and he is now making his last wish," they asked.
Even on the campaign dress of Governor Peter Obi is the picture of Ojukwu and the governor. Jokingly, Ngige had asked Obi if Ojukwu is his running mate or he (Peter) is Ojukwu's running mate.
Ngige's jokes and comics cut the picture of the first and second republic politicians. Some claimed that he might have understudied the flamboyancy of the Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe, Chief Obafemi Awolowo, Dr. Michael Okpara and Dr. Sam Mbakwe. Ngige has a penchant for drawing the crowd of his fellow contestants to himself. Instances abound of rallies of some candidates taking place in a town and he (Ngige) was to attend another rally of the Action Congress (AC). When he got to the boundary of the local government area the electorate would wave their brooms, a symbol of the Action. He would open the door of his jeep, step out and begin to shake the hands of the crowd. He would walk up to an old woman selling banana and groundnut, buy some and share some among the crowd. As the chanting of Onwaa rented the air, his supporters would run after him and for about an hour there would be traffic jam.
Ngige, however, does not have any song in particular that he sings during his campaign rallies. Rather, the crowd would sing the songs and he would chorus and then try some few dance steps.
However, a common song that the crowd sings is "it is your good work that you will be remembered for. It is your good work to the people that matters and not silver and gold. Your good work would be a blessing to you."
After the rally, a Catholic Priest, Anglican Pastor or an Evangelist of the Pentecostal Church would say the closing prayers and bless the crowd.
Architect Mike Nwafor of the African Liberation Party (ALP) is a born-again Christian. He sees the 2010 in Anambra as a year of jubilee. Nwafor is a good preacher and has always urged Anambra people to seek the face of God in the 2010 election. "Anambra has this year as a year of jubilation, God is visiting Anambra State this year and we must prepare ourselves to receiving him. God has a purpose for our state and he is using me as an instrument for that purpose," he said.
Another Evangelist is Prince Nicholas Ukachukwu of the Hope Democratic Party (HDP). Ukachukwu has held several crusades and Christian rallies with notable leaders of the Pentecostal churches. Naturally his campaign rallies are more like religious gatherings blended with politics and his crowd of supporters would watch with rapped attention as he carries out what one of his supporters described as "political evangelisation."
Ichie Mike Ejezie of the All Nigerian Peoples Party (ANPP) is a businessman and sees Anambra State as a large market that needs to be developed. "If we build Anambra State, our people would be empowered. This over-dependency on monthly allocation is not good, but we have all it takes to transform Anambra State," he preached. Ejezie has a lot of businessmen that believe in his ideas and he has always held think-thanks with them on his election campaigns.
While these candidates take Aambra State by the storm, the roadside tailors are having a field day. The expected lull in their shops after Christmas and New Year seasons has not been witnessed due to the campaign dresses worn by party members and supporters.
Tailors exhibit the campaign dresses of the governorship candidates. Similarly, the artists and printers have been smiling to the banks as candidates continue to print T-shirts, faze caps, billboards and banners.
Most outstanding, however, is that bookmakers have been proved wrong on the apprehension that 2010 election in Anambra would be bloody. So far, there has not been that picture of bloodshed or supporters of candidates clashing at rallies or by the roads. They are ironically seen at drinking bars exchanging pleasantries and sharing experiences.
Even the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) chairman, Prof. Maurice Iwu, in a meeting in Awka congratulated the political parties and their candidates on such display of sportsmanship. Many, however, hope that the remaining days to the election would not be days of long knives.

Original_Search_Title: Practical Realities and Options for Barataphati
Headline: Botswana: Practical Realities and Options for Barataphati
Body_Text:
We take it that by now the psychological trauma suffered by the Young Turks in the Barataphati faction of the ruling Botswana Democratic Party (BDP) towards the end of last year has subsided.
Perhaps it is now time to be charting the way forward for the faction that was cruelly maneuvered into a tight corner after it registered a hard-won victory at the controversial Kanye congress in July. We start this piece by looking at how young members of the BDP advancing progressive thoughts have been (mis)handled in a bid to silence them during the presidency of the party's four leaders: Sir Seretse Khama, Sir Ketumile Masire, Festus Mogae and now Ian Khama. Thereafter, we provide three options and their viability for the Barataphati to ponder in order to salvage their political careers. The first option being to remain in the party and hope that things change for the better. The second option is to quit the BDP as individuals and join existing opposition parties. The final option is for the Barataphati to turn their backs on the BDP as a block, form their own party and enter into an alliance with one of the established opposition parties.
Throughout history of the BDP positions of money and influence in the party and government have always been held by a tiny clique whose members are carefully selected and tightly controlled. Progressive ideas by youthful members of the party meant to modernize it and widen its internal democratic space have always been dismissed by the leadership. The plight of the Barataphati Young Turks can be understood in the context of this BDP's strong tradition which is traceable to the 1970s.
Former BDP member of parliament and cabinet minister, David Magang writes in his memoirs The Magic of Perseverance that in 1975 as a youthful member of the party he 'approached President [Sir Seretse] Khama and tabled the proposal that a BDP youth wing be established. As with most novel ideas, my proposal was rejected. The party veterans, more so MPs, were not in the least bit keen on the idea of a youth wing. They did not advance concrete reasons for their stance, but these were discernable from their mutterings and body language. It was clear that they were suspicious of their own shadows; they feared that a junior movement, packed with fresh-blooded party cadres who were armed with a good education would seriously curtail their hold and influence on the party.... As the idea's progenitor, I became famously unpopular with the old guard and was marked out as a rabble-rouser for my otherwise progressive thoughts' (Magang, 2008).
An example during Masire's leadership of the BDP is that of Kabo Morwaeng, a member of the party's youth wing in 1993. In that year members of the party's youth wing backed Morwaeng to express their opinion that the president of the party and the republic should serve for a limited period of two terms. The response from the party's old guard was swift and harsh. Morwaeng was summoned to a disciplinary hearing and tongue lashed.
Members of the youth wing who had sponsored Morwaneng's idea ditched him and he faced the music alone. In addition to that Morwaeng was disqualified from running for a parliamentary seat in a bye-election in Kweneng District. Although he had triumphed in the primary elections the party decided that Gladys Kokorwe, who had come distant third, represent the party under the pretext of women empowerment. So heartbroken was Morwaeng that soon he quit the BDP and joined the opposition Botswana National Front (BNF).
Early in Mogae's presidency Lesang Magang, as chairman of the youth wing openly condemned the granting of the then vice president Ian Khama unprecedented one year 'sabbatical' leave by Mogae. He also called for Khama's resignation. Just like Morwaeng members of the youth wing who had egged Lesang Magang on from behind the scenes left him in the lurch and pledged their loyalty to Mogae and Khama. Lesang Magang was also reported to have openly declared his ambition to become president of the BDP and Botswana some day. This is totally unacceptable in the BDP. Lesang Magang became a marked man and party veterans fought tooth and nail to ensure that he never gained the youth wing chairmanship again.
Although Ian Khama has been president of the BDP for less than two years the Barataphathi Young Turks have suffered harsher and more painful punishment under him more than any of his predecessors combined. Firstly, as indicated above the Barataphati's hard-won victory at the Kanye congress in July 2009 was soon snatched away from them. Khama also appointed his loyalists to numerous party committees without consulting the Barataphathi faction, and effectively neutralised them. The newly elected party's youthful secretary general Gomolemo Motswaledi after losing a historical court case against Khama was suspended from his party position. Previously, he had been barred from contesting national elections in the Gaborone Central Constituency where he represented the BDP. Initially, he had been forced to forget about contesting elections in a safe constituency in his hometown, Serowe.
Another Young Turk, Botsalo Ntuane had also been punished by not being allowed to contest in his home constituency of Nata/Gweta and was forced to contest in a difficult Gaborone constituency. However, Ntuane did win in Gaborone after putting up a spirited campaign against both opposition parties and his BDP. Ill-advised decisions by the leadership of the opposition BNF was also a decisive factor here. Interestingly, during Motswaledi's persecution his fellow Young Turks publicly and vocally stood by him, while faction elders ditched him and declared their loyalty to the party president. This persecution is believed to have been based on trumped up charges.
The Barataphathi Young Turks still live in real fear and danger of being suspended or expelled from their beloved party. They are always looking over their shoulders and anything can happen any time. During the build-up to the Kanye congress the Barathaphathi had courageously and convincingly presented themselves as constitutionalists and defenders of democratic principles. For this they have been treated like terrorists within the party and dealt with accordingly. On the other hand their rival faction, the A-Team came across as consisting of people committed to sycophantic loyalty and whose primary interest was political survival on the cream of Khama's patronage. For this the A-Team are treated like BDP aristocrats by the party leader.
As things currently stand in the BDP the Barataphati could stay put in the party for sentimental reasons and hope that a miracle changes things. As far as we are concerned, this option is the least viable. Recent developments and the Barataphati tragedy show that it is impossible and risky to try and reform the party from within. Tradition in the party dictates against such a move. Remaining in the party also has the added disadvantage of some members of the faction lured with promises of the much craved cabinet positions in order to betray their faction colleagues only to be dumped after being used. A very good example was during the election of four specially elected members of parliament when some Barataphati members were duped into endorsing candidates from the A-Team faction against candidates from the Barataphati faction.
Promises of cabinet positions were used as a carrot that never was. It is also said that one member of the Barathaphati was given a ride on the presidential jet and that was enough to make him part ways with the faction in the critical and strategic election for the secretary general of the party following Motswaledi's five year suspension. Divide and rule tactics are used to weaken the Barataphati by playing some of the faction members off against their comrades. Moreover, the A-Team will continue fighting tooth and nail to ensure the continued marginalisation and frustration of the Barataphati. Therefore, remaining in the party is a sure way for continued anxiety and misery for the Barataphati.
The only way that they can reform the BDP from inside is by campaigning for the president to be elected through the ballot box. This way the Barataphati may be able to push their candidate into the presidency of the party. However, such a move is likely to backfire because the A-Team and 'non-aligned' BDP members would argue that it is aimed at usurping the presidency from Ian Khama, Sir Seretse Khama's son. It should be remembered that Seretse's legacy is the heart and soul of the BDP and it is being kept alive through Ian Khama's presidency of the party and the republic.
The Barataphati should also be informed that since the 1990s the BDP has been winning elections primarily because of the disorganization and disorientation of the BNF. Even Masire in his book Very Brave or Very Foolish does not attribute the BDP's victory in 1999 to Ian Khama but to the split in the BNF that led to formation of the Botswana Congress Party (BCP) in 1998. In the 2009 elections the BDP increased its popular vote by a paltry 1 per cent despite its massive use and misuse of state resources, an array of populist programmes and the 'Khama magic'. The reality is simply that the BDP has been in power far too long (close to 50 years) and if things were equal, naturally it should have lost elections and regroup in opposition like it happens in western democracies. This point is aptly captured by John Holm and Staffan Darnolf (1999) in their discussion of Botswana politics when they write that 'after a prolonged period of rule most ruling parties lose an election, regardless of their policy successes and probity'. The general marginalization of the opposition by the BDP by denying opposition parties state resources is also a factor.
Furthermore, the option of remaining in the BDP is even more risky if the BNF members can elect in July a new leadership that is forward-looking and credible. It could be dawning on the BNF members that under the current leadership their party has one leg in the grave and is headed for obscurity just like the Botswana Peoples Party (BPP). The BPP steadfastly rejected cooperation with other parties since the mid 1960s and it has paid a terrible price as a result. A different leadership could lead the BNF into a strategic and productive cooperation/partnership with the BCP and this would spell doom for both the Barathaphati and A-Team parliamentarians in the marginal constituencies, which are many. The Barathaphati should be aware that in the affairs of man nothing lasts for ever and the end could be nigh for BDP's incumbency.
Given this scenario, the second option for the Barataphati members is to leave the BDP as individuals and join different opposition parties. However, this option is fraught with uncertainties and difficulties. The option may be just as good as remaining in the BDP and enduring the pain and hopelessness in the party. Chances of the Baraphati being genuinely well received in another party are pretty slim. History of factions in both the BNF and BDP shows that it was precisely because of the influx of new members in those parties that hostile factions emerged to destabilize the parties. In the BNF it started in the mid 1980s when new members (some coming from the BDP) joined the party and tried to help modernize it. The old guard resisted this development and an awful lot of resentment against the newcomers became the order of the day. In the BDP the arrival of the likes of Mompati Merafhe, Festus Mogae and others in the late 1980s and early 1990s led to the A-Team and Barataphati rivalry and its attendant rancour that endures to this minute.
Therefore, Barataphati members joining either the BNF or BCP are likely to arouse bitterness that could stimulate instability in those parties. The Barataphati could be treated like illegal immigrants, and would occasionally be reminded of their BDP background where they were part of the machinery that infiltrated and marginalized the opposition for decades. Moreover, they would be branded opportunists and Johnny-come-latelys whose primary motive is to cream off parliamentary and council seats under the BNF or BCP tickets.
The third and most viable option for the Barataphati is to quit the BDP and form their own party strategically under the leadership of Gomolemo Motswaledi. The party should then form partnership with the opposition, preferably the BCP which seems to be the best led party in the land. When the BCP was formed in 1998, under similar circumstances being faced by the Barathaphi in recent months, it had very limited time to campaign for the 1999 elections. By contrast the Barataphati have all the time in the world to form a party and start preparing for the 2014 elections. Furthermore, unlike the BCP in 1998 when it broke away from the BNF with just the cream of the leadership but without much following, the Barathaphati boasts of a significant number of followers nationwide. The faction may even be having more people than the A-Team even though it is safe to say there is a rough parity between the two factions in this regard. Whereas it looks like the BCP needs ten more years to assume state power without joining forces with the BNF, the Barataphati would need less time to attain power if they decide to throw in their lot with the BCP.
Motswaledi demonstrated the strength of human spirit when he braced himself for 'crucifixion' by advocating equal treatment of members in the BDP, a party he has sacrificed so much for. Therefore, his sacrifice should not be in vain. His 'crucifixion' by the BDP strongmen has unwittingly made him a brand name that the Barataphati should amplify and use to soar to new heights of their political careers. The persecution of Motswaledi has actually made him a martyr to many BDP and non-BDP members. In short the Barataphati have things going for them.
A scenario that should be appreciated by both the BCP and the Barataphati is that in a number of African countries, ruling parties that ruled for ages only bit the dust after a significant number of their members got disillusioned and joined forces with the opposition.
Just on their own the opposition parties remain fragmented and hostile to each other, and ruling parties exploit this to remain in power until the cows come home. Therefore, the BCP should look at the Barataphati in this light.
It seems that God could be communicating with the Barataphati through recent private media revelations that a private multi-national corporation has for three decades being the power behind the throne and calling the shots in the BDP as it bankrolled the party's election campaigns and other undertakings. In this regard the Botswana situation has not been too different from what obtains in numerous African countries where such developments have reduced such countries to banana republics.
Given this situation and its possible consequences for the BDP, this is the right time for the Barataphati to form their own entity and work in alliance with a stable opposition party. It could be now or never for the Barataphati Young Turks. They should strike iron while it is still hot by responding to the 'calling' and grab a glorious opportunity. Otherwise they may live to regret it for the rest of their lives. When all is said and done there is no point in remaining in a party that has discarded the ideals for which they joined it, and which treats them like second class citizens. As the Barataphati have often lamented only one man has absolute control of their party, and those who express dissent and alternative opinion are ruthlessly dealt with in a manner not to be forgotten in a hurry.

Original_Search_Title: Handicrafts, Economic Lifewire
Headline: Cameroon: Handicrafts, Economic Lifewire
Body_Text:
The sector groups people of all walks of life and products of all sorts.
Processing natural resources, some of which could be considered by society as waste, to come out with useful commodities, is fast flourishing in the country and could contribute greatly to economic development if the craftsmen are detected, groomed and supported. In Cameroon, no region is left out and the originality of what is produced as well as the calibre of people involved ignites attention.
Wood carving, weaving, dyeing, sculpture making, among others, constitute some of the creative arts of Cameroonians. Unlike some office work and others that may require a certain degree of literacy, crafts work is handled by almost everybody, literate or not. The just-ended Cameroon International Handicrafts Exhibition which held in Yaounde served as a mirror to the country's handicraft potentials. That a citizen could process waste plastic paper to get roofing and pavement tiles, snails could be processed for export and that clothes could be produced from banana stems, are some of the craft works in Cameroon. Over 500 craftsmen from the entire country were in Yaounde to demonstrate their ingenuity. This number was selected from thousands right from the divisional to regional levels, meaning a good number of the country's population have at least a handwork to help them make ends meet if their ingenuity is supported.
That the sector is manned by people of all walks of life and from all parts of the country could be an opportunity to promote the small holders engaged in the activity to curb the disturbing unemployment rate in the country. Promoting the people who have excelled would permit them to emerge and in so doing, creating employment opportunities and reducing the near total dependence on government for scarce jobs. This would also serve as an opportunity for the citizens to maximise the use of the enormous natural resources the country is endowed with and could again catapult the country from an importer to exporter. The number of people doing business in Cameroon with Nigerian-made dresses and how such goods are consumed here are proof that if they were produced locally, citizens could buy at cheaper rates and the country could benefit from foreign earnings through their exportation. However, this is not the case as those who produce with their limited means count only on sales for continuity, thus rendering most home-made goods dearer than imported ones.
To achieve this goal, supporting the crafts people and inculcating the culture of consuming home-made goods in citizens should be carried out simultaneously. Exhibitors at the handicrafts exhibition complained of a low-status recognition given to home-made goods by citizens. There are people who still feel that something made in Cameroon is synonymous with inferiority.

Original_Search_Title: CAF's Handling of Togo Amounts to Abuse of a Victim
Headline: South Africa/Togo: CAF's Handling of Togo Amounts to Abuse of a Victim
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — GIVEN the sheer chaos and the astronomical incompetence at the Confederation of African Football (Caf), we have always imagined that even a team of monkeys would run the old dinosaur far more professionally than the bunch of dunderheads at present in charge of this clumsy mess.
But on closer inspection, it seems the comparison might have been a huge insult to the furry primates.
Clueless Caf president Issa Hayatou and his mob of yes-men have managed to reduce the continental body to such a chaotic shambles that it seems operational strategies are simply made up as they go along.
From where I'm standing, every day seems to be an adventure, and surviving a normal day at the dysfunctional organisation's Cairo headquarters must be more of a death-defying experience than bungee jumping.
Caf was at it again when the perennial mouth-breathers announced this week that Togo had been suspended from the next two editions of the African Nations Cup. And what exactly was the reason for this harsh punishment?
Those bright sparks at Caf decided that they wanted to teach Togo a lesson after the west Africans pulled out of the Angola-held tournament last month following the terrorist attack that left two people dead and several others injured.
The old dinosaur's chief press officer, Suleiman Habuba - who must arguably be the least accessible media official in the world - said the decision was based on article 78 of Caf regulations, which says: "A forfeit notified less than 20 days before the start or during the final competition shall entail, in addition of the forfeit of the entry fee, a maximum fine stipulated by the regulations as well as the suspension of the concerned national association for the following two editions of the African Cup of Nations."
Hayatou further said that the decision was taken in light of the fact that the Togo players actually wanted to continue playing but were ordered back home by their government.
According to the perennially bungling big enchilada, the Togo government's actions were considered interference, and the sleepy heads in Cairo frown on governmental interference.
But wait, folks, this muddled mess gets better. Our man Hayatou apparently quietly sneaked over to the Togolese in the aftermath of the attack and told coach Hubert Velud that they needn't worry about sanctions for withdrawing from the tournament. "He confirmed to us, but I was wary about it all the same, that Togo would not be suspended for the following Africa Cup of Nations," Velud said.
What kind of Mickey Mouse operation is this? It's quite clear that Hayatou and his cronies are running a regular banana republic that is continuing to embarrass the continent.
Togo captain Emmanuel Adebayor is understandably livid and wants Hayatou to quit.
But we all know that the old relic will never quit because he considers Caf his old-age pastime. The Cameroonian has been in charge of this joke of an organisation for the past 23 years and yet the continent has very little to show for it.
Even psychiatric patients would be horrified by the craziness of this latest decision by Caf and it boggles the mind that Togo are being punished for an attack that claimed the lives of their team members.
While the Togolese say they will appeal against the decision, you can't help but feel this is a classic case of a victim being insensitively humiliated a second time by the very people who are supposed to be offering solace.
They still have scars from the traumatic attack and yet Caf seemingly couldn't give a flying toss. Unbelievable!
Small wonder then that there are still people around the world who do not take this continent seriously.
Ntloko is sports editor.

Original_Search_Title: Iman and Oluchi to Judge M-Net Face of Africa 2010 Finale
Headline: Africa: Iman and Oluchi to Judge M-Net Face of Africa 2010 Finale
Body_Text:
Iman, one of the world's premier supermodels, will be on the judges' panel to select the M-Net Face of Africa 2010 at the glittering live finale this Saturday.
She will share the enviable task of selecting Africa's next catwalk superstar with a team of experienced judges from across the continent, including inaugural Face of Africa winner Oluchi.
Iman began her career in 1975 as a model. The daughter of an African diplomat, Iman was born in Somalia and grew up speaking five languages. She studied political science at Nairobi University where she was discovered by legendary photographer Peter Beard.She is muse to fashion designers including YVES. St. Laurent, Versace, Calvin Klein and Donna Karan. During her 14 years as a model, she worked with top photographers Helmut Newton, Richard Avedon, Irving Penn and Annie Liebovitz.
Iman is founder and CEO of IMAN Cosmetics, Skincare and Fragrances, a beauty company that created the first cosmetics and skincare collection designed for women of all skin colours. IMAN Cosmetics, Skincare and Fragrances are sold throughout the world including the United States, Canada, United Kingdom (UK), France, Brazil, Africa and Caribbean countries. In addition to running a global beauty company, Iman is actively involved in several charities, including The Childrens Defence Fund, For all Kids Foundation, and Action Against Hunger.
Being named as M-Net's inaugural Face of Africa established Oluchi as one of the most sought after faces in the business and she remains one of the busiest New York-based female models in fashion today. Within a year of winning Face of Africa, Oluchi achieved what few African models before her had done - she moved to New York and made an immediate connection with the world of art and beauty.
Renowned photographer Steven Meisel captured her image for powerhouse publications Vogue and Elle and before long, the young Nigerian beauty was gracing international catwalks for the likes of John Galliano, Ralph Lauren and Giorgio Armani.
Her current portfolio includes work for Clinique and L'Oréal make-up, Victoria's Secret, H&M, Lancome, Cadillac, Macy's, Gianfranco Ferre, Bergdorf Goodman, Banana Republic and Gap, to name but a few.Oluchi is an inspiration to many women in Africa and across the globe, a fashion icon, a mother to 2-year- old Ugo, wife to top New York-based designer Luca Orlandi and Director of her own model agency, O Model Africa.
Other judges will include Josie Borain, Megan Kruger, John Kaveke, David Tlale, Nduka Obaigbena, and Helen Jennings. Botswana's Kaone Kario, will share the stage with Kenya's Fareed Khimani as presenters of the live finale. The finale will be broadcast on DStv's M-Net channel at 19:00 CAT.

Original_Search_Title: Nnamani Takes a Stand
Headline: Nigeria: Nnamani Takes a Stand
Body_Text:
Lagos — There is sense in the view of informed analysts that whenever the history of efforts at enthroning enduring democracy in the contemporary Nigerian polity is written, former Senate President, Ken Nnamani, would receive a favourable mention. Incidentally, Nnamani was not known to many Nigerians until his emergence as president of the Senate.
Even at that, his emergence hardly had much to do with his personality or qualifications for the job, as much as it did with the situation that confronted him and the nation at the time. Nnamani admitted as much, when he thanked his senate colleagues "for breaking tradition to make me Senate President and for defying all odds to accord me the greatest possible support and friendship," in his acceptance speech at the red chamber of the legislature.
Apparently conscious of the circumstances behind his emergence to the hot seat, Nnamani had reasons to contribute his quota to the growth and development of participatory democracy in the country. His unprecedented courage in handling former President Olusegun Obasanjo's tenure elongation gambit, readily counted for him in this regards. Since that heroic outing, Nnamani has always been accorded red carpet reception at local and international forums. His voice has equally remained weighty in contemporary issues.
It was, perhaps, in that regard that the former Senate President on Friday, January 29, saw reasons to comment on the perceived vacuum in the presidency when he was quoted to have criticised the current Senate's stance on the confusion in the presidency. He described the senate's call on President Umaru Yar'Adua to transmit a letter to it as demanded by the Constitution as belated even as he condemned the Attorney General of the Federation, Michael Aondoakaa's declaration that Yar'Adua is fit without medical evidence.
He said: "We pray President Yar'Adua recovers soon and returns to Nigeria to the embrace of family, friends and compatriots. But obviously, he cannot reasonably continue to act as President. The nation should move on and move away from disaster. Emotions and sentiments have their values in other circumstances but not in the present crisis."
Nnamani went on to suggest to the National Assembly to invoke Section 144 of the Constitution and make the Vice President act pending when the President returns back to work. According to him, "The Constitution has provided sufficient guidance on what should be done when unexpectedly an elected President can no longer continue to exercise executive power as President and Commander-in-Chief of the Federal Republic of Nigeria. That guidance in Section 144 of the Constitution requires that the Federal Executive Council, the body with the greatest proximity to the President, should declare him incapable of exercising executive power so that he could be replaced by the Vice President."
A Lagos-based constitutional lawyer and rights activist, Fred Agbaje, tends to agree with Nnamani when he argued that FEC's declaration on Yar'Adua is nothing but deceit to the Nigerian people. To him, the ministers who constitute the FEC are only playing the bid of their pay master and are only afraid of being relieved of their posts by the President. "As far as I know it, the ministers are Yar'Adua's boot lickers and they should in their own interest put Nigerians first in all they do," Agbaje said.
In a regime of five officials from the South East who held that position from 1999 to 2007, Nnamani, incidentally was among the only two who survived the so-called banana pills, a euphemism for intrigues that saw to the controversial ouster of others. The other survivor was Pius Anyim.
Nnamani, who was born on November 2, 1948 in Enugu, Enugu State studied at the University of Ohio, United State of America where he obtained a Master of Business Administration. He was elected to the senate for Enugu East in 2003. Apart from being the Chairman of the Committee on Federal Character & Governmental Affairs and member of committees on Privatisation, Federal Capital Territory and Appropriation & Finance, Nnamani made it to the top of the legislative arm of government when he took over the reigns of the senate from compatriot, Adolphus Wabara, who resigned due to allegations of corruption.

Original_Search_Title: Opposition Will Continue to Challenge Oni's Lies - Awe
Headline: Nigeria: Opposition Will Continue to Challenge Oni's Lies - Awe
Body_Text:
Lagos — JIDE AWE is the Chairman of Action Congress (AC), in Ekiti State. He recently bagged the prestigious award of the Nigeria Union of Journalists (NUJ), as most consistent politician of the year 2009. in this interview with CORRESPONDENT, YAQOUB POPOOLA, he advances his views on some burning local and national issues.
Excerpts:
The opposition element are mooting the idea of formation of a mega party as one of the ways to check the excesses of the ruling People's Democratic Party (PDP) in the forthcoming election. Do you see anything tangible coming out this?
Well, in the recent past, even as far 1981, 82, 83 during the era of the National Party of Nigeria (NPN) there was this idea of a mega party or multiple parties formation to counter the lawlessness and misrule of that time. But it could not see the light of the day. There is a much orchestrated idea now to form a mega party. If you look at the past and the present, the idea of formation of a mega party was borne out of the non-performance of the ruling party. It is part of the agitation of the democratic forces to wrestle power from the ruling party. But it has always been a failure; failure in the sense that the actors or the movers of that particular idea have not been consistent. The ruling has always been infiltrating their ranks and files. And as a result of this, mega party has not seen the light of the day. And the simple reason is that our politics in Nigeria has not grown from the level of bread and butter. Immediately some of the movers are taken care of, they forget about forming fordable alliance to counter the ruling party. So, as far as I am concerned and my party, AC, we are not opposed to the formation of a mega party if the players are serious. But we remain solidly as AC and we are confronting the misrule of the People's Democratic Party (PDP) head-on, not only in Ekiti State but in Nigeria at large. Because we have realised that the idea of mega party or multiple parties coming to form alliance is more of mouth than action.
But, what can you say about those who pretend to be progressives after they have lost out in the power game in the ruling party?
Like I said earlier, our democracy is still within the rank and file of bread and butter. People who are supposed to be of solid ground to form a formidable party right from the onset but because of this bandwagon issue they are now queuing up behind the ruling party and immediately they realise that they have lost out in the game of power they tend to retrace their steps back to the opposition and claim to be progressives. In the past they used to run to the military that the situation is bad and so on for them to come in, but now the military is no longer fashionable. The forces of democracy have overshadowed that of the military. And which is why the soldiers are now coming back and masquerading as democrats. It is all lie and deceit. I believe sincerely that until the democratic forces genuinely come together to struggle for power, particularly at the formative stage of any political party, that is the only time you can stop all these looters coming back to the progressives as reactionaries. As far as we are concerned, we know ourselves.
Any attempt for them to derail the progressives we will not agree. We know these people right from the first republic and some of them are still alive today. They did a lot during the second republic and now they are at the centre stage. The deceit in the system is what is causing all these problem. But, I think there would be an end to all these when the progressives in this country discover themselves fully. And knowing fully well that the idea of forming a mega party that will not even have a constitution, that will be very difficult for the Independent National Electoral Commission(INEC) to register, because the ruling party is still there. It still monitors the activities of INEC. In fact, of recent the INEC registered five non-existent bodies calling themselves political parties, whereas, the agitation for a mega party had been on ever before that. So, I believed the progressives should not waste their time. We may decide to adopt one of the existing political parties and moved in there and I think AC had been playing that role of a formidable opposition. In all national issues AC has been consistent and it also demonstrates that anywhere it were. AC had demonstrated to the people that if the party is given the opportunity to run the affairs of the states or nation, it would perform.
The ruling party has been accusing the opposition of being a clog in the wheels of progress in the state. Specifically, the ruling PDP accused your party of not seeing anything good in what is doing. What do you have to say about this?
It is laughable to hear this from the ruling party. How can we see anything good when the governor has not done anything? Are we going to manufacture good things for him? He has nothing good. Can you imagine a governor who came and said he wants to establish a new university when the existing ones are just glorified nursery and primary schools? When you look at the National Assembly what is the percentage of the opposition element there in that place. PDP had the majority in the two assemblies, in the state assemblies they are there too. We thought, this is a party which has no direction and the leadership is not in existence. They are just a jamboree of people who came together to loot our treasuries and that is what we have been witnessing in this country. They have touched nearly all the sectors of the economy and they have bastardised them. Nigeria is only lucky to have a modicum of decency in the judiciary that people like Bode George are now cooling off in the prison custody. When you look at the degree of atrocities these people have perpetrated nearly in all sectors of the economy, the opposition is not even fighting them; it is the people of Nigeria that would fight them.
A party in government that has no clear cut policy direction; that has not made any improvement on the economy of the nation; they will say white today, tomorrow it will turn black; none of the promises have been met and the ruling party is now turning to the opposition, which I would say if not for AC where is opposition in this country? Those other parties who won one or two states in the country have nearly decamped to the PDP. It only remains the AC on the field. And the little we can do, we are doing it. If anybody says the opposition is not allowing them to work; take for example now in Ekiti State, is it the opposition that said the government should not pay salaries of workers and all the allowances for the months of December, which is the critical month when everybody is supposed to be celebrating; both Muslims and Christians.
Is it the opposition that said the government should not fulfil all its promises to the people and the abandoned work that had not been completed? I think the primary responsibility of any opposition is to gear up the ruling party, that you lack in certain areas and so areas why can't you find solution to it; and that is exactly what the AC is doing in Ekiti and the nation at large. Most of our activities, which are always reported on the pages newspapers, are telling the ruling party how to do things properly. If anybody is coming out with such accusation against the opposition, people should know that it is a laughable one. The PDP government has no direction, no vision and as a result of this we are all in darkness.
The social services, like electricity, water and good roads are not in existence. Not at all. We have been pointing out these deficits to the government. The schools; teachers are crying, civil servants have nothing to take home. So, if the opposition are saying all these it means the PDP government has not done anything good. We don't pretend about this, because we know the state belongs to us. And we know the problems. I know the tribunal issue will be attended to and the actual winner of that election would be known. Even Segun Oni, himself is not happy sitting on stolen mandate. He's not enjoying it at all. These are the issues on ground. Anybody that is enjoying the facility that does not belong to him, will find it very difficult and that is the crux of the matter. The PDP sits on the wealth of the state and distributes it to whoever it wishes. And it has not been doing that well. And there is no way we can say it has done well.
Nigeria has just been enlisted by the Government of the United States as one of the terrorist nations in the world. This is addition to the high profile of corruption. What does this development portend for us as nation?
The essence of all these is the absence of visionary leadership. We talk of corruption, people tend to be on the side of corruption when they realise that everything that makes life meaningful are not available and people try to cut corners to make themselves comfortable. This idea of power generation, you can see how people live in perpetual darkness. And people will like as much as possible to ease themselves out of this. When they have no means they look for other means. We are taking care of ourselves in the area of potable water, because you have to sink boreholes, make a very good well, even good road networks, you want to get to your house you have to partake in one activities or the other.
So apart from air that is created in abundance by God Almighty, there is nothing to enjoy in this land. And human beings been what we are will like to maker ourselves comfortable. This is why the endemic nature of corruption is very difficult to curb. The most unfortunate things is that these things operate at the highest level. By the time you read about all these people; just recently, I was reading about a bank Chief Executive who was arrested having about 57 houses and other investments. You now realise it that the masses are not in the position to be corrupt. The Corruption is in the high places.We are among the most corrupt people in the world.
In actual fact, the thing follows one another in sequence. When you look at the young man that was arrested for attempted terror attack, you will see he belongs to the nouveauriche class in Nigeria. Terrorism is a very costly terrain which nobody can just go into it. It is borne out of the absence of leadership and high level of indiscipline that operates among the leadership of this country. When two elephants fight as they used to say, it is the grass that suffers. It is the common man in this country that would bear the brunt of the aftermath of this young man. Already, Nigeria has been enlisted as one of the terrorism nations in the world. When you get to any airport in the world today, once you are a Nigerian, or you are smart man before they discover your nationality you are in for it. If a child is not well trained at home, somebody somewhere is awaiting him. And something he or she lacks here will be given to him there.
So the problem that is bedevilling this country is that of leadership and we are in the worst stage now when you cannot say this is the President for three months now, nobody can categorically say this is where Mr President is. I was telling some people the other day that what exists in Saudi Arabia can also exist here. Why can't they transfer a section of the hospital that is treating Mr President to Nigeria? With latest technology, I think that could be done in hours. The facilities could be installed at the National Hospital, Abuja.
We are playing God in this country. God has given us every opportunity in this country but we don't tap it. We are now saying illness is not synonymous with one person. It is true; what we are saying is that if you are sick you have to be treated too in an environment people should know. I believe we are in a state of anomie in this country. Like the Yoruba used to say "if a small calamity befalls somebody greater ones will follow." And that is exactly what is happening in the country. It is a pity that an innocent person will now suffer from the inadequacy of one person.
How do you think the nation can get out of these mess?
There is no solution in sight yet. Even the National Assembly, which is supposed to be the beacon of hope is directionless. Who can you point to today as the head of government? Our Constitution has been flagrantly jettisoned, so to say. The National Assembly, through the Federal Government has suppressed the Constitution because of their inordinate ambitions. Nigeria is moving from a bad situation to worse one, only God knows when the end will to the irrational behaviours of people in power. We are in for it. It is terrible case.
Again, the Yar'Adua-led government is clamouring for reforms in some sectors of the economy like its predecessor. In your own observation do you think we can make anything out this as a people and nation?
That is the nature of a community full of deceitful people. We are deceiving ourselves. We are not sincere about the situation on ground. And if somebody is full of deceit he or she will find it difficult to pinpoint where the problem lies. That is the greatest problem we have in this country. In a situation where there is no clear-cut policy that is meant for this country, we are just beating about the bush and that is why there are no reforms anywhere. Look at the INEC for example that has performed woefully. The President himself accepted that the election that brought him into power was very flawed. The President said he was going to do something about it, he set up a committee and the committee rolled in the report. Up till today they are still looking for a way to suppress it. To even say my minds, there's nothing wrong with the Nigerian Constitution. It is the managers that have problems. Even as bad as INEC, is if we have people of proven integrity not like Maurice Iwu who had turned everything upside down, things will be better; nothing wrong with the system but it is the people. There is no clear-cut policy on issues. When you get to office, you respect the Constitution and say this is the policy of the country. It is only then that we can move forward as a nation. But in a situation where the assemblies of this nation are just sitting down there, they are not interested in the welfare of the people but in the welfare of their pockets; as long we are doing that revolving like a barber's chair, there won't be any solution.
2011 General Elections is around the corner. What should an average Nigerian expect?
What is going to happen in 2011 general elections has begun to manifest. It started in Ekiti here. You saw the way the INEC manipulated the election for the ruling party. We are going to Anambra in February to witness another charade. So, these are indications of what is going to happen in 2011. Whatever happens in Anambra in February 6, will tell a lot about 2011. We are still talking of reforms, which the National Assembly has not find solution to. And it is what comes out that reform that will be used to weather the storm of that 2011 elections. The majority of the people of this country say Iwu should go and somebody of proven integrity should come in to paddle the canoe of the election in this country and nothing has been done in that regards. If you are asked to guess about what will happen in 2011, your guess will be as good as mine. We are going to face a problem which INEC has already started in connivance with ruling PDP. I belief 2007 was not rosy coupled with the absence of the president. There is nobody taking care of things presently and preparing for election. I hope I'm wrong if calamity will not happen even before 2011.
How do you see the punishment been currently meted out to the corrupt officials in this country.
If somebody stole a goat and the government gives him or her 15years imprisonment and another steals a big cow and you are robbing his shoulder, do we now say there is commensurate punishment? People in high places who have mastered corruption, they are doing it with impunity and the law is smiling on them. And that is why till today there is no appreciable success recorded in the fight against corruption. We are only lucky of recent that the judiciary tried to wriggle itself out of the murky waters of Nigerian politics by taking bold steps against some corrupt individuals. I hope the judiciary will not allow corrupt people to manipulate themselves out of the prison, because that will be a dangerous precedent again in this country. I believe sincerely that the fight against corruption should not be limited to the masses. It should be taken to the top echelon of our society whereby those who are involved are publicly disciplined. As long as we are pampering them, the situation will continue. I am in strong support that the high calibre too should face the music.
What is your opinion on the current reforms in the banking sector?
The revelation that is coming out of the banking sector probe is alarming and to some extent embarrassing that those we believe are more or less sacred are the chief carriers of corruption in this country. It is high time something was done. To me, everything is just like a jungle. We are operating a banana republic.
With the way your party's petition is dragging at the tribunal don't you think your supporters may lose interest in the course?
It is a process .This is the first time rigging in any election is spiritedly challenged. Before it used to be let us forget it, it is God's doing .We will go back to the drawing board and start a new process. But this time around ,we are committed to unravelling the secret behind this election. That is why you people are still keeping the hope, because the actors are still very committed to the spirit of that particular election. Again, human beings being what they are, we can be fed up but I know for sure it has to start somewhere. Like I'm used to telling people that freedom is not given, it has to be fought for. We have made our people to realise that we are fighting for electoral freedom. We are trying to create electoral sanity. A society in which people's votes would count; when we have enumerated these to our people, it would be in their heart, no matter the time it takes us to get there. We keep on reminding them that the struggle we embarked upon is not as result of the fact that somebody wants to be governor or commissioners, but because there is decay in the system. It is our duty to remove this decay so that we can have a free flow of election in Nigeria. We want to have it to our glory in this part of the world that the sanity we are talking about will start from this state. That is why we are moving on tenaciously. And the people are not tired.
If you are in revolutionary struggle of this nature, you must constantly inform your people of the various developments, so that those who are still dragging their feet will know that they have to keep fit. We are not counting cost and we still believe in what we are doing and we are tired. We are putting the matter in the hands of God and at the same time we want to unravel the secret.

Original_Search_Title: Presidential Vacuum - The Position of The Law (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Presidential Vacuum - The Position of The Law (2)
Body_Text:
What then is the position of the law where the President is permanently incapacitated, as in the case of Nigeria today, where the President is seriously encumbered with grievous health problem that has become incessant and disturbing? The relevant provision is Section 144 of the Constitution, which provides as follows: (1) "The President (or Vice President) shall cease to hold office if
(a) by a resolution passed by two-third majority of all the members of the executive council of the Federation it is declared that the President or Vice President is incapable of discharging the functions of this office; and
(b) the declaration is verified, after such medical examination as may be necessary, by a medical panel established under subsection (4) of this section in its report to the President of the Senate and Speaker of the House of Representatives.
(2) Where the medical panel certifies in the report that in its opinion the President or Vice President is suffering from such infirmity of body or mind as renders him permanently incapable of discharging the functions of his office, a notice thereof signed by the President of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of Representatives shall be published in the Official Gazatte of the Government of the Federation.
(3) The President or Vice President shall cease to hold office as from the date of publication of the notice of the medical report pursuant to subsection (2) of this section.
(4) The medical panel to which this section relates shall be appointed by the President of the Senate and shall comprise five medical practitioners in Nigeria -
(a) one of whom shall be the personal physician of the holder of the office concerned, and
(b) four other medical practitioners who have, in the opinion of the President of the Senate, attained a high degree of eminence in the field of medicine relative to the nature of the examination to be conducted in accordance with the foregoing provisions.
(5) In this section, the reference to "executive council of the Federation" is a reference to the body of Ministers of the Government of the Federation, howsoever called, established by the President and charged with such responsibilities for the functions of government as the President."
From the above provision, it would be seen that the constitution places the responsibility of initiating the processes for the removal of the President, in cases of incapacity of the President to function, on the Executive Council of the Federation which, according to Section 144 (5), means the body of Ministers of the Government of the Federation. Ordinarily, one would have advised that whenever the President returns to the country or even while still in the hospital, the executive council should set the machinery in motion to comply with the above provision of the law. However, it is submitted that to expect this provision of the law to be implemented by the Executive Council of the Federation is to expect that a camel could easily pass through the eye of the needle, for the simple reason that, these Ministers are appointees of the President who are expected to be his loyalists. Hence, this provision of the law is dead on arrival. In other words, the council may not have the courage or gut to take the bull by the horns and take such step as would relieve their master of his office as President.
It is therefore recommended that whenever the present constitution is being overhauled or amended, this particular provision should be tampered with to take away the responsibility of initiating the removal of the President in such circumstance, as enumerated in Section 144, from the Executive Council of the Federation to the National Assembly, who are expected to act in the larger interest of the nation under the democratic principle of checks and balances. This would in the future, hopefully, save the nation from the present embarrassing situation it has found itself, a situation that has ridiculed and reduced the country to one akin to a Banana Republic within the comity of nations. Nigeria has unfortunately become a country who is a giant only in size but cannot subject itself to the minimum standard of civilized conduct expected of a reputable member of the International community and whose leaders are not embarrassed by anything, no matter how grevious. Not even, systemic and institutional failures occasioned by poverty of leadership!
It is poverty of leadership for a former head of state to spend billions of naira over a period of eight years to organize a transition programme that ended up in a stalemate called June 12 Election Annulment, due to arrogant and stubborn desire not to play by the rules of the game, thereby throwing the nation into avoidable catastrophe. Now, another leader from the same section of the country is failing to play by the rules of the game as laid down by the constitution, in the peculiar circumstances as we now have, so that the country can move on without unnecessary tension. Day in day out, the concept of one Nigeria is being unduly threatened by our leaders. In an analysis by John Campbell, a former U.S. Ambassador to Nigeria and a member of the U.S. Council on Foreign Relations (CFR), posted on the CFR website on 30/12/09, he observed among other things as follows: "Yar'Adua departed to Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, for medical treatment 41 days ago after being diagnosed with acute pericarditis. But his failure to handover the affairs of state to Jonathan has not only sparked off a succession and constitutional crisis, but also created a leadership vacuum, which the American government fears may be the leeway for the military to strike" . That is the candid assessment of our situation today from the perspectives of the Americans.
May I, however, use this medium to send a strong message to our military that they are not welcome at all to intervene in Nigerian politics again. They have always been part of our problems. Most of our former military leaders (with the exception of a few) are today's billionaires and the untouchables, at the expense of the nation. They are above the law and not accountable to anyone. Hence, the military cannot save the nation, except their pockets. They cannot wash their hands completely off the problems we have today, as they handpicked and handed over power to rtd. Gen. Olusegun Obasanjo (their preferred candidate) in 1999, notwithstanding whether or not he won a fair election. Therefore, let the military take notice that whatever political problem we have today in Nigeria would be solved holistically by the people themselves. Democratic problems should be resolved democratically. The military should stick to their constitutional role. You can fool some people some time, but not all the people all the time. Let the military take care of their past leaders who have looted the nation and given them a bad name.
•Concluded
Dele Ojogbede is a Lagos-based legal practitioner

Original_Search_Title: The Road We've Travelled in Search of Law Reform
Headline: Kenya: The Road We've Travelled in Search of Law Reform
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Now look at us. Look what we have done. We never seem to know what we want, do we? We have gone round and round for a good 18 years like dogs chasing their tails and stopped exactly where we began in 1992. Come with me to 1992.
