The absence of President Yar'Adua since November 23, 2009 without handing over to his deputy also compounded the problem for Nigeria. That created a vacuum for a presidential discussion between Nigeria and the United States in the aftermath of the Christmas Day terror incident. The absence of President Yar'Adua and his inability to hand over to the Vice President further gave Nigeria the image of a banana republic - an unserious country - that should not be reckoned with in international issues.
There is no denying that if things continue the way they are in Nigeria, Nigerians should expect more acts that would seem to diminish, embarrass or belittle the nation by other countries. The saying of the elders is true: if a man calls his bowl useless or ordinary, neighbours would use it as trashcan.

Original_Search_Title: Country Needs to Review Foreign Policy - Onyia
Headline: Nigeria: Country Needs to Review Foreign Policy - Onyia
Body_Text:
Dubem Onyia was Minister of State for Foreign Affairs (1999 to 2003) and two-term member of House of Representatives. In this interview with Correspondent, CHIBUZOR EMEJOR, in Abuja, the lawmaker frowns at Nigeria's current foreign policy, which "is not protecting Nigerians", adding that the foreign ministry has not been as proactive and robust as it was in his days. For him, the Constitution has been violated and the citizens are anything but patriotic, hence he insists on his previous call for a Sovereign National Conference. Excerpts...
The United States (U.S.) government has placed Nigeria on the terrorist watch-list. Do you think that Nigeria deserves such treatment?
It is absolutely surprising to me that the United States government can afford to jeopardise the relationship between her and Nigeria, knowing fully that Nigeria is a very crucial and loyal friend of U.S. We have had a very good relationship that ran for some decades now and Nigeria is an important hub not only in the West African sub-region but also in the African continent. As an ally to the U.S., this matter should have been handled better than the way the U.S. government handled it. In the first place, there was no security lapse on the part of Nigerian government because the father of the young man in question went and alerted the United States Embassy on the behaviour of his son. Again, the British government has an intelligent pact with America. We are aware that this young man, Farouk Umar AbdulMutallab has connection with Al-Qaeda. They have been trailing the young man. They have the information and that information, I am sure, was passed on to the American Intelligence, and that was why despite that AbdulMutallab, who comes from a wealthy family, studied in Britain and has no reason to be refused visa by the British government, yet they still refused him visa. This is because he is a risk to the security of Britain. In the past, if the British Embassy refuses entry visa to anybody, it will be absolutely difficult for America to give that person visa because they would like to know why the British Embassy refused him visa. So why did the American Embassy go ahead to issue that young man visa? Again, no Nigerian jubilated or supported the action of AbdulMutallab. So I want to know the criteria on which U.S. based its inclusion of Nigeria in the terrorist list. He (AbdulMutallab) has neither received the support of the Nigerian government nor that of his parents nor the 150 million Nigerians. Even the Northern part of this country that is predominantly Muslims has not marched to the streets to sing the praise of the young man. Nobody has supported him. This is an isolated case. Also, the young man is not based in Nigeria for the past so many years. He did not make contacts with Al-Qaeda in Nigeria. I have not seen where Nigeria went wrong. It is absolutely surprising and shocking to me as a person that the U.S. government decided to place Nigeria on the terrorist watch-list. I pray God that they should go and do a re-think because it will not augur well in the relationship between Nigeria and U.S.
As the former Minister of State for Foreign Affairs, what do you think should be the right approach in resolving this issue?
First of all, dialogue. You see, my first reaction is that I blame the Foreign Affairs Ministry in Nigeria. We don't have a robust, proactive foreign policy as of today because the first action should have been to summon the American ambassador immediately as soon as it happened, even before the placement of Nigeria on the terrorist watch-list. They should have summoned her to answer some questions such as: why did they give the young man visa? Secondly, as soon as the U.S. government decided to place Nigeria on the watch-list, we should have written a strong-worded letter to the U.S. Secretary of State. One would have advised the President do get in touch with President (Barack) Obama. But our President is indisposed. The Foreign Ministry should have written a letter of protest and they would have even followed it up by even going to Atlanta to organise World Press Conference to place Nigeria's position squarely where it belongs. This is not the assignment of Dora Akunyili, the Information Minister. There is no Ministry of Information in America. It is purely the function of the Foreign Affairs. Also, the Nigerian ambassador to U.S. should have been recalled for briefing and given message for the State Department. If it is possible, the Foreign Minister should have travelled to U.S. to have a meeting with U.S. Secretary of State, Hillary Clinton. We started handling the issue when Nigeria has been placed on the terrorist watch-list. Everybody now started to act. It is not the way to handle a matter, and that has to do with foreign relations.
Remember, we have had such situations in the past. There was a time in Bauchi State, when we had religious crisis, when Sharia laws were introduced in two or three states in the North. A woman was to be stoned to death. We knew how we handled it in the Ministry. We handled it perfectly. The international community was crying and shouting. They were going to take action against Nigeria.
How would you assess the present administration's foreign policy? For example, the Olusegun Obasanjo administration made positive impact in the international arena. But looking at the present administration, President Yar'Adua often avoids attending international meetings.
I said it when I was in the office: we had a very good team, Sule Lamido and I. You see, the President is the chief foreign affairs minister. So the inability of the President to make contacts and embark on some foreign trips to consolidate on the gains of Obasanjo's era has taken us many, many years behind. If you go the Foreign Ministry, the staff will tell you that. What two of us, Sule Lamido and I, were doing in the past is what three ministers are doing now. Yet, there is nothing to shows for it. First and foremost, we (Lamido and I) know that we were extensions of the President's office. We worked harmoniously. There was never any quarrel. There was no bickering. In fact, we were used as examples to other ministers of state. But today, there is a quarrel between the minister and ministers of state, quarrel between the minister and permanent secretary. Do you think that under that situation, the centre can hold or any progress can be made? Never! I can give examples of what we achieved during our time. It was during that period we placed Nigeria and Nigerians first. The foreign policy of any country is to protect the citizens and policies of that country. To make sure that we have a good relationship with our neighouring countries in the West African sub-region, we embarked on what we called West African Integration. Before 1999, all the protocols made between Nigeria and ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) were in the shelves. They were in the drawers. Obasanjo, because of his robust foreign policies, said that all those policies plus the new ones that we signed must be brought out, activated and implemented. That was when Ministry of Integration was created. And other countries on the West African sub-region went to create Ministry of Integration in their countries. We were fortunate enough to have the chairman of ECOWAS then, Alfa Konare, who was then the chairman of the French-speaking section of the ECOWAS. So, he was able to assist us in ensuring that the Anglophone and Francophone countries come together in this integration. Again, another President that was very vital in that integration was the former President of Guinea Conakry, Lansana Conté, who is now late. In fact, I remember that he fired the Minister of Finance, who was late in our meeting. At that particular point in time, we were flying all over the West African sub-region up to midnight, in ensuring that we had meeting that would get the integration in place. It was how we put in place this ECOWAS Parliament and ECOWAS Policy Unit. ECOWAS Parliament, we brought to Nigeria while ECOWAS Policy Unit was taken to Ghana.
Secondly, we placed Nigerians, wherever they are, on the priority list. AnywhereNigerians are attacked, whether home or abroad, we would react immediately. For the first time in the history of Nigeria, when Nigerians were about to be attacked or maltreated by the Libyan government, we sent a plane to evaluate Nigerians from Libya. And they were brought home. It has never been done before. That is what is expected to be seen in a democratic government. At that period, if any Nigerian was abused or maltreated by any foreign embassy in the course of seeking entry permit to travel to that country, we would react. We were not interfering in the consular law of any country; it is the duty of any country to issue or deny Nigerians visa. But it must be within the law and courtesy. Now, ministers even go for visas and they are denied.
I will give you another example. Two Nigerians went to Trinidad and Tobago on holiday, and their visiting visa expired. The least we expected the government of Trinidad and Tobago to do was to deport them. But they arrested them and put them in prison, where criminals were kept. The Nigerian ambassador then tried her best to get them released and the authorities refused. When the information got to us, I summoned their ambassador in Nigeria to my office. I cancelled the joint commission meeting I was supposed to hold in that country. I gave them 24 hours to release them or we would sever diplomatic relations with them. In less than 12 hours, those boys were released to our ambassador. When I went to Trinidad and Tobago, I received a letter of apology from not only the Prime Minister, even from their President, for their action. That is the only way you will be proud to hold the Nigerian passport. That is not to say that we encourage criminality. If you commit an offence outside the country, and you are arrested, all we can do is to try and bring you back and punish you. I remember a Nigerian who was to be executed in Thailand. We intervened, 24 hours before the execution. He was brought back to Nigeria and was punished. So if you treat your country as a banana country, other people will treat you as such. But there must be co-operation and cohesion between the leadership of the foreign ministry before these things can be achieved.
We were able to resolve boundary dispute between Nigeria and Equatorial Guinea. The boundary disputes case lasted for over 12 years. Within six months, we were able to resolve it, and signed a treaty with them. They became part of us. They joined us in The Hague during the Bakassi issue. We resolved our boundary dispute with Sao Tome and Principe and we were able to set up Nigeria-Sao Tome Joint Commission that is providing employment for Nigerians and earning Nigeria about $500 million annually. Other countries we resolved our boundary disputes with included Benin, Niger and Chad. We lived in harmony with our neighbours, just because the President himself was very active. In this administration, there was a position that Nigeria contested for in Ethiopia and we lost it to Rwanda. We should use what we have to get what we want. There is no way Rwanda should have beaten us in any position in the international arena. I think our foreign ministry should be more proactive and robust.
We also used Technical Aid Corps set up bythe Ibrahim Babangida regime as strong tool in advancing our foreign policy. So if we allow our citizens to be treated like animals outside this country when ever they travel out, the government should be blamed for it. America or Britain would go to war for one America or British citizen humiliated in any part of the world.
Nigerians are often killed in droves in countries like Thailand, China and even Libya. What could be responsible for this development?
You know, every country has her good and bad eggs. In a basket full of apples, you will always find good and bad apples. Even in America, the number of crimes committed in every second, minute or hour is unimaginable. But they don't go about painting their country black.
A Nigerian was said to be urinating in a wrong place in China. He was killed by a security man.
Yes, that's what I am saying. What was the reaction of the government? It is the duty of the Nigerian government to protect Nigerian citizens wherever they are. We have an embassy in China. The Nigerian government should have reacted immediately. China is a very big ally of Nigeria, because in the United Nations, they protect our interests. Also their investment in Nigeria is increasing by the day. But it depends on the way Nigerian government reacts on certain things that happen in China. In the past, it was easy to get a visa to China, but it is becoming difficult today. We have always advocated the formation of Nigerian Association in any country they reside. We have always advised that Nigerians should register with Nigerian embassy in whatever country they go, so that the Nigerian embassy will be able to wade in, when they have consular problem. We are to blame most Nigerians too, who hardly go to Nigerian embassy to announce their arrival. It is one of the things we should encourage Nigeria to do even before they travel out. The embassies were set up with the tax-payers' money. They are there for their interests. At times, they avoid going to the embassies because some of them engage in illegitimate business and they don't want Nigerian embassy to know what they are doing. So we need to do a lot of awareness campaign.
Also, we need to tell Nigerian diplomats in those places that they are there for the interest of Nigeria and Nigerians; that they should not go there and become masters to Nigerians who come to register or make enquiries. Nigerian would tell you that when they go to the Nigerian embassy, rather than look into their problems, they make things difficult for them. The issue is that our relationship with China is such that the Chinese would have no reason whatsoever to humiliate Nigeria in China. We have been very good to them. We have opened our doors to them. They are doing tremendous business in Nigeria.
On the other hand, foreigners in Nigeria are treated like sacred cows. They are untouchables. The Police hardly stop them on the road, even when they have flagrantly flouted the laws of the land. Is it an inferior complex that makes our security agents to overlook their activities?
It is not inferiority complex. Nigerians are easy-going people. May be because of our culture, our hospitality level is so high. We welcome visitors and we treat them very well, even when they are visitors who are not legally living in Nigeria. For example, no Nigerian police officer has ever stopped a foreigner to ask for his passport. They treat them much better than they would treat Nigerians. It is because of our culture. That is not to say that they should close their eyes and not to do exactly what they are supposed to do as law enforcement agents. I will give you an example. What breaks my heart is our relationship with South Africa. During the struggle for apartheid and independence in South Africa, every Nigerian played a role. The role of our former Head of State, General Murtala Mohammed, might have been responsible for the coup that claimed his life. Again, every Nigerian civil servant, including messengers, contributed to the struggle. His/her money was deducted at source for the struggle in South Africa. Most of the South African leaders including (Thabo) Mbeki were in exile in Nigeria. They were looked after by the Nigerian government. Go and see the way Nigerians are treated there now. For Nigerians to live in South Africa, they have to deposit some amount of money. It is not even a guarantee that they will get visas. What about Nigeria's contributions in Liberia and Sierra Leone? We cannot even quantify the number of Nigerians who lost their lives in these two countries. Some families are still hoping that their breadwinners would come home some day. We spent over $24 billion in both countries, trying to ensure that democracy, peace and stability returned to these countries. Go and see the way we are being treated there now. It is high time we reciprocated. We should use what we have to get what we need. Enough of Father Christmas (Santa Claus). We need to review our foreign policy in the interest of our country and her citizenry. Why should we continue to be giving loans to these countries that do not appreciate our efforts? We can hardly feed ourselves. Even when foreigners come to Nigeria and engage the services of Nigerians, they still maltreat them and pay them peanuts.
How do you foresee Nigeria's socio-economic and political development in 2010?
I will refuse to answer this question, because we are praying for the recovery of President Yar'Adua. Nigeria is currently flying on auto-pilot. I pray earnestly that the President recovers and comes back to Nigeria. Until the President comes back, nothing will happen in this country.
Does it not amount to constitutional breach on the part of the President for failing to write to the National Assembly?
The Constitution has been violated. We can not be crying over spilt milk. What is the remedy? Nigerians are very docile. We have a country called Nigeria, but Nigerians are not patriotic. We are not united. Anything goes in this country. That is why I said we should have Sovereign National Conference (SNC), so that we can come together to ask ourselves questions if we actually want a country called Nigeria. If we want, how do we live in it? We are only united during football match between Nigeria and other countries.
On a personal note, what is your political ambition come 2011?
Let me tell you something. If you ignore history, you will be a victim of history. Until we have electoral reform, where the power to elect the office holders will be vested on the people, you will not see me any longer in that charade called election. It will not be part of destroying my children and grandchildren's future.

Original_Search_Title: Eating Banana With Fork and Knife
Headline: Africa: Eating Banana With Fork and Knife
Body_Text:
As you are reading this the debate on Nigeria's disastrous outing against Egypt is still on.If you are on the side of the NFF, then you will need to be reminded that in 1990, Nigeria was thrashed in the opening match against Algeria and still came back to play in the final, that is why the match between Benin and Mozambique was considered a very good result that will enable us to come back.
For me, I look at Algeria, Cameroon and even Cote d' Ivoire and say what is happening? I even have it on good authority that all the coaches of their World Cup opponents are regretting coming and have packed their bags to go back.
Question. Does Angola signify that Africa will have a terrible World Cup? Or are the teams still savouring qualification and in the process underated others?
Well, we are not here to discuss what you already know or saw, rather let me let you into some behind the scene happenings. For instance, when the Egyptians were taking on the Nigerians you could see their star studded shirt, six stars to be specific denoting the number of times the Pharaohs have won the Nation's cup.
This is the practise even at the World Cup and the European Nations Cup, for kit manufacturers to depict the competition status of their clients.The Eagles have two stars to their credit and Adidas should be made to reflect this please.
General Dominic Oneya is a member of the CAF Nations Cup Organising Committee and is based in Lubango where you have Cameroun, Gabon, Zambia and Tunisia. The CAF delegation is lodged in an impressive environment called Casmir Lodge and the manager of the place does everything to make sure that the team is relaxed and at home.
General Oneya has just finished his dinner and wanted some fruits preferably bananas. She brought the banana in a plate, complete with a fork and knife.
There is a lost look on the Generals face. Eat banana with fork and knife? She said "yes" took the knife, sliced the banana in two, diced it and told the General to proceed. He did, but when he handled the second one, he preferred the normal way, more filling, and traditionally accepted.
I have not finished with the General. On Tuesday, as he was checking his mail, we noticed that the response he got from Yahoo was "Hi Dominic". You see I am one of those who believe that culture and tradition should be respected.
Pray, how do we get to let Yahoo know that this man is not only a retired General in the Nigerian Armed Forces, but was a military governor of two states, former Chairman of the Nigeria Football Association.......etc. how do we get Yahoo to know that Paul can be "hi Paul", Linus Mba can be "Hi Linus" but when it comes to the general, it should be..........I wonder.
In an earlier piece I did on tit bits, I wrote how I was laughed at for daring to believe that all Portuguese words ended with an "O". Let me stretch my belief a bit further by informing that stadium is Estadio, while governor is governo ( of course the "r" in the end had to be removed. If you are heading for the super market, your driver will be told to take you to a supermecado.
Of course, centre is centro, airport, aeroporto and augusto is august, while first aid is primeiros soccoros. If you think I have made my point, obligarto (thank you).

Original_Search_Title: Local Artists Join Hands to Fight Malaria
Headline: Tanzania: Local Artists Join Hands to Fight Malaria
Body_Text:
Dar Es Salaam — THE fight against malaria is taking a new impetus with the launching of Tanzania's biggest ever musical collaboration among top local artists.
Clouds FM General Manager, Ruge Mutahaba announced that over 18 local artists will take part in the concert to kick start the campaign.
The concert dubbed 'Zinduka' is scheduled for Dar es Salaam on February 13 this year. The launching, he said, will be graced by President Jakaya Kikwete.
"From the 18 plus artists who have shown willingness to participate, already Lady JD, Prof Jay, Malow, Bi Kidude, Banana Zorro and Mwasiti have composed a song to kick start the campaign," Ruge said.
Attendants of the press conference today in Dar es Salaam got to see first hand, the music video of the Zinduka anthem that is blended with soothing voices and hip hop lyrics. President Kikwete sporting very casual attire appears in the video, urging everyone in the country to stand up and play a part in eradicating malaria.
Tanzania House of Talent (THT) features in the video and the youngsters are seen engaged in a mix of tap, gumboot dancing and a Ruvuma traditional jig. The function also saw the Minister for Health and Social Welfare, David Mwakyusa unveil the Zinduka billboard that has President Kikwete clad in a T-shirt with his fist in air signifying that malaria is unacceptable, people should arise against it.
Mwakyusa said that although there is an ongoing campaign against malaria already, Zinduka was necessary in that it is out to engage a lot more people including the media, sportsmen, politicians, business people, those in the entertainment industry and was partnering with the US based Malaria No More initiative.
"Together with President Kikwete, I believe that every Tanzanian will join this campaign and realise the dream of wiping out malaria from the country starting now," he said.
Lady Jay Dee believes that music has big room for delivering messages to all Tanzanians in the fight against malaria. Prof Jay said he is honoured to be part of the initiative and to play his part in using his voice through music to make a difference.
"It is slowly dawning on to me that my voice has authority and it gives me inner joy that I can give back to the community by waking people in fighting malaria," Prof Jay said.
'Malaria Haikubaliki' is an initiative urging Tanzanians to think differently about the disease with an objective of increasing practices to prevent malaria such as consistently sleeping under an insecticide-treated mosquito, detecting and treating malaria early and ensuring antenatal care for pregnant women.

Original_Search_Title: Bahati - the Unlikely Gay Basher
Headline: Uganda: Bahati - the Unlikely Gay Basher
Body_Text:
Kampala — At the Imperial Hotel in Entebbe last month, MP David Bahati moved around the room handing out copies of his anti-gay bill to religious leaders of all the major faiths and denominations. Muslim imams in their embroidered skull caps, Anglican and Catholic priests in their collars were joined by Pentecostals, Seventh Day Adventists, Bahais and others at the gathering, a meeting of the Inter-religious Council of Uganda.
The event was only one of a number of appearances that the honourable member is making to get his controversial bill passed. "Through these people we will reach about 95% of the people who believe in God," said Bahati.
The MP has also done at least a dozen interviews with international media and he plans to fly to the US and the UK to make his case this month. Here at home, he has spoken at Makerere University and other public venues; he also plans to go to over 20 secondary schools before the private member's bill comes before parliament again. "We are working day and night to make sure that this bill gets passed," he said.
No less than President Obama and his secretary of state Hilary Clinton have opposed the legislation; the delegation of the European Union has filed a formal protest against it; Rick Warren, a former ally of conservative pastors in Uganda, has spoken out against it; and it has received media attention from CNN, BBC and Time. Despite the criticism, Bahati steadfastly defends it. "In my heart I believe that we are doing the right thing," he said.
Danger to Society
For his determination, Bahati, formerly a backbench NRM, is on the verge of becoming a hero for many religious in the country. Another attendee at the conference in Entebbe was a former teacher of the MP's from Ndorwa West. Onesimus Asiimwe said he is not surprised that the man is at the vanguard of the crusade against gays.
Assimwe, who is now the chaplain to the Anglican archbishop in Uganda, recalls that the future MP was reserved and quiet as a student but "a leader already." He says Bahati always stood out.
"You could see he had a bigger potential than what he was displaying."
Left orphaned when he was three-years-old Bahati was sent to live with his grandmother. Later she also passed away and the boy had to work at the local market selling and carrying bananas to raise money for school fees. He was separated from his siblings and only reunited with them at 13 when his sister came to the market to buy fruit and recognised him.
The man decided to run for parliament partly as a way to give back to the community that helped raised him. In his first term , Bahati is only 35-years-old with a boyish face and an unassuming manner. He seems an unlikely candidate to incite such a virulent debate. Even his strongest opponents have trouble conjuring up strong emotions towards him. The writer of Gay Uganda, a gay blog in Kampala, admits he went to a debate on the bill prepared to hate the man. He found he could not and the strongest insult he uses to describe the MP is that he is "a dupe."
The story of how Bahati came to be the face of the anti-gay campaign in Uganda goes back a couple of years. The MP, a father himself, first became interested in the issue of homosexuality after hearing testimony from sexually molested children. "I'm passionate about the issue of homosexuality because of both the danger for our children and our society," he said.
The bill establishes the death penalty for the offense of "aggravated homosexuality," that is homosexual sex with a person under the age of 18, a disabled person or in an instance when "the offender is a person living with HIV."
However, as many have pointed out, Uganda's criminal code already provides for the death penalty in cases of sexual assault or defilement of a person under 18. And besides that one provision, the bill has nothing to do with the sexual abuse of children at all. In fact, the bill's stated purpose is to prohibit "any form of sexual relations between persons of the same sex." It establishes a sentence of life imprisonment for those who commit "the offense of homosexuality."
So what business does the government have with what two consenting adults of the same sex do in the privacy of their own bedroom? To Bahati what goes on between them is a crime. "If two thieves were in the bedroom, would you stop government from going there? If two terrorists were in the bedroom, would you stop government from going there? When they get out they will influence (society). They are planning to recruit children into this behaviour."
Seeing TV and newspaper reports from the west of homosexuals getting married also helped to motivate Bahati, who speaks of a world-wide "gay agenda." "These are stories that really moved me. I said, 'Wow this can't go on; something must happen.' I saw this happening in our country if we didn't act." He started talking to colleagues about the issue and when it came time to present a bill, the young MP stepped forward.
Just "a lot of noise"
Bahati makes it clear he will not consider backing down, despite outcry at home and abroad. He said he doesn't fear pressure from donor countries, like Sweden, who have threatened to withdraw their contributions to the Ugandan budget either. "I don't think there is any country or any parliament in the world that will cut aid because of this issue of homosexuality," he said. "This is a minority group that is all over the world, with a lot of noise and a lot of connections."
Bahati furthermore downplays the statements that international leaders have issued on the bill. He calls the criticisms made by British Prime Minister Gordon Brown and Canadian PM Stephen Harper at the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting a mere "raising of concerns."
"There is no amount of pressure or intimidation that will deter us from protecting the children and defending family values here in Uganda. We will continue to move forward and stop the recruitment," he said.
So far not even strongly-worded condemnations by some of the world's biggest leaders have been enough to kill Bahati's determination. Perhaps a veto by the president, as recent reports have suggested is in the works, will eventually be required to halt the repressive law.

Original_Search_Title: CUF Readies to Celebrate Revolution Day
Headline: Tanzania: CUF Readies to Celebrate Revolution Day
Body_Text:
Dar Es Salaam — AT a modest makeshift tiny room in Chake Chake, Civic United Front (CUF) women's wing members under Rukia Kassim Ahmed are brainstorming on how to steal the show at the 46th Zanzibar Revolutionary Day celebrations due to be held at Gombani Stadium.
"It's the first time since multi-party politics were reinstated in the country for our party to take part and we want our presence to be felt," says Ms Ahmed as she chaired CUF's women's wing executive committee meeting.
In her company includes Mariam Hamad Bakari, a tough talking member of the committee who thinks Zanzibar has seen a lot of suffering and political persecution since the re-introduction of multi-party politics in early 1990s.
The emergence of CUF as the Spice Islands main opposition has its stronghold in Pemba islands where over 350,000 residents who share almost the same religion and also hold an opinion that they belong to the same ethnic origin.
Since 1995 when the first general elections were held under multi-party set up here, most residents of Pemba and CUF members have boycotted the January 12 Revolutionary celebrations here but this year is different.
"We support the reconciliation initiative being taken by our political leaders to end the suffering and animosity here and that's why we are gearing up to cheer Maalim Seif and President Karume next week," said Ms Bakari.
Most people here are in strong support of the recent meeting of Zanzibar President Amani Abeid Karume and CUF Secretary General, Maalim Seif Shariff Hamad, the first Chief Minister of the Spice Islands to come from Pemba Islands.
Mr. Hamad who served as Chief Minister under former Zanzibar President Ali Hassan Mwinyi in 1980s, was fell out of favour with the administration in late 1980s when he was briefly detained on political grounds.
He was later released and in 1992 when the country reinstated multi-party politics, he joined CUF which was formed on the Mainland by James Mapalala and others.
Mr. Mapalala and other founder members were later sacked from the party because of allegations of trying to betray CUF which became the isles main opposition after 1995 elections.
The CUF and Mr. Hamad have since 1995 rejected elections results which gave former President Dr Salmin Armour the presidency in 1995 and current President Karume victories in 2000 and in 2005 because of rigging allegations.
The people in Pemba have remained strong behind CUF and Hamad such that in January 2001 following disputed election results in 2000, 21 people died after demonstrations to denounce Mr. Karume's administration turned violent.
"We hope that this gesture of normalizing relations between Maalim Seif and President Karume will be implemented to its fullest extend and if need be then let the president be given an extension in office to lead a transition government for one or two years," Mbaruk Ali of Weza Rohoyo CUF branch in Chake Chake.
The idea of giving Mr. Karume an extension in office is so popular among CUF members here, in fact it has been recited by almost everyone who is a strong supporter of Maalim Seif.
In Pemba it's Seif and not CUF that has a following and whatever he says the masses follow.
The ruling Chama Cha Mapinduzi has already denounced any talk of extension of Karume's presidential term but most CUF members who are afraid of the repeat of January 27, 2001 events, want the Zanzibar president to ignore his party for the sake of stability in the troubled Spice Islands which have remained polarized since 1995.
Most Pemba residents who are CUF supporters fear the worst in this year's general elections should the current momentum in the talks to end political animosity collapse after a new head of state is picked after Karume's retirement.
"There are some people in the ruling party who survive when there is political animosity in Zanzibar and these are the people who are opposed to Karume being given a one or two years extension to lead a transition government," argued Saleh Nassoro Juma, CUF Secretary of Chake Chake district.
But CCM's Regional Secretary for Pemba South, Adam Paulo Ngalawa insists that Karume and the ruling party are not ready to violate the two constitutions of Zanzibar and the Union because the peace pact is not solely the work of the outgoing president.
"We are saying who ever comes in office after President Karume the reconciliation process will proceed, there is no going back.
But certainly we need to go to the polls to get people's consent before concluding any deal," argued Mr. Ngalawa.
Last year, the ruling party shook a bee hive here and everything went sour when its National Executive Committee meeting held in Butiama, Mara region suggested that the future of Zanzibar politics be decided by the people through a referendum.
CUF announced to withdraw from the 14 man Mwafaka political process with the ruling party and threatened to resort to street demonstrations and boycotting of all national activities.
The situation was so bad in Pemba such that CUF members blocked selling any food stuffs including bananas to their colleagues in Zanzibar where the majority of the ruling party members reside.
The latest breakthrough is touted as practical way of ending the islands troubled politics ahead of this year's general elections because majority CUF supporters argue that it's a Zanzibaris own initiative other than the three aborted similar accords which were dominated by Mainlanders under CCM.

Original_Search_Title: Redeeming the Country's Image
Headline: Nigeria: Redeeming the Country's Image
Body_Text:
The year 2009 ended with Nigeria unfortunately being in the news globally for the wrong reasons. For a nation that had no history of international terrorism, it was amazing to hear that a citizen, Umar Farouk AbdulMutallab, was caught in a Detroit-bound plane on Christmas Day attempting to set off a bomb, and putting the lives of the 290 passengers and crew on board at risk.
Nigeria ended the year with the unfolding events of that ugly incident. It was also a time when there was a severe shortage of petrol, leading to uneasiness among Nigerians as they spent several hours at filling stations at a time the world was celebrating Christmas and eagerly waiting for the dawn of a new year.
In addition, the President of the country, Umaru Musa Yar'Adua, had been away on health grounds for over a month without officially handing over power to his deputy, Vice President Goodluck Jonathan. The Vice President could, therefore, not act as the President, leading to claims and counterclaims between those in government and the opposition.
Nigeria entered 2010 in that state. But when on January 4, 2010, the United States listed Nigeria as a "security risk state," alongside Yemen, Iraq, Afghanistan, Pakistan, Libya, Lebanon, Saudi Arabia, Somalia and Algeria, it was shocking to many Nigerians. (The USA also announced the creation of another group termed "sponsors of terrorism" featuring Cuba, Iran, Sudan and Syria.) The implication of this categorization is that Nigerians would get special screening when travelling to or through the United States.
This categorisation of Nigeria as a "security risk state," based on a first-of-its-kind act of terrorism by a young man who spent his secondary school days in Togo , his university days in London and post-graduate days in Yemen, was clearly an over-kill. The United States President, Barack Obama, himself had acknowledged that the father of the alleged terrorist, Alhaji Umaru AbdulMutallab, had reported the strange behaviour of his son to the American security authority some months before the incident, an unusual action for a father to take against his son anywhere in the world.
The Minister of Information and Communications, Professor Dora Akunyili, reacted immediately by saying that the action of Mr. AbdulMutallab did not reflect the behaviour of Nigerians. Her words: "It is unfair to include Nigeria in the US list for tighter screening because Nigerians do not have terrorist tendency. AbdulMutallab's act was a one-off thing. He was not influenced in Nigeria. He was not recruited or trained in Nigeria. He was not supported whatsoever in Nigeria. AbdulMutallab's behaviour is not reflective of Nigerians and should not be used as a yardstick to judge all Nigerians. It is unfair to discriminate against over 150 million people because of the behaviour of one person. AbdulMutallab was a well-behaved child from a responsible family who developed the ugly tendency to do what he tried to do because of his exposure outside the shores of Nigeria. Nigerians are peace loving and happy people. We were even voted as the happiest people on earth".
So many other Nigerians have spoken to condemn the action of the U.S., with the National Assembly stating that it may strain the long-standing cordial relationship between Nigeria and the United States. Some people have even called for the severance of relations with the U.S.-based on this act.
What is clear from the action of the US is that Nigeria 's value in the world has seriously been diminished in recent years. Many people have reasoned that if that act was undertaken by a citizen of Indonesia, the largest Islamic nation in the world, Indonesia would not have been put on that list. If that same act was committed by a national of China, India, Brazil, South Korea, South Africa, Egypt, Singapore, or Malaysia , they would not have got the same treatment that Nigeria got.
The bargaining power of Nigeria has diminished terribly because of the political stagnation or retrogression that has been going on in Nigeria for several years now, especially since 2007 when President Yar'Adua took over power. Nigeria 's rating as the giant of Africa has nose-dived. This manifested last year when President Barack Obama chose Ghana rather than Nigeria as the first sub-Saharan African country to visit. That same year, Nigeria was excluded from the G-20 Summit, even though South Africa was invited.
The absence of President Yar'Adua since November 23, 2009 without handing over to his deputy also compounded the problem for Nigeria. That created a vacuum for a presidential discussion between Nigeria and the United States in the aftermath of the Christmas Day terror incident. The absence of President Yar'Adua and his inability to hand over to the Vice President further gave Nigeria the image of a banana republic - an unserious country - that should not be reckoned with in international issues.
There is no denying that if things continue the way they are in Nigeria, Nigerians should expect more acts that would seem to diminish, embarrass or belittle the nation by other countries. The saying of the elders is true: if a man calls his bowl useless or ordinary, neighbours would use it as trashcan.

Original_Search_Title: Matatu Strike Revealed All Things Bad and Ugly
Headline: Kenya: Matatu Strike Revealed All Things Bad and Ugly
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A few years ago, I observed school children doing their school work under streetlights on a busy street in Congo Brazzaville. Determined to succeed against all odds, these children were oblivious of the dangers posed by speeding cars or the risk to their eyesight.
Though moved by their sense of audacity and tenacity to succeed, I pitied them more. For a country rich in oil and other resources, there was no good reason why they did not have electricity in their houses.
Yet, knowing that Congo Brazzaville is in the grip of high-level corruption and much of its wealth stolen and stashed in off-shore accounts, these children were merely victims of servants who see public office as a cash cow.
Last week's strike by public transport operators was yet another stark reminder of what happens when a country allows corruption to rule the roost in any of its organs.
At some point in time, a nation comes to a moment of reawakening when it must confront the tragedy of its shame.
Matatus went on strike over complaints of "police harassment", a euphemism for demand of bribes. Without an alternative public transport system, thousands of commuters, including school going children, were stranded and precious man hours lost.
Blame irascible matatu crews and a traffic police brigade with many corrupt faces in its ranks. The two are joined in the hip by their singular lack of public accountability and contempt for ethic duty.
The matatu industry, an avenue for investment and employment for many, represents the best and worst of the true spirit of enterprise. Self-regulated by often competing interests, the industry has largely degenerated into a by-word for disorder and indiscipline - a Kenyan wild west.
The saving grace is that in the midst of this cacophony of organised chaos, a few notable exceptions, like Double M, 2 NK, among others show that the industry can get its act together and is not beyond redemption.
But the steady decline in service standards by players like the Kenya Bus Services and City Hoppa is a poignant reminder that even angels fall from grace.
There is no doubt that last week's action arose out of genuine and unresolved complaints by the industry against the traffic police whose appetite for bribes is legion. Like marrows growing on a rich dung heap, traffic police feed on the chaos of the matatu industry.
Because there is no genuine, systematic and entrenched commitment from the highest levels within the police force to rein in corruption, there are no prizes for betting that last week's strike is not the last.
True, a few dozen police officers have been arrested and charged over corruption but the numbers pale in comparison to the extent of the problem.
High sounding pontification about zero-tolerance to corruption rings hollow when viewed against obvious equivocation in ridding the force of its bad apples.
There are few "poor" traffic cops, many cannot account for their wealth and it is common knowledge that the department is widely seen as the most lucrative because it assures easy pickings.
That is not to say that there are no traffic police officers committed and faithful to their calling. But these are few and far between - the exception rather than the norm.
Kenyans must be saved from an irascible matatu industry that largely chooses not to play by the rules and a traffic police brigade that enriches itself under the guise of rendering public service.
For the matatu industry, there is an abiding lesson from within its own ranks that order is not bad for business where effective self-regulation ensures enforcement and censure for breaking the rules. This is common sense that must be driven home.
But it is time we seriously debated whether we can do without traffic police. They are bad for business everywhere and the legion of complaints against them calls for a serious rethink on their role and place.
Opting for greater reliance on technology to enforce the traffic code may be the way to lance the boil of corruption on our roads though this may only transfer the problem elsewhere.
Still, this must not paralyse us into inaction because, if there are any lessons to take from last week's strike, it is that our police officers cannot be entrusted to tame a wild west that is their gambling casino. Though Kenya is not a banana republic, we may go bananas unless we act.
Gichinga Ndirangu is a lawyer and policy analyst.

Original_Search_Title: 2010 Rosy for Hana Mining
Headline: Botswana: 2010 Rosy for Hana Mining
Body_Text:
Canadian explorer, Hana Mining, has kicked off the new year with a bang, having recently secured US$4 million (P26.7 million) towards further drilling and the commencement of a scoping study on the company's copper and silver project in Ghanzi.
According to its four-phase plan, Hana Mining will conduct a scoping study this year, which will determine the Ghanzi Project's economic viability. The study will involve establishing indicated and inferred resources, water access studies, project economics and progress infrastructure issues.
Previous studies have estimated that the Ghanzi project could have two billion tonnes of copper and 34.9 million ounces of silver, from 60.4 million tonnes.
The Canadian company also hopes to conduct environmental and social assessments this year as well. Much of the focus for the year will be on Hana's flagship area, the Banana Zone, which represents 67 percent or 40.7 million tonnes of the company's total resources in Ghanzi. The lion's share of the recently secured funds will be directed towards drilling and development efforts this year. Announcing the recent funding, Hana Chief Executive Officer and Chairman Marek Kreczmer said the US$4 million had been raised via a non-brokered private placement of shares. "The private placement consisted of five million shares for a purchase price of 80 cents per share. The net proceeds of this private placement will be used to continue drilling and to commence a scoping study on the Ghanzi project," he said.
The funding is the latest in a slew of capital-raising initiatives designed to advance the Ghanzi project. Last year, Hana conducted a series of highly successful fund raising initiatives including a P19.4 million placement and another for P3 million.
Late last year, the Canadian explorer closed a P916 000 private placement, all designed to advance Phase One of the copper and silver project.
The scoping study under Phase One, is expected to pave way for a Pre-Feasibility Study in 2011, scheduled under Phase Two. This Phase will also mark completion of resource drilling at the Banana Zone and the advancing of the resource from measured to indicate, from which the Pre-Feasibility Study will be based.Between 2012 and 2013, Hana will focus on Phase Three, which involves a Bankable Feasibility Study, non-recourse financing, attainment of mining licences and water rights and establishment of infrastructure.
The last Phase involves the engagement of an engineering, procurement, construction and management contractor, pre-mine development and mine construction. Commercial production is planned to start in 2015, allowing Hana Mining to join the adjacent Discovery Metals Limited as Botswana's only copper and silver producers.
Hana's march to commercial production is being aided, in 2010, by the spike in the price of copper, which this week reached a 17 month high of US$7 440 per tonne. The stronger copper price will prop up Hana Mining's fund raising initiatives and enhance shareholder support towards mine development.
In addition, preliminary studies have indicated that a lucrative mining district is feasible around the Banana Zone and Hana's other copper and silver licence areas. Already, Hana officials are confident the pre-feasibility study will show that the Project has no metallurgical, water or power issues.
Australian media recently quoted Kreczmer as saying Hana would likely consider using diesel generators, although government officials have said the area could be hooked up to the national power grid by 2012.
The Canadian company also hopes that the proposed Trans-Kalahari Railway will run a few kilometres near the copper and silver project, providing Hana with a valuable and cost effective route to the Namibian coast.

Original_Search_Title: Victor Agali is Innocent, says Lawyer
Headline: Nigeria: Victor Agali is Innocent, says Lawyer
Body_Text:
More facts are emerging in the shocking news about the arrest of former Super Eagles lanky striker, Victor Okechukwu Agali in Holland.
Few hours after the report hit the international media and airwaves that the Levadiakos of Greece striker, who once played for Olympique Marseille and OGC Nice of France, had been arrested at Schiphol Airport near Amsterdam for carrying two fake passports, Agali's lawyer, Bob Kaarls has issued a statement protesting the star's innocence.
Kaarls said Agali, fondly called Okey Banana during his early days in Lagos, was returning to Greece after visiting his wife and child in Nigeria, and disclosed that officials at the star's club had indicated that they are willing to pay the bail to secure their striker's freedom.
Dutch authorities claimed that the Schengen visa contained in the second document, issued by Cyprus, did not meet requisite standards, but Kaarls insists it was all a mix-up.
"The (first) passport judged problematic by officials was sent to him in this form by post by Nigerian authorities in 2007," he said.
Additional reports indicated that the Dutch police have already made an official statement on the development and gendarmerie spokesman, Robert van Kapel explained that Agali was apprehended two days after Christmas.
"At passport control, on December 27, he (Agali) was found to be in possession of a fake passport," said Kapel.
Upon further investigation, the 31-year-old striker in transit at Schiphol on a voyage from Lagos to Athens, turned out to have two sets of fake travel documents, said Van Kapel.
"In the one, the page with his personal particulars has been modified, in the other it was the visa," he said.
A judge will now decide whether to extend the footballer's provisional detention for further investigation.
During his itinerant professional career, Gangling Agali has also played for FC Toulon of France, Germany's Schalke 04 and Hansa Rostock, Xanthi Skoda of Greece and Rizespor of Turkey.

Original_Search_Title: Anambra 2010 - Call Him the Wind of Glory
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra 2010 - Call Him the Wind of Glory
Body_Text:
ONCE upon an eventful year, late in 2008, a certain patriot spirit seized hold of the airwaves. It was called 'ikukuoma' in Igbo, poorly interpreted as 'wind of glory' (sic). It soon became a movement which set out to recruit the best hands and minds in the Anambra State political household; deploying huge funds and substantial resources to convince that it was not one end-of-the-year jamboree; or some Christmas joke. It was gradually developing into a mass movement; well-funded, well-received and impactful, headed for the top, but for a later revelation. When the name Nicholas Ukachukwu was mentioned, a few sighs followed, suggesting that names can mean a lot, even in Anambra State.
The usual complaint was not that the man didn't deserve to be governor; nor was there any doubt whether he has the wherewithal to prosecute an election. No. In comparison with Andy Uba, who took the State by storm and nearly redefined democracy, Ukachukwu was an angel waiting to be discovered. Problem was that the man had a suspect temperament, was arrogant and uncouth; and with a brashness not likely to endear him to his supporters, nor to the electorate. Perhaps this was what gave rise to the born-again affectations and close association with wave-making evangelists. If that was the desired effect it was spot-on, for as soon as the man's new direction was made public, some people were ready to give him a second chance.
However, it was not long before events proved that it was a lot easier to get the man out of the bush, than the bush out of the man. In no time complaints about intemperate and overbearing attitudes leaked out in torrents. And people began to look for pastures anew in droves. Away from verbal abuse and condescension. Perhaps only the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) palace coup saved the day. Otherwise, a house of glory would have become a grotto of desolation and shame.
Perhaps another set of events will open a fresh window on the 'wind-of-glory'. The PDP intrigue ended with many victims. On paper, all of forty-six! Among the frontrunners only one man did not burn out. To keep up with the struggle, Chief Ukachukwu kept his Spartan spirit alive by migrating to Hope Party if only to keep his interest in Anambra State Government House intact. Despite that initial disappointment, he embraced hope once again. So seamlessly. By so doing he proved to be irrepressible or defiant. Or both! But this is only part of the story.
Stories had earlier made the rounds at the level of PDP that 'the Wind-of-Glory' already had the national hierarchs in the bag, after buying homes for some abroad and parting with ransoms. The candidacy and tenure at Awka Government House were as goods in the kitty too. Until the PDP macabre dance took the floor and began its grisly routine.
So what manner of man-of-God will rely so much on men and their foibles and take extra-moral measures to become governor in a State in dire need of reinvention and entrenchment of Christian tenets? There were too many paradoxes for comfort. It becomes necessary to take another look at the Hope Party candidate, what he brings to the table and his chances in the February 6, 2010 contest.
It is clear, for a start, that politics in Anambra State has undergone so much devaluation that anything is possible. Just anything. This has affected the quality of persons that put themselves forward for elective positions. At the last count in 2007 there were ex-convicts, stark illiterates, drug barons and scammers, some of whom actually got elected into the state and even national assembly. Such men are known more for their muteness during house sessions, than any form of contributions to advance national discourse. They literally merely occupy space! That was part of the abiding burdens of the Andy Uba era. Standards were obliterated. Or, rather, new ones were created, to accommodate a certain kindred species.
When men consider themselves good enough for positions to which they cannot add value, for which they have no relevant experience it ridicules the whole concept of leadership. In Anambra State, wealthy people no longer sponsor candidates or causes. They now literally buy up everything within sight, including informed opposition to their incongruous desires. Not satisfied anymore being godfathers they now seek to be gods in their own right. Which goes to show how values have changed in the troubled state. If certificates became obstacles, they are quickly arranged, accessed or forged. Meaning that people can claim to have academic doctorates in disciplines without having seen mimeographs or knowing what 101 courses are all about.
This brings to the fore once again our society's skewed perception of success. It is often observed that a restive craving for achievements defines our worldview. Achievements not necessarily related to the impacts we make in advancing society but rather the stations we attain. Better still if those stations entail accumulation, and more accumulation. It becomes a veritable vicious cycle. As most compatriots face the initial challenge of breaking away from poverty and want. Satiation on that expires when wealth is cornered. Then the craving for dominance takes over, making the struggle for power inevitable. And political power is the king! For the reach, the esteem and the perks and perquisites, including unimpeded looting.
Chief Ukachukwu might be forgiven for being like the rest of the pack. His craving for the exalted office does not criminalize him, even though his insistence might criminalize the office of governor. It is trite to wonder at what point our people will recognize their limits and limitations. He was once a senator without being on record for any seminal contributions to law-making. He is best-known for the protracted legal tussle to wrest his stolen mandate from a certain Senator Ugochukwu Uba, Andy Uba's elder brother. It is clear that this counted among the many antidemocratic gaffes credited to the Uba brothers. But pound-for-pound intellectually, Senators Uba and Ukachukwu don't belong to the same league. Although this truism does not, in any way, recommend the banishment of the democratic process.
People like Ukachukwu, having not been lucky to have sound western education can still find fulfillment elsewhere. They should know, ab initio, that they are best suited to some other niches. Fact is that they have a fixation for the appellation "His Excellency". Should abusing high office not then be redefined to include seeking and attaining positions they embarrass, and that will end up embarrassing them back. Making society the real loser.
There are stories of a troubled childhood and some anti-social habits even as a high-school student in DMGS Onitsha. Those ones do not matter as much as what the man made of his god-given talents and the realization that has made him a changed person. What I do not agree with is that he must be governor to deserve respect; or to blot out his past. Anambra State, for goodness sake, is not Bornu, Kogi or Kebbi State where the benchmark for becoming governor is bound to be less rigorous.
'The Wind of Glory" is said to be a dogged go-getter. This is well and good. But how well will things be if part of that aggression is transferred into awarding Scholarships, creating foundations and spreading the gospel. People like Ukachukwu should please save us the agony of taking Anambra State for somewhere in a banana republic. Enough of the insult.
He deserves mention here only for what he did to reinvent PDP in Anambra State. Beyond that he should face his business and try to do some good; if not in Anambra State or Nnewi North Local Government Area, at least in his native Osumenyi. An elite State like Anambra deserves much better!

Original_Search_Title: Yar'Adua - Govt On Brink of Constitutional Crisis--Ex-U.S. Envoy
Headline: Nigeria: Yar'Adua - Govt On Brink of Constitutional Crisis--Ex-U.S. Envoy
Body_Text:
Nigeria is on the brink of constitutional crisis with its ailing president not transferring powers to his deputy and political king makers feuding over his succession, a senior lawyer and a former U.S. envoy have warned.
President Umaru Yar'Adua has been absent from Africa's most populous nation for more than a month receiving treatment for a heart condition in Saudi Arabia, but there have been no official updates on his health for weeks.
Vice President Goodluck Jonathan has been presiding over cabinet meetings but executive powers have not officially been transferred to him, leading to questions over the legality of decisions made by the government in Yar'Adua's absence.
The Nigerian Bar Association, NBA, the umbrella body of all lawyers in the country, has launched legal action against the Attorney-General, asking a top court to declare that Yar'Adua has violated the constitution by omitting to transfer powers.
"We are saying there is a duty on the president to do it, it is not discretionary ... We cannot continue this way, we are not running a banana republic," NBA President, Rotimi Akeredolu told Reuters in a telephone interview on Thursday.
"We are treading a very dangerous path and we have to be very careful. We are trying to paper over a few cracks ... but if we are not careful the whole building will collapse," he said.
The NBA's legal action follows a similar suit already brought by prominent human rights lawyer, Femi Falana.
It also adds to a crisis in the judiciary triggered by the swearing in on Wednesday of a new chief justice, the first time in the country's history the Head of State has been absent for the ceremony and an act some senior lawyers say is illegal.

Original_Search_Title: Anambra 2010 Political Analysis
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra 2010 Political Analysis
Body_Text:
Having had deep thought and reflections on the past, and present petty political maneuverings in my state Anambra, the coast appears to be clearing for 1yom Uche Lilian Ekwunife the Progressive Peoples Alliance candidate for Anambra gubernatorial election.
To elucidate accurately on Anambra political calculus, subject to what is on the ground, it is necessary to dissect the political chances of the main political gladiators carrying the flags of various political parties viz, Action Congress, All Progressive Grand Alliance, Peoples Democratic Party, Labour Party and Progressive Peoples Alliance.
The chances of Dr. Chris Ngige winning the next gubernatorial election in Anambra State may appear bright but the truth remains that it may-be counter productive for Anambraians to entrust their lives on' him again because common sense predicts that having served thirty three (33) months under pressures from the P.D.P renegades and fighting the battles of his life then, which made him to embrace the masses as his last hope chances are there that when pressures no longer exist he will not perform as he ',did in the past in road construction(only) , because his perception will now be, that he will work at his pace (like our present Governor Peter Obi) and deliver half measured dividends of democracy belatedly, at a time graduate unemployment has become bloated leading to upsurge in graduates armed robbery gangs and kidnappers.
Additionally, it may be right to predict that if Anambraians entrust their life on Dr. Chris Ngige, the fight between his group and the Uba family (who projected him in 2003 and forced him on Anambraians) will continue, and this will lead to Anarchy and disintegration in governance.
.The crisis and confusion presently pervading in Anambra State Peoples Democratic Party will make Prof. Chukwuma Charles Soludo regret joining the gubernatorial race coming up in February 6, 2010. Peoples Democratic Party being in tatters in Anambra State and eighty (80) percent of P.D.P Party stalwarts vigorously fighting Soludo's candidacy in P.D.P, it is very likely Soludo / Anosike ticket will fail woefully and Soludo will regret why coming out in the first place? Reports in the media has indicated that bickering has erupted on who heads his campaign organization between group loyal to Soludo and group loyal to Anosike. This portends danger for Anambra State if' Soludo/Anosike ticket eventually succeed, but it is very likely they will not succeed because Soludo's enemies in P.1).13 are vast and varied.
It is predicted that Soludo will eventually be betrayed by those he trusted so much and he is advised to look round thoroughly before leaping because banana peels are laid as red carpet for him to march on.
It is also consequential that if Soludo is eventually allowed by the courts to contest, the chances are there, that Pastor. (Prince) Nicholas Ukachukwu (whose campaign structure during the P.D.P delegates primaries was unstoppable) and senator Annie Okonkwo (who was likely to have a run-off with "Ikukuoma" Prince Nicholas Ukachukwu then) will be Soludo's albatross. These duo who felt most cheated during the P.D.P. gubernatorial primaries will fight Soludo to a standstill even for a four years term and this definitely will bring down the business of governance and create anarchy for the State. Analysts are of the opinion that Anambraians are not gullible to entrench themselves in such a web.
The present Governor of Anambra State, Mr. Peter Obi, may be rejected by Anambra voters for the following reasons, namely: quality of projects and infrastructure provided by his government is inferior to other developmental projects carried out by previous administrations in Anambra State, e.g. roads constructed by his government are ho-hum and. nothing to write home about. Mr. Peter Obi's inability to control the security situation in Anambra has made him a total failure in the business of governing Anambra. It is very likely Peter Obi will be rejected at the Polls, because APC'TA party faithful who supported his campaign in 2003 were snubbed rebuffed, rejected, disgraced and regarded as sycophants when Peter Obi ascended the throne, therefore this will definitely be his Achilles' heel which means he will smell defeat and may not bother going to any tribunal when he finds out that he took the 41h position in the gubernatorial race. The current Labour Party candidate for Anambra State, Mr. Areh is not heard of as a political heavy weight though it is believed in many quarters he is holding fort for Andy Uba. Be it as it may, I will advise my highly respected Andy Uba to tarry a bit for at least four years to allow wounds of the past to heal as Dr. Chris Ngige will capitalize on his entering the terrain to seek sympathy votes, which actualized will likely lead to revenge on past bickering which will further lead to another unending physical and political war that will make Anambra State another "Mogadishu" in Africa.
The Progressive Peoples Alliance candidate for Anambra State Iyom Uche Lilian Ekwunife so far is the most credible and non controversial gubernatorial candidate with the proper and focused vision to guide Anambra State to the promised land. A close watch on the activities of this young woman portends that she is a woman gifted to leadership and extraordinary kindness. Various contributions she made in the house as a member of House of Representatives portends good omen for Anambra State as she has always been fanatical about issues relating to improvement of quality of lives of Anambraians and correctional values to the problems of erosion menace in Anambra State. She also has the most vibrant and practical methodology on improving infrastructure within the confines of Anambra abundant resources. Her ideas on developmental growth for Anambra State is realizable devoid of exaggerations that are unattainable, and her insistence on eradicating armed robbery and kidnapping through a water tight strategy that will be confounding to criminals, shows her seriousness to govern well. It is also to be noted that this noble Amazon of Anambra State is working tirelessly to surpass the achievements of Dr. (Mrs.) Okonjo-Iweala whose brain and ideas achieved a lot for Nigeria, including freeing Nigeria from imperialistic economic enslavement. Additionally, Mrs. Ekwunife's quiet and peaceful disposition will reduce tension to the barest minimum in Anambra State without being unconcerned or weak in matters of firmness in taking tough and good decisions to benefit Anambra State. It is strongly believed that women and majority of the youths will support her candidacy to bring the desired change in Anambra State. She is a silent worker who campaigns tirelessly till mid night. She may spring a surprise.
Nwabufo is a National Co-ordinator, Anambra Lawyers For Good Governance.

Original_Search_Title: Abducted by the Lord's Resistance Army
Headline: Central Africa: Abducted by the Lord's Resistance Army
Body_Text:
Nairobi — This week Oxfam is releasing a new report on the growing threat of the Lord’s Resistance Army, central Africa’s most brutal and longest running rebel movement. Countless youth and children have been killed or abducted. Francois* was just 17 when he was abducted by the LRA last year from his tiny village in northeast DRC. He was kept for five months before he managed to escape:
“I’m from a very small village. One morning the LRA came and took me and a boy from another family. They gave my mother and father 1,000 Francs each (about $1) and said they needed our help. At first we thought they might be the Ugandan army.
As we walked, they tied our hands tight with rope. We walked for the whole day and ate raw cassava straight from the ground. When we got to their camp they put oil on our faces, knees and elbows. It was a ritual to “cleanse” us and stop us thinking about home and where we came from. This place was supposed to be our new home.
Soon after, they attacked a village. A woman there ran away so they shot her dead. There were nine of them in our group and each had a gun, but they didn’t have any spare – they told me that they were looking for new guns so they could give me one. If I got a gun they said I could fight with them, not just walk.
There were long days of walking through endless forest and fields. We walked all the way into south Sudan. It was all about day-to-day survival – we were always looking for food and the next day’s meal.
Sometimes they would attack other villages. In the five months I was with them, they abducted another three children and six women. The women had to cook and were made their wives. Some of them got pregnant. Some had babies with them when they were taken.
We were chased many times by the Ugandan army (UPDF), but we always managed to escape. Another group of LRA who we sometimes met up with got into a fight with the UPDF and many people were killed.
Without a gun, my job was mostly as a guard. I had to watch the roads and alert them if I saw soldiers coming. I was left alone a lot and many times I thought of trying to escape, but I was too scared. I always thought they would catch me.
Eventually, one day I decided to try and escape. I don’t know what was different about that day, but I just ran and didn’t stop until I reached a village. From there I made my way home. Everyone was so happy to see me – they thought I was dead.
Before the abduction I was going to school – I was in the Fifth Form. But not now. I would like to go back though. Getting money for school fees is difficult. I make some money through occasional daily labour and collecting thatch for the roofs of people’s huts – but it is never enough.”

Original_Search_Title: Govt Subsidizes Bread After Unrest
Headline: Mozambique: First Bread Subsidies As From Tuesday
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican government has promised that the first bread subsidies will be sent to the country's bakers as from Tuesday.
The promise to subsidise bread was one of the main government responses to the riots against price rises that shook Maputo on 1-2 September.
The subsidy arrangement was formalised on Saturday in an agreement signed by the Ministry of Industry and Trade and the Mozambican Bakers' Association. Registered bakers will receive a subsidy of 200 meticais (about 5.6 US dollars) for every 50 kilo sack of wheat flour they receive.
Thus the milling companies will continue to receive the full 1,050 meticais for every sack sold, but the bakers will only pay 850 meticais.
Industry and Trade Minister Antonio Fernando admitted that the subsidy will require a major financial effort from the government, but he did not give any exact figure for the sums involved.
He also called on the bakers to take other measures to make bread cheaper, such as mixing cassava flour with wheat flour. Some bakers are already doing this, notably in the northern province of Nampula.
Fernando warned bakers not to cheat their customers by reducing the weight of the loaves they sell. "We shall be inspecting all of this", he said.
The chairperson of the Bakers' Association, Victor Miguel, congratulated the government on what he regarded as "a wise and responsible decision", and promised that his members would implement government decisions to the letter.
The subsidy is being channeled through the Association, and so any bakers who are not registered with the association will not receive the money.

Original_Search_Title: NMJD Scolds African Minerals
Headline: Sierra Leone: NMJD Scolds African Minerals
Body_Text:
Freetown — I was outraged when I read an article entitled "NAHIM KHADI'S RACIST ARROGANCE AND THE CASE AGAINST GIVING FULL CITIZENSHIP TO THE LEBANESE" in the Torchlight Newspaper, owned by the Press Secretary to His Excellency and my good friend and brother, Shekito Tarawallie.
After reading this article and before even cooling down, I was incensed by another opinion piece in Cocorioko, entitled "Reviewing the Citizenship Law: A warning" in which the writer alleges that all Lebanese have failed to integrate and treat Sierra Leoneans as second class citizens.  Cocorioko is a pro-government website whose publisher and CEO is Wilfred Kabs-Kanu, is also Minister Plenipotentiary to the Sierra Leone Permanent Mission to the United Nations in New York and Coordinator of the Africa Union Committee of 10. Both article made me ponder about the question, "Is Sierra Leone a racist nation?"
I suspect the two articles are in reaction to recent stories of Nasser Ayoub's threat to go on hunger strike and Samir Hassaniyeh's comments that our citizenship laws are discriminatory against the Lebanese. I must confess that though in a similar positions as Hassaniyeh and Ayoub (who Hassaniyeh says is a Palestinian), yet I will not be following the latter's threat of hunger strike because I cannot live without my favourite cassava leaves and potato leaves (especially the potato leaves wanpot which my wife cooks so well) for more than a couple of days.
I also do not believe that any violation of my citizenship rights in Sierra Leone has reached the level suffered by Mandela, Gandhi or Martin Luther King to warrant a hunger strike. Hassaniyeh was wrong to suggest that the citizenship laws are only discriminatory against Lebanese. They are discriminatory against all persons not of negro African descent who qualify for Sierra Leone citizenship.
The writer of the Cocorioko piece which is also published in the online edition of the Government owned Daily Mail is probably an American national.  Imagine the hullabaloo if the West had laws similar to the Sierra Leone law that restrict citizenship on the basis of race? The writer erroneously accuses the Lebanese of fighting tooth and nail to oust Nir Guaz aka Skipper.
This is baloney. Skipper was fought by the Momoh and Bambay Kamara because they did not want to pay him his money he had loaned them. The Lebanese Community was benefiting considerably from Skipper and his excessive pricing of diamonds. There was a state of emergency at the time and Skipper had loaned the Government money. The very Government then arrested him and charged him for violating the State of Emergency laws. What did the Lebanese have to do with that? How is this relevant to the issue of citizenship?
The writer also suggests that most bi-racial Lebanese are of Lebanese fathers and black mothers and in distorted and twisted logic concluded that it only means blacks are "meant to be under them and not above them." That is absolute hogwash and is insulting to someone like me who is married to a Sierra Leonean.  The writer's contention is again totally inaccurate.
There are example abound. Lamin Bangura is married to Justice Vivian Solomon, DIG Richard Moigue is married to Olga Solomon, Tunde scott is married to one IFTONI, the late M.B.Sesay and Dr Chernor Jalloh, former Minister of Tourism and Culture under President Kabbah, are all good examples. The writer has obviously not been to the provinces were most of the Lebanese and Syrians populations are fully integrated. The fact that it does not happen more often is not because all Lebanese are racists. There are some people amongst other groups like the Fullahs and krios who also prefer their
daughters to marry their kinsmen. I do not hear anyone complain about some Indians in Sierra Leone who also prefer their children not to inter marry. Does that make them racist? No!  But the writer only attacks the Lebanese.
The writer further states "that there is grave threat of extremism from the Arab population which they could import into our peaceful, co-existence, and religious tolerance communities". He goes on further to allege "that the Lebanese Community was helping Hezbollah and was also involved in buying diamonds for Al-Qaeda. Sierra Leoneans must remember one thing about the Arabs or Lebanese; they could shed blood for anything against Israeli interest.
Giving certain rights to these middle-easterners might bread a Sunni-Shia religious sect in Sierra Leone and a platform for an invisible anti-Israeli and anti-Western interests under a 'snail-work' agenda."
This is gibberish of the highest order and is extremely insular, incomprehensible and inciting. Middle-Easterners including Lebanese, Syrians and Jordanians have been in Sierra Leone since 1893. Most of those who arrived were Maronite Christians. Prominent Lebanese politicians like Druze leader Walid Jumblatt grew up in Sierra Leone.
There has not been any disturbance in Sierra Leone caused by Lebanese. On the contrary the contributions of many afro-Lebanese, like John and Edward Akar and Farid Raymond Anthony, have been acknowledged by many. The writer broadens his attack not just to Lebanese but to all Arabs. What has giving citizenship to non-negros got to do with the shedding blood for Isreali interests?
