A political analyst observed at the weekend that curiously, while President Jonathan had all the while been silent in the face of certain political comments made against him in the campaign process for the presidential ticket of the ruling party, he had no choice than to come out straight this time around to call a spade a spade by telling Abubakar to his face that the safety and well being of Nigerians must not be sacrificed on the altar of personal political ambition.
"Safe for his campaign team that had always tried in their own way to absolve the President of every allegation made against him, ranging from incompetence to the country's economy, Jonathan would hardly come out to react to damning comments against his presidential ambition. But he is said to have done it this time around because the security of Nigerians is at stake and he owe them a duty to protect them as their president", he said, just as he pleaded anonymity.
As observed further, last Thursday was the very first time President Jonathan received a standing ovation at a PDP NEC meeting after addressing the issue of inevitability of violent change as propounded by Abubakar.
For once, Jonathan had proved that he was not a feeble minded President who lacks the will power to assert himself. He proved wrong those who had been mistaking his humility for incompetence; at least, he made them understand that when a lion runs and look back, it is not out of fear. It is to enable him gauge how grounds he has covered.
After all the mudslinging targeted at battering his image by some political element, The President proved that he too was a good gambler. He made use of the right time to ask Nigerians to cross check properly whether Abubakar actually had the leadership qualities in him.
Challenging Abubkar's threat of violent change, the President spoke with the ferocity of a wounded lion while also addressing the 55th National Executive Committee (NEC) meeting of the PDP. The President warned against making treasonable statements. According to him, when the government moves against those making such comments, nobody should complain of being persecuted.
He said, "I am saying so because the utterances of some of us are not very palatable to the ears. And government would no longer tolerate a situation where an individual or group of individuals will threaten the sovereignty and stability of this country.
"If people make statements that are treasonable they will face the law. And nobody should say somebody is being persecuted because of politics.
"That is why I am pleading with the Chairman (of PDP), probably we should have a Code of Conduct for those of us who are aspiring for offices to guide our utterances, what we say and what we do.
"Sometimes, I frown at some people making statements, especially those of us politicians who are making statements challenging the sovereignty of this nation. Government will no longer take that kind of rubbish lightly".
Irked by this development, the appropriate authority saddled with the responsibility of handling matters of security, the State Security Service (SSS) came out to warn politicians against making unguarded statements capable of undermining and subverting the stability of Nigeria.
A statement, signed by public relations Officer of the service Ms Marilyn Ogar, stated: "For some time now, the State Security Service has noted with grave concern the unguarded, reckless and divisive utterances of some prominent politicians. The Service has also noted that these utterances, apart from being very inflammatory and extremely inciting are of deep and grave National Security concern.
"We are again reminded that, democracy is about dialogue, negotiation and compromise. However, a situation where some of those aspiring to rule this Nation, have resorted through their utterances to make "the country ungovernable", or calling for "a violent change", is very despicable and unacceptable to this Service. This, to this Service is beyond politics.
"Accordingly, the State Security Service wishes to seriously warn all politicians to henceforth desist from making unguarded statements, which have the capacity to undermine and subvert the stability of the Nation. For the avoidance of doubt, democracy is about the rule of law.
Accordingly, appropriate sanctions will be brought to bear heavily on any person found violating this warning".
But a defiant Abubakar fired back at the federal government, saying he was just waiting in the wings to be arrested. Reacting to the President's threat to deal decisively with treason last night said he is ready for arrest, Abubakar said tolerance for dissenting views and opponents remains an integral component of democracy.
A statement by his campaign organisation said: "Atiku is a democrat who has at all times toed the path of constitutionality and rule of law in advancing the cause of democracy and therefore can no not be recommending a path that is at variance with his long life struggles to deepen democracy in Nigeria.
"His comment at the National Stakeholders' Conference on Wednesday was borne out of historical reality when the late US President John F.Kennedy warned against the consequences of making peaceful change impossible. "The statement should be taken in its historical context in terms of bitter experiences injustice has caused around the world, and as evidenced in the looming crisis in Cote d'Ivoire. As a responsible and dignified statesman, Atiku will never resort to scare-mongering or any anti-social action which can undermine Nigeria's co-existence."
The questions yawning for answers on the lips of political pundit are: What violent change then would Abubakar recommend and consent to or propogate?
Does he believe that without Him, everything done in Nigeria is wrong?
To this effect, a public analyst, Elvis Okugbo is of the opinion that there is none above the law, not even Abubakar and his likes and if they choose to commit treason they should be decisively be dealt with.
He said, "I was in fact very surprised to read Atiku's comments and even more surprised that nothing was initially done. Atiku and his co-travellers should be made to know that Nigeria is not a banana republic.
"As long as I am concern, Atiku is a forgotten issue, the reason why he sees himself as a leader is because Obasanjo mistakenly took him as a Vice President. Whether you believe me or not, Atiku does not know anything.
He is a Nigerian with no leadership quality or common sense of belonging; rather he believes that money buys anything, not knowing that his ill gotten money cannot buy him the president of Nigeria. His money can only buy him all the properties he has acquired, but not our President".
For Wale Kayo, a social commentator In most cases, those beating war drums have nothing to lose. "Their families are not in Nigeria and those living in Nigeria can be evacuated before you know what is happening.
No common man will fight for these selfish politicians. What is the place of violence in modern day politics? Will their children lead the way when it comes to actualising the violent change?" he queried.
Kayo further said, "President Jonathan should not be treated as a second class leader whose office can be disrespected because of his relatively quiet nature. He is free to contest the election like any other Nigerian and if Nigerians believe in him and show this by supporting him, there is no crime if he becomes elected in 2011.
"Our politicians need to borrow a leaf from Buhari. With the level of support and respect he commands far and near, he has not been regional, arrogant, overbearing or making statements that may heat up the polity. What we need is peace!
Another social commentator, Rev Timothy Okhomhen, said Abubakar is a very desperate man who wants to be President by all means. "We remember what happened in 2006 and 2007 as a result of his desperation for power.
He joined forces with ACN and uttered all manners of utterances against the PDP and the then administration which he was a part of. Why has he now left ACN to PDP he earlier condemned?
Nigerians should be wise and beware of politicians like him. They are wolves in sheep clothing. This is the leader that wants to rule Nigeria, Atiku, singing violence". He said.
Abubakar is also said to have shot the Adamu Ciroma led consensus committee which chose him as the northern consensus presidential candidate on the foot.
Even from his own camp, many observers are lamenting that while the members of the said committee are busy trying to convince Northerners to accept the choice made for them for the 2011 presidential poll, Abubakar is busy destroying the package the northern consensus promoters, like a sucking child who keeps biting its mother on the breast.
Most northerners are also wondering what members of this committee that claim they are championing zoning in Nigeria have to offer? For some of them, it is now clear that they are out for selfish interest, as they appear to part of the stumbling blocks towards a new Nigeria.
Last week, Kano State Governor and presidential aspirant of the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP), Malam Ibrahim Shekarau, described the process that led to the emergence of former Vice- President Atiku Abubakar as the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) consensus candidate of the North as the biggest fraud ever perpetrated in the country.
He said he was not bothered by the selection of Abubakar as the PDP north's candidate in the 2011 elections because it was a decision of nine men and not that of the entire North.
"I am not bothered by the issue of consensus candidate as that does not reflect the wishes of the entire north. The issue of the consensus candidate of the north is the biggest fraud ever perpetrated in this country as they do not have the support of the entire north," Shekarau had said.
A presidency source has said last week that it is obvious that Abubakar would not take that misguided utterance, if he were the sitting President. I think Atiku is going too far in his renewed political desperation.
"Atiku's high-sounding rhetoric would have been more meaningful, if he was himself not a leading member of ancient regime that the mass of the Nigerian people are determined to oust once and for all."
But Abubkar maintained his ground that he was not over charging the polity. Instead, he blamed it all on the President and his actions. According to him, accusing him of causing tension is like putting the saddle on the wrong horse.
He said, "No Nigerian leader in recent memory has caused so much bitterness, division, suspicions and animosity among Nigerians like President Goodluck Jonathan because of his ambition to run in 2011".
Abubkar recalled that a serving minister in the Jonathan administration once threatened that Nigeria could face dire consequences, if President Jonathan was forced out of the presidential race. He said a president that tore the PDP apart and caused so much bitterness within his own ruling party because of his ill-advised ambition should not turn round and accuse anyone else of causing tension.
The statement recalled that with previous scheming to hang a badge of infamy on the necks of his political opponents over the October 1, 2010 bomb blasts, the latest attacks on Atiku is a typical desperate measure to divert attention and blame opponents for causing tension in the country.
But while Abubakar stood his ground, he got another bombshell. 20PDP governors last Thursday endorsed President Jonathan's bid for the ruling party's presidential ticket with their signature boldly signed beside their names.
The 20 governors who signed the communiqué are:
Ibrahim Idris (Kogi), Babangida Aliyu (Niger), Godswill Akpabio (Akwa Ibom), Akwe Doma (Nasarawa), Timipre Sylva (Bayelsa), Gabriel Suswan (Benue), Rotimi Amaech (Rivers), Liyel Imoke (Cross River), Patrick Yakowa (Kaduna), Gbenga Daniel (Ogun), Isa Yuguda (Bauchi), Ibrahim Shema (Katsina), Ikedi Ohakim (Imo), Murtala Nyako (Adamawa), Saidu Dakingari ( Kebbi), Adebayo Alao-Akala (Oyo), Mahmud Shinkafi (Zamfara), Jonah Jang (Plateau), Danjuma Goje (Gombe) and Danbaba Suntai (Taraba).
The governors stated in a communiqué after endorsing the president thus: At the emergency meeting of PDP governors held Thursday 16th day of December, 2010 and having deliberated extensively on democratic practices the world over observed as follows: Democratic systems all over the world recognise the principle of incumbency and continuity.
"Entrenched democratic culture persistent in presidential system and our constitution entitles our President to run for a second term which the PDP governors support.
The governors also recognise the Yar'Adua/Jonathan ticket and therefore hereby support and endorse President Goodluck Ebele Jonathan to contest the 2011 election".
Barely 24 hours after the governors made their position known, Abubakar reacted; saying the endorsement by the governors is to sufficient enough to win the primaries even as he warned the governors not to intimidate the delegates expected to vote at the PDP primaries.
But political analysts say they find the response of Abubakar's media men to their principal's utterances to be silly and un-intelligent.
"If Atiku, a vice president in his desperation to dislodge Jonathan openly threatens to unleash violence against his country, the least his media men as better educated people should do is to show Nigerians some respect by issuing moderated clarifications and not to blunder along with him.
What is tolerance to dissenting voices when the issue is threat of violence to the corporate existence of the country", one of them contended.
As the year 2010 inches closer to the end and with the gradual dawning of the electoral year, 2011, Nigerians are watching. In the political arena, the drums are beating, the flutes are singing and the spectators are holding their breath.
How Abubakar will wriggle out of the mess he has put himself in the presidential race before the PDP primaries is a political miracle and abracadabra political observers say they are waiting to see.

Original_Search_Title: It is Doubtful any Mechanism Outside ICC Would Bring Justice
Headline: Kenya: It is Doubtful any Mechanism Outside ICC Would Bring Justice
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Lest we forget, the most important audience Luis Moreno-Ocampo is playing for are the victims of post-election violence, particularly the IDPs.
They are the primarily the people for whom justice is being sought. Everything else should be secondary. It is easy to overlook this fact because of the eruption of political emotion since the prosecutor named his List of Six. I entirely sympathise with this emotion insofar as Kenya as a state has now appeared on its own List of Shame among the countries in which the ICC has seen fit to intervene.
We may not be a failed state, but the entry of the ICC leaves a huge question mark about our country. As our politicians go bananas, let us pause and remember who is to blame.
Intervention
It bears repeating that we had all the chances to redeem ourselves by setting up a special tribunal that would have forestalled Mr Moreno-Ocampo's intervention. We failed to rise to the occasion.
Nor can one seriously argue that, even without such a mechanism, our existing laws were incapable of bringing justice to the victims who suffered murder, rape and the loss of the property they had worked for in their entire lives.
Let's face it: we were never serious. But here is the nub: all this mess would not have occurred if the governing authority, right before the election, had learned to take the business of political management seriously. Extremely dangerous ethnic divisions were stoked around the time of the 2005 referendum. Hate speech became the norm.
Rather than seek to tackle these matters, there was a callously careless attitude at the top. We saw the results in 2007-2008. Among those on the List of Six are men who genuinely feel they are where they are now because the machinery of state went silent at the hour its voice was needed most by the victims of the violence.
It is not Mr Moreno-Ocampo who is to blame for the destruction of their careers and reputations. It is the governing authority that failed to play its constitutional role. The current anti-ICC venom that is coming from politicians seeks to protect the List of Six, not deliver justice to the ordinary victims. That is the scandalous bit.
Three years after the violence, not a single perpetrator has been convicted. Three years hence, IDPs still live in squalor in camps. Sovereignty is a good thing to have, but not if it becomes a barrier to justice for the victims who suffered most. As it is, we should have grown used to being non-sovereign ever since we bungled our elections in 2007.
Mr Moreno-Ocampo is only one manifestation among many. There was Kofi Annan and his Eminent Persons Group. There were others like Johann Kriegler and Philip Alston. The governing authority would grumble about sovereignty in the face of their prescriptions. But it would go ahead and follow them nonetheless.
A special tribunal to try foot-soldiers of the violence would still be a good thing, even at this late hour when it is obvious that it is being floated only to torpedo the ICC cases.
Torpedo
But from the record of indifference to the suffering of the IDPs this government has shown, I seriously doubt whether any mechanism it fronts will ever deliver justice to those in need. Maybe we'll need something else.
It is painful whenever I hear citizens of other African countries being warned against going the Kenyan way when those countries brace for elections. Ghanaians were in 2008.
Even Robert Mugabe, en route to his own controversial re-election, went on national TV to sternly warn against any Kenyan-style violence. And, true enough, it didn't happen. That's how low our country has sunk; if not quite a joke, then an object of pity nonetheless.

Original_Search_Title: Stars, Cranes in Tough Battle
Headline: Tanzania/Uganda: Stars, Cranes in Tough Battle
Body_Text:
MAINLAND'S Kilimanjaro Stars will be aiming to go a step closer towards their first Cecafa Senior Challenge Cup silverware after almost 16, when they take on defending Champions Uganda in the second semifinal of the regional tournament on Friday evening.
The Stars-Cranes duel, due to kick off at 5pm, will be preceded by an exciting opening semifinal clash between guests Ivory Coast and Ethiopia, kicking off from 3pm.
Kilimanjaro Stars powered into the semifinals after edging out Rwanda 1-0 in a quarterfinal match played under heavy downpour in Dar es Salaam on Wednesday, hours after Uganda had beaten Zanzibar Heroes 5-3 on post match penalties.
Stars last won the tournament in 1994 after beating Uganda Cranes in the final; hence the 2010 Cecafa Challenge Cup has been a challenge for them. Since an opening game defeat to Zambia, Coach Jan Poulsen's team has been forced to fight for its life as they try to restore the country's pride in the region.
Poulsen knows his side must shoulder the burden of expectations from home supporters who are expected to pack the 60,000-seating capacity National Stadium and cheer their boys up.
It is one of the toughest hurdle for the host team as they face up record champions, the Cranes, who are fired up to lift their third trophy in a row, having won the 2008 and 2009 editions in Kampala and Nairobi respectively.
Stars need to be at their best for the entire 90-minutes to have any chances of going through to the final. There is no room for a slow start as they did in Wednesday's quarterfinal against Rwanda.
Wednesday's was another typical nail-biter, with both sides missing several and the Stars only pulling out the win with Shadrack Nsajigwa's spot kick at the 62nd minute. It is waited to see if Poulsen will once again change his starting line-up and tactics.
In his last two matches, Poulsen started without a recognized striker with Nurdin Bakari and Mrisho Ngasa deployed in advanced positions as the coach sought to win the midfield battle.
The formation bore fruit against Burundi, with Bakari notching two goals that put Stars through, but the Danish tactician was forced into changing the system after a doggy first half against Rwanda, and introduced striker John Bocco in the place of midfielder Henry Joseph just after the restart.
The changes paid dividends as Bocco won the penalty, which eventually gave Stars a 1-0 victory and a place in the semis. Stars is likely to try and control the middle again like they did in the second half against Rwanda, and hope to come up with some creative offense that way.
Henry Joseph and Shabaan Nditi will battle it out with Ugandan midfield maestro Tonny Maweijje and Patrick Ochan for supremacy in the middle of the pack. Mohamed Banka is a major doubt after picking up a groin injury against Rwanda.
Uganda is likely to play a more passive counterattacking game, at least in the first half. They will definitely try and get the ball to Emmanuel Okwi and Dan Wagaluka as much as possible.
The game promises an interesting battle between Stars' defence led by Kelvin Yondan, Juma Nyoso, Shadrack Nsajigwa, and Stephano Mwasika who will come up against familiar Ugandan front men Okwi, Wagaluka, Henry Kisseka and Robert Ssentongo should he start.
Ivory Coast have emerged as one of the favourites to win this year's championship but face a banana skin test as they take on an entertaining Ethiopian side in the opening semifinal. Ethiopia's 2-1 quarterfinal win over favourites Zambia proved their credentials and ability to make it to the final.
They are making up for the comparative lack of physical strength with impressive technical ability. Their passing and movement is a delight to watch and it is no wonder they have won many fans from Tanzania.
Bekele Shimeles Godo has been the star of the team so far scoring three of their four goals. The diminutive striker is blessed with pace and ability to beat his man and can also take wonderful free-kicks.
There is quality around him with Omod Okwury Omod, Zinabu Showaatir and Samson Mulugata also playing a vital role. For the Ivorians, the Kipre twins will again play a huge role.
One of them; Tchetche Kipre came off the bench to score twice last time out and may yet get the nod to start while Kone Zoumana will again be lurking in the box eager to take advantage and increase on his two goal tally.

Original_Search_Title: Pablo - Adult Achievers Award
Headline: Uganda: Pablo - Adult Achievers Award
Body_Text:
The second edition of the Young Achievers awards was a glamorous event worth every penny.
Attendance was by invite, something that rarely happens in a banana republic. Being young is good. You know that you are aging when your life ceases to thrive on hope and depends on remembrance.
When every sentence you say begins with, "I remember we used to..." know that you are finished. That's why they say that memories are treasures nobody can steal. No thief is interested in them.
There is a need to massage our forefathers' egos if we are to keep hope alive; otherwise, remembrance might become an infectious syndrome. So, without any further ado about nothing at all, allow me to present to you the first ever Adult Achievers awards.
Journalist of the century award
This award goes to none other than John Nagenda. He knows the dictionary the way the Pope knows the rosary. Very few people understand the first paragraph of his articles. He is a prolific writer whose choice of words leaves you wondering whether you went to school.
He must be a great inspiration to the person who comes up with the titles of The Ebonies' plays. Titles like, 'excruciating conundrum,' 'inextricable dilemma,' 'enigmatic palpitation' ...
Time keeping award
Prime Minister Apolo Robin Nsibambi takes it all. Unreliable sources have it that he wakes up before he sleeps. You make an appointment to meet him at your own risk. Excuses like being delayed in traffic or blaming the weather don't exist in his world. He is John Nagenda's connoisseur. He's also a moving encyclopaedia. That explains the nickname 'Lugubrious.'
Lifetime achievement award
It goes to the minister of Education and Sports, Hon Namirembe Bitamazire. I have a feeling she must have seen the Dead Sea when it was still sick. She has served in almost all governments. Surprisingly, the older she grows, the younger she looks. I have never seen her with grey hair. Her humility and wisdom are what have kept her in form.
Longest serving MP award
Hon Henry Muganwa Kajura. He must know every worker in Parliament by their first name. He has diligently served the people of Bunyoro to a point that he can freely meditate about it in the middle of a parliamentary session. The editors of the Hansard should use him as back-up in case some minutes go missing.
Unbwogable award
Hon Kahinda Otafiire. He's among the few people who call a spade a spade and not a big spoon. He once said he can never be a musician at any point or think about that nonsense. Red Pepper, RDC Fred Bamwine and Godfrey Nyakana are witness to his verbal artillery. The general doesn't mince words.
Activist award
This goes to none other than Dr Miria Rukoza Koburunga Matembe. She doesn't need a microphone when dealing with reproductive health issues. She's always loud and clear. She needs to meet the Al Shabaab and give them a piece of her mind.

Original_Search_Title: Mark- Era of Unity, Stability in Senate
Headline: Nigeria: Mark- Era of Unity, Stability in Senate
Body_Text:
Abuja — When recently, former Senate President Anyim Pius Anyim showered encomiums on Senator David Mark, the  incumbent President of the Senate, not a few watchers of the activities of the upper legislative chamber agreed with Anyim’s observations.
Leading a delegation of the Full Gospel Businessmen’s Fellowship International to Mark’s office, Anyim had commended Mark for leading a stable and united Senate in the past three years and also providing a purposeful leadership that has brought stability to the Red Chamber and the national polity as a whole.  There is no gainsaying the fact that since the inception of the present democratic dispensation, never has the country witnessed such display of unity of purpose among federal lawmakers as in the past three years since Mark has taken charge.
Observers believe this is not just an accident bearing in mind the democratic manner of Mark’s emergence as Senate President, which seriously contrasted with the old practice of clique and cult-like politicking that produced most of his predecessors.Unlike most of his predecessors in the 4th and 5th Senate, who were either puppets of the executive or mindlessly antagonistic to it, Mark benefited from the fact that he emerged  at a time a non-meddling, non-arrogant man as a president. Thus, while asserting and maintaining the independence of the Senate, Mark also avoided pitching the upper legislature against the executive in a needless and rash antagonism. Observers believe this, coupled with Mark’s focused leadership and humble mien, are responsible for the unity and stability that are the hallmarks of the present Senate. Mark’s emergence on June 5, 2007 defied the norm that had been the trend since the inauguration of the Fourth Republic in May 1999.
Hitherto, the presiding officers of the federal legislature often emerged through a combination of bizarre internal political permutation and horse trading within the ruling party and sustained on the whims of the executive. In other words, the party caucus, both within and outside the parliament was mainly responsible for determining who presides over the legislature irrespective of the candidate’s political experience, administrative sagacity and human relations. Also, the tenure of such imposed candidate was often at the mercy and pleasure of the President. But a measure of internal democracy came to play in Mark’s emergence. Thus, it could be said that the outlook of the Senate under Mark’s leadership in the past three and half years has been an unmistakable reflection of the manner of his emergence after a keenly contested open election on the floor of the Senate. His victory over his opponent, a fellow Benue senator, George Akume, was remarkable, as two contenders to the seat, Senators Gbemi Saraki  (PDP, Kwara) and Nuhu Aliyu (PDP, Niger), had earlier stepped down for Akume in a bid to turn the tables against Mark at the election. But at the end of the day, Mark won with 69 votes to Akume’s 39.
After the election, Mark promptly initiated a healing programme whereby he cemented the bond of camaraderie among all senators irrespective of party leaning and camp affiliation during the Senate presidency election.  The unprecedented unity and stability that prevail in the Senate now is thus not a surprise to keen political observers, as virtually all the senators willingly pledged and indeed gave their unflinching support to the new leader.
Not a man to take his colleague’s goodwill for granted, Mark set off on a clean slate with “open-door” policy as his policy compass. He promptly abolished the kitchen cabinet or cabal system of past leadership where the Senate President surrounds himself with a coterie of courtiers who often advise him wrongly based on their selfish interests. The Senate President now promotes a system whereby every senator, ranking or fresh, his party men or opposition elements, has free access to him either in his office or inside the chamber.  He also put in place a system where round pegs are deliberately put in round holes in committee appointments. This is in contra-distinction with the rash politicking of the past where committee chairmanship and membership appointments were instruments of political patronage rather than based on proven ability and proficiency in the relevant committee assignments.
Observers believe that all these naturally beget respect and high regard for the Senate President by his colleagues whom he often addresses as “my bosses” at plenary sessions. This bond of unity in the upper legislative house has also made it impossible for external infiltration within the polity to cause unnecessary division within its ranks and destabilization of its set goals. It is generally believed that Mark’s result-oriented style of leadership, which is largely responsible for his side-stepping the fearsome proverbial “banana peel” that had brought the tenure of many of his predecessors to abrupt, premature and unceremonious ends, is a product of vast administrative experience, spanning over three decades and garnered both in the military and even as a senator these past 11 years. Mark, a former military tactician has been a great influence in not only stabilizing the Senate this past three years plus, but also in engendering an excellent relationship between the upper legislative chamber and the executive arm.
His dexterity in handling the controversial ‘Doctrine of Necessity’ that helped to douse the tension created by the virtual vacuum in the Presidency early this year will go down as one of the finest political solutions ever devised in the history of any nation. This  assessment is without prejudice to the observation by some critics that Mark’s Senate foot-dragged so much that the vacuum created by the prolonged absence of late President Umaru Musa Yar’Adua nearly caused the nation its hard-won democracy. The civil society in particular believed that the National Assembly would have acted earlier to save the country the near destabilization that constitutional crisis  threatened to cause.
However, this senatorial tardiness could be forgiven because between the two chambers of the National Assembly, the Senate still managed to maintain its primacy by belling the cat of Doctrine of Necessity first, thereby literally setting the agenda from which the House of Representatives necessarily took the cue.
Closely related to this is the express approval of necessary funds for the newly reconstituted Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) to urgently begin earnest preparations for next year’s election. Although the budget was considered too big, the Senate, and indeed, the National Assembly appeared to have no choice in the circumstance, than to approve the N87. 7 billion being sought by the Commission. Mark’s remark on the Senate floor on the day the INEC budget was approved is very instructive. He urged his colleagues, while ruling on the heated debate during which most senators expressed utter misgivings over what they called the “arbitrary and embarrassing figure”, to allow the budget bill to be passed in order to ensure credible, free and fair elections in 2011.
He said “in spite of our reservations we have to make sure that INEC conduct free and fair elections. We have gone extra mile to do this in the hope that INEC will conduct a credible elections that would be regarded as credible in the sight of other communities of the world. ” Observers believe that this singular statement is the hallmark of a statesman that the Senate President is. A less experienced and less tactical presiding officer would have displayed unnecessary rigidity where some measure of pragmatism is required to douse a raging or potential conflagration.
Another major landmark of the Mark era is the feat achieved by the amendment to the 1999 Constitution. For the first time since the nation’s independence, the democratic alteration of provisions of an operative constitution was achieved early July this year with the said amendment. The farthest the nation went in its attempt to review any constitution, most of which were military legacies, was in 2006 but the move hit the rocks as the third term agenda was curiously allowed to overshadow the actual need to amend the precarious sections of the constitution.  That the amended constitution is yet to become operational almost two months the National Assembly had completed its assignment is due to the controversy over whether a presidential assent is required or not.  The courts are already sorting out this.
Speaking to THISDAY on the secret of his success so far, Mark said his belief and espousal of collective responsibility and team work as well as the generous Grace of God on him have been his moving spirit and the reason for his accomplishments so far not only as Senate President but also in his entire life endeavours. He said, “I believe in collective responsibility. I believe in teamwork and it is the team spirit of all the senators that has taken us so far. “I owe my success so far to the Grace of Almighty God and to my colleagues, the 190 senators. I thank them for the support and cooperation. The mutual respect and cooperation of all the senators is what has made the success of the Senate”.
To his deputy, Senator Ike Ekweremadu (PDP Enugu), Mark’s “democratic leadership in the Senate has ushered in an unprecedented stability even as his nationalistic articulation and leaning have calmed the polity”. In his own assessment, Senator Victor Ndoma-Egba (PDP, Cross River) described the Senate President as “a very focused leader and consensus builder who is always transparent in dealing with his colleagues”,  adding, “his spirit of consensus building hardly takes the Senate to voting on issues even at closed sessions”. In the view of Senator Patrick Osakwe (PDP, Delta), “Mark is God sent. The action of the Senate under him has kept Nigeria one viz-a-viz the resolution on the doctrine of necessity. That resolution has gone a long way in keeping Nigeria together”.
However, it is not all pluses for the Mark leadership of the Senate these past three years. There are some dark spots, chief among which is the incomprehensible delay, in fact reluctance by Mark’s Senate in passing the Freedom of Information (FOI) Bill. The last Senate, under the equally dynamic Senator Ken Nnamani, had earlier passed the FOI Bill but former President Olusegun Obasanjo refused to sign it into law before the expiration of his tenure. Some observers however believe that if the present Senate is serious about enacting the law, it would have passed it again since the past three years after the exit of Obasanjo with the belief that the country now appears to have a people-oriented President who would not waste time in signing the public-spirited Bill into law.
One issue that is agitating the minds of many Nigerians is the possibility or otherwise of creating new states in the country before the end of the current Senate. At any given opportunity, Mark does not fail to give assurance of the Senate’s readiness to fulfil its own part of the state creation bargain. But how does Mark hope to bring the Senate into fulfilling this oft-repeated promise? He told our correspondent, “The first phase of the Constitution amendment has been completed. In the next stage, we shall focus on creation of states. This is the wish of our people and we believe that creation of more states will ultimately usher in even development and bring governance nearer to the people. Jobs will be created and infrastructural development will take place”.Of course, these are all the lofty benefits of states creation. But is it possible to create more states within the life span of the present Senate? Only time will tell.

Original_Search_Title: Nation to Enter Period of Peace and Maturity
Headline: Kenya: Nation to Enter Period of Peace and Maturity
Body_Text:
Kenya is a colourful country, and nowhere more so than in the world of politics. During the last constitutional referendum in 2005, the opposing sides selected oranges and bananas as their symbols.  The year 2010, meanwhile, is now characterised as the year of the "watermelon". How is one to make sense of the current political, and in turn touristic, situation in this fascinating country?
On August 5th, the vast majority of Kenyans voted "Yes" (with "Yes" supporters donning the colour green) in a referendum  on a new constitution.   In many ways, this is long overdue.  The current constitution was hammered out in Britain in the 60s, when Kenya was granted independence after years of struggle.   On the face of things, the new constitution aims to rid Kenya of its current status quo, decentralize government and clean up Kenyan politics, by devolving power to local government in 47 counties, reducing the powers of the president and making MPs generally more accountable.  There would also be a second chamber of parliament, in place to check the president's powers.
This all sounds very appealing, but the "No" movement in Kenya (characterised by the colour red, for stop) was not without its supporters.  The constitution also contains a land commission, allowing any land that was illegally acquired to be repossessed.  This particular proposal made many in the Rift Valley province, where land is a contentious issue, rather uncomfortable.   Other "No" supporters were Christian Churches, who viewed the new constitutions retention of Muslim Kadhi courts as discriminatory.
Still, despite the overwhelming support shown for the new constitution on August 5th, the battle for a reformed Kenya is not quite over.   Enter those politicians whom the BBC has dubbed "watermelons" – they ostentatiously support the constitution, yet are in fact against it and will work against it if it is eventually introduced.   These Kenyan politicians might be green on the outside, but they're certainly red on the inside.
Keep up with the latest headlines on WhatsApp | LinkedIn
Fruit references aside, the voting in this month's referendum went ahead largely peacefully, with no serious incidents recorded.   This is undoubtedly good news for Kenya's tourism.   Kenyan citizens, Kenyan politicians and anyone who has visited and loves this country will breathe a sigh of relief, and maintain their hopes that Kenya is entering a new era of transparency and peace.   Kenya can now continue on its already rapid path to recovery from the chaos of the disputed 2007 general elections.   According to the Ministry of Tourism, many target market countries, including the United States, South Africa, China, Finland and Russia, had recovered fully by the end of last year.
Tom Powell works for Ziara Safaris, a Kenya tour operator that specialises in private and custom safaris.

Original_Search_Title: Referendum to Change Country Forever - IIEC
Headline: Kenya: Referendum to Change Country Forever - IIEC
Body_Text:
Nairobi — TODAY's referendum will change the country's political landscape and lead to major realignments ahead of the 2012 general election, Interim Independent Electoral Commission chairman Ahmed Issack Hassan predicted yesterday.
Promising to release the referendum results in "record time" through the use of the Electronic Results Transmission System, Issack said the referendum was a defining moment in Kenya's history.
"It will herald a new chapter in our nation's rich history," Issack said at the Bomas of Kenya which is the nerve centre of today's poll.
He asked Kenyans to remain united even after the results are announced and expressed optimism that Kenyans had learnt their lesson and would not allow political differences to convulse the country into violence.
"Unlike what happened in 2005, this time round were are different.
Kenyans have learnt a lesson. We have gone to the brink, to the precipice, almost to a civil war and I don't think we will allow ourselves as a country to go back there again. We have learnt our lessons and we want to prove to ourselves and to the world that we can conduct a peaceful referendum, free and fair, that we will accept the results and move forward," said the IIEC boss.
Similar appeals were made by President Kibaki when he gave his televised address to the nation at 4 pm yesterday. He appealed to Kenyans to vote in large numbers.
"Let us do so peacefully. Let us also embrace one another as brothers and sisters even after the referendum. Let us all play our role in creating a great Kenyan nation, at peace with itself and respected among the greater family of nations," said Kibaki.
He and Prime Minister Raila Odinga have been leading the Yes campaigns for the proposed constitution while Higher Education minister William Ruto, retired President Moi and religious leaders have been pushing for its rejection.
Yesterday Moi denied that he was out to defeat anyone politically.
"I am no longer in competitive politics and neither am l seeking to uplift anyone politically," he said in a statement..
A total of 12,470,433 Kenyans, including 5,605 prisoners, will be eligible to cast their votes today when the polling stations open at 6 am and close at 5pm. There are 27,689 polling stations countrywide.
Some small polling stations will be able to relay their results to both the constituency tallying centres and the IIEC national centre at the Bomas of Kenya very soon after voting ends.
Issack said voting will end at 5pm and counting will start immediately.
Anyone still waiting in the queue will be allowed to vote but access into the polling stations will be blocked after 5pm.
He explained that both old and new generation national identification cards will be used in addition to the voter cards issued by the IIEC.
Hassan said the IIEC had chosen Bomas of Kenya as the venue of the national tallying centre to dissociate it from its predecessor's chaotic management of the December 2007 election results. The now defunct Electoral Commission of Kenya used the Kenyatta International Conference Centre as its national tallying centre in December 2007.
The Bomas of Kenya was established by government in 1971 to preserve, maintain and promote the country's diverse cultural values and to act as a tourist attraction. It was the base for the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission in 2004 which produced the Bomas Draft. A subsitute draft prepared by Attorney General Amos Wako was rejected in the 2005 referendum.
"It is a symbolic measure for us. Maybe we will conclude this journey this time round," Issack said.
The IIEC boss added it was easy to manage security at Bomas of Kenya compared to KICC as it was more secluded.
Yesterday Police Commissioner Mathew Iteere visited Bomas and directed his officers to deal sternly with anyone trying to cause chaos.
Analysts have predicted that the fortunes of key political players depend on the outcome of the poll today.
Raila needs a big win to raise his chances as a presidential candidate in 2012.
Conversely Vice President Kalonzo Musyoka may suffer if Ukambani has a No victory or a weak Yes win.
Narc Kenya leader Martha Karua has managed to cut a niche for herself in the national scene by addressing numerous Yes rallies. Deputy Premier Uhuru Kenyatta has boosted his credibility by his lively campaign for the Yes side.
On the flip-side, Information minister Samuel Poghisio may take a nosedive if Kenyans adopt the proposed constitution in large numbers.
In his campaign William Ruto has proved that he is a dynamic leader but he needs a big No victory in Rift Valley if he is to be a frontrunner in 2012.
A No win would undoubtedly lead to new alliances between Ruto and Yes team members such as Kalonzo and Uhuru.
In the November 2005 referendum the government side (Banana) was defeated by the Orange Democratic Movement of Kenya which later split with Raila establishing ODM and Kalonzo retaining ODM-K. Both ran for the presidency in 2007.
After the Banana defeat, Kibaki sacked the entire cabinet and reconstituted it three weeks later leaving out ministers who had opposed the proposed constitution.
Yesterday Issack said some political parties might continue to use the Red and Green colours selected to symbolise the No and Yes votes.
"Your guess is as good as mine whether the Greens and the Reds can become political movements," said Issack.

Original_Search_Title: Use Oil Revenue to Develop Country's Infrastructure - Economist
Headline: Ghana: Use Oil Revenue to Develop Country's Infrastructure - Economist
Body_Text:
Accra — A development economist, Dr Jeffrey Sachs has advised Ghana to invest her oil revenue in development projects such as construction of more schools, hospitals, roads and other infrastructure that would benefit all Ghanaians.
Speaking at the Millennium Cities Initiative Accra Partners Conference, Dr Sachs said it was important to improve on the country's infrastructure than keep the money in the bank.
The Millennium City Initiative is a project initiated by authorities of Earth Institute, a research and education centre at Columbia University, USA and aimed at helping 11 selected Sub-Saharan cities, to design effective viable strategies to achieve the MDGs.
The project was launched last year, under the theme: "A New Accra for a Better Ghana".
Dr Sachs noted that Accra was a city with great potential but faced with enormous challenges and these challenges notwithstanding, Accra could become an international city compared with any city in the world.
He said when Accra flourished, Ghana and West Africa would follow suit and urged the city authorities to forge a partnership with other stakeholders to accelerate efforts towards a sustainable economic development "bearing in mind that Lagos and Abidjan would always be their competitors".
Dr Sachs called for political will, good economic policies to make the city competitive.
Cataloguing some of the successes chalked by the Accra Metropolitan Assembly (AMA), the Chief Executive, Dr Alfred Vanderpuije mentioned projects such as the Waste to Energy programme that would deal with waste management, the Accra beautification and the use of swap card system.
The AMA, he noted had decided to go 'sinage', a modern technology driven management system whereby billboards, group directional signs, posters and banners engulfing the city would be re-organised to beautify the city as well as enhance the revenue base of the Assembly.
Dr Vanderpuije touched on steps taken by government and the AMA to minimise floods by removing all illegal and unauthorised structures and noted that so far about 600 structures had been demolished in Chemu, Sakaman, Odorkor, Banana Inn and Mataheko.
On roads, he said several roads, walkways and U-drains had been constructed including an 18-kilometre road along the Spintex, East Legon and Adjirigano in addition to a 12-kilometre road at the Guggisberg Avenue and Dansoman Highway under rehabilitation.
Dr Vanderpuije said AMA would continue to demonstrate its commitment to the vision by undertaking activities such as monitoring streets in the Metropolis to ensure that traders did not takeover, ensure proper environmental standards, and enforce building permits on all constructions.
On the up-grading of communities, he said, Chemuana, Mamponse, Mamprobi and Dansoman where allies and drainage system were being constructed, would be replicated in Nima, Maamobi and New Town adding "the Assembly would not renege on its vision".
These, the Accra Mayor noted were the strengths of the Assembly and needed to be completed on schedule to move the vision for a modern city into fruition.
He said there was a new awakening; a day of responsibility and opportunities and asked Ghanaians to reach for high standards to achieve developmental activities for Accra.
Dr Susan Blaustein, Co-Director of Millennium City Initiative (MCI), and Leader of Earth Institute's delegation, said they were analyzing the peculiar problems of Accra and would design appropriate remedies to help transform the city.
There were solidarity messages from the World Bank, University of Ghana and Cities Alliance that urged the city authorities to take the "bull by the horn" and help transform the city.
Under the project, the Earth Institute will partner with the AMA to address some of the city's urban and environmental challenges, and help residents achieve the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).
The initiative is being directed by Dr Sachs, who is leading researchers and scientists from Columbia University and their Ghanaian counterparts to identify practical solutions facing the city and therefore examine the current situation in Accra and study the city's history and promise.
From this knowledge building process and thoughtful exchange, the experts will derive a set of policy recommendations that capitalize on both teams- wide ranging technical expertise, as well as newly available technologies for Accra to ensure that its future becomes sustainable urban landmark.
GNA

Original_Search_Title: When Idi Amin Hosted Pele
Headline: Uganda: When Idi Amin Hosted Pele
Body_Text:
Kampala — Former president Idi Amin hosted Brazilian soccer legend Pelé in Uganda for three days, at a time when Uganda was internationally considered a no-go area. Joe Opio takes us back to the 1976 visit that proved to the international media that Uganda was a safe destination.
Mention Idi Amin and the world will flinch. A genocidal despot with a reputation only comparable to Adolf Hitler's, Amin is still regarded with dread around the universe. Yet, in his motherland Uganda, the fallen dictator isn't entirely a figure of horror. Certainly not among the football fans who, despite knowledge of Amin's deplorable human rights record, are generous enough to remember him as a godsend as far as sports, and football in particular, are concerned.
Amin was notorious for numerous things. To most, he's the bumbling incompetent man, who presided over one of the darkest international regimes after Hitler. To others, he's the bloodthirsty maniac who deported an entire race, sponsored terrorism and was immortalised by Forest Whitaker's Oscar-winning performance in The Last King Of Scotland.
To many Ugandans, however, Amin's universal caricature as presidential clown is both amusing and mystifying. True, Amin was prone to the kind of international faux pas that would have left any self-respecting circus clown blushing. True, the semi-illiterate soldier was given to sudden eruptions of quirkiness that most diplomats considered unbecoming of a head of state. But, as Ugandans will say, that oddball image concealed a cunning man; that buffoonish face hid a shrewd mind.
This much was proven when Amin overthrew the well-schooled Dr. Milton Obote in a bloodless coup. It was further demonstrated by the dictator's political manoeuvres, especially his inspired decision to exploit football as a propaganda tool.
When Amin usurped state power, he was instantly cast as a pariah by an international community determined to dissuade such blatant abuse of democracy. Isolated, Amin went on to alienate any remaining international friends when he embarked first on a purge of his political enemies, and then the implementation of a racist economic strategy that deported Asian minorities from Uganda and nationalised all their businesses.
Such policies were always bound to attract bad press from the foreign media. Amin realised as much and shrewdly decided to counter any such negativity with a publicity coup that would make good reading for all PR students.
Treading a well-beaten path
The year was 1976. As a result of Amin's deplorable human rights record, most countries had blacklisted Uganda as a tourist destination. The international media was hysterically tarring Uganda with the kind of brush that would befit 21st-century North Korea. Amin, desperate to prove that his country wasn't as bad a security risk as portrayed, hit upon a wily scheme: he would invite one of the high-profile figures in the world for a state-sponsored visit. And that's how Uganda came to play host to Edison Arantes do Nascimento, a.k.a. Pelé.
The Brazilian legend, talisman of the iconic World Cup-winning team just six years earlier, arrived in Uganda at his prime. And by the time he left three days later, Amin had secured a 'PR coup' that left the international press eating truckloads of humble pie.
In all fairness, Amin wasn't the first autocrat to exploit sport as a propaganda tool. Despotic regimes, by nature, exist on a desperate need for internal and international goodwill to attain some semblance of legitimacy.
Hitler tried to hijack the 1936 Olympics to showcase his dodgy hypothesis of Aryan supremacy; Mobutu Sésé Seko dispatched a planeload of witchdoctors to inspire the Zairean team at the 1974 World Cup, and Sani Abacha's tyranny coincided with Nigeria's finest triumphs in international football. But Amin's success at luring the most recognisable footballer at the time to his lair remains a feat without parallel.
Just to put the visit in perspective, Pelé was a megastar with universal appeal. Amin was a man with blood-soaked paws. Pelé's Brazil was an all-conquering force that had mesmerised an entire world in 1970 en route to winning the Jules Rimet trophy and retaining it for good. Amin's Uganda was a basket-case banana republic shunned by a sickened international community and used as an example of a failed state.
Yet Amin persuaded Pelé to visit Uganda, spend three days on a state-sponsored vacation, conduct a football drills clinic in the national stadium and fly around the country in a presidential chopper, with the international press corps in tow.
Fred Sekitto, a veteran sports journalist and founder of the Uganda Sports Press Association (USPA), covered Pelé's visit and remains awed that it actually happened at all. "It's perhaps the biggest international visit Uganda has ever seen. And remember we hosted Queen Elizabeth of Britain in 2007 and even then-US President Bill Clinton in 2003. In fact, before Pelé's visit, the famous English player Stanley Matthews had also been to Uganda.
But Pelé's visit overshadowed all those visits.
Pelé was way bigger than the Queen or Clinton." He continues: "Pelé's visit back then was the biggest event that calendar year. We previewed it from January of 1976 up to when he landed at Entebbe International Airport. We dedicated front-page headlines to his every move during his stay and then reviewed it the rest of the year when he departed.
"1976 was Pelé's Year as far as Ugandans are concerned. To put it in context, right now, it would be like Cuba persuading both Cristiano Ronaldo and Lionel Messi to visit Havana. Pelé was even bigger than both Ronaldo and Messi put together and yet he was visiting Uganda, which was an international outcast."
In preparation for Pelé's visit, UTV, the national broadcaster, and Radio Uganda, a state-funded station, had run documentaries highlighting the Brazilian's prowess and triumphs on an almost daily basis. Voice of Uganda, the state-owned newspaper, dedicated much of its sports pages to the latest news about Pelé while rehashing his successes ad nauseam.
Sekitto, who worked for Voice of Uganda then and whose career spanned the better half of four decades, confesses: "Personally, Pelé's visit was the highlight of my sports career. The day he arrived was an unofficial public holiday. No one went to work that day, apart from us journalists. People locked up their businesses and simply stood by the roadside waiting for Pelé.
The only visit that might actually compare to Pelé's is the 1993 visit of Pope John Paul II. It was huge, but it is debatable whether it was more significant than Pelé's. The Pope's visit was at a time when Uganda's image had been given an extreme makeover under President Yoweri Museveni, who had erased the errors of his predecessors and turned Uganda into the darling of the international media.
Pelé's visit, on the other hand, came smack in the middle of the darkest chapter in Ugandan history. Sekitto, a staunch Catholic and a renowned Church leader, admits he can't picture the same Pope visiting Uganda, if it had been as much an international black sheep in 1993 as it was in 1976 under Amin. "It could have been seen as legitimising a horrible regime," Sekitto reasons.
Sekitto has only fond memories of Pelé's visit. As the leading sports reporter of that era, he had an all-access pass to the superstar and even flew with him on Amin's presidential chopper. "Pelé's visit was ostensibly organised by Pepsi, a multinational beverages firm," Sekitto reveals. "But from the moment Pelé set foot on Ugandan soil, the State took over. He was received at the airport by an ensemble of state ministers and high-ranking military officers."
After that red-carpet reception, Pelé was allowed to address the media in a strictly controlled conference, where awkward political questions were off the menu.
"The press officer and the security liaison made it clear that Pelé was in Uganda as a football ambassador and whatever questions we intended to ask must be football-related," Sekitto reminisces. Given that Amin's security apparatus was not known to react to dissent with kid gloves, the media complied and kept their questions within the prescribed boundaries.
Press duties done, Pelé was then chauffeur driven through the streets, where he was simply amazed by the throngs of people that had lined up in anticipation to greet him. "Everyone was aware of who Pelé was and everyone wanted to catch a glimpse of this legend.
Tens of thousands of people had turned up, braving the sweltering heat to just see him in person." Sekitto recalls Pelé's convoy snaking its way from Entebbe to the State House for a dinner with an excited Amin. Amin, an incurable showman with enough zing to make Don King appear media-shy, had watched in envy as his friend and neighbour Mobutu hosted Muhammad Ali's Rumble In The Jungle in 1974.
He thus treated Pelé's visit as an appropriate restoration of parity.
Sekitto comments: "Amin was a boxing fan who had watched enviously as Ali's historic fight against George Foreman was staged right next door. He would have loved to host such an international spectacle. But when that passed him by, he saw Pelé's visit as an opportunity to replicate Mobutu's Rumble In The Jungle."
At the dinner, a beaming Amin implored Pelé to help Ugandan football reach the next level. He asked Pelé for advice on how Uganda could become like Brazil. Amin, who shared a healthy rivalry with Mobutu, told Pelé that, having watched Zaire qualify for the 1974 World Cup, he wanted Uganda to do the same and even go on to win the trophy, unlike Zaire, which had been humiliated 9-0 by Yugoslavia.
Amin, ever the wily operator, had ensured that the entire Ugandan national team, which had qualified for two continental (Africa Nations Cup) tournaments without making any impact, was present at the dinner to feast its eyes on the idol. (Just a year later, the same national team that attended the dinner went on to qualify for the 1978 Nations Cup, where their quest for the ultimate crown was only sabotaged by a 2-0 win in the final by home team Ghana. That 1978 close call remains Uganda's finest footballing moment).
"Pelé imparted a few words of wisdom to the players, concerning discipline and team spirit," Sekitto recalls. "He then interacted freely and cheerfully with all of the gathered guests. He was in a very light mood and his easy manner and trademark down-to-earth smile pleased and endeared him to all present."
Pelé spent the night at the presidential palace. And in the morning, his state-funded vacation kicked off in earnest. Given carte blanche to commandeer the presidential chopper, Pelé flew in it to the picturesque Queen Elizabeth National Game Park. "He spent two days at Hotel Margherita within the Game Park, enjoying the sights and sounds of Uganda's favoured tourist destination.
"The State was taking care of his every whim and upkeep. I had travelled with him in the presidential chopper and I was with him throughout his stay at the Park. He really was a pleasant and even obliging person. You never could have predicted with the way he was so humble that he was the superstar who had captured the imagination of millions all over the globe," Sekitto recalls.
Pelé's two-day stay involved guided tours of the game park and leisurely rides on the presidential yacht down the river that runs through the park.
"Amin ensured that Pelé got the best possible treatment. It was an inspired way of selling Uganda's image abroad. The visit attracted the international media and Amin wanted nothing, nothing whatsoever to besmirch Pelé's stay," Sekitto says.
Pelé was to return to Kampala to conduct a football drills clinic with children aged between seven and 10 at Nakivubo Stadium.
Hillary Nsambu, another seasoned scribe who monitored Pelé's visit, states: "He wasn't going to play an exhibition match. He was just going to take children through basic drills passing, ball control and dribbling. Still, that didn't stop a massive crowd from storming the stadium.
"It was over capacity and the military had to lock the gates and stop the uncontrollable horde outside from gaining entry. Everyone wanted to see Pelé with a ball, whether it was in his hands or at his feet was beside the point. I actually haven't seen Nakivubo Stadium that full to capacity ever again."
Pelé left the country later that evening, having proved Amin's consistent assertion to the international media that Uganda was a safe destination. If a superstar could enjoy himself here for three days, what more reassuring advert did other foreigners need?

Original_Search_Title: Will There Be Justice at Home For Africa?
Headline: Cameroon: Will There Be Justice at Home For Africa?
Body_Text:
SAMUEL Eto'o is the ultimate specimen of an African footballer. Loud and spontaneous, capable of a little ostentation, generous (he gave the Cameroon team $700,000 worth of diamond-encrusted time pieces), hard-skinned, optimistic to the point of being a dreamer and talented - with a few rough edges. The only trait lacking was temper. Until a few days ago.
Appearing before a panel of French journalists, Eto'o blared with rage, hitting the table to send microphones tumbling. He smacked one bewildered newsman with a flying microphone in the face before storming out of the room.
What triggered him off? Roger Milla. Not the man but the mention of his name.
"Eto'o brought lots to Barcelona and Inter but never anything to Cameroon," is what the 1990 World Cup star said. The French scribes wished they hadn't brought it up.
Coward Of The County
Like The Coward Of The County in Kenny Rogers' classic country hit, "twenty years of crawlin' was bottled up inside him, he wasn't holding nothing back he let them (the journalists) have it all…" In Eto'o's case it's 14 years of being under-appreciated.
When he arrived in Barcelona in 2004, he famously said he had to "run like a black man to live like a white man."
"What I was trying to say," he told the Guardian recently, "is that as an African I need to do more than others to be recognised at the same level."
For a man who has had bananas and peanuts thrown at him in stadiums across Spain and Italy; a man who was once forced to celebrate a goal (at Real Zaragoza) by jumping up and down like a monkey, who once cried when accosted by a racist Mallorca fan (when he played for the club), Eto'o appears to have over reacted to Milla's banal assessment of his national team career.
A man who has suffered the prejudice of having his goals put down to the creativity of Barcelona's midfield rather than his finishing ability, and was a makeweight in a deal to purchase a striker with half his abilities should be able to take everything in stride, right? Wrong! Anybody who thinks like that missed the point of Eto'o's rage.
Denied justice
The Inter Milan striker can take it from those white-washed juries in Europe because he expects nothing less. But hearing the same condescending appraisal from one of his own was particularly galling.
That, unfortunately, is a major weakness on this continent. In spite of a whole generation separating the two, Milla feels that slighting Eto'o adds to his own greatness or placement in history. It's a sad sight when African greats behave like co-wives fighting for the attention of a mutual lover.Poor Eto'o. Denied justice in Europe and now also back home. No wonder he lost his head.
Speaking of justice, the Cameroon striker said he watched the tape of England beating his country on the 1990 quarter-finals, through two Gary Lineker penalties and said: "I think the world wasn't ready for an African team to reach the semi-finals."
"The question we have all the time is whether an African team is able to win the World Cup. But the real question is whether the world is ready for an African team to become champions."
Joseph Kabuleta is a Sunday Vision columnist

Original_Search_Title: PNU Start Dodging Rallies
Headline: Kenya: PNU Start Dodging Rallies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — PNU Cabinet ministers are starting to avoid the joint Yes rallies conducted by President Mwai Kibaki and Prime Minister Raila Odinga.
Along with PNU MPs, they were conspicuously absent from the well-attended weekend rally at Embakasi addressed by Kibaki and Raila.
The ministers and MPs have apparently decided to silently boycott future rallies as they believe Raila has hijacked the Yes campaign to strengthen his presidential credentials before 2012.
No PNU Cabinet ministers attended the Saturday rally to push for a Yes vote in the August referendum on the proposed constitution.
Kibaki and Raila were only accompanied by five ministers from ODM: Regional Development Fred Gumo, Medical Services minister Anyang' Nyong'o, Industrialisation moinister Henry Kosgey, Fisheries minister Paul Otuoma, and Lands minister James Orengo.
Energy minister Kiraitu Murungi sent apologies through the joint Yes co-convenor Nyong'o as he is in South Africa on official business.
The only PNU MP who attended was local MP Ferdinand Waititu who was heckled by the crowd over land matters.
Five ODM MPs attended including Nominated MP Rachel Shebesh, Elizabeth Ongoro of Kasarani, John Mbadi of Gwassi, Mohammed Hussein of Mandera Central and Alfred Khangati, Kanduyi.
The Yes campaign secretariat led by ODM's Janet Ong'era and PNU's Peter Kagwanja is expected to meet today at their KICC offices to release the programme for their countrywide rallies.
Vice President Kalonzo Musyoka returned to Kenya on Saturday evening from Juba after representing President Kibaki at the inauguration of Salva Kiir as president of Southern Sudan.
Close confidants of Kalonzo told the Star yesterday that it was unlikely that Kalonzo would have attended the Embakasi rally considering his humiliation after being booed by hecklers during the inaugural rally held at Uhuru Park two weeks ago.
"He still remembers that Uhuru Park rally and he will not want to embarrass himself by giving Raila another opportunity to humiliate him," the sources added.
After the heckling, Kalonzo indicated that he was consulting on whether to continue participating in the Yes rallies.
However Kalonzo told reporters at JKIA on Saturday, "What happened after Uhuru Park is now history" and promised to attend future rallies.
Kalonzo was represented at the Embakasi rally by his close confidants, Kangundo MP Johnstone Muthama and Kibwezi MP Philip Kaloki.
PNU ministers who attended the inaugural rally two weeks ago but were missing at the Embakasi meeting included deputy Prime Minister Uhuru Kenyatta, Public Health minister Beth Mugo, PNU chairman and Internal Security minister George Saitoti and Gender minister Esther Murugi.
"Personally I missed the event because I was attending a funeral service in Nyahururu and I know Uhuru might have been attending another event somewhere in Central," said former Starehe MP and Nairobi PNU chapter co-chair Maina Kamanda.
He said some PNU MPs failed to attend the rally because the venue was changed at the last minute.
"Initially the rally was to take place in Nyeri but was changed to Embakasi since it was easier to mobilize people to attend at short notice as opposed to Nyeri," he added.
Kamanda said people should not read too much in the absence of the PNU Ministers, MPs from the rally.
"There is no fallout in the Yes team. The ministers and MPs have reasons not to attend the rally but that does not mean we are divided. If the President and PM as coalition partners address the joint rallies, that means their respective parties are ably represented," he said.
Close associates of Uhuru said he should not be expected to attend rallies held in every constituency. They said the Yes rally held in Embakasi was a constituency rally and not a national one.
"Uhuru will only attend national rallies for the Yes group, but you cannot expect him to attend every local rally. If he fails to attend national rallies or major regional rallies like in Kisumu, Nakuru, or Mombasa, then you can ask the questions," said one of his confidantes yesterday Saturday's perceived boycott of the rally by PNU will encourage the No campaign against the proposed constitution spearheaeded by Higher education minister William Ruto who claims that most ministers support him.
Another PNU MP said his colleagues stayed away from the rally because ODM and Raila were stealing the show in the Yes camp.
"He is bullying everyone by saying the Yes campaign was a government project. He has forgotten Kiraitu's utterances that cost the banana side a win in 2005," said the vocal Central MP.
"It is such arrogant statements from the PM and his other conspiracies to embarrass leaders like the VP that we do not want to associate with," the PNU MP said.
During the Embakasi rally on Saturday, Raila beat a hasty retreat and said that public funds will not be used to meet the cost of the Yes campaign. He however reiterated that it was a government project to support the proposed constitutiuon.
Cabinet resolved on April 27 to jointly campaign for a Yes vote.
The PNU leadership has become uncomfortable with ODM mobilising Kenyans to attend the rallies.
"The party fears that such a mistake will give the party an opportunity to bring majority of their supporters to the Yes rally which they will use to campaign for 2012,"said a PNU party official.

Original_Search_Title: Court Accuses French Lawyer of Racism
Headline: Tanzania: ICTR Trial Chamber Accuses French Lawyer of 'Racism'
Body_Text:
Arusha — Trial Chamber III of the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR) Monday accused a French lawyer, Vincent Courcelle-Labrousse, of using abusive language with racial connotations against the bench.
"Defence counsel made several utterances which may amount to abusive language with racial connotations," alleged Ugandan presiding Judge, Solomy Balungi Bossa, at the beginning of the afternoon session in the on-going trial of the genocide-accused former Rwandan Youth Minister, Callixte Nzabonimana.
The French lawyer is the lead defence counsel for the accused.
Judge Bossa, flanked by Judges Mparany Rajohson from Madagascar and Bakhtiyar Tuzmukhamedov from Russia, alleged that the counsel went as far as referring the Chamber as "Mongolian Chamber" and the whole Tribunal "a Banana Republic".
"This is an insult not only to the Chamber but also to the whole Tribunal," Judge Bossa charged, demanding an explanation from the lawyer.
Responding, the French lawyer quickly said he had no knowledge of "what the Chamber was talking about". Judge Bossa took the trouble to explain further that the words were spoken among the members of the defence team shortly before lunch-break.
The defence counsel was quick to intervene by saying: "Anything stated outside the hearing process is beyond the jurisdiction of the Trial Chamber."
"The Chamber seizes the matter and will act according to the rules of this Tribunal," Presiding Judge said and closed the discussion.
Nzabonimana is facing five charges-- genocide, conspiracy to commit genocide, direct and public incitement to commit genocide, extermination and murder. He has denied the charges.
His defence case started on April 14. The prosecution presented 19 witnesses and concluded its case on April 13.

Original_Search_Title: MDC-T Snub Iranian Leader
Headline: Zimbabwe: MDC-T Snub Iranian Leader
Body_Text:
The MDC led by Prime Minister Morgan Tsvangirai snubbed the arrival ceremony of Iranian leader Mahmoud Ahmadinejad at the Harare International Airport on Thursday describing the visit as a 'scandal.' The ZANU PF part of the coalition government led by Robert Mugabe invited him as their special guest of honour at this year's Zimbabwe International Trade Fair (ZITF) in Bulawayo.
A smaller faction of the MDC led by Deputy Prime Minister Arthur Mutambara was also not at the airport. His Secretary General Welshman Ncube however claimed most of their cabinet ministers were already in Bulawayo attending an economic forum discussion at the ZITF. He denied there was ever a boycott of the visit by his party.
The MDC-T issued a statement saying, 'As a party, we feel that a country is defined by its friends. We want to place it on record that judging by his record, Ahmadinejad is coming not as a friend of Zimbabwe, but as an ally of those that unilaterally invited him.' The party likened inviting Ahmadinejad to an investment forum to 'inviting a mosquito to cure malaria,' adding that, 'hobnobbing with dubious political leaders confirms stereotypes that we are a banana republic.'
ZANU PF went out to put on a show for the Iranian leader according him a 21-gun salute and inspection of a guard of honour. He was to later tour Chitungwiza based textile company Modzone which is financed by Iran and the Willowvale Mazda Motor Industries, which Iran is also said to be planning to invest in. Reports say Iran is the biggest exhibitor at the Trade Fair which for years has been dogged by a low number of exhibitors because of the devastating economic crisis under the ZANU PF regime.
It was no surprise on Friday when Mugabe and Ahmadinejad took turns to accuse the West of trying to destroy the economies of their countries. "Our nations have a bitter experience of intervention by those big powers. They want to seize the markets of the countries and destroy their economies," Ahmadinejad said as he opened the trade fair. Mugabe responded by backing Iran's controversial nuclear programme which has seen the imposition of US backed economic, trade, scientific and military sanctions against Iran.
The similarities between Ahmadinejad and Mugabe are there for all to see. Both held on to power via elections which were marred by violence and allegations of fraud. The visit brought to life the saying 'birds of a feather flock together.'

Original_Search_Title: Prime Minister Snubs Iran's President
Headline: Zimbabwe: MDC Snubs Ahmadinejad's Arrival Ceremony
Body_Text:
IRANIAN President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad's visit has exposed differences between the country's three main political parties after the Morgan Tsvangirai-led MDC snubbed his arrival ceremony at Harare International Airport yesterday.
Prime Minister Tsvangirai and senior government officials from his party did not turn up to welcome the Iranian leader describing his visit as a "scandal". Even Deputy Prime Minister Arthur Mutambara and ministers from his MDC faction did not turn up to receive the guest of honour to this year's Zimbabwe International Trade Fair (ZITF).
"As a party, we feel that a country is defined by its friends," the MDC-T said. "We want to place it on record that judging by his record, Ahmadinejad is coming not as a friend of Zimbabwe, but as an ally of those that unilaterally invited him."
Ahmadinejad arrived in the country yesterday amid tight security from both the Zimbabwean security forces and the Iranian leader's security team that were milling around the airport.
Four fighter jets were flying over the airport for more than 30 minutes before the Iranian leader arrived in his official Islamic Republic of Iran plane marked EP-AJD. There were ululations from Zimbabwe's Muslim community and praise singing from Apostolic church members.
Helicopters flew over Harare for the better part of the day before Ahmadinejad's arrival and continued to fly hours later in a show of protection for the Islamic leader who is currently engaged in a war of words with the United States and its allies.
Ahmadinejad took close to seven minutes before disembarking because the stairway was not properly fitted to the giant aircraft.
Employees from the National Handling Services had to run around to fix the stairway with close supervision from Ahmadinejad's security personnel.
The Iranian leader was received by a 21-gun salute and inspected a guard of honour before leaving the airport to visit Modzone, a textile company based in Chitungwiza and financed by Iran, and Willowvale Mazda Motor Industries.
Ahmadinejad will officially open the ZITF today before leaving the country for Uganda later in the day, according to official information from the Iranian Embassy.
Political tension within the country's fragile inclusive government is likely to worsen after the MDC led by Tsvangirai described Ahmadinejad's invitation as a unilateral Zanu PF decision.
"Choice of friends defines character and inviting the Iranian strongman to an investment forum is like inviting a mosquito to cure malaria," the party said. "Hobnobbing with dubious political leaders confirms stereotypes that we are a banana republic."
But Welshman Ncube, the secretary-general of the Mutambara-led MDC, said there was no boycott by his party because most ministers were already in Bulawayo attending the economic forum discussion that was going on at the ZITF.
"There was certainly no boycott (by his party) because most of the ministers are already in Bulawayo. I was also in Bulawayo but have returned (to Harare) for the Iranian president's tour of Modzone and the Willowvale Mazda Motor Industries," said Ncube.
Information minister Webster Shamu declined to comment about the "snub" arguing that he was not the MDC spokesperson.
