"Why should I be talking to you about someone who is not here? I am not their spokesperson so you rather go and ask them why they are not here," said Shamu before walking off to join the kilometres-long motorcade.
Iran and Ahmadinejad in particular has been under harsh criticism from Western nations for pressing ahead with uranium enrichment programmes it says are to produce nuclear energy amid fears the militant Islamic state could develop nuclear weapons.
Iran is the biggest exhibitor at the trade exposition Ahmadinejad is scheduled to open.
Ahmadinejad is the first leader from outside the African continent to open the exposition since Independence.

Original_Search_Title: Treasure Forest
Headline: Uganda: Treasure Forest
Body_Text:
Kampala — This low-lying tropical forest in Uganda has a national park - Semuliki. It has several fascinating features. Frederick Womakuyu discovered that it's more than just a game park.
A chill goes down my spine as the vehicle stops under a thick tree canopy. In front of us is a signpost "Welcome to Semuliki National Park." As I recover my breath, two men on my right seated on a bench in a cozy compound, walk up to us. "Welcome to Semuliki.
How can we help you?" they asked, before I told them I had come to enjoy the natural atmosphere of the park.
Employed at the park as ranger guides, the two gentlemen direct me to a small office, where I register upon payment of the sh8,000 gate fee.
Ranger Samson Dhiwerera walks me to a small museum like study room, and points to the stone shaped map of the park that is placed in a glass box to protect it from vandals.
The map shows the location of the eye-catching Sempaya Hot Springs. It also indicates the location of the park in the Bughendera Constituency, about 23km from Bundibugyo town in western Uganda.
Dhiwerera explains that Semuliki National Park lies on a low elevation, between 760m to 770m above sea level.
"It is the only low lying tropical forest in Uganda and a continuation of the Ituri forest in DR Congo.
Whatever you learn about in eastern Congo, you also find in Uganda." Covering 220 square kilometers, the park was gazetted in 1993 under the management of the then Uganda National Parks and Games Department, because of its thick, vegetation.
Dhiwerera says the park's conservation strategy has been changing since the colonial period. "It started as a local forest which belonged to the Bamba and Bakonjo people for wood, wild meat and herbal medicine.
But that was before the Government upgraded it to a reserve in 1930."
During that period, it was called Bwamba Forest, then Semiliki Forest Reserve, under the management of the Forest Authority.
The Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) took over its management from 1986 to date, and named it Semuliki National Park.
At Semuliki National Park the tourist attractions include the Sempaya Hot Springs, bird watching, forest/nature walks and camping.
Surrounding the park are the Batwa people. They are small (about one metre tall on average) with a unique culture of not intermarrying.
These people used to live in the forest, gathering fruits and hunting game meat. They use herbal medicine and often do not seek treatment in hospitals.
They are in themselves a tourist attraction. "But they were evicted to give way for the protection of the forest." To supplement their income, they make baskets, ear rings, chairs and many other crafts."
He explains that whenever tourists visit the park, the Batwa entertain them at a fee. Most do not go to school and are therefore not formally educated.
Twelve kilometres to the east of the park is the great meandering River Semuliki that flows in a zig- zag pattern. The river has become a great tourist attraction with at least three foreign tourists visiting it everyday.
According to Dhiwerera, a group of colonial administrators were passing by the river when they met a Mwamba woman carrying an empty basket was from the market.
They asked her the name of the river, but because she didnt understand thier question she responded: "Semulikikindu" - meaning there's nothing in the basket. So the colonialists thought she meant the river was called Semuliki and the name stuck.
Following the Sempaya and Bumaga trail network, one of the five trails in the park, we are welcomed by the steam from the boiling Sempaya Hot Springs. Two foreign tourists lying down with their legs spread apart.
As the water bubbles and boils, the tourists throw in their eggs and fresh bananas. Just after 20 minutes the eggs and the bananas are ready to eat. The volcanic activity below the ground is what makes the water hot.
When magma comes in contact with cold water, it causes it to boils. That is how hot springs are formed.
But that is as far as scientists can reason, Dhiwerera says, adding, "The Bamba though, believe the hot springs are the work of their gods."
He explains that although they do not explain the process, the local people believe that the hot springs were created by their gods and have a spiritual significance.
He says they have divided the springs into the female and male Sempaya Hot Springs using the knowledge of the local Bamba people. "The local people believe that when they bathe in the water, they are healed.
But during that process, they slaughter a hen and a cock to appease the female and male hot springs." One is called the male hot spring because it is in form of a pool and the other female because it comes out of the ground in form of scattered water basins, Dhiwerera says.
He adds that before the 1990s, the Bamba used to converge at the hot springs every six months to honour different events and their pay respects to the gods.
"They used to bathe in the water, to honour the female and male hot springs. But that changed when the springs were brought under the control of UWA. However, we allowed them to continue carrying out thier activities like rites for twins at the hot springs.
Semuliki National Park has about 400 bird species and over 250 species of butterflies and moths. It also has 300 species of trees and shrubs and 53 mammals, including monkeys, baboons, bush bucks, crocodiles and leopards.
Dhiwerera says the park reunits 20% of tourist gate collections, to the nearby communities.
"We also allow them to get firewood, medicine and fruits from the forest. But more importantly, we market and sell their crafts and provide safe piped water for them."
Amos Kule, a resident of the area, says: "We put UWA under pressure to provide us with this water. They are, exploiting our forest resources, but at least we also get water.".

Original_Search_Title: Country's Imports Rise Eight Percent
Headline: Rwanda: Country's Imports Rise Eight Percent
Body_Text:
Kigali — The National Bank of Rwanda (NBR) has said that the value of imports increased by 8 percent from Rwf1.2 billion in 2008 to Rwf1.3 billion last year.
The bank said that there was a sharp rise in major imports including food, construction materials, intermediate goods and other final consumption goods.
"Imports in 2009 were expected to grow even higher but the proportion is manageable considering the fact that it was in the middle of the global financial crisis," Vivian Mwitirehe, Director of Statistics in the NBR said in an interview.
The Central Bank also said that in terms of volume, total imports increased to 993 tonnes in 2009 from 848.5 tonnes in the previous year.
"While this reflected stronger spending, increase in imports was not the key driver of domestic inflation," Mwitirehe said.
She added that Rwanda's economic growth is likely to rise this year.
The high demand for imports increased Rwanda's trade deficit as exports dropped by as much as 25 percent, a decrease that is largely attributed to the global economic recession.
Mwitirehe said as much as exports decreased and reduced on foreign exchange, government also needed foreign currencies because it improved on the imports receipts.
Export earnings dropped from Rwf272.7 million in 2008 to 192.8 million last year. In volume it reduced to 103 tonnes from 121.6 tonnes in 2009 and 2008 respectively.
The drop is registered in minerals, horticultural produce like bananas, fresh fruits, and beans. In other exports there is also a decrease in handcrafts, hides and skins exports and tourism.

Original_Search_Title: Gahondogo to Replace MP Kanzayire
Headline: Rwanda: Gahondogo to Replace MP Kanzayire
Body_Text:
Kigali — Athanasie Gahondogo, a former legislator, is returning to Parliament to replace Bernadette Kanzayire on the Rwanda Patriotic Front (RPF) ticket
According a recent release by the National Electoral Commission, Gahondogo replaces Kanzayire following the latter's appointment as the deputy Ombudsman.

Original_Search_Title: President Meets With International Advisers
Headline: Mozambique: Guebuza Meets With International Advisers
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambican President Armando Guebuza on Friday met with a group of international advisers to discuss the balance-sheet of the reforms under way in the country, particularly in the economy.
The meeting, attended by members of the government and by 16 of Guebuza's international advisers, also aimed to discuss how the government can speed up its development plans.
